I wanted to see how they'd tackle the question — where they'd start in encouraging the young man to pursue wisdom and virtue. Dionysodorus, the older brother, spoke first. Everyone's eyes were on him, expecting something amazing. And they weren't far wrong, because the man, Crito, launched into a truly remarkable speech — wonderfully persuasive, if you think of it as an encouragement to virtue.

"Tell me," he said, "Socrates, and the rest of you who say you want this young man to become wise — are you joking, or are you actually serious?"

This made me think they'd assumed we were joking earlier when we asked them to talk with the boy, and that's why they'd been joking around and not being serious. So I was even more emphatic: "We are absolutely, profoundly serious."

Dionysodorus said, "Think carefully, Socrates. You might end up having to take back your words."

"I've thought carefully," I said. "And I'll never take back my words."

"All right," he said. "So you're saying you want Cleinias to become wise?"

"Absolutely."

"And right now he's not wise?"

"Well, his modesty wouldn't allow him to claim he is."

"So you want him to become wise," Dionysodorus said, "and to stop being ignorant?"

"That's right."

"You want him to become what he is not, and to no longer be what he is?"

I was completely thrown off by this.

Pressing his advantage while I was still reeling, he added: "You want him to no longer be what he is — which can only mean you want him to die. What lovely friends and admirers you must be, wanting your dear boy to cease existing!"

When Ctesippus heard this, he got seriously angry — as any good boyfriend would — and said, "Stranger from Thurii — if politeness allowed it I'd say something a lot worse — how dare you tell such a disgusting lie about me and the others, that we want Cleinias to die! It's practically unspeakable!"

Euthydemus cut in: "And do you think, Ctesippus, that it's actually possible to tell a lie?"

"Yes," said Ctesippus. "I'd have to be insane to think otherwise."

"When you tell a lie, do you say the thing you're speaking about, or not?"

"You say the thing you're speaking about."

"And the person who says a thing says that thing and no other?"

"Yes," said Ctesippus.

"And that thing he says is one definite thing, distinct from everything else?"

Yes.

"And the person who says that thing says what is?"

Yes.

"And whoever says what is speaks the truth. So Dionysodorus, if he's saying what is, is speaking the truth about you and telling no lie."

"Yes, Euthydemus," said Ctesippus, "but when he says what he said, he's saying what is not."

Euthydemus replied: "And what is not — doesn't exist?"

Right.

"And what doesn't exist is nowhere?"

Right.

"And can anyone do anything to what doesn't exist? Can anyone do to Cleinias something that doesn't exist and is nowhere?"

"I guess not," said Ctesippus.

"Well then — when politicians speak in the assembly, they're doing something, aren't they?"

"Yes, they're doing something," he said.

"And doing is a kind of making?"

Yes.

"And speaking is doing, which is making?"

Yes.

"Then nobody says what is not — because in saying it he'd be doing something, and you've already agreed that nobody can do what doesn't exist. Therefore, by your own logic, nobody speaks falsely. So if Dionysodorus says anything at all, he's saying what is true."

"Yes, Euthydemus," said Ctesippus, "but he's talking about things in a way that doesn't match how they actually are."

"Ctesippus," said Dionysodorus, "are you claiming that anyone talks about things as they actually are?"

"Yes," he said. "Any decent, honest person does."

"And aren't good things good and bad things bad?"

He agreed.

"And you say that decent people describe things as they are?"

Yes.

"Then decent people speak badly of bad things — since they describe things as they are!"

"They certainly do," said Ctesippus. "And they speak badly of bad people too. So let me give you some friendly advice — watch out, or they'll speak badly of you, since I can assure you that good people speak badly of the bad."

"And do they speak big things of the big," Euthydemus jumped in, "and hot things of the hot?"

"Absolutely," said Ctesippus. "And they speak coldly of those who are cold — the way these cold dialecticians are."

"You're being abusive, Ctesippus," said Dionysodorus. "You are being abusive!"

"Not at all, Dionysodorus," he replied. "I actually like you, and I'm just giving you friendly advice. I'm trying to convince you, as a friend, not to be so rude as to say right in front of me that I want my beloved — whom I value above all people — to die."

I could see they were getting heated with each other, so I cracked a joke. "Ctesippus," I said, "I think we have to let our visitors use language however they like and not fight over words. We should be grateful for what they give us. If they know how to 'destroy' people in a way that turns bad, foolish people into good, wise ones — whether this is their own discovery or something they learned — this new kind of death and destruction that lets them get rid of a bad person and turn him into a good one — if they know how to do this, and they clearly do (they just told us that their newly invented art was exactly this) — then let them, in their own terminology, destroy the young man and make him wise. The rest of us too, if they like. But if you young people are nervous about being their test subjects, then let the experiment be tried on old me! Like Medea and her cauldron — I'll offer my elderly person to Dionysodorus. He can kill me, boil me, do whatever he wants — as long as he makes me good in the end."

Ctesippus said, "And I'm volunteering too, Socrates! Let the strangers skin me alive if they want — I'm pretty well skinned by them already — as long as my skin ends up not like poor Marsyas's, turned into a leather bag, but into something made of virtue. And here's Dionysodorus thinking I'm angry with him, when I'm not angry at all. I'm just pushing back when I think he's saying something wrong about me. But don't confuse contradiction with abuse, my illustrious Dionysodorus — they're completely different things."

"Contradiction?" said Dionysodorus. "There's no such thing as contradiction."

"Of course there is," said Ctesippus. "There can't be any question about it. You're not going to tell me, Dionysodorus, that there's no such thing?"

"You'll never prove to me," he said, "that you've ever heard one person contradict another."

"Really?" said Ctesippus. "Then listen right now, because you can hear me contradicting Dionysodorus."

"Can you back that up?"

"Certainly," he said.

"All right then. Doesn't everything have words that describe it?"

Yes.

"Describing its existence or its non-existence?"

"Its existence."

"Right, Ctesippus — and we just proved, if you recall, that nobody can assert a negative. Nobody can affirm what is not."

"So what?" said Ctesippus. "You and I can still contradict each other."

"But can we contradict each other," said Dionysodorus, "if we're both describing the same thing? In that case we'd be saying the same thing, wouldn't we?"

He had to agree.

"And what if neither of us is talking about the same thing? Then neither of us is saying anything about it at all."

He conceded that too.

"But what if I describe one thing and you describe something different — or I say something and you say nothing — is that a contradiction? How can the person who speaks contradict the person who doesn't speak at all?"

Ctesippus fell silent. And I, absolutely amazed, said, "What do you mean, Dionysodorus? I've heard this argument many times before, and I'm always astonished by it. The followers of Protagoras used to be fond of it, and others before them. But it seems to me perfectly marvelous — and wonderfully self-destructive. Here's what it comes down to: there's no such thing as saying something false. When a person speaks, he either speaks the truth or says nothing at all. Isn't that your position?"

He agreed.

"But even if he can't speak falsely — can he think falsely?"

"No, he can't," he said.

"So there's no such thing as a false opinion?"

"No."

"Then there's no such thing as ignorance — no people who are ignorant? Because isn't ignorance — if such a thing exists — just getting the facts wrong?"

"Certainly," he said.

"But that's impossible?"

"Impossible," he said.

"Are you saying this as a clever paradox, Dionysodorus, or do you seriously believe no person is ignorant?"

"Refute me," he said.

"But how can I refute you, if — as you say — it's impossible for anyone to speak falsely?"

"Exactly right," said Euthydemus.

"And I never asked you to refute me," said Dionysodorus. "How can I ask you to do something that doesn't exist?"

"Euthydemus," I said, "I confess these subtle and brilliant devices of wisdom are a bit above my pay grade. I'm afraid I can barely follow them, so forgive me if I ask what might seem like a really stupid question. If there's no such thing as falsehood, or false opinion, or ignorance — then there can't be any such thing as making a mistake either. A person can't get wrong what he's doing, because he's doing what he's doing — right? Isn't that what you're saying?"

"Yes," he said.

"All right — here's my stupid question. If nobody can make a mistake in action, word, or thought, then what on earth have you two come here to teach? Weren't you just telling us you could teach virtue to anyone willing to learn better than anyone else?"

"Socrates," Dionysodorus shot back, "are you really such an old fossil that you're dragging up what I said at the beginning? What's next — are you going to throw my words from last year in my face? You can't deal with what I'm saying now?"

"Well," I said, "your arguments aren't easy to deal with. They're the words of wise men. And actually, I'm not sure what to make of that word 'nonplussed' you just used. What exactly do you mean by it, Dionysodorus? You must mean I can't refute your argument. Tell me if the word means something else."

"No," he said, "it means what you said. Now answer my question."

"What — before you answer mine, Dionysodorus?" I said.

"Answer," he said.

"Is that fair?"

"Perfectly fair," he said.

"On what principle?" I said. "I can only assume you're some supremely wise man who shows up claiming to be a master logician and knows exactly when to answer and when not to. And right now you won't open your mouth because you know you don't have to."

"Stop chattering," he said, "and answer the question."

"All right," I said. "I suppose I must obey, since you're the boss. Ask away."

"Do things that have minds — are they alive or lifeless?"

"They're alive."

"And do you know of any word that's alive?"

"Can't say I do."

"Then why did you ask me just now what 'sense' my words had?"

"Well, because I was being stupid and made a mistake. Or then again — maybe I was right after all to say that words have sense. What do you say, wise man? If I wasn't mistaken, then even you can't refute me, and all your wisdom is stuck. But if I did make a mistake, then you're wrong when you say mistakes are impossible — and you didn't say that very long ago either. I'm starting to think, Dionysodorus and Euthydemus, that this argument is stuck right where it was and isn't going anywhere. Even your amazing skill in the subtleties of logic hasn't figured out how to throw someone else without falling down yourself — no more now than before."

Ctesippus said, "Gentlemen of Chios — or Thurii — or whatever you call yourselves — I'm amazed at you, because you obviously have zero objection to talking complete nonsense."

Fearing a real blowup, I tried to calm Ctesippus down again. "Ctesippus," I said, "what I told Cleinias applies to you too: you don't understand the ways of these foreign philosophers. They're not being serious. They're like the Egyptian sorcerer Proteus — they keep shapeshifting to trick us. So let's be like Menelaus and refuse to let them go until they show us their serious side. When they get serious, their true brilliance will appear. Let's beg and plead with them to let it shine through. And I think maybe I should lead by example again, to show them what I'm looking for. I'll pick up where I left off, as best I can, in hopes that I might touch their hearts and move them to pity — so that when they see how deeply serious and invested I am, they'll get serious too.

"Cleinias," I said, "remind me where we left off. Didn't we agree that a person should study philosophy?"

"Yes," he said.

"And philosophy is the pursuit of knowledge?"

"Yes."

"And what knowledge should we pursue? Can't we say, quite simply — a knowledge that will do us good?"

"Certainly."

"And would we be any better off if we ran around knowing where every deposit of gold was buried in the earth?"

"Maybe," he said.

"But haven't we already proven," I said, "that we'd be no better off even if all the gold in the earth were ours without any effort at all? Even if we knew how to turn stones into gold, that knowledge would be worthless unless we also knew how to use the gold. Remember?"

"I remember perfectly," he said.

"And the same goes for any knowledge — whether it's moneymaking, medicine, or any other skill that knows how to produce something but not how to use what it produces. None of those would do us any real good, right?"

He agreed.

"And if there were a knowledge that could make people immortal, but didn't include the knowledge of how to use immortality — even that would be useless, if we can reason from what we've already established?"

He agreed to all of this.

"Then, my dear boy," I said, "the knowledge we need is one that both produces and knows how to use what it produces."

"True," he said.

"And we definitely don't want to be lyre-makers or anything of that sort. In those crafts, the skill of making and the skill of using are completely separate — they deal with the same object but are totally different arts. The person who makes a lyre and the person who plays it are worlds apart. Right?"

"Right."

"And we obviously don't want the flute-maker's art either — same problem."

He agreed.

"Well then," I said, "suppose we learned the art of speechwriting — would that be the knowledge that makes us happy?"

"I'd say no," said Cleinias.

"Why not?" I asked.

"Because I notice," he said, "that there are some speechwriters who don't know how to use the speeches they compose — just like the lyre-makers who can't play their lyres. And there are other people who can't write speeches themselves but can brilliantly deploy the speeches others write for them. So the art of composing speeches clearly isn't the same as the art of using them."

"Yes," I said, "and I think that's solid proof that speechwriting isn't the knowledge that will make you happy. And yet I did think we might find our answer somewhere in that neighborhood. Because whenever I run into these speechwriters, Cleinias, they always strike me as truly extraordinary people — their art seems lofty and almost divine, and no wonder. Their art is really a branch of the great art of enchantment, barely inferior to it. The difference is that the enchanter's art works on snakes, spiders, scorpions, and other pests, while theirs works on juries, assemblies, and crowds of people — charming and soothing them. Do you agree?"

"Yes," he said. "I think you're right."

"So where do we go from here?" I said. "What art should we turn to?"

"I don't see my way," he said.

"But I think I do," I said.

"What's your idea?" asked Cleinias.

"I think the art of the general is the one most likely to make a person happy."

"I don't think so," he said.

"Why not?"

"The art of the general is basically an art of hunting — hunting men."

"So? What's wrong with that?"

"Because no art of hunting goes beyond the hunting and capturing stage. Once the prey is taken, the hunter or fisherman can't use it — they hand it over to the cook. And it's the same with geometers, astronomers, and mathematicians. They belong to the hunting class too, because they don't create their diagrams — they only discover what was already there. And since they can't use what they find, only catch it, they hand over their discoveries to the dialecticians to actually apply — at least, the ones with any sense do."

"Bravo!" I said. "Very good, my brilliant Cleinias! Is this really true?"

"Absolutely," he said. "Just as a general who captures a city or a camp hands his conquest over to the statesman — because the general doesn't know how to govern what he's won. Or as a quail-catcher hands his birds over to the quail-keeper. So if we're looking for the art that will make us truly happy — one that can not only acquire things but also use them — the art of the general isn't it. We need to look elsewhere."

Crito: Wait — are you telling me, Socrates, that the young man actually said all that?

Socrates: You don't believe me, Crito?

Crito: I certainly don't! Because if he really said those things, then in my opinion he needs neither Euthydemus nor anyone else as his teacher.

Socrates: Well, maybe I'm misremembering. Maybe it was actually Ctesippus who said it.

Crito: Ctesippus? No way.

Socrates: All I know is that I heard these words, and they weren't said by either Euthydemus or Dionysodorus. Perhaps, my dear Crito, they were spoken by some higher being. I'm certain I heard them.

Crito: Yes, Socrates — someone considerably higher, I'd say. But did you take the investigation any further? Did you find the art you were looking for?

Socrates: Find it? My dear fellow, hardly. We were like children chasing songbirds — always just about to catch the knowledge we wanted, but it kept slipping away. Why go through the whole story? Eventually we arrived at the kingly art and asked whether it was the one that produces and causes happiness. And then we fell into a labyrinth — every time we thought we'd reached the exit, we found ourselves right back at the entrance, with as far to go as ever.

Crito: How did that happen, Socrates?

Socrates: I'll tell you. We identified the kingly art with the political art.

Crito: And then what?

Socrates: All the other arts — including the general's art — seemed to hand over their authority to this one. It was the only art that knew how to use what the others produced. So obviously this was the very thing we'd been looking for — the art that sits at the helm of the ship of state, as Aeschylus would put it, steering and governing everything, putting it all to use.

Crito: And weren't you right, Socrates?

Socrates: You can judge for yourself, Crito, when you hear what happened next. Because we picked up the investigation again and asked a question like this: does the kingly art, which has supreme authority, actually produce anything for us? "Of course," was the answer. Wouldn't you say the same, Crito?

Crito: I would.

Socrates: And what would you say the kingly art produces? Suppose medicine had supreme authority over all the subordinate arts. If I asked you a similar question about medicine, you'd say — it produces health?

Crito: I would.

Socrates: And what about your own art, farming — if it had supreme authority over its subordinate arts, what does it produce? Doesn't it supply us with the fruits of the earth?

Crito: Yes.

Euthydemus (Part 2)

Socrates: And what does the kingly art produce when it holds supreme power? Maybe you're not ready with an answer?

Crito: I'm certainly not, Socrates.

Socrates: Neither were we, Crito. But you do at least know that if this is the art we've been looking for, it ought to be useful.

Crito: Of course.

Socrates: And it surely ought to do us some good?

Crito: Of course, Socrates.

Socrates: And Cleinias and I had agreed that knowledge of some kind is the only good.

Crito: Yes, that's what you were saying.

Socrates: Now, all the other results of politics — and there are many: wealth, freedom, peace and stability — turned out to be neither good nor evil in themselves. The political art, if it's the one that's going to do us good and make us happy, ought to make us wise and give us knowledge.

Crito: Yes, that was the conclusion you'd reached, from what you've told me.

Socrates: So does the kingly art make people wise and good?

Crito: Why not, Socrates?

Socrates: What — all people, and in every respect? Does it teach them all the skills — carpentry, shoemaking, and all the rest?

Crito: I don't think so, Socrates.

Socrates: Then what knowledge does it give? And what are we supposed to do with it? It can't be the source of any products that are neither good nor evil in themselves, and it doesn't give us knowledge of anything specific — just the knowledge of itself. So what is it, and what do we do with it? Shall we say, Crito, that it's the knowledge by which we make other people good?

Crito: By all means.

Socrates: And in what way will they be good, and what will they be useful for? Shall we say they'll make still other people good, who'll then make yet more people good, and so on forever — without our ever being able to say what they're actually good at? Because we've set aside all the specific results of politics. It's the same old tune playing again. And we're exactly where we started — if not further back — from knowing what the art of happiness actually is.

Crito: You really do seem to have gotten yourself into quite a pickle, Socrates.

Socrates: And so, Crito, finding myself on the verge of shipwreck, I cried out and desperately begged the two visitors to save me and the young man from the whirlpool of this argument. They were our Castor and Pollux, I said, and it was time for them to be serious and show us — in good, sober earnest — what that knowledge is which would enable us to spend the rest of our lives in happiness.

Crito: And did Euthydemus show you?

Socrates: Oh yes, indeed. He launched into a speech so lofty it went like this: "Would you rather, Socrates," he said, "that I show you this knowledge you've been struggling with? Or shall I prove that you already have it?"

"What!" I said. "You have the power to do that?"

"I certainly do."

"Then I'd much rather you prove I already have it. At my age, that would be a lot more convenient than having to learn something new."

"All right then," he said. "Tell me — do you know anything?"

"Yes," I said. "Lots of things, but nothing very important."

"That'll do," he said. "Now — would you agree that anything is what it is, and at the same time is not what it is?"

"Absolutely not."

"And didn't you say you know something?"

Yes.

"And if you know, you're a knowing person."

"Sure — with respect to the things I know."

"That makes no difference. If you're knowing, you must know all things."

"Certainly not!" I said. "There are plenty of things I don't know."

"And if you don't know, you're not knowing."

"Not knowing those particular things, sure."

"But you're still not knowing. And you just said you are knowing. So you both are and are not knowing at the same time, about the same things."

"Well, that's a pretty little tangle you've tied, Euthydemus! But explain something to me — how does this give me that knowledge we've been looking for? Are you saying that since the same thing can't both be and not be, and since I know one thing, I must therefore know everything? Because I can't be knowing and not knowing at the same time? And if I know everything, then I must possess the knowledge we were searching for? Is that your brilliant insight?"

"Convicted out of your own mouth, Socrates," he said.

"Well, Euthydemus," I said, "hasn't the same thing happened to you? Because if I'm in the same boat as you and your beloved brother Dionysodorus, I really can't complain. Tell me honestly — you two, do you know some things and not know others?"

"Certainly not, Socrates," said Dionysodorus.

"What do you mean?" I said. "You don't know anything?"

"Oh no," he replied. "We do know something."

"Then," I said, "you know everything — if you know anything?"

"Yes, everything," he said. "And that's as true of you as it is of us."

"Oh my!" I said. "What a wonderful thing! What a magnificent blessing! And does everyone else also know everything — or nothing?"

"Naturally," he said. "They can't know some things and not others, because then they'd be knowing and not knowing at the same time."

"So what's the conclusion?" I said.

"Everyone knows everything," he said, "if they know even one thing."

"Oh heavens, Dionysodorus," I said, "I see now you really are being serious — I could hardly get you to that point! And you truly, genuinely know all things? Including carpentry? And leather-cutting?"

"Absolutely," he said.

"And stitching? Do you know stitching?"

"Yes, by the gods — stitching and cobbling too."

"And things like the number of the stars and the grains of sand?"

"Of course! Did you think we'd say no to that?"

"By Zeus," Ctesippus broke in, "I just wish you'd give me some proof that you're telling the truth."

"What kind of proof do you want?" said Dionysodorus.

"Tell me how many teeth Euthydemus has, and Euthydemus can tell us how many you have."

"Won't you just take our word that we know everything?"

"Certainly not," said Ctesippus. "First tell us this one thing, and then we'll believe the rest. If you tell us the number and we count the teeth and you're right, we'll buy everything else you say."

They thought Ctesippus was making fun of them and refused to answer, though they kept insisting in response to each of his questions that they knew everything.

Because Ctesippus had finally thrown off all restraint. No question was too outrageous for him. He started asking them if they knew the most disgusting things imaginable. And the two of them, like wild boars rushing onto the spear points, fearlessly answered that yes, they knew all of it.

Finally, Crito, even I got swept up in my disbelief and asked Euthydemus: "Does Dionysodorus know how to dance?"

"Certainly," he said.

"And can he do acrobatic sword-leaps and spin on a wheel — at his age? Has he really reached that level of skill?"

"He can do anything," he said.

"And have you always known all this?"

"Always," he said.

"Even as children? At your very birth?"

They both said yes.

This we simply couldn't believe. And Euthydemus said, "You're skeptical, Socrates."

"Yes," I said, "and I'd have good reason to be — if I didn't know that you two are wise men."

"But if you'll answer my questions," he said, "I'll get you to confess to equally amazing things."

"Well," I said, "there's nothing I'd like better than to be proven wrong about myself. If I'm actually a wise man and have been all along without knowing it — and you can prove it — there'd be no greater prize in life."

"Then answer," he said.

"Ask," I said. "I'm ready."

"Do you know something, Socrates, or nothing?"

"Something," I said.

"And do you know with what you know, or with something else?"

"With what I know — and I assume you mean with my soul?"

"Aren't you ashamed, Socrates, to ask a question when you're the one being questioned?"

"All right," I said. "But what am I supposed to do? I'll do whatever you say. But when I don't understand what you're asking, you tell me to answer anyway and not to ask for clarification."

"You surely have some notion of what I mean," he said.

"Yes," I said.

"Then answer according to your notion."

"Yes," I said, "but if you mean one thing by your question and I understand it differently and answer based on my interpretation, will you be satisfied with that — even if my answer is off the mark?"

"I'll be very satisfied — but I suspect you won't be."

"Then I definitely won't answer," I said, "unless I understand you."

"You refuse to answer based on your own understanding," he said, "because you'd rather keep yammering on. You're an old fossil."

Now I could see he was getting annoyed with me for making distinctions when he wanted to trap me in his nets of words. And I remembered that Connus always got annoyed with me when I pushed back against him — and then he'd give up on me, figuring I was hopeless. Since I was planning to study under Euthydemus, I figured I'd better let him have his way, or he'd think I was a dunce and refuse to teach me.

So I said, "You're a far better dialectician than I am, Euthydemus — I've never claimed to be a professional. So have it your way. Ask me again, and I'll answer."

"Answer again, then," he said. "Do you know what you know with something, or with nothing?"

"Yes," I said. "With my soul."

"There you go again — answering more than was asked!" he said. "I didn't ask you what you know with. I asked whether you know with something."

"You're right — I said too much. Forgive me, it was just ignorance. I'll answer simply: yes, I always know what I know with something."

"And is that something always the same," he said, "or sometimes one thing and sometimes another?"

"Always the same," I said. "Whenever I know, I know with this."

"Would you stop tacking on extra words?"

"I'm worried the word 'always' is going to get us in trouble."

"It might get you in trouble, but certainly not us. Now answer: do you always know with this?"

"Always — since you won't let me say 'when I know.'"

"And always knowing, do you know some things with this and other things with something else? Or do you know all things with this?"

"All that I know," I said, "I know with this."

"There it is again, Socrates!" he said. "The extra words."

"Fine," I said. "I'll drop 'that I know.'"

"No no — don't drop anything. I'm not asking for favors. Just answer this: would you be able to know all things if you didn't already know all things?"

"That's completely impossible."

"Then add on whatever you like," he said, "because you've just admitted that you know all things."

"I suppose that's true," I said, "since my little qualifier 'that I know' isn't allowed to stand. So yes — I know all things."

"And you've admitted that you always know all things with that by which you know — whether we add the phrase 'when you know them' or not. Because you've agreed that you've always known all things at once — that is, even when you were a child, and at your birth, and while you were growing up, and before you were born, and before heaven and earth existed — you knew all things, since you always know them. And I swear you will continue to always know all things, if I have any say in the matter."

"I certainly hope you'll have that say, reverend Euthydemus," I said, "if you're really telling the truth. But I have a slight doubt about your ability to deliver on this — unless your brother Dionysodorus backs you up. Then maybe you can do it. Tell me, both of you — because in the main, I suppose I can't doubt that I really do know all things, since two prodigiously wise men tell me so — but how can I claim to know things like this, Euthydemus: that the good are unjust? Come on — do I know that or not?"

"You certainly know it."

"Know what?"

"That the good are not unjust."

"Right, yes — I've always known that," I said. "But that's not what I'm asking. What I want to know is: where did I learn that the good are unjust?"

"Nowhere," said Dionysodorus.

"Then I don't know it," I said.

"You're ruining the argument!" Euthydemus said to Dionysodorus. "He'll turn out not to know something, and then he'll be knowing and not knowing at the same time."

Dionysodorus blushed.

I turned to Euthydemus and said, "What do you think, Euthydemus? Doesn't your all-knowing brother seem to have made a little mistake?"

"What?" said Dionysodorus, snapping back instantly. "Am I the brother of Euthydemus?"

"Let that go," I said. "Please don't interrupt, my friend — just let Euthydemus get back to proving that I know the good to be unjust. At least let me learn that much."

"You're running away, Socrates," said Dionysodorus. "Refusing to answer."

"No wonder," I said. "I'm no match for even one of you, let alone both. Of course I'm going to run away from two. I'm no Heracles. And even Heracles couldn't fight the Hydra — a she-Sophist who had the wit to sprout several new heads every time one was cut off. Especially once he saw a second monster — a giant sea-crab, another Sophist, who appeared to have just arrived from a sea voyage — bearing down on him from the left, snapping its jaws. When it got too troublesome, he called in his nephew Iolaus for backup, and Iolaus came through beautifully. But if my Iolaus — my brother Patrocles the sculptor — were to come help, he'd only make things worse."

"All right," said Dionysodorus, "now that you've finished that little performance — tell me, was Iolaus the nephew of Heracles any more than he's yours?"

"I suppose I'd better answer you, Dionysodorus," I said, "because you'll keep pressing — that much I know — out of sheer determination to prevent me from learning anything from Euthydemus."

"Then answer," he said.

"Fine," I said. "Iolaus was not my nephew. He was Heracles' nephew. His father wasn't my brother Patrocles but Iphicles, whose name sounds a bit like Patrocles — Iphicles was Heracles' brother."

"And is Patrocles your brother?" he said.

"Yes," I said. "My half-brother — same mother, different father."

"Then he both is and is not your brother."

"Not by the same father, my friend," I said. "His father was Chaeredemus, and mine was Sophroniscus."

"And Sophroniscus was a father, and Chaeredemus was also a father?"

"Yes," I said. "Sophroniscus was my father, and Chaeredemus was his."

"Then Chaeredemus is different from a father?"

"He's not my father," I said.

"But can a father be anything other than a father? Or are you the same as a stone?"

"I'm definitely not a stone," I said, "though I'm worried you might prove I am one."

"Aren't you different from a stone?"

Yes.

"And being different from a stone, you're not a stone? And being different from gold, you're not gold?"

Right.

"So Chaeredemus," he said, "being different from a father, is not a father?"

"I suppose he's not a father," I said.

"Because if Chaeredemus is a father," said Euthydemus, jumping in, "then Sophroniscus, being different from a father, is not a father. And you, Socrates, have no father at all."

Ctesippus jumped in: "And isn't your father in the same situation? He's different from my father, isn't he?"

"Not at all," said Euthydemus.

"Then he's the same?"

"He's the same."

"I can't say I love the connection," said Ctesippus. "But is he only my father, Euthydemus, or is he everyone's father?"

"Everyone's father," he said. "Do you think the same person can be a father and not a father?"

"That's exactly what I thought," said Ctesippus.

"And do you think gold can be not gold? Or a man can be not a man?"

"Hold on, Euthydemus," said Ctesippus. "You're comparing apples and oranges, as they say. It's absurd to claim your father is the father of all."

"But he is," Euthydemus replied.

"Of men only?" said Ctesippus. "Or of horses and every other animal too?"

"Of all of them," he said.

"And your mother is the mother of all too?"

"Our mother too."

"So your mother has given birth to sea urchins?"

"And so has yours," he said.

"And baby fish and puppies and piglets are your brothers?"

"And yours too."

"And your papa is a dog?"

"And so is yours," he said.

"Let me have a turn," said Dionysodorus. "If you'll answer my questions, Ctesippus, I'll get the same admissions from you in no time. You have a dog, you said?"

"Yes — a real rascal," said Ctesippus.

"And he has puppies?"

"Yes, and they look just like him."

"And the dog is the father of the puppies?"

"I personally witnessed him with the mother," said Ctesippus.

"And isn't the dog yours?"

"He sure is."

"Then he's a father, and he's yours. Therefore: he's your father, and the puppies are your brothers."

"And let me slip in one more quick question," said Dionysodorus, jumping ahead before Ctesippus could respond, "just a small one: do you beat this dog?"

Ctesippus laughed and said, "I certainly do — since I can't beat you instead."

"Then you beat your father," said Dionysodorus.

"You'd have a much better reason to beat yours," said Ctesippus. "What was he thinking when he produced such brilliant sons? Your father — and his fellow puppies — must have gotten an enormous amount of good out of this wisdom of yours."

"Neither he nor you, Ctesippus, have any need of much good," said Euthydemus.

"Including yourself, Euthydemus?" said Ctesippus.

"Including me and every other person. Tell me, Ctesippus — do you think it's good or bad for a sick person to take medicine when he needs it? Or to go to war armed rather than unarmed?"

"I'd say it's good. Though I know I'm about to walk into one of your delightful traps."

"You'll find out soon enough," he said. "Since you admit that taking medicine is a good thing when someone needs it, then surely the more medicine the better? When he takes his dose, a whole cartload of hellebore shouldn't be too much for him."

"Absolutely, Euthydemus," said Ctesippus, "provided the patient is as big as the statue at Delphi."

"And since having weapons in wartime is a good thing, a person ought to have as many shields and spears as possible?"

"Oh, absolutely," said Ctesippus. "And you think one shield and one spear is enough?"

"For someone like you, sure. But would you equip Geryon or Briareus that way? I'd have thought you and your brother, being experts in armored combat, would know better..."

Here Euthydemus fell silent. But Dionysodorus returned to a previous answer of Ctesippus's.

"Don't you think owning gold is a good thing?"

"Yes," said Ctesippus. "The more the better."

"And to have money everywhere and always is a good thing?"

"Absolutely. A tremendous good."

"And you agree gold is a good?"

"I've already said so."

"Then shouldn't a person have gold everywhere and always, and as much as possible stuffed inside himself? Wouldn't the happiest man alive be the one with three talents of gold in his belly, a talent of gold in his skull, and a gold coin stuck in each eye?"

"Well, Euthydemus," said Ctesippus, "the Scythians do consider the people with gold in their skulls to be the happiest and bravest — which is just another version of your 'dog-father' routine. And what's even more extraordinary, the Scythians drink from their own gilded skulls, and look inside their own heads, and hold their own brains in their hands."

"Now tell me," said Euthydemus, "do the Scythians and everyone else see things that have the quality of sight, or things that don't?"

"Things that have the quality of sight, obviously."

"And you see things that have the quality of sight too?"

Yes.

"Then do you see our clothes?"

Yes.

"Then our clothes have the quality of sight."

"They can see brilliantly," said Ctesippus with a grin.

"What can they see?"

"Nothing! But you, my sweet friend, seem to have fallen asleep with your eyes open. And if it's possible to speak and say nothing — that's what you're doing right now."

"Can there not be a silence of the speaker?" said Dionysodorus.

"Impossible," said Ctesippus.

"Or a speaking of the silent?"

"Even more impossible."

"But when you mention stones, wood, and iron bars — aren't you speaking of silent things?"

"Not when I walk past a blacksmith's forge! In there, the iron bars make a terrific racket when you touch them. So your wisdom has slipped up on that one. But please, do tell me how you can be silent while speaking."

(I thought Ctesippus was showing off a bit because Cleinias was watching.)

"When you're silent," said Euthydemus, "isn't there a silence of all things?"

"Yes," he said.

"But if speaking things are included in 'all things,' then the speaking things are silent too."

"Wait," said Ctesippus. "Are all things not silent?"

"Certainly not," said Euthydemus.

"Then, my friend — do all things speak?"

"The ones that speak do."

"That's not what I'm asking," said Ctesippus. "I'm asking whether all things are silent or all things speak."

"Neither and both!" said Dionysodorus, jumping in. "I'm sure you'll be 'nonplussed' by that answer."

At this, Ctesippus — as was his way — exploded into a roar of laughter. "Your brother, Euthydemus, has gotten himself into a real jam! The game is up!" This delighted Cleinias, whose laughter made Ctesippus ten times louder. But I can't help thinking the rascal must have picked up that move from them, because nobody in our time has come close to their level of cleverness.

"Why are you laughing, Cleinias," I said, "at such solemn and beautiful ideas?"

"Well, Socrates," said Dionysodorus, "have you ever seen a beautiful thing?"

"Yes, Dionysodorus," I said. "I've seen many."

"Were they different from the beautiful itself, or the same as the beautiful?"

I was in quite a bind. I felt like I deserved it for opening my big mouth. But I said, "They're not the same as absolute beauty, but each of them has beauty present in it."

"And if an ox is present with you, are you an ox? And if Dionysodorus is present with you, are you Dionysodorus?"

"God forbid!" I said.

"But then how, just because one thing is present with another, will one thing be another?"

"Is that what's stumping you?" I said — because I was starting to try to imitate their technique, which I'd really set my heart on.

"Of course it is," he said. "I and the whole world are puzzled by the nonexistent."

"What do you mean, Dionysodorus?" I said. "Isn't the honorable honorable, and the base base?"

"That's as I please," he said.

"And does it please you?"

"Yes," he said.

"And you'd agree that the same is the same, and the different is different? Surely even a child wouldn't deny that the different is different. But I think, Dionysodorus, that you deliberately fumbled that last question. Because in general, you and your brother seem like excellent craftsmen who do the dialectician's business extremely well."

"And what," he said, "is the business of a good craftsman? Tell me — first of all, whose business is hammering?"

"The blacksmith's."

"And whose is making pots?"

"The potter's."

"And who does the killing, skinning, chopping, boiling, and roasting?"

"The cook," I said.

"And if someone does his proper business, he does right?"

Yes.

"And the cook's proper business, as you agreed, is the chopping and skinning?"

"Yes, I admitted that — but don't be too hard on me."

"So if someone were to kill, chop, boil, and roast the cook, he'd be doing the cook's business. And if someone were to hammer the blacksmith and throw the potter on a wheel, he'd be doing their business."

"Poseidon!" I said. "This is the crowning achievement of wisdom! Can I ever hope to possess such wisdom myself?"

"Would you even recognize it, Socrates, if it became yours?"

"Certainly," I said, "if you'll let me."

"What — do you think you know what's yours?" he said.

"I do — unless you say otherwise. Because you're the foundation and Euthydemus is the capstone of all my wisdom."

"Isn't 'yours' something you have in your power — something you can use however you want? For example, an ox or a sheep — if you could sell it, give it away, or sacrifice it to any god you chose — you'd call it yours. And anything you couldn't sell, give away, or sacrifice, you'd say wasn't really in your power. Right?"

"Right," I said — certain that something spectacular was about to emerge from these questions, and impatient to hear it. "Yes, things like that and only things like that are mine."

"And by animals, you mean living beings?"

"Yes," I said.

"So you agree that only those animals are yours over which you have the power to do all these things I just described?"

Yes.

Then, after a pause during which he seemed lost in contemplation of something enormous, he said: "Tell me, Socrates — do you have an ancestral Zeus?"

At this point, sensing the final move coming like a man caught in a net who makes one desperate twist to escape, I said: "No, Dionysodorus. I don't."

"What a pitiful man you must be!" he said. "You're not even a real Athenian if you have no ancestral gods, no temples, no mark of respectable citizenship."

"Now now, Dionysodorus," I said. "Don't be harsh. When it comes to religion, I've got altars and temples, both household and ancestral — everything any other Athenian has."

"And don't other Athenians have an ancestral Zeus?" he said.

"That title," I said, "isn't found among the Ionians, whether colonists or citizens of Athens. We have an ancestral Apollo, who's the father of Ion. And we have a Zeus of the Family and a Zeus of the Phratry, and an Athena of the Phratry. But 'Ancestral Zeus' — that's not one of our titles."

"No matter," said Dionysodorus. "You admit that you have Apollo, Zeus, and Athena."

"I certainly do."

"And they're your gods?"

"Yes," I said. "My lords and ancestors."

"At any rate, they're yours," he said. "Didn't you just admit that?"

"I did," I said. "What's coming?"

"And aren't these gods living beings? You already agreed that everything with life is an animal. And the gods have life, don't they?"

"They have life," I said.

"Then aren't they animals?"

"They're animals," I said.

"And you admitted that of your animals, the ones that are truly yours are the ones you can give away, sell, or sacrifice to any god you choose?"

"I did admit that, Euthydemus — and I have no way out."

"Well then," he said, "since you admit that Zeus and the other gods are yours — can you sell them, give them away, or do whatever you please with them, just as you would with any other animal?"

At this, Crito, I was completely flattened. Struck dumb. Laid out flat. Ctesippus rushed to my rescue.

"Bravo, Heracles!" he shouted. "What a brilliant argument!"

"Is it Bravo Heracles — or Heracles Bravo?" said Dionysodorus.

"Poseidon!" said Ctesippus. "What horrible word games. I give up — the pair of you are unbeatable."

And then, my dear Crito, the applause became universal. Everyone in the place — the speakers' admirers and the whole audience — was cheering, laughing, clapping, absolutely beside themselves with joy. Until this point, only the Sophists' own fan club had cheered after each successful hit. But now the entire crowd roared with delight, so loudly that the columns of the Lyceum seemed to echo back their joy.

As for me — I was so carried away that I made a speech declaring I'd never seen anything like their wisdom in all my life. I proclaimed myself their devoted servant and piled on the praise and admiration. "What marvelous dexterity!" I said. "How did you acquire such perfection in so short a time? There's so much to admire in what you say, Euthydemus and Dionysodorus, but what I admire most of all is your magnificent indifference to the opinions of others — whether the common crowd or distinguished intellectuals. You care only for people like yourselves. And I truly believe very few people are like you or would approve of arguments like these. Most people are so ignorant of their value that they'd be more embarrassed to use these arguments on others than to have them used on themselves.

"I must also express my admiration for your generous, public-spirited denial of all distinctions — whether between good and evil, white and black, or anything else. The beautiful result is that, as you say, everyone's mouth is sewn shut — including your own, which graciously follows the same rule. This removes all possible grounds for offense.

"But what impresses me most of all is this: your art is so brilliantly designed that anyone can learn it in almost no time. I noticed that Ctesippus was already copying your moves after just a few minutes. Now, this quick learnability is excellent in its way — but at the same time, let me offer a bit of advice: don't hold too many public performances. There's a risk that people will stop valuing an art they can pick up so easily. These sessions would work best if limited to just the two of you. But if you must have an audience, only admit people willing to pay a generous fee — and make sure of that. And if you're really wise, you'll also tell your students to practice only with you and each other. Because only what is rare is valuable. As Pindar says, 'Water is the best of all things' — but it's also the cheapest.

"Anyway — all I ask is that you accept Cleinias and me as your students."

That was the discussion, Crito. We exchanged a few more words and then went our separate ways. I hope you'll come along with me to their next session, since they claim they can teach anyone who'll pay them — no age limit and no aptitude requirements. And I should mention one thing they said specifically for your benefit: learning their art doesn't interfere at all with making money.

Crito: I'm certainly curious, Socrates, and eager to learn. But I'm afraid I'm not cut from the same cloth as Euthydemus. I'm more like the people you described — the ones who'd rather be demolished by these arguments than use them to demolish others. And though it may seem silly for me to advise you, I think you should hear what someone said to me. He's a man of considerable standing — a professor of legal rhetoric — and he came up to me after your session as I was walking around. "Crito," he said, "aren't you paying attention to these wise men?" "No," I said. "I couldn't get close enough to hear — there was too big a crowd." "Well, you missed something worth hearing." "What was that?" I asked. "You would have heard the greatest masters of rhetorical art performing." "And what did you think of them?" I asked. "What did I think?" he said. "The same thing anyone would think after hearing men play the fool and make a big deal out of nothing." That was his exact phrase. "Surely," I said, "philosophy is a wonderful pursuit." "Wonderful?" he said. "What naivety! Philosophy is worthless. And if you'd been there, I think you'd have been embarrassed for your friend Socrates — putting himself at the mercy of men who don't care what they say and pounce on every word. And these men, as I was telling you, are supposedly the leading thinkers of our time. But the truth is, Crito, that the whole enterprise — and the men themselves — are utterly trivial and ridiculous." Now, this criticism of philosophy, Socrates — whether from this man or from anyone — seems wrong to me. But the point about the awkwardness of debating in public with men like that — there I admit he may have had a point.

Socrates: Ah, Crito — these are marvelous men. But what was I about to say? First, tell me — who was this person who came up and criticized philosophy? Was he a lawyer who argues cases in court himself, or one of those people who trains the lawyers — who writes the speeches they use in battle?

Crito: Definitely not a courtroom lawyer. I doubt he's ever even been in court. But they say he knows the business well — he's clever and composes remarkable speeches.

Socrates: Now I understand, Crito. He's one of those amphibious creatures I was about to mention — the kind Prodicus describes as straddling the border between philosophy and politics. They think they're the wisest of all people, and that they're generally considered the wisest too. The only thing standing between them and universal recognition is the rivalry of the philosophers. They figure that if they can just prove philosophy is worthless, nobody will challenge their claim to the crown of wisdom. And they really are clever — in their own estimation. They have a little bit of philosophy and a little bit of political savvy, which gives them some basis for their confidence. They figure they've got just enough of both to stay out of danger while collecting the rewards.

Crito: What do you make of them, Socrates? There's something appealing in that attitude.

Socrates: More appealing than true, Crito. These people don't understand the nature of what's in between. Here's the thing: whenever something is a blend of two things, and one of those things is good and the other is evil, the blend is better than the evil part but worse than the good. If it's a blend of two good things that aim at different ends, it falls short of both in achieving either purpose. And only if both ingredients are bad does the mixture come out better than either one.

Now, if philosophy and politics are both good but aim at different goals, and these people are a blend of both — then they're actually worse than either a true philosopher or a true statesman. Or if one is good and the other bad, they're better than one but worse than the other. The only way their claim would hold up is if both philosophy and politics were bad — and I don't think even they'd say that.

The truth is that these philosopher-politicians who try to be both end up falling short of both. They're really in third place, though they'd love to be first. But we shouldn't be too hard on them. Every person should be respected who sincerely pursues and works at anything that even resembles wisdom. We just need to see them for what they really are.

Crito: I've told you many times, Socrates — I'm in a constant struggle over what to do about my sons. What should I do with them? There's no rush with the younger one — he's still a child. But Critobulus is getting older and needs someone to guide him. And whenever I hear you talk, I can't help thinking there's a kind of madness in the way we obsess about our children — making sure to marry a wife from a good family, piling up money for them — but then not bothering about their education. But then again, when I look at the people who claim to be educators, I'm appalled. If I'm being honest, they all strike me as dreadful. So I don't know how to advise a young man to study philosophy.

Socrates: My dear Crito, don't you realize that in every field, the incompetent practitioners are the vast majority while the truly good ones are rare and beyond price? Take gymnastics, rhetoric, business, or generalship — aren't these all worthwhile pursuits?

Crito: Absolutely, in my view.

Socrates: And in each of them, don't you see that most practitioners are laughable?

Crito: Yes — that's very true.

Socrates: But would you, on that account, avoid all these pursuits yourself and keep your son from them?

Crito: That wouldn't be reasonable, Socrates.

Socrates: Then be reasonable, Crito. Don't worry about whether the teachers of philosophy are good or bad. Think about philosophy herself. Examine her carefully and honestly. If she turns out to be worthless, then steer everyone away from her — not just your sons. But if she is what I believe she is, then follow her and serve her, you and your whole household, and be of good cheer.

CRATYLUS (Part 1)

Persons of the dialogue:
  Socrates
  Hermogenes
  Cratylus

Hermogenes: How about we bring Socrates into our argument?

Cratylus Sure, if you'd like.

Hermogenes: I should explain what's going on, Socrates. Our friend Cratylus here has been arguing about names. He says they're natural, not conventional -- not just arbitrary sounds that people agree to use, but that there's a truth or correctness built into them, and it's the same for Greeks and non-Greeks alike. So I asked him whether his own name, "Cratylus," is a true name, and he says yes. "And Socrates?" "Yes." So I said, "Then every person's name is simply whatever they're called." And he replies, "If the whole world called you Hermogenes, that still wouldn't be your name." When I press him for an explanation, he gets all ironic and mysterious, like he's got some private theory that he could share if he wanted to -- and if he did, he'd completely convince me. So tell me, Socrates, what does this oracle of his mean? Or better yet, tell me your own view on whether names have a natural correctness. I'd much rather hear that.

Socrates: Son of Hipponicus, there's an old saying: "The knowledge of the good is hard to come by." And the knowledge of names is a big part of knowledge. If I weren't poor, I could have taken the fifty-drachma course from the great Prodicus -- the one that's supposed to be a complete education in grammar and language (his own words). Then I'd be able to answer your question about the correctness of names right away. But I've only taken the one-drachma course, so I don't actually know the truth about these things. Still, I'm happy to investigate with you and Cratylus. As for his claim that your name isn't really Hermogenes -- I suspect he's just teasing you. The name "Hermogenes" means "born of Hermes," the god of profit, and he's joking that you don't live up to it because you're always chasing after money but never having any luck. But as I was saying, this kind of knowledge is genuinely difficult, so we'd better leave the question open until we've heard both sides.

Hermogenes: I've talked this over many times, both with Cratylus and others, and I can't convince myself that there's any principle of correctness in names other than convention and agreement. In my view, whatever name you give something is the right one. And if you change it and give another, the new name is just as correct as the old one. We change the names of our slaves all the time, and the new name works just as well. No name is given to anything by nature -- it's all convention and the habits of the people who use them. That's what I think. But if I'm wrong, I'd be happy to learn from Cratylus or anyone else.

Socrates: You may well be right, Hermogenes. Let's see. So your view is that the name of each thing is simply whatever anyone agrees to call it?

Hermogenes: That's my position.

Socrates: Whether the name-giver is an individual person or a whole city?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: All right, let me try an example. Suppose I call a man "horse" and a horse "man." You're saying the man would be correctly called "horse" by me as an individual, while correctly called "man" by the rest of the world -- and the horse would correctly be called "man" by me and "horse" by everyone else? Is that what you mean?

Hermogenes: According to my view, yes.

Socrates: But what about truth? You'd agree that words can be true or false?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: And there are true and false propositions?

Hermogenes: Of course.

Socrates: And a true proposition states what is, and a false proposition states what is not?

Hermogenes: Yes -- what other answer could there be?

Socrates: So within a proposition, there's truth and falsehood?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: But is a proposition only true as a whole, or are its parts true too?

Hermogenes: No, the parts are true as well as the whole.

Socrates: Just the large parts, or every part?

Hermogenes: Every part, I'd say.

Socrates: And is there any part of a proposition smaller than a name?

Hermogenes: No -- that's the smallest unit.

Socrates: Then a name is a part of a true proposition?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And a true part, as you said.

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And isn't a part of a falsehood also false?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: Then if propositions can be true and false, names can be true and false too?

Hermogenes: So it would seem.

Socrates: And the name of anything is whatever anyone says it is?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And will there be as many names for each thing as there are people naming it? And will all of them be true names at the moment they're spoken?

Hermogenes: Yes, Socrates. I can't see any other standard of correctness. You give one name, I give another. In different cities and countries there are different names for the same things. Greeks differ from non-Greeks, and different Greek communities differ from each other.

Socrates: But tell me, Hermogenes -- do the things themselves differ the way the names differ? Are they relative to individuals, as Protagoras says? He claims that "man is the measure of all things" -- that things are to me as they appear to me, and to you as they appear to you. Do you agree with him, or would you say that things have a permanent nature of their own?

Hermogenes: There have been times, Socrates, when I've been so confused that I've taken refuge in Protagoras's view. Not that I really agree with him.

Socrates: Wait -- have you ever been driven to admit that there's no such thing as a bad person?

Hermogenes: No, definitely not. But I've often had reason to think there are plenty of very bad people.

Socrates: And have you ever come across any very good ones?

Hermogenes: Not many.

Socrates: But you have found some?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And would you say that the very good are the very wise, and the very bad are the very foolish?

Hermogenes: I would.

Socrates: But if Protagoras is right, and the truth is just whatever appears to each person, then how can some of us be wise and some foolish?

Hermogenes: That would be impossible.

Socrates: And on the other hand, if wisdom and foolishness really are distinguishable, then I think you'd agree that Protagoras's claim can hardly be correct. Because if what appears to each person is true for them, no one can actually be wiser than anyone else.

Hermogenes: They can't.

Socrates: And you wouldn't want to go along with Euthydemus either, who says that all things belong equally to all people at the same time and always. Because on that view there can't be good people and bad people -- not if virtue and vice belong to everyone equally.

Hermogenes: No, there can't.

Socrates: So if neither view is right -- if things aren't just relative to individuals, and all things don't belong equally to everyone -- then things must have their own proper and permanent nature. They aren't pushed around by us, fluctuating according to our whims. They're independent, and they maintain their own essential nature as prescribed by how things actually are.

Hermogenes: I think, Socrates, that you've hit on the truth.

Socrates: And does this apply only to things themselves, or equally to actions? Aren't actions also a kind of reality?

Hermogenes: Yes, actions are real too.

Socrates: Then actions are also performed according to their own nature, and not according to whatever we happen to think about them? Take cutting, for example. We don't cut however we please, with any random instrument. We cut with the right instrument, and according to the natural process of cutting. The natural process works. Any other way will fail and be useless.

Hermogenes: I'd say the natural way is the right way.

Socrates: Same with burning. Not every approach works -- the right way is the natural way, with the natural instrument.

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: And this holds for all actions?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And speaking is a kind of action?

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: So will a person speak correctly just by speaking however they please? Won't the successful speaker be the one who speaks in the natural way, as things ought to be spoken, and with the natural instrument? Any other approach will result in error and failure.

Hermogenes: I quite agree with you.

Socrates: And isn't naming a part of speaking? When we give names, we're speaking.

Hermogenes: That's true.

Socrates: And if speaking is a kind of action that relates to real things, then isn't naming also a kind of action?

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: And we established that actions aren't relative to ourselves but have a nature of their own?

Hermogenes: Precisely.

Socrates: Then the argument leads us to conclude that names should be given according to a natural process and with the proper instrument, not at our pleasure. That's the only way we'll name things successfully.

Hermogenes: I agree.

Socrates: Now, whatever needs to be cut has to be cut with something?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And whatever needs to be woven or pierced has to be woven or pierced with something?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: And whatever needs to be named has to be named with something?

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: What do we pierce with?

Hermogenes: An awl.

Socrates: And what do we weave with?

Hermogenes: A shuttle.

Socrates: And what do we name with?

Hermogenes: A name.

Socrates: Very good. So a name is an instrument?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: Suppose I ask, "What kind of instrument is a shuttle?" And you answer, "A weaving instrument."

Hermogenes: Right.

Socrates: And if I ask, "What do we do when we weave?" the answer is that we separate the warp from the woof.

Hermogenes: Very true.

Socrates: And the same kind of description could be given of an awl or any other instrument?

Hermogenes: Of course.

Socrates: So now let me ask a similar question about names. If a name is an instrument, what do we do when we name things?

Hermogenes: I can't say.

Socrates: Don't we communicate information to each other, and distinguish things according to their natures?

Hermogenes: Yes, we certainly do.

Socrates: Then a name is an instrument for teaching and for distinguishing natures -- just as a shuttle is an instrument for distinguishing the threads of a web.

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And the shuttle is the weaver's instrument?

Hermogenes: Absolutely.

Socrates: So the weaver will use the shuttle well -- meaning like a weaver? And the teacher will use names well -- meaning like a teacher?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And when the weaver uses the shuttle, whose craftsmanship is he using?

Hermogenes: The carpenter's.

Socrates: And is every person a carpenter, or only the skilled ones?

Hermogenes: Only the skilled.

Socrates: And when someone uses the awl, whose work are they using?

Hermogenes: The smith's.

Socrates: And is every person a smith, or only the skilled ones?

Hermogenes: Only the skilled.

Socrates: And when the teacher uses a name, whose work is he using?

Hermogenes: Now I'm stumped again.

Socrates: Can you at least tell me who gives us the names we use?

Hermogenes: I really can't.

Socrates: Doesn't it seem like the law gives them to us?

Hermogenes: Yes, I suppose so.

Socrates: So the teacher, when he uses a name, is using the work of the legislator?

Hermogenes: I agree.

Socrates: And is every person a legislator, or only the skilled ones?

Hermogenes: Only the skilled.

Socrates: Then not just anyone can give a name, Hermogenes. Only a name-maker can do it -- and that's the legislator, who is the rarest of all skilled craftsmen.

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: And how does the legislator make names? What does he look to? Think about it in light of our previous examples. What does the carpenter look to when making a shuttle? Doesn't he look to whatever is naturally fitted to work as a shuttle?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: And suppose the shuttle breaks while he's making it. Will he make another one by looking at the broken one? Or will he look to the ideal form that he was working from?

Hermogenes: The ideal form, I'd imagine.

Socrates: And wouldn't we be right to call that the true or ideal shuttle?

Hermogenes: I think so.

Socrates: So whatever kind of shuttle is needed -- for manufacturing thin garments or thick ones, linen or wool or any other material -- each one should have the true form of a shuttle. And the maker should produce, in each case, the form that's best suited to that particular kind of work.

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And the same goes for other instruments. When a craftsman has figured out the instrument naturally suited to each job, he has to express that natural form in whatever material he's working with. He doesn't get to make up his own design. For instance, the smith should know how to put into iron the forms of awls suited by nature to their various uses.

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: And the carpenter should know how to put into wood the forms of shuttles suited by nature to their uses?

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: Because the different forms of shuttles naturally correspond to different kinds of weaving. And this is true of instruments in general.

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: Then our legislator must also know how to put the true natural name of each thing into sounds and syllables, and to make and give all names with a view to the ideal name -- if he's going to be a real name-maker. And we should remember that different legislators won't use the same syllables, just as different smiths don't use the same iron to make the same instrument for the same purpose. The form must be the same, but the material can vary, and the instrument will be equally good regardless of what iron it's made from, whether in Greece or abroad. The material doesn't matter.

Hermogenes: Very true.

Socrates: And the legislator, whether Greek or non-Greek, shouldn't be considered worse as long as he gives the true and proper form of the name, whatever syllables he uses. The country doesn't matter.

Hermogenes: Quite true.

Socrates: But who decides whether the proper form has been given to the shuttle, regardless of what wood it's made from? The carpenter who makes it, or the weaver who uses it?

Hermogenes: I'd say the one who uses it, Socrates.

Socrates: And who uses the work of the lyre-maker? Won't it be the person who knows how to direct the work being done and can tell whether it's been done well or not?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: And who is that?

Hermogenes: The lyre-player.

Socrates: And who will direct the shipwright?

Hermogenes: The pilot.

Socrates: And who is best able to direct the legislator in his work and judge whether the job has been done well, whether here or in any other country? Won't it be the person who actually uses the product?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And that's the person who knows how to ask questions?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And how to answer them?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And someone who knows how to ask and answer -- you'd call them a dialectician?

Hermogenes: Yes, that would be the right term.

Socrates: So the carpenter's job is to make a rudder, and the pilot directs him, if the rudder is to be well made.

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: And the legislator's job is to give names, and the dialectician must be his director, if the names are to be given correctly?

Hermogenes: That's true.

Socrates: Then the giving of names is hardly the trivial matter you think it is, Hermogenes, or the work of trivial or random people. Cratylus is right when he says that things have names by nature, and that not just anyone can be a maker of names -- only someone who looks to the name that each thing naturally has and is able to express the true forms of things in letters and syllables.

Hermogenes: I can't really argue with you, Socrates, but I'm having trouble changing my mind all at once. I think I'd be more easily convinced if you could show me what you mean by this "natural fitness" of names.

Socrates: My good Hermogenes, I have nothing to show. Didn't I just tell you -- though maybe you've forgotten -- that I don't know anything about this, and I was only proposing to investigate it together with you? But now that we've been talking, we've made some progress. We've discovered that names do have a natural truth to them, and that not just anyone knows how to give a thing its name.

Hermogenes: Very good.

Socrates: And what is the nature of this truth or correctness in names? That's the next question, if you want to pursue it.

Hermogenes: I certainly do.

Socrates: Then think about it.

Hermogenes: How should I think about it?

Socrates: The best way is to get help from experts, and you'd have to pay them well -- both in money and in gratitude. Those experts would be the Sophists, from whom your brother Callias has -- at considerable expense -- bought a reputation for wisdom. But since you haven't come into your inheritance yet, you'd better go beg him to share what he learned from Protagoras about the fitness of names.

Hermogenes: But that would be totally inconsistent of me! I just rejected Protagoras and his "truth" doctrine, so how could I then treat what he says about names as valuable?

Socrates: Well, if you won't have Protagoras, you'll have to learn from Homer and the other poets.

Hermogenes: And where does Homer say anything about names? What does he say?

Socrates: He talks about them frequently -- notably and impressively in the passages where he distinguishes between the names that gods give to things and the names that humans give. Doesn't he make a remarkable claim about the correctness of names in those passages? Because the gods surely must call things by their right and natural names, don't you think?

Hermogenes: Well, of course, if they call them anything at all, they'd call them correctly. But what passages are you talking about?

Socrates: Do you know what he says about the river in Troy that fought in single combat against Hephaestus? "The gods call it Xanthus," he says, "and men call it Scamander."

Hermogenes: I remember.

Socrates: Well then -- isn't it a significant lesson to learn that the river ought to be called Xanthus rather than Scamander? Or take the bird that, as Homer says, "the gods call Chalcis, and men call Cymindis." Isn't it worth knowing that Chalcis is the more correct name? Or the hill called Batieia by humans and Myrina by the gods? There are many similar observations in Homer and other poets. But those examples might be above our pay grade. The names of Hector's son -- Scamandrius and Astyanax, both of which Homer says were given to the boy -- are more within our range. I think we can figure out what the poet means by "correctness" from that example. You remember the lines I'm referring to?

Hermogenes: I do.

Socrates: Let me ask you, then: which name did Homer think was more correct for Hector's son -- Astyanax or Scamandrius?

Hermogenes: I don't know.

Socrates: Well, if you were asked whether the wise or the unwise are more likely to give correct names, what would you say?

Hermogenes: The wise, obviously.

Socrates: And who's generally wiser in a city -- the men or the women?

Hermogenes: I'd say the men.

Socrates: Now, Homer tells us that the Trojan men called the boy Astyanax -- "lord of the city." But if the men called him Astyanax, the other name, Scamandrius, must have been given by the women.

Hermogenes: That's a reasonable inference.

Socrates: And Homer must have considered the Trojans wiser than their wives?

Hermogenes: Surely.

Socrates: Then he must have thought Astyanax was a more correct name for the boy than Scamandrius?

Hermogenes: Clearly.

Socrates: And what's the reason? Let's think about it. Doesn't Homer himself suggest a very good reason when he says of Hector, "For he alone defended their city and long walls"? This seems like a good reason for calling the son of the city's savior "lord of the city" -- which is what Astyanax means. (In the Greek, "anax" means "lord" or "king.")

Hermogenes: I see.

Socrates: Actually, Hermogenes, I'm not sure I see it myself yet. Do you?

Hermogenes: No, not really.

Socrates: But tell me, my friend -- didn't Homer also give Hector his name?

Hermogenes: What about it?

Socrates: Well, the name Hector seems to me to be very close in meaning to Astyanax. Both are Greek names, and "king" and "holder" mean nearly the same thing -- both describe a ruler. A person is clearly the holder of that which he's king of: he rules it, owns it, holds it. (In the Greek, "Hektor" comes from "echein," to hold, and "anax" means king -- so both names point to the idea of ruling or possessing.) But maybe you think I'm talking nonsense. I'm not entirely sure myself that I've actually found a clue to Homer's views on the correctness of names.

Hermogenes: I assure you, I think you're on the right track.

Socrates: There's good reason, I think, to call a lion cub a "lion" and a foal a "horse." I'm talking about the normal course of nature, when an animal produces offspring of its own kind -- not about freaks of nature. If a horse somehow gave birth to a calf, I wouldn't call it a foal but a calf. And I wouldn't call an inhuman birth a "human," only a natural one. The same applies to trees and everything else. Do you agree?

Hermogenes: Yes, I agree.

Socrates: Good. But keep an eye on me and make sure I'm not pulling any tricks. By the same principle, the son of a king should be called a king. And it doesn't matter whether the syllables of the name are the same or different, as long as the meaning is preserved. Adding or removing a letter makes no difference, as long as the essence of the thing is captured in the name.

Hermogenes: What do you mean?

Socrates: Something very simple. Think about the names of letters. You know that the names of letters aren't usually the same as the letters themselves -- except for four: epsilon, upsilon, omicron, and omega. The names of all the other letters, whether vowels or consonants, are made up of additional letters we tack on. But as long as the meaning comes through and there's no confusion, the name of the letter is perfectly correct. Take the letter beta, for example: the addition of eta, tau, and alpha doesn't cause any problem and doesn't prevent the whole name from conveying the value the original name-giver intended. That's how well he knew how to name letters.

Hermogenes: I believe you're right.

Socrates: And the same can be said of a king. A king will often be the son of a king, a good son of a good father, a noble son of a noble one. And in the regular course of nature, like produces like, so the offspring has the same name as the parent. But the syllables can be disguised until they look completely different to an untrained eye, even though they mean the same thing. It's like how any one of us wouldn't recognize the same drug in different colors and disguises, but a doctor, who looks at the active properties, recognizes it immediately and isn't thrown off by the packaging. In the same way, an etymologist isn't thrown off by the addition, transposition, or removal of a letter or two -- or even by changing all the letters -- as long as the meaning of the thing is preserved. As we just noted, "Hector" and "Astyanax" have only one letter in common -- tau -- and yet they mean the same thing. And "Archepolis" (ruler of the city) shares almost no letters with either, yet the meaning is the same. There are plenty of other names that just mean "king." Similarly, there are several names for a general -- like Agis ("leader"), Polemarchus ("war chief"), and Eupolemus ("good warrior"). And for a doctor: Iatrocles ("famous healer") and Acesimbrotus ("curer of mortals"). I could give many more examples -- names that differ completely in their syllables and letters but mean the same thing. Wouldn't you agree?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: Then the same names -- in terms of meaning -- should be assigned to those who follow the natural course?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And what about those who don't follow the natural course -- the exceptions? For example, when a good and religious man has an irreligious son, the son shouldn't carry his father's name but the name of the class he actually belongs to -- just like when a horse gives birth to a calf, you call the offspring what it is, not what its parent is.

Hermogenes: Quite true.

Socrates: So the irreligious son of a religious father should be called irreligious?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: He shouldn't be called Theophilus ("beloved of God") or Mnesitheus ("mindful of God") or any name like that. If names are given correctly, his should mean the opposite.

Hermogenes: Certainly, Socrates.

Socrates: Then there's the case of Orestes. (In the Greek, "Orestes" suggests "man of the mountains.") He seems to be aptly named -- whether it was chance or perhaps some poet who wanted to capture the brutality, fierceness, and mountain wildness of his nature.

Hermogenes: That's very likely, Socrates.

Socrates: And his father's name also fits his nature.

Hermogenes: Clearly.
