Socrates: Yes -- "Agamemnon" means "admirable for perseverance." (In the Greek, the name suggests "admirable for remaining" or "holding firm.") He's a man who is patient and determined in carrying through his plans, and crowns them with success through sheer endurance. His years-long siege at Troy with that vast army proves the "admirable endurance" signified by the name.

I also think Atreus is rightly named. His murder of Chrysippus and his terrible cruelty to Thyestes were ruinous and destructive to his reputation. The name is a bit altered and disguised, so it's not obvious to everyone, but an etymologist can see the meaning clearly. (In the Greek, you can connect "Atreus" to "ateiros" meaning stubborn, or "atrestos" meaning fearless, or "ateros" meaning destructive -- all of which fit.) The name is right from every angle.

And Pelops is also appropriately named: it means "one who sees only what's near." (In the Greek, "ta pelas horon" -- seeing nearby things.)

Hermogenes: How so?

Socrates: According to the legend, he had no foresight at all about the terrible consequences the murder of Myrtilus would bring on his entire bloodline for generations. He saw only what was right in front of him in his eagerness to win Hippodamia as his bride at any cost.

And everyone would agree that the name Tantalus fits perfectly -- if the stories about him are true.

Hermogenes: And what are those stories?

Socrates: Many terrible misfortunes happened to him during his life -- culminating in the complete ruin of his country. And after death, he had that famous stone suspended over his head in the underworld. All of this lines up wonderfully with his name. (In the Greek, "Tantalus" evokes "talanteia," meaning "weighing down" or "balancing." You could imagine someone wanting to call him "talantatos" -- "the most burdened by misfortune" -- and disguising it into "Tantalus.")

Now, the name of Zeus, who is supposedly Tantalus's father, also has an excellent meaning, though it's hard to understand because it's actually like a compressed sentence split into two parts. Some people call him Zena, using one half, and others call him Dia, using the other half. The two together express the nature of the god. (In the Greek, combining "Zena" and "Dia" gives you "di' hon zen" -- "the one through whom all living creatures always have life.") And it's the business of a name to express nature.

Now, there seems to be something disrespectful about calling Zeus the "son of Kronos" -- since Kronos is proverbially associated with stupidity. You'd expect Zeus to be the child of a mighty intellect. And in fact he is! Because that's actually what Kronos's name means. (In the Greek, "Kronos" is connected to "koros," meaning "sweeping clean" -- not "youth," but rather "the pure and uncontaminated mind.") And Kronos, tradition tells us, was the son of Uranus, whose name is also appropriate -- it comes from "looking upward." (In the Greek, "Ouranos" derives from "horan ta ano" -- "gazing at what's above.") And as the philosophers say, looking upward is the way to develop a pure mind. So the name Uranus is correct.

If I could remember Hesiod's whole genealogy, I'd keep going and try out more of these analyses on the remoter ancestors of the gods. Then I might see whether this wisdom that's suddenly come over me -- I don't know from where -- holds up all the way through.

Hermogenes: You know, Socrates, you sound like a prophet who's just been struck by divine inspiration -- delivering oracles left and right.

Socrates: And you're right, Hermogenes. I believe I caught the inspiration from the great Euthyphro of the deme of Prospalta. He gave me a very long lecture starting at dawn -- he talked and I listened -- and his wisdom and enchanting enthusiasm have not only filled my ears but taken possession of my soul. So here's my plan: today I'll let his superhuman power work through me and finish this investigation of names. But tomorrow, if you're up for it, we'll perform an exorcism and purge ourselves of him -- if we can find some priest or sophist skilled in that sort of purification.

Hermogenes: I'm all for it! I'm very curious to hear the rest of this investigation.

Socrates: Then let's keep going. Where should we start, now that we've gotten a rough framework? Are there any names that testify on their own behalf that they weren't given arbitrarily but have a natural fitness? Names of heroes and ordinary people can be misleading, because they're often named after ancestors whose names may not suit them at all, or they express a wish -- like Eutychides ("child of good fortune"), Sosias ("savior"), or Theophilus ("beloved of God"). We'd better skip those. We'll have a better chance of finding correctness in the names of unchanging realities -- the things that should have been named with the greatest care, and where perhaps some more-than-human power was at work.

Hermogenes: I think so, Socrates.

Socrates: Shouldn't we start with the gods, and show that they're correctly called "gods"?

Hermogenes: Yes, that would make sense.

Socrates: Here's my theory. I suspect that the sun, moon, earth, stars, and sky -- which are still the gods of many non-Greek peoples -- were the only gods known to the earliest Greeks. And since they saw that these things were always moving and running, they called them "theoi" (gods) from their running nature. (In the Greek, "theoi" -- gods -- sounds like "theontes" -- runners.) When they later came to know the other gods, they applied the same name to all of them. Does that seem likely?

Hermogenes: Very likely indeed.

Socrates: What should come after the gods?

Hermogenes: What about "daimones" (spirits) and heroes and humans?

Socrates: Daimones! And what do you think the word means? See if my view is right.

Hermogenes: Let me hear it.

Socrates: Do you know how Hesiod uses the word?

Hermogenes: I don't.

Socrates: Don't you remember that he speaks of a golden race of people who came first?

Hermogenes: Yes, I do.

Socrates: He says of them: "But now that fate has closed over this race, they are holy spirits upon the earth, beneficent, averters of evil, guardians of mortal men."

Hermogenes: What's the point?

Socrates: Well, I don't think he means people literally made of gold, but good and noble people. I'm convinced of this because he also says that we are the "iron race."

Hermogenes: That's true.

Socrates: And wouldn't he say that the good people of our own time belong to that golden race?

Hermogenes: Very likely.

Socrates: And aren't good people wise?

Hermogenes: Yes, they are.

Socrates: Then I'm absolutely convinced he called them "daimones" because they were "daemones" -- wise or knowing. (In the Greek, "daimon" is connected to "daemon," meaning knowing or wise, a word that appears in the older Attic dialect.) Hesiod and other poets say truly that when a good person dies, they receive honor and a great role among the dead, becoming a "daimon" -- a name signifying wisdom. And I'd say the same: that every wise and good person, both in life and death, is more than human -- "daimonion" -- and is rightly called a daimon.

Hermogenes: Then I think I agree with you completely. But what about the word "hero"?

Socrates: That's not hard to explain, since the name has barely been altered. It indicates that heroes were born of love.

Hermogenes: What do you mean?

Socrates: Don't you know that heroes are demigods?

Hermogenes: So?

Socrates: They all sprang from the love of a god for a mortal woman, or a mortal man for a goddess. Look at the word in the old Attic dialect, and you'll see more clearly: "heros" (hero) is just a slight alteration of "eros" (love) -- the heroes sprang from eros. (In the old Attic script, "heros" was written "eros.") Either that, or they were called heroes because they were skilled rhetoricians and dialecticians -- people good at asking questions. (In the Greek, "erotan" means "to question," and "eirein" means "to speak.") So in Attic dialect, heroes turn out to be rhetoricians and questioners -- a tribe of sophists and orators, basically.

But can you tell me why humans are called "anthropoi"? That's the harder one.

Hermogenes: No, I can't. And I wouldn't try even if I could, because I think you're more likely to figure it out.

Socrates: You mean you trust in the inspiration of Euthyphro.

Hermogenes: Of course.

Socrates: And your trust isn't misplaced. Because at this very moment a new and clever idea strikes me, and if I'm not careful, by tomorrow's dawn I'll be wiser than I ought to be. Now pay attention. First, keep in mind that we often add and remove letters from words, shift accents, and give names as we please. Take "Diphilos" ("dear to Zeus"), for instance: to turn it from a phrase into a proper name, we drop one of the iotas and change the middle syllable from an acute accent to a grave. In other cases, letters are inserted rather than removed, and the accent shifts the other way.

Hermogenes: That's true.

Socrates: Well, the word "anthropos" ("human") seems to be exactly that kind of case -- a sentence compressed into a noun. One letter, the alpha, has been removed, and the accent on the last syllable has been changed.

Hermogenes: What do you mean?

Socrates: The word "human" implies that while other animals never examine, reflect on, or look up at what they see, a human not only sees but reflects on and looks up at what they've seen. That's why humans alone are rightly called "anthropos" -- it comes from "anathron ha opopen": "one who looks up at what they have seen." (In the Greek, "anthropos" is derived from "anathrein" -- to look up and examine -- combined with "opope" -- what one has seen.)

Hermogenes: Can I ask you to examine another word that I'm curious about?

Socrates: Certainly.

Hermogenes: I'll take what seems to come next in order. You know the distinction between soul and body?

Socrates: Of course.

Hermogenes: Let's try to analyze them the way we did the previous words.

Socrates: You want me to examine the natural fitness of the word "psyche" (soul) first, and then "soma" (body)?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: If I go with what comes to mind right now, I'd say that those who first used the name "psyche" meant to express that the soul, when in the body, is the source of life -- it gives the power of breath and revival ("anapsychon"). When this reviving power fails, the body dies. And that, if I'm not mistaken, is why they called it "psyche." But wait a moment -- I think I can come up with something that Euthyphro's followers would find more impressive. They'd probably look down on that first explanation. How about this one?

Hermogenes: Let me hear it.

Socrates: What is it that holds, carries, and gives life and motion to the entire body? What else but the soul?

Hermogenes: Just that.

Socrates: And don't you agree with Anaxagoras that mind or soul is the ordering and sustaining principle of all things?

Hermogenes: Yes, I do.

Socrates: Then you could rightly call that power "physeche" -- "that which sustains and holds nature." (In the Greek, this is formed from "physin echei kai ochei" -- "holds and carries nature.") And this has been refined down to "psyche."

Hermogenes: That derivation does seem more scientific than the first one.

Socrates: It does. But I can't help laughing at the idea that this was really the original meaning of the name.

Hermogenes: But what about the next word?

Socrates: You mean "soma" -- the body?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: That one can be interpreted in several ways, and even more if you allow a little rearrangement of letters. Some say the body is the "sema" (tomb or sign) of the soul, as if the soul were buried in our present life. Others say the body is the "sign" of the soul, because the soul expresses itself through the body. (In the Greek, "sema" means both "tomb" and "sign," and "semainei" means "to indicate.") But probably the best etymology comes from the Orphic poets, who believed that the soul is being punished for some sin, and that the body is an enclosure or prison in which the soul is kept safe -- "soma" from "sozein" (to keep safe) -- until the penalty has been paid. On this view, you don't even need to change a single letter.

Hermogenes: I think, Socrates, we've said enough about this category of words. But do we have any more explanations of divine names, like the one you gave for Zeus? I'd like to know whether a similar principle applies to them.

Socrates: Yes, Hermogenes, and there's one excellent principle that we, as sensible people, should acknowledge: we know nothing about the gods -- neither about their natures nor about the names they give themselves. But we can be sure that whatever names they call themselves are true. And this is the best principle. The next best is to say, as we do in prayers, that we'll call them by whatever names or titles they prefer, since we don't know any others. I think that's a fine custom, and I'd like to follow it. So let's make an announcement to the gods first: we're not investigating them -- we wouldn't presume to be capable of that. We're only investigating what humans meant when they gave them these names. Surely there's no harm in that.

Hermogenes: I think, Socrates, you're quite right, and I'd like to proceed as you suggest.

Socrates: Shall we begin with Hestia, as is customary?

Hermogenes: Yes, that would be very proper.

Socrates: What do you think the person who named Hestia had in mind?

Hermogenes: Now that's another very difficult question.

Socrates: My dear Hermogenes, the original name-givers must have been remarkable people. They were philosophers, with a lot to say.

Hermogenes: So? What follows from that?

Socrates: I'd attribute the creation of names to people like that. Even in foreign names, if you analyze them, you can still find meaning. Take what we call "ousia" (being/essence) -- some people call it "esia" and others call it "osia." Now, it's perfectly reasonable for the essence of things to be called "hestia," which is closely related to the first form, "esia." (In the Greek, "hestia" sounds like "esia," which sounds like "ousia.") And there's good reason why the Athenians connected "hestia" to "ousia": because in ancient times they too said "esia" instead of "ousia." And this explains why sacrifices were traditionally offered first to Hestia -- it was natural enough if they thought of Hestia as the essence of things.

Those who used the form "osia," on the other hand, seem to have leaned toward Heraclitus's view that everything flows and nothing stands still. For them, the driving force ("othoun" -- pushing) is the cause and ruling power of everything, so they rightly called it "osia."

That's about all we can say, knowing as little as we do. Now, after Hestia, we should consider Rhea and Kronos, though we've already discussed Kronos. But I'm probably talking total nonsense.

Hermogenes: Why, Socrates?

Socrates: My friend, I've stumbled onto a whole hive of wisdom.

Hermogenes: What kind?

Socrates: Well, rather ridiculous -- and yet plausible.

Hermogenes: How plausible?

Socrates: I keep seeing Heraclitus repeating ancient traditions, as old as the days of Kronos and Rhea, the same kind Homer spoke of.

Hermogenes: What do you mean?

Socrates: Heraclitus says that everything is in motion and nothing is at rest. He compares things to a river's current and says you can't step into the same water twice.

Hermogenes: That's right.

Socrates: Well then, can we avoid concluding that whoever gave the names Kronos and Rhea to the ancestors of the gods held roughly the same view as Heraclitus? Is it a coincidence that they gave both of them names connected to streams and flowing? (In the Greek, "Rhea" suggests "rhein" -- to flow -- and "Kronos" can be connected to "krounos" -- a spring or stream.) Compare the line in Homer -- and I believe in Hesiod too -- about "Ocean, the origin of the gods, and mother Tethys." And Orpheus says, "The fair river of Ocean was the first to marry, and he wedded his sister Tethys, who was his mother's daughter." You see -- it's a remarkable coincidence, and it all points toward Heraclitus.

Hermogenes: I think there's something to what you say, Socrates. But I don't understand the meaning of the name Tethys.

Socrates: Well, that's almost self-explanatory. It's just the name of a spring, slightly disguised. "That which is strained and filtered" can be compared to a spring, and the name Tethys is made up of those two ideas. (In the Greek, "Tethys" evokes "diattomenon" -- strained -- and "ethoumenon" -- filtered.)

Hermogenes: That's clever, Socrates.

Socrates: Of course it is! But what comes next? We've already dealt with Zeus.

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: Then let's take his two brothers -- Poseidon and Pluto, whether we call him by that name or by his other one.

Hermogenes: By all means.

Socrates: "Poseidon" seems to mean "foot-chain" -- the person who named him was being held back by the sea in his travels and couldn't walk any further, so he called the ruler of the waters "Posidesmos." (In the Greek, "posi" evokes feet and "desmos" means chain.) The epsilon was probably just added as decoration. Or maybe not -- maybe the name was originally written with a double lambda instead of a sigma, meaning that the god "knows many things." (In the Greek, "polla eidos" -- knowing much.) Or perhaps it comes from his being the "shaker of the earth" -- from "seiein" (to shake), with pi and delta added on.

As for Pluto, he gets his name from giving wealth -- "ploutos." (In the Greek, "Pluto" directly suggests "ploutos" -- wealth.) Wealth, after all, comes out of the earth below. But most people seem to think that the name "Hades" is connected to "the invisible" ("aeides"), which is why they're afraid of him and prefer to call him Pluto instead.

Hermogenes: And what is the real derivation?

Socrates: Despite all the mistakes people make about this god's power and all their foolish fears of him -- the terror of always being with him after death, of the soul going to him stripped of the body -- I think the god's office and name actually match perfectly.

Hermogenes: How so?

Socrates: Let me explain. But first, a question: which chain holds an animal more tightly and keeps it more firmly in place -- desire, or necessity?

Hermogenes: Desire, Socrates. Desire is far stronger.

Socrates: Then don't you think many people would escape from Hades if he didn't bind the departed with the strongest possible chain?

Hermogenes: They certainly would.

Socrates: And if by the strongest chain, then by some desire -- not by necessity?

Hermogenes: That's clear.

Socrates: And there are many desires?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: Then he must bind with the greatest of all desires, if his chain is to be the greatest?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And is any desire stronger than the thought that associating with someone will make you a better person?

Hermogenes: Certainly not.

Socrates: Then that's the reason, Hermogenes, why nobody who has gone to Hades is willing to come back to us. Even the Sirens have fallen under his spell, along with everyone else. Such is the charm this god can weave into his words. On this view, he's the perfect and accomplished Sophist, and the great benefactor of those in the other world. And even to us on earth he sends abundant blessings from below, which is why he's called Pluto -- "the wealthy one."

Note also that he won't associate with people while they're in the body -- only after the soul has been freed from the body's desires and corruptions. There's real philosophy in that. Because in their liberated state, he can bind souls with the desire for virtue. But while they're confused and maddened by the body, not even his father Kronos could hold them with his famous chains.

Hermogenes: There's a lot of truth in what you say.

Socrates: And the legislator called him Hades not from "the unseen" ("aeides") -- far from it -- but from his "knowledge" ("eidenai") of all noble things. (In the Greek, "Hades" is connected to "eidenai" -- to know -- rather than "a-ides" -- unseen.)

Hermogenes: Very good! And what do we say about Demeter, Hera, Apollo, Athena, Hephaestus, Ares, and the other gods?

Socrates: Demeter means "the giving mother" -- she gives food like a mother. (In the Greek, "Demeter" evokes "didousa meter" -- "mother who gives.") Hera is "the lovely one." (In the Greek, "Hera" suggests "erate" -- lovely.) Zeus, according to tradition, loved and married her. Or possibly the name was given with the sky in mind, and is really a disguise for "air" ("aer"), with the end put at the beginning. You'll see the truth of this if you repeat "Hera" several times.

People dread the name Persephone just as they dread the name Apollo -- and with just as little reason. Their fear comes from not understanding how names work. They keep changing the name to "Phersephone," and that terrifies them. But the real name means that the goddess is wise. (In the Greek, "Pherepapha" comes from "ephaptomene tou pheromenou" -- "touching that which is in motion" -- i.e., grasping moving reality.) Since everything in the world is in motion, the principle that can embrace, touch, and follow all moving things is wisdom. And Hades, who is wise, naturally takes a wise consort. People altered her name to Persephone for the sake of euphony, caring more about how it sounds than what it means.

Now, about Apollo -- as I was saying, people generally think his name has some terrible significance. Have you noticed that?

Hermogenes: Yes, definitely, and what you say is true.

Socrates: But the name, in my view, is actually a beautiful expression of the god's power.

Hermogenes: How so?

Socrates: I'll try to explain. I don't think any single name could better capture all four of his attributes -- music, prophecy, medicine, and archery.

Hermogenes: That must be a remarkable name. I'd like to hear the explanation.

Socrates: It's a harmonious name, as befits the God of Harmony. First, consider that the purifications and cleansings that doctors and priests use -- their fumigations, their ritual washings and sprinklings -- all serve one purpose: to make a person pure in both body and soul.

Hermogenes: Very true.

Socrates: And isn't Apollo the purifier, the washer, the one who cleanses all impurities?

Hermogenes: He certainly is.

Socrates: So in terms of his purifying and cleansing role -- as the physician who prescribes these things -- he could rightly be called "Apolouon" ("the purifier"). (In the Greek, "apolouon" means "washer" or "purifier.") In terms of his prophetic powers and his truthfulness and sincerity, he could be called "Haplous" ("sincere" or "simple"), as the Thessalians do -- all Thessalians call him "Haplous." (In the Greek, "haplous" means "sincere" or "straightforward.") And as "the one who always shoots" ("aei ballon"), because he's a master archer who never misses. Or the name might refer to his musical powers: just as in "akolouthos" ("companion") and "akoitis" ("spouse"), where the alpha means "together," the meaning of Apollo would be "moving together" -- because he's the god who makes all things move together in harmony, whether the poles of heaven or the harmony of song. He moves everything in concert through a harmonious power, which is what both astronomers and musicians will tell you.

He's the god who presides over harmony and makes all things move together -- among both gods and humans. And just as "akolouthos" and "akoitis" have alpha substituted for omicron, "Apollon" is really "homopolon" ("moving all together"). The second lambda was added to avoid the unfortunate sound of "apollon" -- which sounds like "destroyer" ("apolein"). (In the Greek, "Apollon" sounds dangerously close to "apollon" -- destruction.) That suspicion of destructive power still haunts some people's minds because they don't understand the true value of the name. But as I've just shown, the name refers to all the god's powers: the sincere one, the always-shooting one, the purifier, the one who moves all things together. (In the Greek: "haplous," "aei ballon," "apolouon," "homopolon.")

The Muses and music get their name from "making philosophical inquiries" ("mosthai"). (In the Greek, "Mousai" is connected to "mosthai" -- to seek or investigate.) Leto is called by that name because she's such a gentle goddess, so willing ("ethelemon") to grant requests. Or maybe her name is really "Letho," as foreigners often call her -- suggesting her gentle, smooth, easygoing nature.

Artemis gets her name from her sound and well-ordered nature ("artemes" -- healthy, sound), from her love of virginity, or perhaps from her being a "proficient in virtue" ("arete"), or possibly from "hating intercourse" ("ton aroton misesasa"). The person who named her could have had any or all of these in mind.

Hermogenes: What about Dionysus and Aphrodite?

Socrates: Son of Hipponicus, you're asking a solemn question! There's both a serious explanation and a humorous one for each of these names. The serious one you'll have to get from someone else, but I'm happy to share the humorous version -- because the gods love a good joke too.

Dionysus is simply "the giver of wine" -- "didous oinos," which you could playfully call "Didoinusos." (In the Greek, "Dionysos" is derived from "didous ton oinon" -- "giver of wine.") And "oinos" (wine) is properly "oionous," because wine makes those who drink it think ("oiesthai") they have a mind ("noun") when they don't.

As for Aphrodite, born from the foam ("aphros"), we can accept that derivation on Hesiod's authority.

Hermogenes: There's still Athena -- and you, Socrates, as an Athenian, surely won't forget her. And there are also Hephaestus and Ares.

Socrates: I'm not likely to forget them.

Hermogenes: No, indeed.

Socrates: There's no difficulty in explaining Athena's other name.

Hermogenes: What other name?

Socrates: We call her Pallas.

Hermogenes: Right.

Socrates: We'd be right to suppose this comes from armed dancing. Lifting yourself or anything else above the earth, or moving with the hands -- we call that shaking or dancing ("pallein"). (In the Greek, "Pallas" is connected to "pallein" -- to brandish or shake.)

Hermogenes: That's quite true.

Socrates: So that's the explanation of Pallas.

Hermogenes: Yes, but what about the name Athena?

Socrates: That's a weightier matter. Modern interpreters of Homer can help us here, I think. Most of them, when explaining the poet, say he meant by Athena "mind" ("nous") and "intelligence" ("dianoia"). And the original name-giver seems to have gone even further, calling her "divine intelligence" ("theou noesis"), as if to say: "This is she who has the mind of God" -- "theonoa." (In the Greek, "Athena" is connected to "theonoa" -- divine intelligence -- using a dialectical alpha for eta and dropping some letters.) Or maybe "theonoe" means "she who knows divine things better than others" ("theia noousa"). Or perhaps the author wanted to connect the goddess with moral intelligence ("en ethei noesin" -- thinking within character), giving her the name "ethonoe," which over time was altered into "Athena."

Hermogenes: But what do you say about Hephaestus?

Socrates: You mean the noble lord of light? ("Phaeos histora" -- "one who knows light.") (In the Greek, "Hephaistos" is "Phaistos" with an eta added by attraction -- "Phaistos" being the "knower of light.")

Hermogenes: Of course.

Socrates: Well, "Hephaistos" is just "Phaistos" with an eta tacked on. That's obvious to anyone.

Hermogenes: Very probably -- unless a better idea comes along.

Socrates: To prevent that, you'd better ask me about Ares.

Hermogenes: All right, what about Ares?

Socrates: Ares could be named from his manliness -- "arren" (male) -- or from his hard, unyielding nature -- "arratos" (unbreakable). (In the Greek, "Ares" can be connected to "arren" -- male -- or "arratos" -- stubborn, unbreakable.) Either derivation fits the god of war perfectly.

Hermogenes: Very true.

Socrates: And now, by the gods, let's leave the gods alone! I'm afraid of them. Ask me about anything else, and you'll see how Euthyphro's horses can gallop.

Hermogenes: Just one more god! I'd like to know about Hermes, since I'm supposedly not a true son of his. Let's figure him out, and then I'll know whether there's anything to what Cratylus says about me.

Socrates: I'd say the name Hermes has to do with speech. He's the interpreter ("hermeneus"), the messenger, the thief, the deceiver, the bargainer -- all of which involve language. As I was saying earlier, the word "eirein" expresses the use of speech, and there's a recurring Homeric word "emesato" that means "he contrived." Out of these two words -- "eirein" and "mesasthai" -- the legislator formed the name of the god who invented language and speech. We can imagine him addressing us: "My friends, since he is the contriver of speech and tales, you should properly call him Eiremes." But we've improved this, as we think, into "Hermes."

And Iris also seems to get her name from "eirein" (to speak), because she was a messenger.

Hermogenes: Then I'm quite sure Cratylus was right that I'm no true son of Hermes -- I'm really not good with words!

Socrates: There's also good reason, my friend, for Pan being the double-natured son of Hermes.

Hermogenes: How do you figure that?

Socrates: You know that speech signifies all things ("pan") and is always turning them round and round, and has two forms -- true and false?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: Well, the truth in speech is the smooth and sacred form that dwells above among the gods, while falsehood dwells below among humans, rough like the goat of tragedy. (In the Greek, "tragoidia" -- tragedy -- is connected to "tragos" -- goat.) Tales and falsehoods generally belong to the tragic, goatish side of life, and tragedy is their natural home.

Hermogenes: Very true.

Socrates: Then Pan, who declares all things ("pan") and perpetually moves all things ("aei polon"), is rightly called "aipolos" (goatherd) -- being the double-natured son of Hermes, smooth in his upper part and rough and goatlike in his lower regions. And as Hermes' son, he is speech -- or the brother of speech. And that a brother should resemble his brother is no surprise.

But as I was saying, my dear Hermogenes, let's get away from the gods.

Hermogenes: From those gods, absolutely, Socrates. But why shouldn't we talk about another kind of "gods" -- the sun, moon, stars, earth, aether, air, fire, water, the seasons, and the year?

Socrates: You're piling a lot of work on me. Still, if you want, I won't refuse.

Hermogenes: You'd be doing me a favor.

Socrates: Where shall I begin? With the one you mentioned first -- the sun?

Hermogenes: Very good.

Socrates: The origin of the sun's name comes out more clearly in the Doric form, where they call him "halios." This could be because at sunrise he gathers ("halizoi") people together, or because he's always rolling ("aei eilein") around the earth in his course, or from "aiolein" -- to variegate -- because he variegates the things the earth produces.

Hermogenes: But what about "selene" (the moon)?

Socrates: That name is rather unfortunate for Anaxagoras.

Hermogenes: How so?

Socrates: The name seems to anticipate his recent discovery that the moon gets its light from the sun.

Hermogenes: Why do you say that?

Socrates: The two words "selas" (brightness) and "phos" (light) mean much the same thing, right?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And the light of the moon is always both new ("neon") and old ("enon"), if Anaxagoras's followers are right. The sun in its revolution constantly adds new light, while the old light from the previous month remains.

Hermogenes: Very true.

Socrates: The moon is also frequently called "selanaia."

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: And since she has a light that's always old and always new ("enon neon aei"), she could very properly be called "selaenoneoia." And this, when compressed, becomes "selanaia." (In the Greek, the name "selanaia" is built from "selas" -- brightness -- plus "enon" -- old -- plus "neon" -- new -- plus "aei" -- always.)

Hermogenes: Now that's a positively dithyrambic name, Socrates! But what do you say about the month and the stars?

Socrates: "Meis" (month) is named from "meiousthai" (to grow less), because the moon diminishes. "Astra" (stars) seems to come from "astrape" -- which is an improved form of "anastrope," meaning "turning the eyes upward." (In the Greek, "astra" is connected to "anastrephein opa" -- turning the gaze upward.)

Hermogenes: What do you say about "pyr" (fire) and "hydor" (water)?

Socrates: "Pyr" has me stumped. Either the muse of Euthyphro has deserted me, or there's some real difficulty with this word. But let me tell you about the trick I use whenever I hit a wall like this.

Hermogenes: What trick?

Socrates: I'll explain. But first, can you tell me what "pyr" means?

Hermogenes: I really can't.

Socrates: Here's what I suspect: this and several other words are actually of foreign origin. The Greeks, especially those who lived under foreign rule, frequently borrowed words from other languages.

Hermogenes: And the point is?

Socrates: That if you try to find the natural fitness of these names using Greek alone, ignoring the language they actually came from, you're likely to get nowhere.

Hermogenes: Yes, certainly.

Socrates: Well, "pyr" doesn't easily connect to any Greek roots. And the Phrygians have what appears to be the same word, just slightly modified -- as they do with "hydor" (water), "kynes" (dogs), and many others.

Hermogenes: That's true.

Socrates: So we shouldn't force an interpretation -- something to say about them might easily be found from a foreign source. That's how I dispose of "pyr" and "hydor."

Now, "aer" (air), Hermogenes -- this can be explained as the element that "raises things" from the earth ("airei"), or as "always flowing" ("aei rhei"). Or, since the flowing of air is wind, and the poets call winds "aetai" (air-blasts), the name-giver might have meant "aetorroun" -- "air-flow" -- in the sense of "wind-flow" ("pneumatorroun"). And so he used the word "aer."

"Aither" (aether) I'd interpret as "aeitheer" -- "that which always runs in a current around the air." (In the Greek, "aither" comes from "aei thein peri ton aera rheon" -- "always running and flowing around the air.")

The meaning of "ge" (earth) comes out better in the form "gaia," because the earth is truly the "mother" ("gaia" suggesting "genneteira" -- she who gives birth). In Homer, "gegaasi" means "to have been born."

Hermogenes: Good.

Socrates: What's next?

Hermogenes: There are "horai" (the seasons) and the two names for the year: "eniautos" and "etos."

Socrates: The "horai" should be spelled in the old Attic way if you want the probable truth: they're rightly called "horai" because they divide ("horizousin") the summers and winters and winds and the fruits of the earth.

As for "eniautos" and "etos," they appear to be the same idea -- "that which brings to light the plants and growths of the earth in their turn and reviews them within itself." (In the Greek, "eniautos" comes from "en heauto exetazei" -- "examines within itself" -- and is split into two words: "eniautos" from "en heauto" and "etos" from "etazei.") This is similar to how the original name of Zeus was divided into Zena and Dia -- the single idea of a power that reviews things from within gets expressed in two words, "etos" and "eniautos."

Hermogenes: Socrates, you really are making amazing progress.

Socrates: I'm on a roll.

Hermogenes: You certainly are.

Socrates: And I haven't hit top speed yet.

Hermogenes: I'd very much like to know how you'd explain the virtues. What principle of correctness is there in those wonderful words -- wisdom, understanding, justice, and the rest?

Socrates: That's an enormous class of names you're digging up! Still, since I've put on the lion's skin, I'd better not lose heart. So I need to consider the meanings of "phronesis" (practical wisdom), "synesis" (understanding), "gnome" (judgment), "episteme" (knowledge), and all those other wonderful words you mention?

Hermogenes: We can't stop now -- not until we've figured out their meaning.

Socrates: By the dog of Egypt! A not-bad idea just occurred to me. I believe the original name-givers were a lot like many of our modern philosophers: in their constant search for the nature of things, they kept going round and round until they got dizzy. And then they imagined that it was the world spinning, not themselves. They projected their own internal dizziness onto nature, and concluded that nothing is stable or permanent -- that everything is just flux and motion and constant change. That's the framework that seems to be behind the names we've been discussing.

Hermogenes: How so, Socrates?

Socrates: Didn't you notice that the names we just examined all seem to indicate motion and flux?

Hermogenes: No, I never thought of it.

Socrates: Take the first one you mentioned. It clearly points to motion.

Hermogenes: Which one?

Socrates: "Phronesis" (practical wisdom) -- it can mean "perception of motion and flow" ("phoras kai rhou noesis"), or "the blessing of motion" ("phoras onesis"). Either way, it's connected to motion ("pheresthai"). (In the Greek, "phronesis" is built from roots meaning motion, flow, and perception.)

"Gnome" (judgment) clearly implies the pondering ("nomesis") of becoming ("genesis"). To ponder is to consider.

Or take "noesis" (thought) itself -- it's really "neou hesis" (the desire of the new). The word "neos" implies that the world is always in the process of creation. The original name-giver wanted to express the soul's longing for novelty. The original form was "neoesis," but eta replaced the double epsilon.

"Sophrosyne" (self-control) is the preservation ("soteria") of that wisdom ("phronesis") we were just discussing.

"Episteme" (knowledge) is related to this too: it indicates that the good soul follows ("hepetai") the motion of things, neither running ahead nor falling behind. So the word should really be read as "epistemene" -- "she who follows along." (In the Greek, "episteme" comes from "hepesthai" -- to follow along with.)

"Synesis" (understanding) can be seen as a kind of conclusion -- derived from "synienai" (to go along with). Like "epistasthai" (to know), it implies the soul progressing in company with the nature of things.

"Sophia" (wisdom) is darker and seems not to be native Greek. The meaning is: "touching the motion or stream of things." Remember that the poets, when describing the start of rapid motion, often use the word "esythe" (he rushed). There was even a famous Spartan named Sous ("Rush") -- the Spartans use this word for rapid motion. And the touching ("epaphe") of this motion is expressed by "sophia." Everything is supposed to be in motion.

"Agathon" (the good) names what is admirable ("agaston") in nature. Even though all things move, some move faster and some slower, and those that are most admirably swift are called "agathon."

"Dikaiosyne" (justice) is clearly "dikaiou synesis" -- understanding of the just. But the actual word "dikaion" (just) is harder. People agree up to a point about justice, and then they start disagreeing.

Those who think everything is in motion conceive most of nature as a kind of receptacle, and they say there's a penetrating power that passes through everything, the instrument of creation -- the subtlest and swiftest element. It must be the subtlest, because otherwise it couldn't pass through everything, and the swiftest, because it passes by other things as if they were standing still. This element, which superintends all things and pierces through all -- "diaion" -- is rightly called "dikaion." The kappa is just added for euphony. (In the Greek, "dikaion" comes from "dia-ion" -- "passing through.")

So far there's general agreement on justice. But I, Hermogenes, being an enthusiastic student, have been told in confidence that justice is also the cause of the world. Now, a "cause" is "that because of which" something is created. And someone whispers in my ear that justice is rightly so called because it partakes of the nature of causation. I start asking gently: "Well, fine, but if that's all true, I still want to know what justice IS." And they think I'm asking tiresome questions and jumping ahead, that I've already been sufficiently answered. They try to satisfy me with one derivation after another, until they start arguing among themselves.

One says justice is the sun -- the only thing that truly pierces and burns as guardian of nature. And when I happily repeat this beautiful idea, someone laughs and says, "So there's no justice in the world after sunset?" When I beg them to give me their honest opinion, one says "fire in the abstract" -- which isn't very illuminating. Another says, "No, not fire in the abstract, but the abstraction of heat in fire." Yet another ridicules all of this and says, following Anaxagoras, that justice is mind, since mind has absolute power, mixes with nothing, orders all things, and passes through everything.

In the end, my friend, I find myself in far greater confusion about justice than before I started learning about it. But I still think the name was given for the reasons I mentioned.

Hermogenes: I think, Socrates, you're not making this up on the spot. You must have heard it from someone else.

Socrates: And not the rest?

Hermogenes: Hardly.

Socrates: All right, then let me continue, and maybe I can convince you that the rest is original too. What comes after justice? I don't think we've discussed courage ("andreia") yet. Injustice ("adikia") is obviously just a hindrance to the penetrating principle ("diaiontos"), so we needn't bother with it.

The name "andreia" (courage) seems to imply a battle -- a battle in the world of existence. According to the flux doctrine, it's a counter-flow ("enantia rhoe"). If you remove the delta from "andreia," the name directly signifies the thing: courage is not the stream opposed to every stream, but only to the stream that's contrary to justice. Otherwise courage wouldn't be praiseworthy.

The words "arren" (male) and "aner" (man) also contain a similar allusion -- to the principle of upward flow ("tes ano rhoes"). (In the Greek, "aner" suggests "ano rhoe" -- upward flow.)

"Gyne" (woman) I suspect is the same as "gone" (birth). "Thely" (female) seems partly derived from "thele" (the breast), because the breast, like rain, makes things flourish ("tethalenai").

Hermogenes: That seems probable.

Socrates: Yes, and the very word "thallein" (to flourish) seems to capture the growth of youth -- swift and sudden. The legislator expressed this in the name, which is a compound of "thein" (running) and "hallesthai" (leaping).

Look at me galloping along once I hit smooth ground! There are still plenty of important names left to explain.

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: There's the word "techne" (art/skill), for example.

Hermogenes: Very true.

Socrates: That can be identified with "echonoe" -- "possession of mind." You just need to remove the tau and insert two omicrons. (In the Greek, "techne" becomes "echonoe" -- "having mind" -- with minor letter changes.)

Hermogenes: That's a pretty shabby etymology.

Socrates: Yes, my friend, but you have to remember that the original names have been buried and disguised over time. People stuck on and stripped off letters for the sake of euphony, twisting and decorating them in every possible way. Time has played its part too. Take the word "katoptron" (mirror) -- why is the rho in there? Someone who cared nothing for truth but only for how the mouth feels must have added it. These additions pile up until eventually no human being can figure out the original meaning. Another example: the word "sphinx" -- which should properly be "phix," but someone added the sigma.

Hermogenes: That's quite true, Socrates.

CRATYLUS (Part 2)

Socrates: And yet, if you're allowed to add and remove whatever letters you want, names would be far too easy to make, and any name could be adapted to fit any object.

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: Yes, it is. So a wise dictator like yourself should observe the laws of moderation and probability.

Hermogenes: That's my goal.

Socrates: Mine too, Hermogenes. But don't be too much of a stickler, or "you'll unnerve me of my strength." Once you let me add "mechane" (contrivance) to "techne" (art), I'll be at the top of my game. I take "mechane" to be a sign of great accomplishment ("anein" -- to accomplish), since "mekos" means greatness, and "mekos" plus "anein" make up "mechane." (In the Greek, "mechane" is built from "megas" -- great -- and "anein" -- to accomplish.)

But as I was saying, now that I'm at my peak, I'd like to examine two key words: "arete" (virtue) and "kakia" (vice). I don't fully understand "arete" yet, but "kakia" is transparent and fits with everything we've been saying. Since all things are in flux ("ionton"), "kakia" means "kakos ion" -- "going badly." When this bad motion exists in the soul, it gets the general name "kakia" -- vice. (In the Greek, "kakia" comes from "kakos ienai" -- to go badly.)

The meaning of "kakos ienai" is further illustrated by "deilia" (cowardice), which should have come after "andreia" (courage) but got skipped. "Deilia" means the soul is bound by a strong chain ("desmos"), since "lian" means "strength." So "deilia" expresses the greatest and strongest bond on the soul. And "aporia" (difficulty, perplexity) is a related concept -- from "a" (not) and "poreuesthai" (to go) -- like anything that impedes motion and movement.

So "kakia" means going badly, limping and halting, which fills the soul with vice. And if "kakia" is the name for that sort of thing, "arete" (virtue) will be its opposite: first, ease of motion; second, the stream of the good soul flowing unimpeded, without obstruction. It's called "arete" -- or more correctly, "aeirheite" ("ever-flowing") -- and may at some point have had the form "hairete" ("eligible"), suggesting that nothing is more worth choosing than virtue. And this has been hammered into "arete." (In the Greek, "arete" can be derived from "aei rheite" -- ever-flowing -- or "hairete" -- worth choosing.)

I'm sure you'll think this is another one of my inventions. But if the previous derivation of "kakia" was right, then "arete" should be right too.

Hermogenes: But what about "kakon" (bad, evil), which played such a big role in your earlier discussion?

Socrates: That's a very strange word, and I can barely form an opinion about it. So I'll have to resort to my usual trick.

Hermogenes: What trick?

Socrates: Declaring it foreign. I'll give that word foreign origin status too.

Hermogenes: Fair enough. But suppose we leave those and try to work out the logic of "kalon" (beautiful) and "aischron" (ugly/shameful).

Socrates: The meaning of "aischron" is clear: it's "aei ischon rhoes" -- "always preventing flow." This matches everything we've been saying. The original name-giver was a fierce enemy of stagnation, so he gave the name "aeischoroun" to whatever hindered the flow. That's been compressed into "aischron." (In the Greek, "aischron" comes from "aei ischein rhoun" -- always holding back the stream.)

Hermogenes: But what do you say about "kalon" (beautiful)?

Socrates: That's more obscure, though the form has only been changed by converting "ou" into "o."

Hermogenes: What do you mean?

Socrates: This name seems to denote mind.

Hermogenes: How so?

Socrates: Let me ask you: what is the cause of anything having a name? Isn't the principle that assigns the name the cause?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: And that principle must be mind -- whether the mind of gods, humans, or both?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: And isn't mind what "called" ("kalesan") things by their names? And isn't mind the beautiful ("kalon")? (In the Greek, "kalon" -- beautiful -- is connected to "kalein" -- to call or name.)

Hermogenes: That's evident.

Socrates: And aren't the works of intelligence and mind worthy of praise, while other works deserve blame?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: Medicine does a physician's work, carpentry does a carpenter's work?

Hermogenes: Exactly.

Socrates: And the principle of beauty does the works of beauty?

Hermogenes: Of course.

Socrates: And we said that principle is mind?

Hermogenes: Very true.

Socrates: Then mind is rightly called "beauty" because it does the works we recognize and celebrate as beautiful?

Hermogenes: That's evident.

Socrates: What other names are left?

Hermogenes: The words connected with "agathon" (good) and "kalon" (beautiful) -- like "sympheron" (expedient), "lysiteloun" (profitable), "ophelimon" (advantageous), "kerdaleon" (gainful), and their opposites.

Socrates: You can probably figure out "sympheron" (expedient) yourself from the previous examples. It's a sister word to "episteme" -- meaning the motion ("phora") of the soul accompanying the world. Things done on this principle are called "symphora" or "sympheronta" because they're carried along with the world. (In the Greek, "sympheron" comes from "sym-" -- together -- and "phora" -- motion.)

Hermogenes: That's probable.

Socrates: And "kerdaleon" (gainful) comes from "kerdos" (gain), but you have to change the delta to a nu to get at the meaning. This word also signifies good, but in a different way: the name-giver intended to express the power of mixing ("kerannumenon") and universal penetration in the good. But he inserted a delta instead of a nu and made "kerdos." (In the Greek, "kerdos" is connected to "kerannumenon" -- mixing.)

Hermogenes: Well, but what about "lysiteloun" (profitable)?

Socrates: I don't think people mean by "profitable" what pays ("lyei") the shopkeeper. They use it to mean "swift." You should think of the profitable as that which, being the swiftest thing in existence, allows no pause or standstill but always, whenever things start to stop, releases ("lyei") them again, making motion immortal and unceasing. In this sense, the good is rightly called "lysiteloun" -- "that which loosens ("lyon") the end ("telos") of motion." (In the Greek, "lysiteloun" comes from "lyein" -- to loosen -- and "telos" -- end.)

"Ophelimon" (advantageous) comes from "ophellein" -- to increase, to make grow. That's a common Homeric word and has a foreign flavor.

Hermogenes: And what about their opposites?

Socrates: The ones that are simple negatives hardly need discussion.

Hermogenes: Which ones?

Socrates: "Asympheron" (inexpedient), "anopheles" (unprofitable), "alysiteles" (unadvantageous), "akerdes" (ungainful).

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: I'd rather look at "blaberon" (harmful) and "zemiodes" (hurtful).

Hermogenes: Good.

Socrates: "Blaberon" (harmful) is what hinders or damages ("blaptein") the flow ("rhoun"). "Blapton" really means "boulomenon haptein" -- "wanting to hold or bind." And "haptein" (to bind) is the same as "dein" (to fasten), which is always a term of criticism. So "wanting to bind the flow" ("boulomenon haptein rhoun") would properly be "boulapteroun," which has been improved into "blaberon." (In the Greek, "blaberon" is compressed from "boulomenon haptein rhoun" -- wanting to bind the stream.)

Hermogenes: You produce the most curious results, Socrates! When I hear a word like "boulapteroun," I can't help imagining you're making your mouth into a flute and puffing away at some prelude to Athena.

Socrates: That's the fault of the name-makers, Hermogenes, not mine.

Hermogenes: Very true. But what's the derivation of "zemiodes" (hurtful)?

Socrates: What does "zemiodes" mean? Let me point out, Hermogenes, how right I was to say that great changes in meaning come from adding and removing letters. Even a very slight change can sometimes flip the meaning entirely. Take the word "deon" (obligation), which just occurred to me. It reminds me of something I was going to tell you: our fashionable modern language has twisted and disguised and completely altered the original meanings of both "deon" and "zemiodes," even though in the old language the meaning was clear.

Hermogenes: What do you mean?

Socrates: I'll explain. Our ancestors loved the sounds of iota and delta, especially the women, who are the most conservative with language. But now people change iota to eta or epsilon, and delta to zeta -- supposedly to sound grander.

Hermogenes: How do you mean?

Socrates: For example, in very ancient times they called the day either "himera" or "hemera." We call it "hemera."

Hermogenes: That's true.

Socrates: Only the ancient form reveals the name-giver's intention. People longed for ("himeirousi") and loved the light that comes after darkness, so they called it "himera" -- from "himeros" (desire, longing). (In the Greek, the old form "himera" comes from "himeros" -- desire or longing.)

Hermogenes: Clearly.

Socrates: But now the name has been so altered that you can't tell the meaning. Though some people think "hemera" means it makes things "gentle" ("hemera").

Hermogenes: That's my own impression.

Socrates: And did you know the ancients said "duogon" instead of "zugon"?

Hermogenes: They did.

Socrates: "Zugon" (yoke) has no transparent meaning, but the old form "duogon" expresses the binding of two together ("duein agoge" -- "leading of two") for the purpose of pulling. This was changed to "zugon." And there are many other similar examples.

Hermogenes: There are.

Socrates: Following the same line of thought, the word "deon" (obligation) has a meaning opposite to all our other terms for good. "Deon" is a species of good, and yet it means "chain" ("desmos") or "hindrance of motion" -- making it the brother of "blaberon." (In the Greek, "deon" is connected to "desmos" -- a chain or bond.)

Hermogenes: Yes, Socrates, that's quite clear.

Socrates: Not if you restore the ancient form, which is probably the correct one. Read "dion" instead of "deon" -- convert the epsilon back to an iota, the old way. Then the word agrees with all the other good-words. "Dion," not "deon," signifies the good and is a term of praise. So the name-giver hasn't contradicted himself after all. In all these various words -- "deon" (obligatory), "ophelimon" (advantageous), "lysiteloun" (profitable), "kerdaleon" (gainful), "agathon" (good), "sympheron" (expedient), "euporon" (plenteous) -- the same concept is implied: the all-pervading, ordering principle is praised, and the restraining, binding principle is criticized.

And this helps explain "zemiodes" (hurtful): change the zeta back to a delta, as in the ancient language, and it becomes "demiodes." This name, as you'll see, refers to "that which binds motion" ("dounti ion"). (In the Greek, "zemiodes" is connected through its ancient form "demiodes" to "dein" -- to bind -- and "ion" -- going.)

Hermogenes: What do you say about "hedone" (pleasure), "lype" (pain), "epithymia" (desire), and words like those?

Socrates: I don't think they're particularly difficult. "Hedone" (pleasure) is "he onesis" -- the action that tends toward benefit. The original form was probably "heone," but a delta was inserted. (In the Greek, "hedone" comes from "onesis" -- benefit.)

"Lype" (pain) comes from the relaxation ("lyein") the body feels in sorrow.

"Ania" (trouble) is the hindrance of motion ("a" -- not -- and "ienai" -- to go).

"Algedon" (distress), if I'm not mistaken, is a foreign word, derived from "algeinos" (grievous).

"Odyne" (grief) is named from the "putting on" ("endysis") of sorrow.

"Achthedon" (vexation) -- "the word itself labors," as anyone can see.

"Chara" (joy) is the very expression of the soul's free flow and diffusion ("cheo" -- to pour).

"Terpsis" (delight) is so called from pleasure creeping ("herpon") through the soul, which can be likened to breath ("pnoe"). It was originally "herpnoun" but was changed over time to "terpnon."

"Euphrosyne" (cheerfulness) and "epithymia" (desire) explain themselves. "Euphrosyne" ought to be "eupherosyne" -- named from the soul moving ("pheresthai") in harmony with nature. It was changed to "euphrosyne."

"Epithymia" is really "he epi ton thymon iousa dynamis" -- "the power that enters into the passionate part of the soul." (In the Greek, "epithymia" comes from "epi" -- toward -- "thymos" -- spirit/passion -- and "iousa" -- going.)

"Thymos" (passion/spirit) itself comes from "thysis" -- the rushing and boiling of the soul.

"Himeros" (desire/yearning) denotes the stream ("rhous") that most powerfully draws the soul -- flowing with desire and expressing a violent attraction toward things. (In the Greek, "himeros" is connected to "hiemenos" -- rushing -- and "rhous" -- stream.)

"Pothos" (longing) expresses desire for what's not present but absent and "elsewhere" ("pou" -- somewhere). That's why "pothos" applies to absent things, while "himeros" applies to present ones.

"Eros" (love) is so called because it flows in ("esron") from outside. The stream isn't internal but is an influence introduced through the eyes. In ancient times, when they used omicron instead of omega, it was called "esros" (influx). Now, with omega substituted, it's "eros." (In the Greek, "eros" comes from "esrhein" -- to flow in.)

But why don't you give me another word?

Hermogenes: What do you think of "doxa" (opinion, belief) and that category of words?

Socrates: "Doxa" is either derived from "dioxis" (pursuit) -- expressing the soul's march in pursuit of knowledge -- or from the shooting of a bow ("toxon"). The latter seems more likely, and it's confirmed by "oiesis" (thinking), which is just "oisis" (motion) -- the movement of the soul toward the essential nature of each thing.

Similarly, "boule" (counsel, deliberation) has to do with shooting ("bole" -- a throw), and "boulesthai" (to wish or intend) combines aiming and deliberating. All these words follow "doxa" and involve the idea of shooting or aiming. And "aboulia" (lack of counsel) is, by contrast, a mishap -- missing the target, the aim, the goal.

Hermogenes: You're really picking up the pace now, Socrates.

Socrates: I'm almost at the finish line. But before I'm done, I want to explain "ananke" (necessity) and "hekousion" (the voluntary).

"Hekousion" (voluntary) is the yielding ("eikon") and unresisting -- yielding to motion that's in accordance with our will.

But "the necessary" is what resists our will, involving error and ignorance. The idea is taken from walking through a ravine ("anke") -- impassable, rugged, overgrown, impeding your progress. That's where "anankaion" gets its name: "an' anke ion" -- "going through a ravine." (In the Greek, "ananke" comes from "ana" -- through -- and "anke" -- ravine -- and "ion" -- going.)

While my strength holds out, let's press on. I hope you'll keep asking questions.

Hermogenes: Well then, let me ask about the greatest and noblest words: "aletheia" (truth), "pseudos" (falsehood), and "on" (being) -- and while we're at it, why the word "onoma" (name), which is the very subject of our whole discussion, has that name.

Socrates: Do you know the word "maïesthai" (to seek)?

Hermogenes: Yes -- it means the same as "zetein" (to inquire).

Socrates: "Onoma" (name) seems to be a compressed sentence meaning "on hou zetema" -- "being for which there is a search." This is even more obvious in "onomaston" (notable), which states in so many words that "real existence is that for which there is a seeking" ("on hou masma"). (In the Greek, "onoma" comes from "on" -- being -- and "masma/zetema" -- search or seeking.)

"Aletheia" (truth) is also a compressed phrase: "theia ale" -- "divine wandering" -- implying the divine motion of existence. (In the Greek, "aletheia" comes from "theia" -- divine -- and "ale" -- wandering.)

"Pseudos" (falsehood) is the opposite of motion -- another bad name from the legislator, given to stagnation and forced inactivity, which he compared to sleep ("eudein"). The original meaning is disguised by the addition of psi. (In the Greek, "pseudos" is connected to "eudein" -- to sleep -- with a psi added.)

"On" (being) and "ousia" (essence) are really "ion" with the iota broken off. This fits perfectly: being ("on") is also moving ("ion"). And the same goes for not-being: "ouk ion" -- not going. (In the Greek, "on" comes from "ion" -- going -- with the iota dropped.)

Hermogenes: You've really hammered those into shape. But suppose someone asked you: what about the words "ion" (going), "rheon" (flowing), and "doun" (binding)? Show me THEIR fitness.

Socrates: You mean, how would I answer that?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: One approach has already been suggested.

Hermogenes: Which one?

Socrates: Saying that words we can't explain are foreign in origin. And that's probably often true. But it's also possible that the original forms of words have been lost over the ages. Names have been so twisted in every direction that I wouldn't be surprised if the old language, compared to ours, sounded like a foreign tongue to us.

Hermogenes: Very likely.

Socrates: Yes, very likely. But the inquiry demands our full attention, and we can't back down. Because here's the thing: if someone keeps analyzing names into words, and then analyzing those words into their component elements, and keeps repeating this process, eventually the person answering has to give up in despair.

Hermogenes: Very true.

Socrates: And at what point should we give up? Surely when we reach the names that are the building blocks of all other names and sentences. These can't be assumed to be composed of still other names. Take "agathon" (good) -- we said it's a compound of "agaston" (admirable) and "thoos" (swift). And "thoos" is probably made up of other elements, and those of still others. But if we hit a word that can't be broken down any further, we're right to say we've reached a primary element that doesn't need further analysis.

Hermogenes: I think you're right.

Socrates: And if the names you're asking about turn out to be primary elements, we'll need a different method to examine their truth or correctness.

Hermogenes: Very likely.

Socrates: Exactly, Hermogenes. Everything we've discussed so far points to this conclusion. And if the conclusion is sound -- as I believe it is -- then come help me, so I don't say something absurd about the principle behind primary names.

Hermogenes: Let me hear it, and I'll do my best to help.

Socrates: I think you'll agree with me that one principle applies to all names, primary and secondary alike. Viewed simply as names, there's no difference between them.

Hermogenes: Certainly not.

Socrates: All the names we've been explaining were meant to indicate the nature of things.

Hermogenes: Of course.

Socrates: And that's equally true of primary names and secondary ones -- they're all names.

Hermogenes: Surely.

Socrates: But the secondary names, I think, derive their meaning from the primary ones.

Hermogenes: That's evident.

Socrates: Very good. But then how do the primary names -- the ones that can't be broken down further -- show the natures of things, to whatever extent they can? Here's a question: if we had no voice or tongue and wanted to communicate with each other, wouldn't we make signs with our hands and head and body, the way deaf people do?

Hermogenes: We'd have no other choice, Socrates.

Socrates: We'd imitate the nature of the thing. To express lightness and upwardness, we'd raise our hands toward the sky. For heaviness and downwardness, we'd let them drop to the ground. If we were describing a running horse or any other animal, we'd make our bodies and gestures resemble theirs as closely as possible.

Hermogenes: I don't see what else we could do.

Socrates: Right -- bodily imitation is the only way the body can express anything.

Hermogenes: Very true.

Socrates: And when we want to express ourselves using the voice, tongue, or mouth, our expression is simply an imitation of whatever we want to express.

Hermogenes: That must be right, I think.

Socrates: So a name is a vocal imitation of what the namer imitates?

Hermogenes: I think so.

Socrates: Actually, my friend, I don't think we've quite hit on the truth yet.

Hermogenes: Why not?

Socrates: Because if that were right, we'd have to say that people who imitate sheep, roosters, or other animals are naming them.

Hermogenes: That's true.

Socrates: So maybe my approach was wrong?

Hermogenes: It seems so. But I wish you'd tell me, Socrates: what kind of imitation IS a name?

Socrates: First of all, not a musical imitation, even though music is vocal. And not an imitation of what music imitates either. Those wouldn't be names, in my view. Let me put it this way: all objects have sound and shape, and many have color, right?

Hermogenes: Certainly.

Socrates: But the art of naming doesn't seem to be concerned with imitating those qualities. The arts that deal with sound and shape are music and painting.

Hermogenes: True.

Socrates: But isn't there an essence of each thing, just as there's a color and a sound? Isn't there an essence of color and sound themselves, and of anything else that can be said to have an essence?

Hermogenes: I'd think so.

Socrates: Well then, if someone could express the essence of each thing in letters and syllables, wouldn't they be expressing the nature of each thing?

Hermogenes: Absolutely.

Socrates: We called the musician and the painter the two kinds of imitators. What shall we call this imitator?

Hermogenes: I think, Socrates, he must be the namer -- the name-giver we've been looking for.

Socrates: If that's right, then we're in a position to examine the words "rhoe" (stream), "ienai" (to go), and "schesis" (retention) that you were asking about. We can see whether the namer grasped the nature of these things in letters and syllables well enough to imitate their essence.

Hermogenes: Very good.

Socrates: But are those the only primary names, or are there others?

Hermogenes: There must be others.

Socrates: I'd think so. But how do we analyze them further, and where does the imitator begin? Since the imitation of essence is made through syllables and letters, shouldn't we first separate the letters -- the way people studying rhythm first distinguish the basic sounds, then compound sounds, and only after that move on to rhythms?

Hermogenes: Yes.

Socrates: We should begin the same way with letters. First, separate the vowels into classes. Then the consonants and mutes, according to the standard scholarly distinctions. Then the semivowels, which are neither vowels nor mutes. And then classify the vowels themselves. When we've perfected this classification of sounds, we should give them names and see whether there are classes they can all be grouped into. That way we'll see their natures and whether they, like letters, fall into distinct categories. When we've thought all this through, we'll know how to match sounds to the things they resemble -- whether a single letter represents a single thing, or several need to be mixed together.

It's like painting. When a painter wants to depict something, sometimes he uses a single color -- just purple, say -- and sometimes he mixes several colors, as when painting skin tones. He uses his colors as the subject requires. And in the same way, we'll apply letters to the expression of things -- single letters where appropriate, or combinations, forming syllables, and from syllables making nouns and verbs, and from those building up entire language -- large, beautiful, and complete.

Just as the painter creates a figure, so we'll create speech through the art of naming, or rhetoric, or whatever we call it. Not that I mean WE literally do this -- I got carried away. I mean the ancients formed language this way. And what they put together, we need to take apart in the same way, if we want a scientific understanding of the whole subject. We need to check whether both the primary and secondary elements are correctly assigned. Because if they're not, the whole composition will be a sorry piece of work, built in the wrong direction.

Hermogenes: I can well believe that, Socrates.

Socrates: Well, do you think you'd be able to analyze them this way? I'm certain I couldn't.

Hermogenes: Then I'm even less likely to manage it.

Socrates: Shall we give up then? Or shall we do the best we can, saying -- as I said earlier about the gods -- that we know nothing of the truth and are only exploring human opinions? Let us say, before we proceed, that the higher method is the one we or anyone else should follow who wants to analyze language properly. But under the circumstances, as the saying goes, we must do as well as we can. What do you think?

Hermogenes: I very much approve.

Socrates: It may seem ridiculous, Hermogenes, that things should be imitated in letters and syllables. But we can't avoid it -- there's no better principle to look to for the truth of primary names. Without it, we'd have to resort to divine intervention, like the tragic poets who bring in gods on cranes whenever they're stuck. We could say "the gods gave the first names, so they must be right." That would be convenient. Or we could say the names come from some foreign people (since foreigners are older than us). Or we could say that the origins are lost in antiquity. But these are all just clever excuses for having no real explanation.

And yet, any ignorance about primary names means ignorance about secondary ones too, since secondary names can only be explained through primary ones. Clearly, anyone claiming expertise in language should be able to give a very clear account of primary names. Otherwise they'll just be talking nonsense about everything else. Don't you think that's true?

Hermogenes: Certainly, Socrates.

Socrates: My first thoughts about primary names are truly wild and ridiculous. But I'm happy to share them if you want, and I hope you'll share anything better you might have.

Hermogenes: Don't worry -- I'll do my best.

Socrates: First: the letter rho seems to me to be the universal instrument for expressing all motion ("kinesis"). But I haven't yet explained "kinesis" itself, which is really "iesis" (going). The ancients didn't use eta -- they used epsilon -- so the root is "kiein," a foreign form meaning the same as "ienai" (to go). The old word "kinesis" would be correctly given as "iesis" in modern letters. With the foreign root "kiein," allowing for the change of eta and insertion of nu, we get "kinesis" -- which should really be "kieinesis" or "eisis." And "stasis" (rest/standing still) is the negation of "ienai" (going), improved into "stasis."

Now, the letter rho struck the name-giver as an excellent instrument for expressing motion. He uses it constantly for this purpose: in "rhein" (to flow) and "rhoe" (stream), he represents motion with rho. Also in "tromos" (trembling), "trachys" (rough), and in "krouein" (to strike), "thrauein" (to crush), "ereikein" (to bruise), "thryptein" (to break), "kermatizein" (to crumble), "rhymbein" (to whirl). In all these kinds of movement, he generally uses the letter rho -- because, I imagine, he noticed that the tongue is most agitated and least at rest when pronouncing this letter. That's why he used it to express motion.

He uses the letter iota to express the subtle elements that pass through all things -- which is why he employs iota in imitating motion: "ienai" (to go), "hiesthai" (to rush).

Then there's another class of letters -- phi, psi, sigma, and zeta -- whose pronunciation involves a great expenditure of breath. These are used to imitate blowing and windy concepts: "psychron" (shivering cold), "zeon" (seething), "seïesthai" (to be shaken), "seismos" (earthquake). The name-giver always brings these in when he wants to imitate what is "physodes" (windy).

He seems to have thought that the closure and pressure of the tongue in pronouncing delta and tau expressed binding and rest -- stillness in a place. He noticed the liquid movement of lambda, where the tongue slips, and found in it the expression of smoothness, as in "leios" (smooth), "olisthanein" (to slip), "liparon" (sleek), "kollodes" (gluey), and similar words.

The heavier sound of gamma detained the slipping tongue, and the combination of gamma and lambda together gave the notion of something glutinous and sticky, as in "glischros" (sticky), "glykys" (sweet), "gloiodes" (gummy).

He noticed that nu is sounded from within and therefore has a quality of inwardness, so he used it in "endon" (inside) and "entos" (within).

Alpha he assigned to the expression of largeness, and eta to length, because they are big letters. Omicron was the sign of roundness, and so there's plenty of omicron in "gongylon" (round).

And so the original legislator, reducing everything to letters and syllables, impressing names and signs on things, and then building other signs out of them through imitation -- that's how he composed language. At least, that's my view, Hermogenes, of the truth about names.

But I'd like to hear what Cratylus has to say.

Hermogenes: But Socrates, as I was telling you before, Cratylus mystifies me. He says there's a natural fitness of names but never explains what it is, so I can't tell whether his vagueness is deliberate or not. Tell me now, Cratylus -- here in Socrates' presence -- do you agree with what Socrates has been saying about names, or do you have something better? If you do, share it. Either you'll learn from Socrates, or Socrates and I will learn from you.

Cratylus: Come now, Hermogenes -- you don't really think you can learn, or I can explain, any important subject in a moment, do you? Especially not a subject like language, which is perhaps the greatest of all.

Hermogenes: No, of course not. But as Hesiod says, and I agree: "to add a little to a little" is worthwhile. So if you can add anything at all, however small, to what we know, please make the effort. You'd be doing both Socrates and me a favor -- and you certainly owe us that.

Socrates: I'm by no means certain, Cratylus, about the views that Hermogenes and I have worked out. So don't hesitate to share your thoughts. If you've got something better than what I've said, I'll gladly accept it. And I wouldn't be at all surprised if you have. You've clearly been thinking about these things and have had teachers. So if you really do have a better theory about the truth of names, count me among your students.

Cratylus: You're right, Socrates, that I've studied these matters, and I might convert you into a student yet. But I'm afraid the opposite is more likely. I find myself moved to say to you what Achilles says to Ajax in the Prayers: "Illustrious Ajax, son of Telamon, lord of the people, you seem to have spoken entirely to my mind." You, Socrates, seem to me like an oracle, giving answers very much to my liking -- whether you're inspired by Euthyphro or some Muse has long been dwelling in your heart without your knowing it.

Socrates: My dear Cratylus, I've been wondering at my own wisdom for a while now. I don't trust myself. I think I need to pause and ask: "What am I actually saying?" Because there's nothing worse than self-deception -- when the deceiver is always at home and always with you. That's truly terrible. So I should often retrace my steps and try to "look fore and aft," as Homer says.

Now then, where are we? Haven't we been saying that a correct name indicates the nature of the thing? Has that been sufficiently established?

Cratylus: Yes, Socrates. I'm disposed to think that what you say is quite true.

Socrates: So names are given in order to teach?

Cratylus: Certainly.

Socrates: And naming is a craft, with skilled practitioners?

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: And who are these practitioners?

Cratylus: The legislators you spoke of at the start.

Socrates: And does this craft, like other crafts, produce practitioners of varying skill? Some painters are better and some worse?

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: The better painters produce better figures, and the worse produce worse ones. And among builders, the better ones build finer houses, the worse ones build worse ones.

Cratylus: True.

Socrates: And among legislators, some do their work better and some worse?

Cratylus: No -- there I disagree with you.

Socrates: So you don't think some laws are better and others worse?

Cratylus: No, I don't.

Socrates: Or that one name is better than another?

Cratylus: Certainly not.

Socrates: Then all names are correctly given?

Cratylus: Yes -- if they're names at all.

Socrates: Well, what about our friend Hermogenes' name, which we discussed earlier? If he has nothing of Hermes' nature in him, should we say this is a wrong name, or not his name at all?

Cratylus: I'd say it's not his name at all. It only appears to be. His real name belongs to whoever has the nature that corresponds to it.

Socrates: And if someone were to call him "Hermogenes," would that person even be speaking falsely? Because there may be a question of whether you CAN call him Hermogenes if he isn't one.

Cratylus: What do you mean?

Socrates: Are you saying that falsehood is impossible? Because if that's your position, I should point out that there have been plenty of liars throughout history.

Cratylus: But Socrates, how can you say what is not? How can you say something and yet say nothing? Isn't falsehood just saying what is not?

Socrates: That argument is a bit too subtle for a man my age. But let me ask: are you one of those philosophers who think falsehood can be spoken but not really said?

Cratylus: Neither spoken nor said.

Socrates: Not even uttered or addressed? For example, suppose someone met you in a foreign country, took your hand, and said, "Greetings, Athenian stranger, Hermogenes, son of Smicrion." Are you saying those words would have no application to you -- only to our friend Hermogenes here, or perhaps to nobody at all?

Cratylus: In my view, Socrates, the speaker would just be producing meaningless noise.

Socrates: Good enough for me. I just want to know: would the nonsense be true, false, or partly true and partly false?

Cratylus: I'd say the person would be putting himself in motion to no purpose. His words would be a meaningless sound -- like the noise of someone banging on a brass pot.

Socrates: Let's see if we can find common ground, Cratylus. You'd agree that a name is not the same as the thing it names?

Cratylus: I would.

Socrates: And you'd agree that a name is an imitation of the thing?

Cratylus: Certainly.

Socrates: And paintings are also imitations of things, just in a different way?

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: I may not fully understand you, but let me ask: can't both kinds of imitation -- pictures and names -- be attributed and applied to the things they imitate?

Cratylus: They can.

Socrates: Now look at it this way: you can assign the portrait of a man to a man, and the portrait of a woman to a woman?

Cratylus: Certainly.

Socrates: And conversely, you can assign the portrait of a man to a woman, and the portrait of a woman to a man?

Cratylus: Very true -- you CAN.

Socrates: And are both ways of assigning them right, or only the first?

Cratylus: Only the first.

Socrates: That is, the assignment that gives each thing what belongs to it and is like it?

Cratylus: That's my view.

Socrates: Now, since you and I are friends and I want us to understand each other, let me state my position: the first kind of assignment, whether applied to pictures or names, I call right.
