And when applied to names specifically, I call it true as well as right. The other kind -- assigning a name that's unlike the thing -- I call wrong. And in the case of names, false as well as wrong.

Cratylus: That may be true for pictures, Socrates. They can be wrongly assigned. But not names -- names must always be right.

Socrates: Why? What's the difference? Can't I go up to a man and say "This is your portrait," showing him his own likeness, or perhaps the likeness of a woman? When I say "show," I mean bringing it before his eyes.

Cratylus: Certainly.

Socrates: And can't I also go up to him and say "This is your name"? A name, like a picture, is an imitation. So I could bring to his ears the imitation of himself, saying "This is a man," or the imitation of a female, saying "This is a woman." Isn't all that perfectly possible?

Cratylus: I'd like to agree with you, Socrates, so -- granted.

Socrates: That's generous of you, and I think I'm right, though we needn't settle it fully now. But if I can assign names as well as pictures to objects, then the right assignment is what we'd call truth, and the wrong assignment falsehood. And if there can be wrong assignment of names, there can also be wrong assignment of verbs. And if names and verbs can be wrongly assigned, then so can the sentences made from them. What do you say, Cratylus?

Cratylus: I agree. What you say is very true.

Socrates: Furthermore, primary nouns can be compared to pictures. In a picture, you can give all the right colors and shapes, or you might leave some out, or put in too many. Right?

Cratylus: Very true.

Socrates: And the person who gets everything right produces a perfect picture, while the person who adds or leaves something out produces a picture -- but not a good one.

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: In the same way, someone who imitates the nature of things in syllables and letters will produce a good image -- in other words, a good name -- if they get everything right. But if they subtract or perhaps add a little, they'll produce an image that's still an image -- just not a good one. From this I conclude that some names are well made and others poorly made.

Cratylus: That's true.

Socrates: Then the artist of names can sometimes be good and sometimes bad?

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: And we called that artist the legislator?

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: Then, like other artists, the legislator can be good or bad -- if our earlier reasoning holds?

Cratylus: Very true, Socrates. But with language, you see, it's different. When we use grammar to assign the letters alpha, beta, or any others to a particular name -- if we add, remove, or misplace a letter, the name isn't just written wrong, it's not really written at all. It becomes something other than that name entirely.

Socrates: But I'm not sure your view is correct, Cratylus.

Cratylus: How so?

Socrates: What you're saying might be true of numbers. A number must be exactly what it is, or it's a completely different number. Add or subtract a unit from ten and you've got a different number. But this doesn't apply to qualitative things or to representations. In fact, I'd go further: an image that reproduced every single aspect of the original would no longer be just an image.

Let me illustrate. Suppose there are two things: Cratylus, and an image of Cratylus. And suppose some god creates not just a representation of your outward form and color, like a painter would, but also replicates your inner organization -- the same warmth, softness, motion, soul, and mind -- copies all your qualities exactly and places this replica right next to you. Would you say that's Cratylus and an image of Cratylus, or that there are two Cratyluses?

Cratylus: I'd say there are two Cratyluses.

Socrates: Then you see, my friend, that we need to find a different standard of truth in images, and in names too. We can't insist that an image stops being an image just because something is added or left out. Don't you see that images are very far from being exact copies of what they represent?

Cratylus: Yes, I see.

Socrates: And wouldn't it be ridiculous if names were exactly the same as the things they name? Everything would be doubled, and nobody could tell which was the name and which was the reality.

Cratylus: Quite true.

Socrates: Then be brave and admit that one name can be well given and another poorly given. Don't insist that the name be exactly the same as the thing. Allow the occasional wrong letter. And if a wrong letter in a name, then a wrong noun in a sentence. And if a wrong noun in a sentence, then a sentence that doesn't perfectly fit its subject. The thing can still be named and described, as long as the general character of what you're describing is preserved. You'll remember that Hermogenes and I made this very point about the names of letters.

Cratylus: Yes, I remember.

Socrates: Good. When the general character is preserved, the thing is signified -- well, if all the right elements are there; not perfectly, if only some are. I think we'd better accept this, or we'll end up like travelers in Aegina who wander the streets late at night and get told by truth herself that we've arrived too late. Either accept this, or find some entirely new theory of the correctness of names. Because you can't keep saying that a name is the expression of a thing in letters and syllables while also saying one wrong letter destroys it completely -- that's contradictory.

Cratylus: I quite accept what you're saying, Socrates. That seems very reasonable.

Socrates: Good. Since we agree on that, let's ask whether a rightly given name should contain the proper letters.

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: And the proper letters are those that resemble the things?

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: So names that are correctly given are mostly made up of appropriate, similar letters -- otherwise there'd be no resemblance at all. But there will also be some improper elements that spoil the beauty and completeness of the name. You'd agree?

Cratylus: I can't really quarrel with you, Socrates, but I'm not comfortable saying that an incorrectly given name is a name at all.

Socrates: Do you at least admit that a name is a representation of a thing?

Cratylus: Yes, I do.

Socrates: And don't you allow that some nouns are primary and others derived?

Cratylus: Yes, I do.

Socrates: Then if primary names are representations of things, is there any better way to create them than by making them as similar to their objects as possible? Or do you prefer the view of Hermogenes and many others that names are conventional -- meaningful to those who've agreed on them because they already know the things, and that convention is the only principle? Whether you stick with the current convention or make a new one where you call small "large" and large "small" -- it's all the same, they'd say, as long as there's agreement. Which view do you prefer?

Cratylus: Representation through likeness, Socrates, is infinitely better than representation through arbitrary signs.

Socrates: Very good. But if the name is to be like the thing, then the letters out of which primary names are composed must also be like things. Think of painting: how could you compose a picture that looks like anything at all if there were no pigments in nature that resembled the things being depicted?

Cratylus: That would be impossible.

Socrates: In the same way, names could never resemble real things unless the basic elements they're composed of bore some degree of resemblance to the objects the names imitate. And those elements are letters?

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: Then let me invite you to reconsider what Hermogenes and I were saying about individual sounds. Do you agree that the letter rho expresses rapidity, motion, and hardness? Were we right?

Cratylus: I'd say you were right.

Socrates: And that lambda expresses smoothness and softness?

Cratylus: Right again.

Socrates: And yet, as you know, what we call "sklerotēs" (hardness) the Eretrians call "sklerotēr" -- with a rho at the end instead of a sigma.

Cratylus: Very true.

Socrates: So are rho and sigma equivalent? Do they both have the same significance -- rho at the end for them, sigma for us -- or does the word mean something to only one of us?

Cratylus: No, it surely means something to both of us.

Socrates: Insofar as rho and sigma are alike, or insofar as they're unlike?

Cratylus: Insofar as they're alike.

Socrates: Are they completely alike?

Cratylus: Well, at least for the purpose of expressing motion.

Socrates: But what about the lambda in the middle of "sklērotēs"? Lambda expresses softness, not hardness.

Cratylus: Well, maybe the lambda is wrongly inserted, Socrates, and should be changed to a rho -- as you were suggesting to Hermogenes when you talked about adding and removing letters as needed. You were right about that, in my opinion.

Socrates: Fine. But the word is still perfectly intelligible to both of us, isn't it? When I say "sklēron" (hard), you know what I mean.

Cratylus: Yes, my friend -- but that's just custom.

Socrates: And what is custom but convention? I make a sound, I understand what I mean by it, and you know what I mean. That's what you're saying, isn't it?

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: And if when I speak you understand my meaning, I've communicated something to you?

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: And this communication might proceed through unlike sounds just as easily as through similar ones -- as in the lambda of "sklērotēs." But if that's true, then you've made a convention with yourself, and the correctness of names turns out to depend on convention, since letters that are unlike the thing can communicate just as effectively as letters that are similar -- if they're backed by custom and convention.

Even if you draw the sharpest possible distinction between custom and convention, you still have to admit that the meaning of words comes from custom, not just from resemblance. Custom can work through unlike sounds just as well as through similar ones.

And since we're agreed on this, Cratylus, then convention must contribute to how we communicate our thoughts. How could you ever find names resembling every individual number, for instance, unless you allowed convention some authority in determining the correctness of names?

I quite agree with you that words should resemble things as much as possible. But I'm afraid this "resemblance" requirement, as Hermogenes might say, is a somewhat fragile thing, and it needs the mechanical support of convention to work properly. In an ideal world, we'd always use perfectly fitting resemblances, and that would be the most perfect language. The opposite -- arbitrary signs with no resemblance -- would be the most imperfect.

But let me ask: what is the purpose of names? What are they for?

Cratylus: The purpose of names, Socrates, I'd say, is to inform. The simple truth is: whoever knows the names knows the things they express.

Socrates: So you're saying, Cratylus, that since the name and the thing are similar, knowing the name means knowing the thing? Because similar things fall under the same art or branch of knowledge. So whoever knows names will also know things.

Cratylus: That's precisely what I mean.

Socrates: But let's think about what kind of information names give us. Is it the best kind? Or is there another, better kind?

Cratylus: I believe names give us the only and best kind of information. There can be no other.

Socrates: But do you also think that the person who discovers the names thereby discovers the things? Or is learning through names just one method, and there might be another way to investigate and discover?

Cratylus: I believe investigation and discovery work the same way as instruction -- through names.

Socrates: But don't you see, Cratylus, that whoever follows names in searching for things, analyzing their meanings, is in great danger of being deceived?

Cratylus: How so?

Socrates: Because clearly, whoever first gave names did so according to their own understanding of the things. Right?

Cratylus: True.

Socrates: And if that understanding was wrong -- if they named things based on a mistaken conception -- where does that leave us, their followers? Won't we be led astray?

Cratylus: But Socrates, surely the original name-giver must have known what he was doing? Otherwise his names wouldn't really be names at all. And you have clear proof that he didn't miss the truth -- the proof is his perfect consistency. Didn't you notice, when you were going through all those etymologies, that the words all shared a common character and purpose?

Socrates: That's no answer at all, my friend. If the name-giver made a mistake at the start, he could easily have forced everything else into agreement with that initial error. There'd be nothing strange about that -- it's no different from what happens in geometry, where a small, invisible flaw in the first step leads to consistently wrong conclusions all the way down. That's why every person should devote their greatest thought and attention to first principles -- are they rightly established or not? Once you've examined those thoroughly, everything else follows.

Now, I'd be amazed to find that names really are consistent. Let's revisit our earlier discussion. Weren't we saying that everything is in motion, in progress, in flux, and that names express this idea of motion? Isn't that what they seemed to mean?

Cratylus: Yes, absolutely. That's their meaning and the true meaning.

Socrates: Let's look again. Take "epistēmē" (knowledge) -- it's ambiguous. It seems to suggest "stopping the soul at things" rather than "going along with them." (In the Greek, "epi-histēmē" could suggest "epi" -- at -- and "histanai" -- to stop -- rather than "hepesthai" -- to follow.) So maybe we should keep the beginning as is but insert an iota instead of an epsilon.

Or take "bebaion" (certainty/firmness) -- that clearly expresses standing still and position, not motion.

"Historia" (inquiry) bears on its face the "stopping" ("histanai") of the stream.

"Piston" (trustworthy) certainly indicates cessation of motion.

"Mnēmē" (memory) obviously expresses rest in the soul, not motion.

And words like "hamartia" (error) and "symphora" (calamity), when you look at their etymologies, turn out to mean the same thing as "synesis" (understanding) and "epistēmē" (knowledge) and other positive words! (In the Greek, "hamartia" can be analyzed as "hama artein" -- "going along with" -- similar to "syn-ienai" and "hep-esthai.") The same applies to "amathia" (ignorance) and "akolasia" (lack of self-control): "amathia" can be explained as "hē hama theō iontos poreia" -- "the journey of one who travels with god" -- and "akolasia" as "hē akolouthia tois pragmasin" -- "following along with things."

So the names with the worst meanings turn out to be built on the same principle as those with the best. And I'm sure that anyone who took the trouble could find many more examples where the name-giver indicates not motion and progress but rest -- the exact opposite.

Cratylus: Yes, Socrates, but notice: the great majority express motion.

Socrates: So what, Cratylus? Are we counting names like votes? Do we decide correctness by majority rule? Whichever type has the most names wins?

Cratylus: No, that wouldn't be reasonable.

Socrates: Certainly not. But let's leave that question and move on. Were we not agreeing earlier that the first name-givers -- whether Greek or non-Greek -- were legislators, and that the art of giving names was the legislator's art?

Cratylus: Quite true.

Socrates: Then tell me: did the first legislators, who gave the first names, know the things they named, or didn't they?

Cratylus: They must have known, Socrates.

Socrates: Yes, friend Cratylus, they could hardly have been ignorant.

Cratylus: I'd say not.

Socrates: Let's return to the point we left. You were saying that the person who gave names must have known the things he named. Are you still of that opinion?

Cratylus: I am.

Socrates: And you'd say that the giver of the FIRST names also had knowledge of the things he named?

Cratylus: I would.

Socrates: But how could he have learned or discovered things from names if the primary names hadn't been given yet? We just agreed that the only way to learn and discover things is either by discovering the names yourself or learning them from someone else.

Cratylus: I think there's a good deal in what you say, Socrates.

Socrates: But if things can only be known through names, how can we suppose that the original name-givers had knowledge -- or were even legislators at all -- before any names existed, and therefore before they could have known anything through names?

Cratylus: I think, Socrates, the true explanation is this: a power greater than human gave things their first names, and the names given that way are necessarily the true ones.

Socrates: But then how did this inspired name-giver -- or god -- manage to contradict himself? We just showed that some names express motion and others express rest. Was that a mistake?

Cratylus: Well, I suppose one set of those aren't really names at all.

Socrates: And which set did the god make -- the ones expressing motion, or the ones expressing rest? Because, as I said, this can't be settled by counting.

Cratylus: No, not that way, Socrates.

Socrates: But if we have a battle of names -- some claiming to represent the truth and others making the same claim -- how are we to decide between them? We can't appeal to still other names -- we'd need some standard that, without using names at all, shows which set is right. And that standard would have to reveal the truth of things directly.

Cratylus: I agree.

Socrates: But if that's true, Cratylus, then it seems that things can be known without names?

Cratylus: Clearly.

Socrates: And how would you expect to know them? What other way is there except the true and natural way -- through their connections with each other, through their kinship, and through themselves? Because anything other than them, anything different from them, would point to something other and different.

Cratylus: What you're saying seems true, I think.

Socrates: Now hold on. Haven't we agreed several times that names, when correctly given, are likenesses and images of the things they name?

Cratylus: Yes.

Socrates: Then suppose you can learn things through names to whatever degree you like. And suppose you can also learn them from the things themselves. Which method is likely to be nobler and clearer: learning from the image whether the image and the truth it represents have been rightly conceived? Or learning from the truth itself whether the truth and its image have been properly executed?

Cratylus: We must learn from the truth, I'd say.

Socrates: How real existence is to be studied and discovered is a question that's probably beyond both you and me. But we can at least admit this much: knowledge of things is not to be derived from names. Things must be studied and investigated in themselves.

Cratylus: Clearly, Socrates.

Socrates: There's one more point. I wouldn't want us to be misled by the sheer number of names all trending in the same direction. I don't deny that the original name-givers really did create names under the assumption that everything is in motion and flux. That was their sincere belief -- but I think they were mistaken. Having fallen into a kind of whirlpool themselves, they're spinning around and trying to drag us in after them.

There's something, Cratylus, that I often think about, and I'd like your opinion: tell me, do you believe there exists an absolute beauty or good, or any other absolute reality?

Cratylus: Yes, Socrates, I do believe so.

Socrates: Then let's focus on that -- true beauty itself. Not whether a particular face is attractive or anything like that, since all particular things seem to be in flux. Let's ask: isn't true beauty always beautiful?

Cratylus: Certainly.

Socrates: And can we really speak of a beauty that's always passing away, that's one thing and then another? Wouldn't it have changed and vanished while the word was still in our mouths?

Cratylus: Undoubtedly.

Socrates: Then how can something be real if it's never in the same state? Things that remain the same can't change while they remain the same. And if they're always the same, always in the same condition, never departing from their original form, they can never change or be moved.

Cratylus: They certainly can't.

Socrates: And they can't be known by anyone either -- at least not if they're always changing. Because at the very moment the observer approaches, they'd become something different and other, so you could never get any further in knowing their nature or condition. You can't know what has no stable condition.

Cratylus: True.

Socrates: And we can't reasonably say there's knowledge at all if everything is in transition and nothing abides. Knowledge, too, would have to stop being knowledge if it's constantly changing. But if the very nature of knowledge changes, at the moment of change there'd be no knowledge. And if the transition never stops, there'd always be no knowledge -- no one to know and nothing to be known.

But if the knower and the known DO exist permanently, and beauty and goodness and every other thing also exist in this way, then they can't resemble a process or a flow, as we were just supposing. Whether there is this eternal nature in things, or whether Heraclitus and his many followers are right about universal flux, is a difficult question to settle. And no sensible person will put themselves or the education of their mind in the power of names. They won't trust names or name-givers to the point of confidently declaring that they have knowledge -- knowledge that condemns both themselves and everything else to an unhealthy unreality, where everything leaks like a cracked pot, or the world is like a person with a permanently runny nose.

This might be true, Cratylus. But it might also be very much untrue. So don't be too easily convinced of it. Think it over carefully, like a man, and don't accept such a doctrine without serious examination. You're young -- this is the age for learning. And when you've found the truth, come back and tell me.

Cratylus: I'll do as you say. But I can assure you, Socrates, that I've already been thinking about this for a long time. And after a great deal of reflection and effort, I lean toward Heraclitus.

Socrates: Well then, another day when you come back, you can teach me. But for now, go ahead to the country, as you're planning. And Hermogenes here will see you on your way.

Cratylus: Very good, Socrates. But I hope you'll keep thinking about these things yourself.

THEAETETUS (Part 1)

Persons of the dialogue:
  Socrates
  Theodorus
  Theaetetus

Euclid and Terpsion meet in front of Euclid's house in Megara. They go inside, and a servant reads the dialogue aloud to them.

Euclid: Terpsion! Did you just get in from the country?

Terpsion: No, I've been here a while. Actually, I was looking for you in the marketplace and couldn't figure out where you'd gone.

Euclid: That's because I wasn't in the city.

Terpsion: Where were you, then?

Euclid: On my way down to the harbor, I ran into Theaetetus -- he was being carried up to Athens from the army camp at Corinth.

Terpsion: Alive or dead?

Euclid: Barely alive. He's badly wounded, but what's really destroying him is the sickness that's spread through the army.

Terpsion: The dysentery, you mean?

Euclid: Yes.

Terpsion: What a loss that man will be!

Euclid: A terrible one, Terpsion. He's a noble man. Just today I heard people praising the way he conducted himself in the battle.

Terpsion: That doesn't surprise me at all -- I'd be more surprised to hear anything else about him. But why didn't he stop here in Megara?

Euclid: He wanted to get home. I begged him to stay, I gave him every reason to, but he wouldn't listen. So I walked with him part of the way, and as I was heading back, I found myself remembering what Socrates once said about him -- and marveling at how exactly the prediction came true, the way his predictions always did. I believe it was shortly before Socrates' own death that he met Theaetetus, who was just a teenager then. They had a remarkable conversation, and Socrates repeated it to me when I next came to Athens. He was full of admiration for the boy's mind, and he said that if Theaetetus lived, he was certain to become a great man.

Terpsion: Well, the prophecy certainly came true. But what was the conversation? Can you tell it to me?

Euclid: Not from memory, no. But here's the thing: I took notes as soon as I got home that day, then filled them out from memory when I had time. And whenever I went to Athens, I'd ask Socrates about any details I'd forgotten and make corrections when I got back. So I have nearly the whole conversation written down.

Terpsion: That's right -- you told me about this once! I've been meaning to ask you to show me the manuscript ever since, and I've never gotten around to it. Why don't we read it now? I've just come in from the country, and I could badly use a rest.

Euclid: I could use one too -- I walked with Theaetetus all the way to Erineum. Let's go in, and the servant can read to us while we put our feet up.

Terpsion: Perfect.

Euclid: Here's the scroll, Terpsion. One note: I wrote it with Socrates actually talking to the people in the conversation -- Theodorus the geometer from Cyrene, and Theaetetus -- rather than narrating it to me secondhand. I cut out all the connecting phrases, the "I said" and "I remarked" when Socrates spoke about himself, and the "he agreed" or "he disagreed" for the answers. They get tiresome when they're repeated over and over.

Terpsion: Good call, Euclid.

Euclid: Now, boy, take the scroll and read.

Euclid's servant reads.

Socrates: If I cared more about Cyrene, Theodorus, I'd ask you about the scene there -- whether any promising young geometers or philosophers are coming up in that part of the world. But honestly, I'm more interested in our own Athenian youth. I want to know which of them show real promise. I keep an eye out myself, as much as I can, and I ask around about whose company the young men seek. Plenty of them flock to you -- and rightly so, given your eminence in geometry and everything else. So tell me: have you come across anyone worth watching?

Theodorus: I have, Socrates. There's one Athenian boy I've gotten to know who is truly remarkable, and you should absolutely meet him. Now, if he were beautiful, I'd hesitate to praise him this much -- someone might think I was in love with him. But he's no beauty, so I can speak freely. In fact -- don't take this the wrong way -- he looks like you: snub nose, bulging eyes, though both features are less pronounced in him than in you. So with that disclaimer out of the way, let me say this: of all the young people I've ever met -- and I've met a great many -- I've never known anyone with natural gifts like his. He picks things up faster than almost anyone alive, yet he's extraordinarily gentle, and on top of that he's as brave as they come. I've never seen that combination in anyone else, and I wouldn't have believed it could exist. Usually the quick, sharp, retentive minds also come with hair-trigger tempers -- they're like ships without ballast, darting around, more manic than courageous. And the steady, calm types tend to turn sluggish when they face real study, and can't remember anything. But this boy moves through learning and investigation so surely, so smoothly, so effectively -- all with perfect gentleness, like a river of oil flowing without a sound. It's astonishing in someone his age.

Socrates: That is good news. Whose son is he?

Theodorus: I've forgotten the father's name, but the boy himself is the one in the middle of that group coming toward us. He and his friends have been oiling up in the outer courtyard -- looks like they've just finished. See if you recognize him.

Socrates: I know him, though I don't know his name. He's the son of Euphronius of Sunium -- a man who was, by all accounts, exactly the kind of person you're describing in his son. A distinguished man. I believe he left a sizable fortune.

Theodorus: Theaetetus, Socrates -- that's his name. As for the fortune, I think the trustees managed to make it disappear. And yet he's wonderfully generous with money anyway.

Socrates: He sounds like a fine person. Tell him to come sit with me.

Theodorus: I will. Theaetetus! Come over here and sit by Socrates.

Socrates: Yes, do, Theaetetus -- so I can study my own reflection in your face. Theodorus says we look alike. But think about this: if each of us were holding a lyre, and he claimed the two were tuned alike, would we just take his word for it? Or would we first ask whether he was a musician?

Theaetetus: We'd ask.

Socrates: And if he turned out to be one, we'd believe him; if not, we wouldn't?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: So if we cared at all about this supposed resemblance between our faces, we'd want to know whether the man making the claim is a painter or not.

Theaetetus: Definitely.

Socrates: Well -- is Theodorus a painter?

Theaetetus: Not that I've ever heard.

Socrates: Is he a geometer?

Theaetetus: Of course he is, Socrates.

Socrates: And an astronomer, a mathematician, a musician -- an educated man across the board?

Theaetetus: I think so.

Socrates: Then if he comments on our physical resemblance -- whether as a compliment or an insult -- there's no particular reason to take him seriously.

Theaetetus: I suppose not.

Socrates: But if he praises the virtue or wisdom in either of our minds? Then anyone who hears that praise will naturally want to examine the person being praised -- and the person being praised should be willing to show what he's got.

Theaetetus: Very true, Socrates.

Socrates: Then now's the moment, my dear Theaetetus -- time for me to examine and for you to show what you've got. Because believe me, Theodorus has praised plenty of people to me, citizens and foreigners alike, but he has never praised anyone the way he just praised you.

Theaetetus: That's nice to hear, Socrates. But what if he was joking?

Socrates: No, that's not Theodorus' style. And don't think you can wriggle out of your agreement by pretending he might have been kidding. If you try it, he'll have to testify under oath -- and nobody is going to accuse him of perjury. So be brave and stand by your word.

Theaetetus: All right. If that's what you want, I will.

Socrates: First things first: tell me what you study with Theodorus. Some geometry, I assume?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And astronomy, harmony, calculation?

Theaetetus: I do my best.

Socrates: So do I, my boy, so do I -- I'm eager to learn from him, or from anyone who understands these things. And I manage well enough overall. But there's one small difficulty nagging at me, and I'd like you and the others here to help me investigate it. Answer me this: learning means becoming wiser about whatever it is you're learning, doesn't it?

Theaetetus: Of course.

Socrates: And it's wisdom that makes the wise wise?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And is that any different from knowledge?

Theaetetus: Is what different?

Socrates: Wisdom. Aren't people wise about exactly the things they know?

Theaetetus: Sure.

Socrates: So knowledge and wisdom are the same thing?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And right there is the difficulty I can never settle to my own satisfaction: What is knowledge? Can we answer that? What do you all say? Who wants to go first? Whoever misses sits out and gets to be "donkey," as the boys say in their ball game -- and whoever survives every round without missing is crowned king and gets to ask the rest of us any questions he likes... Why so quiet? Theodorus, I hope my love of conversation isn't making me rude. I only want to get us talking, like the friendly, sociable people we are.

Theodorus: Rude? The opposite, Socrates. But do ask one of the young ones instead. The truth is, I'm not used to your style of question-and-answer, and I'm too old to get used to it now. The young men are better suited -- and they'll improve more than I would, since the young improve at everything. You've already started on Theaetetus, so my advice is: keep going, and don't let him off the hook.

Socrates: You hear that, Theaetetus? Theodorus says so -- and you're not going to disobey him. When a wise man gives orders, a young man obeys. So take courage, and tell me, nobly and honestly: what do you think knowledge is?

Theaetetus: Well, Socrates, since the two of you insist, I'll answer. And if I get it wrong, you'll correct me, I'm sure.

Socrates: We will -- if we can.

Theaetetus: All right then. I think the subjects I study with Theodorus -- geometry and the others you listed just now -- are knowledge. And I'd add the craft of the shoemaker and the other craftsmen too. Each and every one of those is knowledge.

Socrates: Theaetetus, that's too much! Generous and openhanded as you are, I ask you for one simple thing and you hand me a whole assortment.

Theaetetus: What do you mean, Socrates?

Socrates: Maybe nothing. But let me try to explain what I think I mean. When you say "shoemaking," you mean the craft of making shoes, right?

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Socrates: And when you say "carpentry," you mean the craft of making things out of wood?

Theaetetus: I do.

Socrates: So in both cases, you're defining what each craft is about?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: But that wasn't my question, Theaetetus. I didn't ask what the various crafts are about, or how many of them there are -- we weren't taking a head count. I asked what knowledge itself is. Am I making sense?

Theaetetus: Perfectly.

Socrates: Look at it this way. Suppose someone asked us about something completely ordinary and trivial -- say, "What is clay?" -- and we answered: "Well, there's potters' clay, and oven-makers' clay, and brickmakers' clay." Wouldn't that be a ridiculous answer?

Theaetetus: It would.

Socrates: Ridiculous first of all because we'd be assuming the questioner understands what clay is just because we tacked on "of the figurine-makers" or "of" some other craftsmen. How can anyone understand the name of a thing when he doesn't know what the thing is?

Theaetetus: He can't.

Socrates: Then someone who doesn't know what knowledge is can't understand "knowledge of shoemaking" either.

Theaetetus: No.

Socrates: Nor "knowledge of" anything else.

Theaetetus: Right.

Socrates: So when someone is asked "What is knowledge?" it's ridiculous to answer with the name of some particular craft. The question was "What is knowledge?" -- and he answers, "Knowledge of this" or "knowledge of that."

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: Besides, he could have given a short, simple answer, but instead he goes the long way around. Take the clay question: he could simply have said, "Clay is earth mixed with water" -- and never mind whose clay it is.

Theaetetus: Now that you put it that way, Socrates, it seems easy. In fact, I think you're asking the same kind of question that came up recently when I was talking with your namesake -- my friend Socrates here.

Socrates: What question was that, Theaetetus?

Theaetetus: Theodorus was drawing diagrams for us about square roots -- showing that the roots of three and five aren't measurable by the unit -- and he took the cases one by one, up to the root of seventeen, where he stopped for some reason. Since there are infinitely many roots, it occurred to us to try to gather them all under a single name or class.

Socrates: And did you find one?

Theaetetus: I think we did. But see what you think.

Socrates: Go on.

Theaetetus: We divided all numbers into two classes. The numbers you can make by multiplying equal factors -- like four, which is two times two -- we pictured as squares, and called them square or equilateral numbers. That was one class.

Socrates: Very good.

Theaetetus: The numbers in between -- like three and five, and any other number you can't make from equal factors, only from a larger number times a smaller one, so that the figure they form has unequal sides -- we pictured as rectangles and called them oblong numbers.

Socrates: Excellent. And then what?

Theaetetus: Every line that forms the side of a square number we called a length. And every line that forms the side of an oblong number we called a root, or power -- because while those lines aren't measurable by the lengths themselves, the squares built on them are. And we did something similar for solids.

Socrates: Boys, that's superb. I don't think Theodorus is in any danger of a perjury conviction.

Theaetetus: But Socrates, I can't answer your question about knowledge the way I answered the one about roots -- and that's clearly what you want. So maybe Theodorus is a fraud after all.

Socrates: Hold on. Suppose someone praised your running and said he'd never met a boy your equal -- and then you lost a race to a grown man in his prime, a champion sprinter. Would the praise be any less true?

Theaetetus: No.

Socrates: And do you think figuring out what knowledge is, is a small matter, like I suggested a moment ago? Isn't it the kind of problem that would strain the very best minds anywhere?

Theaetetus: By heaven, the very best of the best!

Socrates: Then relax. Don't decide that Theodorus was wrong about you. Just give everything you've got to finding out the truth about knowledge -- and about everything else, while you're at it.

Theaetetus: If effort can get us to the truth, Socrates, I'm willing.

Socrates: Come on, then -- you just showed us how it's done. Take your answer about the roots as your model. Just as you gathered all those many roots into one class, try to gather the many kinds of knowledge into one definition.

Theaetetus: I have to tell you, Socrates, I've tried this many times -- whenever reports of your questions reached me. But I can never convince myself that I have a good answer, and I've never heard anyone else give the kind of answer you're looking for. And yet I can't stop worrying at it.

Socrates: Ah -- those are labor pains, my dear Theaetetus. You're not empty. You're pregnant with something, and it's trying to be born.

Theaetetus: I don't know about that, Socrates. I'm just telling you how it feels.

Socrates: You ridiculous boy -- have you never heard that I'm the son of a midwife? A fine, sturdy one named Phaenarete?

Theaetetus: I've heard that, yes.

Socrates: And have you heard that I practice midwifery myself?

Theaetetus: No. Never.

Socrates: Well, I do, my friend -- though keep it to yourself, because the world hasn't found me out. People only say that I'm the strangest of men, and that I drive everyone I talk to into total confusion. Have you heard that one?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: Shall I tell you the reason?

Theaetetus: Please.

Socrates: Then think about how midwifery actually works, and you'll see what I mean. You probably know that no woman who can still conceive and bear children works as a midwife -- only women who are past childbearing age.

Theaetetus: Yes, I know.

Socrates: They say the reason is Artemis, the goddess of childbirth, who is herself childless. She honors women who resemble her. But she couldn't let completely barren women be midwives, because human beings can't master a skill they have no experience of. So she gave the job to women who have borne children but are now too old to bear more.

Theaetetus: Makes sense.

Socrates: And here's something else that makes sense -- in fact I'm certain of it: midwives can tell, better than anyone, who is pregnant and who isn't.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Socrates: And with their drugs and incantations they can trigger labor pains or soothe them, at will. They can bring on delivery for women who are struggling, and, if they judge it best, they can end a pregnancy in its early stage.

Theaetetus: They can.

Socrates: Have you also noticed that they're shrewd matchmakers -- absolute experts at knowing which pairings will produce the best children?

Theaetetus: No, I never knew that.

Socrates: Well, let me tell you, they're prouder of that than of cutting the umbilical cord. Think about it: the same skill that knows how to grow and harvest crops must also know which soil suits which plant or seed. It's one art, not two.

Theaetetus: Yes, the same art.

Socrates: And you don't suppose it's any different with women?

Theaetetus: Probably not.

Socrates: It isn't. But midwives are respectable women with reputations to protect, so they steer clear of matchmaking. They're afraid of being called procuresses -- the name for people who pair off men and women in shady, unskilled ways. And yet the true midwife really is the one true matchmaker.

Theaetetus: Clearly.

Socrates: So that's what midwives do -- important work, but not as important as mine. Here's why. Women don't give birth to real babies one day and counterfeit babies the next, fakes so convincing you can hardly tell the difference. If they did, then telling true births from false ones would be the crowning skill of the midwife's art. Don't you think?

Theaetetus: I do.

Socrates: Well, my art of midwifery is just like theirs -- with a few differences. I attend men, not women. And I watch over souls in labor, not bodies. The supreme moment of my art is this: testing, by every means I have, whether the idea a young mind delivers is a false phantom or something alive, genuine, and true. And there's one more way I'm like the midwives: I'm barren. I can't give birth to wisdom myself. The accusation people have thrown at me for years -- that I ask everyone else questions but never answer any myself, because I have no wisdom in me -- is absolutely true. And here's the reason: the god compels me to deliver others, but has forbidden me to give birth. So no, I'm not wise at all, and I have no discovery to show for myself, no brainchild of my own soul. But the people who spend time with me? Something different happens to them. Some seem hopelessly slow at first; but as we keep at it, all of them -- all the ones the god favors -- make progress that astonishes everyone, themselves most of all. And here's the thing: it's obvious they never learned anything from me. The many beautiful discoveries they bring forth came entirely from inside themselves. The delivery, though -- that was the god's work, and mine.

Here's the proof. Plenty of them didn't realize this. Giving themselves all the credit and despising me, they left me sooner than they should have -- some on their own conceit, some because others lured them away. And after they left, what happened? They miscarried everything that was still in them, and the ideas I had already delivered they ruined through bad nurture -- because they cared more for falsehood and shams than for truth. In the end they looked like complete fools, to themselves and everyone else. Aristeides, son of Lysimachus, is one of them; there are plenty more. And when the runaways come back, begging to be taken in again -- ready to get down on their knees, practically -- then, if my inner voice allows it (and it doesn't always), I take them back, and they begin to grow again.

One more thing: the people who keep my company suffer something the women in labor suffer too, except worse. They feel labor pains -- they're filled, day and night, with confusion and aching perplexity far beyond anything the women endure. And my art can both stir those pangs up and soothe them. So much for them. And then there are others, Theaetetus, who come to me carrying nothing at all. Since I know my art is useless to them, I kindly arrange a match -- and with the god's help I'm pretty good at guessing whose company will do them good. I've matched many of them with Prodicus, and many with other wise and inspired men.

Now, why have I told you this whole long story, my friend? Because I suspect -- and you suspect it yourself -- that you're in labor. You're pregnant with something. So come to me, the midwife's son and a midwife myself, and when I ask my questions, do your very best to answer them. And if, when I examine one of your statements, I conclude it's a phantom and not a true birth, and I gently take it from you and discard it -- don't rage at me the way first-time mothers do over their babies. People before you, my friend, have gotten so furious with me they were literally ready to bite, just because I removed some cherished nonsense of theirs. They never grasped that I do it out of pure goodwill. They have no idea that no god ever wishes evil to man, and that I don't act out of malice either. I simply cannot accept a falsehood and bury a truth.

So -- back to the beginning, Theaetetus. What is knowledge? Try to tell me. And never say you can't. If the god is willing and you take courage, you can.

Theaetetus: Well, Socrates, after a pep talk like that, I'd be ashamed not to give it everything I have. All right. It seems to me that someone who knows something perceives the thing he knows. So, as it looks to me right now: knowledge is nothing other than perception.

Socrates: Good -- bold and direct! That's exactly how you should speak your mind. Now let's examine this child of yours together and find out whether it's a real birth or just a wind-egg. Knowledge is perception, you say?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And in fact you've just delivered no ordinary theory of knowledge -- it's the very one Protagoras used to teach, though he put it differently. He said that man is the measure of all things: of things that are, that they are; and of things that are not, that they are not. You've read him, I assume?

Theaetetus: Oh yes -- many times.

Socrates: And doesn't he mean something like this: things are, for you, the way they appear to you, and they are, for me, the way they appear to me -- you and I each being a man?

Theaetetus: Yes, that's what he says.

Socrates: Well, a wise man doesn't usually talk nonsense, so let's try to follow him. Sometimes the same wind is blowing, and yet one of us feels chilly and the other doesn't -- or one feels slightly cool and the other freezing. True?

Theaetetus: Quite true.

Socrates: So in that moment, is the wind itself, taken on its own, cold or not cold? Or should we side with Protagoras and say it's cold for the one who feels cold, and not for the one who doesn't?

Theaetetus: The second, I suppose.

Socrates: And it appears that way to each of them?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And "appears to him" means the same as "he perceives it."

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: So appearing and perceiving are the same thing, in matters of hot, cold, and everything like them. The way each person perceives things is presumably the way they are -- for that person.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: Then perception is always perception of what is -- and since it's the same as knowledge, it never makes a mistake.

Theaetetus: Apparently.

Socrates: In the name of the Graces! Protagoras must have been an almighty genius. He tossed out this saying as a riddle for the common herd -- people like you and me -- while telling the real Truth (that was actually the title of his book) in secret to his own students.

Theaetetus: What do you mean by that, Socrates?

Socrates: I'm about to lay out a serious doctrine -- one that says nothing is anything in itself; everything is relative. You can't correctly call anything by any name -- "big," "small," "heavy," "light" -- because the big will turn out small and the heavy light. Nothing is any one thing or any one quality. The truth is that everything comes to be -- through motion, change, and mixture with everything else -- always relative to something. We say things "are," but that's sloppy talk: nothing ever is. Everything is always becoming. And on this point, line up all the philosophers one after another -- Protagoras, Heraclitus, Empedocles, the lot -- and they'll all agree, with the single exception of Parmenides. Line up the great poets of both genres too: Epicharmus, the king of comedy, and Homer, the king of tragedy. When Homer sings of

"Ocean, source of the gods, and mother Tethys,"

doesn't he mean that all things are born of flux and motion?

Theaetetus: I think so.

Socrates: And who would dare take up arms against an army that size, with Homer as its general, and not look ridiculous?

Theaetetus: Who indeed, Socrates?

Socrates: Exactly, Theaetetus. And there's plenty more evidence that motion is the source of everything we call being and becoming, while stillness is the source of non-being and destruction. Fire and warmth -- supposedly the parent and guardian of everything else -- are themselves born from movement and friction, and friction is a kind of motion. Isn't that where fire comes from?

Theaetetus: It is.

Socrates: And every animal is generated the same way -- from motion?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: And what about the body? Doesn't rest and laziness ruin its condition, while motion and exercise preserve it for years?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: And the mind? Isn't the soul taught, improved, and maintained by study and practice -- which are motions -- while at rest (which for the soul just means not studying, not paying attention) it learns nothing and quickly forgets what it knew?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: So motion is good for the soul as well as the body, and rest is bad for both?

Theaetetus: Apparently.

Socrates: I could go further: dead calms and windless stillness rot and decay things, while wind and storm keep them fresh. And the crown jewel of the whole argument -- I insist on this one -- is Homer's golden chain. By it he means nothing other than the sun, and his point is this: as long as the sun and the heavens keep circling in their orbits, everything in heaven and earth exists and is preserved. But if they were chained in place and their motion stopped, everything would be destroyed and, as the saying goes, turned upside down.

Theaetetus: I think you've explained his meaning exactly, Socrates.

Socrates: Then apply the doctrine to perception, my friend -- start with vision. What you call "white color" isn't a thing in your eyes, and it isn't some separate thing existing outside them either. Don't assign it any location at all -- because if it had a fixed position, it would just be, sitting still, and there'd be no becoming.

Theaetetus: Then what is color?

Socrates: Follow the principle we just laid down -- that nothing exists on its own -- and you'll see it: white, black, and every other color arise from the eye meeting the right kind of motion. What we call a color is neither the thing striking the eye nor the eye being struck, but something that passes between them -- and it's private to each perceiver. Tell me: would you swear that every color appears to a dog, or to any other animal, exactly the way it appears to you?

Theaetetus: Definitely not.

Socrates: And would you swear that anything appears the same to you as it does to another person? Are you really so sure of even that? Wouldn't it be closer to the truth to say it never appears exactly the same even to you -- because you are never exactly the same?

Theaetetus: The latter, I think.

Socrates: Now, if the thing we measure ourselves against, or the thing we touch, really were big or white or hot in itself, then bumping into something different couldn't change it -- unless the thing itself actually changed. And on the other side, if the perceiver were really big or white or hot in himself, then he wouldn't become different when something different approached him or happened to him -- unless something in him changed. Yet in everyday speech, we casually say things that drive us straight into the most absurd contradictions -- as Protagoras and anyone arguing his line would happily point out.

Theaetetus: What kind of contradictions? What do you mean?

Socrates: A small example will make it clear. Take six dice. Set four next to them, and we say the six are more -- one-and-a-half times as many. Set twelve next to them, and we say the six are fewer -- only half as many. The same six dice: more and fewer at once. Can you or anyone deny it?

Theaetetus: No, that's right.

Socrates: Well then, suppose Protagoras or someone else asks you: "Theaetetus, can anything become bigger or more in any way other than by increasing?" What's your answer?

Theaetetus: If I answer what I really think about this last question, Socrates, I'd say no. But if I keep my earlier answer in mind, I'd say yes -- to avoid contradicting myself.

Socrates: Wonderful! Spoken like an oracle, my boy! And notice: if you answer "yes," it'll be a scene straight out of Euripides -- the tongue convinced, but not the mind.

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: Now, if you and I were professional debaters -- those thoroughbred Sophists who know everything about the mind and argue purely from a surplus of cleverness -- we'd have had a regular sparring match over this by now, banging our arguments together for sport. But we're amateurs. All we want is to see what these ideas actually are and whether they're consistent with one another or not.

Theaetetus: Yes, that's exactly what I'd want.

Socrates: Me too. And since we feel that way, and there's no rush, why don't we calmly and patiently go back over our own thoughts and examine what these appearances in us really are? If I'm not mistaken, we'll start by laying out three claims. First: nothing can become bigger or smaller, in size or in number, as long as it stays equal to itself. Agreed?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: Second: a thing to which nothing is added and from which nothing is taken away neither grows nor shrinks — it stays equal.

Theaetetus: Quite true.

Socrates: Third: what wasn't there before can't exist later without having come into being — without becoming.

Theaetetus: Yes, definitely.

Socrates: Now, these three principles, if I'm not mistaken, go to war with each other in our minds when we think about the dice — or take this example. Suppose I'm a certain height, taller than you. Within a year, without growing or shrinking at all, I might be shorter than you — not because I lost any height, but because you grew. So I am, later on, something I wasn't before — and yet I never became it. But that's impossible: nothing can come to be without becoming, and I couldn't have become shorter without losing some height. I could give you ten thousand examples of contradictions like this, if we accept them at all. I think you're following me, Theaetetus — I suspect you've puzzled over questions like these before.

Theaetetus: Yes, Socrates, and I'm amazed when I think about them — by the gods, I am! I want to know what on earth they mean. Sometimes my head literally swims just contemplating them.

Socrates: I see, my dear Theaetetus, that Theodorus read your nature correctly when he called you a philosopher. Wonder is the philosopher's signature feeling — philosophy begins in wonder. Whoever said that Iris, the messenger of heaven, is the daughter of Thaumas — Wonder — wasn't a bad genealogist. But are you starting to see how the theory we're attributing to Protagoras explains all this perplexity?

Theaetetus: Not yet.

Socrates: Then you'll owe me one if I help you dig up the hidden "truth" of a famous man — or rather, a famous school of thought.

Theaetetus: Of course I'll owe you. Very much so.

Socrates: Then take a look around and make sure none of the uninitiated are eavesdropping. By "the uninitiated" I mean people who believe in nothing except what they can grab with their two hands — people who refuse to accept that actions or processes or anything invisible could possibly be real.

Theaetetus: Yes, Socrates — hard, stubborn characters, those people.

Socrates: Complete barbarians, my boy. The initiates whose mysteries I'm about to reveal to you are far more sophisticated. Here's their first principle: everything is motion. There is nothing but motion, and it comes in two forms — one active, one passive — both in endless variety. From their union and friction comes an endless brood of offspring, also in two forms: the senses, and the objects of sense, which are constantly bursting forth and being born at the very same moment. The senses have names — hearing, sight, smell, the sense of heat and cold, pleasure, pain, desire, fear, and many more — plus countless others with no names at all. And each sense has its matching object: every variety of color has a corresponding variety of seeing, every sound a corresponding hearing, and so on for all the senses and their kindred objects. Do you see, Theaetetus, what this story has to do with our earlier argument?

Theaetetus: Honestly, no.

Socrates: Then pay attention, and I'll try to finish the tale. The point is this: everything is in motion, as I said, and the motion comes in two speeds, slower and quicker. The slow elements move in place, in relation to whatever is near them — and that's how they generate offspring. But the offspring are faster: they travel, moving from place to place. Now apply this to perception. When an eye and a suitable object come together, they give birth to whiteness, along with the sensation that's twinned with it — a birth that never would have happened if either of them had wandered off elsewhere. As sight flows from the eye and whiteness streams from the object, the two combine to produce the color. The eye is filled with seeing — at that moment it really does see, and it becomes not sight itself but a seeing eye. And the object that partnered in producing the color is filled with whiteness, and becomes not whiteness itself but a white thing — white wood, white stone, whatever the thing happens to be.

And the same goes for everything we perceive — hard, warm, all of it. None of it has any independent existence on its own, just as we said before. Everything, of every kind, is generated by motion in the encounter between things. As for "agent" and "patient" — the thing acting and the thing acted on — you can't form any reliable conception of either one existing on its own. There is no agent until it pairs with a patient, and no patient until it pairs with an agent. And what acts as agent in one encounter turns up as patient in another.

The upshot of all this, as I said at the start, is that nothing exists by itself, in its own right. Everything is always becoming, always in relation to something else. "Being" should be struck from our vocabulary entirely — although, out of habit and ignorance, we've kept using the word even in this very discussion. But the great philosophers tell us we mustn't allow even words like "something" or "somebody's" or "mine" or "this" or "that," or any other word that pins a thing down. In the language of nature, things are forever being created and destroyed, coming into being and passing into new forms. No name can fix or hold them — and anyone who tries to nail them down is easily refuted. And we should talk this way not just about individual things but about collections of them — the kinds of bundles we name "man" or "stone" or any animal or class of things.

Well, Theaetetus, aren't these speculations sweet as honey? Don't you like the taste of them?

Theaetetus: I don't know what to say, Socrates. Honestly, I can't even tell whether you're giving your own opinion or just testing me.

Socrates: You're forgetting, my friend, that I don't know any of this, and I don't claim to. None of these ideas are mine. You're the one in labor; I'm just the midwife — barren myself. That's why I'm soothing you, offering you one delicacy after another for you to taste. I'm hoping that in the end I can help bring your own opinion out into the light of day. And once it's born, we'll examine it and decide whether what you've delivered is a real, living child or just a wind-egg — an empty shell. So keep your courage up, and answer like a man: what do you think?

Theaetetus: Ask away.

Socrates: Then tell me once more: is it your view that nothing is, but everything is always becoming — the good and the beautiful included, along with everything else we just listed?

Theaetetus: When I hear you lay it out like that, it seems to make a great deal of sense, and I'm very much inclined to accept it.

Socrates: Then let's not leave the argument unfinished, because there's still an objection to deal with — about dreams and diseases, especially madness, and all the illusions of hearing and sight and the other senses. In every one of these cases, the theory that "to be is to be perceived" looks flatly refuted. In dreams and hallucinations our perceptions are clearly false. Far from saying that whatever appears to someone is, we'd be tempted to say the opposite: nothing that appears is.

Theaetetus: Very true, Socrates.

Socrates: Then how, my boy, can anyone keep claiming that knowledge is perception, or that whatever appears to each person is, for that person?

Theaetetus: I'm afraid to say I have no answer, Socrates, since you scolded me just now for saying exactly that. But I really can't argue that madmen and dreamers believe truly — not when some of them imagine they're gods, and others dream they have wings and are flying through the air.

Socrates: Do you see yet another puzzle hiding in this, especially in the question of dreaming versus waking?

Theaetetus: What puzzle?

Socrates: One I'm sure you've heard people raise: what proof could you give if someone asked us, right now, this very moment, whether we're asleep and dreaming all our thoughts, or awake and actually talking to each other?

Theaetetus: Honestly, Socrates, I don't see how you'd prove one any more than the other — the two states match each other point for point. There's nothing to stop us from supposing this entire conversation is something we're dreaming. And when we dream that we're telling each other our dreams, the resemblance between the two states is downright uncanny.

Socrates: So you see, doubt about the reality of perception is easy to raise — there's even doubt about whether we're awake or dreaming. And since our time is split equally between sleeping and waking, in each state the soul insists that whatever it's thinking at the moment is the truth. Half our lives we swear by the one set of perceptions, half our lives by the other — and we're equally confident both times.

Theaetetus: Absolutely true.

Socrates: And doesn't the same go for madness and the other disorders? The only difference is that the time isn't split equally.

Theaetetus: Right.

Socrates: And surely truth and falsehood aren't going to be decided by a stopwatch — by how long a state lasts?

Theaetetus: That would be ridiculous in all kinds of ways.

Socrates: But do you have any other way of proving for certain which of these opinions is true?

Theaetetus: I don't think I do.

Socrates: Then listen to what the champions of appearance would say on the other side of the argument. As I imagine it, they'd argue like this: can something that is wholly other than another thing have any of the same properties as the thing it differs from? And notice, Theaetetus — by "other" we don't mean partly different. We mean wholly other.

Theaetetus: Put that way, then no — what is wholly other can't be the same in any respect or any sense.

Socrates: So it must also be admitted to be unlike?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: So if a thing happens to become like or unlike anything — itself or something else — we say it's the same when it becomes like, and other when it becomes unlike?

Theaetetus: Right.

Socrates: And weren't we saying earlier that there are many agents — infinitely many — and many patients, infinitely many of those too?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And that different pairings produce different results, not the same ones?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: Then let's use you and me as the example — or anything, really. Take Socrates healthy and Socrates sick. Are those two alike, or unlike?

Theaetetus: You mean Socrates-when-sick taken as a whole, compared with Socrates-when-healthy as a whole?

Socrates: Exactly — you've got my meaning.

Theaetetus: Then my answer is: unlike.

Socrates: And if unlike, then other?

Theaetetus: Right.

Socrates: And you'd say the same about Socrates asleep versus awake, or in any of the other states we mentioned?

Theaetetus: I would.

Socrates: So anything that acts on Socrates is dealing with a different patient depending on whether I'm well or ill.

Theaetetus: Of course.

Socrates: And in each of the two cases, I as the patient and the thing acting on me will produce a different result together?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: Now, the wine I drink when I'm healthy seems sweet and pleasant to me?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: Because — as we already agreed — the agent and patient meet and generate sweetness plus the perception of sweetness, both in motion at once. The perception, coming from the patient's side, makes my tongue a perceiving tongue; the sweetness, arising on the wine's side and swirling around it, makes the wine both be sweet and appear sweet to a healthy tongue.

Theaetetus: Right — that's exactly what we agreed.

Socrates: But when I'm sick, the wine is really acting on a different person, isn't it?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: So the pairing of the gulp of wine with the sick Socrates produces an entirely different result: a sensation of bitterness on the tongue, and bitterness arising and moving about the wine — which becomes not bitterness itself but something bitter, just as I become not perception itself but something perceiving.

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: And I'll never get this exact perception from any other object — a different object would generate a different perception and make the perceiver different too. Nor can the thing acting on me ever produce the same result by meeting some other person and stay the same itself — from a different subject it will produce a different result, and become different in the process.

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: Nor can I have this sensation all by myself, nor the object have this quality all by itself.

Theaetetus: No, certainly not.

Socrates: When I perceive, I have to be perceiving something — there's no such thing as perceiving and perceiving nothing. And the object, whether it becomes sweet or bitter or anything else, has to become that for some perceiver — nothing can become sweet without being sweet to someone.

Theaetetus: Absolutely.

Socrates: Then the conclusion is that we — the agent and the patient — exist, or rather become, only in relation to each other. Necessity binds us together, but binds each of us to the other and to nothing else — not even to ourselves. So we can only be bound to each other. Which means that whenever someone says a thing "is" or "becomes," they must say it is or becomes for someone, or of something, or in relation to something. They must never say — or let anyone else say — that anything is or becomes absolutely, all on its own. That's where our argument lands.

Theaetetus: Very true, Socrates.

Socrates: So since whatever acts on me is related to me and to no one else, I and no one else am the one who perceives it?

Theaetetus: Of course.

Socrates: Then my perception is true for me — because it's always a perception of my own reality. And, just as Protagoras says, I am the judge of what is for me, and of what is not for me.

Theaetetus: So it seems.

Socrates: Then if I'm never wrong, and my mind never stumbles in its grasp of what is or what becomes, how could I fail to know what I perceive?

Theaetetus: You couldn't.

Socrates: So you were exactly right to say that knowledge is nothing but perception. And it all turns out to be the same theory — whether you say with Homer and Heraclitus and that whole crowd that everything is motion and flux, or with the great sage Protagoras that man is the measure of all things, or with Theaetetus that, given all this, perception is knowledge. Am I right, Theaetetus? Shall we say this is your newborn child, and I'm the midwife who delivered it? What do you say?

Theaetetus: I can't disagree, Socrates.

Socrates: Then this is the baby — however it turns out — that you and I have labored to bring into the world. And now that it's born, we have to perform the naming ceremony: carry it in a circle around the hearth and take a good hard look at it, to see whether it's worth raising or is just a wind-egg, a fake. Or do you think your child must be raised no matter what, and not abandoned? Can you stand to watch it cross-examined and rejected, without flying into a rage if your firstborn is taken from you?

Theodorus: Theaetetus won't be angry — he's far too good-natured. But tell me, Socrates, in heaven's name: is the theory actually wrong?

Socrates: You're a lover of theories, Theodorus, and you have the charming notion that I'm a sack stuffed full of them, and can just reach in and pull out one that will overturn the last. But you don't see what's really happening: none of these theories come from me. They always come from whoever's talking with me. I know nothing — only just enough to draw an idea out of someone else's wisdom and receive it fairly. So now I'll say nothing of my own, and instead try to coax something out of our young friend here.

Theodorus: You put it well, Socrates. Do it your way.

Socrates: Shall I tell you, Theodorus, what astonishes me about your friend Protagoras?

Theodorus: What's that?

Socrates: On the whole I'm delighted with his doctrine that what appears to each person is, for that person. But the opening of his book amazes me. Why didn't he begin his "Truth" by declaring that a pig is the measure of all things — or a dog-faced baboon, or some even weirder creature, so long as it has the power of perception? That would have been a magnificently contemptuous way to open: announcing that while we were all gawking at him like he was a god for his wisdom, he was actually no wiser than a tadpole — let alone wiser than any other human being. What an opener that would have been! Because if truth is just whatever each person perceives, if no one can judge another's experience better than that person can, no one is better positioned to say whether another's opinion is true or false, and — as we've said over and over — each person alone is the judge of his own beliefs, and everything he judges is true and right, then tell me, my friend: why exactly is Protagoras the wise one? Why does he deserve to teach others and charge gigantic fees, while we're the ignorant ones who have to go to school to him — when every one of us is the measure of his own wisdom? How can this not be Protagoras playing to the crowd? And I won't even mention what it does to me and my midwife's art — we'd both be cosmically ridiculous — and the same goes for the whole practice of philosophical conversation. Examining and testing each other's opinions and beliefs would be a long, colossal exercise in stupidity, if every person's opinions are right for them — and that's exactly what follows if Protagoras' "Truth" is really true, and not just the great man joking around from the inner sanctum of his book.

Theodorus: He was my friend, Socrates, as you said yourself. I couldn't bear to be the one whose answers get him refuted — but I can't oppose you either, since I agree with you. So please, take Theaetetus back. He was answering you very nicely.

Socrates: Theodorus, if you walked into a wrestling school in Sparta, would you really think you had the right to sit there watching the wrestlers stripped down — some of them looking pretty pathetic — without stripping down yourself and giving them a look at your physique?

Theodorus: Why not, if they'd let me off? Which I'm hoping you will, out of respect for my age and stiff joints. Find some limber young man to grapple with, and leave me out of the gymnasium.

Socrates: Well, Theodorus, if that's your will, it's no ill will of mine, as the proverb-lovers say. So back to the wise Theaetetus. Tell me, Theaetetus — about what we were just saying — aren't you stunned, like I am, to discover that you've suddenly been promoted to the level of the wisest of men? Or rather of the gods — since I assume Protagoras' "measure" applies just as much to gods as to men?

Theaetetus: I assume so too. And yes, I admit it: I'm stunned. When I first heard the doctrine — that whatever appears to each person is, for them — it seemed completely satisfying. Now everything looks different.

Socrates: That's because you're young, my dear boy. You've got quick ears for crowd-pleasing arguments, and they sway you. Protagoras — or someone arguing on his behalf — will have an answer ready. He'll say: "Good people, young and old, you sit around making speeches. You drag in the gods — whose existence or nonexistence I refuse to discuss in speech or in writing. You go on about how dreadful it would be for human reason to sink to the level of the beasts — which plays great with a crowd. But proof? Demonstration? You offer not one word of it. Everything with you is plausibility. If Theodorus or any other geometer tried to do geometry on the strength of what's merely plausible and likely, he wouldn't be worth a thing. So you and Theodorus had better ask yourselves whether, on matters this important, you're willing to settle for probabilities and pretty phrases."

Theaetetus: Well, that wouldn't be fair, Socrates — not by your standards or ours.

Socrates: Then apparently you and Theodorus think we have to come at it some other way?

Theaetetus: Yes, a completely different way.

Socrates: Then here's the way: let's ask whether knowledge and perception are the same thing or different things. That was the real target of our whole argument, wasn't it? That's why we stirred up all these strange questions.

Theaetetus: Absolutely.

Socrates: So — shall we agree that we know everything we see and hear? Take languages: before we've learned a foreign language, when foreigners speak, shall we say we don't hear them? Or that we both hear and know what they're saying? Or take reading: when we look at letters we can't read, shall we insist we don't see them? Or that since we see them, we must know them?

Theaetetus: We'll say, Socrates, that we know exactly as much of them as we see and hear. With the letters, we see and know their shape and color; with the foreign speech, we hear and know the rise and fall of the sound. But what reading teachers and interpreters could tell us about them — that we don't perceive by sight or hearing, and don't know.

Socrates: Excellent, Theaetetus! I won't argue with you there — you deserve room to grow. But look, here comes another difficulty, and you'll have to fight this one off too.

Theaetetus: What is it?

Socrates: This. Someone will ask: "Can a man who has come to know something, and who still has the memory of it, preserved intact, not know the very thing he's remembering, at the moment he remembers it?" I'm being long-winded, I'm afraid. All I'm asking is: can a person who has learned something, and remembers it, fail to know it?

Theaetetus: Impossible, Socrates. That would be monstrous.

Socrates: Am I talking nonsense, then? Think it through. Seeing is perceiving, and sight is a perception — right?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: And by our recent definition, anyone who sees something knows it?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And you'd grant that there's such a thing as memory?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: Memory of something, or of nothing?

Theaetetus: Of something, obviously.

Socrates: Of things one has learned and perceived — that kind of something?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: So a man sometimes remembers what he has seen?

Theaetetus: He does.

Socrates: Even with his eyes shut? Or does he forget the moment he closes them?

Theaetetus: That would be an outrageous thing to say, Socrates.

Socrates: And yet say it we must, if our earlier argument is going to survive.

Theaetetus: What do you mean? I can't quite pin it down — though I have a strong suspicion you're right.

Socrates: Here's the problem. We say the one who sees knows what he sees, because sight, perception, and knowledge are all agreed to be the same thing.

Theaetetus: Right.

Socrates: But the man who saw something, and so knows it, still remembers it when he closes his eyes — even though he no longer sees it.

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: And "not seeing" is "not knowing," since "seeing" is "knowing."

Theaetetus: That follows.

Socrates: Then look at the conclusion: a man who has come to know something, and still remembers it, doesn't know it — because he isn't seeing it. And that's the result we just called monstrous.

Theaetetus: So it is.

Socrates: So the claim that knowledge and perception are the same leads straight to an impossibility?

Theaetetus: It looks that way.

Socrates: Then they must be different things?

Theaetetus: I suppose they must.

Socrates: So we're back to the beginning: what is knowledge? Although — Theaetetus, hold on. What are we doing?

Theaetetus: About what?

Socrates: We're behaving like a no-account fighting rooster: hopping away from the argument and crowing victory before we've actually won.

Theaetetus: How so?

Socrates: We've been acting like professional debaters. We made our agreements about the meanings of words, scored our point through verbal consistency, and congratulated ourselves on winning. We claim to be philosophers, not prize-fighting word-twisters — and yet, without noticing it, we've sunk to the level of those clever gentlemen.

Theaetetus: I still don't see what you mean.

Socrates: Then let me try to explain. We asked whether someone who has learned a thing and remembers it can fail to know it. We pointed out that a man who has seen something can shut his eyes and remember it while no longer seeing it — and so he'd remember and not-know at the same time. We agreed that was impossible. And so the Protagorean tale perished — and yours along with it, the claim that knowledge is perception.

Theaetetus: So it seems.

Socrates: But I suspect, my friend, things would have gone very differently if the father of the first of those two brainchildren were still alive. Protagoras would have had plenty to say in its defense. As it is, he's dead, and we're kicking his orphan around. Even the guardians he left behind won't lift a finger — and our friend Theodorus here is one of them. In the name of justice, it looks like I'll have to come to the child's defense myself.

Theodorus: Not me, Socrates — Callias, son of Hipponicus, is the real guardian of his estate. I drifted away from abstract argument to geometry early on. Still, I'll be grateful if you come to his rescue.

Socrates: Well said, Theodorus. Watch, then, and see what my rescue looks like — because a person can land in even worse paradoxes than the ones we just saw, if he doesn't pay attention to how words are actually used in argument. Should I explain this to you, or to Theaetetus?

Theodorus: To both of us — but let the younger one give the answers. There's less shame for him if he gets tripped up.

Socrates: Then here comes the truly fearsome question. It's this: can the same man know something and also not know the very thing he knows?

Theodorus: Well, Theaetetus? What's our answer?

Theaetetus: He can't — I'd say.

Socrates: Oh yes he can — if you're going to maintain that seeing is knowing. What will you do when some self-assured fellow has you cornered — "trapped in a well," as the saying goes — claps his hand over one of your eyes, and asks whether you can see his cloak with the eye he's covering?
