How will you answer him? There's no escaping this man.

Theaetetus: I'd say: "Not with that eye, but with the other one."

Socrates: So you see and don't see the same thing at the same time?

Theaetetus: Well — yes, in a certain sense.

Socrates: "None of that," he'll snap. "I didn't ask in what sense. I asked one thing: do you know what you don't know? You've just been caught seeing what you don't see. And you've already admitted that seeing is knowing and not-seeing is not-knowing. Add it up yourself."

Theaetetus: And when I add it up, I get the exact contradiction of what I claimed.

Socrates: Yes, you marvel — and you might have suffered far worse if your opponent had kept going. He could have asked whether you can know sharply and also know dully; whether you can know something up close but not at a distance; whether you can know the same thing intensely and faintly — and on and on without end. Questions like these are the ammunition of the mercenary skirmisher of debate, the fighter who argues for pay. The moment you took the position that knowledge is perception, he'd have ambushed you — launched his assault on hearing and smelling and the rest of the senses, shown you no mercy, kept you pinned down while you stood there marveling at his prodigious wisdom. By the time he had you tangled in his net, you'd be paying whatever ransom the two of you agreed on before he'd let you out. But now, you'll ask, what would Protagoras himself say in defense of his doctrine? Shall I speak for him?

Theaetetus: Please do.

Socrates: He'll repeat everything we've already said on his behalf, and then — I imagine — he'll close with us in open contempt. He'll say:

"This fine fellow Socrates found a little boy, asked him whether the same person can remember a thing and not know it, and the boy got scared, couldn't see the trap coming, and said no — and so, Socrates made a laughingstock of me. Here's the truth, you slapdash Socrates. When you cross-examine some doctrine of mine by asking questions, and the person answering gets caught out, then I am refuted only if he answered the way I would have answered. If he says something different, the refuted party is him, not me. Start with this: do you really expect anyone to grant that the memory of a past impression is the same as the impression itself was, at the moment it was happening, to the person having it? Not even close. Or that anyone would shy away from admitting that the same man can know and not know the same thing? Or — if that scares him — that he'd ever concede a man who has become unlike his former self is the same person he was before the change? Indeed, that there is such a thing as 'a man' at all — one man — rather than an endless series of men, a new one for every change he undergoes? I'm choosing my words precisely, you see, since we have to watch out for each other's verbal traps.

"No, my good sir," he'll say. "Come at my actual argument, and do it more generously. Show, if you can, that our perceptions are not private and individual to each of us. Or, granting that they're individual, show that it doesn't follow that what appears to a person thereby becomes — or, if you insist on the word, is — for that person alone. As for your pigs and your baboons — you're not just talking like a pig yourself, you're training your audience to root through my writings like pigs, and that does you no credit. Because I say it again: the truth is exactly as I've written it. Each of us is the measure of what is and of what is not. But people differ from each other enormously in precisely this way: different things are, and appear, to different people. As for wisdom and wise men, far be it from me to deny they exist. Here's what I call a wise man: anyone who can take someone for whom things seem and are bad, and turn things around so they seem and are good for him.

"And don't go chasing the letter of my words this time — let me make my meaning even plainer. Remember what was said earlier: to a sick man, his food seems bitter, and is bitter; to a healthy man, it both is and seems the opposite. Now, there's no call to make either of these men out to be wiser than the other — that's not even possible. And there's no call to declare the sick man ignorant for thinking what he thinks, or the healthy man wise for thinking differently. What's needed is to change the man from one condition to the other — because the other condition is better. Education is the same: a change from a worse condition to a better one. The difference is that a doctor produces the change with drugs, while a sophist does it with words. Not that anyone ever made a man whose beliefs were false start believing truly — it's impossible to believe what is not, or to believe anything beyond what one experiences, and what one experiences is always true. What happens, I'd say, is this: a man whose soul is in a worthless condition believes things that match that condition, and a sound soul gives him sound things to believe. Those beliefs — the ones some people naively call 'true' — are simply better than the others. Not truer. Better.

"And as for the wise, dear Socrates — I'm not calling them tadpoles, far from it. Where bodies are concerned, the wise are the doctors; where plants are concerned, the farmers. Yes, farmers too: when plants are sickly, I say the farmer strips away their bad and diseased sensations and instills good, healthy, sound ones — true ones, even. And wise, skilled public speakers do the same for cities: they make sound things, rather than rotten things, seem just. Because whatever each city judges just and admirable really is just and admirable for that city, as long as it holds those beliefs. But the wise man takes each rotten thing that is and seems just to them, and makes a sound thing be and seem just instead. By the same logic, the sophist who can educate his pupils this way is wise, and worth every penny of his fees. And so: some people are wiser than others, and yet nobody believes falsely. And you, Socrates, like it or not, will have to put up with being a measure — because that's where the argument stands, solid as ever.

"You're welcome to attack it from the ground up, if you like — lay out a counterargument in a full speech. Or do it by questions, if you prefer; no sensible person runs away from questioning, quite the opposite. But do observe one rule: don't ask questions unfairly. It's wildly inconsistent to claim you care about virtue and then spend your time cheating at argument. Cheating, here, means failing to keep the two modes separate — the competitive debate and the genuine discussion. In a debate, play games and trip people up as much as you can. But in genuine discussion, be serious. Set your partner back on his feet; show him only the slips that he made on his own, or that were drilled into him by the company he kept before you. Treat people that way, and those who spend time with you will blame themselves for their own confusion and dead ends, not you. They'll follow you and love you — and hate themselves enough to run from who they are into philosophy, hoping to become different people and shed the selves they used to be. But take the usual approach — the opposite one — and you'll get the opposite result: instead of philosophers, you'll turn your companions into people who, by the time they grow up, despise the whole enterprise. So if you take my advice, you'll come to us not with hostility and combativeness but with goodwill, and honestly examine what we mean when we declare that all things are in motion, and that whatever appears to each person or each city, is — for that person or city. Build on that to investigate whether knowledge and perception are the same or different. But don't do what you did just now — making your case out of the everyday use of words and phrases, which ordinary people yank around any way they please, creating no end of confusion for each other."

There, Theodorus — that's my contribution to your old friend's defense. The best I can scrape together from my small means. If he were alive, he'd have defended his own ideas in far grander style.

Theodorus: You're joking, Socrates. Though I have to admit, your defense of him was downright heroic.

Socrates: Thank you, my friend. But I hope you noticed Protagoras scolding us to take this seriously. His text — "Man is the measure of all things" — was meant as a solemn pronouncement, and he complained that we were running the argument through a boy, letting the boy's timidity count as evidence against his position. He said we were treating him as a joke.

Theodorus: How could I have missed all that, Socrates?

Socrates: Well then — shall we do what he asks?

Theodorus: By all means.

Socrates: But if we're going to respect his wishes, then you and I have to take up the argument ourselves, in complete seriousness, asking and answering each other directly. Because compared to him, the rest of us here are just boys. There's no other way to escape the accusation that we're re-examining his thesis by playing games with children.

Theodorus: Come now — isn't Theaetetus better at following a philosophical investigation than plenty of men with long beards?

Socrates: Yes, Theodorus, but not better than you. So don't imagine that I'm obligated to defend your departed friend by every means I've got while you defend nothing and nobody. No, my good man — don't slip away until we find out whether you really are the measure of diagrams, or whether everyone is equally his own measure, fully self-sufficient in astronomy and geometry and all the other subjects where you're supposed to be the expert.

Theodorus: Sitting next to you, Socrates, nobody escapes getting pulled into an argument. I was talking nonsense just now when I said you'd excuse me — that you wouldn't be like the Spartans and force me to strip and wrestle. Actually, you're worse. The Spartan rule is "strip or leave." You're more like Antaeus, the giant who wrestled every traveler who came near: you won't let anyone who approaches you go until you've stripped him down and made him take a fall with you in argument.

Socrates: That's it exactly, Theodorus — you've diagnosed my condition perfectly. But I'm more relentless than the giants of old, because I've faced an endless parade of heroes. Many a Heracles, many a Theseus, mighty in words, has cracked my head open. And still I keep coming back for more of this rough sport — it grips me like a passion. So come on: take a fall with me. It'll do us both good.

Theodorus: Fine, I give in. Lead me wherever you like. When it comes to argument, you're like fate itself — no one escapes the web you weave. But I won't go a step further than what we've agreed.

Socrates: One round will be enough. And now let's be careful not to slip back, without noticing, into the kind of talk that gets us accused of acting like children.

Theodorus: I'll do my best to avoid it.

Socrates: First, then, let's go back to our earlier objection. Were we right to fault Protagoras — to take offense at his claim that everyone is equally sufficient in wisdom — when he himself admitted that some people are better and some worse, and that the better ones, the ones he called "the wise," really do excel the rest?

Theodorus: Very true.

Socrates: If Protagoras were alive and answering for himself, instead of us answering on his behalf, there'd be no need to review or shore up the argument. But he isn't here, and someone might accuse us of putting words in his mouth. So shouldn't we pin down exactly what he meant? A great deal could be riding on it.

Theodorus: True.

Socrates: Then let's get the basis of agreement not from any third party, but from his own statement, in the fewest possible words.

Theodorus: How?

Socrates: Like this. His words are: "What seems true to a man, is true — for him."

Theodorus: Yes, that's what he says.

Socrates: Well then, Protagoras, we too are voicing the opinion of a man — or rather of all mankind — when we say that everyone considers himself wiser than other people in some areas, and less wise in others. In moments of danger — in war, at sea, in sickness — don't people look up to their commanders as if they were gods, expecting to be saved by them, for exactly one reason: that these men know more than they do? The whole world is full of people looking for teachers and leaders, for themselves and for their animals — and full of others who believe they're qualified to teach and qualified to lead. And all of this implies that people themselves believe that wisdom and ignorance exist among them.

Theodorus: Certainly.

Socrates: And they take wisdom to be true thinking, and ignorance to be false opinion.

Theodorus: Exactly.

Socrates: So how should we handle the argument, Protagoras? Should we say that people's opinions are always true? Or sometimes true and sometimes false? Either way it comes out the same: their opinions are not always true, but sometimes true and sometimes false. Think about it, Theodorus. Would any follower of Protagoras — would you yourself — want to insist that no person ever considers another person ignorant or mistaken?

Theodorus: That's unbelievable, Socrates. Of course people do.

Socrates: And yet that absurdity is exactly what's built into the thesis that man is the measure of all things.

Theodorus: How so?

Socrates: Like this. Suppose you settle something in your own mind and state your opinion to me. By Protagoras' rule, let's grant that it's true — for you. But what about the rest of us? Don't we get to judge your judgment? Or do we somehow always judge that your opinion is true? Aren't there thousands of people, every time you form an opinion, ready to take up arms against you with the opposite opinion, convinced that you've judged falsely?

Theodorus: Oh yes, Socrates — thousands and tens of thousands, as Homer says, and they give me no end of trouble.

Socrates: Well then, shall we say that in those cases your opinion is true for you, but false for the ten thousand others?

Theodorus: That seems to be the only conclusion.

Socrates: And what about Protagoras himself? Suppose neither he nor the multitude believed that man is the measure of all things — which in fact they don't. Then the "Truth" that Protagoras wrote wouldn't be true for anyone at all. And suppose he did believe it, but the multitude doesn't agree. Then you have to start by admitting this: by however much the many outnumber the one, by that much his truth is more false than true.

Theodorus: That follows — if truth really varies with each person's opinion.

Socrates: And here's the best part of the joke. When he admits that everyone's opinions are true, he's admitting the truth of his opponents' opinion — and their opinion is that he's wrong.

Theodorus: Certainly.

Socrates: So he concedes that his own view is false, if he grants that the people who think he's wrong are thinking truly?

Theodorus: Of course.

Socrates: But the other side doesn't admit that they're wrong?

Theodorus: No, they don't.

Socrates: And he — judging from his own writings — has to agree that their opinion is true as well.

Theodorus: Clearly.

Socrates: Then everyone, starting with Protagoras himself, will dispute his doctrine — or rather, he'll concede it. The moment he grants that his opponent holds a true opinion, Protagoras himself is admitting that neither a dog nor any random man is "the measure" of anything he hasn't studied. Am I right?

Theodorus: Yes.

Socrates: So, since everybody disputes Protagoras' truth, it turns out to be true for no one — not for anyone else, and not even for Protagoras himself.

Theodorus: Socrates, I think we're running my old friend a bit too hard.

Socrates: Maybe — but I'm not sure we've overshot the truth. Of course, since he's older, we should expect him to be wiser than we are. If he could just pop his head up out of the underworld right now, he'd probably knock us both flat in no time — me for talking nonsense and you for agreeing with me — and then duck back underground and be gone. But he's beyond our reach, so we have to make do with our own faculties, such as they are, and say what actually seems true to us. And here's one thing nobody will deny: there are enormous differences in human understanding.

Theodorus: On that I completely agree.

Socrates: And isn't the firmest ground exactly the distinction we sketched out on Protagoras' behalf? Namely: with most things — especially immediate sensations like hot, dry, sweet — things really are just as they appear to each person. But if there's any area where we have to allow that one opinion can be better than another, surely it's health and disease. Not every woman or child — or animal, for that matter — knows enough about health to cure themselves. If anywhere, here one person really is wiser than another.

Theodorus: I quite agree.

Socrates: The same in politics. The Protagoreans will say that just and unjust, honorable and shameful, holy and unholy really are, for each city, whatever that city believes them to be and writes into law — that on these questions no individual and no city is wiser than another. But when it comes to deciding what's actually beneficial for the community, even they won't deny that one city's judgment is wiser than another's, one adviser better than the next. They'd hardly dare claim that whatever a city enacts, believing it beneficial, will actually turn out to be beneficial. It's only in that other realm — justice and injustice, piety and impiety — that they confidently insist none of it has any nature or essence of its own: the truth is whatever gets agreed on, at the moment of agreement, for exactly as long as the agreement lasts. And that's the philosophy of plenty of people who don't even follow Protagoras the whole way. But now, Theodorus, a new question is rising up — a bigger one than the last.

Theodorus: Well, Socrates, we have plenty of free time.

Socrates: So we do. And that remark of yours reminds me of something I've noticed many times: how absurdly helpless people who've spent their lives in philosophy look when they have to show up and speak in a courtroom. And it's no wonder!

Theodorus: What do you mean?

Socrates: I mean that people raised in philosophy and free inquiry, compared to people who've been knocking around courtrooms since they were young — well, the difference is like the difference between a free man's upbringing and a slave's.

Theodorus: How does the difference show?

Socrates: It shows in exactly that free time you just mentioned. The free man always has it. He talks through his subject in peace, at leisure. Like us, right now, he wanders from one topic to another, and on to a third; if a new thought strikes him, he starts over — as we're doing — and he doesn't care whether the discussion is long or short. His only goal is to reach the truth.

But the courtroom man is always in a hurry. The water-clock is running, driving him on — no rambling allowed. His opponent stands over him, enforcing the rules, holding the sworn indictment that lays out exactly what may and may not be discussed; he can't stray from it by an inch. He's a slave arguing with a fellow slave before a master who sits there holding the case in his hands. The stakes are never about something neutral — they're always about himself, and often the race is for his very life.

All this has made him tense and sharp. He's learned to flatter his master with words and grovel before him in deeds. But his soul has come out small and crooked. The slavery he's lived in since youth has robbed him of growth, of straightness, of independence. Dangers and fears hit him early, when he was too young and tender to face them honestly, so he was forced into bent and devious ways. He learned to lie and to retaliate from the very beginning, and he's grown warped and stunted. He passes from youth into manhood with no soundness left in him — and yet he's convinced he's become formidable and wise. That's your courtroom man, Theodorus. Shall I paint the companion portrait — the philosopher, one of our own brotherhood? Or should we get back to the argument? We don't want to abuse this freedom of digression we keep claiming.

Theodorus: No, Socrates — not until we've finished. You said it truly yourself: we belong to a free brotherhood, and we are not the servants of the argument. The argument is our servant, and it can wait until we're ready. Who's our judge here? Where's the spectator with any right to censor us or hurry us along, the way they can with the poets?

Socrates: Then since that's what you want, let's talk about the leaders of philosophy — there's no point discussing the second-rate ones. From their youth on, the true philosophers have never known the way to the marketplace, or the law courts, or the council chamber, or any other political gathering. They neither see nor hear the laws and decrees of the city, whether published or proclaimed. The scrambling of political clubs for office, the meetings, the dinners, the parties with hired flute-girls — none of this enters even their dreams. Has something gone well or badly in the city? Has some family scandal come down to a man from his ancestors? The philosopher knows no more about it than he knows how many pints of water are in the sea, as the saying goes. And he doesn't even realize he doesn't know — because he's not staying aloof for the sake of his reputation. The truth is that only his body is here in the city, residing among us. His mind, having concluded that all these human concerns are small and worth nothing, treats them with contempt and flies in every direction — "soaring far and wide," as Pindar says — measuring the earth and the heavens, the things below the earth and above the sky, investigating the entire nature of everything that exists, each thing as a whole, and never lowering itself to anything close at hand.

Theodorus: What do you mean by that, Socrates?

Socrates: Let me illustrate it, Theodorus, with the joke that a clever and witty Thracian servant girl is said to have made about Thales. He was studying the stars, looking up — and fell into a well. She teased him: he was so eager to know what was happening in the heavens that he couldn't see what was right in front of his feet. The same joke applies to everyone who lives in philosophy. The philosopher genuinely doesn't know his next-door neighbor — not just what the man is doing, but practically whether he's a human being or some other creature. Meanwhile, what is a human being? What should such a nature do or undergo, that's different from every other nature? That is what he's asking, that is what he's busy investigating. You follow me, Theodorus?

Theodorus: I do, and it's true.

Socrates: And so, my friend, in private and in public, just as I said at the start: whenever the philosopher is dragged into court, or anywhere else he's forced to deal with what's at his feet and in front of his eyes, he becomes a laughingstock — not just for Thracian servant girls but for the crowd in general — falling into wells and every kind of trouble because he has no experience. His awkwardness is appalling; people take him for a complete fool. When he's insulted, he has no personal slanders to fire back, because he doesn't know anything scandalous about anybody — he's never been interested. So he just stands there at a loss, and everyone laughs at him for it. And when people are being praised and congratulating each other, he can't help laughing out loud — not for show, but honestly — and that makes him look like an outright idiot.

When a tyrant or a king is being glorified, he feels like he's hearing some farmer praised — a pig-keeper, a shepherd, a cowherd — congratulated for the gallons of milk he squeezes out of his herd. Except that the animal kings tend and milk is more dangerous and treacherous than cattle, and the king himself, with no leisure to learn anything, penned in by the walls of his fortress like a herdsman in his mountain pen, ends up just as rough and uneducated as any shepherd. When the philosopher hears about some landowner with ten thousand acres or more — an astonishing fortune! — to him it sounds like next to nothing, because he's used to thinking about the whole earth. And when people sing the praises of family — "he's a gentleman; he can show seven generations of wealthy ancestors" — he thinks the praise comes from dim, shortsighted people who can't see the whole picture: that every single person has had thousands upon thousands of ancestors, and among them, for anyone you pick, there have been rich men and beggars, kings and slaves, Greeks and barbarians, beyond counting. When someone takes pride in a list of twenty-five forebears stretching back to Heracles, son of Amphitryon, the pettiness of it baffles him. Amphitryon had a twenty-fifth ancestor too — who was whatever luck made him — and that man had a fiftieth, and so on back forever. Why can't they do the math? He laughs to himself at their inability to count, and thinks a little arithmetic would cure them of their swollen vanity. In all these situations, the crowd mocks the philosopher — partly because he seems arrogant, partly because he doesn't know the everyday things at his feet and is hopeless in every practical situation.

Theodorus: That's exactly how it goes, Socrates.

Socrates: But, my friend, watch what happens when the philosopher drags one of them up into the heights. When some lawyer is willing to leave behind his "Who did what to whom?" and climb up to look at justice and injustice themselves — what each of them is, how they differ from each other and from everything else. When he's pulled away from his stock phrases about whether a king with piles of gold is a happy man, up to the examination of kingship itself, and of human happiness and misery in general — what they really are, and how a human being should pursue the one and escape the other. When that narrow, sharp, little legal mind has to give an account of all that — then the tables turn, and the philosopher gets his revenge. Dangling at that height, staring down into so much open space, the lawyer gets dizzy. Terrified, lost, stammering broken phrases, he becomes the laughingstock — not to Thracian servant girls or other uneducated people, who can't even see what's happening, but to everyone whose upbringing was the opposite of a slave's.

So there are the two characters, Theodorus. One is the man raised in genuine freedom and leisure — the one you call a philosopher. He can look simple and useless when menial tasks fall to him: he doesn't know how to bundle up bedding, or sweeten a sauce, or sweeten a speech — and we shouldn't hold it against him. The other can do all those little services briskly and neatly, but he doesn't know how to wear his cloak like a free man, much less how to strike up the music of true discourse and rightly sing the life that gods live, and human beings blessed by heaven.

Theodorus: Socrates, if you could persuade everyone of what you're saying, the way you persuade me, there would be more peace and less evil in the world.

Socrates: Evil can never be destroyed, Theodorus — there must always be something standing opposed to the good. And it can't be housed among the gods in heaven, so by necessity it haunts our mortal nature and this earthly realm. That's why we should try to escape from earth to heaven as quickly as we can. And escaping means becoming like God, as far as that's possible — and becoming like God means becoming just and holy, with wisdom.

But, my friend, it isn't easy to convince people of the right reason for pursuing virtue and fleeing vice. The reason isn't the one the world gives — so that you'll seem good rather than bad. That, in my judgment, is just an old wives' tale. Here's the truth. God is never, in any way, unjust. God is perfect justice, and nothing is more like God than the person among us who becomes as just as possible. This is the test of a man's true ability — or of his emptiness and cowardice. To know it is real wisdom and real virtue; not to know it is obvious folly and vice. Everything else that passes for ability and wisdom — political cleverness, technical know-how — is coarse and common by comparison.

So when a man acts unjustly and speaks or does unholy things, it's far better not to let him believe his rottenness is cleverness. People like that glory in the insult. They imagine they're hearing: "These aren't useless dead weight — these are real men, the kind a city needs to survive!" So let's tell them the truth: they are exactly what they don't think they are, and all the more so because they don't know it. Because they don't know the penalty of injustice — and that's the last thing anyone should be ignorant of. The penalty isn't what they think it is. It isn't whippings and executions, which unjust men sometimes dodge entirely. It's a penalty that cannot be escaped.

Theodorus: What penalty do you mean?

Socrates: There are two patterns set up in reality, eternally: the divine pattern, which is supremely happy, and the godless pattern, which is supremely wretched. But these men can't see this. Their foolishness, their utter blindness, hides from them what their evil actions are doing — making them grow more like the one pattern and less like the other. And the penalty they pay is exactly that: they live the life that matches the pattern they're coming to resemble. If we tell them that unless they shed their "cleverness," the place untouched by evil will refuse them after death, and that here on earth they'll live a life shaped like themselves — evil men in the company of evil — they'll hear all this as the babbling of idiots. They're far too clever for it.

Theodorus: Very true, Socrates.

Socrates: True indeed, my friend — I know it well. But there's one odd thing about these people. When they have to reason privately, one-on-one, about the philosophy they despise, and they're brave enough to hear the argument through to the end without running away — then, strangely, they end up dissatisfied with themselves. Their famous rhetoric withers, and they're left as helpless as children. But all this is digression, and we'd better stop, or it will keep flooding in and drown our original argument. Shall we go back to it?

Theodorus: Honestly, Socrates, I'd rather have the digressions — at my age they're easier to follow. But by all means, let's return to the argument.

Socrates: Well then, here's roughly where we were. We were talking about the people who preach that everything is in flux, and that what seems so to each person is so — for that person. We said they're prepared to stand firm on most things, and especially on questions of justice: whatever a city decrees and believes to be just is just, for that city, as long as the decree stands. But when it comes to the good, nobody is bold enough to keep fighting and claim that whatever a city enacts believing it beneficial really is beneficial as long as it's on the books. Anyone who said that would just be playing with the word "beneficial" — and that would make a mockery of our actual question, wouldn't it?

Theodorus: It certainly would.

Socrates: He shouldn't talk about the word, but about the thing the word points to.

Theodorus: Right.

Socrates: Whatever word a city uses, the good — the beneficial — is what it's aiming at when it legislates. Every city makes all its laws with a view to what will benefit it most, as far as its judgment and power can reach. Can lawmaking have any other goal?

Theodorus: Certainly not.

Socrates: But does it always hit the mark? Don't cities often miss?

Theodorus: Yes, I think mistakes do happen.

Socrates: We can see the possibility of error even more clearly if we ask about the whole category that "beneficial" belongs to. The beneficial is about the future, isn't it? When we make laws, we make them on the theory that they'll be useful in the time to come — which is to say, the future.

Theodorus: Very true.

Socrates: Then here's a question for Protagoras, or any of his disciples. "Protagoras," we'll say, "man, by your account, is the measure of all things — of white, heavy, light, all things of that sort. He carries the criterion inside himself, so when he believes things are as he experiences them, he believes what is true — for him." Isn't that so?

Theodorus: Yes.

Socrates: "But Protagoras," we'll go on, "does your doctrine cover the future too? Does each man carry the criterion of what will be, so that whatever he expects to happen actually happens — for him? Take heat. When an ordinary man thinks he's about to catch a fever — that this hot flush coming on is the start of one — and a doctor thinks otherwise, whose prediction should we bet on? Or are both of them right: he'll be hot and feverish in his own judgment, and not feverish in the doctor's?"

Theodorus: That would be ridiculous!

Socrates: And on the question of whether next season's wine will be sweet or dry — surely the vinegrower's judgment beats the harp player's?

Theodorus: Certainly.

Socrates: And on what will sound harmonious or off-key, the musician will be a better judge than the gym instructor — even about what the gym instructor himself will later find harmonious?

Theodorus: Of course.

Socrates: And when a feast is being prepared, the cook will be a better judge than the guest of the pleasure to come — the guest being no cook. Notice we're not yet arguing about present or past pleasure, only about future pleasure: is each person really his own best judge of what will seem pleasant, and be pleasant, to him later? Or take your own case, Protagoras: wouldn't you predict better than any ordinary person which arguments will persuade a jury?

Theodorus: On that point, Socrates, he certainly used to insist — emphatically — that he was better at it than anybody alive.

Socrates: Of course he did, my friend. Nobody would have paid him those enormous fees for conversation if he'd actually convinced his customers that no one — not even a prophet — could judge what will be, and will seem to be, any better than they could judge for themselves.

Theodorus: Nobody at all.

Socrates: And lawmaking, and the question of what's beneficial, are entirely about the future — and everyone will admit that cities, when they pass laws, must often miss what would benefit them most.

THEAETETUS (Part 2)

Theodorus: Quite true.

Socrates: Then we have a fair argument against your master: he has to admit that one person is wiser than another, and that the wiser person is a measure. As for me — the one who knows nothing — I'm under no obligation to accept the honor that Protagoras' defender was just trying to force on me, like it or not: the honor of being a measure of anything.

Theodorus: That's the strongest refutation of him, Socrates. Though he's also trapped another way: by granting that everyone's opinions are true, he grants the truth of the people whose opinion is that he's flat wrong.

Socrates: There are plenty of ways, Theodorus, to refute the doctrine that every opinion of every person is true. But it's much harder to prove that the feelings actually present to a person — the ones that give rise to sensations and the opinions that match them — are false too. Maybe I've been talking nonsense about them. Maybe they really are unassailable, and the people who say they're clear evidence — actual knowledge — may well be right. In that case our friend Theaetetus wasn't so far off the mark when he said perception and knowledge are the same thing. So let's move in closer, as Protagoras' defender wanted, and give this theory of universal flux a good knock to hear whether it rings true or cracked. One thing is certain: there's no small war raging over it, and no shortage of fighters on each side.

Theodorus: No small war indeed. In Ionia the movement is spreading fast — the followers of Heraclitus are the doctrine's most energetic champions.

Socrates: All the more reason, my dear Theodorus, to examine the question from the ground up, the way they themselves lay it out.

Theodorus: Absolutely. Though about these Heraclitean ideas — which, as you say, are as old as Homer or even older — the Ephesians who claim to understand them are completely mad. You can't have a conversation with them about it. True to their own textbooks, they're perpetually in motion. As for staying put on one argument or one question — calmly asking and answering in turn — they can no more do that than they can fly. Actually, "no more" understates it: there isn't a negative strong enough to capture how determined these men are not to have a single particle of rest in them. Ask one of them a question and he pulls little cryptic sayings out like arrows from a quiver and shoots them at you. Ask him to explain what he just said, and you get hit with another freshly coined riddle. You'll never get anywhere with any of them — and they get nowhere with each other. They take enormous care never to let anything settled appear in their arguments or in their own minds, believing, I suppose, that anything settled would be standing still. And they're at war with stillness; they do everything they can to banish it from the universe.

Socrates: Maybe, Theodorus, you've only seen them in combat and never spent time with them during a truce — they're not your crowd, after all. I imagine they save their "peacetime doctrines" for the students they hope to mold into copies of themselves.

Theodorus: Students! My good man, they don't have any. Men like that aren't anyone's students. They just spring up on their own, each one catching his inspiration wherever he happens to find it, and each one convinced his neighbor knows nothing. So as I was about to say: you'll never get a reasoned account out of these men, willingly or unwillingly. We'll have to take the question out of their hands and work through the analysis ourselves, as if it were a geometry problem.

Socrates: Quite right. But on this question — haven't we heard it from the ancients too, who hid their wisdom from the masses inside poetic imagery? They said that Oceanus and Tethys, the origin of all things, are flowing streams, and that nothing stands still. And now the moderns, being so much wiser, declare it openly — so even the cobbler can hear their wisdom and learn it, and stop foolishly imagining that some things rest while others move. Once he's learned that everything is motion, he'll honor his teachers properly. But I almost forgot the opposite camp, Theodorus:

"Alone Being remains unmoved, which is the name for the all."

That's the language of Parmenides, Melissus, and their followers, who insist that all being is one, complete in itself, with nowhere to move. What are we going to do with all these people? Step by step we've accidentally wandered right between the two armies, and unless we can cover our retreat, we'll pay for our recklessness — like players in the wrestling school caught on the center line and dragged in both directions by both teams. I think we'd better start with the group we approached first, the "river gods" of flux. If we find any truth in them, we'll let them pull us over to their side and try to escape the others. But if the partisans of "the whole" seem to speak more truly, then we'll flee to them, away from the people who would set the immovable in motion. And if it turns out neither side has anything reasonable to say, we'll look ridiculous — imagining that nobodies like us could be right while ancient and famous men are wrong. O Theodorus, do you think it's worth going forward when the danger is this great?

Theodorus: Socrates, leaving these two positions unexamined would be intolerable. We have to go on.

Socrates: Then examine we must — since you, who were so reluctant to start, are now so eager to continue. The nature of motion seems to be the place to begin. What exactly do they mean when they say all things are in motion? Is there only one kind of motion, or — as I'm inclined to think — two? I'd like your opinion on this along with mine, so that if I'm going to be wrong, at least I'll be wrong in good company. Tell me: when something changes from one place to another, or spins around in the same place, isn't that what we call motion?

Theodorus: Yes.

Socrates: So there's one kind of motion. But when something stays in the same spot but grows old, or turns black from white, or hard from soft, or undergoes any other change — isn't it fair to call that a second kind of motion?

Theodorus: I think so.

Socrates: Say rather that it must be so. So motion comes in these two kinds: "change" and "motion in place."

Theodorus: You're right.

Socrates: Now that we've drawn this distinction, let's address the people who say everything is in motion and ask them: do all things, on your view, have both kinds of motion — changing as well as moving in place — or do some things move in both ways and others in only one?

Theodorus: Honestly, I don't know how to answer. But I think they'd say all things move in both ways.

Socrates: They'd have to, my friend. Otherwise they'd be admitting that the same things are both moving and at rest, and then it would be no more true to say "everything moves" than to say "everything rests."

Theodorus: That's certain.

Socrates: And if things are to be in motion, with nothing exempt from motion, then everything must always have every kind of motion?

Theodorus: Quite true.

Socrates: Now consider a further point. Didn't we understand them to explain the generation of heat or whiteness or anything else roughly like this: each of these things moves, along with a perception, between the agent and the patient — the thing acting and the thing acted upon. The patient stops being a mere capacity for perceiving and becomes an actual perceiver, while the agent becomes a "qualified thing" rather than a quality. I suspect "quality" sounds like a strange, awkward word to you, and the abstract phrasing isn't landing. So let me give concrete examples. I mean that the producing power, the agent, becomes not heat or whiteness but hot and white, and so on for everything else. Remember, as I said before: neither the agent nor the patient has any independent existence on its own. It's when they come together and generate sensations and the things sensed that one becomes a thing with a certain quality and the other becomes a perceiver. You remember all this?

Theodorus: Of course.

Socrates: We can skip the fine print of their theory. But we mustn't forget to ask them the one question that concerns us: Are all things in motion and flux?

Theodorus: Yes, they'll say.

Socrates: And moving in both the ways we distinguished — moving in place and also changing?

Theodorus: Of course — if the motion is to be complete.

Socrates: Right, because if things only moved in place without changing, we'd still be able to say what kind of things these moving, flowing things are. Wouldn't we?

Theodorus: Exactly.

Socrates: But as it stands, not even white keeps flowing white. Whiteness itself is a flux, a change, always slipping into another color, never to be caught standing still. So can the name of any color ever be used correctly at all?

Theodorus: How could it be, Socrates — for color or any other quality — if the object is escaping in the flux even while we're saying the word?

Socrates: And what about perceptions like sight and hearing, or any other kind? Does the act of seeing or hearing ever stand still?

Theodorus: Certainly not, if everything is in motion.

Socrates: Then we have no more right to speak of "seeing" than of "not-seeing," and no more right to speak of any perception than of any non-perception — if everything shares in every kind of motion?

Theodorus: We don't.

Socrates: Yet perception is knowledge — at least that's what Theaetetus and I were saying.

Theodorus: Very true.

Socrates: So when we were asked "what is knowledge?", our answer was no more an answer about knowledge than about non-knowledge?

Theodorus: I suppose not.

Socrates: A fine result! We polished up our first answer by being so eager to prove that nothing stands still — and if nothing stands still, then every answer on every subject is equally correct. You can say a thing is "thus," or "not thus" — or if you prefer, "becomes thus," so we don't trap them with words that imply rest.

Theodorus: Quite true.

Socrates: Yes, Theodorus — except for the words "thus" and "not thus." They can't even use "thus," because there's no motion in "thus" or in "not thus." The defenders of this doctrine don't yet have any words to express themselves with; they need to invent a whole new language. The only phrase I know that would suit them is "no how" — perfectly indefinite.

Theodorus: Yes, that's exactly the kind of talk they'd feel at home with.

Socrates: And so, Theodorus, we've gotten free of your friend without conceding his doctrine that every man is the measure of all things — only a wise man is a measure. Nor can we accept that knowledge is perception, certainly not on the hypothesis of perpetual flux — unless perhaps our friend Theaetetus can convince us otherwise.

Theodorus: Excellent, Socrates. And now that the argument about Protagoras' doctrine is finished, I'm released from answering — that was the deal.

Theaetetus: Not yet, Theodorus! Not until you and Socrates have dealt with the people who say everything is at rest, the way you proposed.

Theodorus: You young rogue, Theaetetus — teaching your elders to break their promises! No, you should be getting ready to answer Socrates for the rest of the argument.

Theaetetus: Fine, if he wants me to. But I'd rather have heard about the doctrine of rest.

Theodorus: Inviting Socrates into an argument is like inviting cavalry onto open ground. Just ask him — he'll answer.

Socrates: Even so, Theodorus, I'm afraid I can't grant Theaetetus' request.

Theodorus: Can't grant it! Why not?

Socrates: Because I feel a kind of reverence — not so much for Melissus and the others who say "All is one and at rest," as for their great leader himself: Parmenides, "venerable and awe-inspiring," as Homer might put it. I'd be ashamed to approach him in any spirit unworthy of him. I met him once, when he was an old man and I was barely more than a boy, and he struck me as having a glorious depth of mind. I'm afraid we wouldn't understand his words, and would fall even further short of understanding his meaning. Most of all, I'm afraid that the nature of knowledge — the real subject of our discussion — would get shoved out of sight by the crowd of uninvited guests who'd come pouring into our feast of conversation if we opened that door. Besides, the question now stirring is enormous. Treat it in passing and you treat it unfairly; treat it fully and at length, and it eclipses the question of knowledge. We can't allow either. No — I have to use my midwife's art to deliver Theaetetus of the ideas he's carrying about knowledge.

Theaetetus: Very well. Go ahead, if that's what you think best.

Socrates: Then take another look at the subject, Theaetetus. You answered that knowledge is perception, right?

Theaetetus: I did.

Socrates: And if someone asked you, "With what does a person see black and white colors? With what does he hear high and low sounds?" — you'd say, if I'm not mistaken, "With the eyes and with the ears."

Theaetetus: I would.

Socrates: Now, using words loosely instead of with fussy precision is usually the mark of a well-educated mind — the opposite is pedantic. But sometimes precision is necessary, and I think the answer you just gave is, strictly speaking, incorrect. Which is more accurate: to say that we see and hear with the eyes and ears, or through the eyes and ears?

Theaetetus: "Through," Socrates, I'd say — rather than "with."

Socrates: Yes, my boy. Because nobody can seriously suppose that each of us is like a kind of Trojan horse with a crowd of disconnected senses perched inside, never meeting in any single nature. There must be something — call it the mind, or whatever you like — in which they all converge: something that uses the senses as instruments, and through them perceives whatever can be sensed.

Theaetetus: I agree with that.

Socrates: Here's why I'm being so precise. I want to know whether, when we perceive black and white through the eyes, and other qualities through other organs, we perceive them all with one and the same part of ourselves. If asked, you could refer all such perceptions to the body — but maybe I should let you answer for yourself instead of putting words in your mouth. Tell me: the organs through which you perceive warm and hard and light and sweet — they belong to the body, don't they?

Theaetetus: To the body, certainly.

Socrates: And you'd agree that what you perceive through one faculty, you can't perceive through another? You can't perceive the objects of hearing through sight, or the objects of sight through hearing?

Theaetetus: Of course not.

Socrates: So if you have a thought about both of them together, that common perception can't be coming to you through either organ alone?

Theaetetus: It can't.

Socrates: Now, about sounds and colors. First of all, you'd agree that they both exist?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And that each is different from the other and the same as itself?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: And that together they are two, and each one is one?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And you can also consider whether they're like or unlike each other?

Theaetetus: I suppose so.

Socrates: But through what do you perceive all this about them? Neither hearing nor sight can grasp what they have in common. Here's an illustration of the point. Suppose there were any sense in asking whether sounds and colors are salty or not — you could tell me which faculty would handle that question. Not sight, not hearing, but something else.

Theaetetus: Certainly — the faculty of taste.

Socrates: Very good. Now tell me: what is the power that discerns — not just in objects of sense but in everything — those universal notions like "being" and "not-being," and the others we were just asking about? What organs will you assign for perceiving these?

Theaetetus: You mean being and not-being, likeness and unlikeness, sameness and difference — and also unity and the other numbers we apply to things we sense. And you're asking through what bodily organ the soul perceives odd and even numbers and the other concepts of arithmetic.

Socrates: You follow me beautifully, Theaetetus. That's exactly what I'm asking.

Theaetetus: Honestly, Socrates, I can't answer — except that I don't think there's any special organ for these things, the way there is for objects of sense. It seems to me that the mind, by a power of its own, contemplates these universals in everything.

Socrates: You're beautiful, Theaetetus — not ugly, whatever Theodorus said — because the one who speaks beautifully is beautiful and good himself. And besides being beautiful, you've done me a great kindness: you've saved me from a very long discussion, if it's truly clear to you that the soul views some things by itself and others through the organs of the body. That was exactly my own view, and I wanted you to reach it on your own.

Theaetetus: It's quite clear to me.

Socrates: Then to which class do you assign being, or existence? Of all our notions, that's the most universal.

Theaetetus: To the class the soul reaches for by itself.

Socrates: And the same for like and unlike, same and different?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And what about noble and base, good and evil?

Theaetetus: Those seem to me to be essentially relative notions — ones the soul perceives by comparing, within itself, things past and present against the future.

Socrates: Hold on, though. The hardness of something hard — doesn't the soul perceive that through touch? And the softness of something soft, equally through touch?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: But their being — the fact that they exist, what the two of them are, their opposition to each other, and the nature of that opposition — these the soul tries to determine for us by itself, reviewing them and comparing them?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: So the simple sensations that reach the soul through the body — nature gives those at birth to humans and animals alike. But reflections about them — about their being and their usefulness — come slowly and with difficulty, if they come at all, through education and long experience.

Theaetetus: Absolutely.

Socrates: And can someone attain truth if he can't even attain being?

Theaetetus: Impossible.

Socrates: And if someone misses the truth about a thing, can he ever have knowledge of that thing?

Theaetetus: He can't.

Socrates: Then knowledge doesn't live in our sense impressions at all, but in our reasoning about them. There — and not in the raw impression — is where truth and being can be reached.

Theaetetus: Clearly.

Socrates: And would you call two processes by the same name when they're so different from each other?

Theaetetus: That certainly wouldn't be right.

Socrates: So what name would you give to seeing, hearing, smelling, feeling cold, feeling hot?

Theaetetus: I'd call all of them perceiving. What else could you call them?

Socrates: So "perception" is the collective name for all of them?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: Which, as we've said, has no share in reaching truth — since it has none in reaching being?

Theaetetus: None.

Socrates: And therefore no share in knowledge?

Theaetetus: No.

Socrates: Then perception, Theaetetus, can never be the same thing as knowledge?

Theaetetus: Clearly not, Socrates. And now knowledge has been proved, as plainly as anything could be, to be different from perception.

Socrates: But the original goal of our discussion was to find out what knowledge is — not what it isn't. Still, we've made progress: we now know not to look for knowledge in perception at all, but in that other activity — whatever we call it — where the mind is alone, occupied with being.

Theaetetus: You mean, Socrates, if I'm not mistaken, what's called thinking, or forming opinions.

Socrates: You've got it exactly. So now, my friend, start fresh from this point. Wipe everything that came before out of your memory and see if the view is any clearer from here. Tell me, once more: what is knowledge?

Theaetetus: I can't say that all opinion is knowledge, Socrates, because opinions can be false. But I'll venture this: knowledge is true opinion. Let that be my answer. If it gets disproved down the road, I'll try to find another.

Socrates: That's the way to answer, Theaetetus — boldly, not in your old hesitant style. If we're bold, one of two good things will happen: either we'll find what we're looking for, or we'll be less inclined to think we know what we don't know. Either way, we come out richer. So — what are you saying, exactly? That there are two kinds of opinion, one true and one false, and you define knowledge as the true kind?

Theaetetus: Yes. That's my view at the moment.

Socrates: Then is it worth picking up an old thread about opinion?

Theaetetus: Which thread do you mean?

Socrates: There's something that troubles me — often — and leaves me genuinely perplexed, both about myself and about others. I can't make out the nature or origin of this mental experience.

Theaetetus: What experience?

Socrates: How false opinion is even possible. The problem still nags at my mind's eye, and I'm torn between dropping the question and starting over on it in a new way.

Theaetetus: Start over, Socrates — if you think there's the slightest need. Weren't you and Theodorus just saying, quite rightly, that in discussions like this we can take all the time we want?

Socrates: Good point. And maybe there's no harm in retracing our steps and beginning again. Better to do a little well than a lot badly.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: Well then, what's the difficulty? We do speak of false opinion, don't we? We say one person holds a false opinion and another a true one, as though there were some real distinction between them.

Theaetetus: We certainly do.

Socrates: Now, everything whatsoever is either known or not known. (I'm setting aside the in-between cases of learning and forgetting — they don't matter for our present question.)

Theaetetus: With those excluded, Socrates, there's no doubt: a thing must be either known or not known.

Socrates: With that settled, mustn't we say that whoever holds an opinion holds it about something he either knows or doesn't know?

Theaetetus: He must.

Socrates: And if you know a thing, you can't not know it; if you don't know it, you can't know it?

Theaetetus: Of course.

Socrates: Then what happens with false opinion? When a man holds one, does he take something he knows to be some other thing he knows — so that knowing both, he's somehow ignorant of both?

Theaetetus: That's impossible, Socrates.

Socrates: Then does he take something he doesn't know for some other thing he doesn't know? For example: he knows neither Theaetetus nor Socrates, yet somehow gets it into his head that Theaetetus is Socrates, or Socrates is Theaetetus?

Theaetetus: How could he?

Socrates: But surely he can't take what he knows for what he doesn't know, or what he doesn't know for what he knows?

Theaetetus: That would be monstrous.

Socrates: Then where can false opinion be? If everything is either known or unknown, every opinion falls under one of those two headings — and false opinion gets squeezed out entirely.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Socrates: Then suppose we move the question out of the territory of knowing and not knowing, and into the territory of being and not-being.

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Socrates: Maybe the simple truth is this: whoever thinks what is not — thinks something that doesn't exist — necessarily thinks falsely, whatever else is going on in his mind.

Theaetetus: That sounds plausible, Socrates.

Socrates: Then suppose someone puts this to us, Theaetetus: "Is it possible for any man to think that which is not — either as a thing existing on its own, or as a property of something else?" And we answer, "Yes, he can — when he thinks something untrue." Is that what we'll say?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: But does anything parallel happen anywhere else?

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Socrates: Can a man see something — and yet see nothing?

Theaetetus: Impossible.

Socrates: Right. If he sees any one thing, he sees something that exists. Or do you suppose a "one" could ever be found among things that don't exist?

Theaetetus: I don't.

Socrates: So whoever sees some one thing, sees something that is?

Theaetetus: Clearly.

Socrates: And whoever hears anything, hears some one thing — and so hears something that is?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: And whoever touches anything, touches some one thing — which, being one, is?

Theaetetus: That's true too.

Socrates: And doesn't whoever thinks, think some one thing?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: And whoever thinks some one thing, thinks something that is?

Theaetetus: I agree.

Socrates: Then whoever thinks what is not, thinks nothing?

Theaetetus: Clearly.

Socrates: And whoever thinks nothing isn't thinking at all?

Theaetetus: Obviously.

Socrates: So no one can think what is not — neither as a thing existing on its own, nor as a property of something else?

Theaetetus: Clearly not.

Socrates: Then thinking falsely must be something different from thinking what is not?

Theaetetus: So it seems.

Socrates: Then false opinion has no existence in us — not in the realm of being, and not in the realm of knowledge?

Theaetetus: Apparently not.

Socrates: But maybe what we mean by this name is really something like the following.

Theaetetus: Like what?

Socrates: Maybe false opinion is a kind of mistaken-swapping of thoughts: a person makes an exchange in his mind and declares that one real thing is another real thing. That way, he's always thinking of something that is — but he's put one thing in the place of another. And since he's missed the target he was aiming at, we could fairly say he holds a false opinion.

Theaetetus: Now I think you've hit it exactly. When someone mentally swaps in "base" where "noble" belongs, or "noble" where "base" belongs — that's when he truly holds a false opinion.

Socrates: I see, Theaetetus, that your fear of me is gone and you're starting to look down on me.

Theaetetus: What makes you say that?

Socrates: You think, if I'm reading you right, that your phrase "truly false" is safe from attack — that I'll never ask whether there can be a swift that is slow, or a heavy that is light, or any other self-contradictory thing that operates not according to its own nature but according to its opposite's. But I'll let that go; I don't want to discourage you for no reason. So — you're satisfied that false opinion is this kind of mental swap, thinking one thing in place of another?

Theaetetus: I am.

Socrates: So on your view, it's possible for the mind to take one thing for another?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: But when the mind mixes them up like that, doesn't it have to have some conception of both objects, or at least one of them?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: Either at the same time, or one after the other?

Theaetetus: Right.

Socrates: And when you say "thinking," do you mean the same thing I mean?

Theaetetus: What do you mean by it?

Socrates: I mean the conversation the soul has with itself when it's considering something. I'm telling you this as someone who barely understands it himself — but the soul, when it thinks, seems to me to be simply talking: asking itself questions and answering them, saying yes and saying no. And when it finally reaches a decision — whether it gets there gradually or in a sudden leap — when it settles on one answer and stops doubting, that's what we call its opinion. So I'd say that forming an opinion is a kind of speaking, and an opinion is a statement spoken — not out loud to someone else, but silently to yourself. What do you think?

Theaetetus: I agree.

Socrates: Then when someone thinks one thing is another, he's saying to himself that one thing is the other?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: But think back: have you ever said to yourself that the noble is definitely shameful, or that the unjust is just? Or — here's the real test — have you ever tried to convince yourself that one thing is another thing? Even in your sleep, have you ever dared to tell yourself that odd is even, or anything like that?

Theaetetus: Never.

Socrates: And do you suppose anyone else — sane or insane — ever seriously tried to persuade himself that an ox is a horse, or that two is one?

Theaetetus: Certainly not.

Socrates: So if thinking is talking to yourself, then no one who is speaking and thinking about two things, holding both of them in his soul, will say or think that one of them is the other. And by the way — fond of arguing as you are, you'd better not quibble over the word "other" here (I mean, don't insist that "one" and "other" amount to the same thing). My point is simply this: nobody thinks the noble is shameful, or anything of the kind.

Theaetetus: I'll drop the word "other," Socrates. And I agree with you.

Socrates: So if a man has both things in his thoughts, he can't think that one of them is the other?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: And if he has only one of them in his mind and not the other, he can't think that one is the other either?

Theaetetus: True — because then he'd have to be grasping something that isn't in his thoughts at all.

Socrates: Then no one who has both objects in mind, or only one of them, can think that one is the other. So anyone who defines false opinion as "mistaking one thing for another" is talking nonsense. This route, like the one before it, leads nowhere — false opinion can't exist in us this way either.

Theaetetus: No.

Socrates: And yet, Theaetetus, if we don't admit that it exists, we'll be forced into some very strange conclusions.

Theaetetus: Like what?

Socrates: I won't tell you until I've tried looking at the problem from every angle. I'd be ashamed of us if, stuck in our confusion, we got forced into admitting the absurdities I have in mind. If we find a way out and escape them, then we can talk about them calmly as other people's problems, and the embarrassment won't stick to us. But if we end up completely stumped — well, then I suppose we'll have to swallow our pride and let the argument walk all over us, like seasick passengers getting stepped on by the sailors, and let it do whatever it wants with us. So listen, and I'll tell you where I think I see a way out.

Theaetetus: I'm listening.

Socrates: I think we were wrong to deny that a man can mistake something he knows for something he doesn't know. There is, in fact, a way that kind of mistake can happen.

Theaetetus: You mean what I suspected at the time — that I might know Socrates, and then see someone in the distance whom I don't know, and mistake him for you? That's how the error happens?

Socrates: But didn't we abandon that position, because it forced us into the absurdity of knowing and not knowing the very things we know?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: Then let's frame the claim differently. Maybe it will hold up, maybe it won't — but we're in a tight spot, and every argument deserves to be turned over and tested. Tell me: am I right that you can learn something you didn't know before?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: And then another thing, and another?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: Then here's what I want you to imagine. Suppose there's a block of wax in each person's mind. It's bigger in some people, smaller in others; harder in some, moister in others; purer in some — and in some, somewhere in between.

Theaetetus: I'm imagining it.

Socrates: Let's say this wax tablet is a gift from Memory, the mother of the Muses. Whenever we want to remember something we've seen or heard or thought up ourselves, we hold the wax under our perceptions and thoughts, and they stamp an impression into it, the way a signet ring stamps a seal. Whatever gets imprinted, we remember and know — as long as the image lasts. But when the image wears away, or never takes in the first place, we forget. We don't know it anymore.

Theaetetus: Very good.

Socrates: Now, take a person with this kind of knowledge stamped in his wax, who is considering something he sees or hears. Couldn't false opinion arise in the following way?

Theaetetus: What way?

Socrates: By his thinking that what he knows is, sometimes, what he knows — and sometimes, what he doesn't know. We were wrong before when we denied this was possible.

Theaetetus: So how would you fix our earlier statement?

Socrates: I'd start by listing the impossible cases — the ones we have to rule out. First: no one can mistake one thing for another when he isn't perceiving either of them but has the memory-seal of both stamped in his mind. Nor can he mix up two things when he knows only one of them and has no impression at all of the other. Nor can he think that one thing he doesn't know is another thing he doesn't know, or that something he doesn't know is something he knows. Second: he can't think that one thing he perceives is another thing he perceives; or that something he perceives is something he doesn't perceive; or that something he doesn't perceive is some other thing he doesn't perceive; or that something he doesn't perceive is something he does perceive. Third: he can't think that something he both knows and perceives — where his impression matches his perception exactly — is some other thing he also knows and perceives with the impression matching the perception. That case, if anything, is even more inconceivable than the others. Fourth: he can't think that something he knows and perceives, with the memory lined up correctly with the sensation, is some other thing he knows; nor, as long as everything lines up, can he think that a thing he knows and perceives is another thing he merely perceives. And a thing he neither knows nor perceives can't be confused with another thing he neither knows nor perceives; nor can a thing he neither knows nor perceives be confused with another thing he doesn't know, or with another thing he doesn't perceive. In all these cases, false opinion is absolutely and completely impossible. If it's possible anywhere, it's only in the cases that remain.

Theaetetus: And what are those? Tell me — maybe then I'll understand you better. Right now I can't follow you at all.

Socrates: The remaining cases are these: a person may think that things he knows are other things he knows and perceives; or things he doesn't know but perceives are things he knows and perceives; or things he both knows and perceives are other things he knows and perceives.

Theaetetus: Now I understand you even less than before.

Socrates: Then let me put it another way. I know Theodorus, and I hold in my memory what kind of person he is — same with you. But sometimes I see the two of you, and sometimes I don't; sometimes I touch you, sometimes not; sometimes I hear you or perceive you in some other way, and sometimes I have no perception of you at all — yet I still remember you, and I still know you in my own mind.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Socrates: So here's the first point I want you to grasp: a person can know something while perceiving it, or know it while not perceiving it.

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: And something he doesn't know — sometimes he won't perceive it either, and sometimes he'll perceive it and nothing more?

Theaetetus: That's also true.

Socrates: Now see if you can follow me. Suppose Socrates knows Theodorus and Theaetetus, but he sees neither of them, and has no other perception of them. In that case, there's no way he could ever form the thought, in his own mind, that Theaetetus is Theodorus. Am I right?

Theaetetus: Quite right.

Socrates: That was the first of the cases I described.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: The second case: knowing one of you but not the other, and perceiving neither, I could never think that the one I know is the one I don't know.

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: Third case: knowing neither of you and perceiving neither, I can't possibly think that one stranger is some other stranger. And you can take it that I've run through the whole catalog of excluded cases again — all the ones where I can't form a false opinion about you and Theodorus, whether I know both of you, or neither, or one and not the other. And the same goes for perceiving. You follow me?

Theaetetus: I do.

Socrates: So the only opening left for false opinion is this: I know you and I know Theodorus — I have impressions of both of you on my wax block, like seal-stamps from a ring. Then I see you both, but poorly, at a distance. I rush to match the right memory-impression to the right visual impression — to fit each sight into its own stamp, so to speak. If I succeed, recognition happens. But if I fail and swap them — putting the foot in the wrong shoe, fitting the sight of one of you onto the impression of the other — or if my mind slips the way mirrors do, flipping right to left, then mismatching happens, and false opinion is born.

Theaetetus: Yes, Socrates! That's a marvelously exact description of what happens to opinion.

Socrates: There's also the case where I know both of you, and perceive one but not the other, and my knowledge of the one I perceive doesn't line up with my perception — that was the case I described before that you couldn't follow.

Theaetetus: Right, I couldn't.

Socrates: What I was saying was this: if someone knows and perceives one of you, and his knowledge agrees with his perception, he'll never think that person is somebody else whom he also knows and perceives with knowledge agreeing with perception. That was the situation, wasn't it?

Theaetetus: True.

Socrates: But what we left out was the case we're describing now — where false opinion actually arises: when I know both of you, and see both of you (or have some other perception of both), but I fail to hold each memory-seal in line with its matching sensation. Like a bad archer, I shoot wide and miss the mark. And missing the mark, in fact, is exactly what we call being wrong — falsehood.

Theaetetus: And rightly so.

Socrates: So: when perception is present for one of the impressions but not for the other, and the mind fits the seal of the absent perception onto the perception that's actually present, the mind is deceived. In short, if our account is sound: about things a person has never known and never perceived, there can be no error, no deception at all. It's only among things we know and perceive that opinion turns and twists, coming out alternately true and false. True, when the impressions line up straight and square with their sensations. False, when they go crooked and land askew.

Theaetetus: Isn't that beautifully put, Socrates?

Socrates: Beautifully — well, wait until you hear the rest, and then you'll say so with even better reason. Because holding true opinions is a beautiful thing, and being deceived is an ugly one.

Theaetetus: No doubt about that.

Socrates: And here, supposedly, is where the difference comes from. When the wax in someone's soul is deep and plentiful, smooth and worked to the perfect consistency, then the things that come in through the senses stamp themselves into this "heart" of the soul — as Homer calls it, in a phrase that hints at the likeness between heart and wax (in the Greek, his word for "heart," ker, echoes keros, "wax"). In these souls the impressions come out clean and deep, and they last. People like this learn easily, remember well, and don't get their impressions confused. They think truly. Since the impressions are clear and there's plenty of room, they quickly file each one in its proper place on the block — and these are the people we call wise. Do you agree?

Theaetetus: Completely.

Socrates: But when someone's heart is shaggy — the condition our all-wise poet praises — or when the wax is muddy and impure, or too soft, or too hard, the mind has a matching defect. Soft-waxed people learn quickly but forget quickly; hard-waxed people are the reverse. Those whose wax is shaggy and rough and gritty, mixed with earth or filth, get blurry impressions. So do the hard-waxed, because their impressions have no depth — and the soft-waxed too, because their impressions smear together and rub away. And it's blurrier still when all the impressions are crammed and jostled together in some tiny soul with no room. These are the natures that breed false opinion. When they see or hear or think about anything, they're slow matching things to the right impressions; they misassign them, in their dullness, and so they see wrong, hear wrong, think wrong. And that's what we mean when we say someone is deceived about things and ignorant.

Theaetetus: No one could put it better, Socrates.

Socrates: So then — we can now say that false opinion exists in us?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Socrates: And true opinion too?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Socrates: At last we've established it, beyond any doubt: these two kinds of opinion exist?

Theaetetus: Absolutely.

Socrates: Oh, Theaetetus. What a tiresome creature a talkative man is!

Theaetetus: What? Why do you say that?

Socrates: Because I'm annoyed at my own slowness — at my endless chatter. What else can you call it, when a man drags his arguments back and forth in every direction, too dense to be convinced, and simply will not let a question go?

Theaetetus: But what's bothering you?

Socrates: I'm not just bothered — I'm in despair. I don't know what to say if someone asks me: "So, Socrates, you've discovered that false opinion arises neither in our perceptions, when compared with each other, nor in our thoughts, but precisely at the junction where perception meets thought?" "Yes," I'll have to say — beaming, no doubt, like a man who thinks he's made a great discovery.

Theaetetus: I don't see anything to be ashamed of in what we've just shown, Socrates.

Socrates: "So," he'll go on, "your claim is this: a man we merely think of but don't see can never be confused with a horse we also don't see or touch, but merely think of?" Yes, I'll have to say, that's exactly my claim.

Theaetetus: And quite right too.

Socrates: "Well then," he'll say, "by that argument, the number eleven — which is purely a thought — could never be mistaken for twelve, which is also purely a thought." How would you answer him?

Theaetetus: I'd say a person might easily make a mistake between eleven and twelve things he sees or handles — but about the eleven and twelve in his mind alone? No. There's no making that kind of mistake.

Socrates: Really? Do you think no one has ever set five and seven before his mind — not five or seven men or horses, I mean, but five and seven themselves, the abstract numbers, which we agreed are stamped on the wax block, the ones we said could never be the subject of false opinion — has no one ever asked himself what they add up to, with one person answering eleven, and another answering twelve? Or does everyone always think and say twelve?

Theaetetus: By Zeus, no — plenty of people say eleven. And with bigger numbers, they slip up even more. You're talking about numbers in general, I assume.

Socrates: Exactly. And now consider: when that happens, isn't the person taking the twelve on his wax block to be eleven?

Theaetetus: It does seem so.

Socrates: Then haven't we circled right back to our original problem? Whoever makes that mistake is thinking that one thing he knows is another thing he knows. And we said that was impossible — in fact, that very impossibility was our airtight proof that false opinion couldn't exist, since otherwise the same person would have to know and not know the same thing at the same time.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Socrates: So false opinion can't be explained as a mismatch between thought and perception after all — because if that were the whole story, we could never go wrong about pure thoughts. Which leaves us stuck: either false opinion doesn't exist, or it's possible for a man not to know what he knows. Which option do you prefer?

Theaetetus: That's an impossible choice, Socrates.

Socrates: And yet the argument won't let us have both. Still — since we're completely stuck — what if we did something shameless?

Theaetetus: Like what?

Socrates: Like trying to explain what "knowing" actually means.

Theaetetus: What's shameless about that?

Socrates: You don't seem to realize that our entire conversation, from the very beginning, has been a hunt for knowledge — on the assumption that we don't know what it is.

Theaetetus: No, I realize that.

Socrates: Then isn't it brazen of us — not knowing what knowledge is — to go around explaining what "to know" means? The truth is, Theaetetus, our whole discussion has been contaminated from the start. We've said "we know" and "we don't know," "we have knowledge" and "we don't have knowledge," a thousand times, as if we understood each other perfectly while still being in the dark about what knowledge even is. In fact, look — even at this very moment I've used the words "understand" and "in the dark," as if we were entitled to those words when knowledge is exactly the thing we're missing.

Theaetetus: But if you ban all those expressions, Socrates, how will you argue at all?

Socrates: I couldn't — not being the man I am. If I were a true champion of dialectic, things would be different. If only such a man were here!
