Theaetetus: I'd say, Stranger, that what you're describing isn't the teaching of practical skills, but what we here in Greece have come to call education.

Stranger: Yes, Theaetetus, and nearly all Greeks call it that. But we still need to consider whether education can be divided further.

Theaetetus: We do.

Stranger: I think it can be divided at a certain point.

Theaetetus: Where?

Stranger: There's a rougher method of education and a smoother one.

Theaetetus: How do we tell them apart?

Stranger: There's the old-fashioned approach our fathers commonly used with their sons, and which many still use -- either scolding them harshly for their mistakes, or advising them gently. Both of these fall under the general heading of admonition.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: But some people have concluded that all ignorance is involuntary -- that nobody who thinks he's already clever will ever be willing to learn the very things he's convinced he already knows. And they've noticed that the admonishing approach, no matter how much effort it takes, doesn't accomplish much.

Theaetetus: They're quite right about that.

Stranger: So these people set about rooting out the spirit of false confidence in a different way.

Theaetetus: What way?

Stranger: They cross-examine a person's stated beliefs. When he thinks he's saying something meaningful but is actually saying nothing coherent, they easily expose the contradictions in his opinions. They collect these contradictions through the dialectical process, lay them side by side, and show that the person holds opposing views about the same things, in the same respects, at the same time. The person, seeing this, becomes angry with himself and gentler toward others. He's entirely freed from the big prejudices and rigid opinions that had been blocking him. And the process is wonderfully entertaining for anyone listening, while producing the most lasting benefit for the person undergoing it.

Here's the principle, Theaetetus: just as a doctor knows the body won't benefit from food until internal blockages are removed, the purifier of the soul understands that his patient won't benefit from new knowledge until he's been shown his errors. Through refutation, the patient learns humility. He has to be purged of his false beliefs first and brought to think he knows only what he actually knows -- nothing more.

Theaetetus: That's certainly the best and wisest state of mind.

Stranger: For all these reasons, Theaetetus, we have to say that refutation is the greatest and most important of all purifications. A person who hasn't been refuted -- even if he's the Great King of Persia himself -- lives in a terrible state of impurity. He's uneducated and deformed in exactly the areas where someone who wants to be truly fulfilled ought to be at their best.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Stranger: And who practices this art? I'm almost afraid to say: the sophists.

Theaetetus: Why afraid?

Stranger: Because I don't want to give them too much credit.

Theaetetus: And yet the sophist does bear a certain resemblance to this practitioner of purification.

Stranger: Yes -- the same kind of resemblance a wolf has to a dog. Wolves are the fiercest of animals, dogs the gentlest. Anyone who wants to avoid mistakes had better be very careful with comparisons, because they're extremely slippery. But let's go ahead and assume the sophists are these practitioners. I say it provisionally, because I think the line between them will become clear enough if we're careful.

Theaetetus: Likely enough.

Stranger: So let's grant this: starting from the art of discrimination comes purification. From purification we separate off the kind concerned with the soul. Within soul-purification, there's instruction. Within instruction, there's education. And within education, there's the refutation of empty self-conceit -- which our present argument has uncovered. Let's call this the noble art of sophistry.

Theaetetus: Fair enough. But given how many forms the sophist has appeared in, I'm starting to wonder how I can say with any confidence what the sophist really is.

Stranger: You're naturally confused. But he must be even more confused trying to escape us. As the proverb says, when every way is blocked, there's no getting out. Now is the time to press our attack.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: But first, let's pause and catch our breath. While we're resting, let's count up how many forms he's appeared in. First, he turned out to be a paid hunter of wealthy young men.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: Second, he was a wholesaler of intellectual goods -- food for the soul.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: Third, he showed up as a retailer of the same merchandise.

Theaetetus: Yes, and fourth, he turned out to manufacture the intellectual wares he sells.

Stranger: Right. And the fifth -- I'll try to remember it myself. He belonged to the combat class, specifically as a champion of debate who practiced eristic argumentation.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: The sixth was doubtful, but we eventually agreed: he was a purifier of souls, clearing away beliefs that obstruct knowledge.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Stranger: Now, do you see the problem? When someone who's supposed to be a practitioner of one skill has this many names, something's wrong. The fact that so many names get applied to him shows that the common principle underlying all these branches of activity isn't understood.

Theaetetus: I'd think so.

Stranger: Well, we're going to understand him, and no laziness is going to stop us. Let's start again and reexamine some of our findings about the sophist. There was one trait that struck me as especially characteristic.

Theaetetus: What are you referring to?

Stranger: We said he was a disputer, didn't we?

Theaetetus: We did.

Stranger: And doesn't he also teach others the art of disputation?

Theaetetus: He certainly does.

Stranger: And what does he claim to teach people to dispute about? Let's start at the beginning. Does he make them able to argue about divine matters -- things that are invisible to ordinary people?

Theaetetus: He's certainly said to do so.

Stranger: And what about the visible things -- the heavens, the earth, and everything like that?

Theaetetus: He disputes about those and teaches others to do the same.

Stranger: And in private conversation, whenever some general claim is made about coming-into-being or existence itself, we know these people are tremendous arguers who can pass their skill along to others.

Theaetetus: Without a doubt.

Stranger: And don't they claim to make people able to argue about law and politics in general?

Theaetetus: Of course -- nobody would bother with them if they didn't make that claim.

Stranger: In every craft and profession, what the expert should say in response to any question has been written down in accessible form, and anyone who wants to can learn it.

Theaetetus: I take it you're talking about the kind of instructional manuals that Protagoras wrote about wrestling and other skills?

Stranger: Yes, my friend, and about a great many other things. In fact, isn't the art of disputation basically the power to argue about everything?

Theaetetus: Certainly. Nothing seems to be left out.

Stranger: But my dear young friend -- do you really think that's possible? Your young eyes may see things that our duller vision misses.

Theaetetus: What are you getting at? I'm not sure I follow your question.

Stranger: What I'm asking is: can anyone actually know everything?

Theaetetus: Humanity would be blessed if such a thing were possible!

Socrates: But how can someone who's ignorant argue rationally against someone who actually knows?

Theaetetus: He can't.

Stranger: Then what is this mysterious power of the sophist's art?

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Stranger: How do sophists make young people believe they're supremely wise about everything? Because if they didn't actually dispute, or at least appear to dispute well -- and if they didn't seem wiser for their argumentative skill -- then, as you pointed out, nobody would pay them money or want to study with them.

Theaetetus: They certainly wouldn't.

Stranger: But people are willing.

Theaetetus: They are.

Stranger: Because, I'd imagine, the sophists appear to have knowledge of the things they argue about.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: And they argue about everything?

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: So to their students, they seem to be all-knowing?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: But they're not. We've shown that's impossible.

Theaetetus: Impossible, of course.

Stranger: So the sophist has been shown to possess a kind of apparent knowledge, a semblance of expertise about everything -- not the truth.

Theaetetus: Exactly. No better description of him could be given.

Stranger: Let's try an analogy that will make his nature even clearer.

Theaetetus: What is it?

Stranger: I'll tell you, and please pay very close attention. Suppose someone claimed -- not just that he could argue about everything, but that through a single skill he could actually make and do everything.

Theaetetus: Everything?

Stranger: I see you don't catch my meaning right away. You don't understand the scope of "everything."

Theaetetus: No, I don't.

Stranger: By "everything," I include you and me, and also animals and trees.

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Stranger: Suppose someone said he'd make you, me, and every living creature.

Theaetetus: What would "make" mean? He can't be a farmer -- you said he's a maker of animals.

Stranger: Yes, and I'm saying he also makes the sea, the earth, the heavens, the gods, and everything else. What's more, he can make them all in no time and sell them for a few coins.

Theaetetus: That has to be a joke.

Stranger: And when someone says he knows everything and can teach it all to another person cheaply and quickly -- isn't that also a joke?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: And is there any more artful or elegant form of joking than imitation?

Theaetetus: No. And imitation is an incredibly broad category, covering the most diverse kinds of things.

Stranger: Now, we know that a person who claims to make everything through one skill is really a painter. He creates images of real things that share the same names as the originals. And he can fool the less sophisticated sort of children by showing them his pictures from a distance, making them believe he can create anything he wants.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: Well, couldn't there be an imitative art of reasoning too? Isn't it possible to enchant the minds of young people by pouring words into their ears when they're still far from the truth of things -- showing them verbal illusions and making them think those arguments are real, and that the speaker is the wisest of all people on every subject?

Theaetetus: Yes, why shouldn't there be such an art?

Stranger: But as time goes on, and the listeners grow older and come into closer contact with reality, and learn through painful experience to see and feel how things actually are -- aren't most of them forced to reverse many opinions they once held? Doesn't what seemed great look small, what seemed easy look hard, and all their dreamy speculations get overturned by the facts of life?

Theaetetus: That's how it seems to me, as far as I can judge -- though at my age, I might still be one of those seeing things from a distance.

Stranger: And all of us who are your friends want nothing more than to bring you as close to the truth as possible, without the painful reality of learning it the hard way. Now tell me: isn't the sophist clearly a kind of magician, an imitator of real things? Or are we still inclined to think he might have genuine knowledge of all the subjects he argues about?

Theaetetus: But how could he, Stranger? After everything we've said, isn't it clear he belongs in one of the categories of children's play?

Stranger: Then we have to classify him as a magician and a mimic.

Theaetetus: We certainly do.

Stranger: And now our job is to not let the animal escape. We've got him in a kind of dialectical net, and there's one conclusion he definitely won't get around.

Theaetetus: What's that?

Stranger: That he's a kind of illusionist.

Theaetetus: That's exactly my own view of him.

Stranger: So we need to divide the image-making art as quickly as possible and go deeper into the net. If the sophist doesn't slip away, we'll seize him according to our orders and hand him over to reason -- the lord of this hunt -- and announce his capture. And if he creeps into the corners of the imitative art and hides in one of its subdivisions, we'll divide again and track him down until we catch him. Our method of cornering any quarry is one that neither he nor any other creature will ever escape.

Theaetetus: Well said. Let's do it.

Stranger: All right, following the same analytical method as before, I think I can make out two divisions of the imitative art. But I can't yet see which one our target is hiding in.

Theaetetus: Tell me first what the two divisions are.

Stranger: One is the art of making likenesses. A likeness is generally produced by creating a copy that matches the original's proportions -- the same length, breadth, and depth, with the right colors assigned to each part.

Theaetetus: Isn't that always what imitation aims at?

Stranger: Not always. In large works of sculpture or painting, there's a deliberate element of deception. If artists used the true proportions of their beautiful works, the upper parts -- being farther away -- would look too small compared to the lower parts, which are closer to the viewer. So they abandon the real proportions and create only the ones that look right, ignoring accuracy.

Theaetetus: Quite true.

Stranger: Now, something that's different from the original but resembles it -- can't we fairly call it a likeness or image?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And the part of the imitative art that makes such faithful copies -- can we call it the art of likeness-making?

Theaetetus: Let's call it that.

Stranger: And what should we call those imitations that only look like the beautiful original because of the viewer's unfavorable position -- where if someone could get a proper look at them, they wouldn't even resemble what they claim to represent? Since they only appear to be likenesses without actually being accurate, shouldn't we call them "appearances"?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: And there's a great deal of this in painting and in all imitation.

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: The art that produces an appearance rather than a true likeness -- can we fairly call it the art of appearance-making, or the fantastic art?

Theaetetus: Most fairly.

Stranger: So these are the two kinds of image-making: the art of making likenesses, and the fantastic art -- the art of making mere appearances.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: I was uncertain before about which of them to place the sophist in, and I'm still not able to see clearly. He really is a remarkable and baffling creature. He's managed to slip into an impossible position.

Theaetetus: He certainly has.

Stranger: Are you speaking from real understanding, or are you just reflexively agreeing out of habit?

Theaetetus: What are you referring to?

Stranger: My dear friend, we're dealing with a very deep problem here -- no question about that. How can something appear and seem to be, yet not actually be? And how can someone say something that isn't true? These have always been, and still remain, deeply puzzling questions. Because, Theaetetus, how can anyone claim that falsehood genuinely exists without getting tangled in contradictions? The task is genuinely difficult.

Theaetetus: Why?

Stranger: Because anyone who says falsehood exists is boldly asserting that not-being has being. That's what's implied by the very possibility of falsehood. But when I was a young man, the great Parmenides protested against this idea, and he kept saying it until the end of his life -- repeating it in both verse and prose:

"Keep your mind from this way of inquiry, for you will never show that not-being is."

That's his testimony, and when you examine the words carefully, it holds up. Would you mind starting by considering the words themselves?

Theaetetus: Don't worry about me. Just carry the argument wherever it needs to go, and take me with you.

Stranger: Good. Now tell me: do we dare to utter the forbidden words, "not-being"?

Theaetetus: Of course we do.

Stranger: Then let's be serious about this -- no quarreling, no playing around. Suppose one of Parmenides' followers were asked: "What exactly should the term 'not-being' be applied to?" Do you know what sort of thing he'd point to in response?

Theaetetus: That's a hard question -- way too hard for someone like me.

Stranger: Well, one thing is clear: you can't apply the label "not-being" to anything that exists.

Theaetetus: Certainly not.

Stranger: And if not to anything that exists, then not to something.

Theaetetus: Of course not.

Stranger: And it's also clear that when we say "something," we're talking about a thing that exists. To speak of a bare, abstract "something" stripped of all existence is impossible.

Theaetetus: Impossible.

Stranger: Do you see what you're implying by agreeing? Whoever says "something" must be saying some one thing?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: Because "something" in the singular points to one thing, "a pair of somethings" points to two, and "some things" in the plural points to many.

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: Then whoever says "not-something" must be saying absolutely nothing.

Theaetetus: That has to be right.

Stranger: And since we can't allow that a person speaks while saying nothing, whoever says "not-being" doesn't really speak at all.

Theaetetus: The difficulty of the argument can't go any further than that.

Stranger: Don't declare victory yet, my friend. There's still the biggest and most fundamental difficulty remaining.

Theaetetus: What do you mean? Don't hold back.

Stranger: To something that exists, you can attach some other existing thing, right?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: But can anything that exists be attached to what doesn't exist?

Theaetetus: Impossible.

Stranger: And number is something that exists?

Theaetetus: Yes -- if anything has real existence, number does.

Stranger: Then we must not try to attribute number -- whether singular or plural -- to not-being.

Theaetetus: The argument does seem to say we'd be wrong to do that.

Stranger: But how could anyone even express in words, or even think about, things that don't exist or a thing that doesn't exist, without number?

Theaetetus: How indeed?

Stranger: When we say "things that are not," aren't we attaching plurality to not-being?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: And when we say "what is not," aren't we attaching unity?

Theaetetus: Clearly.

Stranger: And yet we maintain that you shouldn't and can't attach being to not-being.

Theaetetus: Absolutely true.

Stranger: Do you see, then? Not-being in itself can't be spoken, uttered, or thought. It's unthinkable, unsayable, unpronounceable, indescribable.

Theaetetus: Completely true.

Stranger: But if that's right, then I was wrong a moment ago when I said the biggest difficulty was still coming.

Theaetetus: What? There's an even bigger one?

Stranger: I'm amazed that, after everything we've said, you don't see the bind that anyone who tries to refute the idea of not-being gets caught in. He's forced to contradict himself the moment he tries.

Theaetetus: What do you mean? Spell it out.

Stranger: Don't expect perfect clarity from me on this. I'm the one who claims not-being has no share in either oneness or plurality -- yet just now I said, and I'm still saying, "not-being" -- as if it were one thing. Do you follow?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And a little while ago I said that not-being is "unsayable, unpronounceable, indescribable." You're keeping up?

Theaetetus: More or less.

Stranger: But when I said "is," didn't I contradict myself?

Theaetetus: Clearly.

Stranger: And in using a singular verb, wasn't I treating not-being as one thing?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And when I called it "indescribable," "unsayable," and "unpronounceable" -- using each of those singular words -- wasn't I referring to not-being as if it were a single thing each time?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: And yet we say that strictly speaking, not-being shouldn't be defined as one or many. It shouldn't even be called "it," since using the word "it" implies a form of unity.

Theaetetus: Exactly right.

Stranger: So how can anyone trust what I'm saying? Here, as always, I'm failing at the refutation of not-being. So as I said, don't look to me for the right way to talk about not-being. Come on -- let's try with you instead.

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Stranger: Summon your courage, as a young person should. Try with all your might to say something about not-being in a proper way -- without smuggling in existence, unity, or plurality.

Theaetetus: It would take remarkable boldness to make the attempt after watching you struggle like this.

Stranger: Fine, let's leave ourselves out of it. But until we find someone who can talk about not-being without using number, we have to admit that the sophist is a clever rogue who won't be flushed from his hiding place.

Theaetetus: Absolutely true.

Stranger: And if we tell him he practices an art of creating appearances, he'll grab our argument and turn it against us. When we call him an image-maker, he'll say: "What exactly do you mean by an image?" And I'd like to know, Theaetetus, how we'd answer that question.

Theaetetus: We'd obviously point to images reflected in water or in mirrors, and to sculptures, paintings, and other copies.

Stranger: Theaetetus, I can see you've never actually dealt with a real sophist.

Theaetetus: Why do you say that?

Stranger: He'll pretend his eyes are shut, or that he doesn't have any.

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Stranger: When you point to things in mirrors or carvings and talk to him as if he has eyes, he'll laugh at you. He'll claim to know nothing about mirrors, streams, or even sight itself. He'll say he's only asking about the idea.

Theaetetus: What idea?

Stranger: The common quality running through all these things you're listing as "many," yet calling by the single name "image" -- as though they were all one thing. So how will you hold your ground against him?

Theaetetus: How else, Stranger, can I define an image except as something shaped in the likeness of the real thing?

Stranger: And by "the real thing," you mean some other true thing? Or what?

Theaetetus: Not another true thing -- just a resemblance.

Stranger: And by "true" you mean what really exists?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And "not true" is the opposite of true?

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: So a resemblance isn't really real, since you say it's not true?

Theaetetus: Well, it is real in a certain sense.

Stranger: You mean, not in a genuinely true sense?

Theaetetus: Right. It's really only an image.

Stranger: So what we call an image is something that's really... not real.

Theaetetus: What a strange tangle of being and not-being we've gotten ourselves into!

Stranger: Strange indeed! See how this many-headed sophist has forced us -- completely against our will -- to admit that not-being somehow exists?

Theaetetus: Yes, I see it now.

Stranger: The difficulty is this: how do we define his art without falling into contradiction?

Theaetetus: What do you mean? Where's the danger?

Stranger: When we say he deceives us with illusions, and that his art is illusory -- do we mean that his art leads our souls to think what's false? Or what else could we mean?

Theaetetus: That's exactly what we mean. What else could it be?

Stranger: And false opinion is opinion that thinks the opposite of the truth? You'd agree?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: So you're saying that false opinion thinks what is not.

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: Does false opinion think that things which don't exist absolutely don't exist -- or that in some sense they do?

Theaetetus: Things that don't exist must be imagined to exist in some sense, if any degree of falsehood is going to be possible.

Stranger: And doesn't false opinion also think that things which definitely do exist don't exist at all?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And that too is falsehood?

Theaetetus: Yes -- falsehood.

Stranger: And along the same lines, a false statement would be one that asserts the nonexistence of things that exist, or the existence of things that don't exist.

Theaetetus: There's no other way a false statement could arise.

Stranger: There isn't. But the sophist will deny all of this. And really, how can any reasonable person accept it, when we ourselves acknowledged just a moment ago that not-being is unsayable, unpronounceable, unthinkable, and indescribable? Do you see his point, Theaetetus?

Theaetetus: Of course. He'll say we're contradicting ourselves by claiming that falsehood exists in opinion and speech. Because to maintain that, we'd have to keep asserting that not-being has being -- which we just admitted is utterly impossible.

Stranger: You remember well! And now it's time to figure out what to do about the sophist. Because if we keep looking for him in the category of illusion-workers and magicians, you can see that the objections and difficulties will be endless.

Theaetetus: They certainly will.

Stranger: We've barely gone through a fraction of them, and they really are infinite.

Theaetetus: If that's the case, we'll never catch the sophist.

Stranger: So should we lose heart and give up?

Theaetetus: Absolutely not -- not if there's even the slightest chance of getting hold of him.

Stranger: Then you'll forgive me if I flinch a little in the grip of such a powerful argument?

Theaetetus: Of course I will.

Stranger: I have an even more urgent request to make.

Theaetetus: What is it?

Stranger: Promise you won't think of me as a parricide.

Theaetetus: Why would I?

Stranger: Because in self-defense, I'm going to have to put my father Parmenides' philosophy to the test. I'm going to have to prove, by main force, that in a certain sense not-being is -- and that being, on the other hand, in a certain sense is not.

Theaetetus: That kind of attempt is clearly necessary.

Stranger: Yes -- even a blind man could see that. Unless these questions are settled one way or another, nobody who talks about false words, false opinions, images, imitations, or appearances -- or about any of the arts that deal with them -- can avoid falling into ridiculous contradictions.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Stranger: And that's why I have to take on my father's argument. If I'm too squeamish about it, I'll have to abandon the whole inquiry.

Theaetetus: Nothing in the world should make us do that.

Stranger: I have one more small request.

Theaetetus: What?

Stranger: You heard me say -- and I've always felt this way -- that I have no stomach for this argument?

Theaetetus: I did.

Stranger: I'm trembling at the thought of what I've said, and I expect you'll think I've lost my mind when you see how I keep shifting and changing direction. But understand: I'm pursuing this investigation entirely for your sake.

Theaetetus: Don't worry. There's nothing improper about attempting this refutation and proof. Take heart and proceed.

Stranger: Then where do I begin this perilous enterprise? I think the road I need to take is this --

Theaetetus: Which road? Tell me.

Stranger: I think we need to start by reexamining the things currently taken for granted. We may have fallen into confusion and been too quick to agree with each other, imagining we had clarity when we didn't.

Theaetetus: Say more precisely what you mean.

Stranger: I think Parmenides, and everyone who's ever tried to determine the number and nature of the things that exist, talked to us rather casually.

Theaetetus: How so?

Stranger: As if we were children, and they were each telling us their own favorite story. One said there are three fundamental principles, that sometimes they're at war and sometimes at peace -- they marry and have children and raise them. Another said there are two principles -- wet and dry, or hot and cold -- and made them marry and live together. Our own Eleatic school, in our part of the world, says that all things are many in name but one in nature -- that's their story, going back to Xenophanes and even earlier.

Then there are Ionian thinkers, and more recently Sicilian ones, who decided it's safer to combine both views and say that being is both one and many, held together by enmity and friendship -- always separating, always coming back together. The sterner school insists on perpetual strife and reconciliation. The gentler school allows for some relaxation and alternation -- sometimes peace and unity prevail under Aphrodite, sometimes multiplicity and war under a principle of conflict.

Whether any of them got it right is hard to say. And ancient, famous thinkers deserve respect -- they shouldn't face frivolous accusations. But one thing can be said about all of them without offense --

Theaetetus: What's that?

Stranger: They went on their way without bothering about people like us. They didn't care whether they were bringing us along or leaving us behind.

Theaetetus: How do you mean?

Stranger: I mean that when they talk about one, two, or more elements that exist or come into being, or about heat mixing with cold, or about separations and mixtures somewhere else in their systems -- tell me, Theaetetus, do you actually understand what they mean by all this? When I was younger, I used to think I understood perfectly well what "not-being" meant -- the very topic we're struggling with right now. And look what a mess we're in over it.

Theaetetus: I see.

Stranger: And quite possibly we're in the same confusion about "being." We might think we understand it perfectly whenever anyone uses the word, while actually being just as ignorant about it as we are about not-being.

Theaetetus: That's possible.

Stranger: And the same goes for all the other terms we just mentioned.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: We can come back to most of them later. But right now, let's tackle the chief term -- the leader of the pack.

Theaetetus: Which one? You clearly think we need to start by investigating what people mean by "being."

Stranger: You're right on my heels, Theaetetus. Here's the approach I have in mind. Let's summon the dualists -- the philosophers who say there are two fundamental principles -- and question them.

"Come," we'll say, "you who claim that hot and cold, or any other pair, constitute all of reality -- what is this word 'are' that you apply to both of them? What do you mean when you say both of them 'are'? How should we understand this? Are we to suppose there's a third thing -- 'being' -- over and above the two, making three in all instead of two?

"Because you obviously can't say that one of your two principles is being while the other isn't -- yet claim both equally exist. If you identified being with just one of the two, that one would contain the other, and they'd end up being one thing, not two."

Theaetetus: Very true.

Stranger: "But maybe you mean to call them both together 'being'?"

Theaetetus: Quite likely.

Stranger: "But then, friends," we'd reply, "those two are still going to collapse into one."

Theaetetus: Exactly right.

Stranger: "So since we're stuck, please just tell us what you mean when you say 'being.' You've clearly always understood your own meaning, while we only thought we understood you. But now we're at a total loss. Please start by explaining this, and don't let us go on thinking we understand you when we completely don't."

There'd be nothing wrong with demanding this answer from the dualists or the pluralists, would there?

Theaetetus: Not at all.

Stranger: And what about those who say everything is one? Don't we need to find out what they mean by "being" too?

Theaetetus: Absolutely.

Stranger: Then let them answer this: "You say only one thing exists?" "Yes," they'll reply.

Theaetetus: Right.

Stranger: "And there's something you call 'being'?"

Theaetetus: "Yes."

Stranger: "Is 'being' the same as 'one'? Are you using two names for the same thing?"

Theaetetus: What will they say, Stranger?

Stranger: It's clear, Theaetetus, that anyone who holds the unity of being will have a hard time answering this -- or any other question.

Theaetetus: Why?

Stranger: Admitting two names while insisting there's nothing but unity is pretty ridiculous, isn't it?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: And it's equally irrational to admit that a name exists as something real.

Theaetetus: How so?

Stranger: Because distinguishing the name from the thing implies duality.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And yet if you identify the name with the thing, you're forced to say the name is a name of nothing. Or if you say it's the name of something, the name turns out to be merely the name of a name, and of nothing else.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And "the one" will turn out to be merely the one of one -- and being, as absolute unity, will amount to nothing but a bare name.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: Now, would they say "the whole" is something different from "the one that is," or the same?

Theaetetus: They'd certainly answer -- and they actually do say it's the same.

Stranger: But if being is a whole -- as Parmenides sings:

"Every way like the fullness of a well-rounded sphere, equally balanced from the center on every side, and neither greater nor less in any way, neither on this side nor on that" --

then being has a center and extremities. And if it has those, it must have parts.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: Now, something that has parts could still have the attribute of unity across all its parts. Being everything and being a whole, it could still in that way be one.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: But something in that condition can't be absolute unity, can it?

Theaetetus: Why not?

Stranger: Because what is truly one, according to right reason, must be absolutely indivisible.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: But something made up of many parts while being called indivisible -- that contradicts reason.

Theaetetus: I understand.

Stranger: So should we say being is one and a whole because it has the attribute of unity? Or should we say being isn't a whole at all?

Theaetetus: That's a hard choice.

Stranger: It really is. Because being, while having in a certain sense the attribute of oneness, turns out not to be the same as "the one." So "the all" is more than one.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And yet if being isn't a whole -- if it merely has the attribute of unity, and if wholeness itself exists as something real -- then being falls short of itself. It lacks something.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: And on this reasoning, being, by lacking being, will become not-being.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And once again, "the all" becomes more than one, because being and the whole will each have their own separate nature.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger

But if the whole doesn't exist at all, then all the same difficulties remain -- plus a new one. Because besides having no being, being could never have come into being.

Theaetetus: Why not?

Stranger: Because whatever comes into being always comes into being as a whole. So anyone who doesn't count wholeness among the things that exist can't speak of either existence or coming-into-being at all.

Theaetetus: Yes, that certainly seems right.

Stranger: And here's another thing: how can something that isn't a whole have any specific quantity? Because whatever has a certain quantity must be the whole of that quantity.

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: There will be countless other problems too, each generating endless headaches for anyone who says being is either one or two.

Theaetetus: The difficulties that are now coming into view prove that. Each objection links to the next, and they keep dragging everything that came before into greater and worse confusion.

Stranger: We're far from having exhausted the more rigorous thinkers who deal with being and not-being. But let's leave them and move on to those who speak less precisely. What we'll find, as a result of examining all of them, is that the nature of being is just as hard to grasp as that of not-being.

Theaetetus: Then let's go to the others.

Stranger: There seems to be a kind of War of Gods and Giants going on among them. They're fighting with each other about the nature of reality.

Theaetetus: How so?

Stranger: Some of them are dragging everything down from heaven and the unseen world to earth. They literally grasp rocks and trees in their hands. They hold onto these things and stubbornly insist that only what you can touch and handle has being or reality. They define being and body as one and the same. If anyone else says that something without a body exists, they utterly despise him and refuse to listen to anything but body.

Theaetetus: I've met men like that. They're a rough bunch.

Stranger: And that's exactly why their opponents defend themselves from above, from an unseen world. They argue fiercely that true reality consists of certain intelligible, incorporeal Forms. The bodies that the materialists claim are the ultimate truth, they break to pieces with their arguments and call them not real being, but just becoming -- a process of generation and motion. Between these two armies, Theaetetus, there's been an endless battle raging.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: Let's ask each side in turn to give an account of what they mean by reality.

Theaetetus: How are we going to get it out of them?

Stranger: With the people who locate reality in the Forms, it'll be less difficult -- they're civilized enough. But getting an answer out of the ones who drag everything down to matter is going to be very hard -- maybe impossible. Here's what we should do.

Theaetetus: What?

Stranger: If we could actually improve them, we would. But if that's impossible, let's at least imagine them as better and more reasonable than they are -- more willing to answer according to the rules of argument. After all, what better people agree to has more weight than what worse people agree to. And we're not interested in people -- we're seeking truth.

Theaetetus: Very good.

Stranger: Then let's address our improved materialists and ask them to state their views. You can interpret for them.

Theaetetus: Agreed.

Stranger: Would they admit that there's such a thing as a mortal animal?

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: And they'd acknowledge that a mortal animal is a body that has a soul?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: Meaning the soul is something that exists?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And they'd say that one soul is just and another unjust, that one soul is wise and another foolish?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: And that a soul becomes just and wise by possessing justice and wisdom, and the opposite by possessing their opposites?

Theaetetus: Yes, they'd say that.

Stranger: But surely they'd admit that anything which can be present or absent is something that exists?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: So they grant that justice, wisdom, the other virtues, and their opposites exist, and that a soul in which they reside exists. But would they say any of these things are visible and tangible, or are they all invisible?

Theaetetus: They'd say that hardly any of them are visible.

Stranger: And would they say they're corporeal -- that they have bodies?

Theaetetus: They wouldn't give a single answer to that. They'd say the soul does have a body. But as for the qualities themselves -- justice, wisdom, and the rest -- they wouldn't dare either deny that they exist or insist that they're all bodily things.

Stranger: Well, Theaetetus, I see genuine improvement in them! The real hard-liners -- the original earth-born types, children of the dragon's teeth -- wouldn't have shown any shame at all. They'd have stubbornly insisted that nothing exists that they can't squeeze in their hands.

Theaetetus: That's pretty much their attitude.

Stranger: Let's push the question further. If they'll admit that even the smallest fraction of being is incorporeal, that's enough. They then need to say what's the common nature shared by both corporeal and incorporeal things -- what they have in mind when they say both of them "are." They may be stuck. And if so, there's a possibility they'll accept a proposal of ours about the nature of being, since they don't have one of their own to offer.

Theaetetus: What's the proposal? Tell me, and we'll see.

Stranger: My proposal is this: anything whatsoever that has any capacity at all to affect something else, or to be affected by something else -- even for a single moment, even if the cause is trivial and the effect is slight -- that thing has real existence. I'm putting forward this definition: being is simply power.

Theaetetus: They accept your proposal, since they've got nothing better to offer.

Stranger: Good. Maybe we'll change our minds about this later -- and so might they. But for now, let's treat this as our working agreement with them.

Theaetetus: Agreed.

Stranger: Now let's turn to the Friends of the Forms. You'll interpret their views for us too.

Theaetetus: I will.

Stranger: We'll say to them: "You distinguish between true being and becoming, correct?"

Theaetetus: "Yes," they reply.

Stranger: "And you'd say that through the body and through perception we participate in becoming, but through the soul and through thought we participate in true being. And you'd say that being is always the same and unchanging, while becoming constantly varies?"

Theaetetus: Yes, that's what we'd say.

Stranger: "Well then, distinguished friends," we'll say, "what is this 'participation' you're talking about in both cases? Do you agree with the definition we just gave?"

Theaetetus: What definition?

Stranger: We said being is an active or passive capacity, arising from powers meeting one another. But, Theaetetus, maybe your ears won't catch their answer -- I know it because I'm used to hearing it.

Theaetetus: What do they say?

Stranger: They reject what we just told the materialists about existence.

Theaetetus: What did we tell them?

Stranger: That any capacity for acting or being acted upon, however slight, is a sufficient definition of being.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: They deny this. They say that the capacity for acting and being acted upon belongs to becoming, but that neither capacity applies to being.

Theaetetus: And isn't there some truth in what they say?

Stranger: Yes. But our reply will be that we need to learn more from them -- specifically, whether they also agree that the soul knows, and that being is known.

Theaetetus: They certainly say so.

Stranger: And is knowing an activity, or a passivity, or both? Or is one of them an activity and the other a passivity? Or does neither involve either acting or being acted upon?

Theaetetus: Clearly neither involves either -- otherwise they'd be contradicting themselves.

Stranger: I see your point. But here's what they'd have to accept: if knowing is active, then being known must be passive. And on that view, being -- insofar as it's known -- is acted upon by knowledge, and is therefore in motion. Because something at rest can't be acted upon.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And by heaven -- can we really be persuaded that motion, life, soul, and mind are absent from perfect being? Can we imagine that being just sits there, solemn and mindless, in awful frozen emptiness -- an everlasting fixture without thought?

Theaetetus: That would be a dreadful thing to accept, Stranger.

Stranger: But should we say being has mind and no life?

Theaetetus: How could that be?

Stranger: Or that it has both mind and life, but no soul to contain them?

Theaetetus: How else would it contain them?

Stranger: Or that it has mind, life, and soul, but -- though ensouled -- remains absolutely motionless?

Theaetetus: All three of those options seem irrational to me.

Stranger: Then we have to include motion, and things that move, within being.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: So, Theaetetus, our conclusion is this: if there's no motion, there's no mind anywhere, about anything, belonging to anyone.

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: And yet this equally follows if we grant that everything is in constant motion -- on that view too, mind can't exist.

Theaetetus: How so?

Stranger: Do you think sameness -- of condition, manner, and subject -- could ever exist without some principle of stability?

Theaetetus: Certainly not.

Stranger: And can you see how mind could exist, or ever come into existence, without stability?

Theaetetus: No.

Stranger: And surely we must fight with everything we have against anyone who would destroy knowledge, reason, and mind -- and yet still dares to speak confidently about anything.

Theaetetus: Yes, with all our might.

Stranger: Then the philosopher who truly reveres these things can't possibly accept the view of those who say everything is at rest -- whether as a unity or in many forms. And he'll be equally deaf to those who say everything is in constant motion. Like children begging, "Give us both!" the philosopher must include both motion and rest in his definition of being and reality.

Theaetetus: Absolutely true.

Stranger: Well then -- do we seem to have a good understanding of being now?

Theaetetus: Yes, I'd say we do.

Stranger: Oh no, Theaetetus. I think we're only just beginning to see the real difficulty of inquiring into being's nature.

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Stranger: My friend, don't you see how deeply ignorant we still are, even though we feel like we're making progress?

Theaetetus: I certainly thought we were! And I have no idea how we ended up in this desperate position.

Stranger: Think about it. Now that we've made these admissions, can't we fairly be asked the same questions we were just asking the people who said everything is hot and cold?

Theaetetus: What questions? Remind me.

Stranger: I will -- by putting those same questions to you right now. That'll move us forward.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: Wouldn't you say that rest and motion are completely opposed to each other?

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: And yet you'd say that both of them equally are -- that both exist?

Theaetetus: I would.

Stranger: And when you say both of them "are," do you mean both of them are in motion?

Theaetetus: Certainly not.

Stranger: Or that they're both at rest?

Theaetetus: Of course not.

Stranger: Then you're conceiving of being as some third, distinct nature -- something under which both rest and motion are equally included. You're observing that they both participate in being, and on that basis declaring that they both exist.

Theaetetus: It does seem like we're glimpsing being as a third thing, when we say both rest and motion exist.

Stranger: So being isn't the combination of rest and motion. It's something different from both of them.

Theaetetus: So it would seem.

Stranger: Being, then, by its own nature, is neither in motion nor at rest.

Theaetetus: That does seem to be the truth.

Stranger: Then where's a person supposed to turn who wants to have any clear or settled idea of being?

Theaetetus: Where indeed?

Stranger: I'd say he can hardly turn anywhere. Because what isn't in motion must be at rest, and what isn't at rest must be in motion. But being has been placed outside both of these categories. Is that even possible?

Theaetetus: Utterly impossible.

Stranger: Here's something else we should keep in mind.

Theaetetus: What?

Stranger: When we were asked what to attach the term "not-being" to, we were in the deepest confusion. Remember?

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: And aren't we now in just as much confusion about being?

Theaetetus: If anything, Stranger, we're in even deeper trouble.

Stranger: Then let's acknowledge the difficulty. And since being and not-being are tangled up in the same confusion, there's hope: when one of them becomes clearer, the other will too. And if we can't make out either one, we may at least be able to steer between them without too much embarrassment.

Theaetetus: Good.

Stranger: Let's investigate how we come to apply many names to the same thing.

Theaetetus: Give me an example.

Stranger: Take a human being. We refer to the same person under many names -- we attribute colors, shapes, sizes, virtues, and vices to him. In all these cases, and ten thousand others, we don't just say he's a man, but also that he's good, and apply countless other attributes. The same goes for anything else: we start with something we treat as one thing, then describe it as many, under many names.

Theaetetus: That's true.

Stranger: And this gives beginners -- whether young or old -- a rich feast to argue about. There's nothing easier than insisting that the one can't be many, or the many one. They love denying that a man is good -- because, they say, man is man and good is good. You must have met people who are thrilled by these sorts of discoveries. They're often elderly folks whose limited intellects are blown away by such insights, which they think represent the height of wisdom.

Theaetetus: I certainly have.

Stranger: Then let's address our questions to these people too, along with everyone else who's ever speculated about being. Let's leave nobody out.

Theaetetus: What questions?

Stranger: Should we refuse to attach being to motion and rest, or anything to anything, treating everything as incapable of mixing with anything else? Or should we throw everything together into one class where everything connects with everything? Or should we say some things mix with some and others don't? Which of these will they prefer, Theaetetus?

Theaetetus: I can't answer for them. Why don't you take each option in turn and see what follows?

Stranger: Good idea. First, let's assume they say nothing can participate in anything else in any way. Then rest and motion can't participate in being at all.

Theaetetus: They can't.

Stranger: But would either of them exist if they don't participate in being?

Theaetetus: No.

Stranger: Well, that concession immediately demolishes everything: the doctrine that everything is in motion, the doctrine that everything is at rest, and the doctrine that being consists of unchanging, eternal Forms. Because all of these attach a notion of being -- claiming things "truly are" in motion, or "truly are" at rest.

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: And the thinkers who sometimes combine things and sometimes separate them -- whether they create unity from plurality or infinity from unity, whether they produce finite elements and build compounds from them, whether they think creation happens in stages or continuously -- none of this talk makes any sense if there's no mixing at all.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And the most ridiculous of all would be the very people who insist on this no-mixing rule and yet won't let us call anything by another name because it participates in some quality from something else.

Theaetetus: Why's that?

Stranger: Because they're forced to use words like "is," "apart," "from others," "in itself" -- and ten thousand more. They can't give these up. These words are the connecting links of all speech. So they don't need anyone else to refute them. As the saying goes, the enemy lives inside their own house. They're constantly carrying an adversary around in their bellies, like that famous ventriloquist Eurycles, contradicting them out loud from within.

Theaetetus: That's exactly right -- a very accurate picture.

Stranger: Now, suppose we assume the opposite: that everything has the power to mix with everything.

Theaetetus: Even I can figure out what goes wrong there.

Stranger: How?

Theaetetus: Because motion itself would be at rest, and rest would be in motion -- if they could be attributed to each other.

Stranger: And that's utterly impossible.

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: So only the third option remains.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: Because one of these three must be the case: either everything mixes with everything, or nothing mixes with anything, or some things mix with some and others don't.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: And the first two have been shown to be impossible.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: So anyone who wants to give a truthful answer will go with the third option: some things mix with some, and others don't.

Theaetetus: Exactly right.

Stranger: This selective mixing can be illustrated by letters. Some letters don't fit together, while others do.

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: And the vowels, especially, are a kind of bond running through all the other letters. Without a vowel, no consonant can be joined to another consonant.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: But does everyone know which letters combine with which? Or does that require a skill?

Theaetetus: It requires a skill.

Stranger: Which skill?

Theaetetus: Grammar.

Stranger: And isn't the same true of musical notes, high and low? The person who has the skill to know which sounds blend together is a musician, and the person who doesn't know isn't?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And we'll find this pattern holds everywhere -- in every case of having or lacking skill.

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: So since we've agreed that categories of being -- like letters -- are sometimes capable and sometimes incapable of mixing, anyone who wants to correctly show which kinds combine and which don't must proceed through argument with the help of knowledge. And he'll need to ask whether there are universal connecting threads that run through everything, making mixing possible -- and again, in cases of division, whether there are other universal categories that make those divisions possible.

Theaetetus: He'll definitely need knowledge for that -- and if I'm not mistaken, the very greatest knowledge there is.

Stranger: And what should we call it? By Zeus -- have we stumbled upon the philosopher's own science without realizing it? Were we looking for the sophist and accidentally found the philosopher?

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Stranger: Shouldn't we say that the division according to classes -- which neither confuses what's different with what's the same, nor treats what's the same as if it were different -- is the business of the dialectical science?

Theaetetus: Yes, that's what we should say.

Stranger: Then the person who can do this rightly is able to perceive clearly: one Form pervading a scattered multitude, and many different Forms contained under one higher Form. And again: one Form knit together into a single whole but pervading many such wholes. And many Forms existing only in separation and isolation. This is the knowledge of classes -- knowing where they can combine with one another and where they can't.

Theaetetus: Exactly right.

Stranger: And you'd assign the art of dialectic only to the pure and genuine philosopher?

Theaetetus: Who else could be worthy of it?

Stranger: In this region we'll always find the philosopher, if we look for him. But like the sophist, he's not easy to see -- though for a different reason.

Theaetetus: What reason?

Stranger: The sophist runs away into the darkness of not-being, where he's learned to feel his way around by habit. He can't be found because of the darkness of the place.

Theaetetus: That seems right.

Stranger: But the philosopher, always conversing through reason with the Form of being, is also hard to see -- but because of excess of light. The souls of most people have no eyes that can endure the vision of the divine.

Theaetetus: Yes, that seems just as true as the other.

Stranger: We may come back to the philosopher later if we feel like it. But the sophist clearly can't be allowed to escape until we've gotten a good look at him.

Theaetetus: Agreed.

Stranger: Since we've established that some classes mix with each other and some don't, and that some mix with a few and others with many, and that there's no reason some shouldn't mix with everything -- let's pursue the inquiry as the argument suggests. We won't try to deal with all the Forms, or we'll get lost in the sheer number. Instead, let's select a few of the most important ones and examine their natures and their capacity for combining with each other. That way, even if we can't grasp being and not-being with perfect clarity, we may at least not fall short in our examination of them -- as far as this inquiry allows -- and perhaps we can assert the reality of not-being and escape unharmed.

Theaetetus: We must do that.

Stranger: The most important of all the categories are the ones we were just talking about: being, rest, and motion.

Theaetetus: By far.

Stranger: And we've said that two of them can't mix with each other.

Theaetetus: They absolutely can't.

Stranger: But being surely mixes with both, since both of them exist.

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: So there are three.

Theaetetus: Right.

Stranger: And each of them is different from the other two, but the same as itself.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: But what do we actually mean by "same" and "different"? Are they two additional kinds beyond our three -- kinds that necessarily intermingle with the other three? If so, are we dealing with five kinds instead of three? Or when we say "same" and "different," are we unconsciously referring to one of the original three?

Theaetetus: We might be.

Stranger: But motion and rest are certainly neither "the same" nor "the different."

Theaetetus: How so?

Stranger: Whatever we apply to both motion and rest in common can't itself be either of them.

Theaetetus: Why not?

Stranger: Because motion would be at rest and rest would be in motion. Whichever one of them -- same or different -- gets attributed to both will force the other to change into its own opposite, since it would be participating in its opposite.

Theaetetus: Exactly right.

Stranger: And yet both motion and rest do partake of sameness and of difference?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: So we can't say that motion is the same, any more than we can say rest is the same, or that motion is the different.

Theaetetus: No, we can't.

Stranger: But what about being and the same -- could they be identical?

Theaetetus: Maybe.

Stranger: But if they were, then by saying motion and rest "have being," we'd also be saying they're "the same." And that clearly can't be right.

Theaetetus: Certainly not.

Stranger: So being and the same can't be one thing.

Theaetetus: Hardly.

Stranger: Then we should treat "the same" as a fourth category, to be added to the other three.

Theaetetus: Absolutely.

Stranger: And should we call "the different" a fifth? Or should we consider "being" and "the different" to be two names for one category?

Theaetetus: That's possible.

Stranger: But you'd agree that some things that exist are what they are in themselves, while others are always in relation to other things?

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: And "the different" is always relative -- always in relation to something else?

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: But that wouldn't be the case if being and the different were the same thing. Because if "the different" were absolute as well as relative -- like being -- then there would be some instance of "the different" that isn't different from anything. But in fact, we find that whatever is different is necessarily what it is in relation to some other thing.

Theaetetus: That's exactly right.

Stranger: Then we have to accept "the different" as our fifth category among the ones we've selected.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And this fifth category pervades all the others. Because each of them is different from the rest -- not because of its own nature, but because it participates in the nature of "the different."

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: Now let's work through each of the five, one at a time.

Theaetetus: How?

Stranger: First, take motion. We affirm that it's absolutely different from rest. What else can we say?

Theaetetus: It is.

Stranger: And therefore it is not rest.

Theaetetus: Certainly not.

Stranger: And yet it exists -- because it partakes of being.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And motion is different from the same?

Theaetetus: Basically, yes.

Stranger: So it's not the same.

Theaetetus: It's not.

Stranger: And yet motion is the same -- because everything partakes of the same.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Stranger: So we have to accept this and not object: motion is the same and is not the same. When we say these two things, we don't mean them in the same way. We call it "the same" in relation to itself, because it partakes of the same. And we call it "not the same" because, through its connection with "the different," it gets separated from the same -- it becomes not that, but other. So it's correctly called "not the same."

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: And if motion itself somehow partook of rest, there'd be nothing wrong with calling motion "stationary."

Theaetetus: Right -- assuming some categories mix with each other and others don't.

Stranger: And we already proved that this kind of mixing among categories is natural, before we got to this part of the discussion.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: Let's continue. Can we say motion is different from "the different"? After all, we've already shown it's different from "the same" and different from rest.

Theaetetus: That's certain.

Stranger: So motion is different and also not different?

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: What's next? Should we say motion is different from three of the five but not the fourth? Because we agreed there are five categories we're examining.

Theaetetus: We surely can't reduce the number below what we found just now.

Stranger: Then we can assert without fear that motion is different from being?

Theaetetus: Without the slightest fear.

Stranger: So the clear result is this: motion, since it partakes of being, really is -- and also is not.

Theaetetus: Nothing could be plainer.

Stranger: Then not-being necessarily exists in the case of motion, and in every category. Because the nature of "the different" enters into all of them, making each of them different from being and therefore, in that sense, nonexistent. So we can truly say of all of them that they are not. And equally, because they partake of being, they are and they exist.

Theaetetus: That seems right.

Stranger: Every category, then, has plenty of being and an unlimited amount of not-being.

Theaetetus: So it seems.

Stranger: And being itself can be said to be different from the other categories.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: Then we can conclude that being is not, insofar as there are other things. Because since it's not those other things -- which are unlimited in number -- it is itself one thing, and is not all the rest.

Theaetetus: That's close to the truth.

Stranger: We shouldn't quarrel with this result. It's in the nature of categories to mix with one another. And if anyone denies what we're saying now -- that being, in a sense, is not -- let him first refute our earlier conclusion about how categories interact, and then he can argue with what follows.

Theaetetus: Nothing could be fairer.

Stranger: Let me ask you to consider one more thing.

Theaetetus: What?

Stranger: When we speak of not-being, we're not, I think, talking about something opposed to being. We're only talking about something different.

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Stranger: When we say something is "not great," does that seem to imply "small" any more than it implies "equal"?

Theaetetus: Certainly not.

Stranger: So when we use negative words -- "not" this or "non-" that -- they don't imply the opposite of the word that follows. They only imply something different from it, or more precisely, something different from the things those words represent.

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: There's another point to consider, if you don't mind.

Theaetetus: What is it?

Stranger: The nature of "the different" seems to me to be divided into parts -- just like knowledge.

Theaetetus: How so?

Stranger: Knowledge is one thing, and yet its various branches each have their own particular name. That's why there are many arts and kinds of knowledge.

Theaetetus: Quite true.

Stranger: And isn't the same true of the parts of "the different," which is also one thing?

Theaetetus: Probably. But how?

Stranger: There's some part of "the different" that's set against the beautiful?

Theaetetus: There is.

Stranger: Does it have a name, or not?

Theaetetus: It does. Whatever we call "not-beautiful" is what's different from the beautiful -- not different from something else.

Stranger: Now tell me one more thing.

Theaetetus: What?

Stranger: The not-beautiful -- isn't it simply a portion of existence that's been marked off from a certain kind of existence and then set against another existing thing?

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: So the not-beautiful turns out to be a case of one being set against another being?

Theaetetus: Very true.

Stranger: Then on this view, is the beautiful more real than the not-beautiful? Is the not-beautiful less real?

Theaetetus: Not at all.

Stranger: And "the not-great" exists just as fully as "the great"?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And in the same way, "the just" has to be put in the same class as "the not-just" -- neither one has any more existence than the other.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: The same applies to everything else. Since the nature of "the different" has real existence, its parts must be considered equally real.

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: Then it seems that the contrast between a part of "the different" and a part of being is, if I may put it this way, just as real as being itself. It doesn't imply the opposite of being -- only what is different from being.

Theaetetus: Beyond question.

Stranger: Then what should we call it?

Theaetetus: Obviously, not-being. And this is exactly the nature of not-being that the sophist forced us to search for.

Stranger: And doesn't it have just as real an existence as any other category, as you were saying? Can I say with confidence that not-being has a secure existence and a nature of its own? Just as the great was found to be great and the beautiful beautiful, and the not-great not-great and the not-beautiful not-beautiful -- in the same way, not-being has been found to be and is not-being. It should be counted as one among the many categories of being. Do you still have any doubt about this, Theaetetus?

Theaetetus: None whatsoever.

Stranger: Do you see that our investigation has carried us beyond the limits Parmenides set?

Theaetetus: In what way?

Stranger: We've pushed further than he forbade us to go, and we've shown him more than he ever wanted investigated.

Theaetetus: How so?

Stranger: Because he says:

"Not-being never is, and do thou keep thy thoughts from this way of inquiry."

Theaetetus: Yes, that's what he says.

Stranger: But we've not only proved that things which are not, are -- we've shown what Form not-being actually takes. We've shown that the nature of "the different" exists and is distributed across all things in their relations to one another. And whatever part of "the different" is contrasted with being -- that is precisely what we've dared to call not-being.

Theaetetus: And I think we were absolutely right, Stranger.

Stranger: Then let no one say that we're asserting some sort of opposite of being and then claiming that opposite exists. We said goodbye to the question of whether being has an opposite long ago -- it may or may not, and it may or may not be definable. But as for our account of not-being: let someone either prove us wrong, or, as long as he can't, let him say what we're saying -- that categories mix with one another; that being and difference pervade everything and interpenetrate each other; that "the different" partakes of being and therefore is, yet is not the same as what it partakes of, but other; and being other than being, it's clearly and necessarily not-being.

And being itself, through partaking of "the different," becomes a category different from the rest. Being different from all of them, it is not each one of them, and is not all the rest. So undoubtedly, there are thousands upon thousands of cases in which being is not. And all other things, whether considered individually or collectively, in many respects are and in many respects are not.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And if anyone is skeptical about this seeming contradiction, he needs to come up with something better. Or if his idea of fun is dragging words this way and that, our argument proves he's not making worthy use of his talents. There's no charm in such puzzles, and there's no difficulty in spotting them. But we can point him toward something else -- something that's noble and also difficult.

Theaetetus: What's that?

Stranger: Something I've already mentioned. Forget these word games -- they're not hard. Instead, a person should be able to follow and critique every argument in detail. When someone says that "the same" is in some way "different," or that "the different" is in some way "the same," he should be able to understand and respond from the speaker's own point of view, in the very respect in which the claim is being made. But to show that somehow the same is different, the different is same, the great is small, or the like is unlike -- and to take delight in endlessly trotting out such contradictions -- that's no real refutation. It's the work of someone who's just beginning to grapple with the problem of being.

Theaetetus: Absolutely.

Stranger: Because, my friend, trying to separate everything from everything else is crude and utterly unworthy of an educated or philosophical mind.

Theaetetus: Why?

Stranger: Total separation is the complete destruction of all reasoning. We can only achieve rational discourse through the weaving together of concepts with one another.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And notice that we were just in time in resisting those separatists and forcing them to admit that things do mix with one another.

Theaetetus: Why was the timing so important?

Stranger: So that we could establish discourse itself as a genuine kind of being. If we couldn't, the worst possible consequence would follow: we'd lose philosophy entirely. And the need to determine the nature of discourse is pressing right now. If we were completely deprived of it, we couldn't hold discourse at all. And we'd be deprived of it if we admitted that there's no mixing of categories whatsoever.

Theaetetus: That's true. But I don't see why we need to determine the nature of discourse right at this moment.

Stranger: Maybe this will help you see.

Theaetetus: What?

Stranger: We've established that not-being is one category among many, distributed across all being.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And now the question is: does not-being mix with opinion and speech?

Theaetetus: How so?

Stranger: If not-being plays no part in statements, then everything we say must be true. But if not-being does play a part, then false opinion and false speech become possible. Because to think or say what is not -- that's what falsehood is. Falsehood arises in the domain of thought and speech.

Theaetetus: Exactly right.

Stranger: And where there's falsehood, there must be deception.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And if there's deception, then the world must be full of images, likenesses, and illusions.

Theaetetus: Of course.

Stranger: And we said the sophist had escaped into that territory and was denying the very possibility of falsehood. Nobody, he argued, either thinks or utters falsehood, since not-being has no part in being.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: But now we've shown that not-being does partake of being. So the sophist won't be able to hold that line anymore. But he'll probably shift his ground and say that some Forms partake of not-being and some don't, and that speech and opinion are among the ones that don't. And he'll keep fighting to the death against the image-making and appearance-making art -- the one we've placed him in -- because, he'll argue, if opinion and speech don't partake of not-being, there can be no such thing as falsehood.

So to counter this evasion, we need to start by investigating the nature of speech, opinion, and imagination. We need to find them, show they have a connection with not-being, and by proving that connection, prove that falsehood exists. Then we'll imprison the sophist there, if he deserves it -- or if not, we'll let him go and look for him in another category.

Theaetetus: What you said about the sophist at the beginning is certainly proving true, Stranger -- he's not easily caught. He seems to have an endless supply of defenses, and every single one has to be broken through before we can reach the man himself. We just barely got through his first defense -- the claim that not-being doesn't exist -- and now here's another: we still have to prove that falsehood exists in speech and opinion. And after that, there'll be another, and another, with no end in sight.

Stranger: Anyone who can make even a little progress should take heart, Theaetetus. What would someone who's discouraged by small gains do if he were making no progress at all -- or even losing ground? A faint heart, as the proverb says, will never take a city. But now that we've come this far, the citadel is ours. What remains is easier.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Stranger: Then, as I was saying, let's first work out a clear idea of speech and opinion. That will give us a solid basis for determining whether not-being has anything to do with them -- or whether they're always true and never false.

Theaetetus: Right.

Stranger: Now then -- let's talk about words, the way we were talking earlier about Forms and letters. That's the direction the answer lies.

Theaetetus: And what's the question about words?

Stranger: Whether all words can be connected with each other, or none, or only some.

Theaetetus: Clearly the last one.

Stranger: I think you mean that words which form a meaningful sequence can be connected, but words that have no meaning in sequence can't?

Theaetetus: What exactly are you saying?

Stranger: What I thought you were implying when you agreed. There are two basic kinds of vocal signs that indicate being.

Theaetetus: What are they?

Stranger: One kind is called nouns, the other verbs.

Theaetetus: Describe them.

Stranger: The sign that indicates an action we call a verb.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And the sign that identifies the one performing the action we call a noun.

Theaetetus: Right.

Stranger: Now, a string of nouns by itself doesn't make a sentence. Neither does a string of verbs by itself.

Theaetetus: I don't follow.

Stranger: I see. When you agreed just now, you had something different in mind. But my point is simply that a bare sequence of nouns or verbs isn't discourse.

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Stranger: I mean that words like "walks," "runs," "sleeps" -- or any other words indicating actions -- no matter how many you string together, they don't make a sentence.

Theaetetus: How could they?

Stranger: And again, when you say "lion," "stag," "horse," or any other words indicating agents -- you don't get a sentence this way either. In neither case do the sounds express any action or inaction, or the existence or nonexistence of anything. Not until verbs are combined with nouns. Then the words fit together, and the smallest such combination produces speech -- the simplest and shortest form of discourse.

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Stranger: When someone says "A man learns" -- would you call that the simplest and shortest sentence?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: Because now he's conveying something about something that is, or is becoming, or has become, or will be. He's not just naming -- he's doing something by connecting a verb with a noun. That's why we say he's engaging in discourse, and we call this connection of words a statement.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And just as some things fit together and others don't, some vocal signs combine to form discourse and others don't.

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: There's one more small point.

Theaetetus: What?

Stranger: A sentence has to have a subject. It can't help it.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And it has to have a certain quality -- a character.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: Now let's pay close attention to what we're doing.

Theaetetus: We must.

Stranger: I'll give you a sentence that combines a subject and an action, using a noun and a verb. You tell me who the sentence is about.

Theaetetus: I'll do my best.

Stranger: "Theaetetus sits." Not a very long sentence.

Theaetetus: Not very.

Stranger: Now tell me: who does the sentence speak about? Who's the subject?

Theaetetus: Me. I'm the subject.

Stranger: And how about this sentence --

Theaetetus: What sentence?

Stranger: "Theaetetus, with whom I'm now speaking, is flying."

Theaetetus: That's also a sentence everyone would agree speaks about me and applies to me.

Stranger: And we agreed that every sentence must have a certain quality.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: So what's the quality of each of these two sentences?

Theaetetus: I'd say one is false and the other is true.

Stranger: And the true one says what's true about you?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And the false one says what's different from the truth?

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: It speaks of things that are not as though they were?

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: It says things are real about you that are not real. Because, as we were saying, for every person and thing, there's much that is and much that is not.

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: Now, the second sentence about you was first of all an example of the shortest form of sentence we defined.

Theaetetus: Yes, we agreed on that.

Stranger: And second, it was about a subject.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: A subject that has to be you and can't be anyone else.

Theaetetus: Unquestionably.

Stranger: And it wouldn't be a sentence at all if it had no subject. We proved that a sentence without a subject is impossible.

Theaetetus: Right.

Stranger: So when "the different" is asserted of you as if it were "the same" -- when not-being is asserted as being -- that kind of combination of nouns and verbs is really and truly false discourse.

Theaetetus: Absolutely true.

Stranger: And from this it follows that thought, opinion, and imagination exist in our minds as both true and false.

Theaetetus: How so?

Stranger: You'll understand better if you first grasp what each of them is and how they differ from one another.

Theaetetus: Then explain it to me.

Stranger: Aren't thought and speech the same thing, with one difference: what we call thought is the soul's silent, internal conversation with itself?

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: But when that stream of thought flows out through the lips and becomes audible, it's called speech?

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And we know that within speech there exists --

Theaetetus: What?

Stranger: Affirmation and denial.

Theaetetus: Yes, we know that.

Stranger: And when affirmation or denial happens silently, in the mind alone -- what do you call it besides opinion?

Theaetetus: That's the only name for it.
