Stranger: And when opinion arises not on its own but through some form of perception -- would you call that anything other than imagination?

Theaetetus: Certainly not.

Stranger: So: since speech can be true or false, and since thought is the soul's conversation with itself, and opinion is the conclusion of thinking, and imagination is the fusion of perception with opinion -- it follows that some of these, being akin to speech, must contain an element of falsehood as well as truth.

Theaetetus: Certainly.

Stranger: Do you realize, then, that we've discovered false opinion and false speech sooner than we expected? Just a moment ago, it looked like we were taking on a task that would never be finished.

Theaetetus: I do realize it.

Stranger: Then let's not be discouraged about what's ahead. Now that we've made this breakthrough, let's go back to our earlier classification.

Theaetetus: Which classification?

Stranger: We divided image-making into two kinds: likeness-making and appearance-making -- the fantastic art.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And we said we weren't sure which one the sophist belonged to.

Theaetetus: We did.

Stranger: And our heads started spinning when it was claimed that there's no such thing as an image, a likeness, or an appearance, because falsehood can't exist in any way, at any time, in any place.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: But now we've shown that false speech and false opinion exist. And that means there can be imitations of real things, and from that condition of the mind an art of deception can arise.

Theaetetus: That's certainly possible now.

Stranger: And we already established that the sophist was lurking in one of the two divisions of image-making.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: Then let's make a fresh attempt. When we divide any class, let's always take the part on the right -- holding on to whatever contains the sophist -- until we've stripped away every general property he shares with others and exposed his specific difference, his unique nature. Then we can display him in his true form, first to ourselves, then to those with a kindred philosophical spirit.

Theaetetus: Very good.

Stranger: You may recall that all skill was originally divided into creative and acquisitive.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And the sophist kept showing up in the acquisitive class -- under hunting, competition, commerce, and so on.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Stranger: But now that the imitative art has enclosed him, it's clear we need to start by dividing the art of creation. Because imitation is a kind of creation -- the creation of images, not real things.

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: First, there are two kinds of creation.

Theaetetus: What are they?

Stranger: One is human, the other divine.

Theaetetus: I don't follow.

Stranger: Remember, we originally defined creative power as anything that brings into existence something that didn't exist before.

Theaetetus: I remember.

Stranger: Now look at the world -- all the animals and plants, the things that grow from seeds and roots, the inanimate substances formed within the earth, both those that melt and those that don't. Should we say these come into existence -- having not existed before -- through the creation of God? Or should we go along with the common opinion?

Theaetetus: Which opinion?

Stranger: That nature produces them from some spontaneous, mindless cause. Or should we say they're created by divine reason and knowledge that comes from God?

Theaetetus: I admit I probably waver on this because of my age. But when I look at you and see you're inclined to credit them to God, I'll defer to your judgment.

Stranger: Well said, Theaetetus. And if I thought you were the kind of person who'd later change his mind, I'd have argued with you gently and made you agree. But since I can see you'll arrive at that belief on your own -- without any argument from me -- toward the view that already attracts you, I won't try to rush what time will accomplish.

So let's suppose that things produced by nature are the work of divine craft, and things made by humans from natural materials are works of human craft. There are two kinds of making: human and divine.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: Now subdivide each of those two sections once more.

Theaetetus: How do you mean?

Stranger: Just as we made a horizontal division of production into divine and human, now make a vertical one.

Theaetetus: Done.

Stranger: So there are four parts in all. Two relate to us and are human. Two relate to the gods and are divine.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And taking the other division: within each half, one part is the making of real things, while the other two parts are the making of likenesses or images. So creative skill is again divided into two.

Theaetetus: Walk me through the divisions again.

Stranger: We, other animals, and the elements from which things are made -- fire, water, and the like -- are all understood to be the creation and work of God.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: And then there are images of them -- things that are not the originals but correspond to them. These too are the creation of a wonderful skill.

Theaetetus: What kind of images?

Stranger: The appearances that arise spontaneously -- in sleep or by day. Things like shadows, which appear when darkness falls across a source of light. Or reflections, which are produced when light from a bright, smooth surface meets an external light and creates a perception that reverses our ordinary sight.

Theaetetus: Yes -- those images are just as much the work of a divine hand as the originals are.

Stranger: And what about human creation? Don't we build one house with the art of construction, and another with the art of drawing -- a sort of dream created by humans for people who are awake?

Theaetetus: Exactly.

Stranger: And all other products of human creation come in pairs the same way: the real thing, made by the art of making the thing itself, and the image, made by the art of imitation.

Theaetetus: Now I understand. And I'm ready to acknowledge that there are two kinds of production, each of them twofold. In the horizontal division, there's divine and human production. In the vertical division, there are real things and images of them.

Stranger: And let's not forget: within the imitative class, one part was supposed to be likeness-making and the other appearance-making -- if it could be shown that falsehood actually exists and belongs to the class of real things.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: And that has been shown. So now, without hesitation, we can count the two kinds as distinct.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: Then let's divide the art of appearance-making once more.

Theaetetus: Where do we make the cut?

Stranger: There's one kind where the appearance is produced using a tool or instrument, and another where the creator of the appearance is himself the instrument.

Theaetetus: What do you mean?

Stranger: When someone makes himself look or sound like another person, that part of the appearance-making art is called mimicry.

Theaetetus: Yes.

Stranger: Let's call this branch the art of mimicry and assign it its territory. As for the other branch -- using tools -- we're tired, so let's leave it to someone else to classify and name.

Theaetetus: Fair enough. Let's give one its territory and leave the other.

Stranger: But there's one more distinction within mimicry, Theaetetus, that's worth our attention. I'll tell you why.

Theaetetus: Go ahead.

Stranger: Some imitators know what they're imitating. Others don't. And what greater line of distinction could there possibly be than the one between ignorance and knowledge?

Theaetetus: None.

Stranger: Now, the kind of imitation we were just talking about -- wasn't it the imitation of those who know? Someone who wants to imitate you would surely have to know you and what you look like.

Theaetetus: Naturally.

Stranger: But what about the Form of justice, or virtue in general? Aren't we well aware that many people, who have no real knowledge of these things but only a sort of opinion, do their best to make it seem like they genuinely hold these opinions -- expressing them as convincingly as they can in word and deed?

Theaetetus: Yes, that's very common.

Stranger: And do they always fail at appearing just when they're not? Or is the opposite true?

Theaetetus: The opposite. They often succeed.

Stranger: Then this kind of person should be called an imitator too -- but distinguished from the other kind, the way the ignorant are distinguished from the knowledgeable.

Theaetetus: True.

Stranger: Can we find a good name for each? This clearly isn't easy. The ancients had a certain confusion about dividing categories into species, so there aren't many names ready to hand. But for the sake of clarity, I'll be bold. Let's call imitation that coexists with mere opinion the imitation of appearances. And imitation that coexists with knowledge we'll call learned or informed imitation.

Theaetetus: Granted.

Stranger: The first kind is what we care about right now. Because the sophist was classified among imitators, but not among those with knowledge.

Theaetetus: Very true.

Stranger: Then let's examine our imitator-of-appearances and see if he rings solid like good iron, or if there's still a crack in him.

Theaetetus: Let's check.

Stranger: There's quite a substantial crack. Look: within the two classes of imitators, one is a simple creature who genuinely believes he knows what he only thinks he knows. The other type has been knocked around among arguments long enough to suspect and fear that he's actually ignorant of what he pretends to know in front of others.

Theaetetus: There are definitely both kinds, just as you describe.

Stranger: Should we call the first the sincere imitator, and the second the ironic or dissembling imitator?

Theaetetus: That sounds right.

Stranger: And should we say the dissembling type has one division or two?

Theaetetus: You tell me.

Stranger: On reflection, I see two. There's the dissembler who delivers long speeches to crowds in public. And there's the dissembler who works in private, using short questions and answers to force the person he's talking with into contradicting himself.

Theaetetus: That's absolutely right.

Stranger: And who's the one who makes the long speeches? The statesman, or the popular orator?

Theaetetus: The popular orator.

Stranger: And what should we call the other one? Is he the philosopher or the sophist?

Theaetetus: He can't be the philosopher -- we've established that he's ignorant. But since he imitates the wise, he'll have a name derived from "wise" -- sophos. What should we call him? I'm pretty confident I can't be wrong in calling him the true and genuine sophist.

Stranger: Then shall we tie up his name the way we did before -- tracing a chain from one end of his genealogy to the other?

Theaetetus: Absolutely.

Stranger: Here, then, is the pedigree of his art: he belongs to the conscious, dissembling section of the art of producing self-contradiction. He's an imitator of appearances -- separated out from the fantastic branch, which itself is a branch of image-making, which is part of creation. And his creation is human, not divine -- the juggling of words. Anyone who says the real sophist is of this blood and lineage will be speaking the absolute truth.

Theaetetus: Without a doubt.

STATESMAN (Part 1)

Persons of the dialogue:
  Theodorus
  Socrates
  The Eleatic Stranger
  Young Socrates

Socrates: I owe you a real debt of thanks, Theodorus, for introducing me to both Theaetetus and our visitor from Elea.

Theodorus: And before long, Socrates, you'll owe me three times as much — once they've finished working out the definition of the Statesman and the Philosopher for you, on top of the Sophist.

Socrates: Sophist, statesman, philosopher! My dear Theodorus, are my ears hearing right? Is that really how the great mathematician and geometer ranks them — all at the same value?

Theodorus: What do you mean, Socrates?

Socrates: I mean you're treating them as equals, when in reality they're separated by a distance no geometric ratio could express.

Theodorus: By Ammon, god of Cyrene — that's a fair hit, Socrates, and it shows you haven't forgotten your geometry! I'll get you back for that another time. But for now, let me ask our visitor — who I hope won't tire of being so generous with us — to take up whichever he prefers next, the Statesman or the Philosopher.

Stranger: That's my obligation, Theodorus. I've started, so I can't leave the job unfinished. But what should we do about Theaetetus?

Theodorus: What do you mean?

Stranger: Should we give him a break and swap in his companion, Young Socrates, instead? What do you think?

Theodorus: Yes, rotate them in, as you suggest. Young people always do better with rest breaks.

Socrates: I think, Stranger, that both of them could be called relatives of mine in some way. One of them, as you've pointed out, has the same ugly face I do. The other shares my name. And we should always be on the lookout for family resemblances in conversation. I was talking with Theaetetus yesterday and just now listened to him answer your questions. But my namesake I haven't examined yet — that'll have to wait for another time. Today let him answer you.

Stranger: Very well. Young Socrates, do you hear what the elder Socrates is proposing?

Young Socrates: I do.

Stranger: And you agree?

Young Socrates: Absolutely.

Stranger: Since you have no objection, I certainly can't either. After the Sophist, then, the Statesman naturally comes next in our investigation. So tell me — should the statesman also be counted among those who possess a kind of knowledge?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: Then we'll need to divide the branches of knowledge, as we did before?

Young Socrates: I'd think so.

Stranger: But the division won't be along the same lines.

Young Socrates: How will it differ?

Stranger: We'll cut at a different point.

Young Socrates: All right.

Stranger: Where do we find the path to the Statesman? We need to locate it, separate it out, and mark it clearly, while putting a different label on every diverging path. That way the mind can conceive of all forms of knowledge under two main classes.

Young Socrates: Finding that path is your job, Stranger, not mine.

Stranger: True, Socrates, but once it's found, it belongs to both of us.

Young Socrates: Fair enough.

Stranger: Now, aren't arithmetic and certain related disciplines purely abstract knowledge — completely separate from action?

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: But in carpentry and all the other manual crafts, the craftsman's knowledge is embedded in his work. He doesn't just know things — he also makes things that didn't exist before.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: Then let's divide all branches of knowledge into two groups: those that are purely theoretical, and those that are practical.

Young Socrates: Let's treat these as two divisions of knowledge, which is one unified whole.

Stranger: Now, are "statesman," "king," "master of a household," and "slave-owner" all the same thing? Or is there a distinct skill corresponding to each of these titles? Actually, let me put it another way.

Young Socrates: Go ahead.

Stranger: If someone in private life has the expertise to advise one of the public physicians, shouldn't he also be called a physician?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: And if someone in private life is capable of advising the ruler of a country, wouldn't we say he possesses the same knowledge the ruler ought to have?

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And the knowledge of a true king is the royal science?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: So anyone who possesses this knowledge — whether he happens to hold power or is just a private citizen — would properly be called "royal," purely on the basis of his expertise?

Young Socrates: He certainly should be.

Stranger: And a household manager and a master amount to the same thing?

Young Socrates: Of course.

Stranger: And a large household can be compared to a small state — as far as governance goes, is there any real difference?

Young Socrates: None at all.

Stranger: Then, returning to the point we were just discussing, it's clear that there's one science covering all of these. Call it royal, political, or household management — we won't quarrel about the name.

Young Socrates: Certainly not.

Stranger: This much is also clear: a king doesn't maintain his rule primarily through manual labor or physical strength. He does it through the intelligence and power of his mind.

Young Socrates: Obviously.

Stranger: So would we say the king has more in common with theoretical knowledge than with manual crafts and practical life in general?

Young Socrates: Definitely.

Stranger: Then we can treat statesmanship and the statesman as identical with the royal science and the king.

Young Socrates: Clearly.

Stranger: And now we'll be proceeding correctly if we subdivide the sphere of theoretical knowledge?

Young Socrates: Very good.

Stranger: See if you can find a natural joint in it — a dividing line.

Young Socrates: What kind of division do you have in mind?

Stranger: Something like this: remember how we identified the art of calculation?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: That's unmistakably a branch of theoretical knowledge?

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: And the only function we'd assign to calculation is judging the differences between numbers — it doesn't do anything beyond that?

Young Socrates: How could we say otherwise?

Stranger: Now, you know that a master builder doesn't work with his own hands — he directs the workers.

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: He contributes knowledge, not manual labor.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: So he can fairly be said to participate in theoretical knowledge?

Young Socrates: Quite right.

Stranger: But unlike the mathematician, he shouldn't consider his job done once he's made a judgment. He has to assign the individual workers their specific tasks and keep directing them until the work is complete.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: So all such knowledge is theoretical, just like arithmetic. But there's a difference between the two types: one merely judges, while the other also directs.

Young Socrates: That seems right.

Stranger: So can we say that all theoretical knowledge divides into two branches — one that judges and one that directs?

Young Socrates: I'd say so.

Stranger: And when people work together, isn't it desirable that they be of one mind?

Young Socrates: Very much so.

Stranger: Well, as long as we agree between ourselves, we needn't worry about what others think.

Young Socrates: Certainly not.

Stranger: Now, in which of these divisions do we place the king? Is he a judge and spectator? Or do we assign him to the directive branch — since he's a ruler?

Young Socrates: The directive branch, clearly.

Stranger: Then we need to see whether the art of directing can be further divided. Here's what I'm thinking: there's a distinction similar to the one between a manufacturer and a retailer, and it separates the king from a herald.

Young Socrates: How so?

Stranger: Well, doesn't a retailer receive goods that others have already produced and sell them on?

Young Socrates: He certainly does.

Stranger: And similarly, a herald receives orders from above and passes them on to others?

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: Then should we lump the king's art together with the arts of the herald, the interpreter, the boatswain, the prophet, and all the similar arts that involve passing along commands? Or — just as we earlier distinguished manufacturers who sell their own products from retailers — should we recognize that supreme rulers issue their own commands? The class of "self-directing rulers" barely has a name, but that's where kings belong. Let's assign them to a science of supreme direction, or self-direction, and leave the others for someone else to name. We're looking for the ruler, not the messenger.

Young Socrates: Very good.

Stranger: So we've drawn a solid line between the person who gives his own commands and the person who passes along someone else's. Now let's see if supreme authority allows further division.

Young Socrates: By all means.

Stranger: I think it does — help me with this.

Young Socrates: Where should we cut?

Stranger: Can we say that all rulers give commands for the sake of producing something?

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: And it's not hard to divide the things produced into two classes.

Young Socrates: How would you divide them?

Stranger: Of all things produced, some are living and some are not.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And using this distinction, we can subdivide the directive branch of knowledge.

Young Socrates: At what point?

Stranger: One part directs the production of lifeless things, the other the care of living ones. That gives us our division.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: Good. We can set one half aside and pick up the other — which can itself be divided into two.

Young Socrates: Which half do you mean?

Stranger: The one that exercises authority over animals, of course. The royal science isn't like being a master builder overseeing lifeless materials. The king has a nobler function — the management and care of living beings.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And the care of living creatures sometimes involves tending individuals, and sometimes managing creatures in groups — in herds?

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: But the statesman isn't a keeper of individual animals — not like a groom who tends a single horse. He's more like the manager of an entire herd of horses or cattle.

Young Socrates: Yes, I see what you mean.

Stranger: Should we call this art of tending many animals together "herd management" or "collective care"?

Young Socrates: Either one — whichever comes up naturally in conversation.

Stranger: Good instinct, Socrates. If you keep being relaxed about terminology, you'll be richer in wisdom when you're old. Now, leaving the name aside — can you see how we might divide herd management in two, so that what we're looking for in twice as large a field can be sought in half the space?

Young Socrates: I'll try. It seems to me there's one kind of management for humans and another for animals.

Stranger: You divided them boldly and directly, and I respect that. But you've fallen into an error we should avoid going forward.

Young Socrates: What error?

Stranger: You shouldn't slice off one small piece and set it against many larger ones. The division should be into genuine classes. It's always better to cut through the middle. You jumped to "humans" because you could see it would get us to our destination, and that made you rush. But you shouldn't chip off too small a piece, my friend. The safer way is to cut down the middle, and that's more likely to find real categories. This principle makes all the difference in any investigation.

Young Socrates: What do you mean, Stranger?

Stranger: I'll try to be clearer, out of appreciation for your sharp mind. I can't explain myself completely right now, but I'll try as we go along.

Young Socrates: What exactly was our mistake in the division?

Stranger: It was like this: imagine someone wanted to divide the human race and did it the way people do around here — splitting off "Greeks" as one category and lumping all the countless other peoples, who have nothing in common and don't even speak the same language, under the single label "barbarians." Because they share one name, they're supposed to be one species. Or imagine dividing numbers by cutting off "ten thousand" as one class and treating everything else as another — just because you've given it one name. You'd get a much better, more balanced division if you split numbers into odd and even, or the human race into male and female. You should only separate off specific groups like Lydians or Phrygians when you can't divide any further into parts that are also genuine classes.

Young Socrates: Very true. But I wish the distinction between a mere part and a true class could be made even clearer.

Stranger: Excellent Socrates, you're asking me something really difficult! We've already wandered further from our original topic than we should, and you want us to wander even further. Let's get back on track for now, and we can follow up on that another time. But I do want to make sure you heard me correctly —

Young Socrates: About what?

Stranger: That a class is always a part, but a part isn't necessarily a class. That's the view I'd want you to always attribute to me.

Young Socrates: Done.

Stranger: Now there's something else I'd like to clarify.

Young Socrates: What's that?

Stranger: Where exactly we went off track. If I'm not mistaken, it was when I asked how you'd divide herd management, and you answered a bit too quickly that there were two kinds of animals: human beings and everything else.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: I noticed that you carved off one part and assumed the remainder was a class, just because you could call all non-human animals "brutes."

Young Socrates: That's right.

Stranger: Now imagine, most courageous of dialecticians, that some intelligent creature — say, a crane — made the same kind of division. Cranes would be one distinguished species, and all other animals, including humans, would be lumped together as "brutes." That's the kind of error we need to avoid.

Young Socrates: How do we stay safe?

Stranger: By not dividing the whole class of animals in one stroke.

Young Socrates: We shouldn't try to take the whole thing at once?

Stranger: That's where we went wrong before.

Young Socrates: How?

Stranger: Remember, the branch of directive knowledge that dealt with living creatures had to do with animals in herds?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: Well, that already implied a division of all animals into tame and wild. Animals whose nature allows them to be tamed we call tame; those that can't be tamed we call wild.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And the political science we're searching for deals — and has always dealt — exclusively with tame animals, and specifically with those that live in groups.

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: So we shouldn't divide by grabbing the whole class at once. And let's not be in too big a hurry to reach political science. That kind of haste has already brought on us the misfortune the proverb warns about.

Young Socrates: What misfortune?

Stranger: Too much haste, too little speed.

Young Socrates: Fair enough, Stranger — we got what we deserved.

Stranger: All right, let's start fresh and try to divide the collective care of animals properly. Completing the argument carefully will probably show you what you're so eager to learn. Tell me —

Young Socrates: What?

Stranger: Have you ever heard of — or maybe you've actually seen — the fish preserves in the Nile and the ponds of the Great King of Persia? Or similar fish ponds closer to home, in wells?

Young Socrates: Yes, I've seen them myself, and I've heard plenty of descriptions of the others.

Stranger: And you've heard about the goose and crane nurseries on the plains of Thessaly, even if you haven't traveled there?

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: I bring this up because here's a new division of herd management: management of water herds and management of land herds.

Young Socrates: There is.

Stranger: And do you agree we should divide collective herd care into these two branches — water and land?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: And we surely don't need to ask which branch contains the royal art — that's obvious to everyone.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: Anyone could further divide the herds that graze on dry land?

Young Socrates: How would you divide them?

Stranger: Into those that fly and those that walk.

Young Socrates: Exactly right.

Stranger: And where do we look for the political animal? Even a fool could tell he's a pedestrian, a walker.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: Now the art of managing walking animals needs to be divided again, like halving an even number.

Young Socrates: Clearly.

Stranger: I can see two paths leading toward the class we're aiming at. One's a shortcut — it chops off a small piece and leaves a large one. The other follows our principle of cutting down the middle, but it's longer. We can take whichever we prefer.

Young Socrates: Can't we take both?

Stranger: Both at once? That's asking a lot! But one after the other — sure.

Young Socrates: Then let's take them in turn.

Stranger: Easy enough, since we're near the end. If we'd been at the beginning or the middle, I'd have pushed back. But since we're close to done, let's start with the longer path while we're fresh. Here's the division. Pay attention.

Young Socrates: Go ahead.

Stranger: Tame walking herd animals divide naturally into two classes.

Young Socrates: On what basis?

Stranger: Some grow horns, and some don't.

Young Socrates: Clearly.

Stranger: Now divide the science of managing walking animals into two corresponding parts and define them. If you try to invent specific names, you'll get tangled up in complexity.

Young Socrates: How should I describe them, then?

Stranger: Like this: the science of managing walking animals divides into one part for the horned herd, and another for the hornless herd.

Young Socrates: Consider it said — everything you've laid out has been well established.

Stranger: The king is clearly the shepherd of a hornless herd.

Young Socrates: That's evident.

Stranger: Shall we break this hornless herd into smaller sections and try to assign the king what's his?

Young Socrates: By all means.

Stranger: Should we distinguish them by split hooves versus solid hooves, or by whether the breed can interbreed with others? You know what I mean.

Young Socrates: What exactly?

Stranger: Horses and donkeys naturally breed with each other.

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: But the rest of the tame hornless herd doesn't interbreed.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: And does the Statesman have charge of the mixed-breeding kind or the pure-breeding kind?

Young Socrates: The pure-breeding kind, clearly.

Stranger: I suppose we need to divide this again, as before.

Young Socrates: We do.

Stranger: Now every tame, gregarious animal has been split up, except for two species. I don't think we should count dogs among herd animals.

Young Socrates: Certainly not. But how do we divide the two remaining species?

Stranger: With a measurement that you and Theaetetus should appreciate, since you're students of geometry.

Young Socrates: What measurement?

Stranger: The diagonal — and then the diagonal of the diagonal.

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: How does the human being walk? Isn't our stride like a diagonal whose square equals two?

Young Socrates: Just so.

Stranger: And the stride of the other kind — its square being the square of two, that is, four — could be called the diagonal of our diagonal.

Young Socrates: Yes! I think I almost understand you.

Stranger: And in this division, Socrates, I spot something that would make for another good joke.

Young Socrates: What's that?

Stranger: Human beings have ended up classified alongside the most free and easygoing creature around — and they've been running a race together!

Young Socrates: That is a very odd coincidence.

Stranger: And wouldn't you expect the slowest to finish last?

Young Socrates: Indeed.

Stranger: And here's an even more ridiculous result: the king turns out to be running alongside the herd, neck and neck with the bird-catcher — the man who, of all people, is most at home in the "airy life."

Young Socrates: Quite right.

Stranger: And this proves something we said during our investigation of the Sophist.

Young Socrates: What's that?

Stranger: That the method of logical division plays no favorites. It doesn't rank the great above the small. It always arrives at the truth in its own way.

Young Socrates: Clearly.

Stranger: Now, I won't wait for you to ask — I'll take you by the shorter road to the definition of a king, on my own initiative.

Young Socrates: By all means.

Stranger: Here's what I think we should have done from the start: divide land animals into bipeds and quadrupeds. Since the winged herd — and that alone — ended up in the same class as humans, we should divide bipeds into feathered and featherless. Once that's done and the art of managing humanity is brought to light, it's time to install our Statesman and ruler in his place like a charioteer, and hand him the reins of state — for that office truly belongs to him.

Young Socrates: Excellent. You've paid me the debt — that is, you've completed the argument. And the digression was the interest, I take it.

Stranger: Now then, let's go back to the beginning and link together the whole chain that makes up the definition of the Statesman's art.

Young Socrates: By all means.

Stranger: Here it is: the science of pure knowledge had a part that was the science of directing. From this we derived a further part — self-direction, as distinct from passing along others' commands. A major division of self-direction was the management of living creatures, and this was narrowed further to the management of herds. Within herds, we focused on walking animals. The chief division there was the art of managing hornless walking animals, and this could only be captured by combining three qualifiers — pure-bred, non-interbreeding herds. The only further subdivision is the art of managing human herds — bipeds — and that's what we were looking for. That is both the royal and the political art.

Young Socrates: Exactly.

Stranger: But have we really accomplished what we claim, Socrates?

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: I mean — have we truly worked this out? There's been a discussion, sure, but it seems to me the investigation isn't perfectly complete. Something is still off.

Young Socrates: I don't follow.

Stranger: Let me try to make the thought clearer for both of us.

Young Socrates: Please do.

Stranger: There were many arts of shepherding, and one of them was the political art, which had charge of one particular herd?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: And our argument defined this as the art of rearing — not horses or other animals, but human beings collectively?

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: But notice a crucial difference that sets the king apart from all other shepherds.

Young Socrates: What are you referring to?

Stranger: Does any other herdsman have rivals who claim to share in the management of his herd?

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: Think about it: merchants, farmers, food suppliers, athletic trainers, and doctors all compete with the so-called statesmen — the herdsmen of humanity. They all insist that they're the ones who truly rear and manage human beings, and not just ordinary people but the rulers themselves.

Young Socrates: Don't they have a point?

Stranger: They very well might, and we'll examine their claims. But here's what we can be sure of: no one challenges the ordinary herdsman as sole feeder and doctor for his flock. He's also their matchmaker and midwife — no one else knows that part of the job. And he's their entertainer and musician, as far as their natures allow; no one soothes his herd better than he does, whether with his natural voice or with instruments. The same goes for animal keepers in general.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: So given all that, can our definition of the king really be correct and complete? Were we right to single him out from ten thousand other claimants as the shepherd and rearer of the human flock?

Young Socrates: I suppose not.

Stranger: Weren't we right to suspect that although we may have sketched a rough outline of the royal form, we haven't yet produced a precise portrait of the Statesman? We can't reveal him as he truly is until we've separated him from all those who crowd around him and claim to share his authority.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: And that's exactly what we must do, Socrates, if we don't want to disgrace our argument at the finish line.

Young Socrates: We certainly can't let that happen.

Stranger: Then let's make a fresh start and travel by a different road.

Young Socrates: What road?

Stranger: Let's mix in a bit of entertainment. There's a famous tale, and a good portion of it will serve our purposes nicely. Then we can go back to our systematic divisions and follow the old path to our destination. Shall we?

Young Socrates: By all means.

Stranger: Then listen to a story that a child would love to hear. And you're not too old for a bit of childish amusement.

Young Socrates: Let's hear it.

Stranger: There really did happen — and will happen again — something like the many marvels preserved in ancient tradition. I'm thinking of the portent associated with the quarrel of Atreus and Thyestes. You've heard about it and remember what they say happened?

Young Socrates: I suppose you mean the golden lamb?

Stranger: No, not that — the other part of the story. The one about how the sun and stars once rose in the west and set in the east, and how a god reversed their course and gave them the motion they have now, as a sign confirming the rights of Atreus.

Young Socrates: Yes, there's that legend too.

Stranger: And we've also heard many times about the reign of Cronus.

Young Socrates: Yes, many times.

Stranger: And the tradition that people in former ages were born from the earth, rather than being born from each other?

Young Socrates: Yes, that's another old story.

Stranger: All these stories, and ten thousand others even more astonishing, spring from the same origin. Most of them have been lost over the ages or survive only in fragments. But the source of them all has never been properly explained — and it's worth explaining now, because the tale sheds light on the nature of the king.

Young Socrates: Wonderful — please tell the whole story and don't leave anything out.

Stranger: Listen, then. There is a time when God himself guides the universe and helps turn it on its course. And there is a time, at the completion of a certain cycle, when he lets go. The universe, being a living creature endowed with intelligence by its maker, then turns around by its own inherent nature and revolves in the opposite direction.

Young Socrates: Why does it do that?

Stranger: Because only the most divine things remain forever unchanged and constant, and the physical universe isn't in that category. The heavens and the cosmos, glorious as they are — having been richly gifted by the Creator — still partake of bodily nature, and so they can't be entirely free from change. But their motion is as uniform and steady as possible; it takes the form of a reversal, which is the smallest possible alteration. Only the lord of all moving things can truly move by himself. And to say that he sometimes pushes the universe one way and sometimes the other would be impious. So here's the picture: sometimes the universe is guided by a divine external power, and it receives fresh life and a kind of immortality from the Creator's renewing hand. Other times, when it's released, it moves on its own. It can do this for immense cycles of time because of its perfect balance, vast size, and the fact that it turns on the tiniest possible pivot — it reverses direction and runs the other way.

Young Socrates: That's a very reasonable account.

Stranger: Now let's think about what follows from this and grasp the phenomenon that explains all these wonders. It's this:

Young Socrates: What?

Stranger: The periodic reversal of the universe's motion.

Young Socrates: How does that explain everything?

Stranger: Of all possible changes in the heavenly motions, this is the greatest and most complete.

Young Socrates: I'd imagine so.

Stranger: And it would naturally produce the greatest changes for the beings living in the universe at the time.

Young Socrates: That makes sense.

Stranger: And we know that living creatures don't survive well when hit by many dramatic changes all at once.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: So a massive destruction necessarily follows — wiping out most of the human race as well. The few survivors are then subject to a series of remarkable experiences, the most notable of which occurs during the transition to the cycle opposite the one we're living in now.

Young Socrates: What experience is that?

Stranger: First, every living thing stopped aging. Mortal nature ceased to grow older — in fact, it reversed. Everything began growing younger and softer. The white hair of the elderly darkened again. The cheeks of bearded men grew smooth and regained their youthful bloom. The bodies of young men in their prime grew smaller and softer, returning day by day and night by night toward the condition of a newborn child — in mind as well as body. Then they continued to waste away until they vanished entirely. And those who had died by violence at the time of the reversal passed through these same changes even faster, disappearing within days.

Young Socrates: But then how were animals created in that era, Stranger? How did they reproduce?

Stranger: They didn't reproduce from each other — that's the point. There was no procreation as we know it. Instead, there was the earthborn race we hear about in legend — people who rose up out of the ground. Our earliest ancestors, who lived closest to the end of the previous cycle and the beginning of this one, are the ones who passed down these traditions to us. And notice how the rest of the story fits together: after age returns to youth, the dead who lie in the earth return to life. The wheel of generation turns backward along with the reversal of the cosmos, and the dead are reassembled and rise again, living in the opposite direction — unless a god has taken some of them away to a different fate. That's why they're called "earthborn," and that's where the legend comes from.

Young Socrates: Yes, that all fits perfectly. But tell me — the blessed, spontaneous life you described under the reign of Cronus — did that belong to the previous cycle or this one? Because the reversal of the stars and sun would have occurred in both.

Stranger: Good — you're following me closely. No, that blessed and effortless life doesn't belong to our current cycle. It belongs to the previous one, when God oversaw the entire revolution of the universe. The various regions of the world were each assigned to subordinate divine beings, the way some places still have local gods today. These demigods were the shepherds of each species and herd of animals, each one providing everything his flock needed. There was no violence, no creatures devouring one another, no war, no conflict of any kind. I could list ten thousand other blessings of that era. The reason human life was said to be "spontaneous" is this: God himself was their shepherd and ruled over them directly, just as humans — who are comparatively godlike — still rule over the lower animals. Under his care, there were no governments, no private ownership of wives and children. Everyone was born from the earth with no memory of the past. They didn't need any of that. The earth gave them fruit in abundance — growing on trees and bushes without being planted or cultivated. They lived naked, mostly outdoors, because the climate was always mild. They had no beds but lay on soft couches of grass that sprang up plentifully from the ground. That was human life in the age of Cronus, Socrates. Our current life, which is said to be under Zeus — well, you know that from your own experience. So which era would you say was the happier one?

Young Socrates: Impossible to say.

Stranger: Want me to decide for you, as best I can?

Young Socrates: Please do.

Stranger: Well, suppose the nurslings of Cronus, with all that boundless leisure and the ability to converse not just with each other but with animals too — suppose they used all those advantages for philosophy, talking with beasts and with one another, and learning from every creature that had some special ability or insight to contribute to the store of wisdom. In that case, it would be easy to see that they were a thousand times happier than people today. On the other hand, if they just ate and drank until they were stuffed, and told stories to each other and to the animals — the kind of stories still attributed to that age — well, that answer would be easy too. But until someone can tell us whether the people of that era loved knowledge and discussion, we'd better let the question drop and get back to the point of the story.

In the fullness of time, when the change was due, and the earthborn race had all passed away, and every soul had completed its appointed number of rebirths and been sown into the earth the required number of times — then the Pilot of the universe let go of the helm and withdrew to his place of observation. At that moment, Fate and the universe's own innate tendency reversed its motion. All the subordinate deities who shared in governing the world, understanding what was happening, also let go of the regions under their control.

And the universe, turning around with a sudden jolt — driven in the opposite direction from beginning to end — was shaken by a tremendous earthquake that caused a new wave of destruction among all living things. Afterward, when enough time had passed, the chaos and confusion and trembling subsided. The great living creature found its calm and settled back into its own orderly course, taking charge of itself and everything within it, carrying out the instructions of its Father and Creator as best it could remember them — more precisely at first, but with diminishing accuracy over time.

The reason for this decline was the element of matter in its nature — the legacy of its original primordial state, which had been full of chaos before achieving its present order. From God the Creator, the world received everything good in it. But from its former condition came seeds of evil and disorder, which the world first absorbed and then passed on to the animals within it.

As long as the divine Pilot was helping nurture the animals, evil was small and good was abundant. But after the separation, when the world was left on its own, at first things went well enough. But as time passed, the forgetting grew worse and worse. The old discord regained its hold and eventually burst forth in full force. Good became scarce and evil overwhelming, until there was a danger of the world and everything in it being destroyed in the storm and sinking into infinite chaos.

That's when God — the orderer of all things — in his tender care, seeing the world in dire straits and fearing that everything might dissolve into chaos, sat down once more at the helm. He gathered up the elements that had fallen into dissolution and disorder, restored the motion that had prevailed under his guidance, set everything back in order, and made the world deathless and immortal once more.

And that is the complete tale. But the first part is what we need for understanding the king.

When the world turned toward our current cycle, the aging process resumed. The tiny creatures that had nearly disappeared began growing again. The newly born children of the earth grew gray, died, and sank back into the ground. Everything changed to match the condition of the universe, necessarily following its pattern in conception, generation, and growth. No animal was any longer brought into being through divine agency — the world was now its own master, and all its parts were meant to grow, reproduce, and sustain themselves under the same self-governing principle.

And so we've arrived at the real purpose of this story. There would be much more to say about the lower animals and the conditions from which they changed, but about human beings, there's less to say — and it's more to the point. Deprived of God's care — the God who had possessed and tended them — humans were left helpless and defenseless. They were torn apart by wild beasts, which had become fierce now that they were no longer under divine control. In those early ages, humans had no skills and no resources. The food that once grew on its own had failed, and they didn't know how to produce their own, because they'd never had to. Pressed by every kind of necessity, the gifts that tradition tells us the gods gave to humanity were bestowed — along with whatever teaching and education was essential. Fire came from Prometheus. The crafts came from Hephaestus and his partner Athena. Seeds and plants came from other gods. From all of these, human civilization was built. For divine care had failed, as I was saying, and people had to manage their own affairs, to be their own masters — just like the universe they imitate, forever changing, living and growing now one way, now another.

Enough of the story. Now let's see how it helps us understand where we went wrong in our earlier portrait of the king and statesman.

Young Socrates: What was the great error you're talking about?

Stranger: There were two. The first was smaller, the second was much larger and grander.

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: When we were asked about a king and statesman of our present age, we actually described the shepherd of the human flock from the other cycle — a being who was a god, when he should have been a man. That was the greater error. The lesser error was that we declared him ruler of the entire state without spelling out how he rules. That wasn't completely wrong, but it wasn't clear or complete either.

Young Socrates: Very good.

Stranger: So before we can have a perfect portrait of the statesman, we need to define the nature of his office.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: And the myth was introduced for two reasons: not just to show that all others are rivals of the true shepherd — the one we're searching for — but also to give us a clearer picture of the one person who deserves the title. He alone among shepherds and herdsmen, in keeping with our analogy, has the care of human beings.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: And I can't help thinking, Socrates, that the divine shepherd from the age of Cronus is on a much higher level than any king. The statesmen of our own time are much more like their subjects — much closer to them in nature, upbringing, and education.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: Still, we need to investigate them all the same — to see whether they stand above their subjects or on the same level.

Young Socrates: Of course.

Stranger: Let's pick up where we were. Remember, we spoke of self-directed authority exercised over animals — not individually but collectively — which we called the art of herd management?

Young Socrates: Yes, I remember.

Stranger: That's where we went wrong. We never actually included the Statesman — he had no place in our classification.

Young Socrates: How did that happen?

Stranger: All other herdsmen "rear" their herds. But "rearing" isn't quite the right term for what the Statesman does. We need a word that applies to all of them.

Young Socrates: True, if such a word exists.

Stranger: Well, isn't "care" applicable to all? "Caring for" herds implies no specific activity like feeding — it's broad enough. We could say "tending," "managing," or "having the care of" herds, and the same word would cover everyone, including the Statesman. That seems to be what the argument requires.

Young Socrates: Exactly right. But what's the next step in the division?

Stranger: The same as before. We divided the art of "rearing" herds by land versus water, winged versus walking, mixed-breeding versus pure-breeding, horned versus hornless. We can now divide the art of "caring for" herds along the same lines, covering both the kingship of today and the rule of Cronus.

Young Socrates: That's clear. But I'm still asking — what comes next?

Stranger: If we'd used the word "managing" instead of "feeding" or "rearing" from the start, no one could have argued that there's no care for humans in the case of the statesman. The objection was fair — plenty of people have a better claim to feeding humans than any king does. But no other art has a better claim to the overall care of human society and the right to rule people in general than the royal science.

Young Socrates: Quite true.

Stranger: Now, Socrates, we have to recognize another major error at the end of our analysis.

Young Socrates: What was it?

Stranger: Even if we were absolutely certain that an art of rearing bipeds existed, we had no right to immediately call it the royal or political art, as if the job were done.

Young Socrates: Certainly not.

Stranger: As we were saying, we first needed to rename it — to shift from "feeding" to "caring" — and then to divide further. There may be significant divisions still to make.

Young Socrates: What kinds of divisions?

Stranger: First, separating the divine shepherd from the human guardian.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And the art of human guardianship itself needs to be subdivided.

Young Socrates: On what principle?

Stranger: Voluntary versus compulsory.

Young Socrates: Why?

Stranger: Because we made a crucial mistake earlier — we were too naive. We lumped the king and the tyrant together, when they're completely different, just as their modes of government are completely different.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: So now let's correct that and divide the care of human beings into two parts: voluntary and compulsory.

Young Socrates: Agreed.

Stranger: And if we call the compulsory management of people by violent rulers tyranny, and the voluntary management of willing herds of willing bipeds politics — then can we say that the person who possesses this art of voluntary management is the true king and statesman?

Young Socrates: I think so, Stranger. It seems like we've finally completed our account of the Statesman.

Stranger: That would be nice, Socrates. But I have to satisfy myself as well as you. And in my judgment, the figure of the king isn't finished yet. Like sculptors who get carried away and make parts too big in their rush, then have to waste time cutting them back down — we too, partly from haste, partly from a grand desire to expose our earlier mistake, and partly because we thought a king deserved grand illustrations — we've taken up a huge lump of myth and had to use more material than was necessary. The story never quite came to an end, and yet our discussion could be compared to a painting that's been outlined nicely but hasn't yet received the life and vividness that come from blending colors. Of course, for intelligent people, a living being is better portrayed in words and argument than in any painting or sculpture.

Young Socrates: Very true. But what imperfection still remains? Tell me.

Stranger: The highest ideas, my friend, can hardly be expressed without using examples. Every person seems to know everything in a dreamy, half-conscious way, and then wakes up and realizes they know nothing.

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: I'm afraid I've awkwardly raised a question about our experience of knowledge.

Young Socrates: Why awkwardly?

Stranger: Because my "example" of examples itself needs an example.

Young Socrates: Go ahead — don't worry about tiring me out.

Stranger: Good — you're an eager listener. Think about when children are first learning their letters.

Young Socrates: What about it?

Stranger: They can recognize individual letters perfectly well in short, easy syllables, and identify them correctly.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: But when those same letters show up in other, harder syllables, the children get confused. They misidentify them — they think and speak incorrectly about them.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: Now, isn't the best and easiest way to teach them the letters they don't yet know —

Young Socrates: To do what?

Stranger: To first take them back to cases where they correctly identified those same letters, and then set those cases side by side with the ones they got wrong — showing them that the letters are exactly the same in both combinations, and always have the same character and the same name. Once you've lined up all the cases they got right alongside all the cases they got wrong, you've given them examples. And through these examples, they learn that each letter is always itself, always the same, regardless of where it appears.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: So this is how examples work: we take something, compare it with a distinct instance of the same thing that we already understand correctly, and out of the comparison, a single true concept emerges that covers both cases.

Young Socrates: Exactly.

Stranger: Is it any wonder, then, that the mind has the same kind of trouble with the alphabet of reality? Sometimes, in simple cases, it grasps the truth firmly. But then, when those same elements show up in the long and difficult syllables of actual experience, it gets confused all over again.

Young Socrates: Nothing surprising about that.

Stranger: And could anyone who starts from a false belief ever hope to arrive at even a small portion of truth and attain wisdom?

Young Socrates: Hardly.

Stranger: So you and I won't be far off if we try to understand the nature of "example" itself through a small, specific instance — and then move from lesser things to the grandest class, the royal class, and use systematic method to discover what the management of cities really is. That way our dream will become reality.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: So let's return to our earlier argument. Since there are countless rivals of the royal class who claim to have the care of states, we need to separate all of them from the true statesman. And as I was saying, we first need a model — an example of this process.

Young Socrates: Exactly.

Stranger: What model is small enough to work with, yet has a real analogy to the political art? How about this, Socrates: if we can't find anything better, let's choose weaving — specifically, the weaving of wool. That should be enough to illustrate our point without dragging in all of weaving.

Young Socrates: That works.

Stranger: Then let's apply to weaving the same process of division and subdivision we've been applying to everything else, moving as quickly as we can through the steps until we reach what we need.

Young Socrates: How do you mean?

Stranger: I'll show you by actually doing it.

Young Socrates: Very good.

Stranger: Everything we make or acquire is either creative or preventive. Preventive things include remedies — both divine and human — and also defenses. Defenses include military weapons and protective gear. Protective gear includes screens of various kinds, and shields against heat and cold. Shields against heat and cold include shelters and coverings. Coverings include blankets and garments. Garments are either single pieces or assembled from multiple parts. Among the assembled kind, some are stitched and some aren't. Among the unstitched, some are made from plant fibers and some from hair. Among the hair-based ones, some are cemented together with water and earth, and others hold together on their own. These last — defenses and coverings that are held together by themselves — we call clothes. And the art that produces them, taking its name from the nature of the work, we can call the art of clothing. Just as we derived "statesmanship" from the state, so the art of weaving — at least the largest part of it, which deals with making clothes — differs only in name from this art of clothing. Just as, in the previous case, the royal science differed only in name from the political science.

Young Socrates: Exactly right.

Stranger: Now let's notice something: the art of weaving clothes has been separated from several other arts in the same family, but not yet from the arts that cooperate with it.

Young Socrates: Which family arts do you mean?

Stranger: I see I've lost you. Let me back up and start from the end. Just now we separated the weaving of clothes from the making of blankets — the difference being that one is put around you and the other under you. Those are the "family arts" I meant.

Young Socrates: I understand.

Stranger: And we've also set aside all articles made from flax and cord, and everything I metaphorically called "plant sinews." We've also separated out the process of felting and the various arts of stitching and sewing, the most important of which is shoemaking.

Young Socrates: Precisely.

Stranger: Then we separated the currier's art, which makes coverings from whole pieces of leather, and the art of building shelters. We subtracted the various waterproofing arts used in construction and carpentry, and all the arts that provide barriers against theft and violence — making lids for containers and fixing doors, which are branches of the joiner's art. We also cut off the manufacture of weapons, which is a section of the vast art of making defenses. And at the very beginning, we separated off the entire art of making antidotes and magical remedies. What we're left with, apparently, is the very art we were looking for: the art of protection against cold weather, which fabricates woolen defenses, and which we call weaving.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: But that's not the whole story, my boy. Because the first step in working with the material is the opposite of weaving.

Young Socrates: How so?

Stranger: Weaving is a kind of joining together, right?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: But the first step is separating the tangled, matted fibers.

Young Socrates: What do you mean exactly?

Stranger: I mean carding. We can't say that carding is weaving, or that the carder is a weaver.

Young Socrates: Certainly not.

Stranger: And if someone called the art of making the warp and the woof "the art of weaving," they'd be saying something false and paradoxical.

Young Socrates: Of course.

Stranger: And what about fulling and mending? Do they have nothing to do with the care and treatment of clothes? Or should we call them weaving?

Young Socrates: Certainly not.

Stranger: And yet all these arts will insist that they're involved in the production and treatment of clothes. They'll contest weaving's claim to exclusive authority — conceding weaving the larger role, but reserving a substantial one for themselves.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: Besides all these, there are the arts that make the tools and instruments of weaving. They too will claim to be at least cooperative causes in every piece of weaving.

Young Socrates: Absolutely.

Stranger: So if we simply define weaving as the greatest and noblest of the arts concerned with woolen garments — is that definition complete? It's true, but isn't it unclear? Don't all those other arts need to be cleared away first?

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: Then our next step is to separate them out, so the argument can proceed in an orderly way.

Young Socrates: By all means.

Stranger: Let's start by noting that there are two kinds of arts involved in everything we do.

Young Socrates: What two kinds?

Stranger: One is cooperative — it provides the conditions. The other is the principal cause — it does the actual work.

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: Arts that don't produce the thing itself but supply the tools necessary for production — without which the actual crafts couldn't do their work — those are cooperative. Arts that produce the thing itself are causal.

Young Socrates: A very reasonable distinction.

Stranger: So the arts that make spindles, combs, and other tools for producing clothes are cooperative, while those that treat and fabricate the clothes themselves are causal.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: Among the causal arts, the work of washing, mending, and all the other preparatory treatments can be grouped together under what we call the fuller's art — it's a division of the broader art of adornment.

Young Socrates: Very good.

Stranger: Carding, spinning, and all the processes involved in actually manufacturing a woolen garment make up a single art that everyone recognizes — the art of working in wool.

Young Socrates: Of course.

Stranger: And wool-working itself has two divisions, each of which belongs simultaneously to two different universal arts.

Young Socrates: How's that?

Stranger: Carding, half the work of the comb, and the other processes that separate fibers apart — all of these belong both to wool-working and to one of two universal arts: the art of combination and the art of division. Specifically, they belong to division.

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: The art of dividing wool and yarn — done one way with the comb and another way by hand — goes by various names, but it all falls under this category.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: Now let's take a wool-working process that belongs to combination rather than division, and separate it into two halves — one for the combining aspect, one for the dividing aspect.

Young Socrates: Done.

Stranger: And now, Socrates, we need to divide the combining side of wool-working further, if we're ever going to satisfactorily pin down the art of weaving.

Young Socrates: We must.

Stranger: Very well. Let's call one part the art of twisting threads, and the other the art of interlacing them.

Young Socrates: By "twisting," do you mean making the warp?

Stranger: Yes — and the woof too. How else is the woof made, if not by twisting?

Young Socrates: There's no other way.

Stranger: Now, try defining the warp and the woof. I think the definitions will be useful.

Young Socrates: How should I define them?

Stranger: Like this: a piece of carded wool that's been drawn out in length and breadth is said to be "pulled out" or "drafted."

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: When this drafted wool is twisted by the spindle into a firm thread, that's called the warp, and the art governing this process is warp-spinning.

Young Socrates: Right.

Stranger: And the threads that are spun more loosely — with a softness proportional to how tightly the warp is interlaced, and how thoroughly the finished cloth is dressed — those are called the woof. The art governing them is woof-spinning.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: And now the nature of weaving — the part we set out to define — should be perfectly clear. When the combining branch of wool-working creates a fabric by regularly interlacing warp and woof, the whole woven product is called a woolen garment, and the art that oversees this is the art of weaving.

Young Socrates: Perfectly put.

Stranger: But then why didn't we just say from the start that weaving is the art of interlacing warp and woof? Why all this long, winding journey?

Young Socrates: I thought there was nothing useless in what you said, Stranger.

Stranger: You might not always feel that way, my friend — and in case any dissatisfaction arises later, let me lay down a principle that applies to arguments in general.

Young Socrates: Go ahead.

Stranger: Let's start by thinking about the whole nature of excess and deficiency. That will give us a rational basis for judging whether a discussion has been too long or too short.

Young Socrates: Let's do that.

Stranger: Here are the key points to consider.

Young Socrates: What are they?

Stranger: Length and shortness, excess and deficiency — the art of measurement deals with all of these.

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: And for our purposes, the art of measurement needs to be divided into two parts.

Young Socrates: Where do you draw the line?

Stranger: Like this: one part measures the relative greatness and smallness of things compared to each other. The other part measures something else — something without which production would be impossible.

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: Don't you think it's natural to say that "greater" only means greater than the "lesser," and "lesser" only means lesser than the "greater"?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: But isn't there also something that exceeds or falls short of a standard of due measure — both in speech and in action? And isn't this the chief mark that distinguishes good people from bad?

Young Socrates: Clearly.

Stranger: Then we have to recognize that "great" and "small" exist and are measured in two ways: not only relative to each other, but also in comparison to a standard — the right measure, the due proportion.

Young Socrates: I'd like to hear why.

Stranger: If we say "greater" only exists relative to "lesser" and vice versa, there will never be any comparison with a standard of due measure.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And wouldn't that destroy all the arts and everything they produce? Wouldn't statesmanship and weaving alike disappear? Because all these arts are constantly on guard against excess and deficiency — not as mere abstractions, but as real threats to the quality of their work. The excellence of every product of art depends on hitting the right measure.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: And if statesmanship disappears, the search for the royal science becomes impossible.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: So just as in our investigation of the Sophist we had to establish that not-being exists — because that's where the argument kept slipping away from us — so now we need to show that "greater" and "lesser" are measured not only against each other, but also against the standard of due proportion. Unless we establish this, we can't claim that anyone — statesman or otherwise — is a genuine master of their craft.

Young Socrates: Yes, we absolutely need to do that again.

Stranger: But Socrates, this is an even bigger project than the one about not-being — and remember how long that took! Still, I think we can fairly assume something like this:

Young Socrates: What?

Stranger: That someday we'll need this concept of a standard of measure when we try to demonstrate absolute truth. But for now, the argument that the very existence of the arts depends on the possibility of measuring things not only against each other but also against a standard of due measure — that argument provides strong and satisfying support for our position. After all, if there are arts, there must be a standard of measure. And if there's a standard of measure, there must be arts. Without one, you can't have the other.

Young Socrates: True. So what's next?

Stranger: The next step is clearly to divide the art of measurement into two parts, as we've been saying. In one part, place all the arts that measure number, length, depth, breadth, and speed against their opposites. In the other, place the arts that measure against the standard of due measure — the fitting, the opportune, the proper, and everything else that implies a mean between extremes.

Young Socrates: Those are two vast divisions, covering very different territory.

Stranger: Here's something that many clever people say, Socrates, thinking they're being wise: that measurement is a universal art that applies to everything. And in a sense they're right — everything that falls within the domain of art does involve measurement in some form. But these people, because they're not used to making distinctions based on real categories, jumble together two very different things — relative comparison and measurement against a standard — as if they were the same. They also make the opposite mistake: dividing things that should be grouped together. The right method is this: when you've first perceived the unity of things, keep investigating until you've found all the real differences within it that constitute distinct classes. And conversely, when you see a multitude of different things, don't rest until you've grasped everything they have in common and gathered them into a single real category. But we've said enough about this. Let's just remember that there are two divisions of measurement, and not forget what they are.

Young Socrates: We won't forget.

Stranger: Now that this discussion is behind us, let's consider a question that concerns not just this argument but the conduct of arguments in general.

Young Socrates: What question?

Stranger: Take the case of a child learning his letters. When he's asked what letters make up a particular word, is the exercise intended to improve his knowledge of that specific word? Or of all words?

Young Socrates: All words, clearly.

Stranger: And is our investigation of the Statesman intended only to improve our knowledge of politics? Or also our reasoning ability in general?

Young Socrates: Clearly the latter, just like the first example — the purpose is general.

Stranger: And nobody in their right mind would analyze the concept of weaving for its own sake. But people seem to forget something: some things have readily visible images that are easy to point out when someone asks about them. But the greatest and most important truths have no visible image at all. When you want to satisfy a questioner's soul about such things, you can't hold anything up for the eye to see — you can only present them through thought and reason. Everything we're saying now is for the sake of those higher truths. And it's always easier to practice the method on small things before tackling great ones.

Young Socrates: Very good.

Stranger: Now let's recall why we said all this.

Young Socrates: What was the point?

Stranger: I wanted to head off any impression of tediousness we might have created — in the discussion of weaving, the myth of the reversed universe, and the earlier discussion about the Sophist and not-being. I was worried they might seem not just long but irrelevant. Everything I've just said was meant to prevent that kind of dissatisfaction going forward.

Young Socrates: Good. Please continue.

STATESMAN (Part 2)

Stranger: So here's the principle I'd like both of us to remember: we should praise or blame the length or shortness of discussions not by comparing them with each other, but by measuring them against what's fitting — using that part of measurement we said we should keep in mind.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: But even fitness isn't the only standard. We shouldn't seek a length that pleases us — that's a secondary consideration at best. And reason tells us that ease and speed of inquiry should be our second priority, not our first. The highest priority of all is to uphold the great method of division according to real kinds — whether the discussion ends up longer or shorter. No one should be offended by length. Longer and shorter should be used freely, whichever is better for sharpening the minds of the participants. And if someone complains that a discussion is too long, they shouldn't just grumble about the length — they should prove that a shorter version would have made the participants better thinkers and more capable of finding the truth. Any other praise or blame isn't worth hearing.

But we've spent enough time on this, and you probably agree. Let's get back to our Statesman and apply the weaving example we've been developing.

Young Socrates: Good — let's do that.

Stranger: The king's art has been separated from the similar arts of shepherds, and indeed from all the arts that deal with herds. But there remain the causal and cooperative arts that are directly involved in running a state, and these need to be distinguished from one another.

Young Socrates: Very good.

Stranger: You should know that these won't divide neatly into two halves. The reason will become clear as we proceed.

Young Socrates: Then let's get on with it.

Stranger: We'll have to carve them up like butchering an animal at the joints, since we can't bisect them cleanly. We should still divide into as few parts as possible.

Young Socrates: What do we do in this case?

Stranger: The same thing we did with weaving: all the arts that make tools were classified as cooperative.

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: Just so, all the arts that produce any kind of implement in a state — large or small — should be considered cooperative. Without them, there would be no state and no statesmanship. But we're not inclined to say that any of them is a product of the royal art itself.

Young Socrates: No, indeed.

Stranger: Separating this class from the rest is tricky, because there's always a plausible case that anything in the world is "a tool for doing something." But there's another class of possessions in a city that I want to mention.

Young Socrates: What class?

Stranger: A class that doesn't have this productive power. Unlike a tool, it's not designed for making things — it's designed for preserving what's already been made.

Young Socrates: What are you referring to?

Stranger: The broad class of containers — vessels of all kinds, built for preserving both liquids and solids, things prepared over fire and things prepared without it. It's a very large class, and if I'm not mistaken, it has absolutely nothing to do with the royal art we're investigating.

Young Socrates: Certainly not.

Stranger: There's also a third class of possessions to note — very different from these, and also very extensive. Things that move or rest, on land or water, honorable and dishonorable. This entire class has one name, because everything in it is designed to be sat upon — it's always a seat for something.

Young Socrates: What is it?

Stranger: A vehicle. And that's certainly not the work of the Statesman — it belongs to the carpenter, the potter, and the coppersmith.

Young Socrates: I see.

Stranger: And isn't there a fourth class, again distinct, that contains most of the things we've mentioned? Every kind of clothing, most types of weapons, walls and enclosures of earth and stone, and a thousand other things? All of these are made for defense, and should properly be considered the work of the builder or the weaver, not the Statesman.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: Should we add a fifth class — ornamentation and drawing, and all the imitations produced by visual art and music, designed purely for amusement? They can all be covered by one name.

Young Socrates: What name?

Stranger: Plaything.

Young Socrates: Of course.

Stranger: That one word fits them all, because none of these things has a serious purpose. Entertainment is their only aim.

Young Socrates: That I understand.

Stranger: Then there's a class that provides raw materials for all the others — the stuff from which the arts I've mentioned fabricate their products. This varied class, the offspring of many different arts — can I count it as the sixth?

Young Socrates: What do you mean exactly?

Stranger: Gold, silver, and other metals; everything that timber-cutting and textile-shearing provide for carpentry and plaiting; the process of stripping bark and skin from plants and animals — the currier's art and similar crafts. These make cork, papyrus, cord, and enable the production of complex things from simple materials. The whole class can be called the primitive, unprocessed materials of human life — and the royal science has nothing whatsoever to do with it.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: The provision of food and everything else that mingles with the particles of the human body to sustain it — this makes a seventh class, which we can call nourishment, unless you have a better term. This belongs to the farmer, the hunter, the athletic trainer, the doctor, and the cook — not to the Statesman's art.

Young Socrates: Certainly not.
