Stranger: So these seven classes cover nearly every kind of property, except for tame animals. Let me review: first, raw materials — which should really have come first. Then tools, containers, vehicles, defenses, playthings, and nourishment. I'm leaving out small items like coins, seals, and stamps, which don't really form a major category of their own — some can be squeezed in with ornaments, others with tools. As for the management of tame animals — which we've already divided into parts — that covers all animal property except one kind: slaves.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: And it's in the class of slaves and attendants that I suspect we'll finally discover the real pretenders to the throne — the rivals of the king in weaving the political fabric. Just as spinners, carders, and the rest were the rivals of the weaver. All the others — the ones we called cooperators — have been cleared away among the occupations already mentioned, separated from the royal and political science.

Young Socrates: I agree.

Stranger: Let's move closer and examine these remaining figures more carefully.

Young Socrates: Let's.

Stranger: Looking from our current vantage point, the most obvious servants turn out to be in the opposite situation from what we might have expected.

Young Socrates: Who are they?

Stranger: People who have been purchased and thereby become property. These are unmistakably slaves, and they certainly don't claim to possess the royal science.

Young Socrates: Certainly not.

Stranger: What about free people who voluntarily serve the other classes in a state — who exchange and equalize the products of farming and the other arts? Some sit in the marketplace, others travel from city to city by land or sea, trading money for goods or goods for money — the money-changers, the merchants, the ship-owners, the retailers. Do any of them claim statesmanship or political science?

Young Socrates: No — unless you mean the politics of commerce.

Stranger: And surely the hired laborers and day workers, who are happy to turn their hand to anything for pay — they don't claim to share in the royal science?

Young Socrates: Certainly not.

Stranger: But what about some other class of public servants?

Young Socrates: Who are they? What services do they perform?

Stranger: Heralds, and scribes perfected through long practice, and various other highly skilled officials connected with the administration of government — what do we call them?

Young Socrates: They're officials and servants of the rulers, as you just said — not rulers themselves.

Stranger: There might seem to be something odd about a servant claiming to be a ruler. And yet I have a feeling that the principal claimants to political science are lurking somewhere in this neighborhood.

Young Socrates: Indeed.

Stranger: Well, let's move closer and test some claimants we haven't examined yet. First, there are the diviners — they have a share of ministerial knowledge and are considered interpreters of the gods to human beings.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: There's also the priestly class. According to tradition, priests know how to give the gods gifts from us in the form of sacrifices the gods will accept, and how to ask for blessings in return. Both of these — divination and priesthood — are branches of ministerial art.

Young Socrates: Yes, clearly.

Stranger: And here I think we're getting warm. Priests and diviners are swollen with pride and prestige. The grandeur of their undertakings inspires genuine awe. In Egypt, the king himself isn't allowed to rule unless he has priestly authority, and if he comes from another class and seizes power, he has to be enrolled in the priesthood. Throughout Greece, the duty of performing the most solemn public sacrifices is assigned to the highest officials. And right here in Athens, the most ancient and sacred national sacrifices are supposed to be performed by the King Archon — the man chosen by lot.

Young Socrates: Precisely.

Stranger: But who are these other kings and priests, chosen by lot, who now come into view — followed by their retinues and a vast crowd — as the previous group exits and the scene changes?

Young Socrates: Who do you mean?

Stranger: A very strange crew.

Young Socrates: How strange?

Stranger: A moment ago, I thought they were animals of every kind. Many of them look like lions and centaurs; many more like satyrs and other weak, shape-shifting creatures — all rapidly changing forms and natures like Proteus. And now, Socrates, I think I finally see who they are.

Young Socrates: Who? You seem to be gazing at some strange vision.

Stranger: Yes — everyone looks strange when you don't know them. And that's exactly what just happened to me. At first sight, coming on them suddenly, I didn't recognize the politician and his troupe.

Young Socrates: Who are they?

Stranger: The chief of Sophists. The most accomplished wizard of them all. He must be separated from the true king and statesman at any cost, if we're ever going to see daylight in this investigation.

Young Socrates: That's not a hope we can afford to abandon.

Stranger: Never, if I can help it. But first, a question.

Young Socrates: What?

Stranger: Monarchy is a recognized form of government?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: And after monarchy, next comes the rule of the few?

Young Socrates: Of course.

Stranger: And the third form is the rule of the many, called democracy?

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: And don't these three expand in a sense into five, producing two additional names from within themselves?

Young Socrates: What are they?

Stranger: People today apply certain criteria — voluntary versus involuntary, wealth versus poverty, law versus lawlessness. Using these criteria, they split the first two forms: monarchy divides into royalty and tyranny.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: And the rule of the few divides into aristocracy and oligarchy.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: Democracy, on the other hand — whether it strictly observes the laws or not, whether the majority rules over the wealthy with their consent or without it — always keeps the same name.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: But do you think any form of government defined by these characteristics — one ruler, few rulers, many rulers; rich or poor; voluntary or forced; written law or no law — can be the right one?

Young Socrates: Why not?

Stranger: Think carefully. Follow me.

Young Socrates: Where?

Stranger: Are we going to stand by what we said at the start, or take it back?

Young Socrates: What did we say?

Stranger: We said that royal power is a form of knowledge — a science.

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: And a distinctive kind of science at that — one we selected from all the rest as having both a judicial and a directive character.

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: And within the directive branch, we distinguished authority over lifeless things from authority over living beings, and proceeded step by step to this point — never losing the idea of science, but unable to determine exactly what kind of science it is.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: Then we're led to this conclusion: the distinguishing principle of the state can't be "the few or the many," or "willing or unwilling," or "rich or poor." Some concept of science has to be part of it, if we're going to be consistent with everything we've said.

Young Socrates: And we must be consistent.

Stranger: So the question is: in which of these various forms of government is the science of governing actually found — the science that's among the greatest and most difficult of all to acquire? We need to discover that, and then we'll be able to see who the false politicians are — the ones who claim to be statesmen but aren't, even though they've fooled a lot of people — and separate them from the truly wise ruler.

Young Socrates: Yes, as our argument has already indicated, that's exactly what we need to do.

Stranger: Do you think the general population of a state could attain this political science?

Young Socrates: Impossible.

Stranger: But in a city of a thousand people, maybe a hundred could? Or say fifty?

Young Socrates: If that were the case, political science would be the easiest of all sciences! You couldn't find that many top-ranked board-game players in a city that size, judged against the best in all of Greece. And you'd certainly find even fewer genuine kings. Remember: we can rightly call anyone who possesses the royal science a king, whether they actually hold power or not. That's what our earlier argument showed.

Stranger: Good reminder. The consequence is that any true form of government can only be the government of one, or two, or at most a few.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: And these rulers — whether they govern with their subjects' consent or without it, with written laws or without them, whether they're rich or poor, whatever the outward form — must be understood to rule on the basis of a scientific principle. It's exactly like a doctor: whether he treats us with or against our will, whether he cuts or burns or inflicts some other painful treatment, whether he works from a textbook or improvises, whether he's rich or poor, whether he purges us or slims us down or even fattens us up — he's a doctor all the same, as long as he exercises authority through genuine medical knowledge, and as long as he actually does his patients good, healing and saving them. And this we lay down as the only proper test of any art of healing — or any art of command.

Young Socrates: Exactly right.

Stranger: Then the only true form of government is the one where the rulers genuinely possess this science and aren't mere pretenders — whether they rule with or without laws, over willing or unwilling subjects, and whether they're personally rich or poor. None of those factors can properly be included in the definition of a ruler.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And whether they serve the public good by purging the state — exiling some citizens, executing others — or whether they shrink the population by sending out colonies like bees swarming from a hive, or enlarge it by bringing in outsiders and granting them citizenship: as long as they act according to wisdom and justice, using their power for the general security and improvement of the state — a state with these characteristics deserves to be called the only true state. All other governments are imitations of this one, some better and some worse. The better imitations are said to be well governed, but they're still just imitations.

Young Socrates: Most of what you've said seems right, Stranger. But the part about ruling without laws — that's a hard thing to hear.

Stranger: You're a step ahead of me, Socrates! I was just about to ask whether you objected to anything. I see we'll need to take up this question of good government without laws.

Young Socrates: Indeed we must.

Stranger: There's no doubt that lawmaking is in some sense the business of a king. And yet the best thing of all is not for the law to rule, but for a wise person to rule — one who possesses wisdom and royal power. Do you see why?

Young Socrates: Why?

Stranger: Because law can't perfectly capture what's most noble and just for everyone, and therefore can't enforce what's truly best. People are different. Actions are different. And the endless, irregular movement of human affairs doesn't allow for any universal, simple rule. No art whatsoever can lay down a principle that will hold good for all time.

Young Socrates: Of course not.

Stranger: But that's exactly what the law is always trying to do — like a stubborn, ignorant tyrant who won't allow anything to be done contrary to his orders, or any questions to be asked. Even when circumstances suddenly change and something would clearly be better for someone than what the law commands — the law won't budge.

Young Socrates: That's exactly how the law treats us.

Stranger: A perfectly simple principle can never be properly applied to a state of affairs that's anything but simple.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: Then if law isn't the perfection of justice, why do we make laws at all? That's the next question to investigate.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: Tell me — don't you have athletic competitions in your city, like in other cities, where people compete in running, wrestling, and so on?

Young Socrates: Yes, they're very common here.

Stranger: And what kind of rules do the professional trainers impose on their athletes? Can you recall?

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: The trainers don't create individualized programs for each person, tailored precisely to their constitution. They work more broadly, prescribing a general regimen that will benefit the majority.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: They assign the same amount of exercise to everyone. They send them out together, let them rest together — whether they're running, wrestling, or doing anything else.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: Now notice: the legislator who has to preside over the whole community and enforce justice can't be expected to prescribe exactly what's right for every individual case, either.

Young Socrates: He certainly can't.

Stranger: He'll lay down laws in general terms for the majority — roughly covering individual cases. Some of these he'll put in writing; others will remain as unwritten traditional customs.

Young Socrates: That makes sense.

Stranger: Of course it does. Because how could anyone sit at every person's side throughout their entire life, prescribing exactly what they should do at each moment? Who could manage that? No one who truly possessed the royal science — if they could actually do this — would impose the constraint of a written law on themselves.

Young Socrates: That follows from what we've said.

Stranger: Or rather, my friend, from what's about to be said.

Young Socrates: And what's that?

Stranger: Picture a doctor or a trainer who's about to travel abroad and expects to be away for a long time. Worried that his patients or athletes won't remember his instructions, he writes them down as notes for them to follow.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: But what if he came back sooner than expected? And what if, due to some unexpected change — unusual weather or some other unforeseen factor — a different treatment turned out to be better? Would he refuse to deviate from his original prescription? Would he stubbornly stick to the old rules, never issuing new instructions, while his patients dared not do anything other than what was originally written — on the grounds that only the prescribed course was healthy, and anything else was harmful and wrong? Viewed in the light of genuine science and real expertise, wouldn't all such rigidity be utterly absurd?

Young Socrates: Utterly.

Stranger: And what about the lawgiver — the one who established written or unwritten rules about what's good or bad, honorable or dishonorable, just or unjust, for the communities of people who live together in cities and are governed by these rules? If the wise lawgiver himself were to return — or someone like him — should he be prohibited from changing the laws? Wouldn't that prohibition be every bit as absurd as the medical case?

Young Socrates: Absolutely.

Stranger: Do you know a popular saying that's relevant here?

Young Socrates: I can't think which one you mean at the moment.

Stranger: People say that if someone knows how to improve the existing laws, he must first persuade his own state to accept the improvement — and only then may he change the laws.

Young Socrates: And aren't they right about that?

Stranger: Perhaps. But suppose a wise person uses a bit of gentle force for his people's own good — what do we call that? Actually, before you answer, let me frame the question using our earlier examples.

Young Socrates: Go ahead.

Stranger: Suppose a skilled doctor has a patient — of whatever age or sex — and compels that patient to do something for their own good that goes against the written rules. What do we call this compulsion? Would anyone dream of calling it a violation of the medical art, or a breach of the laws of health? Nothing could be more unjust than for the patient to accuse the doctor of malpractice or of making the disease worse.

Young Socrates: Absolutely true.

Stranger: In political life, the equivalent of "disease" isn't called disease — it's called evil, or disgrace, or injustice.

Young Socrates: Right.

Stranger: So when a citizen is compelled — against the written law and against custom — to do what's actually juster, better, and nobler than what they were doing before, the very last thing anyone should say about this use of force is that it's disgraceful, evil, or unjust.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: Then let me ask: does it matter whether the person exercising this force is rich or poor? With or without written laws? With or against the citizens' consent? The real question is this: isn't the true principle of government that the wise and good person will direct the affairs of the community in the way that's best for it? Just as a pilot — by continuously watching over the interests of the ship and crew, not by laying down rigid rules but by making his expertise the law — preserves the lives of his fellow sailors? In the same way, couldn't a true form of government be created by people who govern in this same spirit, making the strength of their art superior to the law? Wise rulers can't go wrong as long as they maintain this one great principle: distributing justice to the citizens with intelligence and skill, preserving them, and as far as possible making them better rather than worse.

Young Socrates: No one can argue with that.

Stranger: And no one can argue with the other statement either.

Young Socrates: Which one?

Stranger: That no large number of people, whoever they may be, can attain genuine political knowledge. True government must be found in a small group, or in an individual. All other states are merely imitations of this — some better, some worse.

Young Socrates: What do you mean? I'm not sure I followed your earlier remark about imitations.

Stranger: And yet the point is extremely important, even if we just raise it and move on without fully exploring it.

Young Socrates: What point?

Stranger: Here's the idea — it's not easy or familiar, but let me try to put it this way: suppose the government I've been describing is the only true model. Then all the other forms of government must rely on the written laws of this model if they want to survive. They'll have to follow the practice that most people now approve — even though it's not the best thing in the world.

Young Socrates: What practice?

Stranger: That no citizen should do anything contrary to the laws, and anyone who violates them should be punished with the most extreme penalties, including death. This is perfectly right and good — as a second-best option, once you set aside the first and best option I just described. Let me explain why I call this the second best.

Young Socrates: Please do.

Stranger: I need to go back to my favorite analogies. Only through them can I describe kings and rulers.

Young Socrates: What analogies?

Stranger: The noble pilot and the wise physician — "worth many another man," as the saying goes. Let's use them to discover an image of the king.

Young Socrates: What kind of image?

Stranger: Here's one: think about what we actually suffer at the hands of doctors and pilots. A doctor can save whoever he wants to save, but he can also mistreat whoever he wants — cutting them, burning them, causing pain, and on top of it all demanding payments that amount to a kind of tax on the sick, most of which goes to the doctor and his household rather than to the patient. In the end, he might even take money from the patient's relatives or enemies and finish the patient off. And pilots are just as bad — they intentionally leave you stranded on shore when the ship's about to sail, they cause disasters at sea, they dump the cargo overboard, and are guilty of all kinds of treachery.

Now suppose that, keeping all this in mind, we decided that neither of these arts should be allowed to exercise authority without oversight — over free people or over slaves. So we convene an assembly, either of all the citizens or only the wealthy, and anyone at all — whether they're in the profession or not — is allowed to offer opinions about how to navigate ships or treat diseases: what surgical instruments and medicines to use on patients, what nautical equipment to employ, how to handle dangers from wind and waves, what to do when encountering pirates, and how to fight naval battles with old-fashioned ships. And whatever the crowd decides, based on advice from both experts and non-experts alike — all of that gets inscribed on tablets and columns, or enacted as unwritten national customs. And from that point on, all navigation and all medical treatment must be conducted according to these regulations.

Young Socrates: What a bizarre idea!

Stranger: Now suppose further that the pilots and doctors are appointed annually — chosen either from the wealthy classes or from the whole population by lot. And once elected, they navigate ships and treat the sick strictly according to the written rules.

Young Socrates: That's even worse.

Stranger: But wait — there's more. When the year of office has expired, the pilot or doctor must appear before a review board. The judges are either drawn from the wealthy or chosen by lot from the entire population. And anyone who wants to can bring charges — claiming that during the past year, these officials didn't navigate their ships or heal their patients according to the letter of the law and the ancient customs of their ancestors. If convicted, the judges determine the punishment or the fine.

Young Socrates: Anyone who'd willingly hold office under those conditions deserves whatever they get.

Stranger: And there's one more law we'd need to pass. If anyone is caught investigating the true nature of piloting and navigation, or studying health and the genuine principles of medicine, or inquiring into the winds or atmospheric conditions — in ways that go beyond the written rules — if he has any original ideas about such matters, he's not to be called a pilot or a doctor, but a "stargazing windbag" — a Sophist. And on the grounds that he's corrupting the young by persuading them to practice medicine or piloting in unauthorized ways and exercise arbitrary authority over patients or ships, anyone who meets the legal requirements can file charges against him. If he's found guilty of persuading anyone — young or old — to act contrary to the written law, he's to be punished with the utmost severity. Because no one should presume to be wiser than the laws. After all, the principles of healing and navigation are available to everyone — anyone can learn the written laws and national customs.

Now, Socrates — if this is how we ran things in all the sciences — in generalship, in hunting, in painting, in every branch of craftsmanship, in farming, in horse-breeding, in herding, in divination, in any kind of service work, in board games, in every mathematical science, in every art involving motion — if everything were done according to written regulations instead of genuine expertise — what would happen?

Young Socrates: Every art would be utterly destroyed, with no hope of recovery — because inquiry itself would be illegal. And human life, which is already hard enough, would become completely unbearable.

Stranger: And what if, on top of forcing all these activities to follow written law, we appointed someone — elected by show of hands or by lot — to be the guardian of the laws, and this person ignored the laws and acted against them out of self-interest or favoritism, without any real knowledge? Wouldn't that be even worse than the previous scenario?

Young Socrates: Much worse.

Stranger: Because to go against laws that are based on long experience — laws that reflect the wisdom of advisors who carefully recommended them and persuaded the public to adopt them — that would be a far greater and more destructive error than mere rigid adherence to written law.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: Therefore, since this danger exists, the next-best policy is this: don't allow either any individual or any group to violate the laws in any way whatsoever.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: The laws are copies of the truth — written-down versions of the real principles of action, drafted by people who actually have knowledge. They're the best approximation that writing can achieve.

Young Socrates: They certainly are.

Stranger: And as we were saying, a person who genuinely possesses knowledge — a true Statesman — will do many things within his own sphere by the light of his art, without worrying about the laws, whenever he judges that something better has come up than what he originally wrote down and prescribed for the time when he'd be away.

Young Socrates: Yes, we said that.

Stranger: And any individual or group who has established laws, and then acts contrary to those laws in pursuit of something genuinely better — they'd be doing exactly what the true Statesman does, to the best of their ability.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: Now, if they did this without knowledge, they'd be imitating the truth — and imitating it badly. But if they did it with knowledge, it wouldn't be imitation at all — it would be the genuine article.

Young Socrates: Exactly.

Stranger: And we've already established that no large number of people can acquire genuine knowledge of any art.

Young Socrates: Yes, we have.

Stranger: Then the royal or political art will never be attained by the wealthy class as a whole, or by the general population.

Young Socrates: Impossible.

Stranger: So the closest these lesser forms of government can ever come to the true government of the one scientific ruler is this: never do anything contrary to their own written laws and national customs.

Young Socrates: Very good.

Stranger: When the wealthy class imitates the true form by following the laws, that government is called aristocracy. When the wealthy disregard the laws, it's called oligarchy.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And when one individual rules according to law, in imitation of the one who truly knows — we call him a king. We give him the same name whether he rules by genuine knowledge or merely by correct opinion.

Young Socrates: That seems right.

Stranger: And when an individual truly possesses knowledge and rules — his title will certainly be the same: king. So the five common names for governments really collapse into one.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: But when a single ruler governs without law and without custom, imitating the true statesman while actually being driven by appetite and ignorance — pretending that the only way to act is to override the laws, when in fact he's motivated by selfishness and stupidity — shouldn't he be called a tyrant?

Young Socrates: Absolutely.

Stranger: And this, we believe, is the origin of the tyrant and the king, of oligarchies and aristocracies and democracies. People are disgusted by the idea of one-person rule. They can never be made to believe that anyone could actually be worthy of such authority — someone who is willing and able to rule with virtue and knowledge, dispensing justice fairly to all. They imagine that such a person would be a despot who'd wrong and kill and harm whoever he pleased. But if such a ruler as we describe actually existed, people would have to acknowledge that they'd be lucky to have him. He alone would be the happy ruler of a truly perfect state.

Young Socrates: Of course.

Stranger: But as it stands, the state isn't like a beehive with a natural-born leader who's obviously superior in both body and mind. And so people are forced to gather together, write laws, and try to get as close as they can to the true form of government.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And when the foundation of politics rests on nothing but the letter of the law and custom, with knowledge divorced from action — can we really be surprised at the miseries that exist, and always will exist, in states? Any other art built on such a foundation would destroy everything it touched. What's really remarkable is the natural resilience of the political bond. States have endured this kind of thing forever, and yet some of them survive — even though many, like ships at sea, founder and sink through the incompetence of their pilots and crews, who are completely ignorant of the most important truths. I mean people who know nothing about genuine politics, yet believe they've mastered it more thoroughly than any other science.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: So the question arises: among these imperfect forms of government, which is the least oppressive — though they're all oppressive? And which is the worst? This is somewhat beside our main topic, but considering how it affects everything we do, we need to examine it.

Young Socrates: Yes, we must.

Stranger: Here's a striking fact: of the three forms, the same one is simultaneously the hardest to live under and the easiest.

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: I'm talking about the three forms I mentioned at the start — monarchy, the rule of the few, and the rule of the many.

Young Socrates: Right.

Stranger: If we divide each of these, we get six, from which the true government can be distinguished as a seventh.

Young Socrates: How do you divide them?

Stranger: Monarchy splits into royalty and tyranny. The rule of the few splits into aristocracy — a name with an auspicious meaning — and oligarchy. And democracy, which was a single category before, must now be divided too.

Young Socrates: On what principle?

Stranger: The same one as before, even though the name stays the same in both cases. The distinction between ruling with law and ruling without law applies here too, just as it applies to the others.

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: This distinction didn't matter when we were looking for the perfect state, as we showed earlier. But now that we've set the perfect state aside, and only the other forms remain — the principle of law versus lawlessness divides each of them in two.

Young Socrates: That seems to follow.

Stranger: Monarchy, when bound by good laws, is the best of all six. Lawless monarchy is the most bitter and oppressive.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: The rule of the few, which sits between the one and the many, is also intermediate in quality — both its good and bad forms. But the rule of the many is weak in every respect — unable to accomplish either great good or great evil, compared with the others, because power is divided up too finely among too many people. This makes it the worst of all the lawful governments, and the best of all the lawless ones. If all governments are lawless, democracy is the most tolerable to live under. If all are well-ordered, democracy is the least desirable. Lawful monarchy — royalty — is the best of them all, with the exception of the seventh form: the rule of genuine knowledge. That stands above them all, the way God stands above human beings.

Young Socrates: You're absolutely right, and we should choose that above all.

Stranger: The members of all these states — except the one guided by genuine knowledge — should be viewed not as real statesmen but as partisans. They're defenders of the most monstrous illusions, and they're illusions themselves. Being the greatest imitators and magicians, they're also the greatest Sophists.

Young Socrates: It seems like the label "Sophist," after many twists and turns in the argument, has been very justly applied to politicians.

Stranger: And so our satirical drama has reached its finale. The troop of Centaurs and Satyrs — reluctant as they were to leave the stage — has at last been separated from political science.

Young Socrates: So it seems.

Stranger: But there remain characters who are even more troublesome, because they're more closely related to the king and harder to identify. Examining them is like refining gold.

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: Goldsmiths start by sifting away the obvious impurities — earth and stones and the like. What remains is a confused mixture of valuable elements related to gold — copper, silver, and other precious metals. These are only separated through the fire of further testing, until the gold is left pure and unalloyed.

Young Socrates: Yes, that's how it's said to be done.

Stranger: In the same way, we've sifted away everything alien and uncongenial from political science. What remains is precious and closely related: the noble arts of the general, the judge, and the higher form of oratory that allies itself with the royal art, persuading people to do what's just and helping to guide the helm of state. How do we clear away these final contenders, leaving the one we seek alone and pure?

Young Socrates: That's obviously what we have to try to do.

Stranger: If trying is all that's needed, he'll certainly come to light. And I think the analogy of music can help us reveal him. Let me ask you a question.

Young Socrates: What question?

Stranger: There is such a thing as learning music — or any of the practical arts, for that matter?

Young Socrates: There is.

Stranger: And is there a higher art or science that has the power to decide which of these arts should and shouldn't be learned?

Young Socrates: I'd say there is.

Stranger: And we'd agree this higher science is distinct from the ones it oversees?

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: And should the practical sciences be superior to this governing one? Or should no individual science outrank any other? Or should this governing science be the overseer and master of all the rest?

Young Socrates: The latter.

Stranger: You're saying that the science which decides whether we should learn something must be superior to the science that's being learned or taught?

Young Socrates: Far superior.

Stranger: And the science that determines whether we should use persuasion must be superior to the science that actually persuades?

Young Socrates: Of course.

Stranger: Good. Now, to which science do we assign the power of persuading a crowd through compelling stories rather than through teaching?

Young Socrates: That clearly belongs to rhetoric.

Stranger: And to which science do we assign the power of deciding whether we should use persuasion, or force, or neither — in dealing with any group of people?

Young Socrates: To the science that governs the arts of speech and persuasion.

Stranger: Which, if I'm not mistaken, would be politics.

Young Socrates: Exactly.

Stranger: So rhetoric is quickly distinguished from politics — it's a different species, but one that serves politics.

Young Socrates: Yes.

Stranger: Now, what about another kind of power?

Young Socrates: What kind?

Stranger: The science that deals with military operations against our enemies — is that a science or isn't it?

Young Socrates: How could anyone say that generalship and military tactics aren't a science?

Stranger: And is the art that decides when to go to war and when to make peace the same as generalship, or different?

Young Socrates: If we're being consistent with everything we've said, it must be different.

Stranger: And we must say this higher art rules the lower one, if we're not going to abandon our earlier principle.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And given how vast and formidable the entire art of war is — can we imagine any art superior to it except the truly royal art?

Young Socrates: No other.

Stranger: So the art of the general is merely ministerial — it serves but isn't itself political.

Young Socrates: Exactly.

Stranger: Now let's consider the righteous judge.

Young Socrates: Very good.

Stranger: Does the judge do anything beyond deciding whether the dealings between citizens are just or unjust — applying the standards he receives from the king and legislator? He shows his particular excellence only in this: that he's not corrupted by bribes, fear, pity, or any kind of favoritism or hostility into judging contrary to what the lawgiver has established.

Young Socrates: No, that's precisely what his office involves.

Stranger: Then the judge's power isn't royal. It's the power of a guardian of the law — a minister serving the royal power.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: Looking at all these sciences together, the conclusion is clear: none of them is the political or royal science. The truly royal art should not act directly itself, but should rule over those who can act. The king should know what is and isn't the right moment to take the initiative on matters of the highest importance, while others carry out his decisions.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And therefore the arts we've been describing — each concerned with its own specific domain, and having no authority over one another — properly have their own special names corresponding to their particular activities.

Young Socrates: I agree.

Stranger: But the science that oversees them all, that takes responsibility for the laws and for every aspect of the state's affairs, and that truly weaves them all into a single fabric — if we want to capture its essential nature in a name, we'd most accurately call it politics.

Young Socrates: Exactly so.

Stranger: So now that we've identified the various classes in a state, shall I analyze politics using the model that weaving provided?

Young Socrates: I very much wish you would.

Stranger: Then I need to describe the nature of the royal web — how the various threads are woven into one fabric.

Young Socrates: Clearly.

Stranger: This is a difficult task, but it needs to be done.

Young Socrates: It certainly must be attempted.

Stranger: Here's the first challenge: claiming that one part of virtue is fundamentally different in kind from another is a position that's easily attacked by argumentative people who appeal to popular opinion.

Young Socrates: I don't follow.

Stranger: Let me put it differently. You'd consider courage to be a part of virtue?

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: And temperance — self-control — is different from courage, but also a part of virtue?

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: I'm going to make a bold claim about these two.

Young Socrates: What claim?

Stranger: That in many ways they're fundamentally opposed to each other — two principles that are hostile to one another across a great deal of nature.

Young Socrates: How strange!

Stranger: Very strange — because everyone usually says that all the parts of virtue are friendly to each other.

Young Socrates: Yes, they do.

Stranger: Then let's look carefully at whether this is universally true, or whether some parts of virtue are actually at war with their own kin.

Young Socrates: Tell me how to investigate this.

Stranger: We need to look at all the things we call beautiful but place into two opposite categories.

Young Socrates: Explain — which things?

Stranger: Sharpness and speed — whether in body, in mind, or in the movement of sound — and the artistic imitations of these qualities in painting and music. You've praised such things yourself, or heard others praise them.

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: And do you remember the terms in which they're praised?

Young Socrates: Not really.

Stranger: Let me see if I can capture the thought in words.

Young Socrates: Why not?

Stranger: You think it's easy? Well, consider these qualities in relation to the opposite kinds of action. When we praise speed, energy, and sharpness — whether of mind or body or sound — we express our admiration with a single word: we call them courageous, or manly.

Young Socrates: How so?

Stranger: We call an action energetic and brave, or quick and vigorous, or sharp and powerful. When we use this common term of praise — "courageous" — we're admiring all of them.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: And don't we also praise the quiet mode of action?

Young Socrates: Of course.

Stranger: And don't we say the opposite of what we said about the other type?

Young Socrates: What do you mean?

Stranger: We exclaim "How calm! How measured!" — admiring the slow, deliberate working of the mind, steadiness and gentleness in action, smoothness and depth of voice, every rhythmical movement, and music that carries a proper gravity. We use a different term of praise for all of these — not "courageous," but something that conveys orderliness, or temperance.

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: But when either quality is out of place, the names flip to terms of blame.

Young Socrates: How so?

Stranger: Too much sharpness or quickness or intensity gets called violent, or crazy. Too much slowness or gentleness gets called cowardly, or sluggish. And here's the crucial observation: for the most part, these qualities — courage and temperance — line up as enemies on opposite sides. They don't mix with each other in actual life. And if we follow this further, we'll find that people who embody these different qualities also differ from each other.

Young Socrates: In what way?

Stranger: In all the ways I just mentioned — and probably many more. People who have an affinity for one type of action distribute their praise and blame accordingly: they praise what resembles them and criticize what's different. And from this, many quarrels arise.

Young Socrates: True.

Stranger: Now, when this difference between types is about trivial matters, it's no big deal. But when it affects truly important matters in a state, it becomes the most dangerous of all civic diseases.

Young Socrates: What are you referring to?

Stranger: Nothing less than the entire direction of human life. The orderly, temperance-minded people are always ready to lead a peaceful life, quietly minding their own business. They behave this way at home and are just as eager to keep the peace with foreign states. Because of this excessive love of peace — often indulged at the wrong time — when their influence dominates, the whole population gradually becomes unwarlike. They raise their young people to be just like them. They're at the mercy of their enemies. And so within a few years, they and their children and the whole city often slide imperceptibly from freedom into slavery.

Young Socrates: What a terrible fate.

Stranger: And now think about the courageous types. Aren't they always inciting their country to go to war, because of their excessive love of military life? They raise up powerful enemies against themselves, and they either completely ruin their homeland or else reduce it to slavery under foreign rule.

Young Socrates: That's true too.

Stranger: Then we have to admit that where these two types exist, they always feel the most intense opposition and hostility toward each other.

Young Socrates: We can't deny it.

Stranger: And returning to our original question: haven't we found that significant parts of virtue are in fact at odds with each other — and that this opposition shows up in the characters of the people who embody them?

Young Socrates: Yes, it seems so.

Stranger: Let's take this further.

Young Socrates: Where?

Stranger: Does any constructive art — even in the most trivial project — deliberately mix bad materials with good, when it has a choice? Doesn't every art reject the bad as far as possible, keep what's good and suitable, and from these selected elements — whether alike or different — bring them all together to produce something with a unified nature and purpose?

Young Socrates: Of course.

Stranger: Then the true and natural art of statesmanship will never willingly form a state out of a combination of good and bad people — not if it can help it. It will first test human natures through play and education. After testing them, it will entrust them to the appropriate teachers — who serve as its agents — while the art itself retains command and oversight. Just as the art of weaving constantly directs and oversees the carders and everyone else who prepares the material, ordering the supporting crafts to do the work the master art deems necessary for making the fabric.

Young Socrates: Exactly.

Stranger: In the same way, the royal science — being the mistress of all lawful educators and instructors — won't allow them to train people in ways that produce characters unsuited to the political fabric it wants to create. It will only permit the cultivation of suitable characters. Those who have no share of courage or temperance or any other virtuous disposition — those who are driven by their evil nature into godlessness, arrogance, and injustice — these it removes by death, exile, or the most severe punishments.

Young Socrates: That's commonly said.

Stranger: And those who are wallowing in ignorance and degradation, it yokes into slavery.

Young Socrates: Quite right.

Stranger: As for the rest of the citizens — those from whom education can forge something noble, those who are capable of being blended by a master craftsman — the royal art weaves them together. It takes those whose natures tend toward courage — the stronger element, which serves as the warp — and those who incline toward orderliness and gentleness — the softer element, which serves as the woof. These two types are naturally opposed, and the art seeks to bind and weave them together in the following way:

Young Socrates: How?

Stranger: First, it takes the eternal part of the soul — the divine element — and binds it with a divine cord.

Young Socrates: What does that mean?

Stranger: I mean that true and reasoned conviction about what's honorable, just, and good — and their opposites — when it takes root in the soul, is a divine principle, implanted in a nature that's heaven-born.

Young Socrates: What else could it be?

Stranger: And only the Statesman and the good lawmaker — inspired by the royal muse — can implant this conviction. And they can only do it in people who have been properly educated, the ones we just described.

Young Socrates: That makes sense.

Stranger: As for anyone who can't receive this education — we won't dignify them with any of the titles we've been discussing.

Young Socrates: Right.

Stranger: Now, when the courageous soul takes hold of this truth, it becomes civilized — genuinely capable of participating in justice. Without this truth, it tends toward brutality. Isn't that so?

Young Socrates: Certainly.

Stranger: And conversely, the peaceful, orderly nature — when it shares in these true convictions — becomes genuinely wise and temperate, at least to the degree possible in public life. Without these convictions, it rightly earns the shameful name of foolishness.

Young Socrates: Exactly.

Stranger: Now, can we say that this kind of bond — between truth and the soul — will permanently unite the bad with one another, or the bad with the good? Would any genuine science even attempt to use such a bond on bad material?

Young Socrates: Impossible.

Stranger: But in those who were noble from the start, and who have been nurtured in noble ways — and in them alone — this bond is implanted through law. This is the medicine that art prescribes for them. And of all the bonds that unite the naturally opposed parts of virtue, isn't this the most divine?

Young Socrates: Very true.

Stranger: Once this divine bond is in place, the other bonds — the merely human ones — are easy to create.

Young Socrates: What bonds? What do you mean?

Stranger: The bonds of intermarriage — the ties formed when states or families give and receive children in marriage. Most people make marriage connections without thinking about what's actually best for producing good offspring.

Young Socrates: In what way?

Stranger: They chase after wealth and power in marriage — objectives that are hardly worth criticizing seriously, they're so obviously shallow.

Young Socrates: There's no need to even discuss them.

Stranger: But there's better reason to examine the practice of people who make family connections their chief aim — and to point out their error.

Young Socrates: What error?

Stranger: They act on no sound principle whatsoever. They take the easy path: welcoming people who are like themselves and avoiding those who are different, giving too much weight to what feels comfortable.

Young Socrates: How so?

Stranger: The quiet, orderly type seeks out natures like its own and marries within that class as far as possible. And the courageous type does the same — seeking its own kind. But both types should be doing exactly the opposite.

Young Socrates: Why?

Stranger: Because courage, when it goes untempered by a gentler nature for many generations, may start out blooming and strong — but eventually it explodes into outright madness.

Young Socrates: That makes sense.

Stranger: And the soul that's overly full of modesty, with no element of courage, after many successive generations grows too passive — and eventually becomes completely paralyzed and useless.

Young Socrites That's quite likely too.

Stranger: These are the bonds I said would be easy to create — once both types already share the same true convictions about what's honorable and good. In fact, this single task encompasses the entire work of royal weaving: never allow the temperate natures to be separated from the courageous ones. Weave them together like the warp and the woof — through shared convictions, shared honors, shared reputation, and mutual pledges. From them, form one smooth, even fabric — and to them, entrust the offices of the state.

Young Socrates: How exactly?

Stranger: Where only a single official is needed, choose a ruler who combines both qualities. Where many officials are needed, blend some of each type. Because the temperate ruler is careful, fair, and steady — but lacks drive and decisiveness.

Young Socrates: That certainly seems true.

Stranger: The courageous character, on the other hand, falls short in fairness and caution — but has a remarkable power of action. A city can't thrive in either its public or private life when either quality is missing.

Young Socrates: Certainly not.

Stranger: And this, we declare, is the completion of the political web. The royal science draws courageous and temperate natures together into a single community through shared understanding and friendship, creating the noblest and finest of all the fabrics that political life can produce. It enfolds within this fabric all the other inhabitants of the city — free and slave alike — binding them together under one woven whole. It governs and presides over them, and in every way that happiness is granted to a city, it never fails to secure it.

Young Socrates: Your portrait of the king and statesman, Stranger, is every bit as perfect as your portrait of the Sophist.

PHILEBUS

Part 1

Persons of the dialogue:
  Socrates
  Protarchus
  Philebus

Socrates: All right, Protarchus, pay attention. You're about to take over the position that Philebus has been defending, and here's the position I'm arguing against it. If you don't like mine, you'll need to challenge it. Shall we lay out both sides?

Protarchus: By all means.

Socrates: Philebus has been saying that enjoyment, pleasure, delight, and everything in that family are what's good for every living being. I'm arguing the opposite -- that it's not pleasure but wisdom, intelligence, memory, and their relatives -- sound judgment and true reasoning -- that are better and more worth having for anyone capable of them. For anyone who can share in these things, now or in the future, they're the most beneficial things in the world. Have I stated both positions fairly, Philebus?

Philebus: Perfectly fair, Socrates.

Socrates: And you, Protarchus -- do you accept the position you've been handed?

Protarchus: I don't have much choice, since our excellent Philebus has bowed out.

Socrates: Well, we certainly need to get to the truth of this matter, one way or another.

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: Can we also agree on this --

Protarchus: On what?

Socrates: That you and I should try to identify some condition of the soul, some state of being, that has the power to make all people happy.

Protarchus: Yes, absolutely.

Socrates: And you're saying that pleasure is that state, while I'm saying it's wisdom?

Protarchus: That's right.

Socrates: But what if there's a third option that's better than either one? In that case, we'd both lose. But if this truly happy life turns out to be closer to pleasure than to wisdom, then the life of pleasure still beats the life of wisdom.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And if the better life turns out to be closer to wisdom, then wisdom wins and pleasure loses. Agreed?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: What do you say, Philebus?

Philebus: I say, and I'll always say, that pleasure wins easily. But you'll have to make up your own mind, Protarchus.

Protarchus: You've handed the argument over to me, Philebus -- you don't get a say anymore.

Philebus: Fair enough. I'm washing my hands of this. And I call the goddess herself as my witness that I'm doing so.

Protarchus: You can appeal to us too -- we'll be witnesses to your words. Now, Socrates, whether Philebus likes it or not, let's get on with the argument.

Socrates: Then let's start with the goddess herself -- Philebus says she's called Aphrodite, but her real name is Pleasure.

Protarchus: Very good.

Socrates: The reverence I feel about the names of the gods, Protarchus, goes beyond ordinary human fear -- it surpasses every other worry I have. So I won't risk offending Aphrodite by naming her wrongly. Let her be called whatever she likes. But Pleasure -- that's what I want to talk about, and as I was just saying, we need to begin by understanding her nature. She has one name, so you might think she's one simple thing. But she actually takes the most varied and even contradictory forms. Think about it: don't we say that a reckless person gets pleasure, and that a self-controlled person gets pleasure from his very self-control? Don't we say that a fool feels pleasure when he's full of foolish daydreams and hopes, and that a wise person feels pleasure in his wisdom? Wouldn't anyone who claimed all these opposite pleasures were the same be talking nonsense?

Protarchus: Well, Socrates, they come from opposite sources, sure, but they're not opposites in themselves. I mean, pleasure has to be the one thing most completely like pleasure -- like itself, right?

Socrates: Sure, my friend -- just like color is like color. As far as being colors goes, there's no difference between them. But we all know that black isn't just unlike white -- it's the exact opposite. And shape is like shape -- all shapes belong to one category -- but particular shapes can be totally opposed to each other, and there's an infinite variety of them. We could find similar cases in lots of other things. So don't rely on this argument that would prove the most extreme opposites are really the same. I suspect we'll find the same kind of opposition among pleasures.

Protarchus: Very likely. But how does this undermine the argument?

Socrates: Because even though pleasures are different from each other, you're applying a single label to all of them: you're calling them all "good." Now, nobody can deny that pleasure is pleasure. But someone can argue -- as we're doing -- that pleasures are more often bad than good. Yet you call them all good, while being forced to admit, if pressed, that they're quite different from each other. So tell me: what identical quality exists in both good and bad pleasures that makes you call them all "good"?

Protarchus: What do you mean, Socrates? Do you think anyone who says pleasure is the good is going to accept that some pleasures are good and others bad?

Socrates: And yet you'll admit they're different from one another, and sometimes even opposed?

Protarchus: Not insofar as they're pleasures.

Socrates: That's just going in circles, Protarchus. So we're supposed to say there's no difference between pleasures -- that they're all alike? And those examples I just gave don't penetrate our thick skulls, and we just keep arguing the same way, like the weakest, most inexperienced debaters?

Protarchus: What do you mean?

Socrates: I mean that in self-defense I could follow your example and boldly claim that two completely unlike things are absolutely identical. And the result would be that we'd both prove ourselves to be complete amateurs in reasoning, and the whole argument would be blown away. Let's back up and start fresh -- maybe we can actually come to an understanding.

Protarchus: How?

Socrates: Protarchus, should I have you put my own question to me?

Protarchus: What question?

Socrates: Ask me whether wisdom, knowledge, and intelligence, and those other qualities that I said were good when you first asked me -- aren't they in the same boat as your pleasures?

Protarchus: What do you mean?

Socrates: The kinds of knowledge are numerous and will turn out to have major differences between them. But even if some of them prove to be opposed to each other as well as different -- just like pleasures -- would I deserve to be called a philosopher if I tried to dodge this by saying there's no difference between one kind of knowledge and another? The argument would sink like a bad story, and we'd only survive by clinging to a fallacy.

Protarchus: Well, let's not let that happen to us -- except the surviving part! But I do like the even-handed fairness being applied to both sides. So let's agree: there are many diverse pleasures, and many different kinds of knowledge.

Socrates: And let's not hide the differences between my candidate for the good and yours, Protarchus. Let's bring them into the light, because in the process of testing them, they may show us whether pleasure should be called the good, or wisdom, or some third thing. We're not just competing so that my view or yours wins -- we should both be fighting for the truth.

Protarchus: We certainly should.

Socrates: Then let's nail down the principle this argument rests on, and make our understanding more precise.

Protarchus: What principle?

Socrates: A principle that everyone always has trouble with, and that sometimes catches people off guard.

Protarchus: Be more specific.

Socrates: The principle that just came up -- it's a genuine marvel of nature. The idea that one thing should be many, or many things should be one -- these are extraordinary claims, and anyone who asserts either one is wide open to attack.

Protarchus: Do you mean when someone says that I, Protarchus, am by nature one person but also many -- splitting the single "me" into many versions of "me," even making them opposites of each other: big and small, light and heavy, and ten thousand other ways?

Socrates: Those are the common, well-known puzzles about the one and the many, Protarchus. Everyone has basically agreed by now to set those aside as childish and obvious and actually harmful to real thinking. And people aren't any more impressed by that other trick -- where someone proves that the parts and members of something can be divided up, then admits they're all one thing, and says with a laugh, refuting his own words: "Look at this miracle! The one is many and infinite, and the many are only one!"

Protarchus: But Socrates, what are those other marvels connected with this subject that, as you're suggesting, haven't yet become common knowledge?

Socrates: When the "one," my boy, doesn't belong to the class of things that are born and perish -- like in those everyday examples -- because in those cases, where the unity is something concrete, there's universal agreement that no serious argument is needed. But when someone claims that "human being" is one, or "beauty" is one, or "the good" is one -- that's when genuine interest and controversy arise from the attempt to divide these unities.

Protarchus: What kind of controversy?

Socrates: First: do these unities even really exist? Then: how can each one, being always the same and incapable of coming into being or being destroyed, maintaining its permanent identity -- how can it be conceived as dispersed and multiplied across the infinite world of things that come and go? Or as being somehow whole and yet divided from itself -- which seems like the greatest impossibility of all, since how can one and the same thing be in one place and in many at the same time? These, Protarchus, are the real difficulties about the one and many. If you resolve them badly, they're a source of endless confusion. But if you resolve them well, they're enormously helpful.

Protarchus: Then, Socrates, let's start by clearing up these questions.

Socrates: That's exactly what I'd like to do.

Protarchus: And I'm sure all our friends here will be glad to hear them discussed. Philebus, luckily for us, isn't inclined to stir -- we'd better not provoke him with questions.

Socrates: Good. So where should we begin this great and many-sided battle, with so many issues at stake? How about this?

Protarchus: How?

Socrates: We say that the one and the many become identified through thought, and that now, as always, they run around together in and out of every word we speak. This union of them will never stop -- it's not something that just started. It is, I believe, an eternal property of thought itself, which never grows old. Any young person who first encounters these puzzles is thrilled -- he thinks he's discovered a treasure of wisdom. In the first flush of excitement, he turns over every stone -- or rather, every idea. First he rolls the many into one, kneading them together; then he unfolds and divides them again. He confuses himself first and foremost, then goes on to confuse everyone around him -- older, younger, his own age, makes no difference. He doesn't spare his father, his mother, anyone with ears to hear. He'd barely spare his dog, and a foreigner would have no chance of escaping him -- if only an interpreter could be found.

Protarchus: Socrates, considering how many of us there are, and that we're all young men, aren't you worried that we and Philebus might gang up on you if you insult us like this? We get what you're saying, though. But isn't there some way to clear up all this confusion -- some better method of getting at the truth? If there is, we hope you'll guide us to it, and we'll do our best to follow. This inquiry we're engaged in, Socrates, is not a trivial one.

Socrates: Far from trivial, my boys -- as Philebus calls you. And there never was and never will be a better method than my own favorite, though it has often abandoned me and left me helpless in my hour of need.

Protarchus: Tell us what it is.

Socrates: A method that's easy to point out but very hard to apply. It's the parent of every discovery that any skill or art has ever produced.

Protarchus: Tell us!

Socrates: A gift from heaven, which I believe the gods tossed down to humans through some new Prometheus, along with a blaze of light. And the ancients -- who were better than us and lived closer to the gods -- passed down this tradition: whatever things are said to exist are composed of one and many, and have the finite and the infinite built into them. Since that's how the world is ordered, we should begin every inquiry by positing a single Form for whatever we're investigating. We'll find this unity in everything. Once we've found it, we look for two beneath it -- if there are two -- or if not, three, or some other number, subdividing each of those units in the same way, until we see that the original unity is not just one and also many and infinite, but a specific number of kinds. We must not let the infinite touch the many until we've discovered the entire number of species that fall between the one and the infinite. Only then, after that, may we stop dividing and let the endless individuals drop off into infinity. This is the way of investigating, learning, and teaching one another that the gods handed down to us. But the clever people of our day jump too fast or too slow between one and many. They have no method. They go from unity straight to infinity, and the intermediate steps never occur to them. And that's what separates mere argument from genuine dialectic.

Protarchus: I think I partly follow you, Socrates, but I'd like a clearer picture of what you're saying.

Socrates: I can illustrate with the letters of the alphabet, Protarchus -- the ones you learned as a child.

Protarchus: How do they illustrate it?

Socrates: The sound that passes through our lips -- whether from one person or everyone -- is one, and yet infinite in variety.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: But we don't become experts in language by knowing just that sound is one, or that sound is infinite. What makes someone a grammarian is knowing the number and nature of the different sounds.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: And the same kind of knowledge makes someone a musician.

Protarchus: How so?

Socrates: Sound is one thing in music too, just as in grammar, right?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And there are higher notes and lower notes, and notes of equal pitch -- we can agree on that much?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: But you wouldn't be a real musician if that were all you knew. On the other hand, if you didn't even know that much, you'd know practically nothing about music.

Protarchus: Nothing at all.

Socrates: But when you've learned which sounds are high and which are low, and the number and nature of the intervals between them, and their limits and proportions, and the systems built from them -- the ones our predecessors discovered and passed down to us under the name of "harmonies" -- and the corresponding patterns in bodily movement, which when measured by numbers are called rhythms and meters, and that this same principle should be applied to every case of the one and the many -- when you've learned all that, my dear friend, then you've truly mastered the subject. And you can be said to understand any other subject when you've grasped it the same way. But the infinity of types and the infinity of individuals within each type, when they haven't been classified, makes every one of us infinitely ignorant. And someone who never looks for number in anything will never be numbered among the wise.

Protarchus: I think what Socrates is saying is excellent, Philebus.

Philebus: I think so too, but how does this relate to us and our argument?

Socrates: Philebus is right to ask us that question, Protarchus.

Protarchus: He certainly is, and you need to answer him.

Socrates: I will. But first let me add one small point. I was saying that someone who starts with a single unity shouldn't jump to infinity, but should proceed to a definite number of kinds. Now I'll say the reverse: someone who has to start with infinity shouldn't leap straight to unity either. He should look for some definite number that represents the real variety, and only through those arrive at one. Let's go back to the alphabet for an illustration.

Protarchus: What do you mean?

Socrates: Some god -- or divine man, who in the Egyptian legend is said to have been Theuth -- noticed that the human voice was infinite in variety, and was the first to distinguish within that infinity a definite number of vowels. Then he identified other letters that have sound but aren't pure vowels -- the semivowels -- and these too exist in a definite number. Third, he identified a class of letters we now call mutes -- without voice and without sound -- and divided these, and the vowels and semivowels too, into their individual sounds, counting the number of each. Seeing that none of us could learn any one of them without learning all of them, and recognizing this common bond that united them, he assigned them all to a single art and called it grammar.

Philebus: The illustration, Protarchus, has helped me understand the original point better, but I still feel the same gap I just complained about.

Socrates: You're going to ask, Philebus, what this has to do with our argument?

Philebus: Yes -- that's the question Protarchus and I have been asking for a while now.

Socrates: But you've actually already arrived at the answer, haven't you?

Philebus: How so?

Socrates: Didn't we start by asking which is the better choice -- pleasure or wisdom?

Philebus: Certainly.

Socrates: And we're saying each of them is one thing?

Philebus: True.

Socrates: Well, the exact question that our whole discussion has been building toward is this: how are they each one and also many -- how does each have one general kind but many specific types? And what definite number of types belongs to each of them before they dissolve into infinite individual instances?

Protarchus: That's a very serious question, Philebus, and Socrates has cleverly steered us right to it. Consider which of us should answer him. There's something a bit absurd in my not being able to answer when I've taken over the whole argument -- but if neither of us can answer, that's even worse. Let's think about what to do. Socrates, if I understand you correctly, is asking whether there aren't different kinds of pleasure, and what their number and nature are -- and the same for wisdom.

Socrates: Exactly right, son of Callias. And our earlier discussion showed that if we can't identify the kinds within anything that has unity, likeness, or sameness -- or their opposites -- none of us will be the slightest use in any inquiry.

Protarchus: That seems very close to the truth, Socrates. It would be wonderful if the wise person could know all things, and the next best thing is to know yourself. But why am I saying this right now? I'll tell you. You, Socrates, have given us this chance to talk with you and are ready to help us figure out what the greatest human good is. When Philebus said it was pleasure and delight and enjoyment and all that, you disagreed and offered a different class of goods. And we keep reminding ourselves of what you said -- quite rightly -- so we can examine and compare the two. These goods that you consider superior to pleasure, the real objects worth pursuing, are mind and knowledge and understanding and skill, and the like. There was a dispute about which were better, and we playfully warned you that we wouldn't let you go home until the question was settled. You agreed and put yourself at our disposal. And now, as children say, what's been fairly given can't be taken back. So stop fighting us like this.

Socrates: Fighting you how?

Philebus: Stop confusing us and keep asking questions we can't properly answer yet. Let's not imagine that the whole point of this discussion is just to baffle everyone. If we can't answer, you answer -- you promised you would. So make up your mind: are you going to divide pleasure and knowledge into their different kinds, or would you rather drop it and find some other way to resolve our disagreement?

Socrates: Well, if you put it that way, I've got nothing to worry about, because the words "if you're willing" take away all my fear. And besides, a god seems to have reminded me of something.

Philebus: What's that?

Socrates: I remember hearing, a long time ago -- whether I was awake or dreaming, I can't say -- certain arguments about pleasure and wisdom. The conclusion was that neither one is the good, but rather some third thing, different from both and better than either. If that's clearly established, then pleasure loses the contest, because the good won't be identified with her anymore. Right?

Protarchus: Right.

Socrates: And there won't be any need to sort out the different kinds of pleasure -- or at least, that's what I'm inclined to think. But this will become clearer as we go.

Protarchus: Excellent, Socrates. Please continue.

Socrates: But first, let's agree on a few small points.

Protarchus: What are they?

Socrates: Is the good perfect, or imperfect?

Protarchus: The most perfect of all things, Socrates.

Socrates: And is the good sufficient?

Protarchus: Yes, certainly -- sufficient beyond everything else.

Socrates: And this much nobody can deny: every conscious being desires and pursues the good, is eager to get hold of it, and doesn't care about getting anything that isn't accompanied by good.

Protarchus: Undeniable.

Socrates: Now let's separate the life of pleasure from the life of wisdom and examine each one.

Protarchus: How do you mean?

Socrates: Let's imagine a life of pleasure with no wisdom in it, and a life of wisdom with no pleasure. Because if either of them really is the supreme good, it shouldn't need anything else. But if either one turns out to be lacking something, then it can't really be the supreme good.

Protarchus: That makes sense.

Socrates: Will you help me put both lives to the test?

Protarchus: Of course.

Socrates: Then answer me.

Protarchus: Ask.

Socrates: Would you choose, Protarchus, to live your entire life enjoying the greatest pleasures?

Protarchus: Absolutely.

Socrates: And if you had perfect pleasure, would you feel that anything was still missing?

Protarchus: Not at all.

Socrates: Think carefully. You wouldn't want wisdom, or intelligence, or the ability to plan ahead? You wouldn't even want eyesight?

Protarchus: Why would I? If I have pleasure, I have everything.

Socrates: And living like this, you'd enjoy the greatest pleasures throughout your whole life?

Protarchus: I would.

Socrates: But if you had no mind, no memory, no knowledge, no sound judgment -- you wouldn't even know whether you were enjoying yourself or not, since you'd be completely devoid of intelligence.

Protarchus: Right, I suppose.

Socrates: And without memory, you wouldn't remember that you'd ever felt pleasure. Not the slightest trace of the pleasure you're feeling right now would stay with you from one moment to the next. Without sound judgment, you wouldn't even think you were feeling pleasure while you were feeling it. And without the ability to reason, you couldn't anticipate future pleasure. Your life wouldn't be a human life at all -- it would be the life of an oyster, or a jellyfish. Could it be otherwise?

Protarchus: No.

Socrates: Is that a life anyone would choose?

Protarchus: I can't answer you, Socrates. The argument has struck me speechless.

Socrates: Don't lose heart. Let's turn now to the life of the mind and examine that.

Protarchus: What do you mean by the life of the mind?

Socrates: Would anyone choose to live with all the wisdom, intelligence, knowledge, and memory in the world, but with absolutely no sensation of pleasure or pain -- completely unaffected by those feelings?

Protarchus: Neither life appeals to me, Socrates, and I can't imagine anyone else would choose either one.

Socrates: What about a combination of the two -- a life made from joining both together?

Protarchus: You mean pleasure combined with mind and wisdom?

Socrates: Exactly.

Protarchus: Everyone -- not just some people, but everyone -- would choose this third life over either of the other two, hands down.

Socrates: But do you see what follows from that?

Protarchus: Of course. Two out of three proposed lives are neither sufficient nor desirable -- not for humans, not for any living thing.

Socrates: So now it's clear that neither pleasure alone nor wisdom alone contains the good. Because whichever one contained it would be completely sufficient and perfect and desirable for every living creature capable of such a life. And anyone who chose differently would be choosing against the nature of what's truly desirable -- not by free choice, but from ignorance or some unfortunate compulsion.

Protarchus: That certainly seems right.

Socrates: So I think I've shown well enough that Philebus' goddess can't be identified with the good.

Philebus: Your "mind" isn't the good either, Socrates -- it's vulnerable to the same objections.

Socrates: Maybe my "mind" is, Philebus -- but the true, divine mind? That's a different story. Still, I won't claim first place for mind over the mixed life just yet. What we need to figure out now is the second place. You might say pleasure is the cause of the mixed life's goodness. I might say mind is. And in that case, even though neither would be the good itself, one of them might be the cause of the good. And I could go further and argue against Philebus that the element making this mixed life desirable and good is more closely related to mind than to pleasure. If that's true, pleasure can't honestly claim even second place -- let alone first. She doesn't even reach third.

Protarchus: Honestly, Socrates, it does look like pleasure has taken a fall. She stepped into the ring fighting for the championship, and the argument has knocked her flat. And I have to say, mind showed good judgment in not making the same claim to first place -- she would have been knocked down too. But if pleasure loses not just first place but second as well, she'd be badly damaged in the eyes of her fans. Even they wouldn't find her as attractive as before.

Socrates: Well, shouldn't we just leave her alone then? Why subject her to the ultimate test and expose her further?

Protarchus: Don't be ridiculous, Socrates.

Socrates: What -- because I said we shouldn't "pain" pleasure? That's impossible, isn't it?

Protarchus: That, and the fact that none of us is going to let you go home until you finish the argument.

Socrates: Good grief, Protarchus, that's going to take a while. And right now it's not going to be easy. Going to battle on behalf of mind, competing for second place -- I'm going to need different weapons from the ones I used before. Though some of the old ones might still work. Do I really have to finish?

Protarchus: Of course you do.

Socrates: Then let's be very careful in laying the foundation.

Protarchus: What do you mean?

Socrates: Let's divide everything that exists into two classes -- or rather, if you don't mind, three.

Protarchus: What principle of division?

Socrates: Let's use some of the ideas we just discovered.

Protarchus: Which ones?

Socrates: We were saying, weren't we, that god revealed a finite element in existence, and also an infinite one?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: Let's take those as two of our principles, and add a third: what's produced by combining them. Though I'm afraid I'm rather clumsy at these processes of classification and counting.

Protarchus: What do you mean?

Socrates: I'm realizing we need a fourth class too.

Protarchus: What would that be?

Socrates: The cause of that combination -- the thing that produces the third class by mixing the other two. Add that as a fourth.

Protarchus: And would you like a fifth as well -- a cause of separation, to go with the cause of combination?

Socrates: I don't think so, not right now. But if I need a fifth later, you'll let me add one?

Protarchus: Of course.

Socrates: Let's start with the first three. Two of them are enormously spread out and scattered, so let's try to gather each of them back together and see how each is both one and many.

Protarchus: If you could explain a bit more, maybe I could follow.

Socrates: The two classes I mentioned are the same ones from before: the finite and the infinite. I'll show first that the infinite is in a certain sense many, and we can deal with the finite afterward.

Protarchus: All right.

Socrates: Now think carefully, because this is a tricky, contested question. When you talk about "hotter" and "colder," can you imagine any fixed limit in those qualities?
