Doesn't the "more" and "less" that lives in their very nature prevent them from having any endpoint? Because if they had an endpoint, the "more" and "less" would themselves have to end.

Protarchus: That's absolutely true.

Socrates: We're always saying that the hotter and the colder always have a "more" and a "less" in them.

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: So the argument shows that they never reach an end. And being endless, they must also be infinite.

Protarchus: Yes, Socrates, that's exactly right.

Socrates: Good catch, my dear Protarchus. And your answer reminds me that this word "exactly" that you just used, and words like "gently" or "extremely," have the same significance as "more" and "less." Wherever they show up, they don't allow for fixed quantity -- they're always introducing degrees into things, setting up a comparison of more intense versus less intense, more gentle versus less gentle. At each step, definite quantity disappears. Because as I was saying, if quantity and measure were allowed into the realm of more-and-less and the other comparatives, those comparatives would be driven out of their own territory. Once you introduce a definite quantity, there can't be any "hotter" or "colder" anymore -- because those are always progressing, never standing still. But definite quantity stands still and stops progressing. This proves that comparatives like "hotter" and "colder" belong in the class of the infinite.

Protarchus: What you're saying certainly looks true, Socrates, though as you mentioned, these subjects are hard to follow at first. I think if I could hear the argument one more time, we'd be in solid agreement.

Socrates: Fair enough, and I'll try to accommodate you. But to save time rather than going through endless examples, let me ask: can I use this as the hallmark of the infinite?

Protarchus: Use what?

Socrates: Whatever seems to admit of "more" and "less," or is described by words like "extremely," "gently," "intensely," and the like -- can we assign all of that to the class of the infinite? That's its unifying principle. Remember our earlier point -- that things which are scattered and divided should be brought together and stamped with the mark of a single nature, if possible?

Protarchus: I remember.

Socrates: And everything that doesn't admit of more and less, but instead admits precise opposites -- equality, the equal, the double, any definite ratio of number and measure -- all of that, I think, we can properly assign to the class of the finite. What do you say?

Protarchus: Excellent, Socrates.

Socrates: Now, what nature should we assign to the third class -- the combined one?

Protarchus: I think you'll have to tell me that.

Socrates: No, god will tell you -- if any god will listen to my prayers.

Protarchus: Then pray, and think!

Socrates: I am thinking, Protarchus, and I believe some god has come to our aid.

Protarchus: What do you mean? What's your evidence?

Socrates: I'll tell you -- just listen.

Protarchus: Go on.

Socrates: Weren't we just talking about hotter and colder?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: Add to them drier and wetter, more and less, faster and slower, greater and smaller -- everything that we previously placed under the unity of "more and less."

Protarchus: In the class of the infinite, you mean?

Socrates: Yes. Now mix that with the other class.

Protarchus: Which other class?

Socrates: The class of the finite, which we should have gathered together just as we did the infinite. But we can do it now -- the result will be the same. When the two are combined, a third thing will emerge.

Protarchus: What do you mean by the class of the finite?

Socrates: The class of the equal and the double -- any class that puts an end to opposites being at war with each other, and by introducing number creates harmony and proportion among the different elements.

Protarchus: I see -- you're saying that when you mix the infinite with the class of the finite, various definite forms emerge.

Socrates: Yes, that's exactly what I mean.

Protarchus: Go on.

Socrates: Doesn't the right participation of the finite bring about health? In cases of disease, for example?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And whereas high and low, fast and slow are infinite and unlimited, doesn't the introduction of those same principles of limit bring them under control and perfect the entire framework of music?

Protarchus: Yes, certainly.

Socrates: Or again, when cold and heat are running wild, doesn't the introduction of limit take away the excess and indefiniteness, and bring moderation and harmony?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And from a similar mixture of the finite and the infinite come the seasons, and all the good things of life?

Protarchus: Absolutely.

Socrates: I'm leaving out ten thousand other examples -- beauty, health, strength, and the many excellences of body and soul. Philebus, my handsome friend, the goddess saw the universal excess and wickedness that comes when there's no limit to pleasures and self-indulgence, and she devised the limit of law and order. You say, Philebus, that she torments the soul by doing this. I say she saves it. What do you think, Protarchus?

Protarchus: Her methods suit me perfectly, Socrates.

Socrates: You'll notice I've described three classes?

Protarchus: Yes, I think I understand. You mean the infinite is one class, and the finite is a second class of things. But I'm not entirely sure about the third.

Socrates: That's because the amazing variety of the third class overwhelms you, my friend. But the infinite wasn't difficult -- even though it also contained many varieties -- because they were all unified by the mark of "more and less," and so they appeared as one.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And the finite didn't have many divisions either -- we easily recognized it as naturally one.

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: Right. So when I speak of the third class, understand me to mean any offspring of these two -- a birth into true existence, brought about by the measures that the limit introduces.

Protarchus: I understand.

Socrates: But we said there was a fourth class still to investigate, and you need to help. Doesn't everything that comes into existence necessarily come into existence through some cause?

Protarchus: Yes, of course -- how could anything exist without a cause?

Socrates: And isn't the agent the same as the cause, differing only in name? The agent and the cause are really one and the same thing?

Protarchus: Exactly.

Socrates: And the same goes for the patient, or effect -- they differ only in name too, don't they?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: The cause always naturally leads, and the effect naturally follows?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: So the cause and what is produced by it are not the same, but different?

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And weren't the things that were produced, along with the materials from which they were produced, what filled our first three classes?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And the maker or cause of them has been satisfactorily shown to be distinct from all three -- so it can be called a fourth principle?

Protarchus: Yes, let's call it that.

Socrates: Good. Now that we've distinguished all four, I think we should refresh our memories by reviewing each one in order.

Protarchus: By all means.

Socrates: The first I'll call the infinite, or unlimited. The second is the finite, or limited. Third comes the combination -- a mixed reality that's both compound and generated. And I don't think I'll be far wrong in calling the fourth the cause of mixture and generation.

Protarchus: Not far wrong at all.

Socrates: Now, what's our next question? How did we get here? Weren't we asking whether second place belongs to pleasure or to wisdom?

Protarchus: We were.

Socrates: And now, having worked through these four classes, won't we be better equipped to settle the original dispute about first and second place?

Protarchus: I'd think so.

Socrates: We said, if you remember, that the mixed life -- pleasure combined with wisdom -- was the winner. Right?

Protarchus: Right.

Socrates: And we can see what this life is and which class it belongs to?

Protarchus: Without a doubt.

Socrates: It clearly falls in the third, the mixed class -- which isn't a mixture of just two particular ingredients, but of all the elements of the infinite, bound together by the finite. So we can truly say that it includes the winning life.

Protarchus: Absolutely.

Socrates: And what about your life, Philebus -- the life of pure sweetness? Which of these classes does that belong in? Let me ask you a question before you answer.

Philebus: Go ahead.

Socrates: Do pleasure and pain have a limit, or do they belong to the class that admits of more and less?

Philebus: They belong to the class that admits of more, Socrates. Pleasure wouldn't be perfectly good if she weren't infinite in quantity and degree.

Socrates: And pain wouldn't be perfectly evil either, Philebus. So it can't be the infinite that gives pleasure its degree of goodness. Let's set that aside. But let me ask this -- and Protarchus and Philebus, please think carefully, because making a mistake here would be very serious: which class do wisdom, knowledge, and mind belong to?

Philebus: You're making too big a deal of your favorite god, Socrates.

Socrates: And you're making too big a deal of your favorite goddess. But still, please answer the question.

Protarchus: Socrates is right, Philebus -- we have to answer.

Philebus: Didn't you volunteer to answer for me, Protarchus?

Protarchus: I did, but I'm stuck now. I have to ask you, Socrates, to be our spokesperson, so we don't say something wrong or disrespectful about your candidate.

Socrates: I'd better do as you say, Protarchus. It's not that hard, actually. But did I really throw you off with my playful seriousness when I asked what class mind and knowledge belong to, as Philebus is suggesting?

Protarchus: You certainly did, Socrates.

Socrates: And yet the answer is easy. All philosophers agree with one voice that mind is the king of heaven and earth -- which is really just a fancy way of flattering themselves. And maybe they're right! But I'd like to examine this class of mind a bit more thoroughly, if you don't object.

Philebus: Take whatever approach you like, Socrates. We won't get tired of you -- don't worry about going long.

Socrates: Thank you. Let's begin, then, Protarchus, with a question.

Protarchus: What question?

Socrates: Is this entire thing we call "the universe" ruled by unreason and blind chance? Or, as our predecessors declared, is it ordered and governed by a marvelous intelligence and wisdom?

Protarchus: Those two views couldn't be more different, my dear Socrates! What you just suggested first sounds almost like blasphemy. But the other view -- that mind orders all things -- is worthy of the beauty of the sun, the moon, the stars, and the entire sweep of the heavens. I'll never say or think otherwise.

Socrates: Then should we agree with our predecessors and maintain this doctrine? And not just lazily repeat other people's ideas at no risk to ourselves, but actually take our stand and share the criticism that comes when some clever person says everything is disorder?

Protarchus: That's certainly what I'd want.

Socrates: Then consider the next step of the argument.

Protarchus: Let me hear it.

Socrates: We can see that the elements making up the bodies of all animals -- fire, water, air, and earth (as the storm-tossed sailor cries, "Land!") -- also appear in the makeup of the universe.

Protarchus: That proverb fits us well -- we're certainly storm-tossed in this argument, and at our wits' end!

Socrates: Now here's something to notice about each of these elements.

Protarchus: What?

Socrates: Only a tiny fraction of any one of them exists in us, and a weak, impure version at that -- nothing worthy of its true nature. One example will prove the point for all of them. There's fire within us, and there's fire in the universe.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: The fire in us is small, weak, and dim. But the fire in the universe is magnificent in quantity, beauty, and every power that fire possesses.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: And is the fire in the universe fed, generated, and governed by the fire in us? Or is it the other way around -- the fire in you and me and every other animal depends on the universal fire?

Protarchus: That question hardly deserves an answer.

Socrates: Right. And you'd say the same about the earth in animals versus the earth in the universe, and the same for all the other elements?

Protarchus: Anyone who answered differently would have to be out of his mind.

Socrates: Fair enough. Now, when we see all these elements gathered together in one place, we call them a body, don't we?

Protarchus: We do.

Socrates: And the same can be said of the cosmos -- for the same reason it can be considered a body, since it's made up of the same elements.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: And does our body get its nourishment entirely from this cosmic body, or does the cosmic body get its nourishment from ours?

Protarchus: That's another question that doesn't deserve to be asked.

Socrates: Well, how about this question -- is this one worth asking?

Protarchus: What question?

Socrates: Can our body be said to have a soul?

Protarchus: Obviously.

Socrates: And where did that soul come from, my dear Protarchus -- unless the body of the universe, which contains elements like those in our bodies but finer in every way, also has a soul? What other source could there be?

Protarchus: No other source, Socrates -- that's clearly the only one.

Socrates: Because surely we can't imagine the following, Protarchus. We have these four classes -- the finite, the infinite, their combination, and the cause -- and this fourth one, the cause, enters into all things, giving our bodies souls, providing the art of self-care and medicine, operating in countless other ways to heal and organize, possessing all the attributes of wisdom. We can't seriously imagine that while these same elements exist in the entire heaven and throughout great regions of the universe -- only finer and purer -- this same cause somehow failed to create the noblest and finest things up there too.

Protarchus: That would be completely unreasonable.

Socrates: So if we reject that idea, shouldn't we adopt the other view and maintain that there is in the universe a mighty infinite and a sufficient limit -- as we've often said -- along with a presiding cause of real power that orders and arranges years, seasons, and months? This cause may justly be called wisdom and mind.

Protarchus: Most justly.

Socrates: And wisdom and mind can't exist without soul?

Protarchus: Certainly not.

Socrates: So you'd say that in the divine nature of Zeus, there is soul and mind belonging to a king, because in him there is the power of the cause? And other gods have other attributes that they like to be known by?

Protarchus: Exactly.

Socrates: Don't think these words are thrown around carelessly, Protarchus. They're in harmony with the ancient thinkers who said that mind rules the universe.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And they give us our answer. They imply that mind belongs to that class of the four which we called the cause of everything. Now you have my answer.

Protarchus: I do indeed -- though I didn't quite notice when you gave it.

Socrates: A joke can be refreshing sometimes, Protarchus, when it breaks up a serious argument.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: So, my friend, I think we've now established pretty clearly which class mind belongs to and what its power is.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And the class that pleasure belongs to was identified long ago?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: Then let's remember both results. First, mind is related to the cause and essentially belongs to that family. Second, pleasure is infinite and belongs to the class that has no beginning, middle, or end of its own, and never will.

Protarchus: I'll be sure to remember.

Socrates: Our next task is to examine where pleasure and pain arise and under what conditions they're generated. And we'll start with pleasure, since we examined her class first. But pleasure can't be properly tested apart from pain.

Protarchus: If that's the road, let's take it.

Socrates: I wonder whether you'd agree with me about where pleasure and pain come from.

Protarchus: What do you mean?

Socrates: I'd say their natural home is in the mixed class.

Protarchus: Can you remind me again, dear Socrates, which one is the mixed class?

Socrates: I'll do my best, good sir.

Protarchus: Please do.

Socrates: The mixed class is the one we placed third in our list of four.

Protarchus: The one that followed the infinite and the finite -- the one where you placed health and, if I'm not mistaken, harmony?

Socrates: Excellent. Now please give me your full attention.

Protarchus: Go ahead -- I'm listening.

Socrates: I'm saying that when the harmony in living beings is disrupted, their natural state breaks down, and that's when pain occurs.

Protarchus: That sounds very plausible.

Socrates: And when harmony is restored and nature returns to its proper state, that's the source of pleasure -- if I can put the biggest idea into the fewest words.

Protarchus: I think you're right, Socrates, but could you try to be a little clearer?

Socrates: Don't ordinary, everyday experiences provide the simplest illustrations?

Protarchus: Like what?

Socrates: Hunger, for example, is a disruption -- and painful.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And eating is a restoration -- and pleasurable?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: Thirst is a destruction and a pain, but when moisture replenishes what's dried out, that's pleasure. And when unnatural heat causes separation and breakdown, that's painful, but when the body's natural coolness is restored, that's pleasant.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: And when moisture in the body freezes unnaturally, that's pain, but the natural process of thawing and returning to the normal state is pleasure. In short, here's the general principle -- tell me if it seems right: whenever the natural combination of finite and infinite that makes up living beings is destroyed, that's pain. And whenever everything returns to its proper nature, that's pleasure.

Protarchus: Granted -- what you say has a general truth to it.

Socrates: So here we have one kind of pleasure and pain, each arising from the two processes we've described.

Protarchus: Good.

Socrates: Now let's add that in the soul itself, there's an anticipation of pleasure -- sweet and refreshing hope -- and an expectation of pain -- anxious and fearful.

Protarchus: Yes -- that's another kind of pleasure and pain, belonging to the soul alone, apart from the body, produced by expectation.

Socrates: Right. And in analyzing these pure cases -- pleasure unmixed with pain, and pain unmixed with pleasure -- I think we'll be able to see clearly whether the entire class of pleasure is something desirable, or whether that quality of being wholly desirable really belongs to one of the other classes we've mentioned. We might find that pleasure and pain are like heat and cold -- sometimes desirable, sometimes not -- because they're not good in themselves, but only sometimes participate in the nature of good.

Protarchus: You're absolutely right that this is the trail our investigation should follow.

Socrates: Well then: if we accept that pain arises from the disruption of harmony, and pleasure from its restoration, let's ask what happens when a living creature is experiencing neither disruption nor restoration. Think about this carefully: I'm asking whether any creature in that condition could possibly feel any pleasure or pain, great or small.

Protarchus: No, certainly not.

Socrates: So here we have a third state -- beyond both pleasure and pain?

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: Keep this in mind -- it'll make a big difference in how we judge pleasure. Let me say a few words about it.

Protarchus: Go ahead.

Socrates: There's no reason why a person who chooses the life of wisdom can't live in this neutral state.

Protarchus: You mean he could live without either rejoicing or suffering?

Socrates: Yes. And if I remember correctly, when we compared the different lives, we agreed that no degree of pleasure, great or small, was necessary for someone who chose the life of thought and wisdom.

Protarchus: Yes, we did say that.

Socrates: So such a person would live without pleasure. And who knows -- maybe that's the most divine life of all.

Protarchus: If so, then the gods, at least, must experience neither joy nor sorrow.

Socrates: Certainly not -- there would be something deeply inappropriate about attributing either to them. But whether the gods are indifferent to pleasure is a point we can take up later if it becomes relevant. Whatever we decide, we'll credit it to mind's account in her contest for second place -- assuming she has to concede first.

Protarchus: Fair enough.

Socrates: Now, the other class of pleasures -- the ones we said are purely mental -- comes entirely from memory.

Protarchus: What do you mean?

Socrates: I need to analyze memory first -- or really, perception, which comes before memory -- if we're going to get clear on this.

Protarchus: How will you proceed?

Socrates: Let's imagine that some physical sensations die out before they reach the soul, leaving her unaffected. And other sensations vibrate through both body and soul, sending a shock through each of them.

Protarchus: Granted.

Socrates: And we'd be right in saying the soul is unaware of the first kind but not the second?

Protarchus: Quite right.

Socrates: Now, when I say "unaware," don't think I mean "forgetfulness" in the literal sense. Forgetfulness is losing a memory, but in this case no memory was ever formed. You can't lose something that never existed and never entered your mind. You see the point?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: So just change the terminology for me.

Protarchus: How should I change it?

Socrates: Instead of saying the soul "forgets" when she's unaffected by bodily sensations, call it "unconsciousness."

Protarchus: I see.

Socrates: And the union or communion of soul and body in a single feeling and movement -- we'd properly call that "consciousness"?

Protarchus: Exactly.

Socrates: So now we know what we mean by that term?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And memory, I think, can be rightly described as the preservation of consciousness?

Protarchus: Right.

Socrates: But don't we distinguish memory from recollection?

Protarchus: I think so.

Socrates: By recollection, don't we mean the soul's power to recover on her own some feeling she originally experienced in company with the body?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And when she recovers on her own a lost memory of some conscious experience or piece of knowledge, that recovery is what we call recollection and reminiscence?

Protarchus: Exactly.

Socrates: There's a reason I'm going through all this.

Protarchus: What is it?

Socrates: I want to get the clearest possible picture of pleasure and desire as they exist in the soul alone, apart from the body. And the analysis we've just done helps reveal the nature of both.

Protarchus: Good -- let's move on to the next point, Socrates.

Socrates: There are certainly many things to consider about how pleasure works and what it really is. But first we need to figure out the nature and origin of desire.

Protarchus: Yes, let's look into that -- we've got nothing to lose.

Socrates: Actually, Protarchus, we've got something to lose -- the puzzle! Finding the answer means losing the problem.

Protarchus: Fair point! But let's keep going.

Socrates: We placed hunger, thirst, and similar feelings in the class of desires, didn't we?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: But they're very different from each other. What common nature do we have in mind when we call them by a single name?

Protarchus: By heaven, Socrates, that's not an easy question to answer. But it has to be answered.

Socrates: Then let's go back to our examples.

Protarchus: Where shall we start?

Socrates: What do we mean when we say "a person is thirsty"?

Protarchus: What do we mean?

Socrates: We mean he's empty.

Protarchus: Of course.

Socrates: And isn't thirst a desire?

Protarchus: Yes -- a desire for drink.

Socrates: For drink, or for being filled with drink?

Protarchus: For being filled with drink, I'd say.

Socrates: So a person who is empty desires the opposite of what he's experiencing -- he's empty and desires to be full?

Protarchus: Clearly.

Socrates: But how can someone who's empty for the first time reach any idea of fullness, which he has no present experience of and no past memory of?

Protarchus: He can't.

Socrates: And yet the person who desires certainly desires something?

Protarchus: Of course.

Socrates: He doesn't desire what he's experiencing -- he's experiencing thirst, which is emptiness. What he desires is fullness.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: So there must be something in the thirsty person that somehow grasps the idea of fullness?

Protarchus: There must be.

Socrates: And it can't be the body -- the body is the thing that's empty.

Protarchus: Right.

Socrates: The only remaining possibility is that the soul grasps fullness through memory. What else could it be?

Protarchus: I can't think of anything else.

Socrates: But do you see what follows?

Protarchus: What?

Socrates: There's no such thing as bodily desire.

Protarchus: Why not?

Socrates: Because the argument shows that every living creature's striving is directed toward the opposite of its bodily condition.

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And the impulse driving it toward the opposite proves that it has a memory of that opposite state.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: So by showing that memory draws us toward the objects of desire, the argument also proves that all impulses, desires, and the driving force in every living being originate in the soul.

Protarchus: That's absolutely right.

Socrates: The argument simply won't allow us to say that our bodies hunger or thirst or experience anything similar.

Protarchus: Exactly right.

Socrates: Let me make one more observation. The argument seems to point to a certain kind of life that consists in these experiences.

Protarchus: What experiences, and what kind of life?

Socrates: I'm talking about being emptied and filled, and everything related to the preservation and destruction of living beings -- the pain felt in one state and the pleasure that follows in the other.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: But what about the in-between state?

Protarchus: What do you mean by "in-between"?

Socrates: When someone is actually suffering but remembers past pleasures that would relieve him if they'd only come back -- but he doesn't have them yet. Can't we say he's in an in-between state?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: Would you say he's entirely in pain or entirely in pleasure?

Protarchus: Neither, I'd say. He has two pains: in his body there's the actual experience of pain, and in his soul there's the longing and the waiting.

Socrates: What do you mean by "two pains," Protarchus? Couldn't someone who's empty sometimes have a confident hope of being filled, and at other times be in complete despair?

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: And when he's hoping to be filled, isn't the memory giving him pleasure -- even while his emptiness is causing him pain at the same time?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: So humans and other animals can experience both pleasure and pain simultaneously?

Protarchus: I suppose so.

Socrates: But when someone is empty and has no hope of being filled, that's when the double experience of pain occurs. You noticed this and concluded it was the only possibility.

Protarchus: Exactly right, Socrates.

Socrates: Now, should this investigation of feelings give rise to another question?

Protarchus: What question?

Socrates: Whether we should say that the pleasures and pains we're discussing are true or false. Or are some true and some false?

Protarchus: But how, Socrates, can there be false pleasures and pains?

Socrates: And how, Protarchus, can there be true and false fears, or true and false expectations, or true and false opinions?

Protarchus: I'll grant that opinions can be true or false, but not pleasures.

Socrates: What's that? I'm afraid we're opening up a very serious inquiry here.

Protarchus: I agree with that much.

Socrates: But we should consider, my boy -- and you are one of Philebus' boys -- whether this inquiry is relevant to our argument.

Protarchus: Absolutely.

Socrates: We can't afford long, irrelevant digressions. What we say should be to the point.

Protarchus: Right.

Socrates: I've always been fascinated by the question that's just come up.

Protarchus: How so?

Socrates: You deny that some pleasures are false and others true?

Protarchus: I certainly do.

Socrates: Would you say that no one ever seems to be happy but isn't really, or seems to be in pain but isn't really -- whether dreaming or waking, sane or insane?

Protarchus: That's always been our assumption, Socrates.

Socrates: But were you right? Should we look into whether that assumption holds up?

Protarchus: I think we should.

Socrates: Then let's put the question about pleasure and opinion into more precise terms. Is there such a thing as opinion?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And such a thing as pleasure?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And an opinion must be about something?

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And a person must be pleased by something?

Protarchus: Correct.

Socrates: And whether the opinion is right or wrong, it's still an opinion?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And whether someone is rightly or wrongly pleased, won't they still genuinely feel pleasure?

Protarchus: Yes, that's true too.

Socrates: So how can opinion be both true and false, while pleasure is always true -- when both are equally real?

Protarchus: Yes, that's the question.

Socrates: You're saying that opinion takes on the quality of truth or falsity, so it becomes not just opinion but opinion with a certain character. And that's what you think needs to be examined?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And further: even if we admit that other things have qualities, perhaps pleasure and pain are simple experiences without quality?

Protarchus: Right.

Socrates: But it's not hard to see that pleasure and pain do have qualities -- they can be great or small, more intense or less intense. We said that long ago.

Protarchus: Quite true.

Socrates: And if badness attaches to any of them, Protarchus, we should speak of a bad opinion, or a bad pleasure?

Protarchus: Quite true, Socrates.

Socrates: And if rightness attaches to any of them, shouldn't we speak of a right opinion or a right pleasure? And likewise for their opposites?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And if an opinion is wrong about its object, can't we say the opinion is incorrect -- that it's not judging rightly?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And if we see a pleasure or pain that's mistaken about its object, shall we call it right or good, or give it any honorable name?

Protarchus: Not if the pleasure is mistaken -- how could we?

Socrates: And surely pleasure often accompanies an opinion that isn't true but false?

Protarchus: It certainly does. And in that case, Socrates, as we were saying, the opinion is false -- but nobody would call the actual pleasure false.

Socrates: How eagerly you rush to defend pleasure, Protarchus!

Protarchus: No, Socrates -- I'm just repeating what I've heard.

Socrates: But isn't there a difference, my friend, between a pleasure that's linked to right opinion and knowledge, and one that's linked to falsehood and ignorance?

Protarchus: There must be a very great difference.

Socrates: Then let's investigate that difference.

Protarchus: Lead the way -- I'll follow.

Socrates: Here's my view.

Protarchus: What is it?

Socrates: We agree, don't we, that there's such a thing as false opinion and true opinion?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And pleasure and pain, as I just said, often follow from these -- from true and false opinion.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: And don't opinion and the attempt to form opinions always spring from memory and perception?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: Could the process work something like this?

Protarchus: Like what?

Socrates: Someone often sees an object in the distance, not very clearly, and wants to figure out what it is.

Protarchus: That happens.

Socrates: Soon he starts asking himself questions.

Protarchus: In what way?

Socrates: He asks himself: "What's that standing by the rock under the tree?" That's the sort of question he'd put to himself when he sees something like that.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: Then he might guess the right answer, whispering to himself: "It's a person."

Protarchus: Very good.

Socrates: Or he might get it wrong and say: "No, it's a statue set up by some shepherds."

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And if someone's with him, he says his thought out loud, and what was just an opinion becomes a statement.

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: But if he's walking alone when these thoughts occur, he may keep them in his mind for quite a while.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: Now, I wonder if you'd agree with my explanation of what's happening here.

Protarchus: What's your explanation?

Socrates: I think the soul at times like these is like a book.

Protarchus: How so?

Socrates: Memory and perception meet up, and together with their attendant feelings they seem to write words in the soul. When this internal scribe writes truly, true opinions and true statements result. But when the scribe within us writes falsely, the results are false.

Protarchus: I agree completely.

Socrates: Then let me introduce you to another artist working alongside the scribe in the chambers of the soul.

Protarchus: Who's that?

Socrates: A painter, who after the scribe has done his work, draws images in the soul of the things the scribe described.

Protarchus: But when and how does he do this?

Socrates: When a person takes opinions or statements -- received through sight or some other sense -- and then visualizes the subjects in his mind. Isn't that a very common mental experience?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And the images that match true opinions and statements are true, while those that match false ones are false?

Protarchus: They are.

Socrates: If we're right so far, another question arises.

Protarchus: What?

Socrates: Do we have this experience only in relation to the present and the past, or the future as well?

Protarchus: I'd say all three -- all times equally.

Socrates: Didn't we say earlier that purely mental pleasures and pains can be anticipations of bodily ones? This means that anticipatory pleasures and pains are about the future.

Protarchus: Absolutely.

Socrates: And all those "writings" and "paintings" that we said are produced in us -- do they relate only to past and present, or to the future too?

Protarchus: To the future, very much so.

Socrates: When you say "very much," you mean that all these mental images are essentially hopes about the future, and that human beings are filled with hopes throughout their entire lives?

Protarchus: Exactly.

Socrates: Now answer me another question.

Protarchus: What?

Socrates: A just, pious, and good person is a friend of the gods, isn't he?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And an unjust, thoroughly bad person is the opposite?

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And as we were just saying, all people are always filled with hopes?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And these hopes are propositions -- statements that exist in the minds of each of us?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And the images of hope are also painted in us. A person may often have a vision of a pile of gold, with pleasures piling up, and in the picture he sees himself rejoicing wildly at his good fortune.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And can't we say that good people, being friends of the gods, generally have true pictures presented to them, while bad people have false ones?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: Bad people have pleasures painted in their imagination too, just like good people -- but theirs are false pleasures.

Protarchus: They are.

Socrates: So bad people generally delight in false pleasures, and good people in true ones?

Protarchus: That follows.

Socrates: On this analysis, then, there are false pleasures in people's souls -- ridiculous imitations of the true ones -- and pains of a similar character?

Protarchus: There are.

Socrates: Didn't we agree that someone who holds an opinion has a real opinion, even if it's about things that have never existed, don't exist now, and never will?

Protarchus: Quite true.

Socrates: And that was the source of false opinion -- opining about what doesn't exist. Right?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: So shouldn't we attribute the same kind of character to pleasure and pain -- real but illusory?

Protarchus: What do you mean?

Socrates: I mean this: a person must be admitted to have real pleasure if he's pleased about anything in any way. And he may be pleased about things that don't exist, never existed, and more often than not, never will exist.

Protarchus: Yes, Socrates, that's undeniable.

Socrates: And can't the same be said about fear, anger, and similar emotions -- that they're often false?

Protarchus: Quite so.

Socrates: And can opinions be good or bad in any way other than being true or false?

Protarchus: No other way.

Socrates: And pleasures can't really be called bad except insofar as they're false.

Protarchus: No, Socrates, that's the exact opposite of the truth! Nobody would call pleasures and pains bad because they're false -- they're bad because of some other deep corruption they're susceptible to.

Socrates

Well, we'll talk about pleasures that are corrupt and caused by corruption later, if we want to continue the inquiry. For now, I'd rather use a different argument to show that many false pleasures exist -- or come into existence -- in us, because that might help us reach our final verdict.

Protarchus: Fair enough -- assuming such pleasures exist.

Socrates: I think they do, Protarchus. But this shouldn't rest on a bare assertion -- it needs to be backed up.

Protarchus: Very good.

Socrates: Then let's step into the ring with this new argument, like wrestlers squaring off.

Protarchus: Let's go.

Socrates: We were saying a little while ago that when desires exist in us, the body has its own feelings separate from the soul. Remember?

Protarchus: Yes, I remember you said that.

Socrates: And the soul was supposed to desire the opposite of what the body was experiencing, while the body was the source of whatever pleasure or pain was being felt.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: So now you can figure out what happens in these cases.

Protarchus: What am I supposed to figure out?

Socrates: That in such cases, pleasures and pains come simultaneously -- opposite sensations sitting right next to each other, as we've already shown.

Protarchus: Clearly.

Socrates: And there's another point we agreed on.

Protarchus: What?

Socrates: That pleasure and pain both admit of more and less -- that they belong to the class of infinites.

Protarchus: We did say that.

Socrates: Then how can we judge them accurately?

Protarchus: How can we?

Socrates: Are we trying to judge their relative size and intensity, measuring pleasure against pain, pain against pain, pleasure against pleasure?

Protarchus: Yes, that's what we're doing, and that's how we'll judge.

Socrates: Well, think about sight. Doesn't the nearness or distance of objects distort their true size and make us form false impressions? Don't we find the same illusion happening with pleasures and pains?

Protarchus: Yes, Socrates, and to a far greater degree.

Socrates: So what we're saying now is actually the opposite of what we said before.

Protarchus: How so?

Socrates: Before, it was the opinions that were true or false, and they infected the pleasures and pains with their own truth or falsity.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: But now the pleasures and pains themselves are said to be true or false because they're seen at various distances and compared with each other. When set alongside pains, pleasures appear greater and more intense than they really are, and pains appear worse when set alongside pleasures.

Protarchus: That's bound to happen, for exactly the reason you're giving.

Socrates: So if you strip away from pleasures and pains the element that makes them appear larger or smaller than they really are, you'll agree that this element is illusory. And you'd never call the resulting excess or shortfall of pleasure or pain real or true.

Protarchus: Certainly not.

Socrates: Next, let's see whether we can find pleasures and pains in living beings that are even more false than these, coming from a different direction.

Protarchus: What are they, and how do we find them?

Socrates: Haven't I said repeatedly that pains, aches, suffering, and discomfort of every kind arise from corruption of our natural state -- from buildups and breakdowns, from excess and depletion, from growth and decay?

Protarchus: Yes, you've said that many times.

Socrates: And we've agreed that the restoration of the natural state is pleasure?

Protarchus: Right.

Socrates: But now let's suppose there's a stretch of time during which the body isn't going through any of these changes.

Protarchus: When could that happen, Socrates?

Socrates: Your question doesn't help the argument, Protarchus.

Protarchus: Why not?

Socrates: Because it doesn't stop me from asking mine.

Protarchus: And what was yours?

Socrates: Protarchus, even granting that no such interval actually occurs, I can still ask: what would happen if it did?

Protarchus: You mean what would happen if the body weren't being changed for either better or worse?

Socrates: Yes.

Protarchus: Well then, I'd suppose there would be neither pleasure nor pain.

Socrates: Very good. But unless I'm mistaken, you also believe that we must always be experiencing one or the other -- that's what the wise men say. They claim everything is always in flux, constantly flowing up and down.

Protarchus: Yes, and they're not minor thinkers saying this.

Socrates: Of course not -- they're major thinkers. But I'd like to dodge the force of their argument. Shall I tell you my escape plan? You can come along for the ride.

Protarchus: How?

Socrates: We'll say to them: "Fine. But are we, or living creatures in general, always conscious of what's happening to us? Take growth, for example -- are we always aware of that? On the contrary, aren't we almost entirely unconscious of it and similar processes?" You answer for them.

Protarchus: The second option is clearly correct.

Socrates: Then we weren't right to say, just now, that constant changes always produce pleasures and pains.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: A better and more defensible way to put it would be --

Protarchus: What?

Socrates: That large changes produce pleasures and pains, but moderate and small ones produce neither.

Protarchus: That's the more accurate way to say it, Socrates.

Socrates: But if that's true, then the life I mentioned earlier comes back into view.

Protarchus: Which life?

Socrates: The one we said was free of both pain and pleasure.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: So we can assume there are three kinds of life: one pleasant, one painful, and a third that's neither. What do you say?

Protarchus: I'd say the same -- there are three.

Socrates: If so, then the absence of pain is not the same thing as pleasure.

Protarchus: Certainly not.

Socrates: So when you hear someone saying that the most pleasant thing in the world is to live permanently without pain -- what do you think they actually mean?

Protarchus: I think they're confusing the absence of pain with pleasure.

Socrates: Let's take any three things. Or to make it fancier, let's call the first gold, the second silver, and the third something that's neither gold nor silver.

Protarchus: Fine.

Socrates: Now, can the thing that's neither gold nor silver be gold or silver?

Protarchus: Impossible.

Socrates: And no more can that neutral middle life be rightly thought of or spoken of as pleasant or painful.

Protarchus: Certainly not.

Socrates: And yet, my friend, there are people who think and say exactly that.

Protarchus: There certainly are.

Socrates: And do they think they're experiencing pleasure when they're free from pain?

Protarchus: That's what they say.

Socrates: Then they must think they're experiencing pleasure, or they wouldn't say so.

Protarchus: I suppose not.

Socrates: But if pleasure and the absence of pain are genuinely different things, they're wrong.

Protarchus: And they are undoubtedly different.

Socrates: So shall we take the view that there are three states, as we just said? Or only two -- pain being an evil, and freedom from pain being a good that's called "pleasant"?

Protarchus: But why are we even asking this, Socrates? I don't see the point.

Socrates: That's because you've never heard of certain enemies of our friend Philebus, Protarchus.

Protarchus: Who are they?

Socrates: Certain people who are reputed to be experts in natural philosophy. They deny that pleasure exists at all.

Protarchus: Really!

Socrates: They say that what Philebus' school calls "pleasures" are nothing but escape from pain.

Protarchus: And do you want us to agree with them, Socrates?

Socrates: No -- but I'd like to use them the way you'd use fortune-tellers who get the truth not through any proper method, but through an instinctive revulsion. They have a noble nature's deep disgust for the power of pleasure, and they find nothing wholesome in it. They consider her seductive influence a kind of sorcery, not genuine goodness. That's how you should use their views. After you've considered the various grounds for their hostility, you'll hear from me what I think the true pleasures are. That way we'll examine pleasure's nature from both sides before we render our verdict.

Protarchus: Well said.

Socrates: Then let's form an alliance with these philosophers and follow the trail of their hostility. I imagine they'd argue something like this. They'd start at the beginning and ask: if we wanted to understand any quality -- hardness, say -- would we learn more by looking at the hardest things or the least hard? You answer these stern gentlemen the way you answer me, Protarchus.

Protarchus: By all means. And I'd tell them: look at the most extreme examples.

Socrates: So if we want to see the true nature of pleasure as a class, we shouldn't look at the mildest pleasures but at the most extreme and intense ones?

Protarchus: Everyone would agree with that.

Socrates: And the most obviously intense pleasures, as we've often said, are bodily pleasures?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And do we experience these more intensely when we're sick or when we're healthy? Now we need to be careful here -- we don't want to trip up.

Protarchus: How would we trip up?

Socrates: Because we might be tempted to answer "when we're healthy."

Protarchus: Yes, that's the natural answer.

Socrates: True, but aren't the greatest pleasures the ones that satisfy the greatest desires?

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And don't people with a fever or similar illness feel thirst, cold, and other bodily needs far more intensely? They have deeper wants and greater pleasure when those wants are satisfied?

Protarchus: That's obviously right, once you say it.

Socrates: Then aren't we right to say that if someone wants to see the greatest pleasures, they shouldn't look at health but at sickness? Now, be careful -- don't think I'm asking whether very sick people have more pleasures than healthy people overall. I'm asking about the intensity of pleasure -- where pleasure reaches its most extreme heights. Our task, remember, is to discover what pleasure really is, and what those people mean who say pleasure doesn't even exist.

Protarchus: I think I follow you.

Socrates: You'll have a chance to show me soon enough. Answer this: do you see more intense, more excessive pleasures in self-indulgence or in self-control? Think before you speak.

Protarchus: I see what you're getting at, and the difference is enormous. The self-controlled person is restrained by the old motto "nothing in excess" -- that's their rule. But excessive pleasure, when it takes over the minds of fools and the undisciplined, drives them to madness and makes them shout out loud.

Socrates: Good. And if that's true, then the greatest pleasures and pains will obviously be found in some corrupt state of body and soul, not in a virtuous one.

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: So shouldn't we pick out some of these and see what makes them so great?

Protarchus: We should.

Socrates: Take the pleasures that arise from certain disorders.

Protarchus: What disorders?

Socrates: The pleasures of shameful conditions -- the ones that our stern philosophical friends absolutely detest.

Protarchus: What pleasures specifically?

Socrates: The relief of itching by scratching, for instance, and other ailments where scratching is the only remedy needed. Because -- by heaven -- what are we supposed to call the feeling this produces? Pleasure or pain?

Protarchus: A nasty mixture of both, I'd say, Socrates.

Socrates: I didn't bring this up to embarrass Philebus personally. But without considering these kinds of pleasures and others like them, we can't settle the question before us.

Protarchus: Then we'd better analyze this family of pleasures.

Socrates: You mean the pleasures that are mixed with pain?

Protarchus: Exactly.

Socrates: Some of these mixtures are purely bodily -- in the body alone. Others are purely in the soul -- in the soul alone. And still others are mixtures of pleasure and pain shared by both body and soul, which in their combined state are sometimes called pleasures and sometimes pains.

Protarchus: How does that work?

Socrates: Whenever the body is going through restoration and breakdown at the same time, a person experiences two opposite feelings -- say, being cold while warming up, or being hot while cooling down. He wants one feeling and wants to be rid of the other. The sweet is mixed with the bitter, as the saying goes, and both sensations cling to him, creating frustration that eventually drives him to distraction.

Protarchus: That's a very accurate description.

Socrates: And in these sorts of mixtures, the pleasures and pains are sometimes equal, and sometimes one or the other dominates?

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: For cases where pain exceeds pleasure, think of itching, which we were just discussing -- the tingling you feel when there's a burning, inflamed condition inside, and rubbing and scratching only reaches the surface without touching the affected parts. If you go further and try fire, or as a last resort cold, you might produce the most intense pleasure or pain in the deeper parts, which contrasts and mingles with the pain or pleasure of the outer parts. This happens because what's naturally united gets forcibly separated, or what's separated gets pushed together, and pleasure and pain sit right next to each other.

Protarchus: Right.

Socrates: Other times the element of pleasure dominates, and the slight undercurrent of pain makes the person tingle and creates a gentle irritation. Or the excessive infusion of pleasure whips them into a frenzy -- they leap for joy, strike all kinds of poses, change color, gasp for breath, and end up completely beside themselves, shouting the most irrational things.

Protarchus: Yes, indeed.

Socrates: They'll say, and others will say about them, that they're "dying" from these delights. And the more reckless and dissolute a person is, the more intensely he pursues them in every way. He declares them the greatest of all pleasures and considers whoever lives in the most constant enjoyment of them to be the happiest person in the world.

Protarchus: That's a very accurate portrait of what most people think about pleasure, Socrates.

Socrates: Quite right, Protarchus -- at least about the mixed pleasures that arise from the combination of external and internal bodily sensations. But there are also cases where the mind contributes an opposing element to the body -- whether pleasure or pain -- and the two merge into a single mixture. We talked about this earlier: when a person is hungry, he hopes for food -- pleasure from hope, pain from emptiness. But I need to add what I left out before: in all these cases, and countless others like them, where body and mind are at odds, pleasure and pain combine into one experience.

Protarchus: I believe that's entirely true.

Socrates: There's still one more type of pleasure-pain mixture left.

Protarchus: What is it?

Socrates: The combination that the mind experiences entirely on its own -- purely mental feelings.

Protarchus: What do you mean?

Socrates: Don't we speak of anger, fear, desire, grief, love, jealousy, envy, and the like as pains belonging to the soul alone?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And won't we find them full of the most extraordinary pleasures too? Need I remind you of that anger

  "Which stirs even a wise man to violence,
  And is sweeter than honey and the honeycomb?"

And you remember how pleasures mingle with pains in grief and mourning?

Protarchus: Yes, there's a natural connection between them.

Socrates: And you remember how at tragedies the audience smiles through their tears?

Protarchus: I certainly do.

Socrates: And are you aware that even at a comedy, the soul experiences a mixture of pain and pleasure?

Protarchus: I don't quite follow.

Socrates: I'll admit, Protarchus, that it's not easy to recognize this mixture of feelings at a comedy.

Protarchus: Not easy at all.

Socrates: But the more obscure the case, the more worthwhile it is to examine it. If we can detect the mixture here, it'll be easier to spot in other cases.

Protarchus: Go on.

Socrates: I just mentioned envy. Would you call that a pain of the soul?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: Yet the envious person finds something pleasing in his neighbor's misfortunes?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And ignorance -- what people call cluelessness -- is an evil, right?

Protarchus: Sure.

Socrates: From these observations, learn to understand the nature of the ridiculous.

Protarchus: Explain.

Socrates: The ridiculous is basically the specific name for a particular kind of vice. And among vices in general, it's the one that most directly violates the inscription at Delphi.

Protarchus: You mean "Know thyself."

Socrates: I do. And the opposite would be: "Don't know thyself."

Protarchus: Right.

Socrates: Now, Protarchus, try dividing this into three kinds.

Protarchus: I'm afraid I can't.

Socrates: So you want me to do the dividing?

Protarchus: Yes -- and please do.

Socrates: Aren't there three ways a person can fail to know himself?

Protarchus: What are they?

Socrates: First, about money -- the ignorant person may think he's richer than he actually is.

Protarchus: Yes, that's a very common delusion.

Socrates: Even more often, he'll think he's taller, better-looking, or more physically impressive than he really is.

Protarchus: Of course.

Socrates: But by far the most people deceive themselves about the qualities of the mind -- they imagine they're much better, wiser, and more virtuous than they actually are.

Protarchus: Yes, that's the most common delusion of all.

Socrates: And of all the virtues, isn't wisdom the one that most people lay claim to -- the one that most stirs up competitive posturing and false pretensions?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And we can call this entire condition an evil?

Protarchus: A very great evil.

Socrates: But we need to divide it one more step, Protarchus, if we want to see how this childish sort of envy involves a curious blend of pleasure and pain.

Protarchus: How do we make this further division?

Socrates: Everyone who's foolish enough to hold this inflated view of themselves can be divided -- like the rest of humanity -- into two groups: those with power and those without it.

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: There's your dividing line. Those who are weak and can't retaliate when they're laughed at may truly be called ridiculous. But those who can defend themselves are better described as strong and dangerous. Ignorance in the powerful is hateful and horrible, because it actually hurts people -- in real life and in stories. But powerless ignorance can fairly be classified as ridiculous -- and genuinely is.

Protarchus: That's very true, but I still don't see where the mixture of pleasure and pain comes in.

Socrates: Then let's examine the nature of envy.

Protarchus: Go ahead.

Socrates: Isn't envy an unjust pleasure and also an unjust pain?

Protarchus: It certainly is.

Socrates: There's nothing envious or wrong about feeling happy when your enemies suffer, is there?

Protarchus: No, nothing wrong with that.

Socrates: But feeling joy instead of sorrow at your friends' misfortunes -- that's wrong?

Protarchus: Obviously.

Socrates: And we said that ignorance is always an evil?

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: Now, those three kinds of false self-image we listed -- about beauty, wisdom, and wealth -- are ridiculous when they belong to people who are weak, and hateful when they belong to the powerful. So can we say, as I was saying before, that when our friends are in this deluded state, and they're harmless, they're simply ridiculous?

Protarchus: They are ridiculous.

Socrates: And don't we acknowledge that their ignorance is a misfortune for them?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: But do we feel pain or pleasure when we laugh at it?

Protarchus: Clearly we feel pleasure.

Socrates: And wasn't envy the source of this pleasure we feel at our friends' misfortunes?

Protarchus: It must be.

Socrates: Then the argument shows that when we laugh at the foolishness of our friends, we mix pleasure with envy -- which means we're mixing pleasure with pain. Because we've already agreed that envy is a mental pain, and laughter is pleasant. So we envy and laugh at the same moment.

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And the argument implies that mixtures of pleasure and pain occur in lamentation, in tragedy and comedy -- not only on the stage but on the much larger stage of human life. And in countless other cases too.

Protarchus: I don't see how anyone could deny what you're saying, Socrates, no matter how badly they want to argue the other side.

Socrates: I mentioned anger, desire, grief, fear, love, jealousy, envy, and similar emotions as examples where we'd find a mixture of those two elements we keep naming. Didn't I?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And you realize that everything we've just concluded applies only to grief, envy, and anger?

Protarchus: I see that.

Socrates: Which means many other cases remain?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And why do you think I went into such detail about the mixture in comedy? To convince you that it's not hard to show the mixed nature of fear, love, and the rest. I figured once I'd given you that illustration, you'd let me off the hook and agree as a general principle that body without soul, soul without body, and the two together are all susceptible to every kind of pleasure-pain mixture -- and further discussion would be unnecessary. So now, do I get to leave? Or will you keep me here until midnight? I think I can earn my release without many more words if I promise to give you an account of all these other cases tomorrow. But right now I'd rather sail in a different direction and move on to the issues that still need settling before we can reach the verdict Philebus is demanding.

Protarchus: Very good, Socrates. Handle what remains however you see fit.

Socrates: After the mixed pleasures, the unmixed ones should have their turn. That's the natural and necessary order.

Protarchus: Excellent.

Socrates: I'll try to describe them now. I don't agree with the people who say all pleasures are just cessation of pain. But as I said, I use them as witnesses that some pleasures only seem to be pleasures but aren't really, and that others, though they appear in many impressive forms, are actually tangled up with pain -- partial relief from intense bodily and mental suffering.

Protarchus: Then which pleasures, Socrates, should we rightly consider true?

Socrates: True pleasures are the ones that come from beauty of color and form, most of those that come from scents, those from sounds, and in general any pleasures where the lack isn't painful or even noticed, and the fulfillment is perceptible, pleasant, and unmixed with pain.

Protarchus: I need you to explain that more clearly, Socrates.

Socrates: What I mean is certainly not obvious, so I'll try to be clearer. When I say "beauty of form," I don't mean the beauty of animals or paintings -- which is probably what most people would assume. I mean straight lines and circles, and the flat and solid shapes made from them with lathes, rulers, and protractors. These, I'm saying, are not merely relatively beautiful, the way other things are. They are eternally and absolutely beautiful, and they have their own distinctive pleasures, completely different from the pleasure of scratching an itch. And there are colors with the same character and similar pleasures. Do you follow?

Protarchus: I'm trying, Socrates. Could you make it even clearer?

Socrates: When sounds are smooth and clear, producing a single pure tone, I'm saying they're not just relatively beautiful but absolutely beautiful, and they carry natural pleasures of their own.

Protarchus: Yes, there are such pleasures.

Socrates: The pleasures of smell are a somewhat less elevated type, but they involve no necessary mixture of pain. And any pleasures, however and wherever experienced, that come without any accompanying pain -- I assign them all to a similar class. So there are two kinds of pure pleasures.

Protarchus: I understand.

Socrates: We can add to these the pleasures of learning -- assuming no hunger for knowledge and no pain from that hunger come first.

Protarchus: That seems right.

Socrates: But what if someone who's full of knowledge later loses it -- aren't there pains of forgetting?

Protarchus: Not necessarily. Though there might be times of reflection when a person feels grief over losing what he knew.

Socrates: True, my friend, but right now we're cataloguing only natural, immediate experiences -- not reflective ones.

Protarchus: In that case you're right: the loss of knowledge isn't accompanied by pain.

Socrates: So the pleasures of learning are unmixed with pain. And they don't belong to the masses but to a very few.

Protarchus: Quite true.

Socrates: Now, having properly separated the pure pleasures from those that can rightly be called impure, let me add this to our description: the pleasures that go to excess have no measure, while those that don't are measured. The great, excessive pleasures -- whether they come more or less often -- belong in the class of the infinite, of the more and less, which flows through both body and soul. And the moderate ones belong to the class of measure.

Protarchus: Exactly right, Socrates.

Socrates: There's still more to consider about pleasures.

Protarchus: What?

Socrates: When we talk about purity and clarity, or about excess, abundance, greatness, and sufficiency -- how do these terms relate to truth?

Protarchus: Why do you ask, Socrates?

Socrates: Because I want to test pleasure and knowledge in every way possible, Protarchus. If there's a pure and an impure element in either of them, I want to isolate the pure element for judgment. That'll make it easier for you, me, and everyone to evaluate them.

Protarchus: Absolutely.

Socrates: Let's examine all the pure types. We'll start by selecting a single example.

Protarchus: Which one?

Socrates: Let's start with whiteness.

Protarchus: Very good.

Socrates: What makes whiteness pure? Is it being the greatest in quantity, or the most unadulterated -- the freest from any admixture of other colors?

Protarchus: Obviously the most unadulterated.

Socrates: Right, Protarchus. So the purest white, not the most abundant, is to be considered the truest and most beautiful?

Protarchus: Absolutely.

Socrates: And we'd be perfectly right to say that a small amount of pure white is whiter, more beautiful, and truer than a large amount of mixed white.

Protarchus: Perfectly right.

Socrates: Then we don't need a parade of examples to make this point about pleasure. A single case is enough to prove that a small, pure pleasure -- unalloyed with pain -- is always more pleasant, truer, and more beautiful than a large amount of impure pleasure.

Protarchus: Absolutely -- and one example is plenty.

Socrates: But here's another question: haven't we heard that pleasure is always a process of becoming and never has true being? Certain ingenious thinkers teach this, and shouldn't we be grateful to them?

Protarchus: What do they mean?

Socrates: I'll explain by asking you a question, dear Protarchus.

Protarchus: Go ahead.

Socrates: Let's say there are two kinds of things in the world: one that exists for its own sake, and another that's always striving after something else.

Protarchus: What do you mean by these two kinds?

Socrates: One is noble and self-sufficient. The other is subordinate.

Protarchus: That's pretty cryptic.

Socrates: You've seen beautiful, worthy things, and you've seen devoted lovers pursuing them.

Protarchus: I suppose so.

Socrates: Now search the whole universe for two terms that match this pair and are found everywhere.

Protarchus: Please, Socrates -- be a little clearer, for the third time!

Socrates: It's not complicated, Protarchus. The argument is just being playful. It's hinting that some things exist for the sake of something else -- they're means to an end. And other things are the ends themselves -- what the first group serves.

Protarchus: I think your many repetitions are slowing me down, not speeding me up.

Socrates: As the argument goes on, my boy, the meaning will probably get clearer.

Protarchus: I hope so.

Socrates: Here are two more principles.

Protarchus: What are they?

Socrates: One is becoming -- the process of coming into existence. The other is being -- actual existence.

Protarchus: I accept both -- becoming and being.

Socrates: Good. Now would you say that becoming exists for the sake of being, or being for the sake of becoming?

Protarchus: You're asking whether what we call "being" actually exists for the sake of "becoming"?

Socrates: Yes.

Protarchus: By the gods, you'd better repeat that!

Socrates: Let me put it this way, Protarchus: would you say that shipbuilding exists for the sake of ships, or ships for the sake of shipbuilding? And the same question for every similar case.

Protarchus: Why don't you answer that yourself, Socrates?

Socrates: I'm happy to, but you have to participate.

Protarchus: Of course.

Socrates: Here's my answer: all instrumental, remedial, and material things are given to us for the sake of some process of becoming. And each becoming exists for the sake of some particular being or reality. And all becoming, taken as a whole, exists for the sake of all being, taken as a whole.

Protarchus: Absolutely.

Socrates: Then pleasure, being a process of becoming, must exist for the sake of some being?

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And that which other things serve -- the end they exist for -- must be placed in the class of the good. While that which exists for the sake of something else belongs in a different class, my friend.

Protarchus: Most certainly.

Socrates: So pleasure, being a process of becoming, will rightly be placed in some class other than the good?

Protarchus: Quite right.

Socrates: Then, as I said at the start, we should be very grateful to the thinker who first showed that pleasure is only a process of becoming and has no true being at all. He's obviously laughing at the idea that pleasure could be the good.

Protarchus: Absolutely.

Socrates: And he'd surely laugh at people who make becoming their ultimate goal.

Protarchus: Who are you talking about? What do they mean?

Socrates: I'm talking about people who feel delight when they cure their hunger, thirst, or any other deficiency through some process of restoration, and who treat the process itself as though it were pleasure. They say they wouldn't want to live without these experiences, and others like them.

Protarchus: That does seem to be how they think.

Socrates: And everyone agrees that destruction is the opposite of coming-into-being?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: So anyone who makes this choice would be choosing a life of constant becoming and perishing rather than that third kind of life we discussed -- the one with neither pleasure nor pain, but only the purest possible thought.

Protarchus: It's deeply absurd, Socrates, to claim that pleasure is the good.

Socrates: Deeply. And there's yet another absurdity.

Protarchus: What?

Socrates: Isn't it absurd to say that there's nothing good or noble in the body or anywhere else -- that good exists only in the soul, and that the only good of the soul is pleasure? And that courage, self-control, understanding, and every other virtue of the soul are not really good at all? And on top of that, to be forced to say that someone feeling pain and no pleasure is bad at the moment they're suffering -- even if they're the best person alive? And conversely, that someone feeling pleasure is virtuous to the degree they're pleased, in the very act of being pleased?

Protarchus: Nothing could be more irrational than all of that, Socrates.

Socrates: Good. Now, having subjected pleasure to every kind of test, let's not seem too easy on mind and knowledge. Let's strike them hard and see if they ring true or if there's anything unsound. We need to find the purest elements in each, and then bring the truest forms of both pleasure and knowledge forward for judgment.

Protarchus: Right.

Socrates: Knowledge has two branches: one productive, and the other educational?

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: And in the productive arts and crafts, isn't one part more closely related to true knowledge, and the other less so? Can we treat one as the pure element and the other as the impure?

Protarchus: We can.

Socrates: Let's separate out the dominant, superior element in each.

Protarchus: What is it? How do you separate them?

Socrates: Here's how: if you take away arithmetic, measurement, and weighing from any craft, what's left isn't much.

Protarchus: Not much at all.

Socrates: What remains would be just guesswork -- the trained use of the senses that comes from experience and practice, plus a knack for estimating that's commonly called "skill" and is developed through effort and repetition.

Protarchus: Nothing more than that.

Socrates: Music, for instance, is full of this guesswork. Its harmonies are tuned not by measurement but by practiced estimation -- the flute player is always trying to guess the pitch of each vibrating note. So music is mixed up with a great deal of uncertainty and has little precision.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: And you'll find the same holds for medicine, farming, navigation, and military strategy.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: But construction, on the other hand, uses a whole array of measures and instruments, and achieves a much higher degree of precision than those other crafts.

Protarchus: How so?

Socrates: In shipbuilding, house-building, and other forms of carpentry, the builder has his ruler, lathe, compass, plumb line, and a very clever device for straightening wood.

Protarchus: Very true, Socrates.

Socrates: So let's divide the arts we've been discussing into two categories: those that, like music, are less exact in their results, and those that, like building, are more exact.

Protarchus: Let's make that division.

Socrates: And of the more exact category, the most exact of all are the ones we just called primary.

Protarchus: You mean arithmetic and the related arts of weighing and measuring.

Socrates: Exactly, Protarchus. But aren't even these divisible into two kinds?

Protarchus: Two kinds?

Socrates: Take arithmetic first. There are two kinds: one popular, one philosophical.

Protarchus: How would you distinguish them?

Socrates: There's a big difference, Protarchus. Some arithmeticians count unequal units -- two armies, two oxen, two very large things or two very small things. The other school insists that every unit in ten thousand must be exactly the same as every other unit.

Protarchus: That is indeed a significant difference, and there's good reason to say there are two kinds of arithmetic.

Socrates: And when we compare the practical measurement used in building with philosophical geometry, or the arithmetic used in commerce with pure mathematical calculation -- should we say each pair is one art or two?

Protarchus: Following the logic of what came before, I'd say each pair is two.

Socrates: Right. But do you see why I've brought all this up?

Protarchus: I think so, but I'd like to hear you say it.

Socrates: The argument has been looking for a parallel to our analysis of pleasure. It's been asking whether one kind of knowledge is purer than another, just as one pleasure is purer than another.

Protarchus: That was clearly the goal.

Socrates: And hasn't the argument already shown that different arts have different domains and vary in their degrees of certainty?

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: And just now, the argument first gave a particular art a single name -- making us think it was one art -- and then, as if talking about two different things, asked whether the philosophical version or the practical version has more certainty and purity?

Protarchus: That's exactly the question being raised.

Socrates: So how do we answer, Protarchus?

Protarchus: Socrates, we've reached a point where the difference in clarity between different kinds of knowledge is enormous.

Socrates: Then the answer should be easy.

Protarchus: Certainly. And let our answer be: the arts that involve arithmetic and measurement far surpass all others. And of those, the ones driven by genuine philosophical inquiry are infinitely superior in accuracy and truth.

Socrates: That's your judgment, then. And on your authority, we'll give this answer to anyone who tries to twist our words?

Protarchus: What answer?

Socrates: That there are two kinds of arithmetic, two of measurement, and many other arts that have the same double nature -- yet only one name.

Protarchus: Let's boldly give that answer, Socrates, and hope for the best.

Socrates: So we've identified what we consider the most exact arts and sciences.

Protarchus: Very good.

Socrates: And yet, Protarchus, dialectic will refuse to acknowledge us if we don't give her first place.

Protarchus: And what exactly is dialectic?

Socrates: Obviously the discipline that deals with everything we've been talking about -- all knowledge of being and reality, of sameness and unchangeability. I'm sure anyone with a shred of intelligence would agree that the knowledge dealing with true being and eternal reality is by far the truest knowledge of all. But how would you decide, Protarchus?

Protarchus: I've often heard Gorgias argue that the art of persuasion surpasses every other art by far. It's the best of them all, he says, because everything submits to it -- not by force, but willingly. I wouldn't want to quarrel with either you or him.

Socrates: Sounds like you wanted to desert but were too embarrassed.

Protarchus: Take it however you like.

Socrates: Maybe I've led you into a misunderstanding?

Protarchus: How?

Socrates: My dear Protarchus, I wasn't asking which art is the greatest, the best, or the most useful. I was asking which one has the most clarity, accuracy, and truth -- however humble and impractical it might be. As for Gorgias, he won't quarrel with you if you say his art has the edge in practical usefulness, while the discipline I'm describing is superior in the pursuit of essential truth. It's like our comparison of white colors: a small amount that's pure was said to be truer than a large amount that's mixed. So let's focus carefully. Let's not ask about the comparative usefulness or reputation of the various disciplines, but about the power or faculty -- if there is one -- that the soul has for loving truth and doing everything for its sake. Let's examine the pure element of mind and intelligence, and then we'll be able to say whether this discipline is most likely to possess that faculty, or whether some other has a stronger claim.

Protarchus: Well, I've been thinking about it, and I can hardly imagine any other science or art has a firmer grip on truth than this one.

Socrates: Are you saying that because you notice that the arts in general -- and people engaged in them -- work with opinion and are deeply absorbed in investigating matters of opinion? Even someone who thinks he's studying nature is really dealing with this world -- how things are created, how they act and are acted upon. Isn't that the kind of inquiry that fills his life?

Protarchus: True.

Socrates: He's working not with eternal being, but with things that are becoming -- things that will be, have been, or are coming to be.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: And can we say that any of these things -- which neither are, nor have been, nor will be unchanging -- can ever become certain when judged by the strict standard of truth?

Protarchus: Impossible.

Socrates: How could anything stable be built on what has no stability?

Protarchus: How indeed?

Socrates: So mind and knowledge, when applied to things that are always changing, can't achieve the highest truth?

Protarchus: I'd think not.

Socrates: Then let's set aside you, me, Philebus, and Gorgias, and make one claim on behalf of the argument itself.

Protarchus: What claim?

Socrates: That what is stable, pure, true, and unmixed belongs to the things that are eternal, unchangeable, and unalloyed. Or if not to those exactly, then to whatever is most closely related to them. Everything else must be ranked in a second, inferior class.

Protarchus: Very true.

Socrates: And when it comes to the terms we use for knowing -- shouldn't the finest terms be given to the finest objects?

Protarchus: That's natural.

Socrates: And aren't "mind" and "wisdom" the most honored terms?

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: And these terms apply most truly and precisely when the mind is engaged in contemplating true being?

Protarchus: Certainly.

Socrates: And these were the very terms I put forward as the rivals of pleasure?

Protarchus: They were indeed, Socrates.

Socrates: Now then -- as for the mixture. Here are our ingredients: pleasure and wisdom. We're like craftsmen with our materials laid out before us.

Protarchus: Yes.

Socrates: Time to start mixing?

Protarchus: By all means.

Socrates: But shouldn't we review the key points first, to refresh our memories?

Protarchus: About what?

Socrates: About what I've already said. As the proverb goes, good things are worth saying twice -- even three times.

Protarchus: Certainly.
