And with earth, the most sluggish of the four, which is purged on a fourfold cycle, the result is a fever returning every fourth day — the quartan — which is very hard to shake.

So much for how diseases of the body arise. The disorders of the soul that depend on the body come about like this. We have to agree that disease of the mind is a deficiency of intelligence, and that it comes in two kinds: madness and ignorance. Whatever condition brings on either one deserves to be called disease — and excessive pleasures and pains should be ranked among the greatest diseases the soul can suffer. A person in the grip of overwhelming joy or overwhelming pain, frantically rushing to seize the one or escape the other, can't see or hear anything correctly. He is, at that moment, raving — utterly incapable of using reason at all.

Take a man whose seed runs too plentiful and overflowing around the spinal marrow, like a tree weighed down with too much fruit. He suffers many torments and grabs at many pleasures, in his desires and in what comes of them, and spends most of his life deranged, because his pleasures and pains are so extreme. His soul is made foolish and disordered by his body — and yet people treat him not as sick but as willfully bad. That is a mistake. The truth is that sexual excess is a disease of the soul, caused mostly by the moisture and fluidity that one element produces in us when the bones are loosely knit. And in general, nearly everything labeled "lack of self-control over pleasure," and treated as a disgrace — as though the wicked do wrong on purpose — is not really fair grounds for reproach. No one is willingly bad. The bad become bad through some defective condition of the body and a bad upbringing — things every man hates, and which happen to him against his will.

The same goes for pain: there too, the soul takes great damage from the body. When acid and salty phlegms, or bitter, bilious humors, roam through the body and find no exit, no escape, they get pent up inside, blending their own vapors with the motions of the soul. Mixed in this way, they produce diseases of the soul in every variety and intensity. Carried to the three seats of the soul, they create — depending on which region they attack — endless varieties of bad temper and gloom, of recklessness and cowardice, of forgetfulness and dullness.

And on top of this: when people with constitutions this bad live under bad forms of government, where the talk in public and private is equally bad, and when nothing is done in their youth, through education, to cure these evils — that is how all of us who are bad become bad, through two causes entirely beyond our control. The blame belongs to the planters rather than the plants, to the educators rather than the educated. Still, we should make every effort — through upbringing, through pursuits, through study — to flee vice and seize its opposite. But that belongs to another discussion.

It's now fitting and proper to turn the subject around and ask about the corresponding treatments — what keeps the body and mind sound. After all, it's more right to talk about the good than the bad. Everything good is beautiful, and the beautiful is never out of proportion. So a living creature that is going to be beautiful must have proportion. Now, we notice the minor proportions and reason about them, but we completely overlook the greatest and most important ones. For health and disease, virtue and vice, no proportion or disproportion matters more than the one between soul and body. Yet we never look at this. We never reflect that when a mighty soul rides in a frame too weak and small for it — or the reverse, when a little soul is housed in a big body — the creature as a whole is not beautiful, because it lacks the most important proportion of all. The right balance of mind and body, to anyone with eyes to see it, is the most beautiful and lovely of all sights.

Think of a body with one leg too long, or lopsided in some other way. It's unpleasant to look at; and when it's put to work, it strains and lurches, stumbles through its own awkwardness, and brings endless trouble on itself. We should picture the two-natured compound we call a living being the same way. When the soul in this partnership is more powerful than the body and burns hot, it shakes the whole inner man and fills him with disorders. When it throws itself into intense study and research, it wastes the body away. When it teaches or argues, in public or in private, through rivalries and disputes, it inflames the body's frame, shakes it loose, and brings on discharges — which fools most so-called doctors into blaming the very opposite of the true cause. And the reverse happens too: when a large body, too strong for the soul, is yoked to a small, feeble mind. Human beings have two natural appetites — the body's appetite for food, and the appetite of the divinest part of us for wisdom. The motions of the stronger part prevail and promote their own interest, but they render the soul dull, slow, and forgetful, producing ignorance, the greatest disease of all.

There is one protection against both kinds of imbalance: don't exercise the body without the soul, or the soul without the body. That way each keeps the other in check, and both stay balanced and healthy. So the mathematician, or anyone deeply absorbed in some intellectual pursuit, must give his body its workout too, and take up physical training; and the person devoted to sculpting his body must give the soul its own exercise in return, taking up music and philosophy in all its forms — if he wants to deserve, with any justice, the names beautiful and good.

The parts of the body deserve the same kind of care, on the model of the universe itself. The body is heated and cooled from within by what enters it, dried and moistened by what surrounds it, and suffers all the disturbances both kinds of motion bring. If a person surrenders his body, motionless, to these forces, it is overpowered and destroyed. But suppose instead he imitates what we earlier called the foster-mother and nurse of the universe: he never lets the body rest idle, but keeps it in motion, giving it regular shakings throughout, which form its natural defense against disturbances inner and outer. By moderate exercise he sorts the particles and disturbances wandering through the body, settling them into order with their natural kin — just as we described when speaking of the universe. Then he won't leave enemy stationed beside enemy, breeding wars and disease in the body; he'll settle friend beside friend, and the result is health.

Among motions, the best is the motion a thing produces in itself — for it is most akin to the motion of thought and the motion of the universe. Motion produced by something else is inferior. Worst of all is motion that, while the body lies inert, moves only its parts, and only by outside agency. So of all the ways of cleansing and toning the body, the best is gymnastic exercise. Second best is the swaying motion of a boat, or any form of transportation that doesn't tire you. The third kind may be useful when you're absolutely desperate, but no sensible person would accept it otherwise: I mean the doctor's purgative drugs. Diseases, unless they're truly dangerous, should not be provoked with medicines. Every disease has a structure that resembles, in a way, the nature of a living creature — and the living creature is put together with a fixed term of life. Not just for the species as a whole, but for each individual: every one of us is born with his lifespan set by fate, accidents aside, because our triangles are framed from the start with the power to hold together for just so long, and no man can ever prolong his life beyond it. The structure of diseases works the same way. If you fight one with drugs before its destined time has run, you tend to turn mild diseases severe, and a few into many. So you should manage all of them, as far as your free time allows, by diet and regimen, rather than provoking a stubborn enemy with drugs.

Let that be enough about the composite creature, and about its bodily part, and how a man may train himself and be trained to live the most rational life. But first and above all, we must prepare the part that's going to do the training, making it as fine and as fit for the job as possible. To work through this in detail would be a serious task in itself; but treated in passing, as before, the matter can fairly be summed up like this.

I have said more than once that three kinds of soul are housed within us, each with its own motions. So now let me say, as briefly as I can: whichever of them sits idle, with its motions stilled, inevitably becomes weakest, while the one that's exercised becomes strongest. So we must take care that their motions stay in proportion to one another.

As for the most sovereign kind of soul in us, think of it this way: God gave it to each of us as a guiding spirit — the part that we say lives at the top of the body and lifts us up from earth toward our kindred in heaven. For we are a plant rooted not in earth but in heaven, and that is no mere figure of speech. From the place where the soul first came to be, the divine power keeps our head — our root — suspended, and so holds the whole body upright.

Now, if a man devotes himself to appetite and ambition and labors endlessly to feed them, all his thoughts must become mortal; he ends up mortal through and through, as far as that's possible, because that's the part of himself he has nourished. But the man who has devoted himself to the love of learning and to true wisdom, and has exercised that part of himself above all, must — if he touches truth — think thoughts immortal and divine. So far as human nature can partake of immortality, he will not fall short of it in any way; and because he constantly tends the divine element, keeping the guardian spirit that lives within him in good order, he must be happy above all men.

Now, there is only one way to care for anything: give it the nourishment and the motions that are proper to it. And the motions akin to the divine part within us are the thoughts and revolutions of the universe. These every man should follow. By learning the harmonies and revolutions of the universe, he should correct the circuits in his own head, which were knocked off course at birth — bringing the thinking part into likeness with what it thinks about, restoring its original nature. And having achieved that likeness, he attains the goal of the best life the gods have set before mankind, both for now and for all time to come.

And with that, the task we took on at the start — to tell the story of the universe down to the creation of man — is just about complete. The origin of the other animals can be covered briefly; there's no need to go on at length, and brevity here will keep our whole account in better proportion. So let it be told as follows.

Of the men who came into the world, those who were cowards, or who lived unjust lives, were — on the most plausible account — reborn as women in the second generation. And this is when the gods devised the desire for sexual union, constructing one living thing in us men and another in women. They made each as follows. There is a channel for drink, where liquid passes through the lungs, under the kidneys, and into the bladder, which collects it and expels it under air pressure. The gods bored a connection from this channel into the column of marrow that runs from the head down through the neck and along the spine — the marrow which, earlier in our account, we called "seed." This seed, being alive and now given an outlet to breathe through, planted in that very outlet a living thirst for emission, and so produced our desire to procreate. That is why a man's generative organs are unruly and self-willed, like an animal deaf to reason, and try, in their stinging frenzy of lust, to carry all before them. In women, for the same reasons, what is called the womb or matrix is a living creature inside them with a desire for childbearing. When it stays barren long past its proper season, it grows resentful and angry, and wandering all through the body, it blocks the channels of breath and prevents respiration, driving the woman to extremes of distress and causing all kinds of disease — until at last the desire of the man and the longing of the woman bring the two together. And then, as if plucking fruit from a tree, they sow into the womb, as into plowed soil, living things too small to see and still unformed; these take shape inside, are nourished until they're matured, and are finally brought out into the light of day. So the generation of living creatures is accomplished.

That is how women, and the female sex in general, came to be. As for birds: they were remade — sprouting feathers instead of hair — from harmless but light-headed men, men interested in the heavens but simple-minded enough to think that sight provides the surest proof of things above. Wild land animals came from men who never used philosophy at all and never once wondered about the nature of the heavens, because they had stopped using the circuits in the head and followed instead the parts of the soul that live in the chest. Thanks to these habits, their forelimbs and heads were dragged down to the earth by natural kinship and came to rest there, and their skulls were stretched into all sorts of elongated shapes, wherever each man's circuits of soul had been crushed out of shape by idleness. This is why their kind was created four-footed and many-footed: God gave the more mindless ones more points of support, to bind them that much closer to the ground. And the most mindless of all, whose entire bodies trail along the earth, no longer had any need of feet, so the gods made them footless, to crawl on the ground. The fourth kind, the water animals, came from the stupidest and most ignorant men of all. The gods who were reshaping them judged these souls no longer even worthy of breathing pure air, because all their transgressions had fouled the soul within them. Instead of breathing the fine, clean air, they were pushed down to inhale the deep and murky water. From them came the tribe of fish and shellfish and every creature of the water, which have been assigned the most remote dwellings of all as punishment for the most extreme ignorance. These, then, are the laws by which all animals, then and now, pass into one another — transformed as they lose or gain intelligence and folly.

And now we may say that our account of the universe has reached its end. For this world has received mortal and immortal living creatures and been filled full of them, and has itself become a visible living creature embracing all things visible — an image of the intelligible, a perceptible god, greatest and best, most beautiful and most perfect: this one heaven, the only one of its kind that is, or ever will be.

Critias

Persons of the dialogue:
  Critias
  Hermocrates
  Timaeus
  Socrates

Timaeus I'm so grateful, Socrates, that I've finally reached the end -- like a weary traveler arriving after a long journey, ready to rest at last! And I pray to the being who has always existed, and whom I've just now described, that my words may endure insofar as they've been spoken truly and in a way that pleases him. But if I've unintentionally said anything wrong, I pray he'll impose on me a just punishment -- and the just punishment for someone who's gone astray is to be set right. So, wishing to speak truly in the future about the origin of the gods, I pray he'll give me knowledge, which is the most perfect and powerful of all remedies. And now, having offered my prayer, I hand the discussion over to Critias, who's supposed to speak next, according to our agreement.

Critias And I accept the task, Timaeus. Just as you began by asking for some patience, since you were going to speak about lofty matters, I'm asking for the same -- or even more patience -- for what I'm about to say. And although I know perfectly well that my request might seem a bit presumptuous and rude, I have to make it anyway. Now, would any sensible person deny that you've spoken well? The only thing I can try to show is that I deserve even more indulgence than you, because my subject is harder. And here's my argument: speaking well about the gods to a human audience is far easier than speaking well about humans to humans. When listeners have no experience and are completely ignorant about a subject, that ignorance is actually a huge advantage for the speaker -- and we all know how ignorant we are about the gods. But let me make my point clearer, if you'll follow me.

Everything any of us says is really just imitation and representation. Think about it this way: when painters create images of divine and heavenly bodies, notice the different degrees of satisfaction we feel looking at their work. We're perfectly happy with an artist who can even roughly imitate the earth, its mountains, rivers, woods, the whole universe, and everything moving within it. Since we don't know anything precise about these things, we don't scrutinize or analyze the painting -- all that's needed is some vague, impressionistic rendering and we're satisfied. But when someone tries to paint a human body? We're instantly sharp-eyed about every flaw, and our everyday familiarity makes us harsh judges of anyone who doesn't capture every detail.

The same thing happens with words. We're easily satisfied with a picture of divine and heavenly things that barely resembles them, but we're far more demanding when it comes to mortal, human subjects. So if I can't quite express my meaning perfectly when the moment comes, you'll have to forgive me -- creating convincing portraits of human things is the opposite of easy. That's what I wanted to suggest to you, and at the same time to ask, Socrates, that I be granted not less but more indulgence for what I'm about to say. If this is a fair request, I hope you'll be willing to grant it.

Socrates Of course, Critias -- we'll absolutely grant your request. And we'll extend the same courtesy in advance to Hermocrates as well, along with you and Timaeus. I have no doubt that when his turn comes a little later, he'll make the same request you just did. So, to give him a fresh starting point and spare him from repeating what's already been said, let him understand that the indulgence is already granted in advance. But now, my friend Critias, let me announce the judgment of the audience: they think the last performer was a stunning success, and you're going to need a great deal of indulgence to measure up to him.

Hermocrates That warning you're giving him, Socrates, applies to me too. But remember, Critias -- faint heart never raised a trophy. So you need to charge into this argument like a man. Call on Apollo and the Muses first, and then let's hear you celebrate and display the virtues of your ancient citizens.

Critias Easy for you to say, Hermocrates -- you're stationed last, with someone else going before you. You haven't lost heart yet. The gravity of the situation will reveal itself to you soon enough. Meanwhile, I'll take your encouragement to heart. But besides the gods and goddesses you've mentioned, I'd especially like to invoke Mnemosyne, the goddess of Memory. The entire crucial part of my story depends on her favor. If I can remember and retell enough of what the priests said and Solon brought back, I'm confident I'll satisfy this audience. And now, with no more excuses, I'll begin.

First, let me start by noting that nine thousand years had passed since the war that was said to have taken place between those who lived beyond the Pillars of Heracles and all who lived within them. This is the war I'm going to describe. On one side, the city of Athens was reported to have been the leader and to have fought the war through to the end. On the other side, the commanders were the kings of Atlantis -- which, as I mentioned before, was an island larger than Libya and Asia combined. It was later swallowed by earthquakes and became an impassable barrier of mud, blocking sailors from crossing the ocean beyond.

As the story unfolds, it will reveal the various barbarian nations and Greek families that existed at that time, as they appear one by one. But first I need to describe the Athenians of that era and the enemies they fought, along with the powers and governments of both sides. Let's start with Athens.

In the ancient days, the gods divided the whole earth among themselves by allotment. There was no quarreling -- because you can't reasonably suppose that the gods didn't know what was right for each of them, or that, knowing this, they'd try to grab through conflict what rightfully belonged to someone else. They all received their fair share and were satisfied, and they settled their territories. Once they'd populated them, they looked after us -- their children, their possessions -- the way shepherds tend their flocks. Except they didn't use force or physical violence the way shepherds do. Instead, they governed us like pilots steering from the stern of a ship, which is a gentle way of guiding living creatures. They held our souls by the rudder of persuasion, steering us as they saw fit. That's how they guided all mortal beings.

Now, different gods received different regions and set them in order. Hephaestus and Athena, who were brother and sister -- born from the same father, sharing a common nature, and united in their love of both philosophy and craft -- these two received as their joint portion this land of ours. It was naturally suited for wisdom and excellence. They planted here a race of brave, native-born people and instilled in their minds the principles of government. Their names have been preserved, but their deeds have been lost, because the people who passed down the traditions were destroyed, and ages upon ages went by. Whenever there were survivors, as I've already mentioned, they were mountain dwellers who were illiterate. They'd heard the names of the old leaders but knew almost nothing about what they'd actually done. They were happy enough to give these names to their children, but as for the virtues and laws of their ancestors, they knew only vague traditions. For many generations, they and their children struggled just to stay alive, so they focused on meeting their basic needs and talked about that -- not about events from the distant past. After all, mythology and the study of history don't appear in cities until people have leisure, until the necessities of life have been secured. Not before.

That's why the names of the ancients have come down to us but not their deeds. I draw this conclusion because Solon said that the priests, in their account of that war, mentioned most of the names recorded from before the time of Theseus -- names like Cecrops, Erechtheus, Erichthonius, and Erysichthon, and the names of the women as well. Furthermore, since military service was common to both men and women in those days, the people of that era, following their custom, set up a statue of the goddess in full armor -- a testament to the fact that among all creatures who live together, male and female alike, both sexes can, if they choose, practice the same virtues without distinction.

Now, in those days the country was inhabited by various classes of citizens. There were craftsmen, and there were farmers, and there was also a warrior class, originally set apart by divine founders. The warriors lived by themselves and had everything they needed for their upbringing and education. None of them owned anything privately -- they considered all their possessions common property. They didn't expect to receive from the other citizens anything beyond their necessary food. In fact, they practiced all the pursuits we described yesterday as belonging to our imaginary guardians.

About the land itself, the Egyptian priests said something that's not only plausible but clearly true: the borders in those days were marked by the Isthmus, and toward the mainland they extended as far as the heights of Cithaeron and Parnes. The boundary line came down toward the sea with the district of Oropus on the right, and the river Asopus as the border on the left. The soil was the finest in the world, and in those days it could support an enormous army drawn from the surrounding population. Even the remnant of Attica that exists today can rival any region on earth for the variety and quality of its fruits and the suitability of its pastures for every kind of animal -- which proves my point. But back then, the land was as beautiful as it is now, and far more productive.

How can I back this up? What part of today's land can truly be called a remnant of what it once was? The whole country is just a long promontory jutting far out into the sea from the rest of the mainland, and the surrounding ocean is deep right up to the shore. Many catastrophic floods occurred during those nine thousand years -- that's how much time has passed since the period I'm describing -- and through all these upheavals, there was never any significant buildup of soil washing down from the mountains, as happens in other places. Instead, the earth kept sliding away on all sides and disappearing. The result is that compared to what it once was, all that's left are the bones of a wasted body, so to speak -- like a small island stripped bare. All the rich, soft topsoil has washed away, leaving nothing but the skeleton of the land.

But in its original state, the mountains were tall hills covered with deep soil. The plains we now call Phelleus were full of rich earth, and there was thick forest covering the mountains. You can still see traces of this -- some mountains that now produce nothing but food for bees once had trees large enough to roof the biggest buildings. There were also many other tall trees, cultivated by people, that produced abundant food for livestock. And the land captured the annual rainfall instead of losing it as water runs off bare earth into the sea today. Back then, it had a generous supply of water everywhere, absorbing it into thick clay soil, then releasing it from the high ground into the hollows, creating abundant springs and rivers in every direction. You can still see sacred shrines at the sites where springs once flowed -- which proves that what I'm saying is true.

That was the natural state of the country, and it was cultivated, as you'd expect, by true farmers -- people who made agriculture their calling, who loved excellence, who were noble by nature, and who had the best soil in the world, abundant water, and a beautifully temperate climate overhead.

Now, here's how the city was laid out in those days. First: the Acropolis wasn't the way it is now. A single night of extreme rainfall washed away the earth and exposed bare rock; at the same time there were earthquakes, and then came the extraordinary flood -- the third before the great catastrophe of Deucalion. But in the original era, the Acropolis hill extended all the way to the Eridanus and Ilissus rivers, taking in the Pnyx on one side and bounded by Lycabettus on the other. The whole thing was covered in deep soil and level on top, except for a spot or two.

On the outer slopes and lower reaches of the hill lived the craftsmen and those farmers who worked the nearby land. The warrior class lived by themselves around the temples of Athena and Hephaestus at the summit, and they'd enclosed the whole area with a single fence -- like the wall around one household's garden. On the north side they had shared dwellings and communal dining halls for winter, along with all the buildings they needed for their common life, plus temples. But there was no gold or silver decorating any of it -- they had no use for such things. They took a middle path between poverty and showiness, building modest homes where they and their children and grandchildren grew old, passing them on to the next generation, who were always the same kind of people. In summer, they used the southern side of the hill for their gardens, exercise grounds, and dining halls.

Where the Acropolis stands now, there was a spring that was later choked off by the earthquake, leaving only the small trickles that still exist nearby. But in those days, the spring provided abundant water for everyone -- pleasantly cool in summer and warm in winter.

That's how they lived, serving as guardians of their own citizens and as leaders of the Greeks, who followed them willingly. And they took care to maintain the same number of men and women throughout the ages -- about twenty thousand, the number needed for military purposes, the same as today.

Such were the ancient Athenians. This is how they justly governed their own land and the rest of Greece. They were famous throughout Europe and Asia for the beauty of their bodies and the many virtues of their souls. Of all the people alive in those days, they were the most admired.

And now, if I haven't forgotten what I heard as a child, I'll tell you about the character and origins of their adversaries. After all, friends shouldn't keep their stories to themselves -- they should share them.

But before I go any further, I should warn you: don't be surprised if you hear Greek names given to foreigners. Here's why. Solon, who intended to use this story for a poem, looked into the meaning of the names and discovered that the early Egyptians had translated them into their own language when they first wrote them down. Solon recovered the meaning of each name and translated them back into Greek. My great-grandfather Dropides had Solon's original manuscript, which is still in my possession, and I studied it carefully as a child. So if you hear names that sound Greek, that's the reason. Now, here's how the long story began:

I mentioned earlier, when talking about the gods' allotments, that they divided the whole earth into portions of varying size and established temples and sacrifices in their territories. Poseidon received the island of Atlantis as his lot. He fathered children with a mortal woman and settled them on a part of the island that I'll now describe.

Looking toward the sea, but in the center of the whole island, there was a plain -- said to be the most beautiful and fertile of all plains. Near this plain, also in the center of the island, about fifty stadia inland, there was a mountain, not very high on any side. On this mountain lived one of the original earthborn men of that country. His name was Evenor, and he had a wife named Leucippe. They had a single daughter called Cleito.

Cleito had just reached womanhood when her father and mother died. Poseidon fell in love with her and slept with her. And then he reshaped the very earth around her: he broke the ground and enclosed the hill where she lived on every side, carving out alternating rings of sea and land, one inside the other -- two rings of land and three of water. He turned them as if on a lathe, each ring perfectly equidistant from the center in every direction, so that no one could reach the island at the center. Ships and sailing hadn't been invented yet, so the place was completely inaccessible. But Poseidon, being a god, had no trouble arranging the central island to his liking. He brought up two springs from beneath the earth -- one warm, one cold -- and made every kind of food grow abundantly from the soil.

He also fathered and raised five pairs of twin sons. He divided the island of Atlantis into ten portions and gave to the firstborn of the eldest pair his mother's home and the surrounding territory -- the largest and best portion -- making him king over all the rest. The others he made princes, giving each of them rule over many people and a vast territory.

He named them all. The eldest, the first king, he called Atlas -- and after him the whole island and the ocean were called Atlantic. His twin brother, born after him, received the far end of the island facing the Pillars of Heracles, toward the region now called Cadiz. His name in Greek was Eumelus, but in the local language he was called Gadeirus. Of the second pair of twins, one was called Ampheres and the other Evaemon. The elder of the third pair was named Mneseus, and the younger Autochthon. Of the fourth pair, the elder was Elasippus and the younger Mestor. And of the fifth pair, the elder was Azaes and the younger Diaprepes.

All of these, and their descendants for many generations, were the inhabitants and rulers of numerous islands throughout the open sea. And, as I mentioned before, they also held dominion in our direction -- over the lands within the Pillars -- as far as Egypt and Tyrrhenia.

Now, Atlas had a large and distinguished family, and they kept the kingdom in the family, the eldest son always passing it to his eldest, for many generations. They accumulated a quantity of wealth that no kings or rulers had ever possessed before -- and probably never will again. They had everything they could possibly need, both in the city and in the countryside. Because of the vastness of their empire, many goods were imported from foreign lands, but the island itself supplied most of what they required for daily life.

First of all, they mined from the earth whatever could be found there -- both solid metals and those that could be smelted. And there was one metal that's now just a name but was then something real: orichalcum. They dug it up all over the island, and in those days it was more precious than anything except gold.

There was an abundance of timber for every kind of construction, and plenty of grazing for both domestic and wild animals. In fact, there were even elephants on the island -- large numbers of them. Because just as the land provided for every other sort of creature, whether it lived in lakes, marshes, rivers, mountains, or plains, it also provided for the largest and most voracious animal of all.

And whatever fragrant things the earth produces today -- roots, herbs, woods, the essences that drip from fruits and flowers -- all of these grew and thrived on that island. Cultivated fruits too: the dry, nourishing kind and everything else we use for food -- what we generally call grain and pulse. And the hard-shelled fruits that give us drinks, oils, and ointments. Chestnuts and similar treats that we eat for pleasure and amusement. The delightful dessert fruits we bring out after dinner when we're tired of eating, to console ourselves. All of these, that sacred island, bathed in sunlight, produced in beautiful, wonderful, and limitless abundance.

Blessed with all these riches from the earth, the people set about building their temples, palaces, harbors, and docks. And they organized the entire country in the following way:

First, they built bridges across the rings of water surrounding the ancient capital, creating a road to and from the royal palace. They'd built the palace right at the beginning, on the spot where Poseidon and their ancestors had lived, and each generation of kings kept adding to its splendor, every king outdoing the one before him to the best of his ability, until they'd made the building a marvel of size and beauty.

Starting from the sea, they cut a canal three hundred feet wide, a hundred feet deep, and fifty stadia long, driving it straight through to the outermost ring of water. This created a harbor with an entrance wide enough for the largest ships to sail through. They also cut openings through the rings of land at the bridges -- just wide enough for a single trireme to pass from one ring of water to the next -- and they roofed over these channels to create covered waterways underneath for the ships, since the banks of the land rings rose well above the water.

Now, the largest ring of water -- the one they'd cut the channel to from the sea -- was three stadia wide, and the ring of land next to it was equally wide. The next two rings -- one water, one land -- were each two stadia wide. And the ring of water immediately surrounding the central island was just one stadium wide. The central island itself, where the palace stood, was five stadia in diameter.

They enclosed all of this -- the island, the rings, and the bridges (which were a sixth of a stadium wide) -- with stone walls on every side, placing towers and gates on the bridges where the sea channels passed through. The stone they quarried from beneath the central island and from under the rings, on both the inner and outer sides. It came in three colors: white, black, and red. And as they quarried, they simultaneously hollowed out double docks with roofs carved from the native rock. Some of their buildings were simple -- all one color. But in others, they mixed the different stones together, varying the colors to delight the eye, creating a natural and effortless beauty.

The outer wall, which ran around the outermost ring, they covered entirely with a coating of bronze. The next wall they coated with tin. And the third wall, the one enclosing the citadel itself, blazed with the red light of orichalcum.

Inside the citadel, the palaces were built like this: At the very center stood a sacred temple dedicated to Cleito and Poseidon, surrounded by a golden enclosure. No one was allowed to enter -- it was the spot where the family of the ten princes had first come into the world. Each year, the people brought seasonal offerings of first fruits from all ten districts to this shrine.

Here too was Poseidon's own temple -- a stadium long, half a stadium wide, and proportionately tall, with a strange, exotic appearance. They covered the entire exterior with silver, except for the pinnacles, which were gold. Inside, the ceiling was ivory, elaborately inlaid everywhere with gold, silver, and orichalcum. The walls, columns, and floor were all sheathed in orichalcum.

Inside the temple they placed golden statues. There was the god himself, standing in a chariot drawn by six winged horses, so enormous that his head touched the ceiling. Around him were a hundred Nereids riding on dolphins -- that being the number people believed existed in those days. There were also many other statues inside, dedicated by private citizens. And around the temple on the outside stood golden statues of all the descendants of the ten kings and their wives, plus many other magnificent offerings from the kings and from private citizens, both from the city itself and from the foreign cities under their rule. There was an altar, too, that matched this magnificence in size and workmanship. And the palaces were equally worthy of the greatness of the kingdom and the glory of the temple.

They also had fountains -- one of cold water and another of hot -- flowing generously, and the water was remarkably pleasant and pure. They built structures around these springs and planted appropriate trees. They constructed pools -- some open to the sky, others covered over for use as warm baths in winter. There were separate baths for the kings, separate ones for private citizens, separate ones for women, and separate ones for horses and cattle, each decorated as befitted its purpose. The overflow water they channeled to the sacred grove of Poseidon, where trees of astonishing height and beauty grew thanks to the extraordinary soil. The rest they directed through aqueducts along the bridges to the outer rings. There they'd built numerous temples dedicated to various gods, along with gardens and exercise grounds -- some for men, others for horses -- on both of the two islands formed by the land rings. In the center of the larger island, they'd set aside a racecourse a stadium wide, which ran the entire length of the island for horse racing. There were also guardhouses stationed at intervals. The most trusted guards were assigned to the smaller ring, closer to the Acropolis. The most trusted of all were given houses inside the citadel itself, near the kings.

The docks were packed with triremes and naval supplies, everything ready for use.

So much for the layout of the royal palace.

Leaving the palace and crossing out through the three harbors, you came to a wall that started at the sea and ran all the way around, everywhere fifty stadia from the largest harbor, completely enclosing the whole area, with its two ends meeting at the mouth of the canal leading to the sea. The entire space was densely packed with houses. The canal and the largest harbor were crammed with ships and merchants arriving from every direction, and the sheer number of people created a constant roar of voices and clamor of every kind, day and night.

I've described the city and the area around the ancient palace more or less in Solon's own words. Now I need to try to convey the nature and layout of the rest of the country.

The whole land was said to be very high and sheer on the side facing the sea, but the area immediately around the city was a level plain, surrounded by mountains that descended toward the coast. It was smooth and even, oblong in shape -- stretching three thousand stadia in one direction and two thousand stadia across the center from the coast inland. This part of the island faced south and was sheltered from the north. The surrounding mountains were legendary for their number, size, and beauty -- far surpassing any that exist today. They contained many prosperous villages, as well as rivers, lakes, and meadows with enough food for every animal, wild or tame, and forests of every kind of wood, abundant enough for every purpose.

Now let me describe the plain, shaped as it was by nature and by the labor of many generations of kings over long ages. It was mostly rectangular and oblong, and where it deviated from a straight line, it followed a circular ditch. The depth, width, and length of this ditch were staggering -- almost unbelievable for a manmade work, especially on top of everything else they'd built. But I have to report what I was told. It was dug to a depth of a hundred feet, a stadium wide all the way around, and ten thousand stadia in total length, encircling the entire plain. It collected the streams that flowed down from the mountains, wound around the plain, converged at the city, and drained from there into the sea. From the inland side, straight canals a hundred feet wide were cut through the plain to the ditch, spaced a hundred stadia apart. Through these canals they floated timber down from the mountains to the city and shipped agricultural produce, cutting cross-channels between the canals for transportation to the city. They harvested crops twice a year -- relying on rainfall in winter and irrigation from the canals in summer.

As for the population: each plot on the plain was responsible for providing a military commander over its able-bodied men. Each plot was a square of ten stadia on a side, and there were sixty thousand plots in total. The mountain regions and the rest of the countryside also had a vast population, distributed among the plots and assigned leaders according to their districts and villages. Each leader was required to supply, for war: one-sixth of a war chariot (making ten thousand chariots total), two horses with riders, a pair of chariot horses without a seat plus a mounted warrior who could fight on foot with a small shield and a charioteer to stand behind the fighter and drive the horses. Also: two heavy-armed soldiers, two archers, two slingers, three stone-throwers, three javelin men (all light-armed), and four sailors -- to crew a total fleet of twelve hundred ships.

That was the military organization of the royal city. The other nine kingdoms had their own arrangements, but it would be tedious to go through all the differences.

As for offices and honors, here's how things were set up from the beginning. Each of the ten kings had absolute authority over the citizens in his own territory and in his own city, and in most cases over the laws as well, with the power to punish and execute as he saw fit. But the hierarchy among the kings and their relations with each other were governed by the commands of Poseidon, handed down by law. These were inscribed on a pillar of orichalcum, which stood in the center of the island at the temple of Poseidon. The kings gathered there every fifth year, and then every sixth year, alternating to give equal honor to both odd and even numbers. When assembled, they discussed their common interests, investigated whether any of them had broken the law, and passed judgment.

But before passing judgment, they'd pledge themselves to each other in this way: there were bulls that roamed freely in the temple of Poseidon. The ten kings, left alone in the temple, would pray to the god to let them capture a victim acceptable to him. Then they hunted the bulls -- with no weapons, only wooden staves and nooses. The bull they caught, they led to the pillar and cut its throat so the blood spattered over the sacred inscription.

Now, inscribed on the pillar, alongside the laws, was an oath calling down terrible curses on anyone who disobeyed. So after they'd slaughtered the bull in the traditional manner and burned its limbs, they'd fill a mixing bowl with wine and drop in a clot of blood for each of them. The rest of the bull they burned on the fire, after purifying the column all around. Then each king drew from the bowl with a golden cup, poured a libation onto the fire, and swore that he would judge according to the laws on the pillar, that he would punish anyone who'd already broken them, that in the future he would not willingly violate what was written there, and that he would neither give orders nor obey any ruler who commanded him to act contrary to the laws of their father Poseidon. Each king made this prayer for himself and his descendants, then drank from the cup and dedicated it in the temple of the god.

After they'd eaten dinner and taken care of their needs, when night fell and the sacrificial fire had died down to embers, they all put on magnificent dark blue robes. Then, sitting on the ground in the darkness, by the glow of the embers over which they'd sworn, with every other fire in the temple extinguished, they received and delivered judgment, in case anyone had charges to bring against another. At dawn, they wrote their verdicts on a golden tablet and dedicated it, along with their robes, as a memorial.

There were many specific laws governing the individual kings, inscribed around the temples, but the most important were these: they must never take up arms against one another, and they must all come to the defense of any city where someone tried to overthrow the royal house. Following their ancestors' example, they were to deliberate together about war and other matters, with supreme authority given to the descendants of Atlas. And the king could not impose the death penalty on any of his kinsmen without the consent of a majority of the ten.

Such was the vast power that the god established on the lost island of Atlantis. And eventually he turned this power against our land. Here's why, as tradition tells it:

For many generations, as long as the divine nature within them remained strong, they obeyed the laws and were loyal to the god whose blood they carried. They possessed spirits that were truly great in every way -- combining gentleness with wisdom through whatever life threw at them, and in their dealings with each other. They looked down on everything except virtue. They thought little of their wealth, regarding their mountains of gold and other possessions as a burden rather than a blessing. They weren't intoxicated by luxury, and wealth never robbed them of their self-control. They were clear-headed. They understood that all these material goods are increased by virtue and mutual friendship, but that obsessing over wealth and chasing it destroys both the wealth itself and virtue along with it.

Thanks to this kind of thinking, and because the divine element in their nature endured, all the qualities I've described kept growing and flourishing in them.

But when the divine portion began to fade -- diluted again and again by the mortal element, as it was mixed in too heavily -- and when human nature gained the upper hand, then they could no longer handle their good fortune. They behaved disgracefully. To anyone with eyes to see, they were visibly degraded, because they were losing the most precious of their gifts. But to those who couldn't see what true happiness was, they appeared more glorious and blessed than ever, right at the moment when they were most consumed by greed and unjust power.

Zeus, the god of gods, who rules according to law and has the ability to perceive such things, saw that this honorable race had fallen into a wretched state. Wanting to punish them so they might learn and improve, he gathered all the gods into their most sacred dwelling place -- the one at the center of the universe, which looks out over all of creation. And when he had assembled them, he spoke as follows --

Persons of the dialogue:

An Athenian stranger
  Cleinias, a Cretan
  Megillus, a Spartan

Scene: The road from Knossos to the cave and temple of Zeus, on a hot summer day in Crete.

Athenian: Tell me, friends — is it a god or a man who's credited with creating your laws?

Cleinias: A god, stranger. Absolutely a god. Here in Crete, we say it was Zeus himself. But in Sparta, where our friend Megillus comes from, I believe they'd say Apollo is their lawgiver. Isn't that right, Megillus?

Megillus: That's right.

Athenian: And do you believe the story Homer tells, Cleinias — that every nine years, Minos traveled to meet with his father Zeus on Olympus, and that Zeus inspired him to write the laws for your cities?

Cleinias: Yes, that's our tradition. And there was his brother Rhadamanthus too — you've probably heard of him. He's said to have been the most just man who ever lived, and we Cretans believe he earned that reputation through his righteous handling of justice during his lifetime.

Athenian: A noble reputation, and worthy of a son of Zeus. Now, since you and Megillus were both raised under these institutions, I'd imagine you won't mind giving an account of your government and laws. We can pass the time talking about them as we walk. I'm told it's a considerable distance from Knossos to the cave and temple of Zeus, and there must be shady spots along the way under the tall trees — places to escape this scorching sun. We're not young men anymore. We can stop and rest beneath them whenever we need to, and make it through the whole journey without too much trouble, letting conversation carry us along.

Cleinias: Yes, and as we go further we'll come to groves of cypresses — remarkably tall and beautiful ones — and green meadows where we can sit and talk.

Athenian: Excellent.

Cleinias: Indeed — and even better once we actually see them. Let's get moving.

Athenian: I'm happy to. And first, I want to know: why has the law required you to have communal meals and gymnastic training, and to carry weapons?

Cleinias: I think that's fairly easy to explain, stranger. Just look at our landscape. Crete isn't like Thessaly — it's not a big flat plain. That's why Thessaly has horsemen and we have foot-soldiers. Our rugged, uneven terrain is better suited to getting around on foot. And if you're fighting on foot, you need light equipment — no one can run carrying a heavy load — so bows and arrows make sense because they're light. All these arrangements were designed with war in mind, and I think the lawgiver had military readiness at the heart of everything he did. The communal meals, if I'm not mistaken, were set up for the same reason. He noticed that when men are in the field on campaign, the nature of the situation forces them to eat together for mutual protection. It seems to me he thought the world was foolish for not understanding that all people are always at war with one another. And if during wartime there ought to be communal meals and organized chains of command for an army's protection, those same arrangements should continue during peacetime too. Because what most people call "peace" is really just a name — in reality, every city is in a permanent natural state of war with every other city, even if no herald has proclaimed it. If you look closely, you'll see that this was exactly what our Cretan lawgiver intended. Every institution, public and private, was arranged with war in mind. He was working on the assumption that no possessions or institutions have any value for someone who's been defeated in battle — because all the good things of the conquered pass into the hands of the conquerors.

Athenian: You seem to have been thoroughly trained in the Cretan way of thinking, and very well informed about it. But could you spell out for me a bit more precisely the principle behind it? You seem to be saying that a well-governed state should be organized so as to conquer all other states in war. Am I reading you correctly?

Cleinias: Absolutely. And our Spartan friend here will agree with me, I'm sure.

Megillus: How could any Spartan say otherwise?

Athenian: And does this principle apply only to states, or to villages as well?

Cleinias: Both.

Athenian: The same idea?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: And within the village — the same kind of war between family and family, and individual against individual?

Cleinias: The same.

Athenian: And should each person think of himself as his own enemy too? What do we say about that?

Cleinias: Now there, Athenian stranger — I won't just call you a man of Attica, because you seem to deserve being named after the goddess Athena herself — you've gone right back to first principles. You've thrown real light on the argument, and you'll be in an even better position now to understand what I was getting at: that all people are publicly each other's enemies, and privately, each person is his own enemy.

Athenian: What do you mean by that, exactly?

Cleinias: There's a kind of victory and defeat — the very first and best of victories, the very lowest and worst of defeats — that each person wins or suffers not at the hands of another, but at his own hands. This shows that there's a war going on inside every one of us.

Athenian: Let's turn the argument around. If every individual is either his own master or his own slave, can we say the same principle operates in the household, the village, and the state?

Cleinias: You mean that in each of them there's a principle of being superior or inferior to oneself?

Athenian: Yes.

Cleinias: You're absolutely right to ask that. There certainly is such a principle, and it applies above all to states. A state where the better citizens win out over the mob and the inferior classes can truly be called "better than itself" and deserves praise. When the opposite happens, it deserves blame.

Athenian: Whether the better element is ever really conquered by the worse is a question that needs more discussion, so let's set it aside for now. But I do understand your point. You're saying that citizens of the same blood who live in the same city can form an unjust conspiracy, and if they have superior numbers, they can overpower and enslave the just minority. When that happens, the state is truly "inferior to itself" — and therefore bad. When they're defeated, the state is "superior to itself" — and therefore good.

Cleinias: That's a strange-sounding claim, stranger. But we can't possibly deny it.

Athenian: Here's another case to think about. In a family, there might be several brothers born to the same parents. It's perfectly possible that the majority of them are unjust, with the just ones in the minority.

Cleinias: Perfectly possible.

Athenian: And you and I shouldn't get sidetracked arguing about whether it's technically proper to call this family "superior" when the just prevail and "inferior" when they don't. We're not quibbling over conventional language right now. We're investigating the natural principles of right and wrong as they apply to law.

Cleinias: What you say is absolutely true, stranger.

Megillus: Excellent — I agree entirely, as far as we've gone.

Athenian: Now then. Suppose there were a judge presiding over these brothers we've been discussing.

Cleinias: All right.

Athenian: Which would be the better judge — one who destroyed the bad brothers and set the good ones up to govern themselves? Or one who let the good govern but allowed the bad to live, and made them voluntarily submit? Or perhaps third in the ranking of excellence would be a judge who, finding the family in conflict, didn't destroy anyone at all but instead reconciled them with each other permanently, gave them laws they all agreed to follow, and managed to keep them friends.

Cleinias: That last judge would be far and away the best — the best sort of legislator too.

Athenian: And yet the aim of all the laws this judge would give would be the opposite of war.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: Now consider: would the person who organizes a state and arranges the lives of its people have his eye on foreign war, or on that internal kind of war we call civil strife — the kind that no one would want to see in their own state, and that everyone, once it breaks out, wants to end as quickly as possible?

Cleinias: He'd be focused mainly on preventing civil strife.

Athenian: And would he prefer that civil conflict be ended by the destruction of one side and the victory of the other, or that peace and friendship be restored — so that, once reconciled, the citizens could turn their attention to foreign enemies?

Cleinias: Everyone would prefer the latter, at least in their own state.

Athenian: And wouldn't the lawgiver want the same thing?

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And wouldn't every lawgiver always make laws with an eye to the best outcome?

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: But war — whether foreign or civil — isn't the best outcome. The need for either kind is something to be regretted. Peace and mutual goodwill, those are what's best. And even a state's "victory over itself" shouldn't really be considered a good thing — it's a necessity. It's like saying the body is in its best condition when it's sick and being purged by medicine, while forgetting that there's also a condition of the body that doesn't need any purging at all. In the same way, no one can be a true statesman — whether they're looking after the happiness of an individual or a state — if they focus only, or even primarily, on foreign warfare. No one can be a sound lawgiver who organizes peace for the sake of war, rather than war for the sake of peace.

Cleinias: There seems to be truth in what you're saying, stranger. And yet I'd be very much mistaken if the entire aim and purpose of our Cretan institutions — and the Spartan ones too — isn't war.

Athenian: Probably so. But there's no reason we should rudely quarrel with each other about your lawgivers. Let's question them gently instead, since we're all equally serious about getting to the truth. Follow me closely here. First, let me call up Tyrtaeus as a witness — he was Athenian by birth but also a Spartan citizen, and no man was ever more passionate about war. He writes:

"I sing not, I care not, about any man,"

even if he were the richest man alive and possessed every good thing — and then Tyrtaeus gives a whole list of them — unless he's a brave warrior at all times. I imagine you've heard his poems too. Our Spartan friend here has probably heard more than enough of them.

Megillus: Very true.

Cleinias: And they've made their way from Sparta to Crete as well.

Athenian: All right, let's all put a question to Tyrtaeus together. We'd say to him: "Most inspired poet, the magnificent praise you've given to those who excel in war is proof enough that you're wise and good, and I believe Megillus, Cleinias of Knossos, and I all agree with you completely. But we want to make sure we're talking about the same kind of men. Tell us — do you agree that there are two kinds of war?" Now, even a man far less gifted than Tyrtaeus could answer without any difficulty that yes, there are two kinds. One is what everyone calls civil war — and as we were just saying, it's the worst of all wars. The other, war against foreign nations of different peoples, is a far milder form of conflict.

Cleinias: Much milder, certainly.

Athenian: Well then — when you sing your high praise of war and blame those who fall short, Tyrtaeus, which kind of war are you talking about? Which warriors are you praising? Foreign war, I'd assume, judging from lines like these:

"Who refuse to look upon fields of blood, and will not draw near and strike at their enemies."

So we'd naturally conclude: "You, Tyrtaeus, seem to be praising those who distinguish themselves in foreign and external war." And he'd have to agree.

Cleinias: Obviously.

Athenian: They are good men, those warriors. But we say there are even better men — men whose excellence shows itself in the greatest of all battles. And we have a poet of our own to call as witness: Theognis, a citizen of Megara in Sicily. He says:

"Cyrnus, he who is faithful in a civil conflict is worth his weight in gold and silver."

And we claim that such a man is far better than the other in a much more difficult kind of war — better in roughly the same way that justice, temperance, and wisdom, when combined with courage, are better than courage alone. Because a man can't be faithful and good during civil strife unless he possesses all the virtues working together. But in the external wars Tyrtaeus writes about, plenty of mercenary soldiers will plant themselves on the battlefield ready to die — and yet they're generally insolent, unjust, violent, and among the most senseless people alive.

So where does this lead? What am I trying to show? I'm saying this: the divine lawgiver of Crete — or any lawgiver worth the name — will always and above all else, when making laws, keep his eye on the greatest virtue. And according to Theognis, that's loyalty in the hour of danger, which we might call complete justice. The virtue Tyrtaeus celebrates is good enough, and the poet praised it at the right time. But in rank and importance, it's only fourth-rate.

Cleinias: Stranger, you're demoting our inspired lawgiver to a rank far beneath him!

Athenian: No — I think we'd be demoting ourselves if we imagined that Lycurgus and Minos designed the laws of Sparta and Crete primarily with war in mind.

Cleinias: Then what should we say?

Athenian: What truth and justice demand, I think, when we're speaking on behalf of truly divine excellence: that the lawgiver, when making his laws, had in view not just one part of virtue — and the lowest part at that — but all of virtue. He devised categories of law corresponding to the different kinds of virtue, and not in the way modern lawmakers do. Today's legislators investigate one narrow problem at a time and draft laws as the need arises — one set of laws about property distribution, another about inheritance, another about assault, and on and on for ten thousand other matters. But we maintain that the right way to examine laws is the way we've been doing it just now.

And I admired your approach, actually. You were absolutely right to begin with virtue and say that virtue was the aim of the lawgiver. Where I thought you went wrong was in claiming that all his legislation aimed at only a part of virtue — the least important part — and that's what prompted my subsequent remarks. So will you let me explain how I wish you'd presented the case?

Cleinias: By all means.

Athenian: You should have said something like this: "Stranger, the Cretan laws are rightly famous throughout Greece, because they accomplish what laws are supposed to accomplish — they make the people who live under them happy. They provide every kind of good thing. Now, goods come in two categories: human goods and divine goods. The human goods depend on the divine ones. A state that achieves the greater goods automatically gets the lesser ones too. A state that lacks the greater goods gets neither.

"Among the lesser, human goods, the first is health. The second is beauty. The third is physical strength — including speed and overall fitness. The fourth is wealth — but not the blind kind. Wealth that can see, wealth paired with wisdom.

"Among the divine goods, the first and foremost is wisdom. That's the leader. The second is temperance — self-control. And from the union of these two with courage springs justice. Courage itself comes fourth.

"All these divine goods naturally take precedence over the human goods, and this is the order the lawgiver must follow. After establishing these priorities, he'll design the rest of his laws for the citizens with an eye toward them — the human goods looking up to the divine ones, and the divine goods looking up to their leader, which is mind and wisdom.

"Some of his laws will deal with marriage contracts. Then with the bearing and raising of children, both boys and girls. The lawgiver's job is to take charge of his citizens through every stage of life — youth, maturity, old age — handing out punishments and rewards as needed. In all their dealings with one another, he should study their pains and pleasures and desires, and the intensity of all their passions. He should watch over them and use the voice of the law itself to praise and blame them rightly. He should address anger, fear, and the other disturbances of the soul that come with misfortune, as well as the recoveries that come with prosperity, and the experiences people go through in sickness, war, poverty, and their opposites. For every one of these situations, he should define and teach what's good and what's harmful.

"Next, the lawgiver must pay attention to how citizens make and spend their money, and oversee their contracts and dissolutions of contracts, whether voluntary or involuntary. He should examine how justice and injustice operate in all their various dealings with one another. He should honor those who obey the law and impose fixed penalties on those who don't — right up until the cycle of civic life is complete and it's time to consider proper funeral rites and honors for the dead.

"Finally, the lawgiver should review his entire body of work and appoint guardians to oversee all these matters — some guided by real understanding, others by sound judgment. Then wisdom will bind all his laws together and show them to be in harmony with temperance and justice, rather than with wealth or ambition."

That's the spirit in which I wanted you to explain your laws, Cleinias. And what I want to know is: how is all of this arranged in the laws they attribute to Zeus and to Pythian Apollo — the laws that Minos and Lycurgus handed down? How does the underlying order become visible to someone who has experience with legal systems, whether through study or long practice? Because to the rest of us, this order is far from obvious.

Cleinias: How should we proceed, stranger?

Athenian: I think we need to start over, the way we did before, and first examine the practice of courage. Then we'll move on to discuss another form of virtue, and then another, if you're willing. By taking courage as our model, we'll work through the whole set. And using discussions like these, we'll make the journey pass pleasantly. Once we've covered all the virtues, we'll show — God willing — that the institutions I've been describing are all aimed at virtue.

Megillus: Good plan. And why don't you start by critiquing this admirer of Zeus and Cretan law right here?

Athenian: I'll try to critique both of you and myself too, since the argument belongs to all of us. Tell me — weren't the communal meals first, and the gymnastic training second, both established by your lawgiver with an eye toward war?

Megillus: Yes.

Athenian: And what comes third, and what fourth? I think that's the right way to enumerate the remaining aspects of virtue — never mind exactly what we call them, as long as the meaning is clear.

Megillus: Well, any Spartan would say that hunting comes third.

Athenian: Let's see if we can figure out what comes fourth and fifth.

Megillus: I think I can get to fourth. It would be the routine endurance of pain — something we Spartans display in our hand-to-hand combat exercises, and also in our practice of stealing food while facing the prospect of a thorough beating if caught. Then there's the Crypteia — the secret service — which requires extraordinary endurance. Our young men roam the entire countryside day and night, even in winter, without shoes on their feet or beds to sleep in, serving as their own attendants with no help at all. And then there's the incredible endurance shown in our naked exercises, pushing through the brutal summer heat. There are many more examples like these — so many it would take forever to list them all.

Athenian: That's all well and good, my Spartan friend. But how should we define courage? Is it only about fighting against fears and pain? Or does it also involve fighting against desires and pleasures — and against those seductive flatteries that have the power to make even respectable people melt like wax?

Megillus: I'd say it includes all of those.

Athenian: Now, earlier in our conversation — you'll remember — our friend Cleinias from Knossos was talking about a person or a city being "inferior to themselves." Were you not, Cleinias?

Cleinias: I was.

Athenian: So which person is more truly "inferior" — the one who's overcome by pleasure, or the one who's overcome by pain?

Cleinias: I'd say the one overcome by pleasure. Everyone considers him inferior in a more shameful way than the person overcome by pain.

Athenian: But surely the lawgivers of Crete and Sparta didn't design courage to limp along on one leg — strong enough to fight attacks from one direction but helpless against the seductive assaults from the other?

Cleinias: Strong enough for both, I'd say.

Athenian: Then tell me: what institutions do either of your states have that give people a taste of pleasures — that don't avoid them, the way you avoid pain — but instead put a person right in the middle of them, and use incentives to compel or encourage him to master them? Where is the law dealing with pleasure that matches your laws dealing with pain? Tell me: what do you have that makes your citizens equally tough against both pleasure and pain, conquering what they should conquer, unbeaten by the enemies that are most dangerous and closest to home?

Megillus: I was able to point to many laws directed against pain, stranger. But when it comes to similar institutions dealing with pleasure, I can't think of any great or obvious examples. There might be some minor provisions I could mention.

Cleinias: And I can't point to anything like that in Cretan law either — nothing comparably prominent.

Athenian: That's not surprising, my dear friends. And if, in our search for what's truly good, one of us has to criticize the laws of the others — well, let's not take offense. Let's take it kindly.

Cleinias: You're quite right, Athenian stranger. We'll do as you say.

Athenian: At our age, Cleinias, there should be no room for hurt feelings.

Cleinias: Certainly not.

Athenian: I won't say right now whether criticisms of the Cretan or Spartan systems are justified or not. But I think I can describe what most people say about them better than either of you can. Assuming your laws are reasonably good, one of the best among them is the rule forbidding any young person from questioning whether the laws are right or wrong. Young people must speak with one voice and agree that all the laws are good, since they came from a god. Anyone who says otherwise shouldn't be listened to. But an old man who notices a flaw in the laws is free to share his observation with a ruler or with someone his own age, as long as no young people are around to hear it.

Cleinias: That's exactly right, stranger. Like a prophet who wasn't there at the time, you seem to have understood the lawgiver's intention perfectly. What you say is absolutely true.

Athenian: Well, since no young people are present, and since the lawgiver has given old men free license to speak, there's nothing improper about us discussing these matters now, just among ourselves.

Cleinias: True. So feel as free as you like in criticizing our laws. There's no shame in recognizing what's wrong. Anyone who receives criticism in a generous and friendly spirit will only be better for it.

Athenian: Good. However, I'm not going to attack your laws — not until I've examined them as carefully as I can. I'm just going to raise some questions. Because here's what's striking: you are the only people we know of, Greek or foreign, whose lawgiver commanded citizens to avoid all intense pleasures and amusements — to never touch them. Yet when it comes to pain and fear, as we've been discussing, your lawgiver understood perfectly well that people who've spent their entire lives from childhood avoiding pain, fear, and hardship will run away when they're eventually forced to face those things, and will become the slaves of those who are hardened to them.

Now, it seems to me the lawgiver should have applied the same reasoning to pleasure. He should have said to himself: "If my citizens grow up from youth entirely unfamiliar with the most intense pleasures, and never learn to endure temptation, and aren't trained to resist everything that's base, then the sweet pull of pleasure will overcome them just as surely as fear would overcome the first group. In a different way — actually, an even worse way — they'll become the slaves of people who can handle pleasure, people who've had plenty of experience enjoying it, even when those people are the worst sort of men. Half their soul will be enslaved, the other half free. And they won't deserve to be called truly free men in the fullest sense."

Tell me — do you agree with what I'm saying?

Cleinias: At first hearing, it does seem to ring true. But to rush to a conclusion about such important matters would be pretty childish and naive.

Athenian: Fair enough. Let's move on to the next virtue we planned to discuss. After courage comes temperance — self-control. What institutions will we find in Crete or Sparta that promote temperance, the way your military institutions differ from those of other states?

Megillus: That's not an easy question to answer. But I'd say the communal meals and gymnastic exercises have been admirably designed to promote both temperance and courage.

Athenian: Here's the difficulty with state institutions, though: it's hard to make theory and practice line up perfectly with no room for debate. It's like the human body — a treatment that does good in one way can do harm in another, and you can hardly say that any single regimen suits every constitution. Take the gymnasia and communal meals: they do a great deal of good, but they've also been a source of trouble during civil unrest. We can see this from what happened among the youth in Miletus, Boeotia, and Thurii, where these institutions seem to have had a tendency to corrupt the ancient and natural customs of love — degrading them below the level not only of human beings but even of animals.

This charge can fairly be leveled at your cities above all others, and it's true of most states that place heavy emphasis on athletic training. Whether we should treat these matters lightly or seriously, I think we should recognize that the pleasure arising from the natural union of men and women is natural, while the union of men with men, or women with women, is contrary to nature — a product of unbridled desire. The Cretans are often accused of having invented the story of Ganymede and Zeus specifically to justify their own practices, claiming divine precedent from the very god they believe gave them their laws.

Setting that story aside, we can observe that any serious thinking about legislation comes down almost entirely to pleasure and pain — both in states and in individuals. These are two springs that nature lets flow, and the person who draws from them at the right time, in the right place, and in the right amount is happy. This applies to humans and animals alike, to individuals and states. But whoever indulges ignorantly or at the wrong time will be the opposite of happy.

Megillus: I admit, stranger, that your words are well spoken, and I hardly know how to answer you. But still, I think the Spartan lawgiver was absolutely right to forbid excessive pleasure. As for the Cretan laws, I'll leave their defense to my friend from Knossos. But the Spartan laws regarding pleasure seem to me the best in the world. Our system has completely driven out the thing that leads most of humanity into wild pleasure, bad behavior, and every kind of foolishness. Neither in the countryside nor in the towns under Spartan control will you find revelries and all the provocations to pleasure that come with them. Anyone who encounters a drunken, disorderly person will have him punished severely on the spot — no excuses accepted, not even during a festival of Dionysus. Although I've noticed this kind of thing does happen at your performances "on the cart," as you call them. And among our Tarentine colonists, I've seen an entire city drunk at a Dionysian festival. But nothing like that happens in Sparta.

Athenian: My Spartan friend, these festivals are praiseworthy when there's a spirit of discipline behind them, but they're completely foolish when they're unregulated. An Athenian could easily fire back by pointing out the license that exists among your women. But for all accusations like these — whether they're aimed at the Tarentines, at us Athenians, or at you Spartans — there's one standard reply that settles the question of propriety. When a stranger expresses shock at some local practice, any citizen will naturally say: "Don't be surprised, stranger. This is our custom. You probably do things differently where you're from."

But that's not what we're discussing, friends. We're not talking about people in general — we're examining the merits and defects of the lawgivers themselves. So let's talk at more length about intoxication. It's actually a very important subject, and it takes real discrimination from a legislator to handle it well. I'm not talking about whether people should drink wine at all or not drink wine at all. I'm talking about intoxication itself.

Should we follow the custom of the Scythians and Persians and Carthaginians and Celts and Iberians and Thracians — all warlike peoples — or that of your countrymen, who, as you say, abstain entirely? The Scythians and Thracians, both men and women, drink their wine unmixed and pour it on their clothes, and they consider this a fine and glorious tradition. The Persians are also very fond of various luxuries that you reject, though they indulge with somewhat more restraint than the Thracians and Scythians.

Megillus: My good man, all we have to do is pick up our weapons and we send every one of those nations running.

Athenian: Don't say that, my friend. There have been — and always will be — victories and retreats that can't be explained by such simple logic. We can't use military victory or defeat as reliable proof that a society's institutions are good or bad. Larger states conquer smaller ones all the time — Syracuse has conquered Locri, which appears to have the best government in that part of the world; Athens conquered the Ceans — and there are ten thousand other examples of the same thing. None of that proves anything.
