Athenian: And yet from that state of affairs, everything we now have has developed: cities and governments, arts and laws, and a great deal of both vice and virtue?

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: Well, my friend, how could we possibly expect that people who knew nothing about the good and evil of city life could have reached the heights of virtue or the depths of vice?

Cleinias: I see your point. You're quite right.

Athenian: But as time went on and the population grew, the world gradually became what we see today.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: And the change didn't happen overnight. It took place little by little, over an enormously long period.

Cleinias: That's very likely.

Athenian: At first, the terror of the flood would still be ringing in their ears, and they'd have been too afraid to come down from the highlands into the plains.

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: And the tiny number of survivors would've been desperate to see one another. But the means of traveling — by land or sea — had been almost entirely lost along with all the other arts. Getting from one place to another would have been incredibly difficult. Iron and bronze and all the metals had been jumbled together and buried in the destruction. There was no way to extract ore. And they had almost no way to fell timber either. Even if some tools had survived up in the mountains, they would've quickly worn out and disappeared, and there'd have been no way to replace them until the art of metalworking was rediscovered.

Cleinias: No, there couldn't have been.

Athenian: And how many generations would that have taken?

Cleinias: Clearly a great many.

Athenian: During that whole period, and for some time after, every craft that depends on iron and bronze and similar metals would have vanished.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And war and internal conflict would have died out too, for several reasons.

Cleinias: How so?

Athenian: First, the isolation and desolation these early people experienced would have created strong bonds of affection and goodwill among them. Second, they wouldn't have had any reason to quarrel over food. Pastureland would have been abundant — with so few people left, there'd have been plenty for everyone — except perhaps right at the beginning, in some isolated cases. From their pastures they'd have gotten most of their food: plenty of milk and meat. They'd also have supplemented this with hunting, and there'd have been no shortage of game. They'd have had plenty of clothing, bedding, shelters, and household goods too — both fireproof and regular — because the arts of pottery and weaving don't require iron. God gave humans those two crafts specifically so that even when reduced to the direst circumstances, the human race could still survive and grow.

So people in those days weren't desperately poor. And poverty didn't cause divisions among them. But they couldn't have been rich either, since they had neither gold nor silver. That was just their situation. And a community that has neither poverty nor wealth will always produce the noblest character. Where there's no great wealth, there's no arrogance or injustice. And where there's no poverty, there's no resentment or envy. So they were good people. They were also what you'd call simple-minded — and I mean that as a compliment. When they were told something was good or bad, they believed it in their simplicity and lived accordingly. Nobody was clever enough to suspect anyone else of lying, the way people do now. What they heard about gods and men, they took to be the truth and shaped their lives around it. In every way, they were exactly as we've described.

Cleinias: That fits perfectly with my own views, and Megillus' too.

Athenian: Now, wouldn't these people — living simply for generation after generation, rougher perhaps, more ignorant of the arts generally, and certainly of land and naval warfare, and all those clever tricks that cities breed: legal maneuvering, political factions, every imaginable way of hurting each other in word and deed — wouldn't they, despite being less sophisticated than the people who lived before the flood or than people of our own day, have been simpler, braver, more self-disciplined, and on the whole more just? We've already explained why.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: I want you to understand that everything I've been saying, and everything that follows, is meant to explain one thing: what did the people of that era need in the way of laws, and who were their lawmakers?

Cleinias: And so far, you've been making your case very well.

Athenian: They wouldn't have needed formal lawmakers yet. Nothing of the sort was likely to exist in their time, since they didn't even have writing. They lived by habit and by what are called ancestral customs.

Cleinias: Probably so.

Athenian: But there was already a form of government in existence — one that, if I'm not mistaken, is generally called patriarchy. It still survives in many places, among both Greeks and non-Greeks. Homer tells us the Cyclopes lived this way:

"They have no councils and no formal laws, but they live in hollow caves on the tops of high mountains, and each one lays down the law for his own wife and children, and they don't concern themselves with one another."

Cleinias: That seems like a charming poet of yours. I've read some other verses of his that are quite clever, though I don't know him well. Foreign poets aren't widely read in Crete.

Megillus: But they certainly are in Sparta! Homer seems to be the prince of all poets. Although the way of life he describes isn't Spartan — it's more Ionian, I'd say. But he does seem to confirm exactly what you're arguing, tracing the ancient condition of humanity back to a kind of primitive life through traditional stories.

Athenian: Yes, he does confirm it. And we can accept his testimony that these kinds of governments do arise.

Cleinias: Agreed.

Athenian: And weren't these communities made up of people who had been scattered into isolated families by the devastation? In these groups, the eldest would naturally have ruled — since government originated in the authority of a father and mother. Like a flock of birds, the rest would have followed, forming a single group under patriarchal rule and the sovereignty of their parents — which is, of all forms of authority, the most natural and just.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: After this, they started coming together in larger numbers, expanding the size of their communities. They turned to farming, first at the foot of the mountains. They built rough stone walls and other defenses to keep out wild animals, creating a single larger shared settlement.

Cleinias: Yes, that's how it would likely have happened.

Athenian: And here's something else that probably occurred.

Cleinias: What?

Athenian: When these larger settlements grew out of the smaller original family groups, each family would have survived within the larger community. Every family would still be governed by its eldest member. And because of their long separation from one another, each family would have developed its own customs — its own ways of doing things, both religious and secular — inherited from the parents who'd raised them. Orderly parents would have passed along orderliness; courageous parents, courage. They would naturally have stamped their own values on their children, and their children's children. So by the time these families came together into the larger society, each one already had its own distinct customs and traditions.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And naturally, every group prefers its own customs and isn't as fond of other people's.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: So it seems we've stumbled onto the beginnings of legislation.

Cleinias: Exactly.

Athenian: The next step would be that the people who'd come together would choose some arbiters — people who'd review the laws and customs of all the different families and present the ones they approved to the tribal leaders, who were essentially their kings. These arbiters would let the leaders choose whichever laws they thought best. The arbiters themselves would end up being called "legislators," and they'd appoint magistrates. Out of the old patriarchies, they'd construct some sort of aristocracy — or perhaps a monarchy. And in this transformed system of government, they'd carry on living.

Cleinias: Yes, that would be the natural progression.

Athenian: Now let's talk about a third form of government — one in which all sorts of political arrangements and constitutional forms come together.

Cleinias: What form is that?

Athenian: The one Homer describes as following the second stage. This third form emerged when, as he tells it, Dardanus founded Dardania:

"For holy Ilium had not yet been built on the plain to be a city of speaking men; they were still dwelling at the foot of many-fountained Ida."

In these verses, and in what he said about the Cyclopes, Homer speaks with the voice of divine truth. Poets are a divinely inspired race, and often — with the help of the Muses and the Graces — they hit on the truth.

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: Let's continue with our story, then. It'll probably help illustrate the point we're working toward. Shall we?

Cleinias: By all means.

Athenian: Ilium was built after people descended from the mountains. They settled on a broad, beautiful plain, on a low hill, watered by many rivers flowing down from Mount Ida.

Cleinias: That's how the story goes.

Athenian: And we'd have to assume this happened a very long time after the flood?

Cleinias: Yes, there must have been a very long interval.

Athenian: It's remarkable how completely they'd forgotten the earlier destruction. They placed their city right beneath numerous streams flowing from the heights, trusting for protection to hills that weren't even very high. That takes an extraordinary loss of collective memory.

Cleinias: Clearly a very long time had passed.

Athenian: And as the population grew, many other cities would have been founded.

Cleinias: No doubt.

Athenian: These cities eventually made war against Troy — by sea as well as land, because by that time people were losing their fear of the ocean.

Cleinias: Clearly.

Athenian: The Greeks — the Achaeans — besieged Troy for ten years and finally destroyed it.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: But during those ten years while the Achaeans were camped outside Troy, things back home were falling apart. The young people left behind revolted. And when the soldiers finally came home to their own cities and families, they weren't received properly or fairly. The result was widespread death, murder, and exile. The exiles eventually regrouped under a new name — no longer Achaeans, but Dorians, named after Dorieus, who gathered them together. The rest of the story is told by you Spartans as part of your own history.

Megillus: That's right.

Athenian: So after our long digression from the subject of laws into music and drinking parties, the argument has — almost providentially — circled back to where we started. We've now reached the founding of Sparta, which you rightly say is a sister state to Crete in its laws and institutions. And we're better off for the digression, because we've traced the development of government from a first state through a second and third, each succeeding the other over vast stretches of time. And now a fourth state appears on the horizon — one that was once in the process of being founded and has continued to this day. If we can learn from all this what makes a state well or badly governed, which laws save cities and which destroy them, and what changes produce happiness — then, Megillus and Cleinias, we can start fresh. Unless you have some objection to what we've said so far?

Megillus: If some god promised us that our next investigation into legislation would be as rich and full as this one, I'd walk a very long way to hear it. I'd say a day as long as this one — and we're approaching the longest day of the year — is too short for the discussion.

Athenian: Then I suppose we'd better keep going?

Megillus: Definitely.

Athenian: Let's put ourselves mentally at the moment when Sparta, Argos, and Messene — and the rest of the Peloponnese — were all completely under the control of your ancestors, Megillus. According to the legend, they then divided their army into three parts and settled three cities: Argos, Messene, and Sparta.

Megillus: True.

Athenian: Temenus became king of Argos, Cresphontes of Messene, and Procles and Eurysthenes of Sparta.

Megillus: Yes.

Athenian: And all the people of that time swore oaths that they would help defend these kings if anyone tried to overthrow their rule.

Megillus: True.

Athenian: But can a kingdom really be destroyed by anyone other than its own rulers? By Zeus — have we already forgotten what we said just a short while ago?

Megillus: No.

Athenian: Good. And now we can confirm that earlier point even more strongly, because we've come across historical facts that lead us right back to the same principle. We're not theorizing in the abstract anymore — we're talking about things that actually happened. Here's how it went: three royal families swore oaths with three cities, and the cities swore oaths with the kings, that both rulers and subjects would govern and be governed according to their shared laws. The rulers promised that as time went on, they wouldn't make their power more tyrannical. The subjects promised that as long as the rulers kept their word, they would never overthrow or allow others to overthrow those kingdoms. The kings were to help defend each other's kings and peoples, and the peoples were to help defend each other's peoples and kings. Isn't that right?

Megillus: Yes.

Athenian: And these three states — regardless of whether it was the kings or someone else who wrote their laws — had the strongest possible safeguard for maintaining their constitutions.

Megillus: What safeguard?

Athenian: That if any one of the three cities rebelled, the other two would always come to the rescue.

Megillus: True.

Athenian: Now, many people say that legislators should only impose laws that the general public will readily accept. But that's like telling athletic trainers or doctors to treat their pupils and patients only in ways that feel pleasant.

Megillus: Exactly.

Athenian: Whereas a doctor is often doing well enough if he can restore health and make the body whole without causing too much pain.

Megillus: Certainly.

Athenian: The lawmakers of that era also had another advantage that made their job much easier.

Megillus: What advantage?

Athenian: When they distributed property equally, they avoided the biggest political firestorm there is — the one that erupts whenever someone tries to redistribute land or cancel debts because they can see that without reform, there can never be real equality. Normally, when a lawmaker attempts anything like that, everyone screams, "Don't disturb vested interests!" People curse him for proposing land reform and debt cancellation until the poor man doesn't know which way to turn. But nobody could object to the Dorians distributing land — there was nothing to redistribute. And they had no significant debts, certainly no long-standing ones.

Megillus: Very true.

Athenian: Then why, my friends, did the settlement and legislation of their country turn out so badly?

Megillus: What do you mean? Why are you criticizing them?

Athenian: Three kingdoms were established. Two of them quickly corrupted their original constitution and laws. Only Sparta survived intact.

Megillus: That's not an easy question to answer.

Athenian: But we have to answer it if we're seriously investigating laws. This is our old men's sober game, remember — the one we use to pass the time on our journey, as I said when we first set out.

Megillus: True. We have to figure out why this happened.

Athenian: What laws are more worthy of study than those that governed these cities? What state settlements were ever more significant or more famous?

Megillus: I can't think of any.

Athenian: Can there be any doubt that your ancestors intended these institutions not just for the protection of the Peloponnese, but for all of Greece — in case of an attack by non-Greek powers? Remember, the people around Troy, when they provoked the Trojan War through their arrogance, were relying on the power of the Assyrian Empire under Ninus, which still existed and inspired great fear — just as we now fear the Great King of Persia. The second destruction of Troy was a serious offense against the Assyrians, since Troy was part of their empire. To meet this threat, the army was divided among three cities by the royal brothers, the sons of Heracles. A clever arrangement, it seemed — far better than the original expedition against Troy. For one thing, the people of that day believed they had better leaders in the Heraclidae than in the Pelopidae. And they considered their army superior in courage to the one that went against Troy — after all, the Trojans had been conquered by the Achaeans, but the Achaeans themselves had been conquered by the Heraclidae, Dorians overcoming Achaeans. Isn't it reasonable to think that this is what the men of that era had in mind when they set up their constitutions?

Megillus: Quite reasonable.

Athenian: And wouldn't people who had shared many dangers together, who were governed by a single royal family of brothers, and who had the blessing of oracles — especially the oracle at Delphi — naturally assume that their states would stand firm and last forever?

Megillus: Of course they would.

Athenian: And yet these institutions, which everyone had such high hopes for, all fell apart almost immediately — with the single exception, as I said, of the Spartan portion. And that one surviving third has never stopped fighting against the other two. Whereas if the original plan had held together and they'd stayed united, their military power would have been absolutely unstoppable.

Megillus: No doubt about it.

Athenian: So what destroyed this magnificent alliance? Here's a question truly worth investigating.

Megillus: Absolutely. You won't find more dramatic examples anywhere of how laws and governments can either save or ruin great and noble enterprises.

Athenian: Well, it seems we've arrived at a genuinely important question.

Megillus: Indeed we have.

Athenian: Have you ever noticed, my wise friend, how people — ourselves included, right at this very moment — often look at something and think, "What a magnificent thing! If only someone had known how to use it properly, it could have achieved wonders!" And yet this way of thinking might turn out to be completely mistaken — not just in our case, but in general?

Megillus: What are you getting at? What do you mean?

Athenian: I was just catching myself doing exactly that — admiring the Heraclid expedition, which was so noble and could have produced such wonderful results for Greece, if only it had been managed properly. And I was laughing at myself for it.

Megillus: But weren't you right to say what you said? And weren't we right to agree with you?

Athenian: Perhaps. But I can't help noticing that whenever anyone sees something great and powerful, the immediate reaction is: "If only the owner knew how to use this magnificent possession, imagine how happy they'd be and what great things they'd accomplish!"

Megillus: And isn't that a fair reaction?

Athenian: Think about it. In what sense is this kind of praise actually justified? Let's apply it to our case. If the commanders back then had known how to organize their forces properly, how would they have succeeded? Wouldn't it have been something like this: they would have bound the three states firmly together, preserved the alliance forever, and given themselves freedom and dominion as they pleased — the power to do whatever they wanted, anywhere in the world, Greek or non-Greek. What else could they have been after?

Megillus: Very good.

Athenian: And suppose someone were to express the same kind of admiration at the sight of great wealth, or distinguished ancestry, or anything like that. They'd be praising these things on the assumption that through them, they'd get everything — or at least most of what — they desire.

Megillus: They would.

Athenian: Well now, doesn't our argument show that there's one universal desire shared by all human beings?

Megillus: What is it?

Athenian: The desire that everything — or at the very least, everything within human reach — should turn out in accordance with one's own wishes.

Megillus: Certainly.

Athenian: And since we have this desire at every stage of life — in youth, in adulthood, in old age — we can't help praying for its fulfillment constantly.

Megillus: No doubt.

Athenian: And we join in our friends' prayers too, wishing for them what they wish for themselves.

Megillus: We do.

Athenian: A father loves his son — the elder loves the younger.

Megillus: Of course.

Athenian: And yet the son often prays to get things that the father prays he won't get.

Megillus: You mean when the son is young and foolish?

Athenian: Yes. Or consider this: when the father himself, whether from the foolishness of age or the recklessness of youth, has lost his sense of right and wrong and prays passionately — like Theseus when he cursed his innocent son Hippolytus — do you think a son who does have a sense of right and wrong would join in that prayer?

Megillus: I see what you're saying. A person shouldn't rush to have everything go according to their wishes, because their wishes might conflict with their reason. What every state and every individual really ought to pray and strive for is wisdom.

Athenian: Yes! And if you remember, I said at the very beginning that a statesman and lawmaker should design laws with wisdom as their goal. But you were arguing that a good lawmaker should organize everything with a view to war. And I replied that war involves only one of the four virtues, whereas the lawmaker should keep all of them in view — and especially the one that leads all the rest: wisdom, which has practical judgment and true understanding as its companions, with passion and desire following in their train.

So the argument has come back to the same point. And I'll say it again — call it a joke if you like, or take it seriously: the prayer of a fool is a dangerous thing, because it's likely to produce the exact opposite of what he wants. If you'd rather take that seriously, go ahead. And I suspect you'll find, as I've already argued, that what ruined the Dorian kings and their whole grand plan wasn't cowardice. It wasn't military ignorance, either on the part of the rulers or their subjects. Their downfall was caused by their general moral deterioration — and above all, by their ignorance of what matters most in human life. That was true then, it's true now, and it always will be. And I'll try to demonstrate this as clearly as I can throughout our discussion, if you'll allow me.

Cleinias: Please go on, friend. Compliments are tedious. We'll show how much we value your words not by flattering you, but by giving you our full attention — which is the best way a free man can show his approval.

Megillus: Well said, Cleinias. Let's do exactly that.

Cleinias: Absolutely — God willing. Continue.

Athenian: All right then. Picking up where we left off: the greatest ignorance was the ruin of the Dorian power. And the same is true now as then — ignorance is what destroys. So if that's the case, the lawmaker must do everything possible to cultivate wisdom in states and drive out ignorance.

Cleinias: That's obvious.

Athenian: Now think about this: what really is the greatest ignorance? I'd like to know whether you and Megillus agree with what I'm about to say.

Cleinias: Go on.

Athenian: The greatest ignorance is this: when a person hates what they actually believe, deep down, to be good and noble — and loves and embraces what they know to be wrong and evil. This conflict between pleasure and rational judgment in the soul is, in my view, the worst kind of ignorance. It's also the most widespread, because it affects the largest part of the soul. The part of the soul that feels pleasure and pain is like the common people in a state. And when the soul rebels against knowledge and reason and good judgment — which are its natural rulers — that's what I call folly. Just as in a state, when the masses refuse to obey their rulers and laws. Or in an individual, when sound reasoning is present in the soul but accomplishes nothing — or worse, the opposite of what it should. All of these I call the worst kind of ignorance, whether in individuals or in states. You understand, friend, that I'm talking about something very different from the ignorance of tradespeople who lack technical knowledge.

Cleinias: Yes, my friend. We understand, and we agree.

Athenian: Then let's establish this principle: a citizen who suffers from this kind of ignorance should never be entrusted with any position of authority. He must be labeled ignorant — even if he's a mathematical genius, skilled in every kind of clever intellectual feat. And the opposite kind of person should be called wise — even if, as the proverb says, they can't read or swim. These are the people to whom authority should be given, because they have good sense.

After all, my friends, how can there be even a shadow of wisdom where there's no inner harmony? There can't. The noblest and greatest of all harmonies is rightly called the greatest wisdom. And anyone who lives according to reason shares in it. But someone who lacks reason isn't just failing to save his household — he's actively destroying it. And he's the exact opposite of a savior of the state. He's utterly ignorant of political wisdom. Let that be our established principle.

Cleinias: So established.

Athenian: I take it there must be rulers and subjects in any state?

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: So what are the principles by which people rule and obey — in large states and small ones, and in families too? How many such principles are there? First, isn't there one claim to authority that's always legitimate — the right of parents, and ancestors generally, to rule over their children?

Cleinias: There is.

Athenian: Second, the principle that those of noble birth should rule over the less noble. And third, that older people should rule and younger people should obey?

Cleinias: Naturally.

Athenian: Fourth, that slaves should be ruled and masters should rule?

Cleinias: Obviously.

Athenian: Fifth, if I'm not mistaken, comes the principle that the stronger should rule and the weaker submit?

Cleinias: There's a rule that's hard to disobey!

Athenian: Yes — and a rule that prevails throughout the entire natural world. As the Theban poet Pindar once pointed out. But the sixth principle — and the greatest of all — is that the wise should lead and the ignorant should follow. And this, my dear Pindar — I'd tell him if I could — is not contrary to nature at all. It is the rule of law over willing subjects, not the rule of brute force.

Cleinias: Absolutely right.

Athenian: There's a seventh kind of authority, too — one assigned by lot, which is dear to the gods and a sign of good fortune. Whoever wins the lot rules; whoever doesn't, steps aside and submits. And we'd call this perfectly just.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: "So you see, legislator" — as we might playfully say to anyone who takes up lawmaking too lightly — "the principles of government: how many there are, and how naturally they conflict with one another. Here we've uncovered a fountainhead of political upheaval, and you'd better pay attention to it."

First, let's consider what happened with the kings of Argos and Messene. How did they violate these principles and bring ruin on themselves and the once-great power of Greece? Wasn't it because they failed to understand what Hesiod meant when he said that the half is often more than the whole? His point was this: when taking the whole is dangerous, and taking the half is the safe and moderate course, then the moderate path is actually better than the immoderate one.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: And which is more dangerous — this immoderate spirit appearing in kings, or in ordinary people?

Cleinias: It's probably more common and more destructive in kings, because they lead proud, luxurious lives.

Athenian: Isn't it obvious that the main goal of those kings was to override the established laws — to break the very agreements they'd sworn to uphold by word and oath? This lack of inner harmony may have looked like cleverness, but it was actually the greatest ignorance, and it completely destroyed the whole enterprise through discord and clashing.

Cleinias: Very likely.

Athenian: Good. So what precautions should the original lawmaker have taken to prevent this disaster? Here's the thing: it doesn't take much wisdom to diagnose a problem after the fact. Hindsight is easy. But to have foreseen the solution at the time — that would have taken a much wiser mind than ours.

Megillus: What do you mean?

Athenian: Looking at what happened with you Spartans, Megillus, anyone can see in retrospect what should have been done.

Megillus: Could you be a bit more specific?

Athenian: Nothing could be clearer than what I'm about to say.

Megillus: Which is?

Athenian: This: if you give too much of anything — too large a sail to a ship, too much food to the body, too much power to the mind — everything is thrown off balance. Too much food leads to sickness. Too much power leads to injustice, which is the child of excess. Here's what I mean, my friends: no human soul — especially a young, irresponsible one — can withstand the temptation of unchecked power. Nobody who gets it is immune. They inevitably become infected with the worst of all diseases: folly. And their closest friends end up hating them. When that happens, their kingdom is undermined from within, and all their power slips away. Great lawmakers who understand the principle of moderation should watch out for this danger.

As best we can tell from this distance in time, here's what happened with Sparta:

Megillus: What?

Athenian: A god watching over Sparta, seeing into the future, gave you two royal families instead of one — pulling you closer to the right balance. Then, some human wisdom blended with this divine arrangement. Noticing that Sparta's constitution was still running hot, someone tempered your inborn pride and strength with the moderating influence of age by creating the Council of Twenty-Eight Elders, whose power equaled the kings' on the most important matters. But your third savior saw that the government was still churning and swelling. So he established the Ephors, whose authority resembled that of officials chosen by lot. And with this arrangement, the royal office was blended from the right ingredients and properly balanced — which preserved it and, through it, preserved everything else.

Because if the original lawmakers — Temenus, Cresphontes, and their generation — had been left to their own devices, even the portion of Aristodemus wouldn't have survived. They simply didn't have the experience in lawmaking. Otherwise, they'd never have imagined that oaths alone could keep young men in check once they'd been handed power that could easily become tyranny.

Now that the gods have shown us what kind of government lasts, there's no great wisdom in seeing it after the fact — as I've already said, that's easy. But if anyone could have foreseen all this at the time and found a way to moderate the three kingdoms and unite them into one, they could have saved all those excellent institutions. And if they had? No Persian army — no foreign power at all — would have dared attack us, or would have dared to treat Greece as a pushover.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: Our defeat of the Persians was hardly something to brag about, Cleinias. The disgrace wasn't that the victors failed to win glorious victories on land and sea — they certainly did. No, what brought disgrace was this: of the three allied cities, only one actually fought for Greece. The other two were completely useless. One was so busy waging war against Sparta that it prevented Sparta from helping. And Argos — which had held pride of place when the territories were originally divided — refused to answer the call or send help when asked to resist the barbarian invasion.

There's a lot more that could be said about Greece's conduct in that war, and much of it isn't flattering. You can't really say that "Greece" repelled the invader. The truth is, if Athens and Sparta hadn't fought together, all the Greek peoples would have been thrown into chaos — Greeks mixed with Greeks, Greeks mixed with non-Greeks, non-Greeks mixed with Greeks. It would have been like what we see now in the nations under Persian rule, torn apart by unnatural separations and forced combinations, living in misery.

These, Cleinias and Megillus, are the charges we have to bring against so-called statesmen and legislators, past and present, if we want to analyze why they failed and what could have been done differently. For instance, we said just now that there should be no great, unmixed concentrations of power. The idea was that a state should be free, wise, and internally harmonious, and that a lawmaker should legislate with these goals in mind. And we shouldn't be surprised if we keep proposing different goals for the lawmaker that seem to keep changing. When we say the aim should be self-discipline, or wisdom, or friendship — these all amount to the same thing. If we use a variety of expressions, that shouldn't confuse us.

Cleinias: Let's continue in that spirit. Now, when it comes to friendship, wisdom, and freedom — what exactly should the legislator be aiming for, in your opinion?

Athenian: Listen, then. There are two mother-forms of government from which all others are truly derived. One you can call monarchy, and the other democracy. Persia has the most extreme version of the first, and we Athenians of the second. Almost all other governments, as I was saying, are variations of these two. Now, if you want to have liberty combined with friendship and wisdom, you absolutely must incorporate elements of both forms. That's the emphatic point of our argument: no city can be well governed if it doesn't draw from both.

Cleinias: Impossible to disagree.

Athenian: If a state commits itself exclusively and excessively to monarchy, or exclusively and excessively to democracy, neither one will maintain any balance. Your states — Sparta and Crete — have more balance than most. The same used to be true of Athens and Persia in their early days. But now they've lost it. Want me to explain why?

Cleinias: Absolutely — if it helps clarify our subject.

Athenian: All right, listen. There was a time when the Persians hit a better balance between slavery and freedom. Under Cyrus, the Persians were free men who also ruled over many others. Because the rulers gave their subjects a share of freedom and treated them as equals, the soldiers felt closer to their generals and fought more bravely in times of danger. And if there was a wise man among them who could offer good counsel, he was free to share it publicly. The king wasn't jealous — he allowed full freedom of speech and honored anyone who could give useful advice. The result was that the nation flourished in every way, because there was freedom, friendship, and a genuine sharing of ideas.

Cleinias: That does seem to have been the case.

Athenian: So how was this advantage lost under Cambyses, and then recovered under Darius? Let me take a guess.

Cleinias: This certainly has a bearing on our subject.

Athenian: I'd say that Cyrus, though a great and patriotic general, never gave any attention to education, and never bothered to put his own household in order.

Cleinias: What makes you say that?

Athenian: I think that from his youth onward he was a soldier, constantly at war, and he left the education of his children entirely to the women. And the women raised those boys as pampered darlings of fortune — blessed already, needing nothing more. No one was allowed to cross them in any way. Everyone was forced to praise everything they said and did. That's how they were raised.

Cleinias: A splendid education, that!

Athenian: The kind of education you'd expect from royal women who'd recently become wealthy — and with the men away, occupied with wars and dangers, and no time to supervise.

Cleinias: Fair enough.

Athenian: Meanwhile, their father had vast herds of cattle, sheep, and men. But he didn't realize that the boys he was going to hand everything over to weren't being trained in his own tradition — the Persian way. The Persians are a people of shepherds, sons of a harsh land, a stern mother that produces a tough breed capable of living in the open, going without sleep, and fighting when necessary. But Cyrus didn't notice that his sons were being raised differently. They were educated in the Median style — by women and eunuchs — in what people called the "blessings" of royalty. The result was exactly what you'd expect when children grow up without anyone ever telling them "no."

So after Cyrus died, his sons — stuffed with luxury and self-indulgence — took the throne. First one killed the other because he couldn't stand having a rival. Then the killer himself, driven mad by wine and brutality, lost the kingdom to the Medes and the man they called "the Eunuch," who despised the stupidity of Cambyses.

Cleinias: So the story goes, and it probably happened more or less like that.

Athenian: And tradition tells us that the empire was restored to the Persians through Darius and the seven conspirators.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: Now let's follow the rest of the story. Notice this: Darius was not the son of a king, and he hadn't been raised in luxury. When he came to the throne as one of the seven, he divided the country into seven provinces — and some faint traces of this arrangement still survive. He made laws based on the principle of universal equality within the state. And he enshrined in law the system of tribute that Cyrus had promised, creating a sense of friendship and shared purpose among all Persians. He used money and gifts to win people's loyalty. The result was that his armies enthusiastically conquered territories as vast as those Cyrus had left behind.

Then Darius was succeeded by his son Xerxes. And Xerxes was raised in that same royal, pampered style. "Oh Darius" — we might fairly say — "how could you raise Xerxes the same way Cyrus raised Cambyses? How could you not see the fatal mistake?" Because Xerxes, the product of the same kind of upbringing, met pretty much the same fate. And from that time until now, there has never been a truly great Persian king — though they're all called "Great."

And their decline isn't a matter of bad luck. It's the result of the terrible life that's typically led by the sons of extremely rich and royal families. No boy, no man — young or old — will ever become excellent in character if he's been raised that way. This is exactly the kind of thing the lawmaker needs to think about, and it's what we need to think about right now.

And it's only fair to give you Spartans credit, Megillus: you don't give special honors or a special education based on wealth rather than poverty, or on royal birth rather than common origin, unless your inspired lawmaker has specifically ordered it. Because no one should receive the highest honors in a state simply because they surpass others in wealth — any more than because they're a fast runner or good-looking or physically strong, unless they also have real virtue. And not just any virtue, either — they'd better have self-discipline.

Megillus: What do you mean, friend?

Athenian: Courage is a part of virtue, right?

Megillus: Of course.

Athenian: So think about this: would you want a housemate or neighbor who was extremely brave but had no self-control?

Megillus: God, no!

Athenian: Or how about a craftsman who was brilliant at his trade but a complete scoundrel?

Megillus: Absolutely not.

Athenian: And justice doesn't exist apart from self-discipline, does it?

Megillus: It can't.

Athenian: And our model of a wise person — someone whose pleasures and pains are in harmony with reason — such a person can't lack self-discipline, can they?

Megillus: No.

Athenian: There's another important question about how states should distribute honors.

Megillus: What's that?

Athenian: I want to know this: should self-discipline by itself, existing alone in a person's soul without the other virtues, be praised or criticized?

Megillus: I really can't say.

Athenian: And that's the perfect answer. Because whichever way you'd gone, I think you'd have been wrong.

Megillus: Then I'm glad I held back.

Athenian: Exactly. A quality that's merely an accessory — something that attaches to things that might be praiseworthy or blameworthy — doesn't really call for a verdict on its own. It's better left without comment.

Megillus: You're talking about self-discipline?

Athenian: Yes. But among the other virtues, the one that's most beneficial while also possessing this quality of self-discipline deserves the highest honor. The next most beneficial deserves the next highest honor. And so on — each virtue honored in its proper order.

Megillus: True.

Athenian: And shouldn't it be the lawmaker's job to establish these rankings?

Megillus: It certainly should.

Athenian: How about we leave the detailed rankings to the lawmaker? But the broad classification of laws into first, second, and third priority — that's something we lovers of law can work out ourselves.

Megillus: Sounds good.

Athenian: Here's what we maintain, then: a state that wants to be as safe and happy as human nature allows must distribute honor and dishonor in the right way. And the right way is this: put the goods of the soul first and highest — always assuming self-discipline as the foundation. Put the goods of the body second. And put wealth and property third.

If any lawmaker or state departs from this order by putting money in the place of honor, or by elevating what truly belongs last — can't we say they're committing an unholy and unpatriotic act?

Megillus: Yes. Let's declare that plainly.

Athenian: It was our examination of Persian government that led us into this longer discussion. What we found is that the Persians kept getting worse. And the reason, we argue, is that they stripped their people of too much freedom and imposed too much despotism, destroying friendship and the sense of common purpose. And once those are gone, the rulers stop governing for the benefit of their subjects and the people, and start governing for themselves. If they think they can gain even the smallest advantage, they'll devastate cities and burn friendly nations to the ground. They hate ruthlessly and horribly — and are hated in return. When they need their people to fight for them, they find no shared loyalty, no willingness to risk life on their behalf. Their armies may number in the millions, but those millions are useless on the battlefield. So they end up thinking their salvation depends on hiring mercenaries and foreigners, as if they didn't have enough men of their own. And they can't help being foolish, since their actions broadcast that the ordinary distinctions between right and wrong in a society are trivial compared to gold and silver.

Megillus: Exactly right.

Athenian: So we're done with the Persians, and our conclusion is that their current misgovernment is the result of too much slavery and too much despotism.

Megillus: Good.

Athenian: Next, we need to examine the government of Athens in the same way. We need to show that total freedom and the complete absence of any higher authority isn't nearly as good as moderate government under properly limited authority. That's what Athens had in our ancient constitution, back when the Persians launched their attack on Greece — or rather, on all of Europe.

There were four classes of citizens, arranged by a property assessment, and respect for law was our queen and mistress. It made us willing to live in obedience to the laws of that time. On top of that, the sheer scale of the Persian threat — by land and sea — created such overwhelming fear that we became even more obedient to our rulers and our laws. All these factors combined to produce an extraordinary sense of harmony among us.

About ten years before the naval battle at Salamis, a Persian general named Datis arrived, leading an army sent by King Darius with explicit orders to capture the Athenians and the Eretrians and carry them off. He was told to accomplish this on pain of death. Datis and his enormous force quickly conquered Eretria completely. Then he sent a terrifying report back to Athens: not a single Eretrian had escaped. His soldiers had literally linked hands and swept through all of Eretria like a net.

Whether that report was entirely accurate or not, it struck terror into every Greek heart — and the Athenians most of all. They sent ambassadors in every direction begging for help, but nobody was willing to come — except the Spartans. And they were either delayed by the Messenian war that was going on at the time, or by some other reason we're not told about, and arrived one day too late for the Battle of Marathon.

Then, after a while, reports started coming of even more massive preparations and endless threats from the Persian king. Time passed, and word arrived that Darius was dead and his young, hot-headed son had taken the throne and was pressing ahead with the invasion plan. The Athenians felt sure the whole expedition was aimed at them because of Marathon.

Then they heard about the bridge over the Hellespont, the canal cut through Mount Athos, and the vast fleet of ships. They could see no path to safety — not by land, not by sea. No one was going to help them. They remembered that in the first Persian invasion, when Eretria was destroyed, nobody had come to their rescue or risked an alliance with them. They assumed the same thing would happen again on land. And when they looked to the sea, they couldn't see any hope of survival there either — not against more than a thousand warships.

One slender, desperate chance remained. Their only chance. They looked at Marathon, at that seemingly impossible victory, and drew strength from it. They realized that their only refuge was in themselves and in the gods.

All of this created a powerful spirit of solidarity among them. There was the immediate fear of the moment. But there was also that deeper kind of fear — the reverence they'd developed through obedience to their ancient laws, which I've called "reverence" several times in our discussion. It's the fear that a good person willingly serves, while a coward knows nothing of it. If this reverence hadn't possessed them, they would never have stood together to defend their temples, their burial grounds, their country, and everything they held dear. Little by little, they would have scattered and been picked off one by one.

Megillus: Your words are true, Athenian, and worthy of both you and your country.

Athenian: They are true, Megillus. And it's fitting to share these things with you, since you've inherited your ancestors' virtues. But I'd like both you and Cleinias to consider whether my words don't also bear on our subject — legislation. I'm not just talking for the pleasure of it; I'm making an argument. Notice that the experience of both Athens and Persia was, in a certain sense, the same: just as the Persians led their people into utter slavery, so we Athenians led ours into utter freedom. The question now is: where does this take us? Our earlier arguments have a great deal to say here.

Megillus: True. But I'd like a fuller explanation.

Athenian: I'll give you one. Under the ancient laws, my friends, the people of Athens weren't the masters — they were the willing servants of the laws.

Megillus: Which laws do you mean?

Athenian: Let's start with the laws governing music — that is, the music of that era — so we can trace the growth of excessive freedom from its roots. In those days, music was divided into clearly defined categories. One kind consisted of hymns — prayers to the gods. Another, opposite kind was called lamentations. Then there were paeans, and also dithyrambs — songs celebrating the birth of Dionysus. There was yet another kind they actually called nomoi — "laws" — adding the term "citharoedic" to distinguish them. All of these, and others too, were precisely classified. Performers were not allowed to mix one style with another.

And the authority that maintained these standards and judged the performances and punished those who broke the rules wasn't expressed through hissing, or the crude shouting of the crowd, or through applause and clapping — the way it is today. No: the directors of public education insisted that the audience listen in silence all the way through. Boys, their teachers, and the general public were kept in line with a stick if necessary. The public was willing to maintain this good order. No one would have dreamed of making noise to express their judgment.

Then, over time, the poets themselves ushered in a reign of vulgar, lawless innovation. They were talented people, but they had no understanding of what was right and proper in music. They went wild — like Bacchants possessed with uncontrollable passion — mixing lamentations with hymns, paeans with dithyrambs, imitating flute music on the lyre, jumbling everything together. In their ignorance, they declared that music has no real truth, and that the only way to judge it — good or bad — is by how much pleasure it gives the listener.

By composing these reckless works, and setting equally reckless words to them, they infected the public with lawlessness and boldness. They made ordinary people think they were qualified to judge music and poetry for themselves. And that's how the theaters — which used to be silent — became vocal, as if the audience actually understood what was good and bad in art. Instead of a wise aristocracy of taste, what grew up was a wretched "theatrocracy."

Now, if this democratic judging had been limited to educated people, the damage would have been minor. But once the principle took root in music — this universal delusion of expertise, this general lawlessness — freedom followed. People thought they knew things they didn't know. They lost all sense of fear. And the absence of fear breeds shamelessness. What is shamelessness, after all, but the arrogant refusal to respect the judgment of one's betters — born from an excessive, reckless kind of liberty?

Megillus: Very true.

Athenian: After this kind of freedom comes another: disobedience to rulers. Then the attempt to escape the guidance of parents, mothers, fathers, and elders. As they near the end of this path, they try to escape the control of the laws too. And at the very end comes contempt for oaths and promises and total disregard for the gods. At that point they're acting out the old story of the Titans, who rebelled against the gods — returning to that same ancient pattern and living a life of endless misery.

But why have I said all this? I need to rein the argument in now and then, hold it with bit and bridle, so that — as the proverb says — we don't fall off the donkey. So let's ask again: what was the point of all this?

Megillus: Good idea.

Athenian: The point was this —

Megillus: Which was?

Athenian: We were saying that the lawmaker should keep three goals in mind: that the city he's legislating for should be free, internally unified, and wise. Those were our principles, weren't they?

Megillus: Yes.

Athenian: With those goals in mind, we picked two forms of government — the most despotic and the most free — and we've been asking which one gets it right. What we saw was that each one achieves its best form at a moderate level. When either is pushed to the extreme — extreme slavery or extreme liberty — neither produces good results.

Megillus: Very true.

Athenian: And that was our reason for tracing the settlement of the Dorian army, and the city Dardanus built at the foot of the mountains, and the migration of cities to the seashore, and the earliest humans who survived the flood. Everything we said earlier about music and drinking parties — all of it — served the same purpose: figuring out how a state can best be governed and how an individual can best order their own life.

Now, Megillus and Cleinias: how do we test whether all this has been worth saying?

Cleinias: Friend, I think I see exactly how we can put it to the test. This whole discussion seems to have come at the perfect time — it's exactly what I need right now. Your arrival here with Megillus feels like a kind of omen, and I'll tell you why.

Most of Crete is about to send out a colony. They've put the Knossians in charge of the project, and the Knossian government has appointed me and nine others to manage it. Our instructions are to give the colony whatever laws we think best — whether borrowed from the Cretan model or from anywhere else. They don't care if the laws are foreign, as long as they're good.

So do me this favor — and it'll benefit you as well: let's select from everything we've discussed, and let's imagine we're the original founders of a state. That way, we can continue our inquiry while also building a practical framework that I can actually use for the city we're about to establish.

Athenian: That's wonderful news, Cleinias! If Megillus has no objection, you can count on me to do everything I can.

Cleinias: Thank you.

Megillus: And you can count on me too.

Cleinias: Excellent. Now let's begin building our state.

Athenian

So now, what will this city actually look like? I'm not asking about its name -- that'll probably be determined by geography or the original settlement. Some river or spring, or maybe a local deity, will lend its name to the new colony. What I really want to know is: what's the location? Is it on the coast, or inland?

Cleinias: I'd say, Stranger, that the city we're talking about is roughly eighty stadia from the sea.

Athenian: And are there harbors along the coast?

Cleinias: Excellent harbors, Stranger -- you couldn't ask for better.

Athenian: Oh no. That's not great news. And is the surrounding countryside productive, or does it need a lot of imports?

Cleinias: It hardly needs anything from outside.

Athenian: Is there a neighboring state nearby?

Cleinias: None at all -- and that's actually why the site was chosen. Long ago, the inhabitants migrated away, and the region has been empty for as long as anyone can remember.

Athenian: And does the land have a good mix of hills, plains, and forests?

Cleinias: It's like the rest of Crete in that regard.

Athenian: You mean more rocky than flat?

Cleinias: Exactly.

Athenian: Well, that gives us some hope that your citizens might actually turn out virtuous. If the city were right on the sea, with fine harbors and importing instead of producing its own goods, you'd need some kind of superhuman savior -- a more-than-mortal lawgiver -- to have any chance of keeping the state from moral decay. But the eighty stadia of distance gives us some breathing room, even though the sea is still closer than I'd like, especially with those excellent harbors.

Here's the thing: the sea is a perfectly pleasant daily companion, but it also has a bitter, corrosive quality. It fills the streets with merchants and shopkeepers, and it breeds unreliable, untrustworthy habits in people's souls -- making the state faithless both toward its own citizens and toward other nations.

But there's a silver lining: the country produces what it needs on its own. And because the soil is rugged, it doesn't produce huge surpluses. If it did, there'd be a massive export trade, and gold and silver would come flooding in -- and as we can safely say, nothing is more destructive to a state that aims at justice and noble character. We made this point in our earlier discussion, if you recall.

Cleinias: I remember, and I think we were right then and we're right now.

Athenian: Good. But let me ask -- how's the country supplied with timber for shipbuilding?

Cleinias: There's no fir worth mentioning, no pine, and very little cypress. You won't find much stone-pine or plane-wood either -- the kinds of timber shipwrights always need for the interior of vessels.

Athenian: That's actually another advantage.

Cleinias: How so?

Athenian: Because no city should be able to easily copy its enemies in doing harmful things.

Cleinias: What does that have to do with anything we've been discussing?

Athenian: Remember, my good friend, what I said at the very beginning about Cretan laws -- that they were aimed at one thing only, which was war. You both agreed with that. And I replied that such laws were fine insofar as they promoted virtue, but I criticized them for focusing on only one part of virtue instead of the whole. So now I hope you'll both watch me carefully as I legislate, and hold me accountable if I aim at anything other than virtue -- or at only a piece of it.

Because I believe a true lawgiver is like an archer: he aims only at what's eternally and genuinely good, and he dismisses everything else -- wealth, or any other supposed benefit -- when it's separated from virtue.

Now, when I said that imitating enemies was a bad thing, here's what I was thinking of: consider a situation where a coastal people are harassed by enemies -- the way the Athenians were by Minos. I'm not bringing up old grievances here, but Minos was a great sea power who forced the people of Attica to pay him a terrible tribute. Back then, they had no warships -- not like they do now -- and the country didn't have much shipbuilding timber, so they couldn't easily build a fleet. That meant they couldn't learn to copy their enemy's naval tactics and fight back at sea by becoming sailors themselves.

And honestly? It would've been better for them to have lost those seven youths many times over than to have their heavy-armed infantry -- soldiers who fight on solid ground -- transformed into sailors. Sailors get used to constantly jumping ashore and then sprinting back to their ships. They think there's nothing disgraceful about throwing down their weapons and running away -- and they always have "good reasons" for it. That's the language of naval warfare, and it's anything but worthy of praise. We shouldn't be teaching bad habits -- least of all to the best citizens.

You can see the problem even in Homer. Odysseus rebukes Agamemnon for wanting to drag the ships down to the sea while the Greeks are locked in battle with the Trojans. Odysseus gets angry and says:

"You'd have us haul the ships to sea while the battle rages? That would only fulfill the Trojans' prayers and bring ruin crashing down on us. The Greeks won't keep fighting once the ships are being dragged to the water -- they'll look back over their shoulders and lose heart. Your plan would destroy us."

You see? Homer understood perfectly well that having warships at sea, right next to soldiers on the battlefield, is dangerous. With habits like those, you could train lions to run from deer.

And there's more: naval powers that depend on their fleet for survival can't properly honor the kind of military excellence that actually deserves it. When your safety depends on the pilot, the captain, the oarsman -- all sorts of rather ordinary people -- how can you give honor where it's truly due? A state like that can't get its values straight.

Cleinias: That may be so. But still, Stranger, we Cretans like to say that the battle of Salamis saved all of Greece.

Athenian: Yes, and that's a widely held opinion among both Greeks and non-Greeks. But Megillus and I would say something different: that the battle of Marathon was the beginning of the great deliverance, and the battle of Plataea was its completion -- and that these land battles actually made the Greeks better people. The sea battles at Salamis and Artemisium -- and I'll lump them together, if you don't mind -- well, they helped save Greece, I won't deny that. But they didn't make anyone better.

When we're evaluating what makes a state truly good, we have to look at both its geographical situation and the quality of its laws. Because mere survival -- just staying alive -- isn't the most honorable thing for human beings, no matter what most people think. What matters is living the best possible life while we're alive. I believe we've made this point before.

Cleinias: Yes, we have.

Athenian: Then we only need to ask: are we taking the approach that we've already agreed is best for founding and governing a state?

Cleinias: The best by far.

Athenian: Good. Now let me move to the next question: who will the colonists be? Can anyone come from anywhere in Crete? Is the idea that the population in various cities has grown too large for the available resources? Because I don't think you're planning to send out an open invitation to any Greek who feels like showing up. And yet I notice that settlers have come to Crete from Argos, Aegina, and other parts of Greece. So tell me -- where are you recruiting from for this venture?

Cleinias: They'll come from all over Crete. And among other Greeks, Peloponnesians will be the most welcome. As you rightly noted, there are Cretans of Argive descent. The Cretan people with the finest reputation today are the Gortynians, and they originally came from Gortys in the Peloponnese.

Athenian: Colonization is easier in some ways when the settlers come from a single people -- like a swarm of bees sent out from one hive. This happens when friends leave friends because of population pressure or some similar necessity, or when a faction within a state is forced to emigrate. Sometimes whole cities flee after being completely conquered in war.

Now, this uniformity is an advantage in one respect, but it also creates a problem. There's natural friendship among people who share the same race, language, laws, temples, and religious practices. But colonies made up of such a homogeneous group tend to resist any laws or constitution that differ from what they had back home. Even if the faults of their old laws were what caused the divisions among them in the first place, they still cling to those same customs out of sheer habit -- the very customs that ruined them. This makes them troublesome and rebellious for the leader of the colony, who is supposed to be their lawgiver.

On the other hand, a population drawn from many different sources might be more willing to accept new laws. But getting such a mixed group to work together -- to pull in the same direction, as they say of horses -- is extremely difficult, and it takes years.

Still, nothing does more for the improvement of humanity than good legislation and thoughtful colonization.

Cleinias: I'm sure that's true. But I'd like to understand why you say so.

Athenian: Well, my friend, I'm afraid where my thinking is leading me might sound a bit unflattering to lawmakers. But if the point is relevant, there's no harm in saying it. And really, why am I worrying? I think the same principle applies to pretty much everything human.

Cleinias: What are you getting at?

Athenian: I was going to say that human beings never really legislate at all. It's accidents of every kind that do our legislating for us. Wars overturn governments. The crushing pressure of poverty rewrites laws. Diseases force political innovations -- plagues sweep through, or years of bad harvests pile up one after another. Looking at all this, a person naturally rushes to the conclusion I was heading toward: no mortal truly legislates anything. In human affairs, chance rules almost everything.

And you could say the same about the arts of the sailor, the pilot, the doctor, and the general -- and it would seem perfectly reasonable. But there's something equally true that can be said about all of them.

Cleinias: What's that?

Athenian: That God governs all things, and that chance and opportunity work alongside him in the management of human affairs. But there's also a third factor, a more moderate view: that human skill should play its part too. In a storm, it's surely a huge advantage to have a skilled pilot on board. Wouldn't you agree?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: And doesn't the same principle apply to lawmaking? Even when all the favorable conditions are in place for a state's happiness, the right lawgiver still needs to appear on the scene from time to time.

Cleinias: Absolutely.

Athenian: In every field, the practitioner could rightly pray for certain conditions, and if fortune grants them, then all that's left is to exercise his skill.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And all those other experts we just mentioned -- if asked to name what they'd pray for -- would each offer up their own particular wish?

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: And the lawgiver would do the same?

Cleinias: I believe so.

Athenian: "All right, lawgiver," we'll say to him, "what conditions do you need in a state before you can organize it properly?" How should he answer? Shall I give his reply?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: He'll say: "Give me a state governed by a tyrant. And let the tyrant be young, with a good memory, quick to learn, courageous, and noble by nature. And let him have that quality which, as I said earlier, must accompany all the other parts of virtue if any of them are to be worth anything."

Cleinias: I think, Megillus, that the companion virtue the Stranger is talking about must be self-control?

Athenian: Yes, Cleinias -- self-control in the ordinary sense. Not the kind that certain philosophers inflate into some grand notion of "prudence," but the natural restraint that even children and animals can display -- some living with self-discipline, others without it. When this quality stands alone, without the other virtues, we said it's hardly worth counting as a real good. You understand what I mean.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: So our ideal tyrant must have this quality along with all the others, if the state is to acquire the best possible form of government in the shortest time. Because there isn't -- and never will be -- a faster or more efficient way to establish a good political system than through a tyranny.

Cleinias: Wait -- how on earth can anyone accept such a shocking claim, Stranger?

Athenian: It's really not hard to see, Cleinias -- it just follows the natural order of things.

Cleinias: You're saying we should assume a tyrant who's young, self-controlled, quick to learn, with a good memory, courageous, and noble?

Athenian: Yes -- and add "fortunate." His good fortune must be that he lives at the same time as a great lawgiver, and that some happy accident brings them together. When that happens, God has done the best thing he ever does for a state he wants to make truly prosperous. The second-best scenario is a state with two such rulers working together, and the third-best is three. The difficulty grows as the number increases, and shrinks as it decreases.

Cleinias: So you're saying that the best government emerges from a tyranny -- specifically, from the combination of a good lawgiver and a well-ordered tyrant -- and that the transformation from tyranny to an ideal constitution happens most easily? Less easily from an oligarchy? And least easily from a democracy?

Athenian: Not quite. What I mean is: the transformation is easiest starting from a tyranny, second easiest from a monarchy, third from some form of democracy. Oligarchy comes fourth -- it has the hardest time making this kind of change, because power is held by many potentates.

Here's my reasoning: I'm assuming the lawgiver is the real deal -- a genuinely great one -- and that his strength is united with that of whoever holds power. When the ruling element is small in number but very strong -- as in a tyranny -- the change is likely to be fastest and smoothest.

Cleinias: How so? I don't understand.

Athenian: I've said this several times now. But maybe you've just never actually seen a city under a tyrant?

Cleinias: No, and I can't say I have any great desire to see one.

Athenian: Well, if you did, you'd see exactly what I'm describing.

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: I mean you'd see how easily, and how quickly, a tyrant can change the character of an entire state -- if he wants to. He just has to lead the way, whether toward virtue or vice. He sets the example through his own behavior, praising and rewarding certain actions, criticizing others, and disgracing those who disobey.

Cleinias: But how can we expect ordinary citizens to just follow the leader's example overnight? How can he have the power both to persuade and to compel them?

Athenian: Don't let anyone tell you, my friends, that there's a faster or easier way for states to change their laws than when the rulers lead from the front. That kind of change has never happened any other way, and it never will.

No, the real impossibility -- the truly rare thing -- is something different. It's the thing that's almost never achieved across entire ages. But when it does happen, ten thousand blessings follow -- or rather, every blessing imaginable.

Cleinias: What are you talking about?

Athenian: The difficulty is finding a genuine love of self-control and justice in any powerful form of government -- whether it's a monarchy or an oligarchy based on wealth or birth. You might as well hope to see another Nestor, who is said to have surpassed all men in eloquence but surpassed them even more in his self-restraint. That, however, was back in the days of Troy. In our own time, there's nothing of the sort. But if such a person has ever existed, or does exist now, or will someday exist -- blessed is he, and blessed are those who hear the wise words from his lips.

And this holds true as a general principle: when supreme power coincides with the greatest wisdom and self-control in the same person, that's when the best laws and the best constitution come into being. Never any other way. So let's treat what I've been saying as a kind of sacred legend, an oracle. And let this be our proof that, from one angle, it can be terribly difficult for a city to get good laws -- but from another angle, nothing could be easier or quicker, if our ideal conditions are met.

Cleinias: How do you mean?

Athenian: Let's have some fun, shall we? Old boys like us, amusing ourselves by shaping in words the laws that would suit your state.

Cleinias: Let's get on with it, without delay.

Athenian: Then let's invoke God at the founding of our state. May he hear us and be gracious, and come help us establish our city and its laws!

Cleinias: May he come!

Athenian: But what form of government are we going to give this city?

Cleinias: Could you spell out what you mean a bit more? Are you talking about democracy, oligarchy, aristocracy, or monarchy? Surely you're not suggesting tyranny.

Athenian: All right -- which of you will go first and tell me what his own government should be called?

Megillus: Shouldn't I answer first, since I'm the elder?

Cleinias: Perhaps you should.

Megillus: Well, Stranger, now that I think about it, I honestly can't say what to call the government of Sparta. It looks like a tyranny to me in some ways -- the power of our Ephors is astonishingly tyrannical. And yet sometimes it strikes me as the most democratic of all cities. But then again, it would be absurd to deny it's an aristocracy. And we also have a kingship held for life, which everyone -- not just Spartans -- agrees is the most ancient monarchy of all. So when you ask me point-blank, I really can't say precisely which of these the Spartan system is.

Cleinias: I'm in the same boat, Megillus. I can't confidently say that the government of Knossos fits neatly into any of those categories either.

Athenian: That's because, my excellent friends, you actually have genuine political systems. But the states I was just listing aren't really systems of government at all -- they're just cities where people are dominated by and enslaved to one faction within the state. Each gets its name from whatever group holds power. But if we're going to name a state after its ruler, then a true state ought to be named after the God who rules over those who are wise.

Cleinias: And who is this God?

Athenian: May I use a myth to help answer your question? Would that be all right?

Cleinias: By all means.

Athenian: Long ago, in the primeval world -- well before the cities we've been discussing were ever founded -- there's said to have been, in the age of Cronus, a blessed form of rule and a blessed way of life. The best-governed states that exist today are merely copies of it.

Cleinias: Then we definitely need to hear about this.

Athenian: I agree completely -- that's exactly why I brought it up.

Cleinias: Good. Since the story is directly relevant, please give us the whole thing.

Athenian: Here's what the tradition says. In those early days, human life was one of spontaneous abundance -- everything provided without toil. And the reason, they say, was this: Cronus understood what we ourselves have been arguing -- that no human nature, when given supreme power, can manage human affairs without becoming swollen with arrogance and injustice.

Knowing this, he didn't appoint men to rule over other men. Instead, he placed over us beings of a higher and more divine race -- spirits, or what we might call demigods -- to be the kings and rulers of our cities. He did what we do with our flocks of sheep and other domesticated animals. We don't appoint oxen to lord over oxen, or goats over goats. We ourselves, as the superior species, rule over them. In the same way, God, in his love for humanity, set over us these spirits -- a superior race -- who cared for us with great ease and pleasure, both for themselves and for us. They gave us peace, respect for law, an abundance of justice, and freedom from internal strife. They made the human race happy and united.

This tradition -- and it's a true one -- tells us that any city where a mere mortal rules instead of God has no escape from troubles and suffering.

The lesson is this: we should do everything we can to imitate the life that's said to have existed in the days of Cronus. Whatever part of us is immortal -- our reason -- that's what we must listen to, both in our private lives and in public affairs. We should organize our cities and households according to law, and by "law" I mean the rational ordering of mind.

But if a single ruler, or an oligarchy, or a democracy is driven by a soul that chases after pleasures and desires -- endlessly craving, never satisfied, afflicted with an insatiable sickness -- and this corrupted spirit tramples the laws underfoot and becomes master of a state or an individual, then salvation is hopeless.

So, Cleinias -- will you accept this account?

Cleinias: Absolutely.

Athenian: You're aware, aren't you, that people say there are as many types of law as there are types of government? And we've already listed all the commonly recognized forms. Now, this isn't a minor point -- it's actually crucial. Because what we're really arguing about, once again, is the standard for justice and injustice.

Some people say that laws shouldn't aim at military virtue or virtue in general. They say the only proper aim of law is to protect the interests, the power, and the survival of whatever group currently holds power. That, they claim, is the natural definition of justice.

Cleinias: What do you mean exactly?

Athenian: They say that justice is the interest of the stronger.

Cleinias: Could you be more specific?

Athenian: Sure. Their argument goes like this: "Obviously, whoever holds power in any state makes the laws, right?"

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: "And do you really think," they continue, "that any conquering power -- whether it's a tyranny or a democracy or anything else -- makes laws for any reason other than to keep itself in power? That's the first and foremost goal of every set of laws."

Cleinias: What other goal could they have?

Athenian: "And anyone who breaks these laws is punished as a criminal by the very lawgiver who calls those laws 'just.'"

Cleinias: Naturally.

Athenian: "So that's what justice always is, and how it always works."

Cleinias: Well, that's their view -- if they're right about it.

Athenian: Yes -- and this is precisely one of those false principles of government we were discussing.

Cleinias: Which one do you mean?

Athenian: One of the principles we examined when we asked: who ought to rule whom? We concluded that parents should rule children, the older should rule the younger, the noble should rule the common. There were many principles, and they didn't always agree with each other. One of those principles was exactly this one -- the right of the stronger. And we noted that Pindar considered violence natural and justified.

Cleinias: Yes, I remember.

Athenian: So think about it: to whom should our new state be entrusted? Because here's something that has happened time and again throughout history --

Cleinias: What?

Athenian: When there's a power struggle and one side wins, they monopolize the government completely. They refuse any share to the losing side or their descendants. Both sides live in perpetual suspicion -- the winners terrified that someone with memories of past wrongs will rise up against them.

Now, according to our view, these aren't real political systems at all, and the laws they produce aren't real laws. Laws passed for the benefit of one faction rather than the whole state don't deserve the name. States with such laws aren't genuine communities -- they're just warring parties. And their notion of "justice" is meaningless.

I'm saying all this because in our state, I don't intend to give power to anyone simply because he's rich, or strong, or tall, or well-born. The person who is most obedient to the laws -- that's the one who wins the prize. The highest office and the chief religious authority goes to whoever stands first in this competition. The second-highest office to whoever comes second. And so on down the line.

When I call these rulers "servants of the law" or "ministers of the law," I'm not just playing with words. I genuinely believe that the survival or destruction of a state depends on this principle. Where the law is subordinate to the rulers and has no real authority -- I can see that state heading straight for ruin. But where the law stands above the rulers, and the rulers see themselves as servants of the law -- there I see salvation, and every blessing that the gods can give.

Cleinias: Truly, Stranger, you see with the sharp vision of age.

Athenian: Well, yes -- that's how it works. A man's eyesight for these things is weakest when he's young and sharpest when he's old.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: Now then -- what's the next step? Shall we imagine the colonists have arrived and are assembled before us, and deliver a speech to them?

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: "Friends," we'll say to them, "God, as the ancient tradition tells us, holds in his hands the beginning, middle, and end of all things. He moves in a straight line, following his own nature, toward the fulfillment of his purpose. And Justice walks always at his side, ready to punish those who fall short of the divine law.
