The lawmaker should focus on this — not on the interests of tyrants, whether one or many, and not on the raw power of the people, but on justice. Justice means distributing natural equality among unequals in a way that's proportional.

Still, there are times when every state is forced to use the words "just" and "equal" in a looser sense, just to keep the peace. Equity and flexibility are compromises with strict justice. That's why we're sometimes forced to use the lottery — to prevent popular discontent. When we do, we pray to god and fortune to guide the lot toward what's truly just. Even though we have to use both kinds of equality, we should rely on chance as little as possible.

That, my friends, is how and why a state that wants to survive should operate. A city is like a ship sailing a treacherous sea of politics, vulnerable to all sorts of threats. Rulers must pass the watch from hand to hand, day and night, in an unbroken chain. A large body can never maintain that kind of vigilance with real energy. So the majority of senators will need to spend most of the year tending to their own affairs. We should therefore divide them into twelve groups corresponding to the twelve months, with each group providing one month of service as guardians of the state.

Their job is to be available and responsive — ready to receive any foreigner or citizen who approaches them, whether to share information or to raise one of those questions that cities need to answer when asked by other states, and that they sometimes need to ask themselves. When internal disturbances threaten — as they inevitably will in some form — they must try to prevent them; or, if they've already started, act immediately to inform the city and heal the damage. This rotating body is essentially the executive branch of the state, and it should always control the convening and dissolving of assemblies, both regular and emergency. All of this is to be managed by whichever twelfth of the council is on duty, keeping watch alongside the other officers of state during their month, and resting during the other eleven.

That's how the city will be properly organized. Now — who should oversee the countryside, and how should that be arranged? The entire city and territory have been divided into twelve portions. Shouldn't we appoint superintendents for the city's streets, houses, buildings, harbors, marketplace, fountains, sacred precincts, and temples?

Cleinias: Of course we should.

Athenian: Let's start with the temples. We'll need servants of the temples, along with priests and priestesses. We'll also need road and building superintendents who keep an eye on people — making sure they don't cause harm — and on livestock too, both within the city walls and in the suburbs. Three kinds of officers are needed to serve the city properly. Those responsible for the city itself will be called wardens of the city. Those overseeing the marketplace will be wardens of the market. And those tending the temples will be called priests.

Anyone who holds a hereditary priesthood shouldn't be disturbed. But if there are few or no hereditary priests — which is likely in a brand-new city — then priests and priestesses should be appointed to serve whichever gods currently lack them.

Some of these officers should be elected; others should be chosen by lot. People from every class should mingle together in a friendly way in every district and city, so that the state achieves the greatest possible unity of spirit.

Temple officers should be chosen by lot — that way, the selection is left to god, who can do what pleases him. Anyone who wins the lot must pass a vetting process: first, to confirm that he's physically sound and legitimately born; second, to verify that he comes from a completely unblemished family — no homicide or similar impiety in his own past, and a similarly clean record for both parents.

The laws concerning all religious matters should be brought from Delphi, and interpreters should be appointed to administer them. Priests should serve for one year only, and must be at least sixty years old. The same rules apply to priestesses.

As for the interpreters, here's how they'll be appointed. Divide the twelve tribes into three groups of four. Each group selects four candidates — one from each tribe in the group — repeating the process three times. From the twelve candidates each group produces, the three with the most votes are sent to Delphi after undergoing the usual vetting — nine in all. The god at Delphi then selects one from each group of three. The age and vetting requirements are the same as for priests. These interpreters serve for life. When one dies, the four tribes from his group select a replacement from the deceased's tribe.

In addition to priests and interpreters, we need treasurers to manage temple property, sacred lands, and their revenues and leasing. Three treasurers shall be chosen from the highest property classes for the major temples, two for the mid-sized temples, and one for the smallest. Their election and vetting follow the same process as for generals.

That covers the temples.

Now, everything needs protection. The city's defense should be entrusted to the generals, brigade commanders, cavalry officers, tribal leaders, executive council members, and the wardens of the city and marketplace — once all those elections are complete.

The defense of the countryside works like this. The whole territory has already been divided into twelve portions. Each tribe assigned to a district will provide five wardens of the country and watch commanders annually. Each group of five then selects twelve additional young men from their own tribe — at least twenty-five but no older than thirty. These twelve are assigned to different districts each month on a rotating basis, so they all gain thorough knowledge of the entire country. Both the commanders and the watchmen serve for two years.

After their initial stations are assigned by lot, they move from place to place in order, circling from left to right — that is, from west to east — as their commanders direct. At the start of the second year, the rotation reverses — they move from right to left, place to place — so that as many guards as possible experience every part of the country in every season of the year. In the third year, new wardens and watch commanders are chosen — five per district to supervise the groups of twelve.

While on duty at each station, here's what they should focus on. First and foremost, they protect the country against enemies. They dig trenches, build fortifications wherever necessary — whatever it takes to keep hostile forces from damaging the land or property. They'll use the local draft animals and laborers as their workforce, commandeering them as much as possible during their off-hours.

Their goal is to make the country impenetrable to enemies and easily accessible to friends. They'll maintain roads for people, pack animals, and cattle, keeping them as smooth as possible. They'll manage rainwater — when it comes pouring down from the mountains into the valleys, they'll channel it with earthworks and ditches so the lowlands absorb the rainfall and produce springs and streams, giving even the driest areas a reliable water supply. They'll beautify the springs and rivers with plantings and buildings. If there's a sacred grove or temple precinct nearby, they'll channel water to the temples themselves, making them beautiful in every season.

All around such places, the young men should build gymnasiums for themselves and warm baths for the elderly, stocking them with plenty of dry firewood — a welcome relief for anyone suffering from illness. A weary farmer, worn out from labor, will find far better care there than at the hands of some mediocre doctor.

Building these works will be both useful and beautiful — a delightful pastime, but also serious business. The sixty wardens will guard their districts not only against enemies but also against supposed friends. When disputes arise among neighbors or citizens — whether involving slaves or free persons — the five wardens should decide minor cases on their own authority. For more serious matters involving charges over three minas, the full body of seventeen (the five plus the twelve) shall hear the case. Every judge and magistrate should be accountable for their conduct in office — except those with final decision-making power, like kings.

As for the wardens of the country themselves: if they abuse their authority over the people in their care — imposing unfair workloads, seizing crops or equipment without permission, accepting bribes, or judging cases unjustly — they shall be publicly disgraced. If they yield to flattery, same thing. For any other wrongs they commit against the country's inhabitants: in cases under a mina, they'll submit to the judgment of the local villagers. In larger cases, or in smaller ones where the warden refuses to submit (banking on the fact that his monthly rotation will whisk him away to another district), the injured party can bring suit in the common court. If the warden is found guilty, the plaintiff collects double damages for the defendant's refusal to submit.

During their two years of service, the wardens and overseers must take their meals together at their stations and live communally. Any warden who misses the common meal or sleeps away from his post — even for a single day or night, without orders from his commander or genuine necessity — shall be denounced by the five. His name will be posted in the public square for not keeping his watch, and he'll be publicly shamed as a betrayer of the city (to the extent of his failure). Anyone who meets him may beat him with impunity. If one of the five commanders commits the same offense, the entire company of sixty must deal with it. Any member of the sixty who knows about the offense and doesn't bring the offender to trial is subject to the same penalties as the younger offender — only with a heavier fine and a permanent ban from ever commanding the youth again. The guardians of the law shall carefully oversee all of this, either preventing offenses or punishing them when they occur.

Every citizen should remember this universal principle: anyone who isn't a good servant will never be a good master. A person should take more pride in serving well than in commanding well — serving the laws first of all (which is also service to the gods), and serving honorable elders during his youth.

Furthermore, during their two years of countryside duty, the wardens should eat simply and humbly. When the twelve young men are first chosen, they should meet with the five commanders and agree that they'll be their own servants. They won't use other people's slaves for personal tasks, nor exploit the villagers' or farmers' workers for private advantage — only for public service. In general, they should be prepared to live independently, serving themselves and each other.

Beyond that, in every season — summer and winter — they must stay armed and patrol the entire country thoroughly. This serves a double purpose: they stand guard, and they gain expert knowledge of every corner of the territory. There may be no more valuable kind of knowledge than an intimate familiarity with your own country. For this reason, as well as for the general benefits of pleasure and fitness, the young men should pursue hunting with dogs and every other kind of sport.

This whole corps — whether you call them the "secret service" or the "wardens of the country" (the name doesn't much matter) — represents a vital institution. Everyone who cares about the safety of the state should devote themselves to this service wholeheartedly.

After the wardens of the country, we need to discuss electing the wardens of the marketplace and the wardens of the city. The country wardens number sixty. The city wardens will be three, and they'll divide the city's twelve districts among themselves. Like the country wardens, they'll oversee the roads and the various highways connecting the countryside to the city, making sure all buildings conform to the law. They'll also manage the water supply — ensuring that what the country guardians send flows to the city fountains pure and plentiful, serving as both an ornament and a benefit. These should be men of influence who have the leisure to devote to public affairs.

To elect them: every citizen nominates whoever he likes from the highest property class. Once the votes are tallied and the field is narrowed to six, the election officers choose three by lot. After passing the vetting process, they take office according to the established laws.

Next, the wardens of the market: five shall be elected from the first and second property classes. The procedure mirrors the city wardens' election — ten are elected by vote, then five are chosen by lot from those ten and confirmed through the vetting process.

Everyone must vote for every candidate. Anyone who refuses to vote, if reported to the magistrates, will be fined fifty drachmas and branded a bad citizen. Attendance at the general assembly and council meetings is required for citizens of the first and second classes, with a ten-drachma fine for no-shows. The third and fourth classes face no penalty for absence unless the magistrates have declared attendance mandatory due to some urgent situation.

The wardens of the market shall maintain the marketplace according to established regulations, oversee the temples and fountains within the market, and make sure nobody causes damage. If a slave or foreigner damages property, they're punished with whipping and imprisonment. If a citizen misbehaves in this way, the market wardens may impose fines up to one hundred drachmas on their own authority, or up to two hundred with the city wardens' consent. The city wardens have the same power to punish and fine within their jurisdiction — up to a mina on their own, up to two minas with the market wardens' consent.

Next, it'll be appropriate to appoint directors of music and athletics. We need two kinds of each: one kind focused on education, and the other on managing contests.

For education, we're talking about officials who oversee order and instruction in gymnasiums and schools, manage attendance, and maintain school buildings for both boys and girls. For contests, we need judges — some for athletic competitions among men and horses, others for musical performances.

In music, we should separate the judges: one group for solo performances — rhapsodes, harp players, flute players, and similar artists — and another group for choral performances.

First, let's choose directors for the choruses — boys, men, and young women — who will lead them in dance and handle our other musical arrangements. One director is sufficient for the choruses, and he should be at least forty years old. One director is also enough for the solo performers, judging competitions and guiding the contestants. He should be at least thirty.

The choral director is elected like this: anyone who regularly takes an interest in these matters goes to the meeting. Failure to attend means a fine — the guardians of the law will judge. But people who have no interest in these matters aren't forced to come. The voter nominates whoever he thinks understands music. The nominee can be challenged during the vetting process — opponents argue he lacks skill, supporters argue he has it. Ten are elected by vote; one is chosen by lot from the ten. After passing the vetting, he leads the choruses for the year. The same process applies to the solo music director. The winner of the lot serves as director for that year and delivers judgments through the official judges.

Next: judges for the athletic and equestrian contests. These should be selected from the second and third property classes. The top three classes must participate in the election; the lowest class may abstain without penalty. Twenty are elected by vote, then three are chosen by lot. These three must also pass the vetting process.

If anyone is rejected during the vetting at any stage, replacements are chosen by the same method and undergo the same scrutiny.

One office remains: the superintendent of education for youth — both male and female. One superintendent is sufficient. He must rule according to law, be at least fifty years old, and have legitimate children of his own — both sons and daughters if possible, but at a minimum, one or the other.

Both the person elected and the person doing the electing should understand that of all the great offices of state, this is the greatest. Think about it: the first growth of any plant, if it gets a good start toward its natural potential, has the biggest impact on how it matures. This is true not just of plants but of all animals, wild and tame — and of human beings. We say that humans are civilized creatures, and with proper education and a good nature, they become the most divine and civilized beings of all. But with insufficient or poor education? The most savage creatures on earth.

That's why the lawmaker must never treat the education of children as a secondary or casual concern. The first step is to make sure that the person elected to this role is truly the best citizen in every respect. The lawmaker should spare no effort in making that happen.

Here's how the election works: all the magistrates — except the council and the executive officers — go to the temple of Apollo and vote by secret ballot for whichever guardian of the law they believe would make the best superintendent of education. The candidate with the most votes undergoes a vetting process conducted by all the magistrates who elected him, excluding the guardians of the law. He serves for five years. In the sixth year, a successor is elected the same way.

If anyone holding public office dies with more than thirty days remaining in their term, the relevant officials elect a replacement through the same process. If a legal guardian of orphans dies, the relatives on both the father's and mother's side — including cousins living in the area — must appoint a new guardian within ten days, or face a fine of a drachma per day of delay.

Now, a city without proper courts of law isn't really a city at all. But a judge who sits silently through the proceedings, saying nothing more than the parties themselves — the way it happens in arbitration — will never be able to judge fairly. That's why a large panel of judges rarely judges well, but a small panel of bad judges is just as problematic. The point of contention between the parties needs to be made clear, and time, careful deliberation, and repeated examination are what help resolve doubts.

So here's the procedure: anyone with a legal dispute should first go to his neighbors and friends — the people who know the facts best. If he can't get a satisfactory decision from them, he should try another court. And if two courts can't settle it, a third one gets the final say.

Now, setting up courts of law is essentially choosing another kind of magistrate. Every magistrate has to judge certain matters, and a judge — even if he doesn't hold any other office — is really a very important official on the day he decides a case. So let's treat judges as magistrates too, and talk about who should serve, what kinds of cases they should decide, and how many judges each case needs.

The highest tribunal should be the one that the litigants themselves create by mutual agreement, choosing their own judges. Beyond that, there should be two other courts: one for private disputes, when one citizen accuses another of wronging him and wants a ruling; and one for public cases, when a citizen believes the state itself has been harmed by an individual and wants to defend the public interest.

We still need to address the qualifications and identity of the judges. First, there should be a final court of appeal, open to all citizens who've already been through two lower courts. It's composed as follows: at the end of each year, on the last day but one before the new year begins — in the month after the summer solstice — all the magistrates, both annual and longer-serving, gather at a temple. Calling god to witness, each office dedicates one judge from its ranks as a "firstfruit" — choosing whoever they think is the best and most likely to decide cases fairly and honorably in the coming year. After the selection, these judges undergo vetting in front of the electors themselves, and any who are rejected are replaced by the same process.

These vetted judges hear the cases of people who've exhausted the lower courts. They vote openly. Councilors and the magistrates who elected them are required to attend as observers, and any other citizen may attend if he wishes.

If anyone accuses a judge of deliberately ruling unjustly, he can bring the charge before the guardians of the law. A judge found guilty must pay the injured party damages equal to half the harm done. If the offense warrants a greater penalty, the judges determine what additional punishment is appropriate and how much more the guilty judge owes — to both the public treasury and the plaintiff.

In cases of offenses against the state, the people must participate. When someone wrongs the state, everyone is wronged, and citizens have a right to be upset if they're shut out of the decision. These cases should originate with the people and be finally decided by them, but the trial itself should take place before three of the highest magistrates, agreed upon by both the plaintiff and the defendant. If the parties can't agree, the council chooses between the two proposed options.

In private suits, too, as many citizens as possible should have a role. Anyone who has no share in the administration of justice tends to feel he has no share in the state at all. For this reason, there should be a court in every tribe, with judges chosen by lot, who give their verdicts immediately and are immune to lobbying.

The final word always rests with that highest court — the one we've established to be as incorruptible as anything human can be. That's the court for people who can't resolve their disputes in the neighborhood courts or the tribal courts.

So much for the courts of law — which, as I said, don't fit neatly into the category of "offices" or "not-offices." I've given a rough sketch, with some things laid out and others left for later. The proper place for a detailed statement of procedural rules will be at the end of our legislation. So let's set them aside for now.

We've been mostly occupied so far with establishing the offices of government. Complete precision and unity across every detail of political administration can't be achieved until our discussion has a proper beginning, middle, and end. At this point, we've reached the election of magistrates, and that's a natural stopping place. Now there's no reason to delay any longer — we can begin the actual work of legislation.

Cleinias: I like what you've said, Stranger. And I especially appreciate how you've connected the beginning of this new phase to the end of the previous one.

Athenian: Well, then — so far, this old men's intellectual pastime has gone pretty well.

Cleinias: You mean our serious and noble pursuit, I hope?

Athenian: Perhaps. But I'd like to know if you and I agree about something.

Cleinias: About what?

Athenian: You know how painters are with their work — endlessly adding and removing colors, touching things up, always making their paintings brighter and more beautiful? They never seem to stop.

Cleinias: I know a bit about that from what I've heard, though I've never been much of an art expert myself.

Athenian: Doesn't matter — we can still use the analogy. Imagine someone who set out to paint the most beautiful figure possible, hoping that instead of deteriorating, his work would only improve with time. Don't you see that since he's mortal, unless he leaves behind someone to carry on — someone who can fix whatever time damages, add what the artist left incomplete, and continue to refine and improve the painting — all his great labor will last only a short while?

Cleinias: True.

Athenian

Isn't the lawmaker's situation similar? First, he wants his laws written down with all possible precision. Then, as time goes on and he actually tests his laws in practice, won't he inevitably find gaps? Can you imagine any lawmaker so foolish as to not realize that he'll necessarily leave things out — things that someone coming after him will have to fix, if the constitution and the order of government aren't going to deteriorate?

Cleinias: Of course. That's exactly the sort of thing anyone would want to get right.

Athenian: And if anyone has the means — whether by word or by deed, by whatever method, large or small — to teach people how to maintain and amend the laws, he should say what he has to say and not leave the work half-done.

Cleinias: Absolutely.

Athenian: And isn't that exactly what you and I need to do right now?

Cleinias: Do about what?

Athenian: We're about to legislate, and we've chosen our guardians of the law. Now, you and I are in the evening of our lives, while compared to us, these guardians are young men. So we shouldn't just legislate for them — we should also try to make them into lawmakers themselves, as far as that's possible.

Cleinias: Certainly — if we can manage it.

Athenian: At the very least, we have to try.

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: Here's what we'll tell them: "Friends, guardians and saviors of our laws — when we lay down any law, we'll inevitably leave out many details. That can't be helped. But we'll do our best to cover what's important and give you an outline that you'll need to fill in. Let me explain the principle you should follow. Megillus, Cleinias, and I have talked about these matters many times, and we believe we've come to sound conclusions. We hope you'll agree with us and become our students, keeping in mind what we believe a lawgiver and guardian of the law should always keep in view.

"Our central conviction was this: a person's entire energy throughout life should be devoted to acquiring genuine human excellence — whether that comes through study, habit, some particular method, desire, conviction, or knowledge. This applies equally to men and women, old and young. Everyone should always aim at this. Anything that gets in the way of this goal, the good person should show that he completely disregards.

"And if it finally comes down to this — that a person is forced to choose between becoming an exile from his homeland rather than bowing to the yoke of slavery under inferior rulers — then exile is what he must choose. He must endure all those hardships rather than accept a form of government that's likely to make people worse.

"These are our founding principles. Keep your eyes fixed on this standard of what a true citizen should be. Use it to judge the laws — criticize the ones that can't make citizens better, and embrace the ones that can. Live joyfully within good laws, and say a permanent goodbye to institutions that aim at so-called 'goods' of a different and lesser kind."

Now let's move on to another class of laws, starting with their religious foundations. And we need to return to the number 5,040. The whole number has a great many useful divisors, and the number of each tribe — a twelfth part of the total, formed by twenty-one times twenty (that is, 5,040 divided by 420 equals 12) — has them too. Not only is the whole divisible by twelve, but each tribal number is divisible by twelve as well.

Every portion should be regarded as a sacred gift from heaven, corresponding to the months and to the revolution of the cosmos. Every city has a guiding and sacred principle given by nature, but some cities have divided their territory more wisely than others, more in keeping with what's sacred and fortunate. In our view, nothing could be more right than choosing the number 5,040, which is divisible by every number from one to twelve — with the single exception of eleven. And that's easily fixed: just subtract two households from the total, and the problem disappears. We can prove this at leisure, but for now, let's take the principle on trust.

Let's divide the state accordingly. Each portion gets its own god or divine patron, along with altars and sacred rites. Twice a month, assemblies for sacrifice should be held at these altars — twelve for the tribes and twelve for the city's divisions. The first set of sacrifices should honor the gods and divine things. The second should promote friendship, mutual acquaintance, and every kind of good fellowship among the citizens.

People need to know the families they're marrying into — both the families they're joining and the families they're giving their children to. In matters like these, avoiding mistakes is absolutely crucial. And with this serious purpose in mind, let's establish festivals where young men and young women can dance together, seeing one another and being seen — at the right age, on suitable occasions, and without violating the rules of modesty. There. I said it.

The directors of the choruses will oversee and regulate these festivals. Together with the guardians of the law, they'll fill in whatever details we've left out. As we said before, when the legislator is dealing with countless small particulars, he'll inevitably miss some things. The annual officers who gain actual experience and learn what's needed should make improvements year by year until the system is properly worked out. A ten-year period of experimentation with the sacrifices and dances — covering every detail — should be quite sufficient. If the original lawmaker is still alive, the officers should consult him. If he's passed on, each officer should report any gaps to the guardians of the law and make corrections, until everything is perfected. From that point on, the system should be fixed. They should use these refined laws alongside the ones the lawmaker originally established, and they should never change any of them — if they can possibly help it.

But if some genuine necessity forces a change, all the magistrates must be consulted, the entire citizen body must weigh in, and they must seek guidance from all the oracles of the gods. If everyone agrees, then — and only then — may the change be made. If there's any dissent at all, no change is permitted. The dissenter's objection prevails, as the law requires.

Now then — marriage. Whenever a man over twenty-five, who has seen others and been seen by them at these festivals, believes he's found a compatible match suitable for producing children, let him marry — as long as he's still under thirty-five. But first, let him hear how he should go about finding the right partner.

As Cleinias says, every law should come with a proper prelude.

Cleinias: You've picked the right moment to bring that up, Stranger. You haven't missed a beat.

Athenian: Thank you. Here's what we'll say to the young man who comes from a good family:

"My son, you ought to make the kind of marriage that wise people would approve. They would advise you not to avoid a poor match or chase after a rich one. All else being equal, always give the edge to those of more modest standing, and seek to join your family with theirs. This benefits both the city and the families being united, because balance and symmetry contribute infinitely more to excellence than concentrated, unmixed qualities.

"If you know yourself to be headstrong — the kind of person who gets carried away in everything he does — you should seek to marry into a calm, orderly family. And if you're naturally the opposite — reserved and cautious — seek out the opposite kind of partner.

"Here's the general rule about marriage: every man should pursue not the match that's most pleasing to himself, but the one that's most beneficial to the state. Everyone naturally gravitates toward people who are like them, and this makes the whole city unequal in both wealth and temperament. The result is exactly what we'd least want to happen."

Now, if we added an explicit law — that rich people can't marry rich, powerful can't marry powerful, and that slower temperaments must marry quicker ones and vice versa — well, that would probably provoke both anger and laughter. People have a hard time grasping the idea that a city should be well mixed, like wine in a cup. When you pour wine straight, it's hot and fiery — but when a calmer element tempers it, you get an excellent, balanced drink. The same thing happens with marriage, but almost nobody sees it. So we can't quite put this into law. But we should try to charm people into believing that the balance of their children's characters matters more than piling up excessive wealth through marriage. Anyone who's too eager to marry rich, we should steer away through gentle social pressure — not through the force of written law.

That's our advice about marriage.

And let's remember what we said earlier: that a person should strive for a kind of immortality by leaving behind children and grandchildren to serve god in his place forever. Much more could be said by way of prelude about the duty of marriage.

But if a man won't listen — if he stays unsociable and cut off from his fellow citizens, and is still unmarried at thirty-five — he pays an annual fine. The highest class pays a hundred drachmas, the second class seventy, the third class sixty, and the fourth class thirty. This money is dedicated to Hera. Anyone who doesn't pay the annual fine owes ten times the amount, which the goddess's treasurer shall collect. If the treasurer fails to collect it, he'll be personally liable and will have to account for the money in his audit.

And there's more to the punishment than money. The confirmed bachelor loses all the respect that younger citizens normally show to their elders. No young person is obligated to obey him. If he tries to punish anyone, everyone should come to the defense of the person he's punishing. Any bystander who fails to come to the rescue will be declared by law a coward and a bad citizen.

As for marriage portions — I've already covered that. Let me repeat: no one should feel ashamed of poverty in marriage. The citizens of our state are provided with the necessities of life. Banning dowries means wives are less likely to become arrogant and husbands less likely to become servile and groveling because of their wife's money.

Anyone who follows this law is doing a noble thing. But anyone who defies it — giving or receiving more than fifty drachmas for marriage expenses if he's from the lowest class, more than a mina for the third class, a mina and a half for the second, or two minas for the highest class — must pay an equal amount to the public treasury. The excess gifts themselves are dedicated to Hera and Zeus. Their treasurers will collect the fines, just as Hera's treasurers collect from confirmed bachelors — or pay the penalty themselves.

For betrothals: a father's betrothal is valid in the first degree, a grandfather's in the second, and a brother's (from the same father) in the third. If none of these men are alive, a mother's betrothal holds in the same order. In cases of extraordinary misfortune — when no family member is available — the next of kin and the legal guardians have authority.

As for pre-wedding rituals and other sacred ceremonies related to past, present, or future marriages: the interpreters will handle that. Follow their advice, and you can rest easy.

For the wedding celebration itself: no more than five male and five female friends from each family, plus an equal number of relatives from each side. Nobody should spend more than he can afford. The richest class may spend one mina on the feast; the second class, half a mina; and so on in proportion. Everyone should praise the man who follows this rule. Anyone who goes overboard should be penalized by the guardians of the law — fined as a man with no taste and no understanding of proper wedding etiquette.

Now, getting drunk. It's always inappropriate — except at the festivals of the god who gave us wine. And it's especially dangerous when you're getting married. On this day, of all days, the bride and groom should have their wits about them. They should take care that their children are born to parents who are clearheaded — since nobody knows which day or night heaven will grant them conception.

Beyond that, children shouldn't be conceived when the parents' bodies are wrecked by intoxication. Offspring should be formed from what's solid and steady, calm and well-composed. A drunk person lurches through everything — his body is all over the place, and so is his mind. He's the worst possible seed-sower: unstable, unreliable, likely to produce children who are unsteady in both body and mind.

So throughout the entire year — and really throughout his whole life, but especially during the time he's fathering children — a man should avoid anything that damages his health or involves reckless behavior. He can't help leaving an impression of himself on the souls and bodies of his children, and he'll produce inferior offspring in every way.

And especially on the wedding day and night itself: abstain. The beginning of anything — which is itself a kind of divinity dwelling in human affairs — preserves all things, if it receives proper respect.

Moving on: the newlywed should treat one of the two houses in his allotment as the nest and nursery for his children. That's where he marries, sets up his home, and raises his family — away from his father and mother.

Now, in any close relationship, there needs to be some degree of missing each other, some longing, to cement the bond between different personalities. But too much togetherness — constant contact without the desire that comes from time apart — gradually dissolves affection through sheer saturation. That's why the young couple should leave their parents' homes and establish their own household, like colonists founding a new settlement. They'll visit their parents and be visited in return. They'll have children and raise them, passing the torch of life from one generation to the next, worshipping the gods according to the law, forever.

Now we need to consider what kind of property will be most practical. Most kinds of property are easy enough to understand and acquire. But the question of slaves — that's where it gets really difficult. And the reason is that everything we say about slaves is both right and not right. Our words about them are simultaneously consistent and inconsistent with how we actually treat them.

Megillus: I don't follow you, Stranger. What do you mean?

Athenian: That's not surprising, Megillus. The treatment of the Helots in Sparta is the most debated and controversial example of slavery in the entire Greek world. Some people approve of it, others condemn it. There's less argument about the Mariandynians enslaved by Heraclea, or the Penestae in Thessaly. But looking at all these cases, what should we do about slaves in our state?

The remark I made in passing — which naturally prompted your question — was this: we all agree that we'd like to have the best and most loyal slaves we can get. History is full of cases where slaves proved better than brothers or sons in every way — saving their masters' lives, their property, their entire households. We've all heard those stories.

Megillus: Of course.

Athenian: But we can also say the opposite — that the soul of a slave is utterly corrupt, and no sensible person should ever trust one. The wisest of the poets, speaking of Zeus, put it this way:

"Far-seeing Zeus takes away half the understanding of men whom the day of slavery subdues."

So we're left with two contradictory views. Some people completely distrust their slaves, treating them like wild animals — driving them with whips and goads until their souls become three times — no, many times — more slavish than before. Others do the exact opposite.

Megillus: True.

Cleinias: So given these differences in how people treat their slaves, what should we do in our own state, Stranger?

Athenian: Well, Cleinias, there's no getting around it: human beings are troublesome creatures. They aren't easily managed, and they won't become manageable just because we try to impose the neat categories of "slave," "free," and "master."

Cleinias: That's obvious enough.

Athenian: Troublesome indeed — as the repeated revolts of the Messenians have shown, along with the enormous problems in states that have many slaves speaking the same language, and the endless robberies and lawless behavior of the Italian bandits. When you consider all this, you're honestly at a loss.

Two remedies come to mind. First, don't let your slaves come from the same country or, if possible, speak the same language. That makes them much easier to control. Second, treat them well — not just for their sake but, even more importantly, out of respect for yourselves.

The right way to treat slaves is this: behave properly toward them. If anything, show them even more justice than you'd show your equals. Why? Because a person who genuinely reveres justice — who truly hates injustice — reveals his character most clearly in how he deals with those he could easily wrong. Anyone who remains untainted by cruelty and injustice in his treatment of slaves is sowing the best seeds of virtue in them. The same principle applies to every master, every ruler, every person who holds power over others.

That said, slaves should be punished when they deserve it — not talked to as if they were free citizens, which only makes them arrogant. The language you use with a servant should always be a command. Don't joke around with them, whether they're men or women. Plenty of people foolishly do this, and it makes life worse for everyone — slaves and masters alike.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: Good. Now that each citizen has been provided with an adequate number of suitable slaves to help with their work, let's turn to the question of housing.

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: Since the city is new and previously uninhabited, we need to pay careful attention to all the buildings — how they're constructed, the temples, the walls. Ideally, Cleinias, we should have settled these matters before the marriages. But since we're only talking through a theoretical exercise, there's no harm in changing the order. If our plan ever becomes reality, the buildings will naturally come before the weddings — god willing. But for now, let's just sketch things out in general terms.

Cleinias: Fair enough.

Athenian: The temples should be placed all around the marketplace, and the whole city should be built on high ground in a circle — for defense and for purity. Near the temples, place the government buildings and the courts of law. The courts should be treated as sacred ground — partly because they deal with sacred matters, and partly because they house the gods of justice. This is where homicide trials and other capital cases should be held.

As for walls — Megillus, I agree with Sparta on this one. Let the walls sleep in the earth. Don't dig them up. There's a fine old saying that "walls should be made of steel and iron, not of earth."

Think about it: how absurd would it be to send our young men out every year to dig trenches and build fortifications to keep the enemy off our land — and then turn around and surround ourselves with a wall? Walls are bad for a city's health, in more ways than one. They encourage a kind of mental softness in the inhabitants, inviting people to run behind them instead of standing up to their enemies. People start thinking their safety comes from walls and gates, and that they can just sleep securely. They forget that real security comes from hard work, and that shameful laziness is just an invitation for more trouble.

But if people insist on having walls, then the private houses should be designed from the beginning so that the whole city effectively becomes one wall — uniform buildings with consistent frontage onto the streets, forming a single defensible structure. There's something appealing about a city that looks like one great house, and it's infinitely better for security, too.

Until the initial construction is finished, this should be the citizens' primary focus. The wardens of the city should oversee the work, fining anyone who's negligent. They should enforce cleanliness in everything related to the city, and prevent any private person from encroaching on public property through unauthorized building or excavation. They should also make sure rainwater drains properly, and handle whatever other urban management issues arise. The guardians of the law may pass additional regulations as experience shows them to be necessary, filling in whatever gaps remain.

Now — with the buildings around the marketplace, the gymnasia, the schools, and the theaters all ready and waiting for students and spectators — let's move on to what comes after marriage in our legal code.

Cleinias: By all means.

Athenian: All right, let's assume the marriages have taken place. Now we need to address the first year of married life, before children are born — how the bride and groom should live in a city that aspires to be better than all others. This isn't easy to figure out. We've already run into plenty of difficulties, but this might be the biggest one — and the most unpopular. Still, I have to say what I think is right and true, Cleinias.

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: Anyone who thinks he can legislate for public life while leaving citizens' private lives completely unregulated — expecting people to behave however they want at home but somehow conform to law in their public conduct — is making a serious mistake. Why do I bring this up? Because I'm about to propose that bridegrooms should continue eating at the common tables, just as they did before marriage.

When this custom was first established in your parts of the world, Megillus and Cleinias, I imagine it was the result of some war or similar emergency — the kind of crisis likely to arise in thinly populated areas under pressure. But once people tried it and got used to it, experience showed that communal dining was a tremendous benefit to public order. That's how the custom arose among you.

Cleinias: That seems likely enough.

Athenian: What I said was that this custom may have seemed strange and even dangerous to impose at first, but that it's no longer controversial. There is, however, a related institution that would be the natural next step — an excellent one, if it existed anywhere. But it doesn't. The institution I'm about to describe is so hard to implement that it's like what people say about the legislator "combing wool into the fire" — attempting something impossible and futile.

Cleinias: What on earth are you so hesitant about, Stranger?

Athenian: You'll hear — and I won't waste time beating around the bush. Law and order in a state cause every good thing, while disorder and lawlessness often ruin what's been well established. And that's where we are right now.

Here's the thing, Cleinias and Megillus: among your people, the communal tables for men are — as I said — a heaven-sent and admirable institution. But you've made a mistake in leaving the women completely unregulated by law. They have no equivalent institution of public dining. And the female half of the population — which by nature is more inclined to secrecy and stealth, owing to its relative weakness — has been left without any legal framework. This is a huge mistake.

Because of this neglect, all sorts of things have gone slack among you that could have been far better managed. Failing to regulate women's lives isn't just neglecting half the population. It's actually more than twice as damaging as it sounds, because — to the degree that women's nature is less inclined toward virtue than men's — the consequences of neglect are proportionally greater.

Careful attention to this issue — organizing institutions for both men and women on common principles — would be a tremendous benefit to the city's well-being. But as things stand today, the human race is in such a sorry condition that no sensible person would even dare to bring up the subject of communal dining in places where it's never been practiced. And how could you avoid looking absolutely ridiculous if you tried to force women to eat and drink in public?

Nothing would make women more furious. They're used to living their lives out of the spotlight, and if you tried to drag them into the light, they'd resist with everything they have — far too much for any lawmaker to handle. As I said, in most places they won't tolerate even hearing the idea without raising an almighty uproar. But perhaps in this state, they might.

So if we can agree that our whole discussion about the ideal state hasn't been just idle talk, I'd like to try to prove that this institution is good and proper — if you're willing to listen. If you'd rather not, I'll hold my peace.

Cleinias: There's nothing either of us would rather hear, Stranger. Please, go ahead.

Athenian: Very well. But don't be surprised if I go back a ways. We have plenty of time, and there's nothing preventing us from examining the subject of law from every angle.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: Then let's return to something we've touched on before. Everyone should understand that the human race either had no beginning and will have no end — existing forever in both directions — or that it began an immeasurably long time ago.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And hasn't the world seen the rise and fall of countless states, every kind of orderly and disorderly pursuit, all manner of eating and drinking customs, and endless changes of climate — under which living creatures must have undergone countless transformations?

Cleinias: No doubt.

Athenian: And can't we suppose that grape vines appeared at some point when they hadn't existed before? And olive trees? And the gifts of Demeter and her daughter — grain — for which Triptolemus was the divine agent? And that before these things existed, animals survived by eating each other, as they still do?

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: We also know that the practice of human sacrifice still exists among some peoples. On the other hand, we hear of people who wouldn't even taste the flesh of a cow, who made no animal sacrifices but offered only cakes, fruits dipped in honey, and other pure offerings — abstaining from meat because they believed it was wrong to eat living things and sinful to stain the gods' altars with blood. In those days, people are said to have lived a kind of "Orphic life" — consuming everything that wasn't alive while abstaining from everything that was.

Cleinias: That's the longstanding tradition, and it's probably true.

Athenian: Someone might ask: "What's the point of all this?"

Cleinias: A very fair question, Stranger.

Athenian: So I'll try, Cleinias, to draw the natural conclusion.

Cleinias: Go on.

Athenian: What I see is this: everything in human life depends on three fundamental wants and desires. If these are properly guided, they lead to virtue. If misguided, to the opposite.

The first two are hunger and thirst, which are present from birth. Every animal has a fierce, instinctive drive to satisfy them, and it rebels furiously against anyone who says it can't indulge every appetite and eliminate every discomfort.

The third — the greatest and sharpest desire — erupts last: the fire of sexual passion, which kindles every kind of reckless and irrational behavior in human beings.

These three drives must be mastered by three great forces: fear, law, and right reason. With the help of the Muses and the gods who preside over contests, we can redirect them from what merely feels best to what truly is best, dampening their growth and overflow.

But let me get back on track. After marriage, we need to discuss the birth of children, and after birth, their upbringing and education. As the discussion unfolds, the individual laws will take shape, and eventually we'll arrive at the question of communal dining — whether it should be restricted to men or extended to women as well. When we get closer, we'll have a better view, and we'll be able to determine what prior institutions need to be established first. As I said, we'll see the details more clearly up close and be better equipped to lay down the appropriate laws.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: Let's keep in mind what we've just said. We may need it later.

Cleinias: What do you want us to remember?

Athenian: The three things we identified: first, eating; second, drinking; third, sexual desire.

Cleinias: We'll remember, Stranger.

Athenian: Good. Now let's proceed to marriage law itself, and teach people how they should go about having children — with the warning that if they don't comply, the law has teeth.

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: The bride and groom should keep in mind that they owe the state the best and finest children they can produce. Now, everyone who's involved in any joint endeavor succeeds when they pay attention to what they're doing, and fails when they're careless or absent-minded. So the groom should give his full attention to his bride and to the business of producing children, and the bride should do the same — especially during the time before their children are born.

The women we've chosen as overseers shall monitor these matters. However many of them the magistrates decide on, they'll gather every day at the temple of Eileithyia — the goddess of childbirth — for a third of the day. There, they'll report to each other on any man or woman among the child-bearers who seems to be ignoring the rules laid down during the marriage ceremonies and sacrifices.

The period of supervised childbearing lasts ten years — no longer — from the time the marriage is considered fruitful. If after ten years a couple remains childless, they should consult with their relatives and the women overseers and arrange a divorce for their mutual benefit. If there's any dispute about what's proper or in either party's interest, they should choose ten of the guardians of the law to arbitrate, and accept whatever those guardians decide.

The women overseers have the authority to enter the homes of the young couples — partly to advise and partly to threaten — in order to make them give up any foolish or improper behavior. If the couples persist despite this, the women go to the guardians of the law, and the guardians intervene. If even the guardians can't fix the problem, they bring it before the public. The offender's name is posted, and the guardians swear under oath that they were unable to reform this person. Anyone whose name is posted in this way — and who can't win a court case against those who inscribed it — loses the following citizen privileges:

He may not attend weddings or the celebrations held after a child's birth. If he shows up anyway, anyone may strike him with impunity.

The same rules apply to women. A woman who's posted for disorderly conduct and can't overturn the verdict in court is barred from public appearances, denied civic honors, and excluded from wedding and birthday festivals.

Once a couple has fulfilled their obligation to have children according to the law, if either the husband or wife has sexual relations with someone who is still in their childbearing period, they face the same penalties as those who haven't yet completed their families. After the childbearing period is over, the man or woman who behaves with restraint shall be held in high regard, while those who don't shall be held in the opposite — that is to say, disrepute.

If most people behave decently, these regulations can remain on the books but dormant. But if people start acting out, the laws shall be enforced.

Every person's first year of life marks their beginning. The date of birth should be recorded in the family's ancestral temples. Each phratry — each civic brotherhood — should keep a whitewashed wall inscribed with the names of the successive archons used to reckon the years. Nearby, the names of living phratry members should be written, and erased when they die.

The legal marriage age for women is sixteen to twenty. For men, thirty to thirty-five. A woman may hold office at forty; a man at thirty. Military service for men runs from twenty to sixty. For women, if military service is deemed necessary, the period of service extends from after they've finished bearing children up to the age of fifty. In all cases, what's possible and appropriate for each individual should be taken into account.

Athenian

Now, assuming children of both sexes have been born, it's time for us to consider their upbringing and education. This isn't something we can just ignore, and yet it might seem like a topic better suited to advice and guidance than to actual legislation. In private life, there are all sorts of little things — arising from individual pleasures, pains, and desires — that aren't always obvious, and that run counter to what the lawgiver intends. They make citizens' characters varied and inconsistent, and that's a real problem for a state. These matters are too small and frequent to make into criminal offenses — and if you did criminalize them, you'd actually undermine the written law, because people would get into the habit of constantly breaking the law over trivial things. The result is that you can't legislate about them, but you can't stay silent either. I realize I'm being a bit vague, but I'll try to bring my meaning into the light. I'll admit that what I'm saying right now lacks clarity.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: Am I not right that a good education is one that develops both mind and body as much as possible?

Cleinias: Without a doubt.

Athenian: And isn't it obvious that the healthiest bodies are those that grow up from infancy in the best and straightest way?

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: Here's something else we can observe: the first burst of growth in every living thing is by far the greatest and most dramatic. Many people would even argue that a person at twenty-five isn't even twice the height they were at five.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: And isn't rapid growth without proper, abundant exercise the source of countless problems in the body?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: And the body should get the most exercise during the period when it's receiving the most nourishment?

Cleinias: But wait, Stranger — are we really going to impose all this exercise on newborn infants?

Athenian: Not newborns — even earlier. On babies still in the womb.

Cleinias: What do you mean? During pregnancy?

Athenian: Exactly. I'm not at all surprised you haven't heard of this particular kind of exercise for such tiny creatures. It's unusual, but I'll do my best to explain it to you.

Cleinias: Please do.

Athenian: The practice is easier for us Athenians to understand than for you, because of certain pastimes we take to extremes back home. In Athens, not just boys but often grown men keep quails and roosters that they train to fight each other. And they're far from thinking that the fights themselves provide enough exercise. On top of the fighting, they carry the birds around — the smaller ones in their hands, the bigger ones tucked under their arms — and take them for walks covering many miles, not for the sake of their own health, but for the health of the birds. This proves to anyone with sense that all bodies benefit from being shaken and moved, as long as the movement doesn't lead to exhaustion — whether the motion comes from the body itself, or from being rocked in a cradle, or from the motion of a ship at sea, or from riding a horse, or from any other kind of movement. By mastering their nourishment through motion, bodies gain beauty, health, and strength.

So, accepting all this — what follows? Should we pass a ridiculous law requiring pregnant women to walk around and shape the embryo the way we shape wax before it hardens? And after birth, swaddle the infant for two years? Should we force nurses, under threat of legal penalties, to always be carrying the children somewhere — to temples, to the countryside, to relatives' houses — until the children can stand properly? And require them to make sure the babies' limbs don't get distorted from bearing weight too young? The nurses should continue carrying them until the child completes its third year. They should be strong women, and there should be more than one per child. Should these be our rules, with penalties for neglecting them? No, no — the penalty we'd really face would fall on our own heads soon enough.

Cleinias: What penalty?

Athenian: Ridicule. And the sheer difficulty of getting nurses — with their stubborn and resistant natures — to actually comply.

Cleinias: Then why bring the whole thing up at all?

Athenian: Because when the leading citizens of a state hear about it, they're very likely to arrive at an important realization: that without proper regulation of private life in cities, you can't expect stability in your laws. And anyone who grasps this point may voluntarily adopt the practices I just described, run their household well, and be happy for it.

Cleinias: That's likely enough.

Athenian: So let's continue with our legislation until we've determined the exercises suited to children's souls, just as we've been working through the rules for their bodies.

Cleinias: By all means.

Athenian: Let's take this as our first principle for both the body and soul of very young children: nursing them and keeping them in motion day and night is good for all of them, and the younger they are, the more they need it. Ideally, infants should live as if they were always rocking on a ship at sea. This is the lesson we can draw from the experience of nurses, and also from the motion used in the rites of the Corybantes. When mothers want their restless children to fall asleep, they don't use stillness — just the opposite. They rock them in their arms. And they don't give them silence — they sing to them and wrap them in sweet melodies. The Bacchic women, too, are cured of their frenzy in the same way: through dance and music.

Cleinias: And what's the reason for that, Stranger?

Athenian: The reason is pretty clear.

Cleinias: What is it?

Athenian: The agitation of both the Bacchic worshippers and the children is a kind of fear, rooted in an unhealthy condition of the soul. When someone applies external movement to this kind of inner disturbance, the motion from outside overpowers the terrible, violent motion within, and produces peace and calm in the soul. It quiets the restless pounding of the heart — which is exactly what you want — sending children to sleep, and bringing the Bacchic worshippers, while still awake, to dance to the pipe with the help of the gods to whom they offer their sacrifices. It replaces their frenzy with a sound mind. To put it simply, there's a great deal to be said for this treatment.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: But if fear has this kind of power, we should also realize something: any soul that's been exposed to a lot of fear from early childhood will become more prone to fear. And everyone would agree that this is the path to forming a habit of cowardice, not courage.

Cleinias: No doubt.

Athenian: On the other hand, the habit of overcoming fears and terrors from childhood onward — that can be called an exercise in courage.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: So we can say that exercise and motion in the earliest years of life contribute greatly to building a part of virtue in the soul.

Cleinias: Quite true.

Athenian: Furthermore, a cheerful temperament — or the lack of one — plays a big role in developing either courage or cowardice.

Cleinias: To be sure.

Athenian: So now we need to work out how, and to what extent, we can implant either character in young children, if we choose.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: There's a common view that luxury makes young people discontented, irritable, and wildly overreactive to trivial things. On the other hand, excessive and harsh servitude makes people mean, downtrodden, and hostile to their fellow humans — making them unfit as companions.

Cleinias: But how is the state supposed to educate children who don't yet understand the language of their country and can't appreciate any kind of instruction?

Athenian: Here's how. Every animal that's born tends to make some kind of cry, and this is especially true of human beings. Humans are also more inclined to weep than any other animal.

Cleinias: Quite true.

Athenian: When nurses want to figure out what an infant needs, they use these signs: when something is brought to the baby and it stays quiet, they assume it's happy. When it cries and screams, they assume it's not. Tears and cries are the signals by which children show what they love and hate. And this period — these first three years — is a very significant portion of a life, spent either well or poorly.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: Now, doesn't it seem to you that a discontented and unpleasant nature is one that's full of more grief and sorrow than a good person ought to have?

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: Well then — if during those first three years, every possible effort were made to ensure that our young child experienced as little sorrow, fear, and pain as possible, wouldn't we expect to make their soul more gentle and cheerful in early childhood?

Cleinias: Obviously, Stranger — especially if we could provide them with a variety of pleasures.

Athenian: There I can't agree with you, Cleinias. You're amazing! That approach would be the child's absolute ruin. The beginning is always the most critical stage of education. Let's see if I'm right.

Cleinias: Go on.

Athenian: This point where you and I disagree is extremely important, and I hope you, Megillus, will help settle it. Here's what I maintain: the right kind of life shouldn't chase after pleasures, but it also shouldn't completely avoid pain. It should embrace a middle state — what I just described as gentle and moderate — which we rightly associate with the divine. Now I'm saying that any person who wants to approach the divine should pursue this same middle path. They shouldn't rush headlong toward pleasures, because that won't free them from pain. And we shouldn't let anyone — young or old, male or female — live that way, and least of all the newborn infant. Infancy is when character is most deeply shaped by habit.

In fact, if I weren't afraid of sounding ridiculous, I'd go further and say that a woman during her year of pregnancy should be cared for more carefully than anyone else. She should be kept from violent or excessive pleasures and pains, and should cultivate gentleness, kindness, and a calm spirit during that time.

Cleinias: You don't need to ask Megillus which of us has spoken more truly, Stranger. I myself agree that everyone should avoid a life of pure pleasure or pure pain, and should always pursue a middle course. You've spoken well, and I'm happy to say you've been well answered.

Athenian: Very good, Cleinias. Now let the three of us consider one more point.

Cleinias: What is it?

Athenian: All the things we've been describing are commonly known as "unwritten customs," and what people call "ancestral laws" are of the same kind. The thought we had a moment ago — that we can neither formally call these things laws nor leave them unmentioned — is justified. These customs are the bonds that hold an entire state together. They sit between the written laws that already exist and those yet to be enacted. They're ancestral practices of great antiquity that, when properly maintained, shield and preserve the written law. But if they fall into disorder, they're like the support beams of a building that slip out of place and cause a total collapse — one part drags another down, and the whole beautiful structure falls because the old foundations have been undermined.

Keeping this in mind, Cleinias, you should bind your new state together in every possible way, leaving out nothing — whether great or small — that falls under the heading of laws, customs, or practices. A city is held together by all these things, and they're only durable when they depend on one another. So we shouldn't be surprised to find that many apparently trivial customs and habits keep pouring in and extending our legislation.

Cleinias: Very true. We're inclined to agree with you.

Athenian: Up to age three, for both boys and girls, if someone strictly follows the guidelines we've laid out and makes them a priority, it'll do a great deal of good for the young children. But at three, four, five, and six, the child's nature will need games and play. Now is the time to curb self-will by punishing it — but not in a way that humiliates the child. We said the same thing about slaves: you shouldn't add insult to punishment, which just makes them angry, but you also shouldn't leave them unpunished, which makes them spoiled. The same principle applies to freeborn children.

Children at this age have natural ways of playing that they discover for themselves when they get together. All children between three and six should meet at the village temples, with the families of each village gathering in one place. The nurses must make sure the children behave properly and in an orderly way. The nurses and their groups will be supervised by twelve matrons — one for each group — chosen annually from the women we previously mentioned, the ones who oversee marriage. These matrons will be selected by those marriage-supervisory women, one from each tribe, all of the same age. As soon as each matron is appointed, she'll take up her duties and go to the temples every day, punishing any offenders — slaves or foreigners — with the help of public servants. If any citizen disputes the punishment, she'll bring the case before the city wardens. If there's no dispute, she can handle the punishment herself.

After age six, it's time to separate the sexes — boys with boys, girls with girls. Now the real learning begins. Boys go to teachers of horsemanship, archery, javelin-throwing, and the sling. Girls too — at the very least until they know how to handle these weapons, and especially how to manage heavy armor. Because let me point out something: the way things are done now is almost universally misguided.

Cleinias: In what way?

Athenian: People assume that the right hand and the left hand are naturally suited for different tasks. But when it comes to our feet and legs, no one sees any difference. The truth is, we're essentially crippled in our hands by the foolishness of nurses and mothers. Our limbs are naturally balanced, but we create an artificial difference through bad habits. In some cases this doesn't matter much — for example, when we hold the lyre in the left hand and the plectrum in the right. But in other cases, it's pure foolishness to maintain this distinction.

The Scythians prove our error: they hold the bow out with the left hand and draw the arrow with the right, but they can use either hand for both. And there are many similar examples in chariot-driving and other activities, showing that people who make the left side weaker than the right are working against nature. With something like a horn plectrum or similar instruments, it doesn't really matter. But it makes a huge difference when a warrior has to use iron weapons — bows, javelins, and the like — and especially when fighting in heavy armor against other heavily-armed opponents. There's an enormous gap between someone who's been trained and someone who hasn't, between someone who's practiced gymnastics and someone who hasn't.

Think of it this way: a person who's perfected the Pancratium, or boxing, or wrestling isn't helpless when fighting from the left side. They don't limp and stumble in confusion when their opponent forces them to switch positions. The same principle should hold for heavy-armed combat and everything else. Someone with the ability to attack and defend from both sides should never leave either capability unused or untrained. If a person had the nature of Geryon or Briareus, they ought to be able to throw a hundred darts with their hundred hands!

Now, the magistrates — both men and women — should oversee all of this. The women will supervise the nursing and play of the children, and the men will supervise their education. The goal is that all children, boys and girls alike, should be sound in hand and foot, and shouldn't — if it can be helped — ruin nature's gifts through bad habits.

Education has two branches: gymnastics, which is concerned with the body, and music, which is designed to improve the soul. Gymnastics itself has two branches: dancing and wrestling. One kind of dancing imitates musical recitation and aims at preserving dignity and grace. The other aims at producing health, agility, and beauty in the body's limbs, giving each the proper range of movement — a harmonious motion flowing through everything, forming a natural accompaniment to the dance.

As for wrestling: the tricks that Antaeus and Cercyon invented in their systems, born from petty competitiveness, or the boxing tricks devised by Epeius or Amycus — these are useless for war and don't deserve much discussion. But the art of wrestling while standing upright, keeping the neck, hands, and sides free, working with energy and determination, with controlled strength, for the sake of health — that's always useful and shouldn't be neglected. When we reach that part of our legislation, we'll require both teachers to give instruction freely and students to receive it gratefully.

We also shouldn't leave out war dances from our choruses. Here in Crete, you have the armed dances of the Curetes, and in Sparta, the dances of the Dioscuri. And our own patron goddess Athena, who delights in dance, didn't think it was fitting to dance with empty hands. She put on a full suit of armor and danced in that attire. Our young men and women should imitate her in every way, honoring the goddess, both for the demands of war and for festive occasions.

It'll also be right for the boys, until they reach military age, to march in processions and offer prayers to all the gods — armed, on horseback, in fast and slow formations, performing dances and marches. They should offer prayers to the gods and the sons of gods, and engage in contests and practice bouts with exactly this purpose. These kinds of exercises, and no others, are useful in both peace and war, and beneficial to states and private households alike. Other physical labors, sports, and exercises are unworthy of free citizens, Megillus and Cleinias.

I've now described the kind of physical training I said at the start should be covered. If you know of anything better, please share your thoughts.

Cleinias: It's not easy, Stranger, to set aside these principles of gymnastics and wrestling and propose better ones.

Athenian: Now we need to turn to what still needs to be said about the gifts of the Muses and of Apollo. Earlier, we thought we'd covered everything and only gymnastics remained. But now we can see clearly what points were left out and should be addressed first. So let's discuss them.

Cleinias: By all means.

Athenian: Let me tell you once more — though you've heard me say this before — that extreme caution is needed, both from the speaker and the listener, when dealing with anything that's really unusual and out of the ordinary. Because what I'm about to say is the kind of thing many people would be afraid to put forward. And yet I have a confidence that pushes me ahead.

Cleinias: What do you have to say, Stranger?

Athenian: Here's what I'm saying: in states generally, no one has noticed that children's games have a great deal to do with whether laws remain stable or fall apart. When games are set up so that children always play the same games, entertain themselves the same way, and take delight in the same toys, then the more serious institutions of the state are left undisturbed. But when games are constantly disrupted and changed, when young people never agree about what they like, when they don't have any settled sense of what's tasteful or vulgar — whether in how they carry themselves or how they dress — and when whoever invents something new and unusual in style and appearance is given special honor: well, I'd say there's no greater disaster that can befall a state.

The person who changes children's games is secretly changing the character of the young — training them to despise old traditions and embrace new ones. And I'm telling you, there's nothing more damaging to a society than this kind of talk and this kind of thinking. Do you want to hear just how serious I think this problem is?

Cleinias: You mean the problem of disrespecting tradition in a state?

Athenian: Exactly.

Cleinias: On that subject, you'll find us very receptive listeners — more than receptive, in fact.

Athenian: I'd expect so.

Cleinias: Go on.

Athenian: Well then, let's pay especially close attention to each other's words here. The argument is this: any change whatsoever, except from what's bad, is the most dangerous of all things. This is true of the changing of seasons, of winds, of bodily habits, of the habits of the mind — true of everything, except, as I said, what's bad. Consider someone whose body has adapted to eating any kind of food, drinking any kind of drink, doing any kind of work: at first, these things cause disorder, but over time, the body adapts. The person learns to appreciate variety, and enjoys good health and a pleasant life. But if afterward they're restricted to a superior diet, they're initially disturbed by the change and have difficulty adjusting to the new food.

We can imagine the same principle applies to people's minds and souls. When they've been raised under certain laws that — by some divine providence — have remained unchanged for long ages, so that no one has any memory or tradition of them ever being different, then everyone is afraid and ashamed to change what's established.

The legislator needs to find some way to instill this reverence for tradition, and here's what I'd propose. People tend to think — as I was saying — that when children's games change, they're just games. They don't see the serious and damaging consequences that follow. They go along with whatever children want instead of discouraging them, never considering that these children who innovate in their games will grow up to be different adults from the previous generation. Being different, they'll want a different kind of life. Under that desire, they'll want different institutions and different laws. And none of them stops to think that what follows is what I just called the greatest of evils for a state. Changes in outward fashion aren't such a serious matter. But constant changes in what a society praises and blames in conduct — those are the greatest of evils, and require the utmost foresight.

Cleinias: To be sure.

Athenian: And do we still hold to our earlier claim that rhythms and music in general are imitations of good and evil characters in people? What do you say?

Cleinias: That's the only position we can take.

Athenian: Then we must try by every means possible to prevent our young people from wanting to imitate new styles in either dance or song. And no one should be allowed to tempt them with novel pleasures.

Cleinias: Very true.
