Athenian: Can any of us think of a better way to accomplish this than the method of the Egyptians?

Cleinias: What is their method?

Athenian: To make every kind of dance and melody sacred. First, we should establish festivals — calculating what the year requires, at what times, and in honor of which gods, sons of gods, and heroes they should be celebrated. Next, we should determine which hymns should be sung at each sacrifice, and which dances should accompany each festival. This should be arranged initially by designated officials, and once arranged, the entire citizen body should offer sacrifices and libations to the Fates and all the other gods, and consecrate the various songs to the appropriate deities and heroes.

If anyone introduces different hymns or dances to any of the gods, the priests and priestesses, working together with the guardians of the law, should exclude that person — with the backing of both religion and law. If the person refuses to submit, they'll be liable for the rest of their life to a charge of impiety, which anyone may bring against them.

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: While we're on this subject, let's remember what we owe to ourselves.

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: I mean this: when any person, young or old, encounters something strange or unfamiliar, they shouldn't immediately rush to embrace the new thing. Instead, they should stand and think it over, like someone who's reached a fork where three roads meet and doesn't quite know which way to go. Whether alone or with others, they should say to themselves: "Which is the right path?" — and not move forward until they're satisfied they're going the right way.

That's what we need to do right now. A strange discussion about law has come up that requires the most careful consideration. At our age, we shouldn't be too quick to speak about such weighty matters, or pretend we can settle them instantly.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: Then let's allow ourselves time for reflection, and decide after we've given the subject enough thought. But in the meantime, so we don't get sidetracked from completing the natural order of our laws, let's proceed to the end of them in due order. If God is willing, perhaps the whole exposition, once complete, will shed light on our current difficulty.

Cleinias: Excellent, Stranger. Let's do as you suggest.

Athenian: Then let's accept this paradox: musical compositions are our laws. In fact, the ancients used the same word — "nomoi" — for both lyric songs and laws, so they probably wouldn't have objected much to the way we're using the term. Someone, whether dreaming or waking, must have had an intuition about their true nature.

So let our decree be this: no one, in singing or dancing, shall violate the publicly established and consecrated standards — the general musical customs of our society — any more than they'd violate any other law. Whoever observes this law will face no penalty. But whoever disobeys, as I said, will be punished by the guardians of the law and by the priests and priestesses. Let's consider this our law.

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: Now, can anyone who enacts such a law avoid looking ridiculous? Let's think about this. I believe our only protection will be to first establish certain standards for composers. Here's one of those standards: imagine a sacrifice is taking place, the offerings are burning on the altar according to custom — and then someone standing next to the altar, a son or brother of the celebrant, starts pouring out horrible blasphemies. Wouldn't his words fill his father and relatives with dread and dark forebodings?

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: And yet this is exactly what happens in almost all our cities. A magistrate performs a public sacrifice, and then not one but many choruses show up, take their positions near the altar, and proceed to pour out every sort of awful lamentation over the sacred rites, stirring the audience's souls with the most sorrowful words, rhythms, and melodies imaginable. And the one who makes the citizens weep the most at the very moment the city is offering sacrifice — he takes the prize!

Now, shouldn't we ban music like this? And if our citizens ever do need to hear such laments, then let it be on some unlucky, inauspicious day, with choruses of foreign hired performers — like those professionals who accompany funerals with harsh Carian dirges. That's the sort of setting that would be appropriate for such music, if we allow it at all. And let the singers' costumes not be garlands and gold ornaments, but the opposite.

Enough of all that. Let me simply ask one more time whether we'll adopt this as one of our principles for music —

Cleinias: What principle?

Athenian: That we should avoid every word of ill omen, and that songs of good omen should be heard everywhere and always in our state. I barely need to ask — I'll assume you agree with me.

Cleinias: Absolutely. That law has the full approval of all of us.

Athenian: And what should our next musical regulation be? Shouldn't prayers be offered to the gods whenever we sacrifice?

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And our third rule, if I'm not mistaken, should be this: our poets, understanding that prayers are requests we make to the gods, must take special care not to accidentally ask for bad things instead of good ones. Making a prayer like that would be truly absurd.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: Weren't we convinced just a little while ago that no golden or silver Plutus — no god of wealth — should have a place in our state?

Cleinias: We were indeed.

Athenian: And what has our whole argument been trying to show? Isn't it that poets aren't always capable of knowing what's truly good or evil? If a poet composes a misguided prayer in song or verse, he'll lead our citizens to pray for the exact opposite of what's good — on matters of the highest importance. As I was saying, there can be few bigger mistakes than that. So shall we propose this as one of our laws and standards for the Muses —

Cleinias: What exactly? Please spell out the law more precisely.

Athenian: The law shall be this: no poet may compose anything contrary to the city's accepted standards of what is lawful, just, beautiful, or good. No poet shall be allowed to share their compositions with anyone until they've been shown to the appointed judges and the guardians of the law, and those officials are satisfied with them. We've already indicated who should serve as our legislators for music and as the director of education. So, once more — as I've asked repeatedly — shall this be our third law, standard, and model? What do you say?

Cleinias: Let it be so, by all means.

Athenian: After this, it'll be appropriate to have hymns and praises to the gods, mixed with prayers. And following the gods, prayers and praises should be offered in the same way to demigods and heroes, each suited to their character.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: Next, there should be no objection to a law honoring citizens who have died and who performed good and heroic deeds — whether of the soul or the body — and who were obedient to the laws. It would be very fitting for them to receive public praise.

Cleinias: Quite true.

Athenian: But honoring living people with hymns and panegyrics is a risky business. A person should run their course and reach a worthy end before we praise them. And praise should be given equally to women and to men who've distinguished themselves through virtue.

Here's how songs and dances should be organized. There are many excellent ancient musical compositions and dances, and the newly founded city may freely choose from among them whatever is proper and suitable. Judges of not less than fifty years of age shall make the selection. Any of the old works they consider adequate, they'll include. Anything deficient or completely unsuitable, they'll either reject entirely or revise and improve, calling on poets and musicians for assistance and making use of their creative talent — but always explaining the legislator's wishes, so that the artists adjust their dancing, music, and choral works according to the judges' vision, not their own personal tastes and whims.

Now, irregular, undisciplined music always becomes immeasurably better when it acquires order and structure — when it rejects the sweet, honeyed Muse — though I don't mean we should eliminate pleasure entirely, since pleasure is the very nature of all music. The point is this: if a person is raised from childhood to maturity on orderly, disciplined music, they'll detest the opposite kind when they hear it and call it vulgar. But if they're trained on sweet, popular music, they'll find the more disciplined kind cold and unpleasant. So, as I was saying, neither kind gives more pleasure than the other to someone who hears them — the advantage of one over the other is simply that the disciplined kind makes people better, while the other makes them worse.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: We also need to distinguish, on some general principle, which songs are appropriate for women and which for men, and assign the proper melodies and rhythms to each. It would be terrible for a whole piece to be musically incoherent — rhythmically or harmonically off — and this is what happens when the melody doesn't match its subject. So the legislator must set guidelines for these things too. Both sexes have melodies and rhythms that naturally belong to them, and the difference is clearly indicated by their natural character. What's grand and tends toward courage we can fairly call manly. What inclines toward moderation and temperance — both in law and in common speech — can be called the more womanly quality. That'll be our general framework.

Now let's talk about how these things should be taught, to whom, and when. Like a shipwright who first lays down the keel and from there sketches the outline of the whole ship, I'm trying to map out the patterns of life according to the different natures of people's souls — genuinely asking how, and by what means, we can best navigate this voyage of life.

Now, human affairs are hardly worth taking seriously — and yet we must take them seriously. It's a sad necessity. And having come this far, it makes sense to finish the job if we can find a suitable approach. But what do I mean by that? Someone might well ask, and rightly so.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: What I mean is this: serious matters deserve serious attention, but trivial matters don't. God is the natural and proper focus of our deepest seriousness and devotion. But humanity — as I said before — was made to be God's plaything, and this, rightly understood, is actually the best thing about us. Every man and woman should therefore live accordingly, spending their lives in the noblest forms of play, with a completely different mindset from the one that prevails today.

Cleinias: In what way different?

Athenian: Right now, people think their serious activities should serve their recreation — they view war as a serious pursuit, something that must be managed well for the sake of the peace that follows. But the truth is that there never was, never is, and never will be any real amusement or meaningful instruction in war — which, all the same, we treat as the most serious thing we do. What I'm saying is this: every one of us should live the life of peace as long and as well as they can.

And what's the right way to live? Should we spend our whole lives in play? If so, what kind of play? We should live our lives making sacrifices, singing, and dancing. Through these, a person can win the favor of the gods, defend against enemies, and conquer them in battle. The kind of songs and dances that will accomplish this has already been described, and the paths have been laid out. We should go forward in the spirit of the poet:

"Telemachus, some things you'll discover in your own heart, but other things the gods will suggest to you; for I believe you were not born and raised without the will of the gods."

And this ought to be the outlook of our young citizens. They should trust that what we've told them is enough, and that their own divine inspiration will guide them in what remains — telling them to whom, and when, and to which gods they should offer sacrifices and dances, winning the gods' favor and living according to nature. Humans are, for the most part, puppets — though we do possess some small share of genuine reality.

Megillus: You have a pretty low opinion of the human race, Stranger.

Athenian: Don't be too surprised, Megillus — forgive me. I was comparing us with the gods, and that's what moved me to speak that way. All right, let's grant it: the human race isn't to be despised, but deserves some consideration.

Moving on — next come the buildings for gymnasiums and schools, all open to the public. These should be placed in three locations in the center of the city. Outside the city and in the surrounding countryside, also in three locations, there should be grounds for horse-riding and spacious fields designed for archery and javelin practice, where young people can learn and train. We've already mentioned these, but if the earlier description wasn't detailed enough, let's fill it in now and set it in law.

In each of these schools, there should be living quarters for teachers, who'll be brought in from abroad and paid by the state. They'll teach their students the arts of war and of music. Children shall attend whether their parents want them to or not — there will be compulsory education, as the saying goes, for absolutely everyone, as far as it's possible. The students shall be regarded as belonging to the state rather than to their parents.

My law would apply to girls exactly as it does to boys. Both shall go through the same exercises. I say without any fear of contradiction that gymnastics and horsemanship are as suitable for women as for men. I'm convinced of this by ancient tradition, and in our own time, there are said to be vast numbers of women near the Black Sea — the Sauromatian women — who not only ride on horseback like men but are required to use bows and other weapons just as men do.

And let me go further: if these things are possible, then nothing could be more absurd than what happens in our own societies, where men and women don't pursue the same activities with all their strength and with a shared purpose. A state that does this cuts itself in half instead of being whole — and yet still has the same taxes to pay and the same burdens to bear. What bigger mistake could any lawgiver make?

Cleinias: Very true. And yet much of what you've said, Stranger, goes against what's customary in most states. Still, you spoke well when you said we should let the discussion run its full course, and then choose what seems best. I feel some regret for challenging you earlier. So tell us — what would you like to say next?

Athenian: Just what I said before, Cleinias: if the possibility of these things weren't sufficiently proven by actual evidence, then there might be grounds for objecting. But the evidence is as I've described it, so anyone who rejects this law will have to find some other reason to object. Failing that, our recommendation stands. No one will deny that women should share as fully as possible in education and everything else along with men. Think about it this way: if women don't share in men's way of life entirely, then they must have some other way of life altogether.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And what alternative arrangement can we find anywhere that's better than this partnership we're proposing? Shall we follow the Thracians and many other peoples who use their women to till the soil, herd their flocks, and serve them like slaves? Or shall we do as we do in our part of the world — gathering all our possessions into one household and handing them over to our women as stewards, who also preside over the spinning and weaving?

Or should we take a middle course, as in Sparta, Megillus? In Sparta, girls share in gymnastics and music. Grown women, no longer working at spinning wool, are busy weaving the web of life — no small or mean occupation — and they serve in caring for the household and raising the children. They take a kind of middle path, not participating in the toils of war. But if there were any real need for them to fight for their city and families — unlike the Amazons — they'd be unable to pick up a bow or any other weapon with skill, or to carry a shield or spear. They couldn't even stand their ground and fight nobly for their country when it was being destroyed, striking terror into their enemies simply by being seen in organized formation.

Living the way they do, they'd never dare to imitate the Sauromatian women, who compared to ordinary women would look like men. Let whoever wants to praise your lawmakers do so — but I have to say what I think. The lawgiver ought to be complete and whole, not half a craftsman. He shouldn't let the female sex live softly, spend money freely, and have no structure to their lives while he takes the greatest care of the male sex. That leaves half the state's life blessed with happiness, when he could have made the whole state happy.

Megillus: What should we do, Cleinias? Are we going to let this stranger criticize Sparta like this?

Cleinias: Yes. We've granted him freedom of speech, and we should let him continue until we've completed our work of legislation.

Megillus: Fair enough.

Athenian: Then may I continue?

Cleinias: By all means.

Athenian

What will daily life look like for people whose food and clothing are provided in moderation, who've handed the practice of crafts over to others, and whose farmland — worked by slaves who pay a share of the harvest — brings in enough for a moderate life? Imagine they have common dining halls, with the men eating separately, and nearby, common tables for their families — their daughters and mothers. Each day, male and female officers inspect the diners, observe the company's behavior, and then dismiss them. Afterward, the presiding magistrate and attendants pour libations to whichever gods that day and night are sacred to, and then everyone goes home.

Now, for people whose lives are arranged this way — is there really no important work left to be done? Should each of them just live like a fattened animal? That's neither just nor honorable, and a person who lives like that won't escape the consequences they deserve. The proper fate of an idle, fattened beast is to be torn apart by some other brave animal whose body has been hardened by courageous deeds and honest toil.

Of course, these arrangements will never be perfectly carried out under present circumstances — not as long as women, children, houses, and everything else remain private property. But if we can achieve the second-best form of government, we'll be doing very well. And for people living under this second-best system, there's still a task to accomplish that's far from small or trivial. In fact, it's the greatest task of all, ordained by righteous law.

Here's what I mean: the life that's truly devoted to excellence of both body and soul requires twice — or more than twice — the effort that goes into training for the Pythian or Olympic Games, which already demands a person's full time and attention. There should be no side jobs interfering with the essential work of providing proper exercise and nourishment for the body, and instruction and education for the soul. Day and night aren't long enough to accomplish all of this perfectly. Therefore, all free citizens should organize every hour of their day — from morning to evening and from evening to the next sunrise.

Now, there might seem to be something improper about a legislator dictating the countless details of household management — including something as specific as the duty of staying awake for those who are to be permanent guardians of the whole city. The idea that any citizen should sleep through an entire night instead of being seen by all his servants as the first to wake and rise — whether we call this a law or just a standard of behavior — should be considered disgraceful and unworthy of a free person. Likewise, the idea that the mistress of the house should be awakened by her maids rather than waking them first — this is the kind of thing that every slave, servant, and helper in the house should view as shameful.

If they all rise early, they can get a great deal of both public and private business done — magistrates managing city affairs, and masters and mistresses managing their households — before the sun even comes up. Lots of sleep isn't what nature really requires, either for our souls, our bodies, or the work they do. No one who's asleep is good for anything — they might as well be dead. The person among us who values life and reason most will stay awake as long as possible, setting aside only as much time for sleep as health requires. And that's not much, once the habit of moderation is properly established. Magistrates who keep watch at night are feared by the wicked — whether enemies or their own citizens — and honored and respected by the just and temperate. They're useful to themselves and to the entire state.

A night spent this way, in addition to all the advantages I've mentioned, also instills a kind of courage in the citizens' minds.

When day breaks, it's time for young people to go to their teachers. Now, no animal — not sheep, not any creature — can survive without a shepherd, and children can't be left without tutors, any more than slaves can manage without masters. And of all animals, the boy is the most difficult to handle. The fountain of reason in him isn't yet regulated, making him the most cunning, sharp-witted, and unruly of creatures. He needs to be restrained with many bridles.

First, when he leaves the care of his mother and nurses, he needs tutors to manage him on account of his childishness and foolishness. Then, since he's a free citizen, he needs teachers to instruct him in various subjects. But he's also, in a sense, under the authority of everyone: any free person who comes across him may correct both the boy and his tutor or teacher if any of them is doing something wrong. And whoever encounters such wrongdoing and fails to punish it as it deserves will earn the greatest disgrace. The guardian of the law who serves as director of education should keep a sharp lookout for anyone who encounters these offenses and fails to correct them properly. He should take special care with the training of our children, guiding their natures and always steering them toward what's good, as the law directs.

But how can our law adequately train the director of education himself? So far, everything we've said has been incomplete — nothing has been stated clearly or satisfactorily on this point. Now, as far as possible, the law should leave nothing to his personal discretion but should explain everything, so that he can serve as an interpreter and guide to others.

About dances, music, and choral works, I've already spoken — both about how they should be selected and how they should be revised and consecrated. But we haven't yet addressed, my distinguished director of education, what your students should study in prose. You've been told what military music they should learn and practice. But as for reading and writing, playing the lyre, arithmetic — which, as we were saying, everyone needs to learn — and other subjects required for warfare and the management of households and cities; and beyond that, what's useful to know about the motions of the heavenly bodies — the stars, the sun, and the moon — and the various rules about these matters that the whole state needs for organizing days into months and months into years, so that seasons, sacrifices, and festivals maintain their proper, natural order, keeping the city alert and active, the gods receiving their due honors, and people gaining a better understanding of these things — all of this, my friend, has not yet been adequately explained to you by the legislator.

So pay attention to what I'm about to say. We were telling you that your education in reading and writing was incomplete. The objection was this: you were never told whether a citizen who aspires to be respectable should study these things in detail or not study them at all. The same was true of learning the lyre. But now we're saying: yes, he should study them.

A fair amount of time for a boy of ten to spend on reading and writing is three years. At thirteen, it's the proper time for him to start learning the lyre, and he should continue for another three years — no more, no less. Whether the boy or his father likes or dislikes the subject, he's not to be allowed to spend more or less time on music than the law permits. Anyone who disobeys will be denied the youthful honors we'll describe later.

But first, listen to what young people should actually learn in these early years, and what their teachers should teach them. They should study reading and writing until they can read and write competently. But if nature hasn't given them the ability to achieve perfect handwriting or great speed within the allotted years, let it go. No need to force it.

Now, what about written compositions that aren't set to music — prose works, whether in verse without musical accompaniment or in plain prose with no rhythm or melody at all? We've inherited a great many writings from authors of this kind, and some of them are quite problematic. What are we going to do with them, my excellent guardians of the law? How can the lawgiver properly guide you here? I think he'll find himself in considerable difficulty.

Cleinias: What's troubling you, Stranger? Why are you so perplexed?

Athenian: A fair question, Cleinias. To you and Megillus, my partners in this work of legislation, I need to lay out both the harder parts and the easier parts of the task.

Cleinias: What are you getting at in this case?

Athenian: I'll tell you. The problem is opposing the opinion of countless people.

Cleinias: But haven't we already gone against popular opinion on many important matters?

Athenian: That's quite true. And what you're implying is that the path we're taking may be unpopular with some, but is welcome to just as many others — or if not as many, then at least to people who are no less worthy. You're telling me to press forward along this legislative road that our discussion has opened up, to take heart and not falter.

Cleinias: Exactly.

Athenian: And I won't falter. Here's the thing: we have a great many poets writing in hexameter, trimeter, and every other meter — some serious, some just trying to get a laugh. And the universal opinion is that young people who receive a proper education should be immersed in this poetry, saturated with it. Some people insist they should be constantly hearing it read aloud, always memorizing it, learning entire poets by heart. Others select the best passages and long speeches and compile anthologies, saying these should be committed to memory if a person is to become good and wise through broad learning.

And now you want me to tell all these people plainly where they're right and where they're wrong.

Cleinias: Yes, I do.

Athenian: But how can I sum up my judgment on all of them in a single statement? I think — and if I'm not mistaken, there's general agreement — that every one of these poets has written some things well and some things badly. And if that's true, then I maintain that too much of this kind of learning is actually dangerous for young people.

Cleinias: So how would you advise the guardian of the law to act?

Athenian: In what respect?

Cleinias: What standard should he follow when deciding what the young should learn and what they should be kept from learning? Don't hold back — just tell us.

Athenian: Well, my good Cleinias, I think I'm actually rather fortunate.

Cleinias: How so?

Athenian: I'm not entirely without a standard to offer. When I think back over the words we've spoken from early this morning until now — words that I believe have been inspired by heaven — they seem to me very much like a poem. When I reflected on everything we've said, I felt a natural pleasure, because of all the discourses I've ever learned or heard, whether in poetry or prose, this one seemed to me the most balanced, the most fitting for young people to hear. I can't imagine a better standard than this for the guardian of the law who also serves as director of education.

He can't do better than to instruct the teachers to teach young people these very words, and anything else of a similar nature he might find — whether in poetry, in prose, or even in unwritten discussions that share our spirit. He should make sure to preserve such works and commit them to writing. First, he should require the teachers themselves to learn and approve of these materials. Any teacher who won't should be dismissed. Only those who share his judgment should be employed and entrusted with the instruction and education of our youth.

And with that, let my little digression about reading, writing, and their teachers come to an end.

Cleinias: I don't think, Stranger, that we've strayed outside the proper scope of our discussion. But whether our whole approach is right, I can't say with complete certainty.

Athenian: The truth, Cleinias, will probably become clearer when — as we've said many times — we reach the end of our entire discussion about laws.

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: Now that we've dealt with the teacher of reading and writing, it's time to give our instructions to the teacher of the lyre.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: I think we only need to recall our earlier discussions to give suitable guidelines for this part of instruction and education.

Cleinias: What are you referring to?

Athenian: We were saying, if I remember correctly, that the sixty-year-old members of the chorus of Dionysus should have an especially keen sense of rhythm and musical composition, so they can distinguish between good and bad imitation — that is, imitation of good character versus bad character when under the influence of strong feeling. They should reject the one and display the other in hymns and songs, using them to charm young people's souls and invite them to pursue virtue through the power of imitation.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: With this in mind, the teacher and the student should use the sounds of the lyre because its notes are pure. The player and the student should stay in unison, rendering note for note together. But complexity and variation — where the strings produce one sound while the poet or composer creates another, or where they combine smaller and larger intervals, fast and slow tempos, high and low notes into elaborate harmonies — or again, where they create complex rhythmic variations layered over the lyre's notes — all of that is not suitable for students who need to gain a practical and useful knowledge of music in three years. Mixing opposite principles creates confusion and makes learning difficult. And our young people need to learn quickly — their essential studies are neither few nor trivial, as we'll show in due course.

Let the director of education follow the musical principles we're laying down. As for the songs and lyrics themselves that the chorus masters should teach — their content and character — we've already covered that. They're the same ones which, once consecrated and adapted to the different festivals, we said would benefit the cities by providing wholesome enjoyment.

Cleinias: That too is true.

Athenian: Then let whoever has been elected as director of music take these rules from us as the genuine truth, and may he succeed in his duties! Now let's move on to lay down additional rules beyond the ones already given, about dancing and physical training in general. We've covered what remained to be said about music education — so let's address gymnastics in the same way. Boys and girls both should learn to dance and do gymnastic exercises, shouldn't they?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: Then boys should have male dance instructors and girls should have female dance instructors to train them.

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: Then let's call in once again the person with the greatest responsibility here — the superintendent of youth, that is, the director of education. He'll have plenty on his plate, being in charge of both music and gymnastics.

Cleinias: But how will an elderly man manage such enormous responsibilities?

Athenian: Oh, don't worry, my friend. The law has already given — and will continue to give — him permission to choose any citizens, male or female, as his assistants. He'll know whom to choose, and he'll be careful not to make a mistake, both from a proper sense of responsibility and from awareness of how important his role is. He'll understand that if young people have been well brought up, everything goes smoothly. But if not — well, it's not fitting to describe what happens, and we won't, in case we seem to be speaking ill omens over our infant state.

We've already said a great deal about dancing and general physical movements — since we include under gymnastics all military exercises: archery, throwing weapons, using the light shield, all heavy-armed combat, military formations, troop movements, and encampment, plus everything related to horsemanship. There should be public teachers for all of these, paid by the state, and their students should include men and boys, as well as girls and women, in the entire state.

While they're still girls, they should practice armed dancing and the full art of fighting. Once they're grown women, they should train in military formations, tactics, and the proper way to take up and set down arms — if for no other reason, then in case the entire army has to march out of the city for a campaign, so that the women left behind are capable of guarding the children and the rest of the city. And on the other hand, if enemies — whether foreign or Greek — should attack with overwhelming force and push the fighting right to the city itself (which is far from impossible), what a tremendous disgrace it would be if the women had been so poorly trained that they couldn't fight for their children the way birds fight for their young against any predator, no matter how strong — ready to die or face any danger. Instead, what would they do? Rush straight to the temples, crowd the altars and shrines, and bring upon the entire human race the reproach that of all animals, we're the most cowardly!

Cleinias: Such a lack of training, Stranger, would certainly be both shameful and disastrous for any state.

Athenian: Then suppose we go so far as to make it law that women shall not neglect military training, and that all citizens — male and female alike — shall attend to it?

Cleinias: I fully agree.

Athenian: Now, about wrestling — we've discussed it partly, but I'd say the most important part hasn't been covered. And it's not easy to explain without demonstrating the movements physically, combining words with actions. Only when word and gesture come together will we be able to explain clearly that, of all forms of physical training, wrestling is the one most closely related to the military arts. Wrestling should be pursued for the sake of warfare — not warfare for the sake of wrestling.

Cleinias: Excellent.

Athenian: Enough about wrestling. Let's move on to other forms of bodily movement. This kind of motion can generally be called dancing, and it falls into two categories: one that imitates noble figures, and another that imitates base ones. Each of these has two further subdivisions.

Of the serious kind: one form involves people engaged in war and intense action — it's the exercise of a noble person with a courageous spirit. The other depicts a moderate soul enjoying prosperity and modest pleasures — this can truly be called the dance of peace.

The warrior dance is different from the peaceful one and can rightly be called the Pyrrhic. It imitates the ways of avoiding blows and missiles — ducking, giving way, springing aside, rising up, or dropping down. It also imitates the opposite: attacking postures, like the motions of archery, javelin-throwing, and delivering all sorts of strikes. When the imitation is of brave bodies and courageous souls, and the movement is direct and muscular — giving the limbs a straight, strong motion — that's the true form. But the opposite is wrong.

In the dance of peace, what we need to consider is whether a person moves naturally and gracefully, in the manner of people who properly follow the law.

But before going further, I need to distinguish the type of dancing that's in question from the type that isn't. Which kind is doubtful, and how do we tell them apart?

There are Bacchic-type dances — the kind where they say they're imitating drunken men, named after the Nymphs, Pan, Silenus, and the Satyrs. There are also the dances used in purification rites and mystery ceremonies. This whole category of dancing can't properly be classified as either warlike or peaceful — it doesn't really have a clear meaning at all. I think the best thing is to say it's distinct from both the war dance and the peace dance, and not suitable for a city. So let's set it aside.

Leaving that where it lies, let's turn to the dances of war and peace — those are undoubtedly our concern. The peaceful, non-warlike muse that honors the gods and the sons of gods in dance is entirely associated with a sense of well-being and prosperity. This type can be split into two subtypes: one expresses relief — an escape from some hardship or danger into safety, bringing greater pleasure; the other expresses the preservation and increase of existing blessings, with a less intense pleasure.

In all these cases, the greater the pleasure, the more a person moves their body; the less the pleasure, the less they move. Furthermore, if someone is more disciplined and has learned self-control through training, they move less. But if they're a coward with no training or restraint, they make bigger, more violent movements. And in general, when a person is speaking or singing, they can't keep their body completely still — and so from the imitation of words through gestures, the entire art of dancing was born.

In these various kinds of imitation, some people move in an orderly way, others in a disorderly way. And the ancients, who gave many excellent names to things according to their true nature, gave a truly fine name to the dances of people who are moderate in their pleasures during times of prosperity. They called these dances "Emmeleiai" — dances of order. With this, they established two kinds of noble dance: the war dance, which they called the Pyrrhic, and the peace dance, which they called the Emmeleia, the dance of order. Each received its fitting and proper name.

The legislator should outline these categories in general terms. The guardian of the law should then investigate them in detail, combining dancing with music and assigning to each sacrificial festival what's appropriate. Once he's consecrated everything in proper order, he should make no further changes — whether to the dances or the songs. From that point on, the city and its citizens will continue to enjoy the same pleasures, being as alike as possible in their tastes, and will live well and happily.

I've now described the dances appropriate to noble bodies and generous souls. But we also need to consider and understand the opposite: ignoble characters and thoughts — the kind meant to produce laughter in comedy, with a comic character in their style, song, and dance. Here's the thing: serious matters can't be understood without laughable ones. You can't understand any quality without understanding its opposite — not if you really want to grasp either one. But you can't practice both if you want to have any degree of virtue.

This is exactly why people should learn about comic imitation: so they can avoid doing or saying anything ridiculous or inappropriate out of ignorance. But the actual performance of such things should be left to slaves and hired foreigners. No one should ever take a serious interest in performing comedy, and no free man or woman should be caught making an effort to learn it. There should always be some element of novelty in the comic imitation.

So let these be our regulations — both legal and advisory — for the laughable entertainment we generally call comedy.

Now, what if the serious poets — the ones who write tragedy — come to us and say, "Strangers, may we come to your city and country? May we bring our poetry with us? What's your decision?"

How shall we answer these divine artists? I think our answer should go like this: "Most excellent strangers," we'll say, "we too are poets of a kind — tragic poets, in fact. And our tragedy is the finest and noblest. Our entire state is itself an imitation of the best and noblest life, which we believe is the truest tragedy of all. You are poets, and so are we — creators of the same art, rivals and competitors in the noblest of dramas, the kind that only true law can bring to perfection. So don't expect us to casually allow you to set up your stage in the marketplace, or to let your actors' fine voices ring out above our own, or to let you address our women and children and the general public about our institutions in language different from ours — and often the very opposite of ours. A state would have to be out of its mind to grant you that kind of license before its magistrates had reviewed your poetry and determined whether it was fit for public performance. So, sons and heirs of the gentler Muse: first show your songs to the magistrates. If they find your work is the same as ours, or better, we'll give you a chorus. But if not — well, my friends, we can't."

Let these, then, be the customs established by law regarding all forms of dance and their teaching. And let matters concerning slaves be kept separate from those concerning free citizens — if you don't object.

Cleinias: We can't possibly object when you put it that way.

Athenian: There are still three areas of study appropriate for free citizens. Arithmetic is one. The measurement of length, area, and depth is the second. The third concerns the motions of the stars in relation to one another.

Not everyone needs to work through all these subjects with strict scientific rigor — only a select few, and we'll say who they are later, at the proper point. But for everyone else, not knowing what's necessary for general human knowledge — not knowing the truth of these things — is a disgrace. And yet diving deep into the details isn't easy or even possible for everyone.

There's something in these subjects, though, that's genuinely necessary and can't be set aside. The person who first coined the proverb "not even God can fight against necessity" probably had this in mind — divine necessity, that is. Because when ordinary people use that phrase about mere human necessities, the application is completely ridiculous.

Cleinias: And what kind of necessary knowledge is divine rather than human, Stranger?

Athenian: The kind without which no one can be a god, or a demigod, or a hero who could genuinely guide humanity — or even take serious responsibility for human affairs. A divine being would hardly be one who couldn't count to three, or distinguish odd from even, or count at all, or reckon night from day, or who knew nothing about the motions of the sun, moon, and other heavenly bodies.

It would be absurd to imagine that any of this isn't essential knowledge for someone who aspires to the highest forms of understanding. But exactly which subjects, how many, and when they should be learned — and what should be studied together versus separately, and how they all relate to one another — all of this must be properly grasped first. Then, using these as guides, we can proceed to everything else. That's how natural necessity works — the kind we say no god fights against, or ever will.

Cleinias: I think, Stranger, that what you've just said is very true and consistent with nature.

Athenian: Yes, Cleinias, it is. But it's difficult for the legislator to begin with these subjects. We'll make detailed regulations for them at a more suitable time.

Cleinias: You seem to be worried about our habitual ignorance of these matters, Stranger. But that's no reason to hold back from speaking your mind.

Athenian: I'm certainly concerned about those difficulties. But I'm even more afraid of people who study these subjects badly. Complete ignorance isn't such a terrible or extreme evil, and it's far from the worst thing that can happen. Much worse is excessive cleverness and too much learning combined with a poor upbringing — that's far more destructive.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: Every free citizen should learn at least as much of these subjects as every child in Egypt is taught when learning the alphabet. In Egypt, they've invented arithmetic games for little children, which they learn as a pleasure and amusement. They have them distribute apples and garlands — using the same number for larger and smaller groups of people. They arrange boxers and wrestlers into pairs, whether matched by lot or left over from the draw, and show how their turns come in natural order. Another way they teach is by distributing cups — sometimes gold, sometimes brass or silver, sometimes mixed — and through this, they work the principles of everyday arithmetic into children's play. This makes students far more useful to themselves in practical life and far more alert. It also helps with measurements of length, width, and depth — freeing us from that natural ignorance of these things that's so laughable and embarrassing.

Cleinias: What kind of ignorance do you mean?

Athenian: Oh, my dear Cleinias — I, like you, only learned late in life just how ignorant we are about these matters. To me, we seem more like pigs than humans in this regard. I'm thoroughly ashamed — not just of myself, but of all Greeks.

Cleinias: About what? Tell us what you mean, Stranger.

Athenian: I will — or rather, I'll show you what I mean through a question. Please answer me: you know what length is?

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And breadth?

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: And you know that these are two distinct things, and that there's a third called depth?

Cleinias: Obviously.

Athenian: And doesn't it seem to you that all of these are measurable against each other?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: That is: length is naturally measurable against length, breadth against breadth, and depth against depth?

Cleinias: Undoubtedly.

Athenian: But what if some things are measurable against each other while others are completely incommensurable — and you believe they're all commensurable? Where does that leave you?

Cleinias: Clearly in a very bad position.

Athenian: Now consider: when we compare length and breadth with depth, or breadth and length with each other — don't all Greeks assume these can always be measured against one another?

Cleinias: Quite true.

Athenian: But if they're actually incommensurable in some cases, and yet all of us treat them as commensurable — don't we have reason to be ashamed of our fellow citizens? Couldn't we say to them: "You fine Greeks, isn't this exactly the kind of thing we were saying it's disgraceful not to know — and yet merely having a surface knowledge of it is nothing to be especially proud of?"

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And there are related matters where similar errors crop up.

Cleinias: What are they?

Athenian: The relationship between commensurable and incommensurable quantities. A person of any worth should be able to think through and distinguish these. Different people should challenge each other with these kinds of problems — it's a far better and more elegant way to spend their time than playing checkers as old men do.

Cleinias: I'd say you're right — and these mathematical challenges aren't so different from a game of checkers, in a way.

Athenian: Well, Cleinias, I maintain that these are the studies our young people should pursue. They're harmless, they're not difficult, and learning them will be enjoyable — and they'll benefit the state. If anyone disagrees, let them make their case.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: Then if these studies hold up to scrutiny, we'll include them. If not, we'll drop them.

Cleinias: Agreed. But can't we now, Stranger, formally prescribe these studies as required and fill in this gap in our laws?

Athenian: Let's consider them provisional pledges that can be taken back and removed from our system if they don't satisfy either us, the givers, or you, the recipients.

Cleinias: A fair condition.

Athenian: Next, let's consider whether we're willing to require our young people to study astronomy.

Cleinias: Go on.

Athenian: Here we run into something truly bizarre — something that absolutely cannot be tolerated.

Cleinias: What are you referring to?

Athenian: People say that we shouldn't investigate the nature of the supreme God or the workings of the universe, that we shouldn't busy ourselves searching out the causes of things, and that such inquiries are impious. But the exact opposite is true.

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: What I'm saying might seem paradoxical and out of step with what you'd expect from someone my age. But when a person has a good and true idea that benefits the state and is in every way acceptable to God, they can't keep quiet about it.

Cleinias: That sounds reasonable enough. But will we actually find any good and true ideas about the stars?

Athenian: My friends, at this moment, all of us Greeks are telling lies — if I can put it that way — about those great gods, the Sun and the Moon.

Cleinias: What kind of lies?

Athenian: We say that the sun, the moon, and various other heavenly bodies don't follow the same path — that they wander. We even call them "planets," which means "wanderers."

Cleinias: That's very true, Stranger. In the course of my own life, I've often seen the morning star, the evening star, and various others not following their usual courses but wandering all over the sky. And I've seen the sun and the moon doing what we all know they do.

Athenian: Exactly, Megillus and Cleinias. And I maintain that our citizens and our young people should learn enough about the heavenly gods to be able to offer sacrifices and pray to them in reverent language — and not blaspheme against them.

Cleinias: You're right about that — provided such knowledge is actually attainable. If we're currently speaking incorrectly and could be better informed and learn to use better language, then I fully agree that we should acquire enough knowledge to speak properly. So please, try to explain your whole meaning, and we'll do our best to follow you.

Athenian: There's some difficulty in understanding what I mean, but it's not enormous, and it won't take a great deal of time. Here's my proof: I didn't know these things long ago or in my youth, and yet I can explain them to you fairly quickly. If they were truly hard, I certainly couldn't explain all of them — old man to old men — in any reasonable time.

Cleinias: True. But what is this knowledge you describe as wonderful and fitting for young people to learn, though we're ignorant of it? Try to explain it as clearly as you can.

Athenian: I will. You see, my friends, the common belief about the "wandering" of the sun, the moon, and the other heavenly bodies is not the truth — it's the very opposite of the truth. Each of them follows a single path — not many paths, but one, which is circular. The apparent variety in their movements is just that: apparent.

What's more, we're wrong to think the fastest of them is the slowest, or the slowest is the fastest. Now, if what I'm saying is correct, just imagine we had the same confusion at the Olympic Games about runners. Suppose we called the fastest runner the slowest and the slowest the fastest, and praised the loser as if he were the winner. Our praise would be false, and it certainly wouldn't be welcome to the runners — even though they're only human. But to make the same mistake about the gods — what was merely ridiculous and wrong in the case of humans becomes something far worse, doesn't it?

Cleinias: Worse than ridiculous, I'd say.

Athenian: At the very least, the gods can't be pleased with us spreading a false account of them.

Cleinias: That's certainly true, if that's really what's happening.

Athenian: So if we can demonstrate that this is indeed the case, then all these astronomical matters should be learned to the extent necessary to avoid impiety. But if we can't demonstrate it, then they can be left alone. Let that be our decision.

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: That covers our laws about education and learning. But now we need to turn our attention to hunting and similar pursuits. The legislator's duty turns out to extend beyond mere law-making. There's something that lies in a region between advice and law, and it's come up repeatedly in our discussion — for example, in the education of very young children, where there are things that can't be precisely defined, and treating them as formal legislation would be absurd.

Now, with our laws and the whole framework of our state laid out, the praise of a virtuous citizen isn't complete when we describe them merely as someone who serves and obeys the laws better than anyone else. The higher praise describes the good citizen as someone who passes through their whole life uncorrupted, obedient to the words of the legislator — both when he prescribes laws and when he assigns praise and blame. This is the truest commendation a citizen can receive.

The real legislator should not only write laws but should also weave in everything he considers honorable and dishonorable. And the ideal citizen should strive to uphold these standards no less than the legal rules backed by punishments.

Let me give an example to make my meaning clear — a kind of evidence for what I'm saying. Hunting is a broad subject that covers many things. There's hunting creatures in the water, hunting creatures in the air, and a great deal of hunting land animals of every kind — and not just wild beasts. The hunting of human beings also deserves attention: there's hunting people in war, and there's often a hunting of people through friendship — which can be either praised or blamed. Then there's the "hunting" done by thieves and robbers, and that of armies against armies.

Now, the legislator laying down laws about hunting can neither ignore all these varieties nor create detailed threatening ordinances with rules and penalties covering every one of them. What's he supposed to do? He should praise and blame different kinds of hunting with an eye toward the exercise and development of young people. And the young person, in turn, should listen obediently. Neither pleasure nor pain should deter them from following the legislator's commendations and instructions rather than the penalties he imposes by law.

With that understood, here comes our moderate praise and censure of hunting — praising the kind that makes young people's souls better, censuring the kind that has the opposite effect.

So let's address our young people directly, as a kind of prayer for their well-being: "Dear friends," we'll say to them, "may no desire or passion for sea-fishing, angling, or catching water creatures ever take hold of you — whether you're awake or asleep — by hook or by net, that lazy method. And may no desire for capturing human beings — through piracy at sea — ever enter your souls and make you cruel, lawless hunters. As for the temptation of stealing in the city or countryside, may it never even cross your mind. And may the sneaky art of bird-catching, which is hardly worthy of free people, never appeal to any young person among you."

What remains for our citizens, then, is only the hunting of land animals. And here, the kind that involves sleeping in shifts while hunting by night — where the hunters are basically lazy — is not to be commended. Nor is the kind that relies on rest between bouts of activity, where the wild strength of beasts is overcome by nets and traps rather than by the victory of a hardworking spirit.

The only kind of hunting that's truly approved is the best kind: hunting four-footed animals with horses, dogs, and one's own body, where the hunters pursue their quarry by running it down, striking it, and hurling weapons at it with their own hands. Those who care about developing godlike courage should practice this kind of hunting personally.

The praise and blame assigned to all these activities has now been declared. And let the law be as follows: no one shall prevent these sacred hunters from pursuing the chase wherever and however they wish. But the hunter who relies on nets and snares at night shall not be allowed to hunt anywhere. The bird-hunter in the mountains and wild places shall be permitted, but on cultivated land or consecrated wilderness, he shall not — and anyone who encounters him may stop him. As for the hunter in waters: he may hunt anywhere except in harbors, sacred streams, marshes, or pools — with the single requirement that he not pollute the water with poison.

And now we can say that all our regulations about education are complete.

Cleinias: Very good.

LAWS

Book 8

Athenian: Next, with the help of the Delphian oracle, we need to establish festivals and make laws about them. We have to determine which sacrifices will be good for the city and to which gods they should be offered. But when and how often they should be offered -- that's something we can partly regulate ourselves.

Cleinias: The number, yes.

Athenian: Then let's start by setting the number. Let the total be 365 -- one for every day -- so that at least one magistrate is always sacrificing to some god or divine spirit on behalf of the city, its citizens, and their property. The interpreters, priests, priestesses, and prophets should meet with the guardians of the law and fill in the details that the legislator necessarily has to leave out. And really, they're exactly the right people to notice what's been left out.

The law will say this: There are twelve feasts dedicated to the twelve gods after whom the tribes are named. Each month, the citizens shall sacrifice to each of these gods and organize choruses along with musical and athletic contests, arranging them to suit both the gods and the seasons of the year. They'll also have festivals specifically for women, distinguishing between those that should be separate from men's festivals and those that needn't be.

Furthermore, they must not mix up the rites of the underworld gods with those of the heavenly gods, but keep them separate. They should give Pluto his own rites in the twelfth month, which is sacred to him, as the law requires. Warriors shouldn't feel any aversion toward such a deity -- they should actually honor him as humanity's constant best friend. Because the connection of soul and body is in no way better than their separation, and I'm prepared to argue that point seriously.

Beyond this, the people who organize these matters need to consider that our city has no rival among existing cities when it comes to leisure and access to life's necessities. Like an individual, it ought to live happily. And those who'd live happily should first of all do no wrong to one another, and shouldn't be wronged by others either. The first part isn't that hard; the great difficulty is acquiring the power to not be wronged. Nobody can be perfectly secure against being wronged unless they've become perfectly good. The same goes for cities: a good city enjoys a life of peace, while a bad one has war both inside and outside its walls.

Since that's how things are, the citizens ought to practice for war -- not during wartime, but while they're at peace. Every city with any sense should take the field for at least one day every month, and more if the magistrates see fit. They shouldn't worry about winter cold or summer heat. They should go out as a full body, including their wives and children, whenever the magistrates decide to lead out the whole population -- or in separate groups when summoned.

They should always make sure there are games and sacrificial feasts alongside these exercises. They should hold tournaments that imitate real battles as closely as possible. They should award prizes for victory and valor to the competitors, passing judgments and public honors on one another based on how they perform in the contests and in their whole lives -- praising those who seem the best, and criticizing those who are the opposite.

As for poets who celebrate the victors -- not just any poet should be allowed. First, a poet must be at least fifty years old. And he shouldn't be someone who has musical and poetical talent but has never done anything noble or distinguished in his own life. Only those who are themselves good people and honored by the state, creators of noble deeds -- their poems should be sung, even if they're not the most musically gifted.

The judgment of these poems shall rest with the director of education and the other guardians of the law. They alone shall grant this privilege, and they alone shall be free to compose songs. Nobody else shall dare to sing a song that hasn't been approved by the guardians of the law -- not even if his music is sweeter than the songs of Thamyras and Orpheus. Only poems judged sacred and dedicated to the gods, and works by good people in which praise or blame has been properly assigned and that have been deemed to serve their purpose well.

The same regulations about warfare and freedom of expression in poetry should apply equally to men and women. The legislator should think through the question in his own mind: "Who are my citizens, the ones I've organized this city for? Aren't they competitors in the greatest of all contests, with countless rivals?" "Of course," would be the natural reply. Well, if we were training boxers or wrestlers or any other kind of athlete, would they never train until the actual contest arrived? Would we do nothing to prepare in advance? Surely, if we were boxers, we'd have been training for many days beforehand, practicing all those punches and blocks we planned to use in the real fight. And to get as close to reality as possible, we'd put on boxing gloves instead of cestuses, so we could practice our strikes and defenses with everything we had.

If there were a shortage of sparring partners, we wouldn't let the mockery of fools stop us from hanging up a dummy and practicing on that. And if we had no opponent at all, living or otherwise, wouldn't we just shadow-box by ourselves? How else would we ever learn self-defense?

Cleinias: That's really the only way, Stranger.

Athenian: So shall the warriors of our city -- who are destined to enter the greatest of all contests when the time comes, fighting for their lives, their children, their property, and the whole city -- be worse prepared than boxers? Should the legislator hold back from ordering them to train, just because he's afraid their practice sessions might look ridiculous to some people?

No. He should command soldiers to perform lighter exercises without weapons every day, making all dancing and gymnastics serve this purpose. And he should also require more serious armed exercises at least once a month, with contests against one another throughout the country -- seizing positions, setting ambushes, and imitating every aspect of real warfare. They should fight with boxing gloves and hurl javelins, using weapons that are somewhat dangerous and as close to the real thing as possible, so that the training isn't completely free from fear. It should have real terrors that, to some degree, reveal who has courage and who doesn't. Honor and dishonor should be distributed accordingly, preparing the whole city for the genuine struggles of life.

If someone dies in these practice battles, the killing is involuntary. We'll have the killer purified according to law and declare him clean, reasoning this way: if a few people die, others just as good will be born to replace them. But if fear itself dies -- if the citizens never find a way to test who's superior and who's inferior -- that's a far greater evil for the state than losing a few people.

Cleinias: We're in complete agreement, Stranger. The whole state should practice these things, and we should make laws about them.

Athenian: Now, does anyone know why these kinds of dances and contests hardly exist in actual cities -- at least not to any significant degree? Is it because people and their legislators are just ignorant?

Cleinias: Perhaps.

Athenian: Not at all, my dear Cleinias. There are two causes, and they're more than enough to explain the problem.

Cleinias: What are they?

Athenian: The first is the love of wealth. It completely absorbs people and never gives them a moment to think about anything beyond their own private possessions. Every citizen's soul is fixated on daily profit. People are ready to learn any skill, pursue any career that leads to money, and they laugh at everything else. That's one reason why a city won't take these contests -- or any other good and honorable pursuit -- seriously.

Because of this insatiable love of gold and silver, every person will stoop to any trick or trade, respectable or not, in hopes of getting rich. They'll do anything, sacred or profane, utterly degrading -- so long as they can stuff themselves like animals with every kind of food and drink and satisfy their every appetite.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: So let's mark this down as one reason states fail to properly cultivate the art of war or any other noble aim. It turns decent, moderate people into merchants and ship captains and servants. And it converts the naturally brave into thieves and burglars and temple-robbers and violent, tyrannical bullies -- many of whom are actually quite talented, but deeply unfortunate.

Cleinias: What do you mean by "unfortunate"?

Athenian: How can you call anyone anything other than truly unfortunate whose soul is forced to go through life constantly starving?

Cleinias: All right, that's one cause, Stranger. But you mentioned another.

Athenian: Thank you for the reminder.

Cleinias: You were saying that the insatiable, lifelong love of wealth is one thing that absorbs people and prevents them from properly practicing the arts of war. Granted. Now tell me -- what's the other?

Athenian: You think I'm stalling because I'm stuck?

Cleinias: No, but we think you're being a bit too hard on the money-loving temperament. You seem to have a particular grudge against it in this discussion.

Athenian: That's a very fair pushback, Cleinias. I'll move on to the second cause.

Cleinias: Go on.

Athenian: I say it's the forms of government themselves -- democracy, oligarchy, tyranny -- which I've discussed at length already. But really, none of these are true governments at all. None of them exercises voluntary rule over willing subjects. They should really be called states of discord: the government may rule by choice, but the subjects always obey against their will and have to be coerced. And the ruler lives in fear of his subjects, so he won't allow them to become noble, or rich, or strong, or brave, or warlike in any way.

Those are the two chief causes of almost all the evils I've been talking about. But our state has escaped both of them. Its citizens have the greatest leisure, they're not subject to one another's tyranny, and I think our laws will make them the opposite of money-lovers. So we may reasonably suppose that this is the only existing constitution that would accept the education we've described and the military training we've outlined.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: Now, the next point to remember about athletic contests: only the military kind should be practiced and should have prizes for victory. Non-military contests should be dropped. Let's describe and establish the military ones by law. And first, let's talk about running and speed.

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: The most militarily useful quality of all is general physical agility, whether on foot or with the hands. For escaping or capturing an enemy, you need speed of foot. For hand-to-hand combat, you need power and strength.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: But neither reaches its peak performance without weapons.

Cleinias: How could it?

Athenian: So our herald, following prevailing practice, will first summon the runner -- who will appear armed, because we won't give prizes to unarmed competitors. First to enter will be the one running the single course in armor. Next, the runner in the double course. Third, the horse-course runner. Fourth, the long-distance runner. Fifth, we'll send out a man in heavy armor to run a course of sixty stadia to a temple of Ares. And we'll send another, more heavily armed, to run over smoother ground. Finally, the archer: he'll run in full archer's equipment over a hundred stadia across mountains and every kind of terrain, to a temple of Apollo and Artemis. That's the order of the contest. We'll wait for them to return and award prizes to the winners.

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: Let's set up three age divisions for these contests: boys, beardless youths, and men. The youths will run two-thirds of the full course, and the boys half, whether they're competing as archers or heavy-armed. As for the women: girls who haven't yet reached maturity should compete in the stadium, the double course, the horse-course, and the long-distance race, running on the racecourse itself. Those from thirteen up until marriage should continue competing, provided they're no more than twenty years old. They must compete up to age eighteen. They'll enter the arena in appropriate clothing.

Those are the regulations for running contests, for both men and women.

Now, for contests of strength: instead of wrestling and similar heavy-contact sports, we'll institute armed combat -- one against one, two against two, and so on up to ten against ten. As for what a combatant should and shouldn't suffer or do to win -- just as wrestling masters have laid down rules about what's fair and unfair -- we should bring in expert judges to help us legislate these armed fights. They'll determine who deserves to be called the victor, what moves are forbidden, and what counts as a defeat. These same rules shall apply to women until they're married, as well as to men.

The pancration will have a counterpart in light-armed combat: contestants will fight with bows, light shields, javelins, and by throwing stones with slings and by hand. Laws shall be made about this too, and prizes awarded to whoever best follows the rules.

Next in order, we'll need to legislate about horse-racing. But we don't actually need many horses -- in a country like Crete, they can't be much use, so naturally we don't put great effort into breeding them or into horse races. Nobody among us keeps a chariot, and any rivalry in such matters would be completely out of place. It would make no sense to establish contests that don't fit our country's character.

So we'll give prizes for individual horses -- for colts that haven't yet shed their baby teeth, for intermediate-age horses, and for fully grown ones. This way our equestrian games will suit the land's nature. Let them compete according to the law, and let the cavalry colonels and generals jointly decide about all courses and the armed competitors in them.

For unarmed contestants -- whether in gymnastics or horse-racing -- we have nothing to say. But the Cretan archer or javelin-thrower who fights in armor on horseback is genuinely useful, so we might as well include competitions for that among our games. As for women: they shouldn't be forced to compete by law. But if their previous training has made them strong enough and they want to take part -- girls and grown women alike -- let them, with no disgrace attached.

So that covers athletic competitions and how to learn them. We've also discussed the daily training routines under the guidance of instructors. And most of what relates to music has already been settled. As for rhapsodes and similar performers, and the choral contests to be held at festivals -- all of that will be arranged once the months, days, and years have been assigned to their respective gods and divine spirits, whether the festivals occur every three years, every five years, or however the gods inspire people to organize them.

At the same time, we can expect that the musical contests will be overseen by the judges, the director of education, and the guardians of the law, meeting together for this purpose and acting as legislators for the timing, nature, and conditions of the choral contests and all dancing. The original legislator has already laid down frequently what these should be in terms of language, song, and the blending of harmony with rhythm and dance. His successors should follow his lead, matching games and sacrifices appropriately to their proper times and establishing public festivals.

It's not hard to figure out how to put these matters in good order. And adjusting the details won't do any great good or harm to the state. But there is another matter of great importance and difficulty -- the kind of thing a god should legislate on, if it were possible to get a divine ruling. But since we can't count on divine help, it seems we need some bold person who especially values plain speaking and will say outright what he thinks is best for the city and its citizens. Someone who'll prescribe what's good and fitting for the whole state amid the corruption of human souls, opposing the most powerful appetites, with nobody to help him -- standing alone and following reason only.

Cleinias: What are you talking about, Stranger? We don't understand what you mean yet.

Athenian: That's fair. Let me try to explain more clearly. When I came to the subject of education, I pictured young men and women socializing freely with each other. And naturally, a kind of worry came over me. I couldn't help thinking: how do you manage a city where the young people are well-fed and well-raised, where they don't have to do the kind of backbreaking, soul-crushing labor that stamps out desire, and where their only real obligations their whole lives are sacrifices, festivals, and dances? In a city like that, how are they going to resist the desires that drive so many men and women to ruin -- desires that reason, acting as law, commands them to resist?

Now, the rules we've already established may succeed against most of these desires. The prohibition on excessive wealth goes a long way toward promoting self-control. Our entire educational system imposes discipline on the young. And the watchful eye of the rulers is always on them. These measures do, as far as humanly possible, regulate desire in general.

But how do we guard against unnatural sexual loves in either sex -- the source of countless evils for individuals and cities alike? How do we devise a remedy and an escape from such a serious danger?

This is genuinely difficult, Cleinias. In many areas, Crete and Sparta provide great help to those who make distinctive laws. But on the matter of sexual love, I have to say frankly -- they're completely against us.

If someone were to follow nature and lay down the law that existed before the time of Laius, declaring these desires to be contrary to nature -- pointing to the animal kingdom as proof that such unions are unnatural -- he might well prove his point. But he'd be completely at odds with the customs of your own states.

Furthermore, these practices clash with a principle we say the legislator should always observe: we're always asking which of our laws promotes virtue and which doesn't. Now suppose we grant that such loves are considered, by current law, to be honorable -- or at least not disgraceful. To what extent will they contribute to virtue? Will such passions plant the habit of courage in the soul of the one who's pursued? Or the principle of self-control in the soul of the pursuer? Nobody would believe that. In fact, it's the opposite -- everyone will criticize the weakness of the person who gives in to pleasure and can't resist it. Everyone will condemn as effeminate the man who plays the woman's role. So who would ever think of establishing such a practice by law? Certainly nobody who had a clear picture of what true law looks like.

How can I prove this? You need to understand the nature of friendship and desire, and of these so-called loves. There are really two kinds, and from those two a third arises that shares the same name. It's this shared name that causes all the confusion and difficulty.

Cleinias: How so?

Athenian: There's the love between equals -- where like is dear to like in virtue, and equal to equal. And there's the love born from need -- where the one who has plenty is dear to the one who lacks. When either of these friendships becomes excessive, we call it love.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: Now, the friendship that arises from opposites -- where one has and the other wants -- is raw and crude, and often has no deep bond of community. But the friendship that arises from likeness is gentle and creates a bond that lasts a lifetime.

As for the third kind, the mixture of the two -- well, first of all, it's hard even to figure out what the person gripped by this mixed love actually wants. He's pulled in two directions and caught between two principles. One urges him to enjoy the beauty of youth; the other forbids him.

The one is a lover of the body. He hungers after beauty the way you'd hunger after ripe fruit, and he wants to satisfy himself with no regard for the character of the person he desires. The other holds physical desire to be secondary. He looks with his soul rather than lusting with his body, his soul desiring the other's soul in the proper way. He regards satisfying bodily lust as degrading. He reveres and respects self-control, courage, nobility of spirit, and wisdom, and wants to live purely with the one he loves, who is equally pure.

The love that mixes these two is the third kind I've described.

Since there are these three kinds of love, should the law prohibit and ban all of them from our state? Or isn't it obvious that we'd want the kind of love that's rooted in virtue and desires the beloved to become the best person possible? The other two, if we can manage it, we should try to prevent.

What do you say, friend Megillus?

Megillus: I think you're absolutely right in everything you've been saying, Stranger.

Athenian: I knew I'd have your agreement, my friend, and I accept it. I don't need to analyze your Spartan customs on this point any further. Cleinias can give me his opinion some other time. For now, let's move on to the laws.

Megillus: Very good.

Athenian: On reflection, I see a way to impose this law that's easy in one respect but incredibly difficult in another.

Megillus: What do you mean?

Athenian: We all know that most people, despite their lawless natures, are very strictly and precisely restrained from one particular kind of sexual intercourse -- and not against their will, but entirely with their will.

Megillus: When is that?
