Athenian: When someone has a brother or sister who's attractive. The same unwritten law applies to sons and daughters, and it's a perfect safeguard. No one -- openly or in secret -- ever has sexual contact with them. The very thought never even enters most people's minds.

Megillus: Very true.

Athenian: A single little word extinguishes all such desires, doesn't it?

Megillus: What word?

Athenian: The declaration that these acts are unholy, hateful to the gods, and utterly shameful. And isn't the reason simply this: no one has ever said otherwise. From earliest childhood, every person hears people speaking the same way about this -- always, everywhere. They hear it in comedy, they hear it in the solemn language of tragedy. When the poet puts a Thyestes or an Oedipus or a Macareus on stage -- someone who's had secret intercourse with a sibling -- he shows the character, once discovered, choosing death as the penalty for their sin.

Megillus: You're quite right. Tradition, when no opposing voice ever challenges it, has an extraordinary power.

Athenian: So doesn't this confirm what I said? That the legislator who wants to tame any passion that dominates people can easily see how to subdue it? He should consecrate the tradition that these things are evil in the eyes of everyone -- slaves and free, women and children, throughout the entire city. That will be the surest foundation for his law.

Megillus: Yes. But will he ever succeed in getting all of humanity to speak with one voice on this?

Athenian: Good objection. But wasn't I just saying that I had a way to make people follow natural love and abstain from unnatural -- a way that doesn't intentionally destroy the seeds of human reproduction or sow them in barren ground where they can't take root? A way that commands people to abstain from any sexual act that isn't likely to produce life?

Now, if a law to this effect could be made permanent and gain the kind of authority that already prevents sexual relations between parents and children -- if it could achieve that level of power over other desires too -- it would be the source of ten thousand blessings.

First, it would be following the order of nature. It would restrain people from sexual frenzy and madness, from adultery, from excessive eating and drinking. It would make them true friends to their own spouses. And countless other benefits would follow if such a law could be enforced.

Now, I can picture some vigorous young man standing nearby, overhearing this proposal, and yelling in crude language that we're making foolish, impossible laws, filling the room with his protests. And that's exactly why I said I knew a way to establish and maintain such a law that's very easy in one respect but extremely difficult in another.

It's not hard to see that such a law is possible, and how it could work. As I was saying, once the principle is truly consecrated as sacred, it would master every person's soul and terrify them into obedience. But things have now reached such a point that even then, the desired result seems almost unattainable -- just as people say it's impossible for a whole city to maintain the practice of communal meals.

And yet that's partly disproven by the fact that communal meals already exist among you. Still, even in your cities, communal meals for women are considered unnatural and impossible. I was thinking about the rebelliousness of the human heart when I said that establishing these things permanently is very difficult.

Megillus: Very true.

Athenian: Shall I try to find some persuasive argument that proves such laws are possible and not beyond human nature?

Cleinias: By all means.

Athenian: Is a person more likely to resist the pleasures of sex and follow the rules when their body is in good condition through training, or when they're out of shape?

Cleinias: They'll be far more self-controlled when they're in training.

Athenian: Haven't we heard of Iccus of Tarentum? In his dedication to the Olympic and other contests -- driven by both his devotion to his sport and his naturally disciplined character -- he never had sexual contact with a woman or a man during his entire training period. The same is said of Crison, Astylus, Diopompus, and many others. And yet, Cleinias, these men were far less educated in mind than your citizens and mine, and far more vigorous in body.

Cleinias: You're right -- the ancients did consistently confirm this about those athletes.

Athenian: So they had the discipline to abstain from what's generally considered a great pleasure, all for the sake of winning at wrestling or running or the like. And our young people will be incapable of the same endurance for a far nobler victory -- the noblest of all? We'll tell them this from youth onward, charming them, as we hope, into believing it through stories, sayings, and songs.

Cleinias: What victory are you talking about?

Athenian: Victory over pleasure. If they win that battle, they'll live happily. If they lose it, the opposite. Beyond that, won't the fear of impiety help them master what other, lesser people have already mastered?

Cleinias: I'd expect so.

Athenian: Well, since we've reached this point in our legislation and hit a wall because of human weakness, I'll state simply what our law should say:

Our citizens ought not to be worse than birds and other animals, which are born in great numbers and yet remain virgin and unmated until the proper age for breeding. When they reach that age, they pair off -- male with female -- and live out the rest of their days in devotion and faithfulness, holding firmly to their original bond.

Surely, we'll say to our citizens, you should be better than the animals.

But if they're corrupted by the rest of the Greeks and the common practices of other peoples -- if they see with their own eyes and hear with their own ears about so-called "free love" everywhere around them, and they can't resist the temptation -- then the guardians of the law, acting as secondary legislators, shall devise a second law to deal with them.

Cleinias: And what law would you recommend if the first one fails?

Athenian: Obviously, Cleinias, the one that naturally follows.

Cleinias: Which is?

Athenian: Our citizens shouldn't let their pleasures grow stronger through indulgence. Instead, they should redirect that energy into other parts of the body through hard physical work. And this will happen if sexual activity is never practiced shamelessly.

They should be ashamed of frequent intercourse, and they'll find that pleasure, when rarely enjoyed, is a less tyrannical master. Not being caught should be considered honorable -- sanctioned by custom and established as law by unwritten tradition. Being caught, on the other hand, should be considered disgraceful -- though it's not as honorable as abstaining entirely.

This way, there will be a second standard of honor and dishonor, creating a second level of moral conduct. Three principles will encompass all those weak-willed people whom we call "inferior to themselves" -- they form a single class -- and will compel them not to transgress.

Cleinias: What are these three principles?

Athenian: Reverence for the gods, love of honor, and the desire for beauty -- not of the body, but of the soul. These may sound like romantic ideals, but they're the noblest ideals imaginable, if only they could be realized in every state. And God willing, on the matter of sexual conduct, we may be able to enforce one of two standards.

Either, first: no one shall touch any freeborn or noble person except their lawfully wedded spouse, nor scatter their seed among prostitutes or in barren, unnatural unions.

Or, failing that, at the very least: we'll abolish sexual relations between men entirely. And as for women -- if any man has relations with any woman other than the one who came into his household through sacred marriage rites (whether she was formally married to him or acquired in any other legitimate way), and he commits this offense openly in the sight of all -- we'll be right to decree that he be stripped of his civic honors and privileges and considered what he truly is: a foreigner to the city's values.

Let this law, then -- whether it's one law or should really be called two -- be established concerning sexual love in general and the sexual relations that arise from desire, whether rightly or wrongly pursued.

Megillus: For my part, Stranger, I'd gladly accept this law. Cleinias can speak for himself and tell you his opinion.

Cleinias: I will, Megillus, when the right opportunity comes. But for now, I think we should let the Stranger continue with his laws.

Megillus: Very good.

Athenian: We'd gotten about as far as establishing the communal meals, which would be difficult in most places but which no one in Crete would think of changing. The question might come up about what form they should take -- should they be like they are here in Crete, or like those in Sparta, or is there some third kind that might be better than either? The answer wouldn't be hard to find, but the discovery wouldn't do much good, since they're already quite well organized as they are.

Setting communal meals aside, then, let's move on to the means of providing food. In most cities, people make their living in many ways and from many sources -- generally from two: sea and land. But our city relies on land alone. And this makes the legislator's job much easier. Half as many laws will be enough -- actually, much less than half. And they'll be laws better suited to free people.

Our legislator has nothing to do with laws about shipowners, merchants, retailers, innkeepers, tax collectors, mines, money-lending, compound interest, and countless other such things. He can say goodbye to all of that. He gives laws to farmers and shepherds and beekeepers, and to the guardians and superintendents of their tools. He's already legislated about the bigger matters -- marriage, the begetting and raising of children, education, the establishment of offices. Now he has to turn his attention to those who provide food and the labor of preparing it.

So let's start with a class of laws we'll call the laws for farmers. And let the very first be the law of Zeus, god of boundaries:

No one shall move a boundary marker -- not of a fellow citizen who's a neighbor, nor of a stranger who lives at the border of the land. Understand that this truly means "moving the immovable." Everyone should be more willing to move the biggest boulder that isn't a landmark than the smallest stone that marks the sworn boundary between neighbors, the line of friendship and enmity. Zeus, the god of kinship, witnesses the citizen's oath, and Zeus, the god of strangers, witnesses the stranger's. When they're roused, terrible are the wars they stir up.

Whoever obeys this law will never know the fatal consequences of disobedience. But whoever breaks it shall face a double punishment -- the first from the gods, the second from the law. Let no one willfully move a neighbor's boundary markers. If anyone does, let any landowner report him, and let them bring him to court. If he's convicted of secretly or forcibly re-dividing land, let the court determine what he should suffer or pay.

Next: many small injuries between neighbors, through their accumulation, can pile up into a crushing weight of resentment and make living next door to someone a genuinely bitter thing. A person ought to be very careful about offending their neighbor, especially about encroaching on their neighbor's land. It's easy for anyone to cause harm, but not everyone can do good.

Whoever encroaches on a neighbor's land and crosses their boundaries shall make good the damage. And to cure him of both his rudeness and his pettiness, he shall pay double damages to the injured party. The country wardens shall handle these matters and act as judges, assessing the damage. In more important cases, as we've already said, the full panel of wardens from whichever of the twelve districts is relevant shall decide. In lesser cases, the commanders shall judge.

If anyone grazes their cattle on a neighbor's land, the wardens shall assess the damage and set the penalty. If anyone steals another person's bees by luring them away with noise, he shall pay damages. If anyone sets fire to their own wood and takes no care to protect their neighbor's property, they'll be fined at the magistrates' discretion. If someone plants too close to their neighbor's land without leaving a proper distance, they'll be punished in accordance with the rulings of many lawgivers, which we may freely use. The great legislator of our state needn't bother determining every little thing that any ordinary person could decide.

For instance, farmers have long had excellent laws about water, and there's no reason for us to reinvent them. Anyone who wants may draw water from the main stream onto their own land, as long as they don't cut off the spring that clearly belongs to another owner. They can direct the water however they please, except through a house, temple, or tomb -- but they must be careful not to cause damage beyond their own channel.

If there's a naturally dry area that soaks up the rain and causes a water shortage, let the landowner dig on their own property down to the clay layer. If they find no water at that depth, they may get water from their neighbors -- as much as their household servants need for drinking. If the neighbors are also running short, they'll receive a fixed daily measure set by the country wardens, on those terms sharing their neighbors' water.

If heavy rain falls and someone on lower ground injures a farmer above them by refusing to let the water drain off -- or if someone on higher ground recklessly dumps water onto their lower neighbor -- and they can't work it out between themselves, let either party call in a city warden (if in the city) or a country warden (if in the country) and get a ruling on what each of them must do. Anyone who won't abide by the decision shall suffer for their stubborn and spiteful temperament and pay a fine to the injured party worth double the damage, because they refused to submit to the magistrates.

Now, the sharing of the harvest shall be regulated as follows. The goddess of autumn gives us two gracious gifts: one is the joy of Dionysus -- grapes and fruit that aren't meant to be stored. The other is fruit that nature intends for keeping. So let this be the law concerning the fruits of autumn:

Anyone who tastes the common, storable fruits of autumn -- whether grapes or figs -- before the harvest season that coincides with the rising of Arcturus, whether on their own land or someone else's, shall pay a fine: fifty drachmas (sacred to Dionysus) if they pick from their own land, one mina if from a neighbor's, and two-thirds of a mina if from anyone else's.

As for the "choice" grapes and "choice" figs, as they're currently called: if someone picks them from their own land, they may do so however and whenever they like. But if they take them from someone else's land without permission, they shall always be punished under the law that says you shouldn't take what you didn't put there.

If a slave touches any of this fruit without the landowner's consent, he shall receive one lash for every grape on the bunch or every fig on the tree.

A resident alien may purchase the "choice" fruit, and then pick it as he pleases. But if a stranger is just passing through and wants something to eat, let him take some "choice" grapes for himself and one companion, free of charge, as a gesture of hospitality. The law does, however, forbid strangers from sharing in the storable kind. If anyone -- master or slave -- takes the storable fruit out of ignorance, the slave shall be beaten, and the free person dismissed with a warning and told to take from the other kinds of fruit -- the ones that aren't suitable for making raisins, wine, or dried figs.

As for pears, apples, pomegranates, and similar fruits: there's no disgrace in taking them secretly. But anyone caught under the age of thirty shall be struck and driven off -- without serious injury -- and no free person shall have any legal claim for such blows. Strangers may eat these fruits just as they may eat the autumn fruits. And if a citizen over thirty eats them on the spot without carrying any away, he shall be treated the same as the stranger -- allowed to have his fill. But if he won't follow this rule and tries to carry some off, let him risk disqualification from the competitions of virtue, in case anyone reports his behavior to the judges when the time comes.

Water is the most important nutrient for gardens, but it's easily polluted. You can't poison the soil, the sun, or the air -- the other elements that nourish plants -- nor can you divert or steal them. But all of these things can easily happen with water, which is why it needs legal protection.

Here is the law: If anyone intentionally pollutes another person's water -- whether it's a spring or water collected in a reservoir -- by poisonous substances, by digging, or by theft, the injured party shall bring the case before the city wardens and submit a written claim for the value of the loss. If the accused is found guilty of contaminating the water with harmful substances, he shall not only pay damages but also purify the stream or cistern that holds the water, in whatever manner the religious interpreters prescribe for each specific case.

Regarding the transport of harvested crops: a person may carry their own produce through any place where they either do no harm to anyone, or where they stand to gain three times as much as their neighbor loses. The magistrates should oversee these matters, as they should all cases where one person intentionally harms another or their property through fraud or force, or by misusing their own property.

For all these matters, a person should bring their complaint before the magistrates and receive damages, as long as the injury is no more than three minas. If the claim involves a larger amount, they should bring their suit to the public courts and have the offender punished. And if any magistrate appears to be imposing penalties in an unfair spirit, they shall be liable to pay double to the injured party. Anyone may bring charges against magistrates for wrongdoing in any particular case before the public courts.

There are countless little procedural details about methods of punishment, filing suits, issuing summonses, and witnesses for summonses -- for example, whether two witnesses are required for a summons, or how many -- and all such details that can't be left out of legislation but are beneath the dignity of an experienced legislator. These lesser matters, lesser as they truly are compared to the greater ones, should be regulated by the younger generation through laws modeled on the patterns we've set down, reflecting their own experience of what's useful and necessary. Once those rules are properly established, let there be no changes. Let the citizens live by them.

Now, regarding craftsmen and artisans, the rules should be as follows:

First, no citizen or servant of a citizen should work in the manual trades. A person who's going to preserve and maintain the public order of the state already has a full-time job -- one that requires extensive study and wide-ranging knowledge. It doesn't allow for moonlighting. And hardly any human being is capable of doing two jobs or practicing two trades well, or of practicing one trade themselves while supervising someone else in a different one.

So let this be our first principle: no one who's a metalworker should also be a carpenter. And if someone is a carpenter, he shouldn't supervise metalwork alongside his own craft, using the excuse that overseeing multiple workers brings in more revenue than his own trade alone. Every person in the state shall have one craft and earn their living from that alone.

The city wardens shall enforce this law. If any citizen turns toward any occupation other than the study of virtue, they shall punish him with disgrace and loss of standing until they bring him back to his proper course. If any foreigner practices two trades, they shall punish him with imprisonment, fines, and expulsion from the state, until they compel him to be one person, not many.

As for payments, contracts for work, and disputes: if anyone wrongs a citizen or is wronged by one, for amounts up to fifty drachmas, the city wardens shall decide the case. For larger amounts, the public courts shall decide according to law.

No one shall pay any import or export duty. As for frankincense and similar perfumes used in worship that come from abroad, and purple dye and other colors not produced locally, and materials for any craft that must be imported but aren't essential -- no one should bring these in. Likewise, no one should export anything the country needs.

There shall be inspectors and superintendents for all of this, drawn from the guardians of the law -- specifically the twelve next in seniority after the five senior ones.

For weapons and all military equipment -- if there's a need to import any craft, plant, metal, chain, or animal for use in war -- the cavalry commanders and generals shall have authority over imports and exports. The city shall send them out and receive them, and the guardians of the law shall make proper laws about them.

But there shall be absolutely no retail trade for profit -- whether in these or any other articles -- anywhere in the city or the country.

Regarding food and the distribution of the country's produce, the right approach seems to be roughly the Cretan custom. Everyone should divide the fruits of the earth into twelve parts and consume them that way.

The twelfth portion of each product -- wheat, barley, and the rest of the earth's produce, along with the animals for sale in each of the twelve districts -- shall be divided into three parts: one for free citizens, one for their servants, and one for craftsmen and foreigners in general, whether they're permanent residents who have to earn a living like everyone else, or visitors who've come on business with the state or some individual.

Only this third portion of necessities should be required to be sold. Nobody shall be forced to sell from the other two-thirds.

Now, how will these portions be most fairly distributed? Well, clearly, the distribution is equal in one sense but unequal in another.

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: The earth inevitably produces food of varying quality -- sometimes better, sometimes worse.

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: Given that, none of the three portions should be larger than the other two -- not the one for masters, not the one for slaves, not the one for strangers. The distribution should be equal and identical for all. Each citizen shall take their two portions and distribute them among slaves and free people, with the authority to determine the quantity and quality of each share. Whatever's left over, they shall distribute by measured amounts among the animals that need to be fed from the earth, based on the total number of animals.

Next, our citizens should have separate, well-ordered houses. Here's the proper arrangement for people like them:

There shall be twelve hamlets, one in the center of each twelfth of the territory. In each hamlet, they shall first set aside a marketplace, along with temples to the gods and their attendant divine spirits. If there are any local deities of the Magnetes, or sacred sites of ancient gods whose memory has been preserved, they shall pay them their traditional honors. But Hestia, Zeus, and Athena shall have temples everywhere, together with the god who presides over each of the twelve districts. The first houses shall be built around these temples, on the highest ground, to provide the safest and most defensible place of retreat for the guards.

For the rest of the country, they shall organize things like this: They'll divide the craftsmen into thirteen groups. One group they'll establish in the city proper, subdivided into twelve sections distributed among the twelve city districts. The rest shall be settled in the villages across the countryside. In each village, they'll settle various classes of craftsmen, chosen to suit the needs of the farmers.

The chief officers of the country wardens shall oversee all of this, determining how many craftsmen each place needs, and of what kind, and settling them where they'll cause the least trouble and do the most good for the farmers. The city wardens shall handle similar arrangements within the city.

Now, the wardens of the marketplace should attend to the marketplace's details. Their first concern, after seeing to the temples in the marketplace, should be to prevent anyone from cheating in their dealings. Second, as guardians of good behavior and public order, they should punish anyone who needs it.

Regarding goods for sale, they should first make sure that the products citizens are required to sell to foreigners are actually being sold as the law prescribes. And the law is as follows:

On the first day of the month, the persons in charge -- whether foreigners or slaves acting on behalf of citizens -- shall present to the foreigners their share. First, a twelfth of the grain: foreigners shall purchase their grain for the whole month, along with other cereals, on the first market day. On the tenth of the month, one party shall sell and the other buy enough liquids to last the whole month. On the twenty-third, there shall be a sale of animals by those willing to sell to those wanting to buy, along with farming implements and other goods that farmers sell -- things like hides and all kinds of clothing, whether woven, felted, or otherwise made -- which foreigners must purchase.

As for retail trade in these items -- barley or wheat flour, or any other kind of food -- no one shall sell them to citizens or their slaves, nor shall any citizen buy them. Instead, foreigners shall sell these goods in the foreigners' market to craftsmen and their slaves, exchanging wine and food -- what's commonly called retail trade. Butchers shall sell cuts of meat to foreigners, craftsmen, and their servants.

Any foreigner who wants may buy firewood wholesale on any day from those who manage it in the countryside, and may sell it to other foreigners as much as he wants, whenever he wants.

All other goods and equipment that are likely to be needed shall be sold in the common market, at whatever locations the guardians of the law and the wardens of the market and city select at their discretion. At these locations, goods shall be exchanged for money and money for goods, with neither party giving credit. Whoever gives credit must accept the consequences -- whether they get their money back or not -- because in such transactions, the law won't protect them.

Whenever property has been bought or sold in amounts greater in quantity or value than the law allows -- the law having set limits on how much a person may increase or decrease their possessions -- the excess shall be recorded in the books of the guardians of the law. If there's been a decrease, there shall be an erasure. The same rules shall apply to the registration of property belonging to resident aliens.

Anyone who wishes may come and settle as a resident alien, on certain conditions. A foreigner may dwell in the land if he's willing and able, but he must practice a trade and not stay longer than twenty years from the time he registers. He shall pay no residency tax, however small, except good behavior -- no other tax for buying and selling either.

When the twenty years are up, he shall take his property and leave. If during those years he happens to distinguish himself by doing the state some significant service, and he believes he can persuade the council and assembly to grant him an extension or allow him to stay permanently, let him go make his case. Whatever the city agrees to at his request shall take effect.

For the children of resident aliens who are craftsmen: the clock starts at age fifteen. Let them stay for twenty years and then go where they please. Any who wish to remain may do so, if they can persuade the council and assembly. And if a resident alien departs, let him erase all the entries that were made in his name in the register kept by the magistrates.

Athenian

The next natural step in our legislation, after everything we've covered, is lawsuits. We've already dealt with suits related to agriculture, but the more important ones are still ahead. Let me go through them by their standard categories, lay out the punishments for each offense, and specify who should judge them.

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: There's something embarrassing about legislating, as we're about to do, for every detail of criminal behavior in a state that's supposed to be well-governed and perfectly suited for the practice of virtue. To assume that in such a state someone will commit crimes as terrible as those found in the worst places, and that we have to anticipate this person's existence by writing laws to deter and punish him -- that's a bit disgraceful, frankly. But we're not like the ancient lawmakers, who gave laws to heroes and sons of gods, being themselves (according to popular belief) descendants of gods, legislating for others who were also children of divine parents. We're just men, making laws for the sons of men. So there's nothing uncharitable in suspecting that some of our citizens may be like a seed that's touched an ox's horn -- with a heart so hard that no amount of fire can soften it. Among our citizens, there may be people who can't be tamed by all the strength of the laws. For their sake -- though it's an unpleasant task -- I'll begin with my first law, about robbing temples, in case anyone dares to commit such a crime. I don't expect or imagine that any well-raised citizen would ever catch this infection, but their servants, foreigners, and foreigners' servants might be guilty of many impieties. With them especially in mind -- though also keeping a watchful eye on the weakness of human nature in general -- I'll proclaim the law about temple robbers and similar incurable, or nearly incurable, criminals.

As we've already agreed, every such law should come with a short prelude. So here's what we'll say to the criminal -- the one whom some tormenting desire tempts, night and day, to go and rob a temple. We'll address him in as few words as possible:

"Friend, the impulse driving you to rob temples isn't an ordinary human affliction. It's not a visitation from heaven, either. It's a kind of madness, born from ancient and unexpiated crimes in your family line -- a recurring curse. You must guard against it with all your strength. Here's how: When this thought enters your mind, go and perform rites of purification. Go as a humble petitioner to the temples of the gods who ward off evil. Seek out the company of those who are called good men among you. Listen to them describe how every person should honor what's noble and just, and try to repeat their words yourself. Run from the company of the wicked -- run and don't look back. If these remedies lighten your condition, well and good. But if they don't, then accept that death is nobler than such a life, and depart from this world."

That's the prelude we sing to all those who have thoughts of unholy and treasonous acts. For the one who listens, the law has nothing more to say. But for the one who's disobedient once the prelude is over, we cry out loudly:

Anyone caught in the act of robbing a temple -- if he's a slave or a foreigner -- shall have his crime branded on his face and hands. He'll be beaten with as many stripes as the judges see fit and cast out naked beyond the borders of the land. This punishment will probably bring him to his senses and reform him, since no penalty the law inflicts is designed to cause evil. It always aims to make the offender either better, or at least not as much worse as he would have been otherwise.

But if any citizen is found guilty of some great and unspeakable crime -- against the gods, or his parents, or the state -- let the judge consider him incurable. After all, if someone who received an excellent education and upbringing from youth onward still couldn't keep himself from the worst crimes, there's no saving him. His punishment shall be death, which for him will actually be the least of evils. His example will benefit others: he'll perish in disgrace and be cast beyond the borders. But let his children and family, if they reject their father's ways, be honored. Let them receive public recognition for having nobly and courageously escaped from evil into good. None of their property should be confiscated by the state -- the citizens' allotted land must always remain the same and equal.

As for collecting fines: when someone clearly deserves a fine, he shall pay it from whatever he owns beyond his assigned lot, but not a penny more. To ensure accuracy, the guardians of the law should check the property registers and report the precise figures to the judges, so that no lot goes uncultivated for lack of funds. But if someone deserves a greater penalty, let him undergo a long and public imprisonment and be stripped of his civic honors -- unless some of his friends are willing to post bail and help him pay the fine.

No criminal shall go unpunished -- not for a single offense, not even if he's fled the country. The penalty should match the crime: death, imprisonment, beatings, degrading positions in public, removal to some temple on the borders, or fines, as we've said. In capital cases, the judges shall be the guardians of the law together with a court selected on merit from the previous year's magistrates. The procedural details -- how cases are brought to court, how summonses are served, and so on -- can be left to the next generation of lawmakers. But the manner of voting we must determine ourselves.

The vote shall be given openly. Before voting, the judges shall sit in order of seniority, facing the plaintiff and defendant. All citizens who can spare the time should attend and take a serious interest in listening to these cases. First, the plaintiff makes one speech. Then the defendant makes one. After the speeches, the eldest judge begins examining the parties and conducts a thorough inquiry into what's been said. After the eldest has spoken, the remaining judges proceed in order, each examining whichever party seems to have left gaps in the evidence -- whether something was stated poorly or left out entirely. Any judge with nothing to ask passes the examination to the next. All the judges shall set their seals on whatever testimony they find relevant and place these written records on the altar of Hestia. The next day, they'll meet again, conduct the same examination, and again seal the evidence. After they've done this three times and have gathered sufficient witnesses and evidence, each judge shall cast a sacred vote, having sworn by Hestia to decide justly and truthfully to the utmost of their ability. And so the suit comes to an end.

Next, after crimes against the gods, come crimes against the state itself. Whoever subjects the city to faction by handing power to a tyrant, who enslaves the laws and stirs up civil strife through violence and illegal means -- that person is the greatest enemy of the entire state. But the man who takes no part in such schemes, yet holds high office and either doesn't know about the treason or, knowing about it, is too cowardly to act on behalf of his country -- he's nearly as bad. Every citizen worth anything will report the conspiracy to the authorities and bring the traitor to trial for his violent and illegal attempt to overthrow the government. The judges for such cases shall be the same as for temple robbery, the procedure shall be the same, and a majority vote shall condemn to death.

But here's a general rule: the disgrace and punishment of the father must not be visited on his children -- except when someone's father, grandfather, and great-grandfather have all been executed. In that case, the state shall send the family away with all their possessions to the city and country of their ancestors, keeping only the original allotted lot. And from citizens who have more than one son of at least ten years of age, the family shall select ten candidates, as nominated by the father or grandfather on either the mother's or father's side. They shall send these names to Delphi, and whomever the god chooses shall be established as heir of the failed house. May he have better fortune than his predecessors!

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: And there shall be a third general law: the same rules about judges and trial procedure -- and the same rules about whether descendants may remain or must depart -- shall apply equally to all three categories: traitors, temple robbers, and those who violently subvert the laws of the state.

Now, for theft. Whether someone steals much or little, there shall be one law and one punishment for all: first, pay back double the amount stolen, provided the thief has enough property beyond his allotment. If he doesn't have enough, he shall be imprisoned until he either pays or persuades the person who won the verdict to forgive him. But if someone is convicted of stealing from the state -- well, if he can persuade the city to pardon him, or if he pays back twice the amount, he'll be released from prison.

Cleinias: Wait -- are you really saying that theft is all the same, whether the thief takes a lot or a little, from sacred or secular places? There are all kinds of differences in thefts. Shouldn't the lawmaker adapt to these differences and impose completely different penalties?

Athenian: Excellent point. I was running ahead too fast, Cleinias, and you've pulled me back to my senses -- reminding me of something I'd already been thinking about: that legislation has never yet been properly worked out. Remember the comparison I made earlier? I compared the people we're legislating for to slaves being treated by slave-doctors. Imagine this: one of those empirical physicians -- the kind who practice medicine without any real understanding of the science -- walks in on a gentleman-doctor talking to his gentleman-patient, using language that's practically philosophy, starting from the root causes of the disease and discussing the entire nature of the body. The empirical doctor would burst out laughing. He'd say what most so-called doctors always have on the tip of their tongue: "You fool! You're not healing the sick man -- you're educating him! He doesn't want to become a doctor. He just wants to get well."

Cleinias: And wouldn't he have a point?

Athenian: Perhaps. And he might make the same observation about us -- that in all this talk about laws, we're really giving our citizens an education, not a legal code.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: But here's where we're fortunate.

Cleinias: How so?

Athenian: We're not under any pressure to produce laws on a deadline. We're free to examine every form of government, figure out what's best and what's most practical, and how both goals can actually be achieved. Right now, in this very moment, we can choose: do we aim for what's best, or what's most immediately necessary? Which shall it be?

Cleinias: There's something funny about putting it as an either-or choice, as if we were legislators under some urgent deadline that can't wait until tomorrow. But we're more like people gathering stones and raw materials, piling them up at our leisure, and then selecting what's suitable for our construction when we're ready. Let's think of ourselves that way -- not as men frantically building, but as people partly collecting materials and partly assembling them. Some of our laws, like stones, are already set in place. Others are just lying around, ready to be used.

Athenian: That's right -- our approach to law will be more natural that way. But there's another issue facing lawmakers that I need you to think about seriously.

Cleinias: What is it?

Athenian: In every city you'll find all sorts of writings. Some are by legislators; others are by different kinds of writers.

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: Should we pay more attention to those other writers -- poets and the like, who've recorded their advice about how to live, whether in verse or prose -- rather than to the writings of legislators? Or should we give the lawmakers' writings priority above all?

Cleinias: The lawmakers', far above all others.

Athenian: And shouldn't the lawmaker, alone among writers, feel free to share his views on what's beautiful, what's good, and what's just -- and to teach how these things should be pursued by anyone who wants to live a happy life?

Cleinias: Absolutely.

Athenian: And isn't it true that if it's disgraceful for Homer and Tyrtaeus and other poets to lay down bad principles about life in their writings, it's even more disgraceful for Lycurgus and Solon and other lawmaker-writers? Of all the writings found in any city, those about laws -- when you actually open them up and read them -- should be by far the noblest and the best. Everything else should either agree with them, or be laughed off the page if it disagrees. Here's the question we should ask ourselves: shouldn't the laws of a state have the character of loving and wise parents, rather than tyrants and masters who just issue commands and threats, scribble their decrees on the walls, and walk away? That's the gentler vision of law we should aim for -- whether or not we can fully achieve it, at least we'll show we're willing to try. And may the result be good. God willing, it will be.

Cleinias: Excellent. Let's do it that way.

Athenian: So let's return to our careful examination, as we proposed, of everything relating to temple robbery, theft of all kinds, and offenses in general. And we shouldn't get frustrated if, in the middle of legislating, we've enacted some laws but haven't made up our minds about others. We're not finished lawmakers yet -- we're works in progress. If you don't mind, let's take these matters up now.

Cleinias: By all means.

Athenian: On the subject of honor and justice, let's try to figure out where we're being consistent with ourselves and where we're not -- and where ordinary people, from whom we'd certainly like to differ, agree or disagree among themselves.

Cleinias: What inconsistencies are you noticing in us?

Athenian: I'll try to explain. If I'm not mistaken, we all agree that justice -- just people, just things, just actions -- is always admirable. If someone argued that just people, even when they're physically ugly, are still perfectly beautiful because of the excellence of their sense of justice, nobody would call that inconsistent.

Cleinias: They'd be quite right.

Athenian: Maybe so. But let's think more carefully. If everything that's just is also admirable, then we have to include just punishments in that "everything" -- since just punishments go hand in hand with just actions.

Cleinias: And what follows from that?

Athenian: The conclusion is that a just action, insofar as it participates in justice, also participates equally in what's admirable.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And wouldn't a just punishment, by the same logic, also have to be admirable to the same degree -- if we're being consistent?

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: But what if we admit that a punishment is just yet also shameful? Then the word "shameful" gets applied to justice, and the just and the admirable end up at odds.

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: It's not hard to understand. The laws we've already enacted seem to announce principles that are the exact opposite of what we're saying.

Cleinias: The opposite of what?

Athenian: We enacted, if you recall, that the temple robber and the enemy of lawful order may justly be put to death. And we were about to enact all sorts of similar penalties. But we stopped, because we realized these punishments are infinite in number and severity, and they are at once the most just and also the most shameful of all sufferings. So doesn't justice end up being sometimes the same as what's admirable, and other times the exact opposite?

Cleinias: That does seem to be the case.

Athenian: And that's how ordinary people use their language -- they rip apart what's admirable and what's just, treating them as completely disconnected.

Cleinias: Very true, stranger.

Athenian: Then let's look at our own position, Cleinias, and see how consistent we are on these matters.

Cleinias: Consistent in what way?

Athenian: I think I stated this clearly in our earlier discussion, but if I didn't, let me say it now --

Cleinias: Say what?

Athenian: That all bad people are bad involuntarily -- always. And from this, I need to draw a further conclusion.

Cleinias: What conclusion?

Athenian: That the unjust person may be bad, but he's bad against his own will. Now, it's a contradiction to say that a voluntary action is done involuntarily. So anyone who maintains that injustice is involuntary must also hold that the unjust person commits injustice involuntarily. That's my position too: I maintain that all people commit injustice involuntarily. If some argumentative person objects -- "Sure, people are unjust against their will, but plenty of them do wrong willingly" -- I disagree. No one does wrong willingly.

But then, how do I avoid contradicting myself? Suppose you, Cleinias, and you, Megillus, were to say to me: "Well, stranger, if that's all true, then what about legislating for our city of Magnesia? Should we legislate or not?" Of course we should, I'd reply. "Then will you distinguish between voluntary and involuntary crimes, and make the punishments heavier for voluntary offenses and lighter for involuntary ones? Or will you punish everything the same, on the grounds that there's no such thing as voluntary crime?"

Cleinias: Good question, stranger. So what's the answer?

Athenian: A very fair question. Here's what we need to do first --

Cleinias: Do what?

Athenian: Let's remember what we established earlier: that our ideas about justice are deeply confused and contradictory. With that in mind, let's ask ourselves whether we've found a way through the difficulty. Have we ever properly determined how these two categories of action -- voluntary and involuntary -- actually differ from each other? In every state, every lawmaker who's ever lived has recognized two kinds of actions: voluntary and involuntary. And they've legislated accordingly. But is our new principle -- that nobody does wrong willingly -- just going to sit there like an oracle from god, spoken but never explained or defended? You can't base legislation on an idea that nobody understands. That's impossible. Before we legislate, we have to prove that these are genuinely two different categories and explain what the difference between them is. That way, when we assign a penalty to either one, everyone can understand our reasoning and have some basis for judging whether the penalty fits.

Cleinias: I agree with you, stranger. One of two things has to be true: either we stop claiming that all unjust acts are involuntary, or we explain what we mean and prove it.

Athenian: The first option is completely intolerable to me. I can't refuse to speak what I believe is the truth -- that would be wrong and unholy. But if acts of injustice can't be divided into voluntary and involuntary in the usual way, then I need to find some other way to distinguish between them.

Cleinias: You're absolutely right, stranger. There can't be two opinions among us on that point.

Athenian: All right, then think about this: people harm each other in all kinds of ways in the course of daily life, and there are plenty of examples of both voluntary and involuntary harm.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: Now, I don't want anyone to assume that all harms are automatically "injuries," and that these injuries come in two flavors -- voluntary and involuntary. The involuntary harms that people cause are just as numerous and just as serious as the voluntary ones. And please consider carefully whether what I'm about to say is right or wrong, because here's my key point: Cleinias and Megillus, I deny that someone who harms another involuntarily is committing an involuntary "injury." I wouldn't legislate about such an act as if it were an involuntary injury. I'd say that such a harm, whether great or small, isn't really an injury at all.

And here's the flip side: when a benefit is wrongly given, the person who gave it can often be said to have committed an injustice. You see, my friends, the mere act of giving or taking something away shouldn't automatically be labeled just or unjust. What the lawmaker needs to examine is this: did the person act from a just intention and principle, or not? He has to keep his eye on the distinction between injustice and mere harm. When there's harm, the law should, as far as possible, make it good -- saving what's been ruined, raising up what's fallen, mending what's been broken or wounded. And once compensation has been provided for the injustice, the law should always try to turn the parties involved from feelings of hostility into friendship.

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: Now, as for unjust harms -- and unjust gains, too, when injustice brings profit -- we can heal as many of these as are capable of being healed. Think of them as diseases of the soul. And the cure for injustice runs in this direction:

Cleinias: What direction?

Athenian: When anyone commits an injustice, large or small, the law will instruct and compel him either never to do such a thing again, or never to do it willingly, or at least to do it far less often. And in addition, he must pay for the damage he caused. Whether the goal is achieved through words or actions, through pleasure or pain, through granting or removing privileges, through fines or rewards -- whatever method the law uses to make a person hate injustice and love the nature of justice, or at least stop hating it -- this is the noblest work that law can do.

But if the lawmaker sees that someone is incurable, he'll assign a law and a penalty for that person. He knows perfectly well that for people like this, there's no benefit in continuing to live. By departing, they'd do a double service to everyone else: they'd serve as a warning to others not to offend, and they'd rid the city of bad citizens. In such cases -- and only such cases -- the lawmaker should impose death as the punishment.

Cleinias: What you've said seems very reasonable. But could you do me a favor and explain a bit more clearly the difference between harm and injustice, and how the voluntary and involuntary get tangled up in them?

Athenian: I'll try. Here's what's generally understood about the soul: one element in its nature is passion -- whether you call it a condition or a part of the soul -- and it's hard to fight against and resist. By sheer irrational force, it overturns a great many things.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: And pleasure is not the same as passion. It operates with a different kind of power, achieving its goals through persuasion and the force of deception.

Cleinias: Quite true.

Athenian: And we can truthfully say that ignorance is a third cause of crimes. But the lawmaker should usefully divide ignorance into two kinds. There's simple ignorance, which leads to lighter offenses. And then there's double ignorance -- ignorance combined with the delusion that you're wise. Someone under this second kind's influence thinks he knows everything about matters he actually knows nothing about. When this double ignorance is backed by power and strength, the lawmaker will treat it as the source of enormous and monstrous crimes. But when it's accompanied by weakness, it produces only the mistakes of children and old people. The lawmaker will treat these as mere errors and write the mildest and most merciful laws for them.

Cleinias: You're perfectly right.

Athenian: We all observe that some people are superior to pleasure and passion, and others are inferior to them. That's a common thing to say.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: But nobody has ever heard anyone say that one person is "superior to ignorance" and another "inferior to it."

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: Yet all three of these -- passion, pleasure, and ignorance -- drive people toward fulfilling their desires, often pulling them in opposite directions at the same time.

Cleinias: Yes, that happens often.

Athenian: And now I can define clearly and without ambiguity what I mean by justice and injustice. When anger and fear, pleasure and pain, jealousy and desire tyrannize over the soul -- whether or not they actually cause harm -- I call that injustice. But when a person's belief about what's best -- wherever in human nature you think this faculty resides, whether in states or in individuals -- has control of the soul and directs the person's life, then even if it's sometimes mistaken, whatever is done in accordance with it should be called just. The governing principle in each person that obeys this rational rule is what's best for their whole life, and it's to be called just -- even though harm done through honest mistake is often thought by many to be "involuntary injustice."

Let's not quarrel about terminology. We've identified three sources of wrongdoing. Let me bring them back into sharper focus. The first was the painful kind -- what we call anger and fear.

Cleinias: Right.

Athenian: The second consists of pleasures and desires. And the third involves hopes and aspirations toward what's truly best -- though this last category subdivides into three. So now we have five sources of human action, and for these five we'll create two kinds of law.

Cleinias: What are the two kinds?

Athenian: One kind covers acts of violence done openly, in broad daylight. The other covers acts done in darkness, through secret deception -- and sometimes with both violence and deception combined. The laws dealing with this second kind should be especially severe.

Cleinias: Naturally.

Athenian: Now let's return from this digression and finish the work of legislation. We've already enacted laws about temple robbers, traitors, and those who corrupt the laws to overthrow the government. It's quite possible that someone commits one of these crimes while in a state of madness, or while suffering from disease, or under the influence of extreme old age, or in a fit of childish foolishness -- essentially being no better than a child. If this is demonstrated to the judges trying the case, on the appeal of the criminal or his advocate, and the judges determine that this was indeed his condition when he committed the offense, then he simply has to pay for whatever damage he caused. Beyond that, he's exempt from other penalties -- unless he killed someone and has blood on his hands. In that case, he must leave for another land and country, and live there for a year. If he returns before the legally appointed time, or even sets foot in his native land, the guardians of the law shall confine him in the public prison for two years, and then release him.

Now that we've begun talking about homicide, let's try to lay down laws for every different kind. First: violent and involuntary homicides.

If anyone kills a friend accidentally during an athletic contest at the public games -- whether the victim dies immediately or later from the blows -- or if the same thing happens during war, military exercises, or practice combat (whether armed or unarmed), ordered by the authorities, then once the killer has been purified according to the law from Delphi governing such matters, he shall be innocent.

The same applies to doctors: if their patient dies against their will, the law holds them blameless.

If someone kills another person with his own hand, but unintentionally -- whether unarmed or with some weapon or projectile, or by giving food or drink, or through the application of heat or cold, or by suffocation, whether by his own hand or through the agency of others -- he shall be considered the responsible party and shall suffer one of the following penalties:

If he kills someone else's slave, believing the slave was his own, he must compensate the dead man's master for the loss, or pay a fine of twice the slave's value as assessed by the judges. He must also undergo purification rites -- greater and more numerous than those for someone who killed at the games. What exactly these rites are, the divinely appointed interpreters shall have authority to declare.

If a man kills his own slave, he shall undergo the legally required purification and be clear of the homicide charge.

If a man unintentionally kills a free person, he shall undergo the same purification as someone who killed a slave. But he should also take to heart an ancient story, which goes like this: A person who has suffered a violent death, when newly dead -- if he had the soul of a free man in life -- is angry at the one who killed him. Being himself filled with terror and panic from his violent end, he's shaken when he sees his killer walking around in all his familiar haunts. This disturbs the dead man's spirit, and his disturbance, combined with the killer's guilty conscience, is transmitted to the murderer with overwhelming force, destabilizing both his actions and his mind. For this reason, the killer must stay away from all the victim's usual places for an entire year, keeping clear of any spot the dead man frequented, throughout the whole country. If the dead man was a foreigner, the killer must also stay away from the foreigner's home country for the same period.

If someone voluntarily obeys this law, the victim's next of kin, seeing that everything has been done properly, shall take pity on him, make peace with him, and show him every kindness. But if someone disobeys -- if he dares to visit any of the temples and sacrifice while still impure, or refuses to stay in exile for the appointed time -- then the next of kin shall bring him to trial for murder. If convicted, every part of his punishment shall be doubled.

And if the next of kin fails to prosecute the crime? Then the pollution falls on his own head. The murdered man will fix the guilt on his kinsman, and anyone who wishes may compel the negligent relative to leave the country for five years, according to law.

If a foreigner unintentionally kills another foreigner living in the city, anyone who chooses may prosecute the case under the same rules. If the killer is a resident alien, he must leave for one year. If he's a complete foreigner, then in addition to the purification -- whether he killed a foreigner, a resident alien, or a citizen -- he shall be banished for life from the country governed by our laws. If he returns illegally, the guardians of the law shall put him to death and hand over his property, if he has any, to the victim's nearest relative. If he's shipwrecked and driven onto the coast against his will, he may camp on the seashore with his feet in the water, watching for a chance to sail away. If he's brought ashore by land and isn't his own master, the first magistrate he encounters in the city shall release him and send him safely across the border.

Next: if someone kills a free person with his own hand, and the deed is done in a fit of passion. Here we have to draw an important distinction. There are two scenarios: First, when someone suddenly and without any intention to kill causes another's death through blows or the like, on a momentary impulse, and immediately regrets it. Second, when someone is insulted in word or deed, nurses the desire for revenge, and intentionally kills, feeling no remorse afterward.

We have to treat these as two distinct kinds of homicide, both arising from passion. They occupy a middle ground between the fully voluntary and the fully involuntary, though each is really only a shadow or likeness of one or the other. The person who nurses his anger and takes revenge deliberately, after an interval and with calculated planning, resembles the voluntary killer. The person who acts instantly, in the heat of the moment, without premeditation, approaches the involuntary -- but even he isn't truly involuntary. He's only an image of the involuntary.

So when it comes to homicides committed in hot blood, it's genuinely difficult to decide whether we should classify them as voluntary or involuntary. The best and truest approach is to treat them as likenesses -- one resembling the voluntary, the other the involuntary -- and distinguish them based on whether there was premeditation or not. We should punish more severely those who kill with angry premeditation, and more leniently those who strike in the moment without forethought. What resembles the greater evil deserves heavier punishment; what resembles the lesser evil, lighter punishment. That's the rule our laws will follow.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: Let's proceed. If anyone kills a free person with his own hand, and the deed is done in a moment of anger without premeditation, the offender shall suffer the same penalties as someone who committed involuntary homicide, plus an exile of two years -- to learn to control his passions.

But someone who kills from passion with premeditation shall likewise suffer the base penalties, plus an exile of three years instead of two. His punishment is longer because his passion was greater.

Here's how their return should work -- and this is where the law runs into difficulty, because sometimes the killer judged by law to be worse is actually the less cruel one, while the one judged less cruel may really be worse and may have carried out the killing more savagely, whereas the other may have been gentler. But generally, the degrees of guilt will be as I've described. The guardians of the law must weigh all these factors.

When a killer of either kind has completed his term of exile, the guardians shall send twelve judges to the borders of the land. These judges will have spent the intervening time investigating the criminals' behavior during exile. They'll decide whether to pardon the killer and allow his return, and the killers must accept their judgment.

But if someone who has returned home commits the same act again in a moment of anger, he shall be exiled permanently. If he returns, he shall suffer the same fate as the foreigner in the analogous case.

If someone kills his own slave, he shall undergo purification. If someone kills another person's slave in anger, he shall pay the owner twice the slave's value.

If any killer disobeys the law and pollutes the marketplace, the games, or the temples without undergoing purification, anyone who wishes may bring suit against the victim's next of kin for permitting this, and against the murderer as well. The accuser may compel the one to demand, and the other to undergo, double fines and double purification rites. The accuser himself shall receive the fine in accordance with the law.

If a slave kills his master in a fit of passion, the dead man's relatives may do with the killer whatever they please -- provided they don't spare his life. They'll be considered ritually pure.

If a slave kills a free person who isn't his master, the slave's owner must surrender him to the victim's relatives. They are required to put him to death, but may do so in any manner they choose.

And if -- though this is rare, it does sometimes happen -- a father or mother kills a son or daughter in a moment of passion, by blows or some other violence, the killer shall undergo the same purification as in other cases and be exiled for three years. When the exile returns, the husband must separate from the wife, and the wife from the husband. They may never again have children together, share a roof, or participate in the same sacred rites with the family of the person they killed. Anyone who violates these rules by committing an act of impiety may be brought to trial by anyone who wishes.

If a husband kills his wife in a fit of anger, or a wife kills her husband, the killer shall undergo the same purification, and the exile shall be three years. When the killer returns, he or she may have no part in sacred rites with their children and may not sit at the same table with them. Any parent or child who disobeys shall be liable to prosecution for impiety by anyone who wishes.

If a brother or sister kills a sibling in a fit of passion, the same purification and exile shall apply as for parents who kill their offspring. They may not live under the same roof or share in the sacred rites of those family members who've lost a brother or child. Anyone who disobeys shall be subject to the law of impiety.

If anyone is so violent in his rage against his parents that he dares to kill one of them in the madness of his anger -- and if the murdered parent, before dying, freely forgives the killer -- then let the killer undergo the purification prescribed for involuntary homicide, completing all the required rites, and he shall be considered purified.

But if the parent does not forgive? Then the perpetrator of such a deed is subject to multiple laws at once. He faces the harshest penalties for assault, for impiety, and for robbery of a temple -- because he has robbed his parent of life. If a person could be killed more than once, the one who kills his father or mother in a fit of rage would most justly deserve many deaths.

Think about it: this is the one person whom no law in the world will allow to kill his parents, even in self-defense -- even if his parents are trying to kill him. The lawmaker will command him to endure any extremity rather than commit that act. How, then, could someone who does commit it deserve any punishment less than death? Death, then, shall be the penalty for anyone who kills a father or mother in a fit of passion.

If brother kills brother in a civil conflict, or in any similar circumstance, and the other struck first, and the killer was only defending himself -- let him be free from guilt, as he would be if he'd killed an enemy. The same applies if a citizen kills a citizen, or a foreigner kills a foreigner, in self-defense.

If a foreigner kills a citizen or a citizen kills a foreigner in self-defense, the same rule applies. Likewise if a slave kills a slave. But if a slave kills a free person in self-defense, he is subject to the same law as someone who kills a parent. And the rule about a parent's deathbed forgiveness -- the remission of penalty in cases of parricide -- shall apply equally to every other such remission. Whenever a victim voluntarily forgives the person who harmed him, believing the act was involuntary, the perpetrator shall undergo purification and remain in exile for one year, according to law.

Enough about violent, involuntary, and passion-driven murders. Now we must address voluntary crimes -- killings done with full injustice and premeditation, driven by pleasures, desires, and jealousies.

Cleinias: Very good.

Athenian: Let's start by identifying, as best we can, the various kinds and causes. The greatest cause is lust -- desire that seizes a soul already maddened by craving. And this lust is most commonly found where the passion is strongest and most widespread among the masses: I mean the power of wealth, which breeds endless desires for more and more, arising from both natural disposition and a wretched lack of education.

And what causes this bad education? The false praise of wealth that's trumpeted everywhere, among both Greeks and non-Greeks. People treat wealth as the greatest of goods, when in reality it's only the third greatest. This is the lie that damages both them and their descendants. Here's the truth that should be proclaimed in every state: wealth exists for the sake of the body, just as the body exists for the sake of the soul. Wealth and body are good things -- nature intends them to serve the soul -- but they rank below it. Third in the order of excellence. This principle teaches us that whoever wants to be happy shouldn't seek to be rich -- or rather, he should seek to be rich justly and with self-control. If people did that, there'd be no murders in our cities requiring purification by yet more blood.

But as I said from the beginning, greed is the chief cause and source of the worst cases of voluntary homicide.

The second cause is ambition. It breeds jealousy -- a troublesome companion, most of all for the jealous person himself, but also a danger to the leading citizens of the state.

The third cause is cowardly and unjust fear. When someone is doing something, or has done something, that he desperately wants to keep secret, he'll kill anyone who might expose him -- if he can't find another way to silence them.

Let all of that serve as a prelude to crimes of violence in general. And I must also mention a tradition that's firmly believed by many and received from those who are learned in the sacred mysteries: they say that such deeds will be punished in the underworld, and that when the perpetrators return to this world, they'll suffer the same fate they inflicted -- ending their lives in the same manner, at another's hand.

If the person contemplating murder believes this, and the mere prelude makes him dread such a penalty, there's no need to go further with the law. But for those who won't listen, let the following law be proclaimed and recorded:

Whoever deliberately and with premeditation kills any of his own kinsmen with his own hand shall first be stripped of all legal rights. He shall not pollute the temples, the marketplace, the harbors, or any other public gathering place -- whether or not anyone formally forbids him, because the law itself, speaking for the entire state, forbids him, and always has, and always will.

If a cousin or closer relative of the dead person, whether on the father's or mother's side, fails to prosecute and have the killer declared an outlaw -- then that relative is tainted by the pollution himself and incurs the hatred of the gods. The curse of the law calls down human voices against him too. And beyond that, he becomes liable to prosecution by anyone willing to seek justice on behalf of the dead.

Whoever wishes to avenge a murder shall observe all the required purification ceremonies -- lavations and whatever else the god commands in cases of this kind. He shall issue a formal proclamation and then compel the perpetrator to face justice according to law.

The lawmaker can easily show that all this must be accompanied by prayers and sacrifices to the gods who are concerned with preventing murders. But which gods, exactly, and what the proper procedure should be for conducting these trials in accordance with religious law -- that the guardians of the law, together with the interpreters and prophets and the god himself, shall determine. Once they've determined it, they'll carry on with the prosecution.

The case shall be tried by the same judges appointed for temple robbery cases. If convicted, the killer shall be put to death and shall not be buried in the country of the person he murdered -- that would be both shameless and impious.

If he flees and refuses to stand trial, let him flee forever. But if he ever sets foot anywhere in the murdered person's country, the first relative of the deceased -- or any other citizen -- who encounters him may kill him without penalty, or else bind him and deliver him to the magistrates among the case's judges, so they can execute him.

The prosecutor shall require the accused to post bail -- three sureties deemed sufficient by the magistrates trying the case, who will guarantee the accused's appearance at trial. If the accused is unwilling or unable to provide these sureties, the magistrates shall take him into custody, hold him in chains, and produce him on the day of trial.

If someone doesn't commit a murder with his own hands but engineers another person's death -- being the author of the deed in intention and design -- and then continues living in the city with a soul stained by the guilt of murder, he shall be tried the same way, with one exception: the surety requirement. If found guilty, his body may be buried in his native land after execution. In all other respects, his case shall be treated the same as the previous one.

Whether a foreigner kills a citizen, a citizen kills a foreigner, or a slave kills a slave -- there shall be no difference when it comes to murder by one's own hand versus murder by contrivance, except regarding the sureties. As we said, sureties shall be required only of the person who actually committed the killing, and the prosecutor shall bind them over at the time of accusation.

If a slave is convicted of deliberately killing a free person, either by his own hand or by contrivance, the public executioner shall lead him toward the dead man's tomb, to a spot where he can see it, and inflict as many lashes as the person who caught him orders. If the slave survives the lashing, he shall then be put to death.

If anyone kills a slave who has done nothing wrong -- killing him out of fear that the slave might reveal some shameful or criminal secret, or for any similar reason -- he shall face the same penalty for murder as if he'd killed a citizen.

There are things that are terrible and unpleasant to legislate about, but impossible to leave unaddressed. If, for instance, there are murders of family members -- perpetrated by relatives, either by their own hands or through scheming, and done voluntarily with pure malice -- the sort of thing that happens most often in poorly governed and poorly educated states, though it might occur even where you'd least expect it. Let me repeat the story we told a little while ago, in the hope that anyone hearing it will be more inclined to voluntarily refrain from murders that are utterly abominable.

The tale -- or myth, or saying, or whatever we should call it -- has been clearly set forth by priests of old: the justice that guards and avenges the blood of kindred follows the law of retaliation, and decrees that whoever has committed murder must inevitably suffer what he has done. He who has killed a father shall himself be killed by his own children in some future time. He who has killed a mother shall of necessity be reborn as a woman and lose his life at the hands of his own offspring in ages to come. Where a family's blood has been polluted, there is no other purification. The stain cannot be washed away until the murderous soul has given life for life, appeasing and laying to rest the wrath of the entire family.

These are the retributions of heaven, and they should be enough to deter anyone. But if they're not -- if some fatal impulse drives a person to deliberately and with full intent murder his father, mother, siblings, or children -- then the earthly lawmaker legislates as follows:

The same proclamations about outlawry and the same surety requirements from the earlier cases shall apply. If convicted, the servants of the judges and the magistrates shall execute him at an appointed place outside the city, where three roads meet. There they shall strip his body naked. Each of the magistrates, on behalf of the whole city, shall take a stone and cast it upon the dead man's head -- thus symbolically delivering the city from pollution. After that, they shall carry him to the borders of the land and cast him out, unburied, according to law.

And what about the person who kills the one who, as people say, should be his own best friend? I mean the suicide -- the person who violently deprives himself of his allotted share of life, not because the law of the state requires it, not because some painful and unavoidable catastrophe has befallen him, not because he's been made to suffer irremediable and intolerable shame, but who out of sheer laziness or lack of courage imposes upon himself an unjust penalty.

For this person -- what purification rites should there be, and what kind of burial? God knows. The next of kin should consult the interpreters and the relevant laws, and follow their instructions. Those who die in this way shall be buried alone. No one shall be laid beside them. They shall be buried without ceremony or honor in the uncultivated border areas between the twelve districts, in nameless places. No column or inscription shall mark where they lie.

If a beast of burden or any other animal causes someone's death -- except in the case of a public athletic competition -- the kinsmen of the deceased shall prosecute the animal for murder. The wardens of the countryside, as many as the kinsmen appoint, shall try the case. If the animal is convicted, it shall be killed and cast beyond the borders.

And if any lifeless object causes someone's death -- except for a thunderbolt or some other divine missile from the gods -- whether a person is killed by something falling on him or by his falling on it, the next of kin shall appoint the nearest neighbor as judge, thereby absolving himself and the whole family of guilt. The guilty object shall be cast beyond the border, just as we said about animals.

If someone is found dead and the killer is unknown, and after a thorough search can't be found, the same proclamation and interdict shall be issued as in previous cases. They shall proceed against the unknown killer, and a herald shall announce in the marketplace that the person who killed so-and-so, having been convicted of murder, shall never set foot in the temples or anywhere in the dead man's country. If he appears and is discovered, he shall die and be cast out unburied beyond the border.

Let this, then, stand as our law on murder. And let cases of this sort be handled accordingly.

Now let's address the cases and circumstances in which a killer is rightly free from guilt.

If someone catches a thief breaking into his house at night, and kills him, he shall be guiltless. If someone kills a mugger in self-defense, he shall be guiltless. Anyone who does violence to a free woman or a young person shall be killed with impunity by the victim, or by the victim's father, brothers, or sons. If a man finds his wife being assaulted, he may kill the assailant and be guiltless in the eyes of the law. And if someone kills another person while defending his father, mother, children, siblings, or spouse from deadly harm -- when those family members have done nothing wrong -- he shall most certainly be guiltless.

So much, then, for the nurturing and education of the living human soul -- the training without which a person cannot live, and the punishments for violent deaths. Let this be enacted.

We've already discussed the nurturing and education of the body. What comes next in order is violence that people commit against one another -- voluntary and involuntary assaults -- which we'll now distinguish, as best we can, by their nature and frequency, determine the appropriate penalties, and assign them their proper place in our legal code.

Even the most basic lawmaker would agree that wounds and injuries should come next after homicide. Let wounds be classified the same way we classified homicides: involuntary wounds, wounds inflicted in passion or from fear, and wounds inflicted voluntarily and with premeditation.

Before laying out these laws, we need to issue a general proclamation along these lines: Human beings must have laws and conform to them, or their lives would be as bad as those of the most savage beasts.
