And here's why: no person's nature is capable, on its own, of knowing what's best for human society. And even if someone did know, he wouldn't always be able -- or willing -- to put that knowledge into practice.

First, there's the difficulty of recognizing that the true art of politics concerns the public good, not the private good. The public good is what holds states together; private interest only tears them apart. Both public and private good -- for individuals and for states alike -- are better served when the state's interests come first, the individual's second.

Second, even if someone understands this in theory, the moment he has absolute and unchecked power he'll never hold firm to these principles or keep putting the public good ahead of his own. Human nature will always pull him toward selfishness and greed, making him avoid pain and chase pleasure without any rational basis. He'll push these desires to the front, crowding out what's more just and better. And so this darkness in his soul will eventually fill both him and the entire city with evils.

Now, if a person were born so divinely gifted that he could naturally grasp the truth, he'd have no need of laws to govern him. There's no law or system that's higher than knowledge, and it would be unholy to treat a genuinely wise mind as the subject or slave of anything -- it should be the ruler of all. I'm talking about a mind that's truly free and in harmony with nature. But such a mind barely exists anywhere. And that's exactly why we must choose law and order, which are second best. Laws can only see things as they generally are; they can't survey every individual case.

That's why I've said what I've said.

Now we need to determine what penalty a person should pay or suffer for hurting or wounding someone. It's easy to imagine all the questions that come up in these cases: What was the wound? Who was wounded? How, and when? The details are endless, and they differ enormously from case to case. It's impossible to let courts decide all of them, but equally impossible to let them decide none. There's one thing courts must always determine: the question of fact -- did it happen? But for the lawmaker to try to prescribe the punishment in every last case, from the smallest to the greatest? That's just about impossible.

Cleinias: So what's the solution?

Athenian: The solution is that some things should be left to the courts, and some things the lawmaker must decide himself.

Cleinias: And which should the lawmaker decide, and which should he leave to the courts?

Athenian: Here's my answer. In a state where the courts are corrupt and silent -- where judges hide their opinions and decide cases behind closed doors -- or worse, in a state where the courts are rowdy and chaotic, like a theater audience, clapping and booing for one side or the other -- that's a very serious problem affecting the whole state. A lawmaker forced to write laws for courts like that should keep their power to a minimum, deciding the punishments for only the smallest offenses and handling most things himself.

But when a state has good courts, with well-trained judges who've been rigorously tested, it's both fair and advantageous to let them determine most penalties for the guilty. So we shouldn't be criticized for not legislating about that whole range of cases that even far less educated judges than ours could handle -- assigning each offense what's proportionally due to both the perpetrator and the victim. We believe the judges we're training will be the best qualified people in the world to handle these decisions, and we can entrust the greater part to them.

Still, as I've said before, we should give the judges an outline -- a model of the punishments to be imposed -- so they won't cross the line of justice. That practice served us well before, and now, as we return to the work of legislation, it's worth repeating.

Here's the law on wounding: If anyone has the purpose and intent to kill someone who is not his enemy and whom the law does not authorize him to kill, and he wounds him but fails to kill him, the person who had that intent and inflicted that wound deserves no pity. He deserves no consideration whatsoever -- he should be regarded as a murderer and tried for murder.

And yet, out of respect for the fortune that in a way smiled on him, and the providence that took pity on both him and his victim -- saving one from a fatal blow and the other from a cursed fate -- as a thank-offering to this guiding power, and so as not to defy its will, the law will set aside the death penalty in such a case. Instead, it will require the offender to emigrate to a neighboring city for the rest of his life, where he'll retain all his possessions. But if he caused lasting injury to the victim, he must pay compensation as assessed by the court -- the same judges who would have tried the case if the victim had died.

If a child intentionally wounds a parent, or a servant his master -- death shall be the penalty.

If a brother or sister intentionally wounds a brother or sister and is found guilty -- death shall be the penalty.

If a husband wounds a wife, or a wife wounds a husband, with intent to kill, the offender shall go into permanent exile. If they have young children, the guardians shall manage the property and care for the children as orphans. If their children are grown, the children are under no obligation to support the exiled parent, but shall possess the property themselves.

If the person who meets with such a misfortune has no children, the relatives of the exile -- out to the degree of cousins' children, on both the father's and mother's side -- shall gather together, consult with the guardians of the law and the priests, and appoint one of the 5,040 citizens as heir of the house. Here's the reasoning: no house among the 5,040 belongs solely to its occupant or even to the whole family. It's the public and private property of the state. And the state should strive to have its houses as holy and blessed as possible.

If any house is stricken with misfortune and stained by impiety -- if the owner leaves no heirs but dies unmarried, or married but childless, having been executed for murder or some other crime against the gods or the state for which death is the explicit legal penalty -- or if a childless citizen has been permanently exiled: first, let that house be purified and undergo the proper expiatory rites. Then let the kinsmen of the house, together with the guardians of the law, search for the family in the state that has the highest reputation for virtue and also for good fortune -- one in which there are several sons. From that family, they shall take one son and introduce him to the father and forefathers of the dead man as their son. For the sake of the good omen, let him be called by that family name, so that he may continue their line, keep their hearth fire burning, and serve their sacred rites with better fortune than his predecessor had. Once they've offered this prayer, they shall make him the heir according to law. The offender, meanwhile, shall be left nameless, childless, and portionless when catastrophes like these overtake him.

Now, some boundaries in life don't quite touch each other -- there's a borderland between them. We were saying that acts done from passion are exactly like that: they fall in between the voluntary and the involuntary.

If someone is convicted of inflicting wounds in a fit of passion: first, if the wound is curable, he shall pay twice the amount of the injury. If the wound is incurable, four times the amount. If the wound is curable but causes great and notable disgrace to the wounded person, he shall pay fourfold.

And whenever someone, by wounding another, injures not just the victim but also the state -- by rendering the wounded person unable to defend his country against its enemies -- then, beyond all other penalties, he must compensate the state for the loss. In addition to serving his own terms of military service, he must serve on behalf of the disabled person and take his place in war. If he refuses, he shall be liable to prosecution for refusal of military service.

Whether the compensation should be twofold, threefold, or fourfold shall be determined by the judges who convict him.

If a brother wounds a brother in a similar way, the parents and relatives from both sides -- including children of cousins, whether on the father's or mother's side -- shall meet to judge the case and entrust the assessment of damages to the parents, as is natural. If the estimate is disputed, the paternal kinsmen shall determine it. If they can't reach agreement, they shall refer the matter to the guardians of the law.

When similar charges of wounding are brought by children against their parents, the required judges shall be people over sixty years of age who have children of their own -- not adopted. If the parent is convicted, these judges shall determine whether the parent ought to die or suffer some other punishment either greater than death or at any rate not much less. No relative of the accused shall serve as a judge, even if he meets the age requirement of the law.

If a slave in a fit of anger wounds a free person, the slave's owner shall hand him over to the wounded man, who may do as he pleases with him. If the owner doesn't hand him over, he must make good the injury himself. And if anyone claims the slave and the wounded man are conspiring together, let him argue the point. If he loses, he pays triple. If he wins, the free man who conspired with the slave shall be liable to prosecution for kidnapping.

If anyone unintentionally wounds another, he shall simply pay for the harm done. No lawmaker can control chance. In such cases, the judges shall be the same as those appointed when children sue their parents, and they shall assess the amount of the injury.

All the injuries we've discussed, and every kind of assault, are acts of violence. And here's the principle every man, woman, and child should understand: the elder has precedence over the younger in honor, both among the gods and among people who want to live in safety and happiness.

It's a foul thing, hateful to the gods, to see an older man assaulted by a younger one in the city. It's right and reasonable that a young man, when struck by an elder, should bear the blow patiently, storing up for himself the same honor when he's old.

Here's the law: Everyone shall show respect to anyone twenty or more years their senior, whether male or female, treating them as they would a father or mother. He shall keep his hands off anyone old enough to have been his parent, out of reverence for the gods who preside over birth. He shall likewise keep his hands off any foreigner, whether long-established or newly arrived. He shall not presume to correct such a person with blows, either as the aggressor or in self-defense.

If he believes a foreigner has struck him out of arrogance or insolence and deserves punishment, he shall bring the foreigner to the wardens of the city -- but he shall not strike him himself. That way the foreigner is kept far from the possibility of raising his hand against a citizen. The wardens of the city shall take the offender and examine him, remembering their duty to Zeus, the God of Strangers. If the foreigner appears to have struck the citizen unjustly, let him receive as many blows with the lash as he inflicted, and let his arrogance be put down. But if the foreigner is innocent, let them warn and rebuke the man who brought the complaint, and let both go.

If one person strikes another of the same age or somewhat older, who has no children -- whether it's an old man striking an old man or a young man striking a young man -- the person struck shall defend himself naturally, without a weapon, using only his hands.

Anyone over forty who dares to get into a brawl -- whether as the aggressor or in self-defense -- shall be considered rude, ill-mannered, and slavish. It's a disgraceful label, but that's exactly what makes it an appropriate punishment.

The obedient citizen will yield to such advice. But for the disobedient, here's the law: If anyone strikes a person who is twenty or more years older than himself -- first, any bystander who is not younger than the combatants and not their equal in age shall separate them, or be disgraced under the law. But if the bystander is the same age as the victim or younger, he shall defend the injured person as he would a brother, a father, or an even older relative.

Furthermore, anyone who dares to strike an elder shall be tried for assault, as I've said. If found guilty, he shall be imprisoned for at least a year. If the judges think a longer sentence is warranted, their decision is final.

If a foreigner or resident alien strikes someone twenty or more years his senior, the same rule about bystanders assisting shall apply. A convicted foreigner who isn't a resident shall be imprisoned for two years. A resident alien who violates the law shall be imprisoned for three years -- unless the court assigns a longer term.

Any bystander who was present during any of these incidents and failed to assist as the law requires shall be fined: one mina if he belongs to the highest property class; fifty drachmas for the second class; thirty for the third; twenty for the fourth. The court for these cases shall be composed of generals, taxiarchs, tribe-commanders, and cavalry commanders.

Laws serve two purposes: partly to instruct good people in how to live together as friends, and partly to deal with those who refuse to be instructed -- whose spirits can't be broken, softened, or kept from plunging into evil. These are the people who force the lawmaker to speak what he's about to speak. He legislates for them out of necessity, while hoping his laws will never need to be used.

Whoever dares to lay violent hands on his father or mother, or any still older relative -- having no fear of the wrath of the gods above or the punishments described in the world below -- whoever transgresses in contempt of ancient and universal traditions, as if he were too clever to believe in them, requires the most extreme preventive measures.

Now, death is not the worst thing that can happen to a person. The punishments said to await people in the underworld are far worse. But although these tales are absolutely true, they have no effect on souls like these. If they did, there would be no killers of mothers and no impious hands raised against parents. The punishments of this world, imposed during a person's lifetime, must therefore be at least as terrifying as what awaits below -- if that's even possible.

Here's the law: If anyone dares to strike his father or mother, or their parents, while being of sound mind -- then anyone present must come to the rescue, as we've already said. A resident alien who comes to the rescue shall be given the honor of a front-row seat at the games. If he doesn't come to the rescue, he shall suffer permanent exile. A non-resident who comes to the rescue shall receive public praise. One who doesn't shall receive public blame.

If a slave comes to the rescue, he shall be set free. If a slave doesn't come to the rescue, he shall receive a hundred lashes, administered by the wardens of the marketplace if the incident occurs there, or by the city wardens if it happens elsewhere in the city, or by the commanders of the country wardens if it happens in the countryside.

Anyone who's nearby at the time -- whether young or old, male or female, local resident or visitor -- must come to the rescue and denounce the attacker as impious. Anyone who fails to come to the rescue shall fall under the curse of Zeus, the god of kinship and of ancestors, according to law.

If anyone is found guilty of assaulting a parent, he shall first be banished forever from the city into the countryside, and he shall keep away from the temples. If he doesn't keep away, the wardens of the country shall punish him with beatings or in any way they see fit. If he returns to the city, he shall be put to death.

And if any free person eats or drinks with him, or has any kind of dealings with him, or even voluntarily touches him in passing -- that person shall not enter any temple, the marketplace, or the city until he has been purified. He must recognize that he's been tainted by a curse. If he disobeys the law and pollutes the city and the temples, any magistrate who sees this and fails to prosecute shall face it as the most serious charge at his performance review.

If a slave strikes a free person -- whether a foreigner or a citizen -- anyone present must come to the rescue or pay the prescribed penalty. The bystanders shall bind the slave and deliver him to the injured party. The injured party shall put the slave in chains and inflict as many lashes as he pleases. But after punishing him, he must return the slave to his master, as the law requires. He may not keep the slave or deprive the owner of his property.

Here's the law: A slave who strikes a free person without orders from a magistrate shall be received in chains by his owner from the person he struck. The owner shall not release the slave until the slave has convinced the person he struck that he deserves to be released.

The same laws shall apply in all relations between women and women, and between men and women.

Athenian

Now that we've covered assaults, let's wrap up all acts of violence under one general law. It goes like this: No one shall take or carry off anything belonging to their neighbor, and no one shall use another person's property without the owner's consent. These offenses are the source of all the problems we've been discussing — they always have been, and they always will be. The worst of them are the excesses and outrages of the young, and they're most serious when directed against religion — especially when they violate public and sacred rites, or the ceremonies shared by tribes and brotherhoods. The second most serious are offenses against private religious rites and burial places. Third in severity — leaving aside the cases we've already discussed — are insults against parents. Fourth are acts of violence against the authority of public officials, when someone takes, carries off, or uses their property without consent. And fifth is any violation of an individual citizen's rights that calls for legal remedy. We need a single law covering all of these.

Now, we've already laid out the punishments for sacrilege, whether committed through fraud or force. What we still need to determine is the punishment for those who speak or act with contempt toward the gods. But first, let's give them a warning. It goes something like this:

No one who genuinely believes in the gods, as the laws require, has ever willingly committed an unholy act or spoken an unlawful word. If someone does, it's because they hold one of three beliefs: first, that the gods don't exist at all; second, that they exist but don't care about human beings; or third, that they exist and care, but can be bought off with sacrifices and prayers.

Cleinias: So what should we say or do about these people?

Athenian: Well, my friend, let's first hear the objections they'll throw at us. I suspect they feel rather superior, and they won't hold back.

Cleinias: What objections?

Athenian: Something disrespectful, along these lines: "People of Athens, Sparta, and Knossos — fair enough: some of us deny the gods exist entirely, while others, as you say, think they don't care about us, and still others think they can be swayed by gifts. Now, you yourselves agreed that before punishing us and making threats, you should first argue your case and try to convince us. So we're taking you up on that. Try teaching us first — persuade us that gods exist, using solid evidence. Show us they're too good to be bribed or turned from the path of justice. Because when we hear the most respected poets, orators, prophets, and priests saying these things about the gods, most of us don't resolve to avoid wrongdoing — we figure we can do wrong and then make up for it afterward. From lawmakers who claim to be reasonable rather than harsh, we expect persuasion first. Prove to us that gods exist — if not more eloquently than others have tried, then at least more truthfully. Who knows? Maybe we'll listen. If that's a fair request, then accept our challenge."

Cleinias: But is it really that hard to prove the gods exist?

Athenian: How would you go about it?

Cleinias: How? Well, for starters — the earth, the sun, the stars, the entire universe, the beautiful order of the seasons, the way they're divided into years and months. And then there's the fact that every Greek and every foreigner believes in them.

Athenian: I'm worried, my dear friend — though I won't say I'm too concerned — about the contempt these godless people would pour on arguments like that. You don't really understand what's driving their unbelief. You assume they've just given in to pleasure-seeking.

Cleinias: Well, what other reason is there?

Athenian: One that you, living in a different world, would never guess.

Cleinias: What is it?

Athenian: A particularly devastating kind of ignorance — one that passes itself off as the highest wisdom.

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: In Athens, there are writings preserved — the kind of thing your more disciplined society refuses to tolerate. They discuss the gods in both prose and verse. The oldest of these works describe the origins of the heavens and the world, and before long they get into stories about how the gods were born and how they behaved toward each other. Whether these stories have been good or bad for people in other respects, I'd rather not criticize too harshly — they're ancient, after all. But when I consider them from the standpoint of how children should think about their parents, I can't call them praiseworthy, useful, or even true. But I'll say no more about the old writers.

It's the younger generation and their so-called wisdom that I can't let pass. Just look at what their ideas lead to. When you and I argue for the existence of the gods and point to the sun, moon, stars, and earth as evidence of divine power, someone schooled in these new philosophies will say those things are just earth and stone — lifeless matter that couldn't possibly care about human affairs. They'll say all religion is just a tissue of pretty words and make-believe.

Cleinias: Even one such teacher would be bad enough, Stranger. And you're saying there are many of them, which is far worse.

Athenian: So then — what do we do? Should we imagine that one of these godless people is confronting us, challenging the very basis of our legislation? Should we picture them saying, "How outrageous that you're making laws on the assumption that gods exist!" Should we defend ourselves? Or should we drop it and get back to the laws, before this introduction becomes longer than the legal code itself? Because if we're going to engage with the godless on their own terms — partly arguing, partly warning, partly reasoning — this discussion is going to stretch on for quite a while before we get to the actual laws.

Cleinias: Yes, but Stranger — how many times have we already said that on this occasion there's no reason to prefer brevity over thoroughness? As the saying goes, nobody's chasing us. It would be pitiful and absurd to choose the shorter path over the better one. It matters enormously that we prove, one way or another, that gods exist, that they're good, and that they care about justice more than humans do. A demonstration of this would be the finest and noblest introduction to all our laws. So let's take our time, without impatience or hurry, and marshal every argument we can. Let's bring the full force of persuasion to bear.

Athenian: Given how serious you are about this, I feel like offering a prayer that I'll succeed. But I need to press forward at once. Who can stay calm when called upon to prove that gods exist? Who can help feeling anger toward the people who have forced us into this argument? I'm talking about those who refuse to believe the stories they heard as infants from their mothers and nurses — stories repeated in play and in earnest, like charms; stories they heard in sacrificial prayers, accompanied by the sights and sounds that so delight children; stories they watched their parents act out with absolute conviction during sacrifices, praying to the gods with intense sincerity, prostrating themselves in worship. They've seen Greeks and foreigners alike, at sunrise and sunset, through every twist of fortune, kneeling and bowing — not as people who suspect the gods might not exist, but as people for whom the gods' existence is beyond all question and all doubt.

When people dismiss all of this on no solid grounds — as anyone with a shred of intelligence would agree — and when they force us to make the argument we're now making, how is anyone supposed to address them gently? And yet the attempt has to be made. It wouldn't do for half of humanity to go mad with indulgence while the other half goes mad with outrage. So we'll speak to these lost and misguided souls without passion. Let's imagine we've picked out one of them and are reasoning with him calmly, holding back our anger.

"My son," we'll say, "you're young. Time will change many of the opinions you hold now. Wait a while. Don't rush to judgment on the highest matters — and the highest of all, which you currently think is nothing, is this: knowing the gods rightly and living accordingly.

"Let me point out something important, something I'm quite sure about: you and your friends are not the first to hold this view about the gods. In every generation, more or fewer people have caught the same disease. I've known many of them, and I can tell you this: no one who adopted the belief that gods don't exist while young ever held that same view into old age. The other two opinions sometimes persist — the idea that gods exist but don't care about humans, or that they care but can be bribed — but the outright denial of the gods? That almost never lasts.

"So my advice is this: wait. Consider whether it might be true. Ask others, and above all, ask the lawgiver. In the meantime, be very careful not to offend against the gods. For the lawgiver's duty — now and always — is to teach you the truth about these matters."

Cleinias: Everything you've said so far, Stranger, is excellent.

Athenian: Quite true, Megillus and Cleinias. But I'm afraid we've stumbled onto a strange doctrine without realizing it.

Cleinias: What doctrine?

Athenian: One that many people consider the wisest of all.

Cleinias: Please, speak more plainly.

Athenian: The doctrine that everything that exists, has existed, or will exist comes about either by nature, by design, or by chance.

Cleinias: Isn't that true?

Athenian: Well, these philosophers are probably onto something. In any case, let's follow their reasoning and see what they and their students actually mean.

Cleinias: By all means.

Athenian: Here's what they say: the greatest and most magnificent things in the universe are the products of nature and chance, while the lesser things are the work of human craft. Art, they say, takes the raw materials that nature has already produced on a grand scale and shapes them into the smaller, less impressive things we typically call "artificial."

Cleinias: What do you mean exactly?

Athenian: I'll spell it out. They say that fire, water, earth, and air all exist by nature and chance — none of them by design. The larger bodies that came next — earth itself, the sun, the moon, the stars — were created from these entirely lifeless elements. These elements move around according to chance and various natural affinities: hot attracts cold, dry attracts moist, soft attracts hard, and so on through every combination of opposites thrown together by necessity. This is how the entire heavens were created, according to them — all the celestial bodies, all animals, all plants, all the seasons — not through the action of mind, they insist, not through any god or purposeful design, but purely through nature and chance.

Art, they say, came later — mortal art born from mortal beings. It produced certain playful imitations of reality, images that don't share much truth in themselves — the kind of thing music, painting, and the companion arts create. Some arts do have a more serious purpose and cooperate with nature: medicine, agriculture, gymnastics, for example. Politics, they say, cooperates with nature too, but to a lesser degree and with more artifice. And legislation, they claim, is entirely artificial — built on assumptions that have no basis in truth.

Cleinias: What do you mean by that?

Athenian: In the first place, my dear friend, these thinkers say that the gods don't exist by nature — they're human inventions, products of law and convention. Different gods in different places, depending on what the locals agreed upon when they wrote their laws. And they say that what's truly honorable is one thing by nature and another thing by law. Justice, they insist, has no existence in nature at all. People are constantly arguing about it and changing the rules, and whatever changes get made by human art and legislation have no natural basis — they're just whatever happened to get decided at the time.

This, my friends, is the kind of thing being said by people considered wise — by poets and prose writers alike — and it's seeping into the minds of the young. They're told that the highest form of right is might, and so the young fall into godlessness, believing the gods aren't the kind of beings the law tells them to imagine. This breeds political factions — philosophers inviting young people to live a "natural" life, which really means living in dominion over others rather than in lawful obedience.

Cleinias: What a terrifying picture you've painted, Stranger! Think of the damage this does to young people, and through them to entire families and cities!

Athenian: You're right, Cleinias. So what should the lawgiver do when this corruption has been festering for a long time? Should he simply stand up and threaten everyone — declaring that if they won't say and believe the gods are as the law describes, if they won't shape their conduct to match the law's standards of honor, justice, and virtue, then they'll face death, or beatings, or imprisonment, or loss of citizenship, or confiscation of property and exile? Or should the lawgiver, while making his laws, try to weave in the spirit of persuasion and soften his words as much as possible?

Cleinias: Why, Stranger, if there's any possibility of persuasion, then a lawgiver worth his salt should never tire of persuading! He should leave nothing unsaid in defense of the ancient belief that gods exist, and of all the truths you were just discussing. He should defend the proposition that law and craft both exist by nature — no less than nature itself — since they're creations of mind working in accordance with right reason. That's what you seem to be arguing, and I'm inclined to agree.

Athenian: Enthusiastic Cleinias! But aren't these things difficult for a crowd to grasp? And don't they take a dismally long time to explain?

Cleinias: Stranger, if we didn't lose patience when the subjects were drinking and music, shall we grow tired now when the subject is the gods and divine matters? And here's the great advantage of written legislation: the laws, once set down, sit still. They can be studied and reconsidered at any future time. So if they seem difficult at first hearing, there's no cause for alarm — even a slow reader can go over them again and again. And if they're long but useful, then it seems to me neither reasonable nor pious for anyone to refuse to examine them with all their might.

Megillus Stranger, I agree with everything Cleinias is saying.

Athenian: Yes, Megillus, and we should do exactly as he proposes. If godless arguments weren't scattered all across the world, there'd be no need to defend the existence of the gods. But since they are everywhere, such arguments are needed. And who should come to the defense of the most fundamental laws when they're under attack from bad people, if not the lawgiver himself?

Megillus No one is better suited for it.

Athenian: All right then, Cleinias — I need you as my partner here. Tell me: doesn't this materialist view hold that fire, water, earth, and air are the first elements of everything? That these people call this "nature"? And that they think the soul was formed afterward, out of these elements? This isn't just my guess about what they mean — it's exactly what they're saying.

Cleinias: Exactly.

Athenian: Then, by heaven, we've found the source of this misguided theory held by all these natural philosophers! Examine their arguments very carefully. This isn't a trivial matter — their godlessness is deeply serious. It's not just that they reason badly. It's that their bad reasoning misleads others. That's what I truly object to.

Cleinias: You're right. But I'd like to understand how this works.

Athenian: I'm afraid the argument will seem unusual.

Cleinias: Don't hold back, Stranger. I can see you're worried this discussion will carry us beyond the bounds of legislation. But if there's no other way to demonstrate our shared belief that gods exist — the very gods our laws are said to honor — then let's take this path, my friend.

Athenian: Then I need to lay out the strange argument these people make — the ones who manufacture the soul according to their godless theories. What they claim is the first cause of generation and destruction is actually last, and what's actually last they put first. That's the root of their mistake about the true nature of the gods.

Cleinias: I still don't follow.

Athenian: Nearly all of them, my friends, seem to be ignorant of the nature and power of the soul — especially its origin. They don't realize that the soul is among the first of all things, that it existed before all physical bodies, and that it's the primary cause of all their changes and transformations. And if this is true — if the soul is older than the body — then mustn't the things that belong to the soul's nature also be prior to the things that belong to the body?

Cleinias: They must be.

Athenian: Then thought, attention, mind, art, and law are prior to hard and soft, heavy and light. And the great original works and actions are works of design — they come first. Nature and the works of nature — though people wrongly apply the word "nature" to them — come second, and are governed by art and mind.

Cleinias: But why is the word "nature" wrong?

Athenian: Because the people who use it mean "nature" is the first creative power. But if soul turns out to be the primeval element — not fire or air — then in the truest sense, soul is what most deserves to be called "natural." And this is true if you can prove that soul is older than the body. Otherwise, it isn't.

Cleinias: You're quite right.

Athenian: Shall we take this up as our next point of investigation?

Cleinias: Absolutely.

Athenian: Let's be careful, though. This argument has a youthful, deceptive appeal — it could easily trip us old men up and make fools of us. We might be aiming at something grand and end up missing even the basic point. Here's what I suggest. Imagine the three of us have to cross a swift river. I'm the youngest of us and the most experienced with rivers, so I'll go first by myself, while you two wait safely on the bank. I'll test whether the river is safe for older men like yourselves. If it is, I'll call you over, and my experience will help guide you across. If it's too deep, the only one at risk will be me. Fair proposal?

What I mean is this: the argument ahead is probably going to be too difficult and too deep for you, since you're not accustomed to this kind of philosophical questioning. A torrent of questions could leave you dizzy and confused — not a pleasant experience. So I think it's better if I ask the questions and answer them myself while you listen from safe ground. That way I can work through the entire proof that the soul is prior to the body.

Cleinias: Excellent, Stranger. Please do exactly that.

Athenian: Come, then. If ever there was a time to call on the gods, this is it. Let's invoke them with all seriousness to help us demonstrate their own existence. Holding tight to that rope, let's venture into the deep.

Here's how I'd start. If someone asks me: "Stranger, is everything at rest and nothing in motion? Or is the opposite true? Or are some things in motion and some at rest?" I'd answer: some things are in motion, some at rest.

"And don't things that move move in a place, and things at rest stay in a place?" Of course. "And some move or rest in just one place, while others move through more than one?" By "moving in one place," we mean the kind of motion you see when a globe spins — rotation around a center, like a ball on a lathe. In that rotation, the same motion carries both the larger and the smaller circle around at the same time, distributed proportionally — faster at the rim, slower at the center. Here's something remarkable: the same motion produces different speeds in proportion to the size of the circles. "Quite true."

And by "moving through more than one place," we mean things that travel from point to point, sometimes having one center of motion, sometimes more than one, as they spin on their axes. When they collide with something stationary, they split apart. When they meet other bodies moving toward them from the opposite direction, they combine into a unity. "Agreed."

And when they combine, they grow. When they divide, they diminish — assuming their basic constitution holds. If it doesn't, that's a different kind of destruction entirely.

"When are all things created, and how?" They're created when a first principle grows from one dimension to two, from two to three, and at the third dimension becomes perceptible to the senses. Everything that changes and moves in this way is in the process of coming into being. It has real existence only when it's at rest. When it passes into a different state, it's destroyed completely.

Have we now covered all the kinds of motion and classified them — with the exception, my friends, of two?

Cleinias: Which two?

Athenian: Precisely the two that our whole inquiry is really about.

Cleinias: Say more.

Athenian: Our inquiry is ultimately about the soul, isn't it?

Cleinias: It certainly is.

Athenian: Then let's say there's one kind of motion that can move other things but can't move itself. That's one kind. And there's another kind that can move both itself and other things — working through combination and separation, growth and diminution, generation and destruction. That's another kind of motion.

Cleinias: Granted.

Athenian: Let's call the motion that moves other things but is itself moved by something else the ninth kind. And the motion that moves itself and everything else — the motion involved in every action and every experience, the true source of change and motion in all things — let's call that the tenth.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: Now, of these ten kinds of motion, which should we say is the mightiest and most powerful?

Cleinias: I'd have to say the motion that can move itself is ten thousand times superior to all the others.

Athenian: Well said. But may I correct something I said earlier?

Cleinias: What correction?

Athenian: When I called self-motion the tenth kind, that wasn't quite right.

Cleinias: What was wrong about it?

Athenian: In the true order of things, what I called the tenth is actually the first — first in origin and first in power. And what comes after it, the motion I strangely labeled the ninth, is really second.

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: Think about it. When one thing is moved by another, and that thing by something else, and so on — can there ever be a first mover in such a chain? How could something moved by something else ever be the beginning of change? That's impossible. But when the self-moved moves other things, and those move still others, and so thousands upon thousands of bodies are set in motion — mustn't the very beginning of all this motion be the change of the self-moving principle?

Cleinias: Absolutely. I agree completely.

Athenian: Or let me put it another way. If, as most of these philosophers have the nerve to claim, everything were originally at rest in one undifferentiated mass — which of our principles of motion would be the first to arise?

Cleinias: Obviously self-motion. There could be no change arising from an external cause in a mass where everything is at rest. The change would have to begin within.

Athenian: Then we must conclude that self-motion, as the origin of all motions — the first to arise among things at rest and the first among things in motion — is the oldest and mightiest principle of change. And what is changed by another thing but sets other things in motion is second.

Cleinias: That's exactly right.

Athenian: Now let me ask a question at this point in the argument.

Cleinias: What question?

Athenian: If we saw this self-moving power existing in something made of earth, water, fire, or any combination of elements — what would we call it?

Cleinias: Are you asking whether we'd call a self-moving power "life"?

Athenian: That's exactly what I'm asking.

Cleinias: We certainly would.

Athenian: And when we find soul in anything, we have to say the same thing — that it has life?

Cleinias: We do.

Athenian: Now, bear with me here — you'd agree that we can know things in three ways?

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: We can know the essence of something, the definition of that essence, and the name. And there are two kinds of questions we can ask about anything.

Cleinias: Two?

Athenian: Sometimes a person gives you the name and asks for the definition. Other times they give you the definition and ask for the name. Let me illustrate.

Cleinias: Go ahead.

Athenian: Take numbers. Some numbers can be divided into two equal parts. When a number can be divided that way, we call it "even." So the name is "even," and the definition is "a number divisible into two equal parts."

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: My point is this: whether we start from the definition and arrive at the name, or start from the name and arrive at the definition, we're talking about the same thing. "Even" means "a number divisible into two equal parts" — same thing, two ways of expressing it.

Cleinias: Exactly.

Athenian: So what's the definition of what we call "soul"? Can we give it any other definition than the one we've already arrived at — the motion that can move itself?

Cleinias: Are you saying that the essence we've defined as "the self-moved" is the same thing that goes by the name "soul"?

Athenian: Yes. And if that's true, can we still say there's anything lacking in the proof that soul is the first origin and moving power of everything that exists, has existed, or will exist — and of all their opposites? Soul has been clearly shown to be the source of change and motion in all things.

Cleinias: Nothing is lacking. It's been proven beyond any doubt that soul, as the source of all motion, is the oldest of all things.

Athenian: And the kind of motion produced in one thing by the action of another — motion that never involves any self-moving power, being merely the change of lifeless matter — that's second in rank, or as far down the scale as you like?

Cleinias: Exactly.

Athenian: Then we're right — absolutely and perfectly right — when we say that soul is prior to body, that the body is second and comes afterward, and that the body was born to obey the soul, which is its ruler.

Cleinias: Nothing could be more true.

Athenian: And do you remember what we agreed to earlier — that if soul is prior to the body, then the things belonging to soul must also be prior to the things belonging to the body?

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: Then character, temperament, wishes, reasoning, true beliefs, reflections, and memories are all prior to length, width, depth, and physical strength — if soul is indeed prior to the body.

Cleinias: Necessarily so.

Athenian: And the next step: mustn't we accept that soul is the cause of good and evil, of what's noble and base, of justice and injustice, and of all other opposites — if we're saying soul is the cause of all things?

Cleinias: We must.

Athenian: And since soul orders and inhabits everything that moves, in whatever way it moves, mustn't we also say that soul orders the heavens?

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: One soul, or more than one? More than one — I'll answer that for you. We certainly can't suppose there are fewer than two: one that's the source of good, and another that's the source of evil.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: Yes. So soul directs all things in heaven, on earth, and in the sea through its own movements — movements we describe with words like will, deliberation, attention, reasoning, true and false belief, joy, sorrow, confidence, fear, hatred, love, and all the other primary impulses of the soul. These in turn set in motion the secondary, physical changes in material things, guiding everything toward growth and decay, composition and decomposition, and all the qualities that accompany them — heat and cold, heaviness and lightness, hardness and softness, whiteness and blackness, bitterness and sweetness.

When soul truly receives divine mind and works with it, she governs everything rightly, guiding all things toward their proper fulfillment. But when she keeps company with folly, she does exactly the opposite. Shall we accept all this, or do we still have doubts?

Cleinias: No room for doubt at all.

Athenian: So shall we say that the soul which governs heaven and earth and the entire world is a principle of wisdom and virtue — or a principle that has neither?

Cleinias: How would you answer?

Athenian: Like this: if the whole path and movement of the heavens, and of everything in them, is by nature akin to the movement, revolution, and calculation of mind — if it proceeds according to kindred laws — then clearly we must say that the best soul governs the world and guides it along the path of good.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: But if the world moves wildly and erratically, then it's an evil soul that guides it.

Cleinias: Also true.

Athenian: Now, what kind of motion does mind exhibit? This is a hard question to answer well, so let me help.

Cleinias: Good.

Athenian: Let's not try to look directly at the answer, as if staring straight into the sun at noon — we'd only blind ourselves. I mean, let's not pretend we can fully grasp the nature of mind with mortal understanding. It's safer to look at an image.

Cleinias: What image?

Athenian: Of our ten kinds of motion, let's pick the one that most closely resembles mind. I'll remind you of it and then answer the question on behalf of all of us.

Cleinias: That sounds right.

Athenian: You remember our distinction between things at rest and things in motion?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: And that among things in motion, some move in one place while others move through more than one?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: Of these two, the motion that stays in one place — rotating around a center like a sphere on a lathe — is most closely related to the circular movement of mind.

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: I'm saying that both mind and circular motion share the same qualities: they move in the same way, in the same place, around the same center, in relation to the same thing, according to a single proportion and order — like the motion of a spinning globe. That's a fair image of mind, and it does us credit.

Cleinias: It certainly does.

Athenian: And the opposite kind of motion — the kind that's irregular, never in the same place, never around the same center, with no consistent proportion, order, or pattern — that would be akin to mindlessness and folly.

Cleinias: Absolutely.

Athenian: So after everything we've said, it's not hard to state clearly: since soul drives all things in their courses, either the best soul or its opposite must of necessity govern the revolution of the heavens.

Cleinias: And judging by our argument, Stranger, it would be impious to suggest that any soul other than the most perfect governs the heavens.

Athenian: You've grasped my meaning perfectly, Cleinias. Now let me ask you another question.

Cleinias: Go ahead.

Athenian: If soul drives the sun and moon and the other stars through their courses, doesn't it drive each one individually?

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: Then let's take one of them as our example. The same reasoning will apply to all.

Cleinias: Which one?

Athenian: Everyone can see the body of the sun. But no one sees its soul — or the soul of any other body, living or dead. And yet there's strong reason to believe that this invisible nature surrounds all of them, though it can only be perceived by the mind. So let's use mind and reflection to grasp the following point.

Cleinias: What point?

Athenian: If soul drives the sun, we won't go far wrong in proposing one of three possibilities.

Cleinias: Which three?

Athenian: Either the soul that moves the sun resides within that round, visible body — the way our soul lives within us and carries us around. Or the soul provides itself with an external body made of fire or air, as some thinkers claim, and physically pushes the sun along. Or third, the soul has no body at all but guides the sun through some extraordinary and wondrous power.

Cleinias: Yes — the soul must operate in one of these three ways.

Athenian: And this soul of the sun, which is necessarily greater than the sun itself — whether it carries the sun along in a chariot to give us light, or acts from outside, or guides it by whatever mysterious means — this soul ought to be considered a god by every person.

Cleinias: Yes — by anyone with the slightest trace of sense.

Athenian: And what about the stars, the moon, the years, the months, and the seasons? Mustn't we say the same thing? Since souls possessing every kind of excellence are the causes of all these things, those souls are gods — whether they inhabit physical bodies and govern the heavens from within, or whatever their mode of existence may be. Could anyone who accepts all this still dare to deny that the universe is full of gods?

Cleinias: No one would be that mad, Stranger.

Athenian: Good. Now, Megillus and Cleinias, let's offer terms to the person who has until now denied the existence of the gods, and then move on.

Cleinias: What terms?

Athenian: Either he must teach us that we were wrong to say that soul is the origin of all things, and produce a better argument — or, if he can't do better, he must yield to us and live the rest of his life believing that gods exist. Let's consider whether we've said enough, or not enough, to those who deny the existence of the gods.

Cleinias: More than enough, Stranger.

Athenian: Then we'll say no more to them. Now we need to address the person who believes gods exist but thinks they don't care about human affairs.

"My friend," we'll say to him, "your belief in the gods is driven by a kind of natural kinship — something in you is drawn to them, leading you to honor and believe in them. But then you see wicked, unjust people prospering in both private and public life — people who aren't truly happy but are wrongly judged to be, people celebrated by poets and writers alike. And this shakes your faith. Or perhaps you've personally witnessed monstrous acts of impiety, and you've seen people use criminal means to climb from nothing to the heights of power and wealth. When you observe all this, you don't want to blame the gods — they're your relatives, after all — but you can't make sense of it either. So, caught between confused reasoning and unwillingness to criticize the gods, you've ended up believing they exist but don't concern themselves with human affairs.

"To prevent this opinion from deepening into still greater impiety, let's see if we can reason it away before it takes root. We'll build on the argument we already made to the person who denied the gods entirely. Megillus and Cleinias, answer for our young friend as you did before. And if we hit any rough patches, I'll take over and carry you across the river, just as I did last time."

Cleinias: Very good. Do as you say, and we'll help however we can.

Athenian: It probably won't be hard to prove that the gods care about small things as much as great ones. After all, our friend was present and heard what we said — that the gods are perfectly good, and that caring for all things is fundamental to their nature.

Cleinias: Yes, he certainly heard that.

Athenian: So let's consider together what we mean by the virtue we're attributing to them. Surely we'd agree that being wise and self-controlled belongs to virtue, and the opposite belongs to vice?

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And courage is part of virtue, cowardice part of vice?

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: And one is honorable, the other disgraceful?

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: And whatever is base and contemptible — those are human failings. The gods have no share in anything of the sort.

Cleinias: Everyone would agree with that.

Athenian: Now — would we count carelessness, laziness, and self-indulgence as virtues? What do you think?

Cleinias: Absolutely not.

Athenian: They belong to the opposite category?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: And their opposites — diligence, attentiveness, discipline — belong to the category of virtue?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: So anyone who truly possesses all these good qualities wouldn't be lazy, heedless, or self-indulgent — like those useless drones the poets compare to stingless bees?

Cleinias: A perfect comparison.

Athenian: Then we certainly can't attribute to God a nature that God himself would despise. Anyone who says such a thing is completely out of line.

Cleinias: Of course not. How could God have a nature he hates?

Athenian: Think about it this way. If someone is responsible for managing something — whether they're god or human — and they pay attention to the big picture while neglecting the small details, wouldn't we be entirely wrong to praise them? It has to be for one of two reasons.

Cleinias: What are they?

Athenian: Either they think neglecting small things doesn't matter to the whole, or they know it matters but neglect those things out of carelessness and laziness. Is there any other explanation? Obviously, when something is genuinely beyond someone's power to manage — when even a god or lesser being lacks the strength or ability to handle it — that's not negligence. That's just limitation.

Cleinias: Obviously not.

Athenian: Now, let's put the question to both groups of offenders at once — the ones who say the gods can be bribed, and the ones who say the gods don't care about details. We're three, and they're two, so let's say to them: "First, you both acknowledge that the gods hear and see and know all things, and that nothing within the range of sense or knowledge can escape them. Do you accept that?"

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: "And do you accept that they possess all the power that mortals and immortals can possess?"

Cleinias: Of course they'll accept that too.

Athenian: "And all five of us — the three of us and the two of them — have agreed that the gods are good and perfect."

Cleinias: Without question.

Athenian: But if they're everything we've agreed they are, is it even conceivable that they'd act out of carelessness or laziness? Think about it: in human beings, inactivity is born of cowardice, and carelessness is born of inactivity and self-indulgence.

Cleinias: Exactly right.

Athenian: Then no god could ever be negligent out of laziness or carelessness — because gods have no cowardice in them.

Cleinias: Perfectly true.

Athenian: So if the gods do neglect the smaller and lesser matters of the universe, the only remaining explanation is that they know such things don't need their attention. What other explanation could there be?

Cleinias: None.

Athenian: So, my excellent friend — which interpretation should I take from your position? That the gods are careless because they're ignorant and don't know they ought to attend to these things? Or that they do know what's right but, like the lowest sort of people, choose the worse course because they're overcome by pleasure or pain?

Cleinias: That's impossible.

Athenian: Consider this: doesn't everything that has a soul share in the divine nature? And isn't man the most religious of all creatures?

Cleinias: That can't be denied.

Athenian: And we agree that all mortal creatures belong to the gods, just as the whole of heaven does?

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: So whether someone considers these possessions great or small, it would go against the gods' nature to neglect them — since the gods own us, and they're the most attentive and excellent of all owners. But there's another point to consider.

Cleinias: What's that?

Athenian: Perception and power work in opposite ways when it comes to difficulty. It's harder to perceive small things than large ones — but it's easier to manage and control small things than large ones.

Cleinias: Much easier.

Athenian: Consider a doctor who's willing and able to cure a patient's whole body. If he pays attention only to the major organs and ignores the smaller parts, how will the patient fare?

Cleinias: Not well at all.

Athenian: The same goes for generals, pilots, household managers, statesmen — every kind of manager you can name. The big things don't hold together without attention to the small. As builders say: the large stones don't sit right without the smaller ones.

Cleinias: Of course not.

Athenian: Then let's not consider God inferior to human craftsmen. A good craftsman uses the same skill to perfect both large and small work. And God, the wisest of all beings — who is both willing and able to take care of things — can't be compared to some lazy good-for-nothing who gives up on the easy tasks and only bothers with the hard ones.

Cleinias: Never, Stranger! Let's never entertain such an impious and false idea about the gods.

Athenian: I think we've argued enough with the person who accuses the gods of negligence.

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: He's been forced to see his error. But I think he still needs some words of comfort.

Cleinias: What comfort would you offer?

Athenian: Let's say to the young man:

"The ruler of the universe has arranged all things with an eye to the excellence and preservation of the whole. Every part, as far as possible, acts and is acted upon in a way appropriate to its nature. Over every part, down to the smallest fraction, overseers have been appointed who have worked out their governance with infinite precision. And your life, insignificant man, is one of those parts. Small as it is, it contributes to the whole. But what you fail to realize is this: every created thing exists for the sake of the whole, so that the life of the whole may flourish. You were created for the sake of the whole — not the whole for the sake of you.

"Every doctor, every skilled craftsman, does everything for the sake of the whole — working toward the common good, shaping the part to serve the whole, not the whole to serve the part. And you're resentful because you don't understand how what happens to you serves the good of the universe as far as the laws of creation allow.

"Now, since the soul passes through different bodies and undergoes all kinds of changes — whether through its own choices or through the influence of other souls — all that remains for the cosmic player is to move the pieces accordingly: sending the better nature to a better place, the worse to a worse place, giving each its proper portion."

Cleinias: What do you mean by that?

Athenian: I'm describing how the gods' oversight of everything could be perfectly easy. Imagine if someone were to rearrange all things at random, without regard to the whole — creating a living creature of water from fire, for example, instead of following the orderly progression of transformations, where many things come from one and one from many in a regular sequence. The complexity would be infinite. But the ruler of the world actually has a remarkably straightforward task.

Cleinias: How so?

Athenian: Here's how. When the king — the cosmic ruler — saw that all our actions are animated by soul, and that there's much virtue and much vice in them; that soul and body, though not eternal like the popular conception of gods, are nonetheless indestructible once they've come into being (for if either could be destroyed, living creatures would cease to be generated); and when he observed that goodness of soul naturally benefits, while evil naturally harms — seeing all this, he arranged each part so that goodness would most easily and effectively triumph over evil in the grand scheme of things.

He devised a universal plan determining where each type of soul would go and what place it would occupy. But the development of character and quality he left to individual free will. Each of us becomes what we are through the direction of our desires and the nature of our soul.

Cleinias: Yes, that's probably right.

Athenian: Everything that has a soul carries within itself a principle of change. As it changes, it moves in accordance with the law and order of destiny. Souls that have undergone only minor changes move less and stay closer to the earth's surface. But those that have changed more drastically and become more corrupt sink down into the abyss — into Hades and the regions below, the very places whose names fill people with terror, places they glimpse in dreams both during life and after death.

And whenever the soul takes on more goodness or more evil — through its own efforts or the powerful influence of other souls — when it enters into communion with divine virtue and becomes divine, it's carried to a different and better place, one perfect in holiness. But when it keeps company with evil, it changes its place accordingly.

"This is the justice of the gods who dwell on Olympus."

Young man — you who imagine the gods have forgotten you — know this: if you become worse, you'll join worse souls. If you become better, you'll join better ones. In every cycle of life and death, you'll do and suffer what like justly suffers at the hands of like. This is heaven's justice, and neither you nor any other unlucky soul will ever escape it. The powers that ordained it made sure of that. Take it seriously — because it will certainly take you seriously.

If you say, "I'm small — I'll burrow into the earth and hide," or "I'm great — I'll fly up to heaven and escape" — you're neither small enough nor great enough to avoid the fitting consequence, whether it comes in this life, in the world below, or in some still harsher place.

And this also explains those cases that troubled you — wicked people who seemed to rise from misery to greatness through unholy deeds. You thought you were seeing proof of divine neglect. But you were seeing a mirror, and you didn't know how to read the reflection. You didn't understand how the gods make all things work together for the good of the whole.

Bold man — do you really think you don't need to understand this? Without understanding it, no one can form any true idea of happiness or unhappiness, or hold any rational discussion about the meaning of life.

If Cleinias here and this worthy company of ours can convince you that you don't know what you're talking about when it comes to the gods, then God himself will help you. But if you want to hear more, listen to what we say to our third opponent — assuming you have any capacity to understand at all. I believe we've sufficiently proven that the gods exist and that they care about human beings. What remains is the third claim: that the gods can be bribed by the wicked and turned aside by gifts. This is something we must not concede to anyone, and every person should refute it with all their strength.

Cleinias: Very good. Let's do exactly that.

Athenian: Well then — I call on the gods themselves as my witnesses — tell me: if the gods are to be bribed, how would that work? Who are they, and what's their nature? They must at least be the rulers who constantly govern the entire heavens, mustn't they?

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: And what earthly rulers can we compare them to? Or who can be compared to them? Where can we find a smaller image of what's greater? Are they like charioteers driving teams of horses? Or like pilots steering ships? Maybe they're like generals commanding armies? Or doctors fighting against the diseases that attack the body? Or farmers anxiously watching the seasons' effect on their crops? Or shepherds tending their flocks?

We've established that the world is full of many good things and also many evils — more evils than goods, in fact. There's an unending battle raging, one that requires extraordinary vigilance. The gods and lesser divine beings are our allies in this battle, and we are their charges. What destroys us is injustice, arrogance, and folly. What saves us is justice, self-control, and wisdom. These virtues exist in their fullest form in the life of the gods, though traces of them can be found among human beings too.

But here on earth, we know there are souls driven by injustice — souls that can be compared to animals fawning on their keepers, whether those keepers are dogs, shepherds, or the most accomplished masters. For these corrupt souls, as wicked people openly declare, use flattery, prayers, and incantations to ingratiate themselves with their keepers, and they're allowed to profit at others' expense. And this sin, which goes by the name of dishonesty, is really the same kind of evil as disease in a body, pestilence in a season, and — when it appears in cities and governments — injustice.

Cleinias: Exactly right.

Athenian: So what must someone actually be claiming when they say the gods are always lenient to wrongdoers, as long as the wrongdoers share the spoils? It's as if wolves tossed a piece of their prey to the guard dogs, and the dogs, bought off by the offering, let the wolves tear the flock apart. Isn't that exactly the argument of anyone who says the gods can be bribed?

Cleinias: Precisely.

Athenian: Now, to which of those categories of guardian could anyone compare the gods without absurdity? Would anyone say the gods are like pilots who get drunk on offerings of wine and rich food, and end up capsizing the ship with everyone aboard?

Cleinias: Certainly not.

Athenian: Surely they're not like charioteers bribed to throw the race and let another team win?

Cleinias: That would be a horrifying image of the gods.

Athenian: And they're not like generals, or doctors, or farmers, or shepherds. No one would compare them to guard dogs that have been silenced by wolves.

Cleinias: Unspeakable.

Athenian: And aren't the gods the supreme guardians of all, watching over our highest and most important concerns?

Cleinias: The very highest.

Athenian: Then shall we say that these guardians of our noblest interests — the best guardians in existence — are inferior in virtue to dogs, or even to moderately decent human beings, who would never betray justice for the sake of bribes impiously offered by wicked people?

Cleinias: Absolutely not. The very idea is intolerable. Anyone who holds such a view deserves to be singled out as the most wicked and impious person alive.

Athenian: Then can we say that our three propositions have been sufficiently demonstrated? That gods exist, that they care about human beings, and that they can never be persuaded to commit injustice?

Cleinias: You have our complete agreement.

Athenian: I've spoken with some heat, I know — because I'm passionate in my opposition to wicked people. Let me tell you why, dear Cleinias. I didn't want the wicked to think that because they have clever arguments on their side, they can do whatever they please and believe whatever they want about the gods. That passion pushed me to speak too forcefully. But if we've succeeded even a little in making these people hate their own vices and love their opposites, then this introduction to our laws on impiety will not have been in vain.

Cleinias: Let's hope so. And even if we haven't fully succeeded, our style of argument won't disgrace the lawgiver.

Athenian: Now, after this introduction, the law itself must follow — the law that will put our arguments into practice. To all impious persons, the message is this: abandon your ways and turn to piety. And for those who refuse, the law on impiety shall be as follows:

If anyone is guilty of impiety in word or deed, any person who witnesses it shall report it to the magistrates, in support of the law. The magistrates who first receive the report shall bring the accused before the appropriate court, as the law requires. And if any magistrate, after receiving such a report, fails to act, that magistrate shall himself be tried for impiety by anyone willing to uphold the laws. If anyone is convicted, the court shall determine the punishment for each specific act of impiety. All such criminals shall be imprisoned.

There shall be three prisons in the state. The first is the ordinary prison, near the marketplace, for the general run of offenders. The second shall be near the Nocturnal Council and shall be called the "House of Reformation." The third shall be located in some remote, desolate region in the heart of the country, and shall bear a name suggesting retribution.

Now, there are three causes of impiety, as we've discussed, and each cause produces two varieties — making six types of impiety in total, which deserve to be distinguished and should not all receive the same punishment.

Consider the person who doesn't believe in the gods but has a naturally righteous character — someone who hates wickedness, despises injustice, avoids unrighteous people, and is drawn to the righteous. Then consider the person who, besides believing the world has no gods, is also intemperate — someone who happens to have a sharp memory and a quick wit. Both are unbelievers, but the second does far more harm than the first.

The first type may talk loosely about the gods, about sacrifices and oaths. By laughing at other people's beliefs, he may lead others into similar views — if he goes unpunished. But the second type, who holds the same opinions and is called "clever," is full of cunning and deceit. From this class come tyrants and demagogues, generals and founders of secret cults, and the so-called Sophists with their ingenious schemes. There are many varieties of unbelievers, but legislation is needed for only two: the hypocritical type, whose crime deserves death many times over, and the type who merely needs confinement and a talking-to.

Similarly, the belief that the gods don't care produces two more types of offense, and the belief that the gods can be bribed produces two more — six types in all.

Those who have fallen into error purely from lack of understanding — not from malice or corrupt character — shall be placed by the judge in the House of Reformation for a period of no less than five years. During this time, they shall have no contact with other citizens, except for members of the Nocturnal Council, who shall visit them with the aim of healing their souls. When their imprisonment is up, if any of them has come to their senses, they shall be restored to normal society. But if they haven't reformed and are convicted a second time, they shall be put to death.

As for the truly monstrous cases — those who not only deny the gods or claim the gods are negligent or bribable, but who go further, holding humanity in contempt, claiming to conjure the souls of the living, promising to summon the dead, and offering to charm the very gods with sacrifices and prayers, all for the sake of money — people who would destroy individuals, entire families, and whole cities for profit — anyone convicted of such crimes shall be confined in the prison in the center of the country. No free person shall ever visit them. They shall receive their food rations from public servants, as directed by the guardians of the law. And when they die, their bodies shall be cast beyond the borders, unburied. If any free citizen helps to bury them, that citizen shall face prosecution for impiety by anyone willing to bring the charge. However, if the convicted person leaves behind children fit for citizenship, the guardians of orphans shall care for them, just as they would for any other orphans, from the day of their parent's conviction.

In all these cases, there should be one additional law that will make people in general less likely to offend in word or deed, and less foolish — because they won't be allowed to practice unauthorized religious rites. Here is the straightforward rule:

No one shall maintain private religious shrines. When someone wishes to sacrifice, they shall go to the public temples and hand their offerings to the priests and priestesses who oversee these sacred matters. They may pray, and anyone who wishes may join them in prayer.

The reasoning is this: setting up gods and temples properly is no small matter — it requires great intelligence. And women especially, but men too, when they're sick or in danger or facing any kind of difficulty, or when they experience some stroke of good fortune — they have a way of consecrating the moment, vowing sacrifices, promising to build shrines to gods, demigods, and divine heroes. And when people are shaken by terrifying visions and dreams, they seek remedies in altars and temples, and they'll fill every house and every village square with them — placing them in the open air, or wherever the vision occurred. For all these reasons, the law must be obeyed.

There's another consideration as well. The law keeps an eye on the impious, ensuring they don't imagine they can propitiate the gods in secret — raising private temples, building private altars, offering private sacrifices and prayers — while actually multiplying their offenses infinitely. Such behavior brings divine punishment not only on themselves but on those who tolerate it, people who may be far better than they are. And in the end, the whole state reaps the harvest of their impiety — which, in a sense, it deserves.

God will not blame the lawgiver for enacting the following: No one shall possess shrines of the gods in private houses. If someone is found maintaining them and performing any sacred rites not publicly authorized — assuming the offender isn't also guilty of some other grave and impious crime — the person who discovers it shall inform the guardians of the law. The guardians shall order the offender to transfer their private rites to the public temples. If they refuse to comply, penalties shall be imposed until they do.

And if someone is proven guilty of impiety — not the trivial kind that comes from youthful foolishness, but the serious kind that grown adults commit — whether they have sacrificed publicly or privately to unauthorized gods, their sacrifice is impure, and they shall be punished with death. Whether the act was committed in earnest or from childish frivolity shall be determined by the guardians of the law before they bring the case to court and prosecute the offender for impiety.

Laws — Book 11

Athenian: Next, we need to properly regulate how people deal with each other in everyday life. The principle is actually very simple: don't touch what's mine, and don't take even the smallest thing that belongs to me without my permission. And may I be wise enough to treat others the way I'd want them to treat me. Let's start with buried treasure. I should never pray to the gods to find treasure that someone else buried for themselves and their family — someone who wasn't one of my own ancestors. And if I do stumble across such a treasure, I shouldn't dig it up. I should never have dealings with those so-called fortune-tellers who advise me to take what was entrusted to the earth, because the gain to my wallet wouldn't come close to matching what I'd lose in justice and virtue of soul. Justice in the soul is a better possession, in a better part of myself, than any amount of money in the bank. There's an old saying that applies to many things — "Don't move what shouldn't be moved" — and this is one of them. And we'd do well to believe the common tradition that says people who do such things end up without descendants.

Now, as for someone who doesn't care about having children and couldn't care less about the lawgiver — someone who digs up what neither he nor any ancestor of his buried, without the permission of the person who put it there, violating the simplest and most noble of laws, a rule laid down by no ordinary man: "Don't pick up what you didn't put down" — well, what this person deserves from the gods, only god knows. But here's what I'd have happen in practice: the first person who sees him should report him to the wardens of the city, if it happened in the city; to the wardens of the agora, if it happened in the marketplace; or to the wardens of the country and their commanders, if it happened in the countryside. Once the report is filed, the city shall send to Delphi, and whatever the oracle says about the money and the person who took it, the city shall carry out. If the informer is a free person, he'll earn the honor of having done the right thing. If he's a free person who fails to report it, he'll earn the dishonor of having done the wrong thing. If the informer is a slave, the state shall free him and pay his master the purchase price. But if a slave knows and doesn't report it, he'll be punished with death.

The same principle applies, in matters great and small, to lost property: if someone leaves something behind, whether on purpose or by accident, anyone who comes across it should leave it alone. These things are under the protection of the Goddess of the Roads, and the law dedicates them to her. But if someone defies this law and takes the property home — well, if the item is small and the person who takes it is a slave, any citizen at least thirty years old who catches him may give him a thorough beating. If the person is free, he'll be considered a mean-spirited law-breaker and shall pay ten times the value of what he took to the person who left it.

Now, if someone accuses another of possessing something that belongs to him — whether it's a large amount or small — and the other person admits he has the item but denies it belongs to the accuser, here's the procedure: if the property is registered with the magistrates according to law, the claimant shall summon the possessor, who must bring the item before the magistrates. If the public register shows who the rightful owner is, that person takes it and goes on his way. If the property is registered to someone who isn't present, anyone who posts sufficient bond on the absent owner's behalf can take the item as that person's representative. But if the property in question isn't registered with the magistrates at all, it shall stay in the custody of the three eldest magistrates until the case is tried. If the item is a living animal, the losing party shall pay the magistrates for its upkeep, and they shall settle the case within three days.
