This assembly shall be in constant conversation and discussion about the laws of their own city, about any especially good laws they hear about from elsewhere, and about branches of knowledge that seem useful — the kind that shed light on the examination of laws and without which the subject becomes murky and uncertain. Whatever knowledge the elders approve of, the younger members shall study with complete dedication. If any of the invited young men proves unworthy, the whole assembly shall blame the person who invited him. The rest of the city shall keep watch over the young men who distinguish themselves in this assembly — honoring them especially if they succeed, but holding them in even lower regard than others if they turn out to be inferior.

This is the assembly that the returning observer shall report to immediately upon coming home. If he's discovered anyone who has something valuable to say about the making of laws, education, or upbringing — or if he's made observations of his own — he shall share them with the whole assembly.

If he comes home neither better nor worse than when he left, he should at least be praised for his dedication. If he comes home significantly improved, he deserves even more praise. And not only while he lives — after his death, the assembly shall honor him with fitting tributes.

But if on his return he appears to have been corrupted — if he pretends to be wise when he isn't — then he shall have no contact with anyone, young or old. If he listens to the authorities and keeps to himself, he may live as a private citizen. But if he won't, and if he's convicted in court of meddling with education and the laws, let him die. And if he deserves to be indicted but none of the magistrates indicts him, that shall count as a mark against those magistrates when the rewards of virtue are being decided.

That's the kind of person who should go abroad, and those are the conditions. Now let's talk about receiving foreigners. There are four kinds of visitors we need to address.

The first kind is the seasonal trader — like a migratory bird — who shows up in summer, flies across the sea chasing commerce as long as the weather holds, and then moves on. These people shall be received in marketplaces and harbors and public buildings near the city but outside it, supervised by the magistrates appointed for that purpose. Those magistrates shall ensure that every foreign visitor receives fair treatment, but the visitor shall not be allowed to introduce any innovations. Official dealings with him shall be kept to the necessary minimum.

The second kind is a genuine sightseer — someone who comes to see and hear the festivals of the Muses. Hospitable accommodations should be provided for them at the temples, and the priests and temple staff should look after them. But they shouldn't stay longer than a reasonable time. Let them see and hear what they came for, and then let them go, having neither suffered nor caused any harm. If any dispute arises involving them — up to fifty drachmas — the priests shall be the judges. For anything larger, the case goes before the market wardens.

The third kind is someone who comes on official business from another country. He shall be received with public honors, hosted exclusively by the generals and cavalry commanders, and the person who entertains him — along with the city council — shall have sole responsibility for his care.

The fourth kind corresponds to our own observers — a visitor from another land who comes to study ours. These visits will be rare, and the visitor should be at least fifty years old. He may be coming to see something remarkable in other states, or to show something equally remarkable from his own. Let such a person go uninvited to the doors of the wise and the distinguished — being such a person himself. Let him go, for example, to the house of the superintendent of education, confident that he's a fitting guest for such a host. Or let him visit the homes of those who've won the prize of virtue, to exchange ideas — learning from them and teaching them in turn. When he's seen and heard everything, he shall depart as a friend taking leave of friends, and they shall honor him with gifts and appropriate marks of respect.

These are the customs by which our city should receive all visitors of either sex from other countries, and should send forth its own citizens — showing respect to Zeus, the god of hospitality. We won't forbid foreigners from our meals and sacrifices, the way they do in Egypt, and we won't drive them away with brutal proclamations.

When someone acts as a surety, let him formalize the arrangement clearly in a written document, in the presence of at least three witnesses if the amount is under a thousand drachmas, and at least five witnesses if it's above a thousand. The agent of a dishonest or untrustworthy seller shall be personally responsible — both agent and principal shall be equally liable.

If someone wants to search for something in another person's house, he shall enter wearing nothing but a short tunic, without a belt, after first swearing by the customary gods that he expects to find the item there. Then he may conduct his search, and the homeowner shall open the house and allow him to search everything, both sealed and unsealed.

If the homeowner won't allow the search, the person who was prevented shall take him to court, estimating the value of what he's looking for. If the homeowner is convicted, he pays double the item's value.

If the homeowner happens to be away, the people living in the house shall let the searcher examine the unsealed property. On the sealed items, the searcher shall place an additional seal and appoint whoever he likes to guard them for five days. If the homeowner is away longer than that, the searcher shall bring the city wardens with him, open the sealed property as well, and then — together with the household members and the wardens — seal everything back up as it was.

There shall be time limits on disputed property, after which the possessor can no longer be challenged.

Houses and land can't be disputed in our state. But for other property: if someone has been openly using and displaying items in the city, the marketplace, or the temples, and no one has claimed them — and someone later says they were searching for those items the whole time while the possessor made no attempt to hide them — if this goes on for a year, the seeker loses his right to claim after the year is up. If the items aren't shown in the marketplace or the city but only in the countryside, and no owner comes forward within five years, the claim is barred forever. If the items are used in the city but kept inside the house, the time limit is three years — or ten years if they're on private property in the country. And if someone has the items in a foreign country, there's no time limit at all. The owner can claim them whenever he finds them.

If anyone forcibly prevents another person from being present at a trial — whether that person is a principal party or a witness — and the person prevented is a slave (whether his own or someone else's), the case shall be declared incomplete and invalid. But if the person prevented is a free citizen, besides the case being invalid, the one who prevented him shall be imprisoned for a year and can be prosecuted for kidnapping by anyone who wants to bring the charge.

If anyone forcibly prevents a rival competitor from being present at an athletic, musical, or any other kind of contest, the person who discovers this shall inform the presiding judges, who shall ensure the would-be competitor is free to participate. If they can't manage it, and the person who blocked the competitor wins the prize, then the prize of victory shall be awarded to the one who was blocked. He can have himself inscribed as the winner in whatever temples he chooses. The one who interfered shall not be allowed to make any dedication or inscription referencing that contest, and in any case he'll be liable for damages — whether he won or lost.

If anyone knowingly receives stolen property, he shall suffer the same punishment as the thief. If someone harbors an exile, the penalty is death.

Every person should regard the state's friends and enemies as their own. If anyone makes peace or war on his own account, without the state's authority, he too shall face the death penalty — just like the one who harbors an exile. And if any faction of the city declares war or peace independently, the generals shall indict the instigators, and if convicted, death shall be the penalty.

Those who serve their country should serve without accepting bribes. And we shouldn't make excuses or approve the saying that "you should take gifts as a reward for good deeds, just not for bad ones." Knowing which you're really doing, and standing by that knowledge, is no easy thing. The safest course is simply to obey the law that says: accept no bribes for services. Whoever disobeys and is convicted shall simply die.

For purposes of taxation, every citizen ought to have his property assessed. Each tribe's members should also file a register of annual income with the country wardens, so that there are two valuations available. Public officials can then use whichever assessment they think is better in any given year — whether they prefer to take a percentage of total value or of annual revenue, after deducting what's paid to the common dining tables.

Regarding offerings to the gods: a moderate person should show moderation in what he offers. The land itself and the hearth of every home are already sacred to all the gods, so no one should dedicate them to the gods a second time.

Gold and silver, whether in private hands or in temples, breed envy in other cities. Ivory, being the product of a dead body, isn't a proper offering. Iron and bronze are instruments of war. But offerings of wood are fine — let someone bring whatever he likes, as long as it's carved from a single block. Same with stone for public temples. For woven work, no one should offer more than one woman can produce in a month. White is the color that suits the gods, especially for woven goods. Dyes should only be used for military decorations.

The most divine gifts are birds and painted images, and they should be the kind a single artist can complete in a day. All other offerings should follow a similar rule of modesty.

Now that the entire city has been divided into its parts — the kind and number we've described — and we've established laws covering all the most important contracts, as far as we could, the next step is to see that justice is properly administered.

The first tier of courts shall consist of judges chosen jointly by the plaintiff and the defendant. These should really be called arbiters rather than judges. The second tier shall be the village and tribal courts corresponding to our twelve-part division of the land, where litigants go when the stakes are higher and the first arbiters couldn't settle things. The defendant, if he loses a second time, shall pay a fifth more than the original damages. If he still isn't satisfied and wants to try a third time, he can take the case to the select judges. If he loses there too, he pays the full damages plus half again as much.

For the plaintiff: if he loses before the first judges but presses on to the second court and wins, he gets the original damages plus an extra fifth. If he loses, he pays that same amount. If he's still not satisfied and insists on going to a third court, then if he wins he gets the damages plus half again. If he loses, he pays half the amount he was claiming.

As for the assignment of judges to courts by lot, filling out their numbers, appointing court officers, scheduling cases, managing votes and procedural delays, the order in which cases are heard, the deadlines for filing responses and appearing — all of these things we've already addressed. But there's no harm in saying what's right twice or even three times. All the smaller, easier procedural details that the senior lawmaker left out can be filled in by a younger colleague. Private courts will be adequately regulated this way.

For public and state courts, and the courts that magistrates use in administering their offices — many respectable systems already exist in other states, designed by good people. The guardians of the law can draw on these through careful study, adapting what's needed for our new state, testing things through experience, and adjusting until every detail seems right. Then they'll set the final seal on these procedures and make them permanent.

As for the silence that should be maintained by judges, the avoidance of ill-omened words and their opposites, and the different conceptions of justice, goodness, and honor that exist in our state compared with others — some of this has already been mentioned, and more will be said as we approach the end.

Anyone who wants to be a fair judge should keep all these things in view and should possess written texts about them, so he can study and learn. Of all forms of knowledge, the knowledge of good laws has the greatest power to improve the person who studies it. Otherwise there'd be no meaning in the fact that our divine and admirable law has a name related to "mind" — nomos and nous. And of all other words — praise and blame of individuals found in poetry and prose, whether written down or spoken in daily life, whether argued over contentiously or agreed with too easily — the one sure touchstone is the writings of the lawmaker. The righteous judge should carry these in his mind as an antidote to all other words. In this way he makes himself and the city stand upright: for the good, he ensures the continuance and growth of justice; for the bad, he works toward conversion — from ignorance, from lack of self-control, from every kind of wrongdoing — as far as their flawed minds can be healed.

But for those whose life's thread is truly finished — death is the only remedy for souls in their condition. And I'll say it again, as I've said before. Judges and chief judges who act on these principles will deserve the praise of the whole city.

When the year's lawsuits are completed, the following rules shall govern their enforcement: First, the judge shall assign to the winning party all the property of the losing party, except for bare necessities. This assignment shall be announced by a herald immediately after each decision, in the hearing of the judges. When the month following the court session arrives — unless the winner has already been paid — the court shall follow up on the case and hand over the loser's assets. If the loser doesn't have enough to pay, and the shortfall is at least one drachma, the insolvent person shall have no right to bring any lawsuit against anyone else until he's satisfied the winner's claim. Other people, however, can still bring suits against him.

If anyone who's been convicted refuses to acknowledge the authority of the court that convicted him, the magistrates whose authority is being flouted shall bring him before the court of the guardians of the law. If he's found guilty there, he shall be put to death as a subverter of the entire state and its laws.

And so a man is born and raised. He begets and raises his own children. He has his dealings with other people — suffers punishment when he does wrong, receives satisfaction when he's been wronged. And eventually, in the fullness of time, he grows old under the protection of the laws, and his end comes in the natural order.

Regarding funeral rites for the dead, of either sex: the religious ceremonies that are properly performed — whether they belong to the gods of the underworld or of this world — shall be determined by the interpreters, and their authority on this shall be absolute.

Burial sites must not be on land suited for farming. There shall be no large or small monuments on productive ground. The dead shall occupy that part of the country naturally suited for receiving and concealing bodies, with as little impact on the living as possible. No one — living or dead — shall deprive the living of the sustenance that their foster-mother, the earth, is naturally disposed to provide.

The burial mound shall be piled no higher than five men could build in five days. The headstone shall be no larger than what's needed to hold the dead person's eulogy in four lines of verse. The body shall not be laid out at home any longer than necessary to confirm the person is truly dead and not merely unconscious. Generally speaking, the third day after death is a reasonable time for carrying the body to the grave.

Now, we must believe the lawmaker when he tells us that the soul is in every way superior to the body, and that even in life, what makes each of us who we are is the soul alone. The body merely follows us around, shaped in our likeness. That's why it's quite right to say that the bodies of the dead are our images, our shadows — for the true and immortal being of each of us, which we call the soul, departs to give an account before other gods. That's an inspiring hope for the good, but terrifying for the wicked, as our ancestral traditions tell us.

Those traditions also say there isn't much you can do for someone after they're dead. The living — that's who everyone should be helping, all their kin. While a person is alive, help them be the holiest and most just they can be, so that after death they won't have serious offenses to answer for in the world below.

If that's true, then no one should waste money under the illusion that this lifeless mass of flesh being buried has anything to do with the real person. The real person — son, brother, beloved, whoever it is they think they're laying in the earth — has gone away to fulfill his own destiny. The duty of the living is to tend to the present, to spend moderately, and to treat the lifeless body as a fitting altar to the gods below. But moderation shouldn't be confused with stinginess.

Here's the law: total funeral expenses for someone of the highest property class shall not exceed five minas. For the second class, three minas. For the third, two minas. For the fourth, one mina. These are reasonable limits.

The guardians of the law should take special care of every stage of life — childhood, adulthood, every age. And at the very end, there should be one guardian of the law presiding over the funeral, chosen by the deceased's friends. It shall be to his credit if things are managed with fairness and moderation, and a mark against him if they aren't.

The laying out of the body and other ceremonies should follow custom. But when a statesman adopts custom as his guide, we have to allow him certain changes. It would be wrong, for example, to order anyone to weep or to refrain from weeping over the dead. But he may forbid wailing, and he may require that the voice of mourning not be heard outside the house. He may forbid bringing the body into the open streets and processing through them with mourners. And he may require that the funeral party be outside the city before daybreak.

These are our laws on these matters. Whoever obeys is free from penalty. Whoever disobeys even one of the guardians of the law shall be punished by all of them with a suitable penalty.

Other forms of burial — and the denial of burial, which shall be refused to temple robbers, patricides, and the like — have already been addressed in earlier laws. So our work of legislation is very nearly complete.

But in every endeavor, the end doesn't consist in simply doing something, acquiring something, or establishing something. The real end is reached only when we've provided for the perfect and lasting continuation of what we've built. Until we do that, our creation remains unfinished.

Cleinias: That's well said, Stranger. But I'd like you to explain more clearly what you mean.

Athenian: Cleinias, many things were wisely said and sung in ancient times. The saying about the Fates was one of them.

Cleinias: Which saying?

Athenian: The one that says Lachesis — the Allotter — is the first of the Fates, Clotho — the Spinner — is the second, and Atropos — the Unchangeable — is the third. It's Atropos who preserves the things we've spoken of, the things we compared to cloth made permanent by fire: both she and fire create the quality of irreversibility.

I'm talking about the force that gives a state and its government not just health and physical survival, but law — or rather, the preservation of law — in its very soul. And unless I'm mistaken, this is what our laws are still missing. We still need to figure out how to implant in them this quality of being unchangeable.

Cleinias: That would be no small achievement — finding how to give anything that kind of permanence.

Athenian: But it certainly can be done. That much I see clearly.

Cleinias: Then let's not even think about stopping until we've given our laws this quality. It would be ridiculous to spend all this effort and then set the whole thing on a shaky foundation.

Athenian: I agree completely. I'm with you on that.

Cleinias: Good. So what, in your view, is going to save our government and our laws? How do we do it?

Athenian: Didn't we say there must be a council in our city, formed as follows? The ten oldest guardians of the law, along with everyone who has won prizes of virtue, shall meet in the same assembly. The council is also to include those who've traveled abroad hoping to learn something useful for preserving the laws — men who've returned safely, been tested in these matters, and proved themselves worthy of participation. Each member selects a young man of at least thirty, having first judged for himself whether the young man is worthy by nature and education, and then proposing him to the others. If they also think he's worthy, they accept him. If not, the rejection is kept secret from the general public, and especially from the rejected candidate himself.

The council was to meet early in the morning, when everyone is most free from other business, public or private. Didn't we say something like this earlier?

Cleinias: Yes, we did.

Athenian: Then, returning to this council — I'd say that if we set it firmly in place as the anchor of the state, our city, equipped with everything it needs, will preserve everything we want it to preserve.

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: Now is the time for me to speak the full truth, in all seriousness.

Cleinias: Well said. I hope you'll follow through.

Athenian: Consider this, Cleinias: in everything that exists, there is a natural guardian, a natural savior. In a living creature, the soul and the head are the primary saviors.

Cleinias: Again — what do you mean?

Athenian: The well-being of those two is what preserves every living thing.

Cleinias: How so?

Athenian: The soul, among other things, contains mind. The head, among other things, contains sight and hearing. When mind combines with the noblest senses and becomes one with them — that can truly be called the salvation of any living being.

Cleinias: Yes, exactly.

Athenian: Yes, indeed. But what does that intelligence focus on when — combined with the senses — it saves ships in storms and in fair weather alike? On a ship, when the pilot and sailors unite their perceptions with the guiding mind of the helmsman, don't they save both themselves and their vessel?

Cleinias: Absolutely.

Athenian: We don't need a lot of examples here. Think about it: what would a general aim at, if he were seeking victory? What would a doctor aim at?

Cleinias: Right.

Athenian: Doesn't the general aim at victory and superiority in war? And don't the doctor and his staff aim at producing health in the body?

Cleinias: Obviously.

Athenian: A doctor who's ignorant of what we just called health — of what the body needs — or a general who doesn't know what victory means, or anyone ignorant of the specifics of the skills we just described — none of them can be said to truly understand those fields.

Cleinias: They can't.

Athenian: So what about the state? If someone turns out to be ignorant of the goal the statesman should be aiming at, should he be called a ruler at all? And even if we do call him that, will he ever be able to preserve something when he doesn't even know what he's trying to preserve?

Cleinias: Impossible.

Athenian: Then if our settlement is going to be complete, we need some institution that can tell us what the aim of the state is, and how to reach it, and which law or which person will give us the best guidance toward that end. Any state that lacks such an institution is likely to operate without mind or reason, doing everything by sheer chance.

Cleinias: Very true.

Athenian: So in which part or institution of our state can we find this kind of guardian power? Can we say?

Cleinias: I'm not entirely sure, Stranger. But I have a suspicion you're referring to the assembly you just said should meet at night.

Athenian: You understand me perfectly, Cleinias. And as our argument implies, this council must possess every virtue. The beginning of virtue isn't guessing at many things and getting it wrong. It's looking steadily at one thing and directing all our efforts toward it.

Cleinias: Exactly.

Athenian: So now we can see why it's no surprise that states go astray. The reason is that their lawgivers aim at such different things. Some set it down that certain individuals should rule the state, whether they're good or bad, and call that justice. Others say the goal is making citizens rich, never mind whether they end up as someone else's slaves or not. Others are focused entirely on freedom. And some try to aim at two things at once — they want to be free while also ruling over other states. The "wisest" men — or so they think they are — look at all these goals and similar ones, but there isn't a single unified aim that they honor above all others and orient everything toward.

Cleinias: Then our earlier point stands, Stranger. We said that all laws should look to one thing only, and we agreed that the right name for that one thing is virtue.

Athenian: Yes.

Cleinias: And we said virtue has four kinds?

Athenian: Exactly.

Cleinias: And that mind is the leader of the four, and that the other three virtues — and everything else — should defer to it?

Athenian: You're following beautifully, Cleinias. Stay with me to the end. We've already said that the mind of the pilot, the mind of the doctor, the mind of the general — each looks to the one thing it should look to. Now let's turn to the mind of the statesman, and put our question to it as though it were a living being:

"Remarkable creature — what are you looking at? The doctor can name his single aim in life. The general can name his. But you, who claim to be superior to all other intelligent beings — when you're asked, you can't say what your aim is?"

Can you tell me, Megillus? And you, Cleinias? After all the explanations I've given you — can you say clearly what the aim of political intelligence is?

Cleinias: We can't, Stranger.

Athenian: But shouldn't we want to see it — to understand where to find it?

Cleinias: For example, where?

Athenian: For example: we've said there are four kinds of virtue. Since there are four, each must be one thing.

Cleinias: Of course.

Athenian: And yet we call all four by a single name. We say courage is virtue, wisdom is virtue, and the same for the other two — as if they weren't really many, but one thing: virtue.

Cleinias: Right.

Athenian: It's not hard to see how these two — say, courage and wisdom — differ from each other. They have different names, after all. The same goes for the others. But it is hard to explain why we call these two, and the rest, by the single name "virtue."

Cleinias: What do you mean?

Athenian: Let me make it clear. Let's divide this into questions and answers.

Cleinias: Again — what do you mean?

Athenian: Ask me: "What is this one thing you call virtue, and then turn around and describe as two — one part being courage, the other wisdom?" I'll tell you how it works: one of them has to do with fear. Even animals share in it, and very young children. I'm talking about courage. A courageous temperament is a gift of nature, not of reason. But a wise and understanding soul — that has never existed, doesn't exist, and never will exist without reason. It's an entirely different thing.

Cleinias: True.

Athenian: So I've told you how the two are different. Now you tell me how they're one and the same. Imagine I ask you: "In what way are the four one?" And once you've answered that, you'll have earned the right to ask me: "In what way are they four?" Then let's take it further. When something has both a name and a definition, is it enough for real knowledge to know only the name and not the definition? Can someone who's supposed to know about great and important truths be ignorant of all this without disgrace?

Cleinias: I don't think so.

Athenian: And is there anything greater for a lawmaker, a guardian of the law — for someone who thinks he surpasses all other people in virtue and has won the prize of excellence — than these very qualities we're talking about: courage, temperance, wisdom, justice?

Cleinias: How could there be?

Athenian: And shouldn't the interpreters, teachers, and lawgivers — the guardians of the other citizens — be able to surpass everyone else in explaining to those who need to learn, or whose misdeeds call for punishment and correction, what virtue and vice actually are? Or should some poet who's wandered into the city, or some random person claiming to educate the young, prove himself superior to the man who's won the prize for every virtue? And if the guardians aren't adequate in speech and action, if they lack proper knowledge of virtue — can we be surprised when such an unguarded city suffers the same fate as so many others do today?

Cleinias: Surprised? No.

Athenian: Then should we follow through on what we've said? Or can we find a way to give our guardians a more precise understanding of virtue — in both word and deed — than the average person has? Can we somehow make our city resemble a rational being with a functioning head and senses, because it possesses such a guardian power?

Cleinias: What exactly is your comparison, Stranger?

Athenian: Don't we see that the city is the body, and the younger guardians — chosen for their natural talents — are stationed in the head of state, their souls full of eyes with which they survey the whole city? They keep watch, pass their observations on to the memory, and report everything that happens to the elders. And those we compared to the mind — the old men who have many wise thoughts — they deliberate, using the younger men as their agents, advising with them. In this way, both together truly preserve the entire state.

Is this how our state should be organized? Or should all citizens be equal in their training and knowledge? Should certain people receive more thorough education than others?

Cleinias: That they should all be equal, my good friend, is impossible.

Athenian: Then we need to move to a more rigorous level of training than anything we've discussed so far.

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And isn't the kind of training we need exactly the one we were just describing?

Cleinias: Exactly.

Athenian: Didn't we say that a craftsman or guardian, if he's to be truly excellent, shouldn't just see many different goals — he should press forward to the one, and knowing it, order everything with a view to it?

Cleinias: Yes.

Athenian: And is there any sharper method of examining anything than the ability to look at a single idea drawn from many different things?

Cleinias: Perhaps not.

Athenian: Not "perhaps not" — "certainly not," my friend, is the right answer. No one has ever discovered a truer method than this.

Cleinias: I'll defer to your judgment, Stranger. Let's proceed as you suggest.

Athenian: Then it seems we must require the guardians of our divine state to grasp, first and foremost, what that principle is which is the same in all four virtues — the same thing present in courage, temperance, justice, and wisdom that, being one, we rightly call by the single name "virtue."

This, my friends — if you'll agree — we must hold on to and not let go until we've explained it adequately. Tell me: is it to be understood as one thing, or as a whole, or as both, or in some other way? Are we likely to be in a truly virtuous condition if we can't even say whether virtue is many things, or four, or one?

If we take counsel together, we'll find some way to give this principle a place in our city. But if you've decided we should just let the matter drop, we will.

Cleinias: We must not, Stranger — I swear by the god of strangers, we must not! In our view, you're speaking the absolute truth. But we'd like to know how you'll make this happen.

Athenian: Hold on — before you ask that, let's first make sure we agree that it does need to happen.

Cleinias: Of course it does — if it can.

Athenian: And what about the good and the honorable? Should we take the same approach? Should our guardians only know that each is many, or also how and in what way they are one?

Cleinias: They have to understand how they're one.

Athenian: And should they just think about it privately, unable to articulate what they believe?

Cleinias: Absolutely not. That would be the condition of a slave.

Athenian: And can't the same be said about all good things — that the true guardians of the laws should know the truth about them, be able to explain it in words, and carry it out in action, judging what is and isn't well-ordered according to nature?

Cleinias: Certainly.

Athenian: And isn't the knowledge of the gods — which we explored with such care — one of the noblest forms of knowledge? To know that the gods exist, and to understand how great their power is, as far as human understanding allows? We can excuse the mass of citizens for merely following the voice of the laws. But we cannot accept as guardians anyone who hasn't labored to acquire every possible piece of evidence about the gods. Our city is absolutely forbidden to choose as a guardian of the law, or to place in the highest order of virtue, anyone who isn't divinely inspired and hasn't devoted himself to these questions.

Cleinias: That's only just, as you say — that anyone who's lazy about these matters, or incapable of engaging with them, should be excluded from positions of honor.

Athenian: Are we confident that two things lead people to believe in the gods, as we said before?

Cleinias: What two things?

Athenian: One is the argument about the soul — that it is the oldest and most divine of all things that come into being. When motion reaches the soul, it gives it perpetual existence.

The other is the argument from the orderly motion of the stars and of all things under the mind that designed the universe. No one who has looked at the world with care and understanding has ever come away more godless from the experience. The opposite happens — though most people assume otherwise. They think that people who study these things through astronomy and the associated sciences must end up atheists, because what they see looks like things happening by blind necessity rather than by an intelligent will pursuing what's good.

Cleinias: What's actually the case?

Athenian: Exactly the opposite of what people used to think. There was a time when people thought the sun and stars had no soul. Even back then, people wondered at them, and some who studied them more carefully began to suspect that if these bodies were truly soulless and mindless, they could never have moved with such astonishing mathematical precision. Even then, some dared to propose that mind was the force ordering the universe.

But these same thinkers made an error about the soul — they thought it was younger than the body, not older. And that mistake overturned everything. Or rather, it overturned themselves. Because when they looked at the heavenly bodies, everything appeared to be made of stones and earth and other lifeless stuff, and they assigned the causes of everything to these dead materials. That's what gave rise to so much atheism and confusion. It provoked the poets to heap abuse on philosophers — comparing them to dogs howling at the void, and other such insults.

But now, as I was saying, the situation is reversed.

Cleinias: How so?

Athenian: No one can be a true worshiper of the gods who doesn't understand these two principles: that the soul is the oldest of all things that are born, that it's immortal, and that it rules over all bodies. Moreover — and I've said this several times now — anyone who hasn't studied the mind of nature visible in the stars, who hasn't gone through the preparatory training, who hasn't seen how music connects to all these things and harmonized them with laws and institutions — such a person can't give a reasoned account of things that have reasons. And anyone who can't acquire this knowledge on top of the ordinary virtues of a citizen is not fit to be a ruler of the whole state. He should be subordinate to other rulers.

So, Cleinias and Megillus, let's consider whether we should add to all the laws we've discussed one more: that this nocturnal assembly of magistrates, which has also participated in the entire educational program we've outlined, shall be established by law as a guard set for the salvation of the state. Shall we propose this?

Cleinias: Of course, my good friend — absolutely, if it's in any way possible.

Athenian: Then let's all make a shared effort to achieve it. I'll gladly do my part. I've thought long and hard about these matters and have considerable experience with them. And I expect I'll be able to find others who can help too.

Cleinias: I agree, Stranger. We should follow the road where God is leading us. And the question of how to travel it rightly — that's what needs to be worked out and explained.

Athenian: Megillus — Cleinias — we can't legislate the details of this council until it's actually constituted. When it is, then we'll determine what authority it should have. But explaining how all of this should be organized properly would require a long discussion.

Cleinias: What do you mean? What's this new point?

Athenian: First, you'd need to draw up a list of people who are suited for council duty by their age, their studies, their character, and their habits. Next, it won't be easy for them to discover on their own what they should study, or to find someone who's already figured it out and become that person's student. On top of that, trying to prescribe in advance exactly when and how long each subject should be studied would be pointless. The students themselves don't know what's truly been learned until the knowledge has actually settled into their souls. So all these details, while they couldn't properly be called secret, might be called impossible to lay out in advance. Stated ahead of time, they'd be meaningless.

Cleinias: Then what should we do, Stranger, given all this?

Athenian: As the proverb says, the answer isn't hidden — it's out in the open for all of us. We have to stake everything on the throw, risking triple sixes or triple ones. And I'm willing to share the risk with you by stating and explaining my views on education and upbringing — the question that keeps resurfacing.

The risk is not small or ordinary. And I'd urge you, Cleinias, in particular, to take this to heart. If you organize the city of the Magnetes properly — or whatever name God may give it — you'll win the greatest glory. Or at least you'll be remembered as the most courageous of men by those who come after.

Dear companions — if we can only establish this divine assembly, we should hand the city over to them. Not one of us present here, lawmakers as we've been, would hesitate to do that. And then the state will be made perfect. It will become a waking reality — what just a short while ago we were trying to create as a dream, as a vision in the mind. We were mixing together reason and intelligence in a single image, hoping our citizens might be properly blended and rightly educated. And dwelling in the citadel of the land, having been educated in the way we described, they would become perfect guardians — the likes of which we've never seen in our entire lives — by virtue of the saving power that is in them.

Megillus Dear Cleinias, after everything that's been said, either we have to convince the Stranger to stay — by begging him, by any means necessary — and make him a partner in founding this city, or we have to give up the whole undertaking.

Cleinias: You're absolutely right, Megillus. I'll do my best. And you have to help me.

Megillus I will.
